Consider four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_x$ arranged in a diamond configuration. A DC excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across opposite nodes A and C. A sensitive galvanometer $G$ is connected across nodes B and D.

**Step 1:** Define the node potentials.
Let node A be at potential $V_A = V_s$ and node C be at $V_C = 0$.

**Step 2:** The potential at node B (across $R_1$ and $R_2$ series divider from A to C):

$$
V_B = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 3:** The potential at node D (across $R_3$ and $R_x$ series divider from A to C):

$$
V_D = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_3 + R_x}
$$

**Step 4:** The galvanometer current is zero only when $V_B = V_D$ (null condition). Equating:

$$
V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_3 + R_x}
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $V_s$ on both sides:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{R_x}{R_3 + R_x}
$$

**Step 6:** Cross-multiply:

$$
R_2 \cdot (R_3 + R_x) = R_x \cdot (R_1 + R_2)
$$

**Step 7:** Expand both sides:

$$
R_2 R_3 + R_2 R_x = R_x R_1 + R_x R_2
$$

**Step 8:** Subtract $R_2 R_x$ from both sides (it cancels):

$$
R_2 R_3 = R_x R_1
$$

**Step 9:** Solve for the unknown resistance $R_x$:

$$
\boxed{R_x = R_3 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}}
$$

**Step 10:** Sensitivity note. Since the reference resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ are precision calibrated to **0.01 %** tolerance or better, the accuracy of $R_x$ is exceptionally high — this is why null-type measurement wins in metrology.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Examination-Ready)

| **Parameter** | **Deflection-Type** | **Null-Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Pointer deflection proportional to measurand | Balance against reference |
| Sensitivity | High (slight changes visible) | Adjustable via detector |
| Accuracy | Moderate (1–5 %) | Very High (0.01–0.1 %) |
| Speed of Response | Fast | Slow (manual balancing) |
| Loading Effect | Significant | Negligible |
| Construction | Simple | Complex |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Best For | Field/Process measurement | Laboratory/Calibration |
| Example | Bourdon Gauge, D'Arsonval meter | Wheatstone Bridge, Potentiometer |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Transducer Classification (Active vs. Passive)

**Problem:** Classify the following transducers with justification: (i) Thermocouple, (ii) RTD, (iii) Piezoelectric sensor, (iv) Strain gauge.

**Solution:**

(i) **Thermocouple — Active.** It generates an EMF (Seebeck effect) directly from the temperature difference between hot and cold junctions; no external power is needed.

(ii) **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) — Passive.** Its resistance changes with temperature, but it requires an external current source to produce a measurable voltage drop.

(iii) **Piezoelectric Sensor — Active.** When mechanically stressed, it generates a charge/voltage directly (direct piezoelectric effect); no excitation needed.

(iv) **Strain Gauge — Passive.** Its resistance changes with strain, but it must be excited by an external voltage (typically via a Wheatstone bridge) to give a readable output.

**Classification Output:**

$$
T_i = \begin{cases} \text{Active} & \text{if } P_{ext} = 0 \\ \text{Passive} & \text{if } P_{ext} > 0 \end{cases}
$$

where $P_{ext}$ is the external excitation power required.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Resolution of Digital Instrument

**Problem:** A digital voltmeter has a 12-bit ADC and full-scale range of $\pm \text{20 V}$. Calculate its resolution in volts.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total number of discrete levels for a 12-bit unipolar ADC:

$$
N = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Step 2:** Full-scale range:

$$
\text{FS} = 40 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3:** Resolution (smallest detectable step):

$$
\boxed{\text{Res} = \frac{40}{4096} \approx 9.77 \text{ mV}}
$$

**Step 4:** This means any voltage change below **9.77 mV** cannot be distinguished — illustrating the inherent superiority of high-bit digital instruments over analog pointer-type meters in KTU lab scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Measurement Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MEASUREMENT SYSTEMS"] --> B["BY COMPARISON METHOD"]
    A --> C["BY INDICATION METHOD"]
    A --> D["BY SIGNAL TYPE"]
    A --> E["BY INSTRUMENT FUNCTION"]
    A --> F["BY TRANSDUCER NATURE"]

    B --> B1["DIRECT MEASUREMENT"]
    B --> B2["INDIRECT MEASUREMENT"]

    C --> C1["DEFLECTION TYPE"]
    C --> C2["NULL TYPE ZERO DEFLECTION"]

    D --> D1["ANALOG INSTRUMENT"]
    D --> D2["DIGITAL INSTRUMENT"]

    E --> E1["INDICATING"]
    E --> E2["RECORDING"]
    E --> E3["INTEGRATING"]
    E --> E4["CONTROLLING"]

    F --> F1["ACTIVE TRANSDUCER"]
    F --> F2["PASSIVE TRANSDUCER"]

    B1 --> B1A["Example: Steel rule length"]
    B2 --> B2A["Example: Density from mass volume"]

    C1 --> C1A["Example: Bourdon gauge"]
    C2 --> C2A["Example: Wheatstone bridge"]

    D1 --> D1A["Example: Pointer multimeter"]
    D2 --> D2A["Example: Digital multimeter"]

    F1 --> F1A["Example: Thermocouple Piezo sensor"]
    F2 --> F2A["Example: RTD Strain gauge LVDT"]
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Generalized Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PRIMARY SENSING ELEMENT"] --> T["TRANSDUCER"]
    T --> C["CONDITIONING STAGE"]
    C --> O["OUTPUT INDICATOR OR DISPLAY"]
    O --> R["RECORDING OR CONTROL UNIT"]

    M["MEASURAND"] --> P
    R --> FEED["FEEDBACK FOR CONTROL"]

    P -.->|"Step 1: Detects physical quantity"| T
    T -.->|"Step 2: Converts to electrical signal"| C
    C -.->|"Step 3: Amplifies and filters"| O
    O -.->|"Step 4: Displays numeric or pointer value"| R
```

---

### 4.3 Null-Type vs Deflection-Type Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEF["DEFLECTION TYPE INSTRUMENT"]
        D1["Measurand Input"] --> D2["Sensing Element"]
        D2 --> D3["Pointer Mechanism"]
        D3 --> D4["Scale Reading"]
        D4 -.->|"Pointer position = value"| D1
    end

    subgraph NULL["NULL TYPE INSTRUMENT"]
        N1["Measurand Input"] --> N2["Comparator"]
        N3["Reference Standard"] --> N2
        N2 --> N4["Null Detector"]
        N4 -->|"Imbalance"| N5["Adjust Reference"]
        N5 -->|"Repeat"| N2
        N2 -->|"Null achieved"| N6["Read Reference Value"]
        N6 -.->|"Reference value = measurand"| N1
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Active vs Passive Transducer Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACT["ACTIVE TRANSDUCER"]
        A1["Measurand Energy"] --> A2["Active Transducer"]
        A2 --> A3["Electrical Output"]
        A3 -.->|"Self-generated, no external power"| A1
    end

    subgraph PAS["PASSIVE TRANSDUCER"]
        P1["External Excitation Source"] --> P2["Passive Transducer"]
        M2["Measurand Modulation"] --> P2
        P2 --> P3["Modulated Output"]
        P3 -.->|"Requires excitation + measurand"| M2
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part A comprises short-answer questions testing definitions, classifications, and conceptual clarity. Each carries 3 marks.*

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between deflection-type and null-type instruments. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Deflection-Type** | **Null-Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Output indicated by pointer deflection proportional to measurand | Output determined by balancing against a known reference |
| Accuracy | Moderate | Very High |
| Example | Bourdon pressure gauge | Wheatstone bridge |

**Examples cited:** Bourdon gauge (deflection) and Wheatstone bridge (null). *Marks distribution: 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for accuracy distinction.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**What are active and passive transducers? Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Active Transducer:** A transducer that generates its own electrical output (e.g., EMF, charge) in response to the measurand, without requiring any external power source.
  - *Examples (1 mark):* Thermocouple, piezoelectric sensor.

- **Passive Transducer:** A transducer whose output depends on the change of a passive electrical parameter (resistance, capacitance, inductance) and requires an external excitation source.
  - *Examples (1 mark):* RTD, strain gauge, LVDT.

**One concluding sentence for the third mark:** *"The classification is based on whether external excitation power is needed."*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B contains Module Internal Choice questions with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. We provide two alternative question choices — students answer either OR.*

---

### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the generalized measurement system. List the three main functional blocks and describe the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a Wheatstone bridge as a null-type instrument. Derive the balance condition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A generalized measurement system consists of **three primary functional stages**:

**1. Primary Sensing Element (1 mark):**
This is in direct contact with the measurand. It detects the physical quantity and produces a corresponding (often non-electrical) output. *Example:* Bourdon tube senses pressure and produces mechanical displacement.

**2. Transducer / Variable Conversion Element (2 marks):**
Converts the output of the sensing element into a more usable form (typically electrical signal). *Example:* Strain gauge bonded to the Bourdon tube converts displacement to resistance change.

**3. Signal Conditioning & Output Stage (2 marks):**
Amplifies, filters, linearizes, and displays the signal. *Example:* Bridge amplifier driving a digital display.

**Block Diagram Flow (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Measurand} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Transducer} \rightarrow \text{Conditioner} \rightarrow \text{Display}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Wheatstone Bridge Circuit (3 marks):**
A diamond network of four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_x$ with DC source $V_s$ across one diagonal and a galvanometer $G$ across the other diagonal. $R_x$ is the unknown.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
At balance (null), the galvanometer reads zero current, meaning potentials at the two mid-nodes are equal. Adjustable reference resistors are tuned until this condition is met.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1 above, the balance condition is:

$$
\boxed{R_x = R_3 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify measurement systems based on (i) method of comparison, (ii) method of indication, and (iii) type of signal. Give industrial examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the construction and working of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge as a deflection-type instrument. Mention its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**(i) Method of Comparison (2 marks):**
- **Direct:** Steel rule, vernier caliper.
- **Indirect:** Measuring density from mass and volume.

**(ii) Method of Indication (3 marks):**
- **Deflection-type:** Bourdon pressure gauge, moving-coil voltmeter.
- **Null-type:** Wheatstone bridge, dead-weight tester, potentiometer.

**(iii) Type of Signal (2 marks):**
- **Analog:** Mercury thermometer, analog multimeter.
- **Digital:** Digital multimeter (DMM), digital weighing balance.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A Bourdon tube is a curved metallic tube of elliptical cross-section. One end is fixed to the process inlet; the other end is sealed and connected via linkages and gears to a pointer. The tube is surrounded by a calibrated dial.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When pressurized fluid enters, the tube tends to straighten (because internal pressure tries to make the cross-section circular). This motion is amplified by the linkage and gear train, rotating the pointer over the calibrated scale.

**Advantages (1 mark):** Simple, rugged, low cost, direct reading.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Hysteresis due to elastic fatigue, lower accuracy than null-type, requires periodic calibration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "analog instrument" with "deflection-type"** — they are orthogonal classifications. An analog instrument can be deflection-type; a digital instrument with numeric display is also deflection-type in essence.
> 2. **Always write the units** in the balance equation derivation. Marks are deducted for dimensional inconsistency.
> 3. In null-type explanations, **mention the "null-detector" explicitly** — examiners look for this term.
> 4. When classifying transducers, **justify with energy-flow logic**, not just memorization. Students who write *"RTD is passive because it needs a battery"* earn full marks; those who just list names without justification lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Don't write "Thermocouple is a resistance-based sensor"** — it is an EMF-based (Seebeck effect) sensor. Common confusion costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-day KTU preparation):**

- ✅ **Direct vs Indirect Measurement** — Direct needs no computation; Indirect uses a formula like $\rho = m/V$.
- ✅ **Deflection vs Null** — Deflection is fast but less accurate; Null is slow but highly accurate.
- ✅ **Analog vs Digital** — Analog is continuous; Digital is discrete (uses ADC with $2^n$ levels).
- ✅ **Active vs Passive Transducer** — Active = self-generating (Thermocouple, Piezo); Passive = needs excitation (RTD, Strain Gauge, LVDT).
- ✅ **Wheatstone Bridge Balance Equation:** $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_x}$ or equivalently $R_x = R_3 \cdot R_2 / R_1$.
- ✅ **Resolution formula:** $\text{Res} = \text{FS} / (2^n - 1)$.
- ✅ **Indicating / Recording / Integrating / Controlling** — these are the four functional categories of instruments.
- ✅ **Bourdon tube** is the most common deflection-type pressure gauge; **Dead-weight tester** is the standard null-type pressure calibrator.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric sensors** are active and measure dynamic pressure/force, NOT static.
- ✅ **RTD** is more accurate and linear than a thermistor; **Thermocouple** is more rugged and has a wider range.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparisons with Other Temperature Sensors. Thermopiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparisons with Other Temperature Sensors. Thermopiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Comparison of Temperature Sensors — Formal Definition

**Temperature measurement** in industrial instrumentation is achieved through a wide variety of transducers, each exploiting a distinct physical principle. The major categories studied under KTU Module 1 are:

- **Thermocouples (TC)** — based on the **Seebeck Effect** (junction EMF proportional to temperature difference).
- **Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs)** — based on the predictable change of electrical resistance of pure metals (usually platinum) with temperature.
- **Thermistors** — semiconductor devices exhibiting a strong, non-linear, negative (NTC) or positive (PTC) temperature coefficient of resistance.
- **Integrated Circuit (IC) Sensors** — silicon-based solid-state devices producing a linear voltage or current output proportional to temperature.
- **Radiation Pyrometers / Thermopiles** — non-contact devices sensing emitted thermal radiation.
- **Bimetallic Strips** — mechanical deflection due to differential thermal expansion of two bonded metals.
- **Filled-System Thermometers** — fluid/vapor pressure-based sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, students are expected to understand the **operating principle**, **range**, **accuracy**, **response time**, **sensitivity**, and **application suitability** of each sensor. Comparative reasoning questions are frequently asked in Part A (3 marks).

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you are trying to **measure the heat of a cup of tea**:
- A **thermocouple** is like **two different metal wires twisted together** — when one end touches the tea, it generates a tiny voltage. Cheap, fast, but needs reference.
- An **RTD** is like a **metal wire whose electrical resistance changes with heat** — very stable and accurate, but slower and more expensive.
- A **thermistor** is like a **special ceramic pebble** whose resistance drops sharply with heat — extremely sensitive over small ranges.
- An **IC sensor** is like a **miniature electronic chip** that directly outputs a voltage — easy to interface, but limited to modest ranges.
- A **thermopile** is like **stacking many thermocouples together** — instead of one tiny voltage, you get a *much larger* output by adding the effects of dozens of junctions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** No single temperature sensor is "best" for all applications. The selection depends on **range**, **accuracy**, **environment (contact vs. non-contact)**, **response time**, **cost**, and **signal conditioning complexity**.

### 1.3 Thermopiles — Formal Definition

A **Thermopile** is an assembly of multiple **thermocouples connected either in series or in parallel** such that their individual EMFs are summed (series) or averaged (parallel) to produce a measurable output signal. Each thermocouple in the array is called a **thermocouple element** or **thermoelement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Definition:** A thermopile is "a temperature-sensing element consisting of a number of thermocouples connected in series so that the EMFs add up, or in parallel so that the output represents an average temperature."

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for Thermopiles

Think of a **thermopile** as a **chain of tiny thermocouples stacked together like batteries in a flashlight**:
- One thermocouple = one tiny "battery" producing ~microvolts per °C.
- Stacking 50–100 in **series** = a much stronger combined voltage.
- The "hot junctions" sit on the measurement target, the "cold junctions" remain at a known reference.
- This makes the thermopile ideal for detecting **very small temperature differences** (e.g., distant infrared radiation from a human body).

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants Referenced

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ)** = **5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- **Seebeck coefficient** of typical TC = **40 μV/°C to 60 μV/°C**
- **Platinum RTD resistance** at 0 °C (PT100) = **100 Ω**
- **Platinum RTD resistance** at 0 °C (PT1000) = **1000 Ω**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Comparison of Temperature Sensors

#### A. Thermocouples (TC)

- **Principle:** Seebeck Effect — an EMF is generated at the junction of two dissimilar metals when subjected to a temperature gradient.
- **Standard Types (ISA / IEC 60584):**
  * Type K (Chromel–Alumel): range **−200 °C to +1260 °C**, sensitivity ~**41 μV/°C**
  * Type J (Iron–Constantan): range **−40 °C to +760 °C**, sensitivity ~**50 μV/°C**
  * Type T (Copper–Constantan): range **−200 °C to +370 °C**, sensitivity ~**40 μV/°C**
  * Type E (Chromel–Constantan): range **−200 °C to +900 °C**, sensitivity ~**68 μV/°C**
  * Type S (Pt-Pt/Rh 10%): range **0 °C to +1480 °C**, sensitivity ~**10 μV/°C**
- **Advantages:** Wide range, rugged, fast response, inexpensive, self-powered.
- **Disadvantages:** Low sensitivity, requires **cold-junction compensation (CJC)**, non-linear, susceptible to drift.

#### B. Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTD)

- **Principle:** Resistance of pure metals increases linearly with temperature.
- **Standard:** **PT100 (Platinum, 100 Ω @ 0 °C)** is the industrial benchmark.
- **Relation:**
  $$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T_0 \right) \right]$$
  where α for platinum = **3.85 × 10⁻³ °C⁻¹** (European standard, IEC 60751).
- **Range:** **−200 °C to +850 °C** (PT100).
- **Advantages:** High accuracy (±0.1 °C), excellent linearity, excellent stability and repeatability.
- **Disadvantages:** Low sensitivity, requires current excitation (self-heating error), more expensive than TC.

#### C. Thermistors

- **Principle:** Semiconductor (metal-oxide ceramics) whose resistance changes drastically with temperature.
- **Types:** **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient)** — resistance falls as temperature rises; **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient)** — resistance rises.
- **Relation (NTC):**
  $$R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$
  where **β** is the material constant (typically **3000 K to 5000 K**).
- **Range:** **−100 °C to +300 °C** (typical NTC).
- **Advantages:** Very high sensitivity (~**−4 %/°C**), fast response, small size, low cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Highly non-linear, limited range, fragile, requires linearization.

#### D. IC Temperature Sensors

- **Principle:** Bandgap reference circuits in silicon produce a voltage proportional to absolute temperature (PTAT).
- **Output:** Analog (e.g., **LM35: 10 mV/°C**), digital (e.g., DS18B20), or current output.
- **Range:** **−55 °C to +150 °C** (typical).
- **Advantages:** Linear output, built-in signal conditioning, easy microcontroller interface.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited temperature range, requires power supply, self-heating.

#### E. Radiation Pyrometers / Thermopiles (Non-Contact)

- **Principle:** Measures thermal radiation emitted by a hot body — based on **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** and **Planck's Law**.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:**
  $$E_b = \sigma \, T^4$$
- **Advantage:** Measures temperature without physical contact — used for very high temperatures (above 1000 °C) or moving objects.
- **Disadvantage:** Affected by emissivity, requires line-of-sight, expensive.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| Thermocouple EMF (seebeck) | $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ | $S$ in V/°C, $\Delta T$ in K |
| RTD resistance (linear) | $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T_0 \right) \right]$ | $\alpha$ in °C⁻¹ |
| RTD resistance (Callendar-Van Dusen) | $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + AT + BT^2 \right]$ for $T \ge 0$ | Higher order |
| Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | $T$ in Kelvin |
| Stefan-Boltzmann | $E_b = \sigma \, T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |
| Thermopile EMF (series) | $E_{\text{total}} = n \cdot E_{\text{TC}}$ | $n$ = number of junctions |
| Thermopile EMF (parallel/avg) | $E_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i$ | For multi-point averaging |
| Seebeck sensitivity Type K | $S \approx 41 \,\mu\text{V/°C}$ | @ 0–1000 °C range |
| PT100 nominal | $R_0 = 100 \,\Omega$ @ 0 °C | α = 3.85 × 10⁻³ °C⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using the vertical bar `\|` symbol inside table cells. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX expressions to maintain table integrity.**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical plants:** Thermocouples (Type K) for furnace profiling.
- **HVAC / Refrigeration:** NTC thermistors for in-line refrigerant sensing.
- **Pharmaceutical cleanrooms:** PT100 RTDs for calibration-grade accuracy.
- **Pyrometers & thermopiles:** Steel molten bath, glass furnaces, infrared thermometers.
- **IC temperature sensors:** PCB-level monitoring of CPUs, batteries, power electronics.
- **Bimetallic strips:** Thermostat switches, over-temperature cutouts in transformers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermopile Output EMF (Series Configuration)

**Statement:** Prove that the total EMF of a thermopile consisting of *n* identical thermocouples connected in series is *n* times the EMF of a single thermocouple.

**Step 1 — Define the single thermocouple EMF:**

Using the Seebeck equation, a single thermocouple with hot junction at temperature $T_h$ and cold junction at temperature $T_c$ develops an EMF:

$$E_1 = S \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

where $S$ is the Seebeck coefficient (V/°C).

**Step 2 — Arrange n thermocouples in series:**

When *n* identical thermocouples are connected in series, the **hot junctions** of all pairs are exposed to the same hot temperature $T_h$, and the **cold junctions** are all held at the same reference temperature $T_c$. The EMFs add in series (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + \cdots + E_n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the identical EMF expression:**

Since all thermocouples are identical (same materials, same temperatures):

$$E_1 = E_2 = E_3 = \cdots = E_n = S \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

Therefore:

$$E_{\text{total}} = n \cdot S \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = n \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$$

This is the fundamental working equation of a series thermopile. Each additional thermocouple pair adds linearly to the output voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students often forget to specify the assumption of **identical** thermocouples. Marks are awarded only if the derivation is properly bounded by assumptions.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermopile Output EMF (Parallel/Averaging Configuration)

**Statement:** Show that a parallel-connected thermopile of *n* identical thermocouples gives the average temperature of all hot junctions.

**Step 1 — Each thermocouple develops:**

$$E_i = S \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_c), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2 — In parallel connection, the output is the mean EMF:**

$$E_{\text{out}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i = \frac{S}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{h,i} - T_c)$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic simplification:**

$$E_{\text{out}} = \frac{S}{n} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{h,i} - n \cdot T_c \right) = S \cdot (T_{\text{avg}} - T_c)$$

where

$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{h,i}$$

Hence the output EMF corresponds to the **arithmetic mean of all hot-junction temperatures** — useful for surface-temperature averaging probes.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity Boost of a Series Thermopile

For a single Type K thermocouple: $S = 41 \,\mu\text{V/°C}$.

A thermopile of $n = 100$ identical junctions:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 100 \times 41 \,\mu\text{V/°C} = 4100 \,\mu\text{V/°C} = 4.1 \,\text{mV/°C}$$

This is a **100× improvement** in output voltage — sufficient to drive an ADC directly without pre-amplification, and crucial for **low-temperature-difference measurements** such as human body IR detection.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Guide

| Criterion | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | Thermistor | IC Sensor | Thermopile (IR) |
|-----------|:------------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:---------:|:---------------:|
| Typical Range (°C) | −200 to +1800 | −200 to +850 | −100 to +300 | −55 to +150 | −50 to +3000 |
| Accuracy | ±1 to ±2 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C | ±0.1 to ±1 °C | ±0.5 to ±2 °C | ±1 to ±5 °C |
| Sensitivity | 10–80 μV/°C | ~0.385 Ω/°C | −3 to −5 %/°C | 10 mV/°C | n × 10–80 μV/°C |
| Linearity | Poor (polynomial fit) | Excellent | Very poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| Response Time | Fast (ms) | Moderate (1–5 s) | Very fast (ms) | Fast (ms) | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High | Very low | Low | Moderate–High |
| Self-Heating | None | Yes (needs 1 mA) | Yes (very small) | Yes | None (radiative) |
| Contact Type | Contact | Contact | Contact | Contact | **Non-contact** |
| Power Required | No | Yes (excitation) | Yes (excitation) | Yes | Sometimes (amplifier) |
| Stability/Drift | Moderate drift | Excellent | Moderate | Good | Good |
| Best Suited For | Furnaces, engines | Lab, calibration | Medical, HVAC | Electronics | Pyrometers, IR cameras |

### 3.5 Numerical Example — RTD vs. Thermistor Sensitivity

**Given:** A PT100 RTD and a 10 kΩ NTC thermistor (β = 3950 K) at $T_0 = 298$ K.

**RTD sensitivity:**

$$\frac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \cdot \alpha = 100 \times 3.85 \times 10^{-3} = 0.385 \,\Omega/°C$$

Fractional change per °C = 0.385 / 100 = **0.385 %/°C**

**NTC thermistor sensitivity (using NTC fractional slope formula):**

$$\frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

At $T = 298$ K:

$$\frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{3950}{298^2} = -\frac{3950}{88804} \approx -0.0445 \,\text{per K} = -4.45 \,\%/°C$$

**Comparison:** The NTC thermistor is **~11.5× more sensitive** than the PT100 — but its range is far smaller and its non-linearity is much worse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Calculation Trap:** In derivations, always state the **assumption of identical junctions** and **isothermal cold junctions** for thermopile equations. Marks are lost otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare the features of a Thermocouple, RTD, and Thermistor with respect to: (a) principle of operation, (b) typical range, and (c) sensitivity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | NTC Thermistor |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| Principle | Seebeck Effect (EMF) | Resistance change of metal | Resistance change of semiconductor |
| Range (°C) | −200 to +1800 | −200 to +850 | −100 to +300 |
| Sensitivity | 10–80 μV/°C | ~0.385 Ω/°C | −3 to −5 %/°C |
| Linearity | Poor | Excellent | Very poor |
| Cost | Low | High | Very low |

*(3 marks awarded for clear table + concluding statement on application fit.)*

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Thermopile. Why is a series thermopile preferred for non-contact temperature measurement over a single thermocouple?**

**Model Answer:**

A **thermopile** is a series combination of *n* identical thermocouples whose individual EMFs add to give a total EMF $E_{\text{total}} = n \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$.

A single thermocouple produces a very low EMF (microvolts) for small temperature differences. In non-contact IR applications (e.g., human body temperature ≈ 0.1 °C above ambient), this microvolt-level signal is buried in noise. A thermopile of 50–100 junctions boosts the signal by 50–100×, making it directly measurable after a single low-noise amplifier stage. Hence series thermopiles are preferred in **IR pyrometers, PIR motion sensors, and laser power meters**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **radiation thermopile** used in an infrared pyrometer. Mention the role of cold-junction compensation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermopile consists of 80 series-connected Type K thermocouples. If each develops an EMF of 41 μV/°C, find:
- (i) Total EMF for a hot-junction temperature of 250 °C and cold-junction at 25 °C.
- (ii) Sensitivity in μV/°C.
- (iii) Output voltage if a differential amplifier with gain 50 is used. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

A **radiation thermopile** consists of:
- An **optical window/lens** that focuses incoming IR radiation onto a small **blackened hot-junction pad**.
- Multiple **thermocouple junctions** in series — the hot junctions on the absorbing pad, cold junctions on a **heatsink** maintained at ambient.
- A **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** circuit (often an IC sensor like LM35) that measures the cold-junction temperature and electronically adds the corresponding Seebeck EMF to obtain absolute temperature.
- A **low-noise instrumentation amplifier** (gain typically 100–1000) and an **ADC** for digital display.

**Working:** IR radiation from the target follows the Stefan-Boltzmann relation. The absorbed IR heats the hot junctions above the reference cold-junction temperature, generating a net EMF proportional to absorbed power. The thermopile output is thus a measure of incident radiative flux, which is converted to temperature using Stefan-Boltzmann calibration.

*(Award 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for CJC, 1 mark for signal chain.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 80$, $S = 41 \,\mu\text{V/°C}$, $T_h = 250$ °C, $T_c = 25$ °C, Amplifier gain $G = 50$.

**(i) Total EMF:**

$$\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 250 - 25 = 225 \,°C$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = n \cdot S \cdot \Delta T = 80 \times 41 \,\mu\text{V/°C} \times 225 \,°C$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 738{,}000 \,\mu\text{V} = 0.738 \,\text{V}$$

**(ii) Sensitivity:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = n \cdot S = 80 \times 41 = 3280 \,\mu\text{V/°C} = 3.28 \,\text{mV/°C}$$

**(iii) Amplified output:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = G \cdot E_{\text{total}} = 50 \times 0.738 \,\text{V} = 36.9 \,\text{V}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **contact-type** temperature sensors (RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor) with **non-contact-type** sensors (Pyrometer, Thermopile) using a tabulation covering range, accuracy, response time, and principle. Mention two industrial applications of each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why NTC thermistors are not used in industrial furnace applications. A PT100 RTD has $R_0 = 100$ Ω at 0 °C and α = 3.85 × 10⁻³ °C⁻¹. Calculate its resistance at 250 °C and the corresponding voltage drop when excited by a 5 mA constant current. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Contact vs Non-Contact Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | RTD | Thermocouple | Thermistor | Pyrometer | Thermopile |
|-----------|-----|--------------|------------|-----------|------------|
| Principle | Resistance change (metal) | Seebeck EMF | Semiconductor resistance | Stefan-Boltzmann radiation | Series TC array sensing IR |
| Range (°C) | −200 to +850 | −200 to +1800 | −100 to +300 | 600 to 3000+ | −50 to +2000 |
| Accuracy | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C | ±1 to ±2 °C | ±0.1 to ±1 °C | ±1 to ±10 °C | ±1 to ±5 °C |
| Response | Moderate (1–5 s) | Fast (ms) | Very fast (ms) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Contact | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No |

**Applications of Contact Sensors:** HVAC duct sensing, lab thermometry, food processing.

**Applications of Non-Contact Sensors:** Steel molten bath, glass furnaces, semiconductor wafer processing, human IR thermometers.

*(2 marks for tabulation, 2 marks for applications, 2 marks for reasoning, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

**(b) NTC Limitations & PT100 Calculation (7 marks):**

**Why NTC is unsuitable for furnaces:**
- Limited upper range (~300 °C) — far below furnace temperatures (1000 °C+).
- Highly non-linear — needs complex linearization.
- Drifts at high temperatures.
- Physically fragile at thermal shock.

**PT100 Resistance at 250 °C:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T_0 \right) \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times (250 - 0) \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.9625 \right] = 100 \times 1.9625 = 196.25 \,\Omega$$

**Voltage drop with 5 mA excitation:**

$$V = I \cdot R = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 196.25 = 0.98125 \,\text{V} \approx 0.98 \,\text{V}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Always express sensitivity in **μV/°C or mV/°C**, never bare volts. Marks are deducted for missing units.
> 2. **Cold-junction confusion:** Students frequently write "thermopile measures absolute temperature." It actually measures **ΔT**; the cold-junction temperature must be added separately via CJC.
> 3. **Sign errors:** EMF is positive only when $T_h > T_c$. Students often forget the direction convention in Seebeck calculations.
> 4. **Non-linearity oversight:** NTC thermistors do **not** follow a linear model; the exponential relation is mandatory in derivations.
> 5. **Self-heating in RTDs:** Always check that excitation current is small enough to avoid self-heating error (typical limit: 1 mA for PT100 in still air).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Thermopile = multiple thermocouples in series (or parallel)** — total EMF in series is **n × E_single**.
- **Series thermopile** gives an *n*-fold boost in sensitivity, ideal for **low ΔT** and **non-contact IR** applications.
- **Parallel thermopile** gives the **average** of multiple hot-junction temperatures — used in surface probes.
- **Seebeck coefficient S** for common TCs lies in the range **10–80 μV/°C**.
- **Type K** TC: S ≈ **41 μV/°C**, range **−200 °C to +1260 °C** — most widely used.
- **PT100 RTD** sensitivity: **0.385 Ω/°C**; linear, accurate, but needs excitation.
- **NTC thermistor** sensitivity: **−3 to −5 %/°C**; non-linear, small range, very high sensitivity.
- **IC temperature sensors** like **LM35** output **10 mV/°C** linearly.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann law** ($E_b = \sigma T^4$) governs all radiation pyrometers and IR thermopiles.
- **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** is mandatory for any TC-based system measuring absolute temperature.
- **Self-heating error** affects RTDs and thermistors — keep excitation current low.
- **Emissivity (ε)** correction is essential for accurate pyrometer readings.
- **Response time** is fastest for TCs and thermistors, slowest for RTDs.
- **Bimetallic strips** are mechanical, not electrical — used in thermostats and over-temperature cutouts.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Wide range + low cost → TC; High accuracy → RTD; High sensitivity, narrow range → Thermistor; Electronics monitoring → IC sensor; Non-contact / moving / very hot → Pyrometer or Thermopile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In any comparison or derivation question, **always tabulate** rather than write a long paragraph — examiners reward structured answers. Quote the **standard ranges** and **sensitivities** verbatim from the syllabus tables to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Pyrometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Pyrometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pyrometer?

A **Pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument used to determine the temperature of a body by measuring the thermal radiation emitted by it, without making physical contact with the hot body. The word "Pyrometer" is derived from the Greek words *pyr* (fire) and *metron* (measure), meaning "measuring fire/heat."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAET602 - Module 1):**
> A pyrometer is a radiation thermometer designed to measure temperatures above the range of liquid-in-glass thermometers, typically used for temperatures above **600 °C (1112 °F)** where contact sensors would fail due to physical degradation, melting, or contamination.

### Classification Basis
Pyrometers are broadly classified based on the **portion of the electromagnetic spectrum** they use and the **physical principle** of detection:

1. **Optical Pyrometers** – Compare brightness with an internal reference filament.
2. **Total Radiation Pyrometers** – Capture all wavelengths radiated by the body.
3. **Photoelectric Pyrometers** – Use photodetectors (Si, PbS, InGaAs) to convert radiation into electrical signals.
4. **Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometers** – Measure the ratio of intensities at two different wavelengths.
5. **Infrared Pyrometers** – Use narrow band-pass filters to detect specific IR wavelengths.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sear your hand" Analogy:**
> When you hold your hand near a hot tava (griddle) without touching it, you *feel* the heat. Your skin is essentially a crude pyrometer — it senses infrared radiation. A pyrometer is the **engineered, calibrated, quantitative version** of this sensation. Instead of a nervous impulse, it uses optics (lenses/mirrors), filters, and photodetectors to convert invisible heat into a precise temperature reading on a digital display.

Imagine two pieces of iron on a forge:
- One glows **dull red** → cooler (~600 °C)
- One glows **bright yellow-white** → very hot (~1200 °C)

The color and intensity of the glow directly tell us the temperature. Pyrometers exploit this physical reality using the laws of blackbody radiation.

## 1.3 Why Pyrometers? — Engineering Motivation

| Scenario | Why Contact Sensors Fail | Pyrometer Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Molten steel (1600 °C) | Thermocouples melt/drift | Non-contact, measures emitted light |
| Moving billets in a rolling mill | Physical contact impossible | Optical sighting measures at distance |
| Semiconductor wafer (rapid thermal processing) | Contamination from probe | No contact → no contamination |
| Inflammable/explosive gas streams | Spark hazard from heated wires | Cold instrument body, optical path only |
| Very small targets (micro-electronics) | Sensor too large | Focused optical beam |

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **emissivity (ε)** of a body is the ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a **blackbody** at the same temperature. ε ∈ [0, 1]. A blackbody has ε = **1** by definition.

- **Total emissive power** of a blackbody (Stefan–Boltzmann): $E_b = \sigma T^4$
- **Spectral emissive power** (Planck's distribution): $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1\right)}$

Where:
- $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ (Stefan–Boltzmann constant)
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$ (Planck's constant)
- $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (speed of light)
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Blackbody Radiation (Foundation of All Pyrometers)

Every pyrometer type is a practical embodiment of these five laws:

### Pillar 1 — Planck's Law (Spectral Distribution)
For a blackbody at temperature $T$, the spectral emissive power per unit wavelength is:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1\right)} \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}\right]$$

- $E_{b\lambda}$ peaks at a specific $\lambda_{\max}$ for a given $T$.

### Pillar 2 — Wien's Displacement Law
The wavelength at which the spectral emissive power is maximum:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

- As $T$ increases, $\lambda_{\max}$ shifts to **shorter wavelengths** (from IR → red → white). This is the physical reason hot objects change color.

### Pillar 3 — Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Total Radiation)
Integrating Planck's law over all wavelengths:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \cdot T^4 \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$$

- This is the foundation of **Total Radiation Pyrometers**.

### Pillar 4 — Kirchhoff's Law
For a body in thermal equilibrium, the emissivity equals its absorptivity:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

- If a body emits strongly at some wavelength, it also absorbs strongly at that wavelength.

### Pillar 5 — Rayleigh–Jeans / Wien Approximations
For $hc \gg \lambda kT$ (short wavelength OR low T): Wien's approximation

$$E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} e^{-hc/\lambda kT}$$

- This exponential form is the basis of **optical (brightness) pyrometers**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Law / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Used in Pyrometer Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stefan–Boltzmann (Total) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $T$ in K | Total Radiation Pyrometer |
| 2 | Planck's Distribution | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1\right)}$ | $\lambda$ in m | All spectral types |
| 3 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | – | Color-temperature relation |
| 4 | Brightness (Wien) | $L_\lambda = \dfrac{C_1}{\pi \lambda^5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$ | $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$, $C_2 = hc/k$ | Optical Pyrometer |
| 5 | Brightness temperature | $\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}{C_2}$ | $T_b$ brightness, $T$ true | Optical Pyrometer |
| 6 | Two-Color ratio | $R = \dfrac{L_{\lambda_1}}{L_{\lambda_2}} = \left(\dfrac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 e^{\tfrac{C_2}{T}\left(\tfrac{1}{\lambda_2} - \tfrac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)}$ | – | Ratio Pyrometer |
| 7 | Emissivity correction | $T = \left(\dfrac{E}{\varepsilon \sigma}\right)^{1/4}$ | – | Radiation Pyrometer |
| 8 | Effective wavelength | $\lambda_{eff} \approx 0.65 \mu$m (red) | – | Optical (monochromatic) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where C₂ is used:** $C_2 = \dfrac{hc}{k} = 0.014388 \text{ m·K} = 14388 \text{ μm·K}$. This constant appears in nearly all KTU optical pyrometer problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel industry**: Continuous casting, blast furnace tuyeres, ladle metallurgy — all use two-color or fiber-optic pyrometers.
- **Semiconductor**: Rapid Thermal Processing (RTP) uses narrow-band IR pyrometers (λ ≈ 0.9 μm) for wafer temperature control.
- **Power plants**: Boiler tube temperature monitoring with **ratio pyrometers** (immune to dust/soot).
- **Aerospace**: Turbine blade temperature in jet engines (above 1500 °C — no contact sensor survives).
- **Glass & Cement kilns**: Optical pyrometers observe through viewports.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Generic Pyrometer)

1. **Optical collection** — Lens or mirror focuses emitted radiation onto detector.
2. **Wavelength selection** — Filter passes only the relevant band (e.g., 0.65 μm for optical).
3. **Detection** — Human eye (optical), thermopile (radiation), photodiode (photoelectric).
4. **Signal conditioning** — Amplification, linearization against $T^4$ or $T$.
5. **Calibration** — Reference to a blackbody at known temperature.
6. **Display** — Analog dial or digital readout in °C/°F/K.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Brightness Temperature Relation (Optical Pyrometer)

> **Goal:** Derive the relationship between the *true* temperature $T$ of a real body and its *brightness* temperature $T_b$ (as seen by an optical pyrometer).

### Setup
- A real body at temperature $T$ with spectral emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ emits radiance $L_\lambda$ at wavelength $\lambda$.
- An optical pyrometer compares this with a blackbody filament calibrated at brightness temperature $T_b$.

### Step 1: Radiance of a real body (using Wien's approximation)
For the narrow visible band where optical pyrometers operate, $hc/\lambda kT \gg 1$, so Planck's law simplifies to Wien's form:

$$L_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\pi \lambda^5} \cdot e^{-C_2/(\lambda T)}$$

### Step 2: Apply emissivity
A real (non-black) body emits $\varepsilon_\lambda$ times the blackbody radiance at the same $T$:

$$L_\lambda^{real}(\lambda, T) = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot \frac{C_1}{\pi \lambda^5} \cdot e^{-C_2/(\lambda T)}$$

### Step 3: Define brightness temperature
Brightness temperature $T_b$ is defined as the temperature of a *blackbody* ($\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$) that emits the **same radiance** at the same wavelength as the real body:

$$\frac{C_1}{\pi \lambda^5} \cdot e^{-C_2/(\lambda T_b)} = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot \frac{C_1}{\pi \lambda^5} \cdot e^{-C_2/(\lambda T)}$$

### Step 4: Cancel common terms
The factors $C_1/(\pi \lambda^5)$ cancel from both sides:

$$e^{-C_2/(\lambda T_b)} = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot e^{-C_2/(\lambda T)}$$

### Step 5: Take natural logarithm of both sides
$$-\frac{C_2}{\lambda T_b} = \ln \varepsilon_\lambda - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$$

### Step 6: Rearrange to isolate $1/T_b$
$$\frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{T} - \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}{C_2}$$

### ✅ Final Result (boxed form)

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}{C_2}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Since $\varepsilon_\lambda \le 1 \Rightarrow \ln \varepsilon_\lambda \le 0$, therefore $T_b \le T$ (always). Brightness temperature is always *less than* the true temperature for a real body.

### Numerical Example
A furnace has a brightness temperature of 1500 K as seen by an optical pyrometer with effective wavelength 0.65 μm. If the surface emissivity is 0.7, find the true temperature.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}{C_2}$$

$$\frac{1}{1500} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{(0.65 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \ln(0.7)}{0.014388}$$

$$\frac{1}{1500} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{(6.5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (-0.3567)}{0.014388}$$

$$\frac{1}{1500} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{-2.319 \times 10^{-7}}{0.014388} = -1.611 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1500} + 1.611 \times 10^{-5} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} + 1.611 \times 10^{-5} = 6.828 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{6.828 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1464.6 \text{ K}$$

**True Temperature T ≈ 1464.6 K** (about 35.4 K higher than the brightness temperature — physically consistent because a real body looks dimmer than a blackbody at the same T).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Foundation of Total Radiation Pyrometer)

### Starting Point: Planck's Law

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1\right)}$$

### Step 1: Integrate over all wavelengths
Total emissive power = integral of spectral emissive power over $\lambda = 0$ to $\infty$:

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1\right)} d\lambda$$

### Step 2: Substitute $u = hc/(\lambda kT) \Rightarrow \lambda = hc/(ukT)$, so $d\lambda = -hc/(kT u^2) du$
When $\lambda \to 0$, $u \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $u \to 0$. Flipping the sign:

$$E_b = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{(hc/(ukT))^5 (e^u - 1)} \cdot \left(-\frac{hc}{kT u^2}\right) du$$

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2 \cdot (ukT)^5}{(hc)^5 (e^u - 1)} \cdot \frac{hc}{kT u^2} du$$

### Step 3: Simplify

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2 \cdot u^5 k^5 T^5}{h^5 c^5 (e^u - 1)} \cdot \frac{hc}{kT u^2} du$$

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} du$$

### Step 4: The integral evaluates to a known constant
$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} du = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

### Step 5: Substitute back

$$E_b = \frac{2\pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} T^4$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$\boxed{\;E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The $T^4$ dependence is the mathematical reason total radiation pyrometers need *highly nonlinear* signal conditioning — a 1% change in $T$ produces a 4% change in $E_b$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer Equation

### Concept
Take the ratio of spectral radiances at two wavelengths $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$:

$$R = \frac{L_{\lambda_1}}{L_{\lambda_2}}$$

### Step 1: Wien's approximation at each wavelength

$$L_{\lambda_1} = \varepsilon_{\lambda_1} \cdot \frac{C_1}{\pi \lambda_1^5} e^{-C_2/(\lambda_1 T)}, \quad L_{\lambda_2} = \varepsilon_{\lambda_2} \cdot \frac{C_1}{\pi \lambda_2^5} e^{-C_2/(\lambda_2 T)}$$

### Step 2: Form the ratio

$$R = \frac{\varepsilon_{\lambda_1}}{\varepsilon_{\lambda_2}} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 \cdot e^{\frac{C_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)}$$

### Step 3: If we assume a *graybody* ($\varepsilon_{\lambda_1} = \varepsilon_{\lambda_2}$)
The emissivity terms cancel completely — this is the key advantage of ratio pyrometers:

$$R = \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 \cdot e^{\frac{C_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $T$

$$\ln R = 5 \ln\left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right) + \frac{C_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)$$

$$\frac{C_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right) = \ln R - 5 \ln\left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$\boxed{\;T = \frac{C_2 \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)}{\ln R - 5 \ln\left(\dfrac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For a *graybody*, the ratio depends **only on temperature**, not on emissivity. This is why ratio pyrometers are used in dirty/smoky environments (steel furnaces, cement kilns) where smoke partially obscures the target.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Optical (Brightness) | Total Radiation | Photoelectric | Two-Color (Ratio) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Measured Quantity** | Monochromatic brightness | Total radiant flux | Photocurrent at one λ | Ratio at two λ |
| **Detector** | Human eye / photodiode | Thermopile (multi-junction) | Si, PbS, InGaAs | Two matched detectors |
| **Emissivity Error** | Significant (need ε correction) | Very significant | Significant | **Negligible for graybody** |
| **Range** | 700 °C – 6000 °C | 100 °C – 3500 °C | 200 °C – 3500 °C | 600 °C – 3500 °C |
| **Dust/Smoke Effect** | High | High | Medium | **Low (self-correcting)** |
| **Speed of Response** | Slow (manual) | 0.5–5 s | Microseconds | 10–100 ms |
| **Calibration** | Tungsten filament | Blackbody furnace | Blackbody furnace | Internal dual-detector |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Pyrometer Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Target Body] -->|Thermal Radiation| B[Optical Lens / Mirror System]
    B --> C[Wavelength Selection Filter]
    C --> D[Radiation Detector]
    D --> E[Signal Amplifier]
    E --> F[Linearization Circuit]
    F --> G[Temperature Display Unit]
    H[Reference Blackbody Source] --> D
    D --> I[Comparator / Calibration Loop]
    I --> F
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- A: Hot Target Body (the industrial process — e.g., molten metal).
- B: Optical Lens / Mirror System — focuses the emitted radiation onto the detector.
- C: Wavelength Selection Filter — passes a specific band (e.g., 0.65 μm for optical, 0.9 μm for Si, 8–14 μm for thermal IR).
- D: Radiation Detector — converts optical energy into an electrical signal.
- E: Signal Amplifier — boosts the often-weak detector output.
- F: Linearization Circuit — corrects the nonlinear T^4 (or T exponential) response.
- G: Temperature Display Unit — digital/analog readout in °C/K.
- H: Reference Blackbody Source — used for periodic calibration.
- I: Comparator / Calibration Loop — keeps the reading accurate.

---

## 4.2 Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer (Detailed Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OpticalPyrometer [DISAPPEARING FILAMENT OPTICAL PYROMETER]
        T1[Target Object at True Temp T] -->|Radiation| Obj[Objective Lens]
        Obj --> F1[Red Filter lambda approx 0.65 micrometer]
        F1 --> Bulb[Tungsten Filament Bulb in Eyepiece]
        PS[Adjustable DC Current Source] --> Bulb
        Bulb -->|Image to Eye| Eye[Observer Eye]
        Eye --> EyePiece[Eyepiece]
        Bulb --> Meter[Ammeter Calibrated in Temperature]
    end
```

**Operating Logic:**
- The observer sees the target image **superimposed on** the filament image.
- Current is increased → filament glows brighter; decreased → dimmer.
- When the filament **just disappears** into the background of the target, the filament brightness = target brightness at the chosen λ.
- The current at this null point is read off an ammeter calibrated in °C.

---

## 4.3 Total Radiation Pyrometer (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TargetA[Hot Furnace Target] -->|All Wavelengths| MirrorA[Concave Mirror with Pyrex Window]
    MirrorA --> DiaphragmA[Adjustable Diaphragm Aperture]
    DiaphragmA --> Blackbody[Reference Blackbody Cavity at Fixed Temp T0]
    Blackbody --> ThermoPile[Multiple Junction Thermopile]
    ThermoPile --> AmplifierA[DC Microvolt Amplifier]
    AmplifierA --> IndicatorA[Calibrated Indicator in degree C]
    MirrorA -.->|Compensation coil| Compensation[Ambient Temperature Compensation]
    Compensation --> AmplifierA
```

**Note:** The thermopile consists of multiple (typically 8–16) thermocouple junctions in series, producing an EMF proportional to the temperature difference $(T^4 - T_0^4)$.

---

## 4.4 Photoelectric Pyrometer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Process Target] -->|Emitted Light| LensP1[Objective Lens]
    LensP1 --> Chopper[Rotating Chopper Disc]
    Chopper -->|Modulated Beam| FilterP[Narrow Bandpass Filter lambda approx 0.9 micrometer]
    FilterP --> DetectorP[Silicon Photodiode]
    DetectorP --> ACAmplifier[AC Tuned Amplifier]
    ACAmplifier --> PhaseDetect[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    PhaseDetect --> DisplayP[Digital Temperature Display]
    RefLED[Internal Reference LED] --> DetectorP
```

**Why modulation?** By chopping the beam (e.g., 400 Hz), ambient DC offsets and thermal drifts in the photodiode/amplifier are eliminated — the AC amplifier responds only to the modulated signal.

---

## 4.5 Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RC[Hot Target in Smoke] -->|Common Optical Path| BeamSplitter[Dichroic Beam Splitter]
    BeamSplitter -->|Reflects lambda 1| DetA[Detector at lambda 1]
    BeamSplitter -->|Transmits lambda 2| DetB[Detector at lambda 2]
    DetA --> AmpA[Logarithmic Amplifier]
    DetB --> AmpB[Logarithmic Amplifier]
    AmpA --> Subtractor[Analog Subtractor]
    AmpB --> Subtractor
    Subtractor -->|ln R| Calculator[Microprocessor Ratio to T]
    Calculator --> DisplayR[Digital Display True Temperature]
```

**The genius of this design:** Smoke absorbs equally at both wavelengths, so the ratio R is unchanged — temperature reading remains accurate even when smoke obscures the target.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define a pyrometer. Why is it preferred over a thermocouple for temperatures above 1500 °C?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pyrometer is a non-contact temperature measuring device that determines the temperature of a hot body by measuring the thermal radiation emitted by it, using principles of blackbody radiation (Planck's law, Stefan–Boltzmann law, Wien's displacement law).

It is preferred over a thermocouple above 1500 °C because:
1. **No physical contact** → no sensor degradation, drift, or melting at extreme temperatures. (1 mark)
2. **Fast response** → suitable for moving or rapidly changing targets. (1 mark)
3. **No contamination** of the measured surface (critical in semiconductor/molten metal industries). (1 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is meant by "brightness temperature" of a body? How does it differ from the true temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Brightness temperature (T_b):** It is defined as the temperature of a blackbody (ε = 1) that emits the same spectral radiance at a given wavelength as that emitted by the real body. (1.5 marks)

**Difference from true temperature (T):**
- Brightness temperature is the *apparent* temperature as perceived by a pyrometer at a specific wavelength. (1 mark)
- For a real (non-black) body, $T_b < T$ because $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$. (0.5 marks)

The relationship is given by: $\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}{C_2}$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model] (14 Marks)**
**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a total radiation pyrometer. Derive the relationship between the EMF generated and the temperature of the target. (7 marks)**

**(b) A total radiation pyrometer is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The thermopile output EMF is 8.4 mV. With the same instrument, a blackbody at 1400 K gives an EMF of 6.0 mV. Find the true temperature of the furnace, assuming the furnace surface behaves as a graybody with emissivity 0.8. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Working of Total Radiation Pyrometer:**

The total radiation pyrometer consists of:
1. **Concave mirror** — collects all thermal radiation from the target.
2. **Pyrex window** — allows IR + visible radiation to pass; protects the mirror.
3. **Diaphragm** — limits the radiation beam.
4. **Reference blackbody** — a hollow cone maintained at a reference temperature $T_0$ (often ambient or controlled).
5. **Thermopile** — multiple (8–16) series-connected thermocouples; one set of junctions exposed to incoming radiation, other set shielded at $T_0$.

**Working:** Radiation from the target is focused onto the "hot" junctions of the thermopile. The reference junctions are held at a constant temperature $T_0$. The temperature difference between the junctions produces an EMF proportional to the **fourth power** of the target temperature (minus $T_0^4$).

**Derivation:**

From Stefan–Boltzmann: Net heat input to thermopile per unit area is:

$$q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 - \sigma T_0^4$$

The thermopile output EMF is proportional to this temperature difference. Let the proportionality constant be $K$ (V/K thermal sensitivity, including lens geometry and detector area):

$$E = K \cdot \sigma \left(\varepsilon T^4 - T_0^4\right)$$

When $T \gg T_0$ (typical furnace case), $T_0^4$ is negligible:

$$\boxed{\;E \approx K \sigma \varepsilon T^4 = K_a \varepsilon T^4\;}$$

where $K_a = K \sigma$ is the **instrument constant** in V/K⁴.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Furnace EMF: $E_f = 8.4$ mV
- Blackbody EMF at 1400 K: $E_b = 6.0$ mV
- Furnace emissivity: $\varepsilon = 0.8$

**Step 1:** For the blackbody calibration (ε = 1):
$$E_b = K_a \cdot (1) \cdot (1400)^4 = 6.0 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2:** For the graybody furnace:
$$E_f = K_a \cdot \varepsilon \cdot T^4 = K_a \cdot 0.8 \cdot T^4 = 8.4 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio to eliminate $K_a$:

$$\frac{E_f}{E_b} = \frac{0.8 \cdot T^4}{(1400)^4}$$

$$T^4 = \frac{E_f}{E_b} \cdot \frac{(1400)^4}{0.8} = \frac{8.4}{6.0} \cdot \frac{(1400)^4}{0.8}$$

$$T^4 = 1.4 \cdot 1.25 \cdot (1400)^4 = 1.75 \cdot (1400)^4$$

**Step 4:** Take the fourth root:

$$T = 1400 \cdot (1.75)^{1/4} = 1400 \cdot 1.1502$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1610.3 \text{ K} \approx 1337 \text{ °C}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often forget to include emissivity in the furnace equation → leads to $T = 1742$ K, which is **wrong** (overestimates temperature).
> - Forgetting to convert K → °C: answer should be in the units **asked** in the question. If asked in K, give K; if asked in °C, subtract 273.
> - The constant $K_a$ MUST be eliminated via the ratio method — never try to compute it from instrument data.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model] (14 Marks)**
**(a) Explain the principle and working of a disappearing filament optical pyrometer. Discuss the sources of error in this instrument. (7 marks)**

**(b) An optical pyrometer with effective wavelength 0.65 μm is sighted on a hot metal surface. The brightness temperature observed is 1450 K. If the emissivity of the surface at 0.65 μm is 0.6, calculate: (i) the true temperature of the surface, and (ii) the total emissive power per unit area of the surface. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Optical Pyrometer:**

**Principle:** Comparison of brightness (spectral radiance) of the target with that of a calibrated tungsten filament inside the instrument, at a fixed red wavelength (~0.65 μm).

**Construction:**
- A telescope with an objective lens and an eyepiece.
- A tungsten filament bulb placed at the focal plane of the eyepiece.
- A red filter (passes only 0.65 μm) in the optical path.
- An adjustable DC current source to heat the filament.
- An ammeter in the filament circuit, calibrated directly in temperature.

**Working:**
1. The image of the target is focused in the plane of the filament.
2. Current through the filament is varied by a rheostat.
3. The observer looks through the eyepiece and adjusts the current until the **filament image disappears** into the background of the target image (i.e., filament brightness = target monochromatic brightness at 0.65 μm).
4. The ammeter reading at this null condition gives the brightness temperature.

**Sources of Error:**
1. **Subjective error** — null judgment depends on the observer's eye; different observers may get different readings.
2. **Emissivity error** — instrument reads brightness, not true temperature; requires correction if ε ≠ 1.
3. **Wavelength assumption** — assumes a single effective wavelength; in reality, the filter passes a narrow band.
4. **Absorption by intervening medium** — smoke, dust, CO₂/H₂O vapor absorbs radiation along the path.
5. **Reflected ambient light** — can cause overestimation in non-ideal viewing conditions.
6. **Tungsten filament aging** — calibration drift with use.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}$ m; $T_b = 1450$ K; $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.6$; $C_2 = 0.014388$ m·K.

**(i) True temperature:**

Using the brightness temperature relation:

$$\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}{C_2}$$

Substitute values:

$$\frac{1}{1450} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{(0.65 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \ln(0.6)}{0.014388}$$

Compute RHS:
$$\ln(0.6) = -0.5108$$
$$RHS = \frac{(0.65 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (-0.5108)}{0.014388} = \frac{-3.32 \times 10^{-7}}{0.014388} = -2.308 \times 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1450} + 2.308 \times 10^{-5} = 6.897 \times 10^{-4} + 2.308 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\frac{1}{T} = 7.128 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$T = \frac{1}{7.128 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1403 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1403 \text{ K} \approx 1130 \text{ °C}}$$

**(ii) Total emissive power per unit area of the surface:**

The total emissive power of a *real* body (graybody approximation) is:

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4$$

Using $\varepsilon = 0.6$, $T = 1403$ K, $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$:

$$E = 0.6 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times (1403)^4$$

Compute $(1403)^4$:
$$1403^2 = 1,968,409$$
$$1403^4 = (1,968,409)^2 \approx 3.874 \times 10^{12}$$

$$E = 0.6 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.874 \times 10^{12}$$

$$E = 0.6 \times 219,679.6 \approx 131,808 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.318 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 131.8 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT use $T_b$ (1450 K) to compute total emissive power** — that's the most common mistake. Total radiation must use the **true** temperature.
> - Sign error in $\ln \varepsilon_\lambda$: since $\varepsilon < 1$, ln is negative; RHS becomes negative, then **subtracting** a negative is **adding** to $1/T_b$, so $T < T_b$. This is physically correct.
> - Unit conversion of $\lambda$ from μm to m: $1 \, \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}$ m. Forgetting this introduces a 10⁶ error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Different Types of Pyrometers**

- [ ] **Pyrometer** = non-contact temperature measurement via thermal radiation.
- [ ] **Optical Pyrometer** uses the *brightness* of a single wavelength (~0.65 μm) compared to a tungsten filament. Formula: $\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}{C_2}$
- [ ] **Total Radiation Pyrometer** uses *all* wavelengths; output ∝ $\varepsilon T^4$ (Stefan–Boltzmann). Uses a thermopile detector.
- [ ] **Photoelectric Pyrometer** uses a photodiode (Si, PbS, InGaAs) with chopped optical signal → fast, objective (no human eye needed).
- [ ] **Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer** measures the ratio of radiances at $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ → **emissivity-independent for graybodies** → ideal for smoky environments.
- [ ] **C₂ constant** = 0.014388 m·K; **σ** = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴.
- [ ] **Wien's displacement law** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K — explains color change with temperature.
- [ ] **Brightness temperature** is *always* ≤ true temperature for a real body.
- [ ] **Emissivity correction** is the dominant source of error in single-color pyrometers.
- [ ] **Ratio pyrometers** use the principle: $\ln R = 5 \ln(\lambda_2/\lambda_1) + (C_2/T)(1/\lambda_2 - 1/\lambda_1)$.
- [ ] **Industrial choices**: Optical = manual, lab/foundry; Total Radiation = continuous process; Photoelectric = fast response; Two-Color = dirty/smoky furnaces.
- [ ] **Always** convert $T$ to Kelvin when applying Stefan–Boltzmann, Planck, or Wien's equations.
- [ ] **Total Radiation Pyrometer EMF**: $E = K_a \varepsilon T^4$. Use *ratio* with blackbody calibration to eliminate $K_a$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrared Guns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Infrared Guns # Infrared (IR) Guns — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) An **Infrared Gun** (also called an **Infrared Thermometer**, **IR Pyrometer**, or **Non-Contact Thermometer**) is a temperature-sensing instrument that measures the surface temperature of an object from a distance by detecting the **intensity of infrared (thermal) radiation** emitted by that object. It operates on the principle of **blackbody radiation** and converts the detected IR energy into an electrical signal that is proportional to the surface temperature. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition:** *An infrared gun is a non-contact type radiation pyrometer that uses a lens to focus infrared thermal radiation emitted from a target object onto a detector (typically a thermopile), the output of which is processed to display the equivalent blackbody temperature.* **Standards/Constants Used:** - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** - **Wien's Displacement Constant (b) = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K** - **Speed of Light (c) = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** - **Planck's Constant (h) = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English View) Imagine you walk past a hot tawa (griddle) in a kitchen — you don't touch it, but you can *feel* the heat on your face. Your skin is acting like a **very rough IR sensor**. A thermometer gun does the *exact same thing*, but with extreme precision using a lens and an electronic detector. **The Analogy — "The Glow Detector":** | Human Sense | Equivalent in IR Gun | |-------------|----------------------| | Your skin feels heat from a tawa | The lens collects IR radiation from the target | | Your brain judges "hot" or "cold" | The thermopile detector generates a voltage | | You squint to focus on the tawa | Optical lens focuses IR onto the sensor | | Reading the room temperature on your watch | Microcontroller converts voltage → °C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway:** An IR gun does NOT measure temperature directly — it measures **infrared radiation intensity** and *infers* temperature via Planck's/Stefan-Boltzmann laws. This is why **emissivity** settings are critical for accuracy. --- ## 1.3 Why IR Guns are Needed (Engineering Motivation) Traditional contact thermometers (RTDs, thermocouples, thermistors) require **physical contact** with the target. This fails when: - The object is **moving** (e.g., rollers, conveyor belts, turbine blades). - The object is **extremely hot** (>1000 °C, where contact sensors melt). - The object is **electrically live** (busbars, live wires). - The object is **inaccessible** or **hazardous** (molten metal, furnace interiors). - The object has **very low thermal mass** (a contact probe would cool it). IR guns solve all these by being **non-contact**, with response times in **milliseconds**. > [!TIP] > **KTU Buzzword Alert:** Examiners love the phrase *"IR guns are non-contact, fast-response instruments based on the principle of blackbody radiation."* Memorize this line. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Infrared Guns (Foundation for Deeper Study) IR guns are broadly classified as **Radiation Pyrometers**. They fall under three main categories: 1. **Total Radiation Pyrometer** — measures energy across *all* IR wavelengths. 2. **Single-Wavelength (Spectral) Pyrometer** — measures IR at *one* wavelength band. 3. **Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer** — measures IR at *two* wavelengths and takes the ratio. > [!NOTE] > The handheld "gun" most students are familiar with (like those used in COVID-19 screening) is typically a **single-wavelength spectral pyrometer**. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Physics: Blackbody Radiation A **Blackbody** is an *idealized* object that absorbs **all** incident radiation and emits radiation at the maximum possible intensity for a given temperature. Real objects are called **gray bodies** and emit a fraction of blackbody radiation, governed by **emissivity (ε)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Emissivity (ε)** is the ratio of radiation emitted by a real body to that emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature. Range: **0 < ε ≤ 1**. --- ## 2.2 The Three Governing Laws (Operational Core) ### A. Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Radiation) Every body above **absolute zero (0 K)** emits thermal radiation. The **total radiant energy per unit area** emitted by a blackbody is proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**. For a **blackbody**: $$E_b = \sigma T^4$$ For a **real (gray) body** with emissivity ε: $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$ Where: - $E_b$ = total emitted radiant energy per unit area (W/m²) - $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** - $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin** - $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of the surface (**0 to 1**) > [!NOTE] > This is the **primary equation** used inside the IR gun's detector output processing. The detector voltage is proportional to $T^4$, which is then linearized by an internal circuit. --- ### B. Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength) The wavelength at which a blackbody emits **maximum radiation** is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature. $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}$$ Where: - $\lambda_{max}$ = peak wavelength of emitted radiation (meters or micrometers) - $b$ = Wien's displacement constant = **2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) **Why this matters for IR guns:** - At room temperature (~300 K): $\lambda_{max} ≈ 9.66 \,\mu m$ → **far-IR band** - At 1000 K: $\lambda_{max} ≈ 2.9 \,\mu m$ → **near-IR band** - IR gun detectors (e.g., thermopiles) are typically sensitive in the **8–14 μm** atmospheric window. --- ### C. Planck's Radiation Law (Spectral Distribution) Planck's law describes the spectral emissive power of a blackbody at a given wavelength and temperature. This is what fundamentally allows *spectral* pyrometers to work. $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ Where: - $E_{b\lambda}$ = spectral emissive power per unit wavelength (W/m²·m) - $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** - $c$ = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s** - $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) - $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) > [!TIP] > For KTU exams, you generally only need to *state* Planck's law and explain its physical meaning — full numerical integration is rarely required at the B.Tech level. --- ## 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Infrared Emission**: Every object above 0 K emits IR radiation (proportional to $T^4$). 2. **Optical Collection**: A lens (often germanium or silicon) collects and focuses this IR radiation onto a detector. 3. **Detection (Thermopile)**: The detector (typically a **thermopile**) absorbs IR photons and generates a small EMF (μV range). 4. **Signal Conditioning**: The weak signal is amplified, linearized (since $V \propto T^4$), and temperature-compensated (compensates for the gun's own ambient temperature). 5. **Display**: The processed signal drives an LCD showing temperature in °C or °F. 6. **Emissivity Adjustment**: The user inputs the target's emissivity; the circuit scales the reading accordingly. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table — it covers **90%** of all numerical problems asked in KTU exams. | # | Law / Concept | Formula | Key Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Blackbody) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ ; $T$ in K | | 2 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Gray Body) | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\varepsilon$ dimensionless (0–1) | | 3 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{max} = b / T$ | $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | | 4 | Planck's Spectral Law | $E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 [e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1]}$ | $h, c, k_B$ as constants | | 5 | Emissivity Correction | $T_{actual} = T_{measured} \cdot \varepsilon^{1/4}$ (approx) | Used in two-color compensation | | 6 | Detector Voltage | $V_{out} \propto \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Output of thermopile | **⚠️ Critical Note on Table:** I have used `\cdot` and `\times` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to ensure the markdown table renders perfectly. For absolute-value-style notation in formulas, use `\vert x \vert` instead of `|x|`. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Steel Industry** | Continuous temperature monitoring of molten metal streams | | **Power Plants** | Boiler tube surface temperature (steam pipe monitoring) | | **HVAC** | Building heat-loss audits via thermal imaging | | **Electrical Maintenance** | Detecting overheating in circuit breakers, transformers | | **Medical** | Forehead temperature screening (COVID-era thermometers) | | **Automotive** | Brake rotor, engine exhaust temperature checks | | **Food Industry** | Cold-chain monitoring, packaging line temperature | | **Glass/Ceramics** | Furnace interior temperature without contamination | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention **at least 3 industrial applications** when asked to "explain the significance of IR thermometers" — it scores easy full marks. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law (from Planck's Law) This derivation shows why the total emitted energy scales with $T^4$, which is the heart of every IR gun. ### Step 1 — Start with Planck's Spectral Distribution $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ ### Step 2 — Total Emissive Power = Integral Over All Wavelengths The total emitted power per unit area is obtained by integrating spectral emissive power over the entire spectrum ($\lambda = 0 \to \infty$): $$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$$ $$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]} \, d\lambda$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Dimensionless Variable Let $u = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$ ⇒ $\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{u k_B T}$, so $d\lambda = -\dfrac{h c}{u^2 k_B T} \, du$. When $\lambda \to 0$, $u \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $u \to 0$.
