Negating limits flips the sign: $$E_b = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\left(\dfrac{h c}{u k_B T}\right)^5 \left[ e^u - 1 \right]} \cdot \left( -\dfrac{h c}{u^2 k_B T} \right) du$$ ### Step 4 — Simplify the Powers The numerator and denominator powers of $\lambda$ collapse: $$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2 \cdot u^5 (k_B T)^5}{(h c)^5 [e^u - 1]} \cdot \frac{h c}{u^2 k_B T} \, du$$ Collecting terms: $$E_b = \frac{2\pi (k_B T)^4}{(h c)^3} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} \, du$$ ### Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral The integral is a standard result from thermodynamic calculus: $$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} \, du = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify $$E_b = \frac{2\pi (k_B T)^4}{(h c)^3} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^3} \cdot T^4$$ ### Step 7 — Recognize the Stefan-Boltzmann Constant By definition: $$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^3}$$ Plugging values: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴. Therefore: $$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$$ > [!NOTE] > This proves that **total emitted thermal energy is proportional to $T^4$**, justifying why IR gun detectors produce a $T^4$-dependent voltage that must be linearized for a digital °C readout. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law ### Step 1 — Start with Planck's Law $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ ### Step 2 — Find Condition for Maximum At peak wavelength, $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$. Differentiating with respect to $\lambda$ and setting to zero yields the transcendental equation: $$5 (1 - e^{-x}) = x$$ where $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{max} k_B T}$. ### Step 3 — Numerical Solution The root of this equation is: $$x = 4.9651...$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange for $\lambda_{max}$ $$\frac{h c}{\lambda_{max} k_B T} = 4.9651$$ $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{h c}{4.9651 \, k_B T}$$ ### Step 5 — Group Constants into Wien's Constant Define: $$b = \frac{h c}{4.9651 \, k_B} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical example (KTU-style):** Find $\lambda_{max}$ for the human body surface at $T = 306$ K (≈ 33 °C): > > $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{306} \approx 9.47 \,\mu m$$ > > This is why medical IR thermometers and human-screening IR guns use detectors sensitive in the **8–14 μm band** — it's the peak emission region for body-temperature targets. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem) **Problem:** A steel billet radiates as a gray body with emissivity ε = 0.85. The IR gun measures its surface temperature as 950 °C. Compute: 1. The total emitted power per unit area. 2. The peak wavelength of emission. ### Solution **Step A — Convert to Absolute Temperature:** $T = 950 + 273 = 1223$ K **Step B — Compute Emitted Energy (Gray Body):** $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.85 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1223)^4$$ Compute $1223^4$: $$1223^2 = 1{,}495{,}729$$ $$1223^4 = (1{,}495{,}729)^2 \approx 2.237 \times 10^{12}$$ Therefore: $$E = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.237 \times 10^{12}$$ $$E \approx 1.078 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 107.8 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$ **Step C — Compute Peak Wavelength:** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1223}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 2.37 \,\mu m}$$ > [!NOTE] > At ~2.37 μm, this falls in the **near-IR / short-wave IR** region — exactly why high-temperature industrial pyrometers use **InGaAs** or **silicon** detectors rather than thermopiles. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked) | Parameter | Contact Sensor (Thermocouple) | IR Gun (Pyrometer) | |---|---|---| | Mode of Operation | Contact required | Non-contact | | Response Time | Seconds | Milliseconds | | Upper Range Limit | ~2300 °C (with special probes) | No upper theoretical limit | | Accuracy | ±0.1 % to ±1 % | ±1 % to ±5 % (emissivity dependent) | | Affected by Emissivity? | No | **Yes — critically** | | Affected by Distance? | No | **Yes — D:S ratio matters** | | Can measure moving objects? | Difficult | Yes (ideal) | | Can measure hazardous targets? | No | Yes | | Cost (basic) | Low | Moderate | > [!TIP] > **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Compare contact and non-contact temperature measurement methods."* This matrix is your answer template. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of an IR Gun ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Target Object
Hot Surface] --> B[Optical Lens
Germanium or Silicon] B --> C[IR Detector
Thermopile] C --> D[Amplifier Stage
Low-noise Op-amp] D --> E[Linearization Circuit
Compensates T to the power 4] E --> F[Ambient Temp Compensator
NTC Thermistor Reference] F --> G[Microcontroller / ADC
Digital Conversion] G --> H[LCD Display
Temperature in degree C or F] I[User Emissivity Knob
epsilon 0 to 1] --> G J[Battery Power Supply
9V or Li-ion] --> G J --> D ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Signal Path of an IR Gun ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([IR Radiation Emitted
by Target]) --> Lens1[Step 1: Optical Lens
Collects and Focuses IR] Lens1 --> Filter[Step 2: Bandpass Filter
Selects Wavelength
e.g. 8 to 14 micrometer] Filter --> Detect[Step 3: Thermopile Detector
Converts IR to Voltage] Detect --> Amp[Step 4: Amplification
microVolt to mV range] Amp --> Linear[Step 5: Linearization
T to power 4 correction] Linear --> Comp[Step 6: Emissivity Compensation
Multiplier for epsilon] Comp --> ADC[Step 7: ADC Conversion
Analog to Digital] ADC --> LCD[Step 8: LCD Display
Final Temperature Reading] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison of Three Pyrometer Types ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TotalPyrometer T1[All Wavelength Detector] --> T2[Wideband Thermopile
No Filter] --> T3[Stefan Boltzmann Output
V proportional to T power 4] end subgraph SpectralPyrometer S1[Single Wavelength Filter] --> S2[Narrowband Detector
e.g. 0.65 micrometer] --> S3[Planck Law Output
Single lambda] end subgraph TwoColorPyrometer R1[Wavelength 1 Filter] --> R2[Detector 1] R3[Wavelength 2 Filter] --> R4[Detector 2] R2 --> R5[Ratio Circuit] R4 --> R5 R5 --> R6[Emissivity Independent Output] end ``` --- ## 4.4 Distance-to-Spot (D:S) Ratio Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IR Gun
Sensor Tip] ---|Distance D| B[Target Surface] B --> C[Measurement Spot Diameter S] note[Note: D to S ratio of 12 to 1 means
at 12 inches distance, the spot is 1 inch] A -.-> note ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **D:S Ratio Engineering Rule:** If the gun's D:S ratio is **12:1** and you stand **12 inches** from the target, the IR gun measures an averaged temperature over a **1-inch diameter** spot. **Always ensure the target fills the spot** — otherwise, the reading will include background temperatures and be inaccurate. --- ## 4.5 Key Components Layout (Cross-sectional Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IRGunBody H[Outer Casing
Ergonomic Pistol Grip] --> Tr[Trigger Switch
Activates Laser Pointer] H --> L[Optical Lens Assembly
Front End] H --> D[Detector Housing
Thermopile with Thermistor] H --> PCB[Main PCB
Microcontroller and ADC] H --> Bat[Battery Compartment
Bottom of Handle] H --> Scr[LCD Screen
Top Rear] L --> D D --> PCB PCB --> Scr Bat --> PCB end ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define an infrared thermometer. State any two of its industrial applications.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An infrared thermometer (also called an IR gun or radiation pyrometer) is a **non-contact temperature-measuring instrument** that detects the infrared thermal radiation emitted by a target object and converts it into an electrical signal proportional to its surface temperature. **Two industrial applications:** 1. **Steel Industry** — Continuous monitoring of molten steel temperature in continuous casting operations. 2. **Electrical Maintenance** — Identifying overheating joints in switchgear, busbars, and transformers without de-energizing the system. *(Other valid applications: HVAC audits, food cold-chain monitoring, furnace tube temperature, automotive brake testing, medical screening.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023] State Stefan-Boltzmann Law. What is the significance of the emissivity factor in IR thermometry?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The total radiant energy emitted per unit area by a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**. $$E_b = \sigma T^4$$ **Significance of Emissivity (ε):** - Real objects are not perfect blackbodies; they emit only a fraction of blackbody radiation. - This fraction is the **emissivity (ε)**, ranging from **0 to 1**. - For a real surface: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. - The IR gun must be set to the correct ε of the target, otherwise it will read **too low**. For example, polished metals (ε ≈ 0.05–0.2) cause huge under-readings if the gun is left at the default ε = 0.95. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K.** Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's laws require **Kelvin**. Marks lost every year. > 2. **Using emissivity = 1 by default.** Always state the assumed ε explicitly. > 3. **Vague diagrams.** Box-and-arrow block diagrams earn marks; sketch-and-label diagrams don't. > 4. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always write W/m² or μm with the numerical result. > 5. **Confusing IR gun with IR camera.** They are different. The gun is a **point instrument**; the camera is an **imaging instrument**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]** **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an infrared thermometer. Discuss the role of the optical lens and the thermopile detector. **(7 Marks)** **Q. (b)** A furnace wall has an emissivity of 0.78. The IR thermometer (set to ε = 0.78) reads a temperature of 850 °C. Determine: 1. The total radiated energy per unit area. 2. The wavelength at which maximum radiation occurs. 3. What would the IR gun display if the emissivity setting were mistakenly left at 0.95? **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** **Construction (Block-by-block):** 1. **Optical Lens** — A convex lens made of IR-transmissive material (germanium, silicon, or zinc selenide) is placed at the front end. It collects and focuses IR radiation from the target onto the detector. 2. **Bandpass Filter** — A spectral filter restricts the detector's response to a specific wavelength band (typically 8–14 μm for medical/industrial use, or 0.65 μm for very high-temperature use). 3. **Thermopile Detector** — A series of thermocouple junctions arranged in a hot-cold configuration. The "hot" junctions are blackened to absorb IR; the "cold" junctions are shielded and kept at the gun's internal ambient temperature (measured by an NTC thermistor for reference). 4. **Amplifier Stage** — A low-noise instrumentation amplifier boosts the μV-level thermopile EMF to mV. 5. **Linearization Circuit** — Corrects the nonlinear $V \propto T^4$ relationship to produce a linear °C output. 6. **Microcontroller + ADC** — Digitizes the signal, applies emissivity correction, and drives the display. 7. **LCD Display** — Shows the temperature reading. 8. **Laser Pointer (optional)** — Aiming aid for precise targeting. **Working Principle:** - The target surface emits IR radiation according to Stefan-Boltzmann law: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. - This radiation is focused by the lens onto the thermopile's hot junctions. - The temperature difference between hot and cold junctions produces an EMF proportional to incident IR intensity. - This EMF is amplified, linearized, and corrected for emissivity, then displayed as the target's surface temperature. **Key roles:** - *Optical lens:* collects and concentrates diffuse IR into a focused beam onto a small detector area — increases signal strength and reduces background noise. - *Thermopile detector:* converts IR radiation energy into measurable EMF without requiring any cooling or moving parts — robust and reliable. --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** **Given Data:** - Emissivity of furnace wall: $\varepsilon = 0.78$ - Measured temperature: $T = 850 \,°C = 1123 \,K$ - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ - $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K **Part (b.1) — Total Radiated Energy:** Using Stefan-Boltzmann law for a gray body: $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$ $$E = 0.78 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1123)^4$$ First compute $1123^4$: $$1123^2 = 1{,}261{,}129$$ $$1123^4 = (1{,}261{,}129)^2 \approx 1.590 \times 10^{12}$$ $$E = 0.78 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.590 \times 10^{12}$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 70{,}303 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 70.3 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$ **Part (b.2) — Peak Wavelength:** By Wien's displacement law: $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1123}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 2.58 \,\mu m}$$ **Part (b.3) — What if ε was set to 0.95 instead of 0.78?** The IR gun measures emitted energy and back-calculates assuming ε = 0.95. But the **actual** energy corresponds to ε = 0.78. The gun will therefore **overestimate** the temperature (because it assumes more energy was emitted per unit temperature than reality). The relationship between true temperature and indicated temperature (for fixed actual emitted energy) is: $$T_{indicated}^4 \times \varepsilon_{set} = T_{actual}^4 \times \varepsilon_{actual}$$ $$T_{indicated} = T_{actual} \times \left(\frac{\varepsilon_{actual}}{\varepsilon_{set}}\right)^{1/4}$$ $$T_{indicated} = 1123 \times \left(\frac{0.78}{0.95}\right)^{1/4}$$ $$= 1123 \times (0.8211)^{1/4}$$ $$= 1123 \times 0.9529$$ $$\approx 1070 \,K = 797 \,°C$$ $$\boxed{\text{IR gun would display approximately } 797 \,°C}$$ This is a **53 °C error** due to incorrect emissivity setting — a critical lesson for industrial practice. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **Q. (a)** Explain the following laws of thermal radiation, stating their relevance to infrared thermometry: 1. Stefan-Boltzmann Law 2. Wien's Displacement Law 3. Planck's Law **(7 Marks)** **Q. (b)** Compare total radiation, single-wavelength, and two-color (ratio) pyrometers with respect to their working principle, dependence on emissivity, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** **1. Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The total radiant energy emitted per unit surface area of a blackbody is proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**. $$E_b = \sigma T^4$$ **Relevance:** The thermopile detector in an IR gun produces an EMF proportional to the *total* incident IR energy. This voltage is therefore proportional to $T^4$. Without the $T^4$ relationship, the entire measurement chain would not work. This law also defines the **maximum theoretical sensitivity** of IR thermometers. **2. Wien's Displacement Law:** The wavelength at which a blackbody emits the maximum amount of radiation is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature. $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}, \quad b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$ **Relevance:** This law tells the IR gun designer **which wavelength to detect**. For room-temperature objects (~300 K), peak emission is ~9.7 μm, so detectors and filters in the **8–14 μm atmospheric window** are used. For very hot objects (>1000 K), peak emission shifts to ~2 μm, requiring **silicon or InGaAs** detectors instead. **3. Planck's Law:** The spectral emissive power of a blackbody as a function of wavelength and temperature: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ **Relevance:** Planck's law is the *parent equation* from which both Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's laws are derived. It is used in **spectral pyrometers** to compute the monochromatic intensity at a chosen wavelength, which is then matched against the detector output to derive temperature. It is also the foundation for understanding why **two-color pyrometers** can be made largely emissivity-independent. --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** | Feature | Total Radiation Pyrometer | Single-Wavelength (Spectral) Pyrometer | Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer | |---|---|---|---| | **Wavelength Range** | All wavelengths (broadband) | Single narrow band (e.g., 0.65 μm) | Two narrow bands (e.g., λ₁ and λ₂) | | **Detector** | Thermopile (broadband) | Photocell / photodiode (silicon, InGaAs) | Two matched photodetectors | | **Governing Law** | Stefan-Boltzmann ($E \propto T^4$) | Planck's Law at chosen λ | Ratio of Planck's Law at two λ | | **Emissivity Dependence** | Strong — needs accurate ε | Strong — needs accurate ε | **Weak** — ratio cancels ε (if ε same at both λ) | | **Suitable Range** | Medium to high (above ~600 °C) | Very high (>700 °C, up to 6000 °C) | High to very high, ideal for smoky/furnace environments | | **Typical Application** | General industrial temperature | Molten metal, furnace interiors | Steel mills, dirty/smoky environments, small targets | | **Complexity** | Low | Moderate | High (needs two detectors + ratio circuit) | | **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | **Conclusion:** The **two-color pyrometer** is the most robust against emissivity variation, smoke, and partial obstructions, but it is more expensive and complex. The **total radiation pyrometer** is the simplest and cheapest. The **spectral pyrometer** is the industry workhorse for clean, high-temperature targets. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final-day revision summary — read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - [ ] **Definition:** IR gun = non-contact thermometer based on IR radiation detection. - [ ] **Primary Law:** Stefan-Boltzmann: $E_b = \sigma T^4$ (blackbody), $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ (gray body). - [ ] **Secondary Laws:** Wien's displacement ($\lambda_{max} = b/T$), Planck's spectral law. - [ ] **Constants:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ ; $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K. - [ ] **Always convert °C → K** before using these laws (add 273). - [ ] **Emissivity (ε):** Range 0 to 1. Polished metal ≈ 0.05; human skin ≈ 0.98; black paint ≈ 0.97. - [ ] **Emissivity error correction:** $T_{true} = T_{indicated} \times (\varepsilon_{set} / \varepsilon_{actual})^{1/4}$. - [ ] **Three types:** Total radiation (broadband), Spectral (single λ), Two-color (ratio). - [ ] **Atmospheric window:** 8–14 μm is used for room-temperature IR thermometers. - [ ] **D:S ratio:** Indicates the measurement spot size at a given distance. - [ ] **Detector types:** Thermopile (broadband), Thermopile + silicon (narrowband), InGaAs (high temp). - [ ] **Applications (remember at least 3):** Steel, power plants, HVAC, electrical, medical, food, glass. - [ ] **Advantages:** Non-contact, fast (ms), no contamination, can measure moving/hazardous targets. - [ ] **Disadvantages:** Emissivity-sensitive, expensive, needs line of sight, affected by smoke/dust. - [ ] **Common exam pitfall:** Forgetting unit conversion to Kelvin — *the #1 marks killer!* > **End of Notes — Infrared Guns | Module 1 | PCAET602 Industrial Instrumentation**

topics.title: Electronic Temperature Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electronic Temperature Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electronic Temperature Switch** is a solid-state (or hybrid electro-mechanical) instrument that continuously senses the temperature of a process medium via an electronic transducer and produces a **binary (ON/OFF) output** when the measured temperature crosses a pre-configured setpoint. Unlike a continuous transmitter, the output is discrete — typically a relay contact closure, an open-collector transistor output, or a logic-level signal — and is used to actuate alarms, heaters, coolers, or process interlocks.

The "switching" action is fundamentally derived from a **comparator circuit** that compares a temperature-proportional voltage against a precision reference voltage, with **Schmitt-trigger hysteresis** introduced to prevent relay chattering around the setpoint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic lies at the intersection of *sensors* (Module 1) and *signal conditioning* (Module 1/2). Examiners frequently test the **op-amp comparator with hysteresis** derivation and the **IC sensor interfacing** — both are compulsory high-weightage areas.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Home Geyser Thermostat"

Imagine a household electric geyser. Inside it sits a **bimetallic strip**. When the water gets hot enough, the strip bends and physically *breaks* the circuit, switching the heating coil OFF. When the water cools slightly, the strip straightens and the circuit is *made* again, turning the coil ON.

Now replace that crude mechanical strip with a **precise electronic sensor** (like an LM35 giving 10 mV/°C), feed that tiny voltage into an **op-amp comparator**, and use the comparator's output to drive a **relay** instead of a contact. That is, in essence, an **electronic temperature switch** — the same logic, executed with orders-of-magnitude better accuracy, faster response, and remote setpoint adjustability.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Common IC sensor sensitivity | **10 mV/°C** (LM35 family) |
| Current-output sensor coefficient | **1 µA/K** (AD590 family) |
| Reference voltage (typical) | **2.5 V** (TL431 / band-gap) |
| Op-amp supply rails | **±5 V, ±12 V, ±15 V** |
| Hysteresis band (typical industrial) | **2 °C – 10 °C** |
| Relay coil voltage | **5 V, 12 V, 24 V DC** |
| Switching accuracy | **±0.5 °C to ±2 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **1 µA/K** constant of the AD590 is essentially the **Boltzmann constant scaled up** — the underlying physics is the **band-gap reference principle** of integrated silicon.

## 1.4 Why Use Electronic Over Mechanical Temperature Switches?

| Feature | Mechanical (Bimetallic) | Electronic |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±5 °C – ±10 °C | **±0.5 °C or better** |
| Remote setpoint | Not possible | **Trimpot / Digital** |
| Hysteresis control | Fixed by geometry | **Adjustable via resistors** |
| Life expectancy | ~10⁵ cycles | **>10⁷ cycles** |
| Response time | Slow (seconds) | **Fast (ms)** |
| Output interface | Pure contact | **Relay / Transistor / Logic** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Electronic Temperature Switch

Every electronic temperature switch — regardless of manufacturer — comprises **five universal functional blocks**:

1. **Temperature Sensing Element** — converts thermal energy into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — amplifies, linearizes, and filters the raw sensor output.
3. **Reference Voltage Source** — provides a stable, temperature-independent comparison threshold.
4. **Comparator with Hysteresis (Schmitt Trigger)** — compares conditioned signal against reference and produces clean digital output.
5. **Output Driver Stage** — relay coil driver, LED indicator, open-collector output, or triac for AC loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Example:** The *Honeywell T775* series and *Watlow LS* series are real-world packaged implementations of this exact block diagram. KTU questions often implicitly reference such industrial variants.

## 2.2 Detailed Operation — Step by Step Logic

### Step 1: Sensing
The sensor produces a temperature-proportional voltage (LM35) or current (AD590). The output is **small and analog**.

### Step 2: Conditioning
A non-inverting amplifier (gain = 1 + R_f/R_in) scales the sensor voltage to match the comparator's common-mode range. For example, an LM35 at 100 °C gives 1000 mV; this may be directly compared without amplification, but for 0–5 V ADC compatibility, a gain of 5 is applied.

### Step 3: Reference Generation
A precision **band-gap reference** (e.g., TL431 = 2.495 V) or the op-amp's own offset is used to set the **setpoint voltage** corresponding to the desired trip temperature.

### Step 4: Comparison & Hysteresis
The op-amp (LM741, LM311, LM393) operates in **open-loop** mode. The differential input is:
- *Positive* if (V_sensor > V_setpoint) → Output saturates to **+V_sat** (or V_OH).
- *Negative* if (V_sensor < V_setpoint) → Output saturates to **−V_sat** (or V_OL).
- A **positive feedback resistor** (R_f) from output to non-inverting input creates two distinct threshold voltages: **V_TH (Upper)** and **V_TL (Lower)**. The difference is the **hysteresis voltage V_H**.

### Step 5: Output Actuation
The comparator output drives a **transistor switch** (e.g., BC547) that energizes a **relay coil**. The relay contact then switches the actual load (heater, alarm, motor contactor).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these **three equations** — they appear in nearly every 14-mark KTU question on this topic. Numerical values for setpoint resistors or hysteresis components are almost always asked.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | V_sensor = S × T | Sensor output voltage; S = sensitivity (V/°C), T = temperature |
| 2 | V_TH = V_ref × R2/(R1+R2) + V_sat × R1/(R1+R2) | Upper threshold of inverting Schmitt trigger |
| 3 | V_TL = V_ref × R2/(R1+R2) − V_sat × R1/(R1+R2) | Lower threshold of inverting Schmitt trigger |
| 4 | V_H = V_TH − V_TL = 2 × V_sat × R1/(R1+R2) | Hysteresis band width |
| 5 | T_set = V_setpoint / S | Setpoint temperature in °C from a setpoint voltage |
| 6 | I_OUT = 1 µA/K × T(K) | AD590 current output (Kelvin) |
| 7 | T(°C) = V_OUT(mV) / 10 | LM35 direct conversion |
| 8 | T(K) = V_OUT(mV) / 10 | LM335 direct conversion (output in Kelvin) |
| 9 | R_T = R_0 × exp[B × (1/T − 1/T_0)] | NTC thermistor resistance (Steinhart-Hart simplified) |
| 10 | R_T = R_0 × (1 + αΔT) | RTD (PT100) linear approximation |

> **Note on table syntax:** Vertical bars (such as in `R1+R2`) have been deliberately avoided to preserve markdown table integrity. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in any handwritten/exam answer.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **HVAC Systems:** Electronic switches in chillers cut off compressors if refrigerant temperature exceeds safe limits.
- **Oven Control:** Bakery and ceramic kilns use electronic switches with ±1 °C accuracy — mechanical thermostats cannot achieve this.
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Lithium-ion packs use electronic temperature switches to disconnect charging MOSFETs above 60 °C, preventing thermal runaway.
- **Industrial Motors:** Embedded PT100-based switches trip motor windings if insulation class temperature limits are exceeded.
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Refrigerated trucks use LM35-based switches to log/switch alarms if the cargo temperature deviates from the pharmaceutical spec (2–8 °C).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis Equations

Consider the **inverting Schmitt trigger** with positive feedback. The non-inverting input is tied to a node where V_ref, R1, and feedback resistor R2 meet.

### Circuit Setup
- Input signal V_in is applied to the **inverting (−)** terminal via input resistor R_in.
- The **non-inverting (+)** terminal voltage V_+ is set by the voltage divider formed by R1 (to V_ref) and R2 (to V_out).
- Assume op-amp saturation voltages: V_out = +V_sat or −V_sat.

### Derivation of V_+ (Superposition)

Apply **Millman's theorem** (or superposition) at the non-inverting node:

$$
V_+ = \frac{\dfrac{V_{ref}}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_2}}{\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}}
$$

This is the standard two-source superposition across a parallel combination.

Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by R1·R2:

$$
V_+ = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 + V_{out} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

The comparator switches when V_in = V_+.

### Case A: Output is at +V_sat (Output HIGH)

When V_out = +V_sat, substitute to find the **Upper Threshold V_TH**:

$$
V_{TH} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 + V_{sat} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Rewriting in the KTU-standard "split" form:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

> **Textual interpretation:** The upper threshold is a *weighted sum* of the reference voltage and the positive saturation voltage, weighted by the opposite resistor.

### Case B: Output is at −V_sat (Output LOW)

When V_out = −V_sat, substitute to find the **Lower Threshold V_TL**:

$$
V_{TL} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 - V_{sat} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

In split form:

$$
V_{TL} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

### Case C: Hysteresis Band V_H

Subtract V_TL from V_TH:

$$
V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL} = \left[ V_{ref}\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} + V_{sat}\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} \right] - \left[ V_{ref}\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - V_{sat}\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} \right]
$$

The V_ref terms cancel (subtraction of equal terms):

$$
V_H = 2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

> **Key insight:** The hysteresis band is **independent of V_ref** — it depends *only* on the saturation voltage and the resistor ratio. This is why hysteresis can be tuned by changing R1/R2 alone, leaving the setpoint adjustment (via V_ref or R1/R2 trade-off) clean.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Frequently Asked in KTU

> **Problem (Typical 7-mark KTU sub-question):**
> An LM35-based electronic temperature switch uses an LM311 comparator with ±V_sat = ±13 V. R1 = 10 kΩ, R2 = 90 kΩ, V_ref = 2.50 V. The op-amp is supplied with ±15 V. Find:
> (a) Upper threshold V_TH
> (b) Lower threshold V_TL
> (c) Hysteresis band V_H
> (d) Switching temperatures in °C

**Given:** R1 = 10 kΩ, R2 = 90 kΩ, V_ref = 2.50 V, V_sat = 13 V.

### (a) Upper Threshold V_TH

$$
V_{TH} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
V_{TH} = 2.50 \cdot \frac{90}{100} + 13 \cdot \frac{10}{100}
$$

$$
V_{TH} = 2.50 \cdot 0.9 + 13 \cdot 0.1 = 2.25 + 1.30
$$

$$
V_{TH} = 3.55 \text{ V}
$$

### (b) Lower Threshold V_TL

$$
V_{TL} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
V_{TL} = 2.50 \cdot 0.9 - 13 \cdot 0.1 = 2.25 - 1.30
$$

$$
V_{TL} = 0.95 \text{ V}
$$

### (c) Hysteresis Band V_H

$$
V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 3.55 - 0.95 = 2.60 \text{ V}
$$

Verification using direct formula:

$$
V_H = 2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 2 \cdot 13 \cdot \frac{10}{100} = 2.60 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

### (d) Switching Temperatures (LM35: 10 mV/°C = 0.010 V/°C)

For Upper trip (heater OFF, say):

$$
T_{TH} = \frac{V_{TH}}{0.010} = \frac{3.55}{0.010} = 355 \text{ °C}
$$

For Lower trip (heater ON):

$$
T_{TL} = \frac{V_{TL}}{0.010} = \frac{0.95}{0.010} = 95 \text{ °C}
$$

> **Engineering observation:** The setpoint is *not* the arithmetic center of the band. The **midpoint** temperature is (355+95)/2 = 225 °C, which corresponds to V_ref scaled by the divider: 2.50 × 0.9 = 2.25 V, i.e., 225 °C. This is the *natural* setpoint when R1 and R2 are equal in their roles.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Trade-offs

| Sensor Type | Output Form | Sensitivity | Range | Linearity | Cost | Best Use in Temp Switch |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LM35** | Voltage | 10 mV/°C | −55 to +150 °C | Excellent | Low | General-purpose electronic switch |
| **LM335** | Voltage | 10 mV/K | −40 to +100 °C | Excellent | Low | Kelvin-based absolute switching |
| **AD590** | Current | 1 µA/K | −55 to +150 °C | Excellent | Medium | Long-distance, noise-immune switching |
| **PT100 RTD** | Resistance | 0.385 Ω/°C | −200 to +850 °C | Very Good | High | Industrial high-accuracy switching |
| **Thermocouple (K)** | Voltage | ~41 µV/°C | −200 to +1350 °C | Needs linearization | Medium | High-temperature furnace switches |
| **NTC Thermistor** | Resistance | Non-linear (β) | −55 to +150 °C | Poor (needs log amp) | Very Low | Low-cost, narrow-range switches |
| **Bimetallic** | Mechanical displacement | — | 0 to 300 °C | Moderate | Very Low | Legacy mechanical switches (non-electronic baseline) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[PROCESS TEMPERATURE] --> S2[SENSOR ELEMENT LM35]
    S2 --> S3[SIGNAL CONDITIONING AMPLIFIER]
    S3 --> S4[INVERTING INPUT OF COMPARATOR]
    R1[VOLTAGE REFERENCE TL431] --> R2[NON INVERTING INPUT]
    S4 --> S5[OP AMP COMPARATOR LM311]
    R2 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[POSITIVE FEEDBACK RESISTOR R2]
    S6 --> R2
    S5 --> S7[OUTPUT BUFFER TRANSISTOR BC547]
    S7 --> S8[RELAY COIL 12V DC]
    S8 --> S9[RELAY CONTACT OUTPUT TO LOAD]
    S8 --> S10[LED INDICATOR STATUS]

    classDef sensor fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef ckt fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class S1,S2 sensor
    class S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,R1,R2 ckt
    class S8,S9,S10 output
```

## 4.2 Comparator with Hysteresis — Detailed Schematic View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORBLOCK [TEMPERATURE SENSOR STAGE]
        A1[LM35 SENSOR] -->|10 mV per deg C| A2[RC LOW PASS FILTER 100 nF]
    end

    subgraph SIGNALCOND [SIGNAL CONDITIONING STAGE]
        A2 --> A3[NON INVERTING AMP GAIN 5]
        A3 --> A4[SCALED SENSOR VOLTAGE 0 to 5 V]
    end

    subgraph COMPARATORSTG [COMPARATOR WITH HYSTERESIS]
        A4 --> C1[INVERTING INPUT]
        B1[TL431 REFERENCE 2.5 V] --> B2[RESISTOR R1 10 k]
        B2 --> C2[NON INVERTING NODE]
        C2 --> C3[FEEDBACK RESISTOR R2 90 k FROM OUTPUT]
        C1 --> C4[LM311 OP AMP]
        C2 --> C4
        C4 --> C5[OUTPUT PLUS OR MINUS Vsat]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTSTG [OUTPUT DRIVER STAGE]
        C5 --> D1[BASE RESISTOR 1 k]
        D1 --> D2[BC547 NPN TRANSISTOR]
        D2 --> D3[RELAY COIL 12V]
        D2 --> D4[PROTECTION DIODE 1N4007]
        D2 --> D5[LED INDICATOR WITH 470 OHM]
        D3 --> D6[SPDT CONTACT FOR LOAD]
    end

    classDef scls fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef ccls fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    classDef ocls fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 scls
    class B1,B2,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 ccls
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5,D6 ocls
```

## 4.3 Hysteresis Transfer Characteristic (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Vin LOW] -->|crosses VTL rising| P2[Output SWITCHES to PLUS Vsat]
    P2 --> P3[VTH higher than VTL]
    P3 -->|Vin HIGH drops below VTL| P4[Output RETURNS to MINUS Vsat]
    P4 --> P5[VH equals VTH minus VTL]
    P5 --> P1

    classDef state fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef hyst fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 state
    class P5 hyst
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Choosing Temperature Switch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{REQUIRED ACCURACY}
    Q1 -->|BETTER than 1 deg C| P1[USE RTD PT100 OR IC SENSOR LM35]
    Q1 -->|WITHIN 5 deg C| P2[USE NTC THERMISTOR OR BIMETALLIC]
    Q1 -->|ABOVE 600 deg C| P3[USE THERMOCOUPLE TYPE K]
    P1 --> Q2{NEED REMOTE SETPOINT}
    P2 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|YES| P4[USE DIGITAL POT OR DAC WITH MCU]
    Q2 -->|NO| P5[USE TRIMPOT FOR VREF ADJUST]
    P4 --> P6[ADD HYSTERESIS RESISTORS]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[FINAL OUTPUT RELAY OR TRANSISTOR]

    classDef q fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef p fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2 q
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 p
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define an electronic temperature switch. List any four advantages of electronic temperature switches over mechanical bimetallic thermostats."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An electronic temperature switch is a device that senses temperature using an electronic transducer (RTD, thermistor, IC sensor) and produces a binary output (relay/transistor) when the measured temperature crosses a pre-set threshold. The switching action is implemented using a comparator circuit with hysteresis.

**Advantages (½ mark each, pick any four):**
1. **Higher accuracy** — typically ±0.5 °C vs ±5 °C for bimetallic.
2. **Adjustable setpoint** — via potentiometer or digital input.
3. **Adjustable hysteresis** — via resistor selection, not mechanical rework.
4. **Faster response time** — millisecond range vs seconds.
5. **Longer operational life** — no mechanical contact wear.
6. **Remote mounting and signaling** — output is electrical, can be transmitted.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is hysteresis in a temperature switch? Why is it necessary?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hysteresis in a temperature switch is the intentional difference between the **turn-ON temperature** and the **turn-OFF temperature** of the output. It is implemented using **positive feedback** in the comparator (Schmitt trigger configuration).

**Necessity (any two points, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Prevents relay chattering:** Without hysteresis, when the temperature hovers exactly at the setpoint, the relay would rapidly turn ON and OFF, damaging the contacts and the connected load.
2. **Provides a dead band:** Allows the temperature to drop (or rise) by a finite amount before re-switching, accommodating the thermal inertia of the process.
3. **Improves noise immunity:** Small fluctuations in the sensor signal around the setpoint do not cause false triggering.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice Instruction as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]**
> *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an electronic temperature switch using an LM35 sensor and an op-amp comparator. Derive the expressions for upper and lower threshold voltages."**

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1: Circuit Description (2 marks)**
The circuit consists of:
- **LM35** temperature sensor producing V_sensor = 10 mV/°C × T.
- **Non-inverting amplifier** (optional, gain G) to scale the sensor voltage.
- **Op-amp LM311** in inverting comparator mode.
- **Reference voltage V_ref** at the non-inverting node via resistor R1.
- **Positive feedback resistor R2** from output to non-inverting input.
- **Output stage** — a transistor (BC547) driving a relay coil, with a freewheeling diode (1N4007) and an LED indicator.

**Step 2: Operation (2 marks)**
- When V_sensor (scaled) < V_+ (the threshold), the op-amp output goes to +V_sat.
- When V_sensor > V_+, the output goes to −V_sat.
- The transistor switches OFF/ON, de-energizing/energizing the relay.
- The relay contact controls the heater or alarm load.

**Step 3: Derivation (3 marks)**
At the non-inverting node, using superposition (Millman's theorem):

$$
V_+ = \frac{V_{ref}/R_1 + V_{out}/R_2}{1/R_1 + 1/R_2} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 + V_{out} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Switching condition: V_in = V_+.

**Upper Threshold (V_out = +V_sat):**

$$
V_{TH} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} + V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}
$$

**Lower Threshold (V_out = −V_sat):**

$$
V_{TL} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"An electronic temperature switch uses an LM35 sensor and an LM311 comparator with ±V_sat = ±14 V. The reference voltage is V_ref = 2.5 V, R1 = 4.7 kΩ, and R2 = 47 kΩ. Calculate the upper threshold, lower threshold, hysteresis band, and the corresponding switching temperatures in °C."**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** R1 = 4.7 kΩ, R2 = 47 kΩ, V_ref = 2.5 V, V_sat = 14 V, S_LM35 = 10 mV/°C.

**Step 1: Compute the divider ratio.**

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{4.7}{4.7 + 47} = \frac{4.7}{51.7} = 0.0909
$$

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{47}{51.7} = 0.9091
$$

**Step 2: Upper Threshold V_TH.**

$$
V_{TH} = 2.5 \times 0.9091 + 14 \times 0.0909
$$

$$
V_{TH} = 2.2727 + 1.2727
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{TH} = 3.545 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 3: Lower Threshold V_TL.**

$$
V_{TL} = 2.5 \times 0.9091 - 14 \times 0.0909
$$

$$
V_{TL} = 2.2727 - 1.2727
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{TL} = 1.000 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 4: Hysteresis Band V_H.**

$$
V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 3.545 - 1.000
$$

$$
\boxed{V_H = 2.545 \text{ V}}
$$

**Verification by direct formula:**

$$
V_H = 2 \times V_{sat} \times \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = 2 \times 14 \times 0.0909 = 2.545 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Switching Temperatures.**

$$
T_{TH} = \frac{V_{TH}}{0.010} = \frac{3.545}{0.010} = 354.5 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{TL} = \frac{V_{TL}}{0.010} = \frac{1.000}{0.010} = 100.0 \text{ °C}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The switch trips OFF (high) at 354.5 °C and trips ON (low) at 100.0 °C, with a hysteresis band of 254.5 °C. Such a wide band is unsuitable for precision control; reducing R2 (or R1) tightens the band.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Explain the operating principle of an electronic temperature switch using an AD590 IC temperature sensor. Draw the circuit diagram and discuss its advantages over a thermistor-based switch."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: AD590 Operating Principle (3 marks)**
The AD590 is a **two-terminal integrated circuit temperature transducer** that produces an output **current proportional to absolute temperature**:

$$
I_{OUT} = 1 \text{ µA/K} \times T(K)
$$

For example, at 25 °C = 298.15 K, the output is 298.15 µA. The current is converted to a voltage using a precision load resistor R_L (e.g., 10 kΩ) connected to ground. At 25 °C:

$$
V_{OUT} = 298.15 \text{ µA} \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 2.9815 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Circuit Diagram Description (2 marks)**
- AD590 is connected to a load resistor R_L, generating V_T = 1 µA/K × R_L.
- This voltage feeds the inverting input of a comparator.
- The non-inverting input is tied to a reference (TL431 = 2.5 V) via a divider.
- The comparator output drives a transistor-relay stage.
- Positive feedback through a resistor provides hysteresis.

**Step 3: Advantages over Thermistor-based switch (2 marks)**
1. **Linearity:** AD590 is inherently linear (1 µA/K) — no log amplifier or linearization needed, unlike NTC thermistor.
2. **Noise immunity:** Current output is unaffected by lead resistance and EMI — critical for long cable runs in industrial plants.
3. **Wide range:** −55 to +150 °C with a single IC, no range switching.
4. **Calibration-free:** Factory calibrated to ±0.5 °C; thermistors require batch-specific Steinhart-Hart constants.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A temperature switch is designed using a PT100 RTD in a Wheatstone bridge configuration. The bridge is excited with 5 V. The bridge is balanced at 0 °C. The comparator reference is 2.5 V. If the RTD resistance changes to 138.5 Ω, calculate (i) the bridge output voltage assuming the other three arms are each 100 Ω, and (ii) the temperature at which the switch triggers if the comparator saturates at ±12 V with R1 = R2 = 10 kΩ."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bridge Output Voltage (3 marks)**
Bridge configuration: R1 = R2 = R3 = 100 Ω (fixed arms), R4 = R_T = 138.5 Ω (PT100 at the new temperature), V_ex = 5 V.

Bridge output voltage (V_A − V_B):

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \left( \frac{138.5}{100 + 138.5} - \frac{100}{100 + 100} \right)
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \left( \frac{138.5}{238.5} - 0.5 \right) = 5 \cdot (0.5807 - 0.5)
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot 0.0807 = 0.4036 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Verify Temperature (1 mark)**
For PT100, α = 0.385 Ω/°C, R0 = 100 Ω at 0 °C.

$$
\Delta R = 138.5 - 100 = 38.5 \text{ Ω}
$$

$$
T = \frac{\Delta R}{\alpha \times R_0} = \frac{38.5}{0.385} = 100 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3: Comparator Threshold (3 marks)**
With R1 = R2 = 10 kΩ, the divider is symmetric:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 0.5, \quad \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = 0.5
$$

$$
V_{TH} = V_{ref} \cdot 0.5 + V_{sat} \cdot 0.5 = 2.5 \times 0.5 + 12 \times 0.5 = 1.25 + 6.00 = 7.25 \text{ V}
$$

Since the bridge output is only 0.4036 V at 100 °C, the switch has *not yet* triggered. The bridge output will reach 7.25 V at a much higher temperature — let us find it.

For triggering:

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \left( \frac{R_T}{100 + R_T} - 0.5 \right) = 7.25 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\frac{R_T}{100 + R_T} - 0.5 = 1.45
$$

This yields R_T/(100+R_T) = 1.95, which is **physically impossible** (the ratio cannot exceed 1). This means the simple bridge cannot reach 7.25 V with a 5 V excitation; an **instrumentation amplifier (gain ~25)** is required to scale the 0.4036 V bridge output up to comparator level.

> **Engineering takeaway:** The problem demonstrates that RTD-based switches almost always need an **instrumentation amplifier stage** between the bridge and the comparator. This is a frequently-tested *design observation* in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the saturation voltage is bounded by supply, not infinity.** If the supply is ±15 V, V_sat ≈ ±13 to ±14 V (not ±15 V). Marks deducted for "V_sat = 15 V" in calculations.
> 2. **Confusing LM35 (°C) with LM335 (Kelvin).** LM335 output is 2.98 V at 25 °C, NOT 0.25 V. This single error costs ~3 marks.
> 3. **Drawing the comparator with the feedback on the inverting input.** This makes it a *non-inverting* Schmitt trigger with different threshold formulas. Always double-check: **feedback to non-inverting input** for an *inverting* comparator.
> 4. **Skipping the bridge amplifier stage** in RTD-based questions. Examiners expect a mention of INA128 or similar.
> 5. **Mixing up V_TH and V_TL.** V_TH is the **upper** threshold (output transitioning from LOW to HIGH); V_TL is the **lower** threshold (HIGH to LOW transition). Marks are split 50-50; an inversion loses 7 marks outright.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet-Form Density (High-Yield Items Only)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Electronic temperature switch = sensor + comparator + hysteresis + output driver.
- [ ] **Five functional blocks:** Sensor → Conditioner → Reference → Comparator with Hysteresis → Output Stage.
- [ ] **LM35:** Voltage output, **10 mV/°C**, calibrated in **°C**, range **−55 to +150 °C**.
- [ ] **LM335:** Voltage output, **10 mV/K**, calibrated in **Kelvin**, range **−40 to +100 °C**.
- [ ] **AD590:** Current output, **1 µA/K**, output converted to voltage using load resistor R_L.
- [ ] **PT100 RTD:** α = **0.385 Ω/°C**, requires **Wheatstone bridge** + **instrumentation amplifier**.
- [ ] **Thermocouple K:** ~**41 µV/°C**, needs **cold-junction compensation** and **linearization** (ITS-90 tables).
- [ ] **NTC Thermistor:** Resistance drops with temperature; uses **Steinhart-Hart equation**; non-linear.
- [ ] **Inverting Schmitt trigger thresholds:**

$$
V_{TH} = V_{ref}\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} + V_{sat}\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}
$$

$$
V_{TL} = V_{ref}\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - V_{sat}\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}
$$

- [ ] **Hysteresis band** is independent of V_ref:

$$
V_H = 2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

- [ ] **Hysteresis purpose:** Prevents relay chattering, provides dead band, improves noise immunity.
- [ ] **Comparator ICs to remember:** **LM311** (single, open-collector), **LM393** (dual, open-collector), **LM741** (general purpose, ±V_sat).
- [ ] **Reference ICs:** **TL431** (2.495 V), **LM336** (2.5 V), **band-gap references** built into op-amps.
- [ ] **Relay driver:** NPN transistor (BC547) with **1 kΩ base resistor** and **1N4007 freewheeling diode** across the coil.
- [ ] **Bridge excitation voltage:** Typically 5 V or 10 V; bridge output at full scale is in **millivolts** → amplifier mandatory.
- [ ] **KTU favorite formula trio:** V_TH, V_TL, V_H — *memorize with derivation*.
- [ ] **Industrial examples:** Honeywell T775, Watlow LS, Omron E5C-series — all implement this block diagram.
- [ ] **Why hysteresis is independent of V_ref:** Because the V_ref contribution *appears equally* in V_TH and V_TL and cancels upon subtraction.
- [ ] **AD590 hook:** R_L selection directly determines V/°C scale (e.g., R_L = 10 kΩ → 10 mV/K).
- [ ] **Cold-junction compensation** is mandatory for thermocouples — never omit in KTU answers.
- [ ] **Sensor selection rule of thumb:** Use IC sensors for ≤150 °C, RTD for ≤850 °C, thermocouples for higher.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluidic Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fluidic Sensors # Fluidic Sensors — Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Fluidic Sensor** is a measurement device that exploits the physical, thermodynamic, and fluid-dynamic properties of a working fluid (gas or liquid) to detect, quantify, or transduce a physical, chemical, or mechanical input into a measurable output signal. These sensors operate on principles such as **Bernoulli's equation**, **continuity equation**, **laminar/turbulent flow transition**, **Coanda effect**, and **fluidic oscillation**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Note:** Fluidic sensors form the bridge between *primary sensing elements* (which convert process energy) and *secondary transducers* (which convert it to electrical signals). They are extensively used where electrical intrusion is hazardous (explosive environments, EMI-sensitive zones, medical implants). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Intuition" Imagine you are watering your garden. When you **partially block** the hose opening with your thumb, the water shoots out faster and farther. You have intuitively demonstrated **Bernoulli's principle** — a reduction in cross-sectional area causes a pressure drop and a velocity increase. A fluidic sensor works on the exact same idea: - **Input (Process variable)** → Throttles/diverts/oscillates the fluid. - **Fluid response** → Pressure drop, velocity change, or oscillation frequency. - **Output (Measurable signal)** → Pressure reading, frequency, or displacement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Operating Domains of Fluidic Sensors:** > 1. **Pneumatic Domain** — Air or inert gas is the working fluid (clean, safe, intrinsically explosion-proof). > 2. **Hydraulic Domain** — Oil or water is the working fluid (high force transmission, industrial automation). > 3. **Fluidic-Logic Domain** — Pure fluid streams (no moving mechanical parts) perform logic, used in **Fluidics / Fluidic Amplifiers**. ### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics | Classification Axis | Categories | |---|---| | Working Fluid | **Pneumatic**, **Hydraulic**, **Gas-dynamic** | | Sensing Principle | **Differential Pressure**, **Bernoulli-type**, **Oscillator-based**, **Jet-deflection** | | Output Signal | **Analog (pressure, flow)**, **Digital (frequency)** | | Application | **Industrial Process**, **Aerospace**, **Biomedical**, **Nuclear** | ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used - **Density of air at STP:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Density of water at 4°C:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Atmospheric pressure (standard):** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Governing Principles A fluidic sensor's operation rests on three pillars of fluid mechanics. Every KTU numerical problem will reduce to applying these equations. ### Pillar 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass) For an incompressible fluid flowing through a conduit of varying cross-section: $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$ - **Why it matters:** Any obstruction, restriction, or nozzle change forces the fluid to accelerate to maintain mass flow rate. - **How a sensor uses it:** A *Venturi* or *Orifice* forces velocity change → translates to measurable pressure drop. ### Pillar 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy) For a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline: $$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$ - **Why it matters:** When velocity increases, static pressure *decreases* — this is the foundation of nearly all fluidic pressure/flow sensors. - **How a sensor uses it:** Pitot-static tubes, orifice plates, and Venturi meters exploit the velocity–pressure interchange. ### Pillar 3 — Coanda Effect (Wall-Attachment Phenomenon) A free jet of fluid, when passed near a curved wall, tends to "stick" to the wall due to entrainment of surrounding fluid creating a low-pressure region. - **Why it matters:** Allows a small control flow to switch a large power jet — the basis of **fluidic amplifiers** and **digital fluidic logic**. - **How a sensor uses it:** Wall-attachment nozzles, flip-flop amplifiers, proportional amplifiers. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility Snapshot:** > - **Aerospace:** Pneumatic angle-of-attack and air-data sensors in aircraft. > - **Process Plants:** Orifice-based flow meters account for ~50% of industrial flow measurement worldwide. > - **Medical:** MEMS fluidic pressure sensors for catheters and intracranial pressure monitoring. > - **Nuclear/Hazardous:** Pure fluidic-logic controllers operate in environments where any spark would be catastrophic. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the table below. Every 14-mark KTU numerical on this topic will combine 2–3 of these equations. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units (SI) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s | | 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s | | 3 | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g z = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli's equation | Pa | | 4 | $\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$ | Velocity-derived pressure drop | Pa | | 5 | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$ | Orifice flow equation (ISO 5167) | m³/s | | 6 | $\beta = d/D$ | Diameter ratio (orifice) | dimensionless | | 7 | $v = \sqrt{2gH}$ | Torricelli's velocity (Pitot) | m/s | | 8 | $P_{total} = P_{static} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Pitot-static relation | Pa | | 9 | $f = K \cdot \dfrac{v}{L}$ | Fluidic oscillator frequency (Strouhal scaling) | Hz | | 10 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (laminar/turbulent check) | dimensionless | | 11 | $h = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}$ | Strouhal–frequency relationship | — | | 12 | $F = \rho Q (v_{out} - v_{in})$ | Momentum-based thrust (jet sensor) | N | Where: - $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically $0.6$ to $0.65$ for sharp-edged orifice) - $\beta$ = diameter ratio - $K$ = Strouhal number constant (≈ 0.2 for vortex shedding) - $L$ = characteristic length of oscillator ## 2.3 Engineering "Why-How" Reasoning Chain > **Why** does a fluidic sensor exist in the industrial ecosystem? > Because some measurement environments (high voltage switchyards, MRIs, explosive chemical plants, nuclear reactors) **forbid** electrical or electronic sensors — they need intrinsically safe, electromagnetic-interference-immune devices. > **How** does it deliver measurement? > By converting a process variable (pressure, velocity, force, temperature) into a **fluid-dynamic response** (pressure change, frequency shift, jet deflection), which is then either read directly (analog) or converted to an electrical signal via a secondary transducer (capacitive, piezoresistive, or optical). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop Across an Orifice Plate (KTU High-Yield) **Problem Setup:** An incompressible fluid flows through a pipe of diameter $D$. An orifice plate of diameter $d$ is inserted, creating a **vena contracta** (minimum area) downstream. Derive the relationship between the pressure drop and the flow rate. --- ### Step 1 — Apply Continuity Equation At the upstream section (1) and the vena contracta (2): $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$ $$\frac{\pi D^2}{4} v_1 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} v_2$$ Rearranging: $$v_1 = \left(\frac{d^2}{D^2}\right) v_2 = \beta^2 v_2$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (neglecting elevation change) $$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$ Solve for the velocity at the vena contracta: $$P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute $v_1 = \beta^2 v_2$ $$P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \, v_2^2 \,(1 - \beta^4)$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $v_2$ $$v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Volumetric Flow Rate The **ideal (theoretical)** flow rate is: $$Q_{ideal} = A_2 v_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$ ### Step 6 — Apply Discharge Coefficient $C_d$ The real flow is lower due to viscous effects and contraction. Multiplying by $C_d$: $$\boxed{\,Q = C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}\,}$$ This is the **ISO 5167 standard orifice flow equation** — used in nearly every process industry. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pitot-Static Tube Velocity Measurement **Problem Setup:** A Pitot-static probe is immersed in a flowing stream. The stagnation (total) pressure is measured at the front opening, and static pressure is measured at the side wall. Derive the velocity. --- ### Step 1 — Stagnation Condition At the front opening, the fluid is brought to rest ($v_2 = 0$). Apply Bernoulli between a far-field point (1) and the stagnation point (2): $$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + 0$$ ### Step 2 — Identify Terms - $P_1$ = static pressure (measured by side tap) = $P_{static}$ - $P_2$ = stagnation pressure (measured by front tap) = $P_{total}$ - $v_1$ = free-stream velocity = $v$ ### Step 3 — Rearrange $$P_{total} - P_{static} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for Velocity $$\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_{total} - P_{static})}{\rho}}\,}$$ This is the **Pitot-static equation** — directly used in aircraft airspeed indicators. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fluidic Oscillator Frequency (Vortex Shedding) **Problem Setup:** A bluff body placed in a flow sheds alternating vortices (von Kármán vortex street). Derive the shedding frequency. --- ### Step 1 — Define Strouhal Number The dimensionless Strouhal number is: $$\text{St} = \dfrac{f \cdot D}{v}$$ Where $D$ is the characteristic body width and $v$ is the free-stream velocity. ### Step 2 — Solve for Frequency $$\boxed{\,f = \text{St} \cdot \dfrac{v}{D}\,}$$ For a cylindrical bluff body, $\text{St} \approx 0.21$ in the Reynolds range $10^3$ to $2 \times 10^5$. This principle is the basis of **vortex shedding flowmeters**, a very common KTU problem. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Question:** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) flows through a 100 mm diameter pipe fitted with a 50 mm diameter orifice. A differential manometer reads $\Delta P = 10$ kPa. If $C_d = 0.62$, find the volumetric flow rate. **Solution:** $$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5$$ $$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - (0.5)^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$Q = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000}{1000 \times 0.9375}}$$ Inside the square root: $$\dfrac{20{,}000}{937.5} = 21.33$$ $$\sqrt{21.33} = 4.62 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.62$$ $$\boxed{\,Q \approx 5.62 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.62 \text{ L/s}\,}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Sensor for Which Job? | Sensor Type | Best Suited For | Range | Accuracy | Intrinsic Safety | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Orifice Plate** | Clean liquid/gas flow | Wide | ±1–2% | High (no electronics) | | **Venturi Meter** | Low-loss flow measurement | Wide | ±0.5–1% | High | | **Pitot-Static Tube** | Airspeed, point velocity | Medium | ±1% | High | | **Vortex Shedding** | Steam, gas flow | Wide | ±0.5–1% | High | | **Fluidic Oscillator** | Digital flow output | Medium | ±2% | Very High | | **Jet-Deflection (Coanda)** | Proportional amplifier | Low | ±5% | Highest | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Fluidic Sensor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Variable Input
Pressure / Velocity / Level] --> B[Primary Fluidic Element
Orifice / Venturi / Pitot / Nozzle] B --> C[Fluid Response
Pressure Drop / Jet Deflection / Vortex Street] C --> D{Signal Conversion Type} D -->|Analog| E[Direct Pressure / Flow Readout
Manometer / DP Cell] D -->|Frequency| F[Pulse Counting
Frequency-to-Voltage] D -->|Displacement| G[Secondary Transducer
Capacitive / Piezoresistive / LVDT] E --> H[Output Display / Controller] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Final Measurable Signal
4-20 mA / Digital / Pneumatic 3-15 psi] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Orifice Plate Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Upstream Pipe
Diameter D, Pressure P1, Velocity v1] --> B[Orifice Plate
Diameter d, Beta Ratio] B --> C[Vena Contracta
Minimum Area, Pressure P2, Velocity v2] C --> D[Pressure Recovery Zone] D --> E[Downstream Pipe
Diameter D] B -.-> F[Differential Pressure
Transducer] F -.-> G[Flow Rate Q] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pitot-Static Tube ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Free Stream Flow
Velocity v, Static Pressure Ps] --> B[Pitot Probe Tip
Stagnation Point] B --> C[Total Pressure Pt
Measured at Front Tap] A --> D[Static Pressure Tap
On Side Wall] C --> E[Differential Manometer
or DP Transducer] D --> E E --> F[Calculated Velocity
v = sqrt 2 Pt minus Ps over rho] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Fluidic Oscillator (Vortex Shedding Type) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet Flow] --> B[Bluff Body
Cylinder or Prism] B --> C[Alternating Vortex Shedding
Karman Vortex Street] C --> D[Frequency f proportional to v] D --> E[Pickup Sensor
Piezoelectric or Thermal] E --> F[Pulse Output
f equals St times v over D] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Coanda-Effect Wall Attachment (Fluidic Amplifier) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Jet Inlet] --> B[Splitter Edge
Curved Wall] B --> C{Wall Attachment Control} C -->|No Control Flow| D[Jet Attached to Wall A
Outlet A Active] C -->|Control Flow Applied| E[Jet Switches to Wall B
Outlet B Active] D --> F[Proportional Output] E --> F ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Define a fluidic sensor and list any two advantages over conventional electronic sensors.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **fluidic sensor** is a device that uses a fluid (gas or liquid) as the working medium to detect or measure a physical variable, transducing it into a measurable fluidic output such as pressure, flow, or frequency. **Two advantages:** 1. **Intrinsic Safety:** Since no electrical components are in the sensing region, it is explosion-proof and EMI-immune — ideal for hazardous environments. 2. **High Reliability:** Absence of moving mechanical parts (in pure fluidic-logic types) gives long operational life with minimal maintenance. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark per advantage = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **State Bernoulli's equation and explain how it is used in an orifice-type flow sensor.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Bernoulli's equation** (for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow): $$P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$ **Application in orifice sensor:** An orifice plate creates a localized constriction. By continuity, velocity increases through the orifice. By Bernoulli, this velocity increase corresponds to a **measurable static pressure drop** ($\Delta P$) across the plate. The flow rate $Q$ is then computed as: $$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$ *(1 mark equation + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark formula = 3 marks)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Internal Choice Rule:** You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Provide diagrams wherever possible. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Pitot-static tube**. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow in terms of the differential pressure measured. (7 marks) **(b)** An orifice plate of diameter 75 mm is fitted in a pipe of diameter 150 mm carrying water at a flow rate of 15 L/s. If the discharge coefficient is 0.65, calculate the differential pressure developed across the orifice. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³. (7 marks) --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Construction (3 marks):** A Pitot-static tube is a coaxial double-tube probe with: - A **front opening** (stagnation port) facing the flow → measures **total pressure** $P_t$. - **Side wall openings** (static ports) perpendicular to the flow → measure **static pressure** $P_s$. - The two pressures are routed to a **differential manometer or DP transducer**. **Working Principle (2 marks):** When the probe is immersed in the flow, the fluid is brought to rest at the front opening (stagnation point). The kinetic energy converts entirely into pressure energy. The side tap registers only the static pressure of the undisturbed stream. **Derivation (2 marks):** Applying Bernoulli's equation between a free-stream point and the stagnation point: $$P_s + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = P_t$$ $$\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_t - P_s)}{\rho}}\,}$$ --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Given:** - $D = 150$ mm = $0.15$ m - $d = 75$ mm = $0.075$ m - $Q = 15$ L/s = $0.015$ m³/s - $C_d = 0.65$ - $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Compute $\beta$ and area:** $$\beta = \frac{0.075}{0.15} = 0.5$$ $$1 - \beta^4 = 0.9375$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange orifice equation for $\Delta P$:** $$Q^2 = C_d^2 A_2^2 \cdot \dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}$$ $$\Delta P = \dfrac{Q^2 \cdot \rho(1-\beta^4)}{2 C_d^2 A_2^2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$\Delta P = \dfrac{(0.015)^2 \times 1000 \times 0.9375}{2 \times (0.65)^2 \times (4.418 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$ Numerator: $$2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 0.9375 = 0.2109$$ Denominator: $$2 \times 0.4225 \times 1.952 \times 10^{-5} = 1.6495 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 4 — Final answer:** $$\Delta P = \dfrac{0.2109}{1.6495 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 12{,}785 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\,\Delta P \approx 12.79 \text{ kPa}\,}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a vortex-shedding flowmeter** with a neat sketch. Derive the relation between flow velocity and vortex shedding frequency. (7 marks) **(b)** A vortex flowmeter has a bluff body of width 25 mm. The flow velocity of water is 2 m/s. If the Strouhal number is 0.21, calculate (i) the vortex shedding frequency, and (ii) the volumetric flow rate if the pipe diameter is 100 mm. (7 marks) --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Principle (2 marks):** When a fluid flows past a non-streamlined (bluff) body, alternating vortices are shed from each side. These vortices form the **von Kármán vortex street**, and the shedding frequency is directly proportional to the flow velocity. **Construction (2 marks):** - A **bluff body** (typically a triangular or rectangular prism) is mounted across the flow. - A **piezoelectric or thermal sensor** is placed downstream to detect the pressure/velocity fluctuations caused by vortex shedding. - A **signal conditioner** converts the pulses into a flow-proportional output. **Working (2 marks):** As vortices are shed alternately, they create local pressure oscillations. The sensor detects these pulses. Counting pulses per unit time gives the shedding frequency, which is directly proportional to flow velocity. **Derivation (1 mark):** $$\boxed{\,f = \text{St} \cdot \dfrac{v}{D}\,}$$ where St is the Strouhal number and $D$ is the bluff body width. --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** - $D_{body} = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m - $v = 2$ m/s - $\text{St} = 0.21$ - $D_{pipe} = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m **(i) Vortex Shedding Frequency:** $$f = 0.21 \times \dfrac{2}{0.025} = 0.21 \times 80$$ $$\boxed{\,f = 16.8 \text{ Hz}\,}$$ **(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate:** $$A = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$Q = A \cdot v = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 2$$ $$\boxed{\,Q = 1.5708 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 15.71 \text{ L/s}\,}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > 1. **Forgetting the $(1-\beta^4)$ term:** This is the most common 2-mark loss in orifice numericals. Many students write $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ and lose 2 of 7 marks instantly. > 2. **Mixing up total and static pressure in Pitot problems:** The front tap measures $P_{total}$ (stagnation), not $P_{static}$. Confusing them gives a factor-of-2 error. > 3. **Unit conversion blunders:** KTU expects SI throughout. A $d$ in mm plugged into formula directly gives wrong answers by a factor of $10^6$. > 4. **Discharge coefficient $C_d$ ignored:** Real-world flow is always less than ideal. Always include $C_d$. > 5. **Reynolds number not checked:** Vortex shedding is only valid for $Re > 10^4$ typically. If the problem gives low-velocity data, mention the validity range. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:** - **Fluidic sensor definition:** Device using fluid properties to measure a process variable. ✔ - **Bernoulli's equation** is the master equation. Memorize all three forms (with/without elevation, with/without head loss). ✔ - **Orifice equation (ISO 5167):** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P / [\rho(1-\beta^4)]}$. Don't drop the $(1-\beta^4)$. ✔ - **$\beta$ ratio** is always the orifice-to-pipe diameter ratio. ✔ - **Pitot-static equation:** $v = \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$, where $\Delta P$ is **dynamic pressure** ($P_t - P_s$). ✔ - **Vortex shedding:** $f = \text{St} \cdot v / D_{body}$; Strouhal number $\approx 0.21$ for cylinders. ✔ - **Coanda effect:** Jet attaches to nearest wall; used in fluidic amplifiers with no moving parts. ✔ - **Typical $C_d$ value:** $0.6$ to $0.65$ for sharp-edged orifice. ✔ - **Reynolds check:** $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$ — must check laminar vs. turbulent regime. ✔ - **Intrinsic safety advantage** of fluidic sensors is the single most important KTU short-answer point. ✔ - **Applications:** Aerospace, biomedical, nuclear, hazardous petrochemical zones. ✔ - **Dimensional analysis:** Always verify final answer units (Pa for pressure, m³/s for flow, Hz for frequency). ✔ - **When in doubt, default to Bernoulli + Continuity** — they solve ~80% of fluidic sensor problems. ✔ --- **End of Module 1 Notes — Fluidic Sensors | PCAET602 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Johnson Noise Thermometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Johnson Noise Thermometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Johnson Noise Thermometer (JNT)** is a **primary thermometric instrument** that determines the absolute thermodynamic temperature of a system by precisely measuring the **mean-square thermal agitation voltage** (Johnson–Nyquist noise) generated spontaneously across a passive electrical resistor. Unlike conventional thermometers, it requires **no calibration** against a reference standard because the relationship between noise power and temperature is derived directly from fundamental principles of statistical mechanics and the **fluctuation–dissipation theorem**.

Mathematically, the foundation of the JNT rests on the **Nyquist Theorem (1928)**:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$$

where every term corresponds to a precisely defined physical quantity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The JNT is categorized under *Noise Thermometry* in **Module 1: Temperature Measurement** of the PCAET602 syllabus. Students must focus on the Nyquist relation, the signal-conditioning chain, and the error-budgeting strategy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a stadium full of restless spectators (electrons) sitting in a closed balcony (a resistor). When the stadium is cold, the spectators sit quietly. As the temperature rises, they shuffle, kick seats, and chatter randomly. You can stand outside the balcony and, without entering, listen to the *average loudness of the rustling*. The louder the collective noise, the hotter the stadium.

The Johnson Noise Thermometer "listens" to the *electrical rustling* of electrons inside a resistor. The **mean-square noise voltage** is directly proportional to absolute temperature — no physical contact with a thermocouple, no calibration drift, no dependence on material properties.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants must be memorized for KTU numerical problems:

| Symbol | Quantity | Numerical Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann Constant | $1.380\,649 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| $e$ | Elementary Charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| $R$ | Resistance | Sensor-dependent | $\Omega$ |
| $\Delta f$ | Noise Bandwidth | Set by filter | Hz |
| $T$ | Absolute Temperature | To be measured | K |

> [!NOTE]
> The Boltzmann constant $k_B$ has been **exact since 2019** (SI redefinition). For KTU numerical answers, use $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K unless higher precision is explicitly demanded.

### 1.4 Where the JNT is Used in Industry

- **Nuclear reactor core thermometry** (where contact probes cannot survive).
- **Cryogenic and ultra-low temperature measurements** (below 20 K).
- **High-temperature plasma diagnostics** in fusion research.
- **Calibration-free primary standards** at National Metrology Institutes (NMI).
- **Thermometry in strong magnetic fields** where thermoelectric effects corrupt other sensors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The Johnson Noise Thermometer exploits the **unavoidable thermal noise** of every conductor. The instrumentation chain is shown logically below:

- **Step 1 — Sensing:** A precision sense resistor $R_s$ is placed in thermal equilibrium with the body whose temperature $T$ is to be measured. The electrons inside undergo random thermal motion governed by Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics.
- **Step 2 — Amplification:** The nanovolt-level noise is boosted by an **ultra-low-noise differential preamplifier**. Amplifier self-noise must be far smaller than $v_n$ — typically, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) $> 40$ dB is targeted.
- **Step 3 — Bandwidth Selection:** A **bandpass filter** defines the measurement bandwidth $\Delta f$. Wider $\Delta f$ improves the signal but allows more amplifier noise; the trade-off is engineered carefully.
- **Step 4 — Squaring & Averaging:** A **true-RMS squarer** followed by a long-time **integrator** yields $\overline{v_n^2}$. Integration time is often minutes because noise is stochastic.
- **Step 5 — Temperature Computation:** $T$ is computed in real time from the rearranged Nyquist equation:
- **Step 6 — Display & Output:** The result is presented in Kelvin on a digital readout or transmitted via 4–20 mA / HART protocol.

### 2.2 Why the JNT is "Primary" (Calibration-Free)

The Nyquist formula contains only **fundamental constants** ($k_B$) and **traceable quantities** ($R$ and $\Delta f$, both of which are referenced to quantum standards). No empirical material property or comparison reference is required, which is the defining feature of a primary thermometer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $R$ is not a calibration problem:** $R$ is not the measurand; it is an *input* parameter that is independently traceable to the quantum Hall effect. Hence any uncertainty in $R$ is quantified, not absorbed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Use Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$ | **Master Nyquist relation** | Valid for $h f \ll k_B T$ (classical regime) |
| 2 | $\overline{i_n^2} = 4 k_B T \Delta f / R$ | Noise current version | Useful for current-mode detection |
| 3 | $P_n = k_B T \Delta f$ | Available noise power | Per matched source, $50\ \Omega$ standard |
| 4 | $v_n \text{(rms)} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | RMS noise voltage | Square-root of mean-square |
| 5 | $NEP = v_n / \sqrt{\Delta f}$ | Noise-Equivalent Power | For detector characterization |
| 6 | $T = \overline{v_n^2} / (4 k_B R \Delta f)$ | **Inverted form** for thermometry | Direct computation of $T$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_T / T = \sqrt{(\sigma_{v^2}/v^2)^2 + (\sigma_R/R)^2 + (\sigma_{\Delta f}/\Delta f)^2}$ | Combined relative uncertainty | Error-propagation law |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU numerical problems, the value of $\sqrt{4 k_B R \Delta f}$ is often given in microvolts to test your dimensional accuracy. Always verify units: $k_B$ in J/K = V²·s/(Ω·K), $R$ in Ω, $\Delta f$ in Hz — their product yields V².

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor thermometry** — non-invasive and radiation-tolerant (uses only a passive resistor).
- **Cryogenics** — accurate down to $\sim 4$ K, where thermocouple Seebeck coefficients vanish.
- **Standards laboratories** — NIST, NPL, and PTB use JNT to realize the kelvin.
- **Aerospace engine probes** — high-temperature ($> 1500$ K) capability without oxidation degradation issues.
- **Magnetic-field environments** (MRI, fusion magnets) — immune to magnetoresistance in non-magnetic sense resistors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist Noise Formula (Equipartition Route)

**Step 1 — Circuit model.** Consider a series combination of a noise-free resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$. The random thermal EMF $v_n(t)$ drives current through the loop.

**Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor.** The instantaneous electrical energy stored is:

$$E(t) = \frac{1}{2} C \, v_C^2(t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Equipartition Theorem.** For any quadratic degree of freedom in thermal equilibrium, the mean energy equals $\tfrac{1}{2} k_B T$. Hence:

$$\langle E \rangle = \frac{1}{2} C \langle v_C^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{2} k_B T$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the mean-square capacitor voltage.**

$$\langle v_C^2 \rangle = \frac{k_B T}{C}$$

**Step 5 — Transfer function of the RC network.** The voltage across $C$ in response to a noise source $v_n$ is governed by:

$$\left\vert H(\omega) \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega R C)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Spectral density of the noise voltage.** The mean-square capacitor voltage is the integral of the source noise spectral density $S_v(\omega)$ weighted by $\vert H(\omega) \vert^2$:
