**Given:** $P_{\max} = 4 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$, $x_{\max} = 0.010\,\text{m}$, $A_e = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$, $E = 1.93 \times 10^{11}\,\text{N/m}^2$, $R = 0.025\,\text{m}$, $b = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $h = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$.

**(i) Required Spring Rate $k$ (2 marks):**
From $x = P A_e / k$:

$$k = \frac{P_{\max} \cdot A_e}{x_{\max}} = \frac{(4\times 10^{5})(1.5\times 10^{-3})}{0.010} = \frac{600}{0.010}$$

$$\boxed{k = 60{,}000\,\text{N/m} = 60\,\text{N/mm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Axial Thrust $F_{\max}$ (2 marks):**

$$F_{\max} = P_{\max} \cdot A_e = (4\times 10^{5})(1.5\times 10^{-3}) = 600\,\text{N}$$

**(iii) Required Number of Convolutions $n$ (3 marks):**
From $k = \dfrac{E b h^3 n}{2 R^2}$, solving for $n$:

$$n = \frac{2 k R^2}{E b h^3}$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{2 \cdot (6\times 10^{4}) \cdot (0.025)^2}{(1.93\times 10^{11})(4\times 10^{-3})(2\times 10^{-4})^3} \\
&= \frac{(1.2\times 10^{5})(6.25\times 10^{-4})}{(1.93\times 10^{11})(4\times 10^{-3})(8\times 10^{-12})} \\
&= \frac{75.0}{(1.93\times 10^{11})(3.2\times 10^{-14})} \\
&= \frac{75.0}{6.176 \times 10^{-3}} \\
n &\approx 12{,}144
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This absurdly large $n$ value reveals that the *combination of dimensions* (large $R$, thin $h$, soft material) cannot achieve the *desired stiffness* of $k = 60\,\text{N/mm}$. The design must be revisited — typically by:
> 1. **Increasing $h$** (wall thickness), since $n \propto 1/h^3$ (cubic sensitivity), or
> 2. **Reducing $R$** (mean radius), since $n \propto R^2$.
>
> For exam credit, the *methodology* and the *formula inversion* are what earn full marks. State clearly that the design needs revision.

**Optimization Check (bonus credit, 1 mark):** To bring $n$ into a practical range ($n \leq 20$), we need $h$ such that:
$$h^3 \geq \frac{2 k R^2}{20 \cdot E b} = \frac{2(6\times 10^4)(6.25\times 10^{-4})}{20 (1.93\times 10^{11})(4\times 10^{-3})}$$
$$h^3 \geq \frac{75}{1.544 \times 10^{10}} \geq 4.86 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^3 \Rightarrow h \geq 1.69 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \approx 1.7\,\text{mm}$$

So a wall thickness of at least **1.7 mm** would be needed for a practical design — but this conflicts with the stated 0.20 mm, hence the impractical $n$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Critical Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Dimensional Inconsistency:** Always express $E$ in Pa (N/m²) and all lengths in **metres** when using the SI form of the spring-rate formula. Mixing mm and m is the **#1 source of wrong numerical answers** in KTU numericals.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting $n$ in the formula:** The spring rate formula $k = E b h^3 n / (2 R^2)$ already includes the total number of convolutions $n$. Do **not** multiply by $n$ twice.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Confusing Effective Area with Cross-Sectional Area:** $A_e$ is **not** $\pi R^2$. It is **smaller** than the trough area and is given by the $(R_o^2 + R_o R_i + R_i^2)/3$ formula. Many students lose 2 marks here.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Skipping the Diagram:** For any 7-mark sub-question that contains the word "explain" or "describe," the KTU marking scheme explicitly awards **at least 1 mark for a labeled diagram**. Draw one — even a simple one.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Wrong Units in Final Answer:** A deflection computed in metres but reported in mm (or vice versa) loses the "correct units" mark. Always box the final numerical answer **with units**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **bellows** is a thin-walled, one-piece metallic element with multiple convolutions that converts pressure into axial displacement.
- [x] It is the **softest** of the three primary mechanical pressure sensors (Bellows < Bourdon < Diaphragm in stiffness).
- [x] **Spring rate formula:** $k = \dfrac{E \, b \, h^3 \, n}{2 R^2}$ — depends *cubically* on wall thickness and *inversely quadratically* on mean radius.
- [x] **Effective area formula:** $A_e = \dfrac{\pi}{3}\left(R_o^2 + R_o R_i + R_i^2\right)$ — acts as the *equivalent flat-piston area*.
- [x] **Force balance:** $P \cdot A_e = k \cdot x$, giving $x = P A_e / k$.
- [x] **Sensitivity** $S = A_e / k$; higher $A_e$ and lower $k$ both increase sensitivity.
- [x] **Materials:** Phosphor bronze, beryllium copper, SS-304, Monel, Inconel; PTFE coating for corrosive media.
- [x] **Types:** Seamless (hydroformed), Welded (disk), Formed; Convolute profile most common.
- [x] **Key applications:** Pressure gauges, DP transmitters, force-balance loops, aneroid barometers, pneumatic relays, expansion joints, protective boots.
- [x] **Advantages:** Large deflection, high sensitivity, low spring rate, naturally suited to DP measurement.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Limited pressure range, susceptibility to fatigue, temperature sensitivity, higher cost than Bourdon.
- [x] **Two opposed bellows** on a balance beam = the **classic force-balance DP transmitter** (Foxboro design).
- [x] A **capsule** (used in barometers/altimeters) = **two bellows joined back-to-back** for double the deflection.
- [x] In **displacement measurement**, the bellows is sealed on one end and attached to the moving object on the other; the resulting internal pressure or restoring force is measured.
- [x] **Natural frequency** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m_t}$ — must be >> 10× the process signal bandwidth for distortionless measurement.
- [x] Always **box** your final numerical answer with correct units — this is a separate marks-earning item in KTU valuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** For KTU PCAET602, the **bellows** is most often tested as a *sub-part* of a larger question on "pressure measurement" or "transmitters." Memorize the **three formulas** ($F = P A_e$, $k$ formula, $A_e$ formula) and the **force-balance architecture** — these four items alone will let you answer ~80% of all bellows-related exam questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bourdon Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bourdon Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bourdon Tube** is a mechanical elastic-pressure-sensing element used in analog pressure gauges for the measurement of gauge, vacuum, and differential pressures. It consists of a flattened, curved (generally C-shaped, spiral, or helical) metallic tube of non-circular cross-section (typically oval, elliptical, or flat-oval), closed at one end and open to the process pressure at the other. When pressure is applied to the inside of the tube, the cross-section tends to become more circular, causing the curved tube to straighten partially. This straightening motion is converted into a proportional rotational displacement of a pointer via a mechanical linkage (typically a sector-and-pinion or a cam arrangement), providing a visual indication of pressure on a calibrated scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAET602 – Module 2):** The Bourdon Tube is the foundational primary sensing element for **high-range pressure measurement** (typically above 1 kg/cm²). KTU expects students to master its working principle, derivation of deflection, and the geometric/mathematical relationship between applied pressure and angular tip displacement.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine a **flattened party balloon** shaped like a banana and sealed at one end. If you try to blow air into the open end, what happens? The banana-shaped balloon tries to straighten out and become more circular in cross-section. The higher the pressure you blow, the more it straightens.

That is **exactly how a Bourdon Tube works**:

- The tube is made of metal, curved in a C-shape, and has a **flattened (non-circular) cross-section**.
- When pressurized fluid enters the open end, the pressure pushes the flat walls apart, trying to make the cross-section circular.
- Because the tube is curved, this cross-sectional change forces the curved tube to **uncoil slightly** (straighten).
- The straightening motion at the sealed (free) tip is tiny — only fractions of a millimeter — but a clever **gear linkage amplifies it** to move a needle across a dial.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Examples:**
> - **Steam boiler pressure gauges** in thermal power plants
> - **Oxygen cylinders** in hospitals
> - **Hydraulic press gauges** in manufacturing units
> - **Compressor discharge gauges** in refrigeration plants
> - **Diesel engine lubrication oil pressure** indicators

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Range:** **1 kg/cm² to 7000 kg/cm²** (≈ 0.1 MPa to 700 MPa)
- **Typical Materials:** **Phosphor Bronze, Beryllium Copper, Stainless Steel (SS 316), Monel, Inconel**
- **Standard Accuracy Class (KTU Reference):** **±0.5% to ±2% of Full-Scale Deflection (FSD)**
- **Operating Temperature Limit:** **Up to 600 °C** (with proper material selection)
- **Hysteresis:** Typically **±0.25% to ±0.5% of FSD**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Theory (The "Why" and "How")

The Bourdon Tube operates on the principle of **elastic deformation combined with non-circular cross-section instability**. Let us break this down layer by layer:

**Step 1 — Geometric Inequilibrium (Why the tube is curved):**
A tube with a perfectly circular cross-section does not deflect when pressurized internally because stresses are symmetric. A flattened (oval/elliptical) cross-section breaks this symmetry, creating an internal moment that drives curvature change.

**Step 2 — Pressure-Induced Strain (The Trigger):**
When pressure $P$ acts on the inner walls of the curved tube, the outer fiber of the bend (longer radius) experiences **tensile stress**, while the inner fiber (shorter radius) experiences **compressive stress**. The net effect is a bending moment.

**Step 3 — Cross-Section Circularization (The Deformation):**
The internal pressure forces the major axis ($a$) of the oval cross-section to shrink and the minor axis ($b)$ to grow, driving the cross-section towards circularity ($a \to b$).

**Step 4 — Curvature Change (The Output):**
Because the cross-section shape is changing, the centroidal radius of curvature $R$ must change. The tube straightens — the radius $R$ increases, producing angular tip deflection $\theta$.

**Step 5 — Mechanical Linkage Amplification:**
The free tip displacement $x$ (linear) is converted to angular pointer motion $\phi$ via a **sector-and-pinion gear** with mechanical advantage $N$. The pointer sweep is therefore:
$$\phi = N \cdot \theta$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta\theta = P \cdot \dfrac{1 - \nu^{2}}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R^{2}}{bh} \cdot \dfrac{(\beta - 1)}{\beta + \dfrac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}}$ | Angular deflection of C-type Bourdon (master equation) | $P$=Pressure (Pa), $E$=Young's Modulus (Pa), $\nu$=Poisson's Ratio, $a,b$=semi-axes of oval (m), $h$=wall thickness (m), $R$=curvature radius (m), $\beta = a/b$ |
| 2 | $\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h} \cdot C_{c}$ | Simplified linearized form (commonly used in KTU derivations) | $C_{c}$ = shape constant (typically 0.3 to 0.5 for C-type) |
| 3 | $x = R \cdot \Delta\theta$ | Linear tip displacement of the free end | $x$ in metres |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot h \cdot \Delta\theta}{C_{c} \cdot R^{2}}$ | Pressure expressed as a function of measured deflection | Useful for gauge design problems |
| 5 | $\phi = N \cdot \Delta\theta$ | Pointer angular sweep on the dial | $N$ = gear train ratio (typically 50 to 200) |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{h}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress in the tube wall | $\varepsilon$ in Pa; used for fatigue and safety analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In exam problems, the *master equation* (Row 1) is rarely required in full form. KTU typically tests the **simplified proportionality** $\Delta\theta \propto P R^{2} / (E \cdot b \cdot h)$ and the **linearized form** (Row 2). Memorize the proportionalities — they are worth 2-3 marks in derivations.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bourdon Tubes are the **backbone of industrial pressure measurement** in:

- **Oil & Gas:** Wellhead pressure monitoring, pipeline pressure recording
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum pressure, feedwater pump discharge
- **Chemical Plants:** Reactor pressure, distillation column overhead
- **HVAC:** Refrigerant line pressure gauges
- **Process Safety:** Pressure Safety Valves (PSV) upstream instrumentation
- **Calibration Laboratories:** Dead-weight testers often use Bourdon tubes as reference comparators

They remain dominant in **harsh environments** where electronic transducers fail (high temperature, RFI/EMI, explosive atmospheres) because they are **purely mechanical, intrinsically safe, and explosion-proof by construction**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bourdon Tube Angular Deflection (Master Equation)

We will derive the relationship between applied internal pressure $P$ and the resulting angular deflection $\Delta\theta$ at the free end of a C-type Bourdon tube.

**Assumptions (Standard KTU Convention):**
1. The tube material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law.
2. The tube has a constant oval cross-section throughout its length.
3. Plane cross-sections remain plane after deformation (Euler-Bernoulli beam assumption).
4. The tube wall is thin: $h \ll a, b$.
5. End effects at the sealed tip and the pressure inlet are neglected.

**Step 1 — Stress in the Tube Wall (Thin-Wall Pressure Vessel Theory):**

For a thin-walled closed cross-section, internal pressure $P$ produces a circumferential (hoop) stress:
$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{h}$$

where $r$ is the local radius of the cross-section. For an elliptical cross-section with semi-major axis $a$ and semi-minor axis $b$, the stress is non-uniform. At the tip of the major axis (outer fiber), the radius of curvature is small, so the stress is maximum. At the tip of the minor axis, the stress is minimum.

The **average hoop stress** in the elliptical cross-section is:
$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{P \cdot a \cdot b}{h \cdot (a + b)} \cdot 2$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Inequilibrium (Why the Tube Uncurls):**

Because the outer fiber (major axis) is under higher tensile stress than the inner fiber (minor axis), there is a net **circumferential bending moment** per unit length:
$$M = \int_{A} \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

Evaluating this integral for the elliptical cross-section (a lengthy calculation, hence the simplified form in KTU exams):
$$M = P \cdot a \cdot b \cdot \frac{a - b}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Curvature Change via Bending Equation:**

For an initially curved beam, the change in curvature is related to the bending moment by:
$$\Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}$$

For a thin-walled elliptical tube, the **area moment of inertia** about the neutral axis is:
$$I = \frac{\pi \cdot a^{3} \cdot b \cdot h}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Combining All Terms:**

Substituting $M$ and $I$ into the bending equation:
$$\Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{P \cdot a \cdot b \cdot \frac{a - b}{2}}{E \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot a^{3} \cdot b \cdot h}{4}}$$

$$\Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{2P \cdot (a - b)}{E \cdot \pi \cdot a^{2} \cdot h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Curvature Change to Angular Deflection:**

The change in curvature over the tube arc length $L$ produces the angular deflection:
$$\Delta\theta = L \cdot \Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{2 P L (a - b)}{E \pi a^{2} h}$$

Since the tube subtends a central angle $\alpha$ at the centre, $L = R \alpha$. Substituting and including Poisson's ratio correction (the cross-section does not deform purely elastically — radial contraction occurs):
$$\Delta\theta = \frac{P R \alpha}{E} \cdot \frac{(1 - \nu^{2}) (a - b)}{\pi a^{2} h}$$

**Step 6 — Final Master Equation (Cast in β-form):**

Defining the **eccentricity ratio** $\beta = a / b$:
$$\boxed{\;\Delta\theta = P \cdot \frac{(1 - \nu^{2})}{E} \cdot \frac{R^{2} \alpha}{b h} \cdot \frac{(\beta - 1)}{\beta(\beta + 1) \cdot 2\pi}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Simplified Version (Use this in exams):** When the shape constant, Poisson effect, and proportionality are combined into a single constant $C_c$, the exam-friendly form is:
> $$\Delta\theta \approx C_c \cdot \frac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h}$$
> This proportionality is **the only thing you need to write** for a 7-mark derivation question on "Derive the Bourdon tube deflection equation."

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bourdon Tube Types

| Parameter | **C-Type** | **Spiral** | **Helical** | **Twisted** |
|-----------|------------|------------|-------------|-------------|
| Geometric Form | Single C-arc, ~250° | Multiple turns in one plane | Helical coil (3D) | Helical with twisted cross-section |
| Deflection Magnitude | Low (2-5°) | Medium (30-50°) | High (50-70°) | Highest (up to 90°+) |
| Mechanical Amplification Required | High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| Pressure Range | High (up to 700 MPa) | Medium-High | Medium | Medium-Low |
| Manufacturing Cost | Lowest | Moderate | High | Highest |
| Common Application | Industrial gauges, test rigs | Recording manometers | Differential pressure recorders | Precision instruments, altimeters |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★★★ (Most asked) | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ |

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem Solved)

> **Problem:** A C-type Bourdon tube has a mean radius $R = 30$ mm, semi-minor axis $b = 4$ mm, wall thickness $h = 0.5$ mm, and is made of phosphor bronze with $E = 110$ GPa and $\nu = 0.33$. If the applied pressure is $P = 10$ MPa, calculate the angular deflection $\Delta\theta$. Use $C_c = 0.4$ and $\alpha = 270° = 4.712$ rad.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Plug values into the simplified equation:
$$\Delta\theta = C_c \cdot \frac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h}$$

Step 2: Numerator:
$$C_c \cdot P \cdot R^{2} = 0.4 \times (10 \times 10^{6}) \times (0.030)^{2}$$
$$= 0.4 \times 10^{7} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 3600 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 3: Denominator:
$$E \cdot b \cdot h = (110 \times 10^{9}) \times 0.004 \times 0.0005 = 220{,}000 \text{ N}$$

Step 4: Result:
$$\Delta\theta = \frac{3600}{220{,}000} = 0.01636 \text{ rad}$$

Step 5: Convert to degrees:
$$\Delta\theta = 0.01636 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.94°$$

**Step 6: Linear tip displacement:**
$$x = R \cdot \Delta\theta = 0.030 \times 0.01636 \approx 0.491 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Pointer sweep (assuming gear ratio $N = 100$):**
$$\phi = N \cdot \Delta\theta = 100 \times 0.94° = 94°$$

> This is a typical dial sweep — the gear linkage amplifies the sub-1° tube motion into a readable pointer movement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge

The following block-level schematic maps the complete signal and mechanical path of a Bourdon tube gauge, from pressure input to pointer output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PROCESS PRESSURE INPUT] --> N1[PRESSURE INLET PORT]
    N1 --> N2[FLAT OVAL CROSS SECTION TUBE C SHAPE]
    N2 --> N3[ELASTIC DEFORMATION STAGE]
    N3 --> N4[ANGULAR TIP DEFLECTION DELTA THETA]
    N4 --> N5[LINKAGE LEVER ARM]
    N5 --> N6[SECTOR GEAR]
    N6 --> N7[PINION GEAR]
    N7 --> N8[POINTER SHAFT ROTATION PHI]
    N8 --> N9[POINTER NEEDLE]
    N9 --> N10[CALIBRATED DIAL SCALE]
    N10 --> N11[PRESSURE READING INDICATION]
    N2 --> N12[MECHANICAL DAMPENER OPTIONAL]
    N12 --> N4
```

### 4.2 Detailed Cross-Sectional Geometry Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLATTENED METAL STRIP] --> B[FOLD TO OVAL CROSS SECTION]
    B --> C[BEND OVAL TUBE TO C SHAPE]
    C --> D[SEAL FREE END WITH PLUG]
    D --> E[ATTACH PRESSURE INLET AT FIXED END]
    E --> F[BRAZE OR WELD LINKAGE LEVER]
    F --> G[COUPLE TO SECTOR PINION ASSEMBLY]
    G --> H[MOUNT POINTER ON PINION SHAFT]
    H --> I[CALIBRATE AGAINST DEAD WEIGHT TESTER]
    I --> J[FINAL BOURDON GAUGE READY]
```

### 4.3 Signal Path & Deflection Amplification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRIMARY_ELEMENT
        S1[PRESSURE P] --> S2[BENDING MOMENT M]
    end
    subgraph TRANSDUCER_STAGE
        S2 --> S3[ANGULAR DEFLECTION DELTA THETA]
    end
    subgraph LINKAGE_STAGE
        S3 --> S4[LINEAR TIP MOTION X]
        S4 --> S5[GEAR AMPLIFICATION FACTOR N]
    end
    subgraph INDICATION_STAGE
        S5 --> S6[POINTER ROTATION PHI]
        S6 --> S7[DIAL SCALE CALIBRATED IN PRESSURE UNITS]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block represents a physical component in the gauge. The arrows represent **causality** — pressure causes bending, bending causes deflection, deflection is amplified, and the amplified motion drives the pointer. The amplification ratio $N$ (sector-to-pinion teeth ratio) is the **single most important design parameter** for gauge sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Bourdon tube. List any two materials used for its construction.*

**Model Answer:**
A Bourdon tube is a curved, flattened metallic tube with a non-circular cross-section (usually oval) that converts internal fluid pressure into a proportional angular displacement at its free end, which is then mechanically amplified to drive a pointer on a calibrated dial.

**Two materials:**
1. **Phosphor Bronze** — used for low-to-medium pressure (up to 50 MPa), excellent fatigue resistance.
2. **Beryllium Copper** — used where higher elasticity and corrosion resistance are required.
*(Other accepted: SS 316, Monel, Inconel, Ni-Span C)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State any three advantages and one disadvantage of C-type Bourdon tubes.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Simple construction, low manufacturing cost, easy to replace.
2. Suitable for very high pressures (up to 700 MPa).
3. Intrinsically safe — no electrical power required; suitable for hazardous areas.

**Disadvantage:**
1. Produces only small tip displacement (2° to 5°), requiring a complex amplification linkage, which introduces hysteresis and friction errors.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a C-type Bourdon tube pressure gauge. Discuss how the angular deflection at the free end is converted to a pointer displacement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A C-type Bourdon tube has the following specifications:
- Curvature radius $R = 25$ mm
- Semi-minor axis $b = 3$ mm
- Wall thickness $h = 0.4$ mm
- Tube material: Phosphor bronze ($E = 110$ GPa)
- Applied pressure $P = 8$ MPa
- Shape constant $C_c = 0.4$

Calculate: (i) Angular deflection $\Delta\theta$ in degrees, and (ii) Linear tip displacement $x$ in mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The C-type Bourdon tube is a curved metallic tube with an oval/elliptical cross-section, fixed at one end (pressure inlet) and sealed at the other (free end). When fluid pressure enters the inlet, it acts on the inner walls. Because of the **non-circular cross-section**, the pressure produces an **unequal circumferential stress distribution** — the outer fiber of the bend is in tension, the inner fiber in compression. This generates a **bending moment** that causes the cross-section to become more circular. Since the cross-section shape is changing, the **radius of curvature $R$ must also change** — the curved tube straightens slightly.

The free end deflects angularly by $\Delta\theta$. This motion is transmitted via a **linkage lever arm** to a **sector gear**, which meshes with a **pinion gear** mounted on the pointer shaft. The gear train provides mechanical amplification by a factor $N$ (typically 50-200), so the pointer sweeps through a much larger angle $\phi = N \cdot \Delta\theta$ across the calibrated dial. Friction is minimized by jewel bearings, and a hairspring provides restoring torque to eliminate backlash.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Angular deflection:**

Using the simplified equation:
$$\Delta\theta = C_c \cdot \frac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h}$$

Substituting values:
$$\Delta\theta = 0.4 \cdot \frac{(8 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (0.025)^{2}}{(110 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (0.003) \cdot (0.0004)}$$

Numerator:
$$0.4 \times 8 \times 10^{6} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = 2000 \text{ N·m}$$

Denominator:
$$110 \times 10^{9} \times 0.003 \times 0.0004 = 1.32 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{2000}{1.32 \times 10^{5}} = 0.01515 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:
$$\Delta\theta = 0.01515 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.868°$$

**(ii) Linear tip displacement:**

$$x = R \cdot \Delta\theta = 0.025 \times 0.01515 = 3.79 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.379 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently make the following errors and lose 2-3 marks:
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** $E$ is in GPa; you MUST convert to Pa ($E = 110 \times 10^9$ Pa) before substituting. Mixing GPa and m units will give an answer off by 9 orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Radius vs. diameter confusion:** In the equation, $R$ is the **centroidal radius of curvature**, NOT the tube diameter.
> 3. **Forgetting the shape constant $C_c$:** Without it, the simple proportionality gives a raw theoretical value that over-estimates deflection by 2-3×.
> 4. **Radian-degree conversion:** Final answers for pointer/angle problems should be in **degrees** unless the question specifies radians.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare the C-type, spiral-type, and helical-type Bourdon tubes based on (i) deflection magnitude, (ii) pressure range, (iii) amplification requirement, and (iv) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the simplified expression for the angular deflection of a C-type Bourdon tube, clearly stating all assumptions. Show that $\Delta\theta \propto \dfrac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Parameter | C-Type | Spiral-Type | Helical-Type |
|-----------|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Deflection Magnitude | 2-5° (smallest) | 30-50° (medium) | 50-70° (large) |
| Pressure Range | High (up to 700 MPa) | Medium (up to 100 MPa) | Medium-Low (up to 30 MPa) |
| Amplification Required | High (gear ratio 100-200) | Low (gear ratio 10-20) | Very Low (direct drive possible) |
| Typical Application | Industrial pressure gauges, test rigs | Recording manometers, process recorders | Differential pressure recorders, altimeters |

**C-type** is the simplest and cheapest but requires the heaviest amplification linkage. **Spiral** wraps multiple turns in a single plane, multiplying deflection linearly with the number of turns. **Helical** extends this to 3D, providing very high deflection and excellent damping due to the longer fluid path. The trade-off is increased manufacturing complexity and reduced pressure range due to longer unsupported lengths.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Thin-walled tube: $h \ll a, b$.
2. Homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material.
3. Plane cross-sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli beam).
4. Uniform cross-section along tube length.
5. End effects neglected.

**Derivation Outline:**

Internal pressure $P$ produces a non-uniform hoop stress in the elliptical cross-section:
$$\sigma_{hoop}(\theta) = \frac{P \cdot r(\theta)}{h}$$

The stress is higher on the major axis ($a$) than on the minor axis ($b$). This creates a net bending moment per unit length:
$$M = P \cdot a \cdot b \cdot \frac{a - b}{2}$$

By the curved-beam bending equation:
$$\Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}$$

With the moment of inertia for a thin elliptical tube:
$$I = \frac{\pi \cdot a^{3} \cdot b \cdot h}{4}$$

Combining and including Poisson's correction:
$$\Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{2P(a - b)(1 - \nu^{2})}{E \pi a^{2} h}$$

Angular deflection over arc length $L = R \alpha$:
$$\Delta\theta = L \cdot \Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{2 P R \alpha (a-b)(1-\nu^{2})}{E \pi a^{2} h}$$

Factoring out constants into a single shape constant $C_c$:
$$\Delta\theta = C_c \cdot \frac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h}$$

Hence, $\Delta\theta \propto \dfrac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bourdon Tube (Module 2, PCAET602)**

- **Principle:** Non-circular (oval) cross-section + internal pressure → cross-section circularizes → curved tube straightens → free-end angular deflection.
- **Material Choices:** Phosphor bronze (low P), Beryllium copper (medium P, corrosion-resistant), SS 316 / Inconel (high T, high P).
- **Master Proportionality:** $\Delta\theta \propto \dfrac{P \cdot R^{2}}{E \cdot b \cdot h}$ — **memorize this exact form**.
- **Linear tip motion:** $x = R \cdot \Delta\theta$.
- **Pointer sweep:** $\phi = N \cdot \Delta\theta$, where $N$ = sector-to-pinion gear ratio.
- **Standard Range:** 1 to 7000 kg/cm²; **avoid using Bourdon for vacuum below 1 kg/cm²** — use diaphragm or capsules instead.
- **Bourdon Tube Types (by deflection):** C-type (lowest) < Spiral < Helical < Twisted (highest).
- **Linkage:** Sector-pinion is the most common amplification method; modern designs use **canted springs** to reduce hysteresis.
- **Overpressure Protection:** A **blow-out plug** at the case back is mandatory for safety — it vents if the tube ruptures.
- **Common Errors:** Forgetting unit conversion of $E$; confusing centroidal radius with tube cross-section dimension; omitting the shape constant; mixing up which axis is major vs. minor.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Bourdon tubes are **intrinsically safe**, **explosion-proof**, and remain the **gold standard** for high-pressure indication in hazardous process industries.
- **Comparison Heuristic:** If the exam asks for a primary element for **high pressure > 1 MPa** — answer **Bourdon Tube**. For **low pressure < 0.1 MPa** — answer **Diaphragm/Capsule**. For **differential pressure** — answer **Capsule or Diaphragm pair**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diaphragm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Diaphragm ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diaphragm (KTU 2024 Definition):** A diaphragm is a thin, flexible, circular metallic or non-metallic plate (usually corrugated or flat) that is firmly clamped around its circumference and is used as a **primary sensing element** for the measurement of pressure, force, or differential pressure. It converts the applied pressure into a measurable mechanical displacement (deflection) at its center. In industrial instrumentation, diaphragms belong to the class of **elastic pressure sensors** — devices that translate a pressure signal into a small linear or angular displacement proportional to the magnitude of the applied pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** Diaphragms are studied as one of the primary elastic elements (along with bourdon tubes and bellows) used for pressure transduction. The focus is on (a) constructional features, (b) material selection, (c) deflection analysis using thin-plate theory, and (d) design trade-offs between sensitivity, range, and linearity. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of a Drummer's Drumhead:** When you push your thumb into the center of a stretched drum skin clamped at the edges, it depresses inward. The harder you push (pressure), the deeper it goes (deflection). A **diaphragm works on the exact same principle** — except instead of your thumb, a process fluid pushes against it, and instead of sound, the deflection is read by a strain gauge, LVDT, or capacitive plate. **Key Boundary Condition (Intuitive):** The edge of the diaphragm is **clamped** (like the drumhead's rim is fixed to the drum body). Only the **center is free to deflect**. This is what makes the deflection predictable and mathematically describable. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > **Material Properties Often Encountered in Diaphragm Calculations:** > - **Young's Modulus ($E$):** $\mathbf{2 \times 10^{11}\ \text{N/m}^{2}}$ for steel, $\mathbf{1.07 \times 10^{11}\ \text{N/m}^{2}}$ for phosphor bronze > - **Poisson's Ratio ($\mu$):** $\mathbf{0.3}$ for most engineering metals > - **Density ($\rho$):** $\mathbf{7850\ \text{kg/m}^{3}}$ for steel > - **Standard Diaphragm Diameter Range:** $\mathbf{10\ \text{mm}\ \text{to}\ 150\ \text{mm}}$ > - **Typical Thickness:** $\mathbf{0.05\ \text{mm}\ \text{to}\ 2\ \text{mm}}$ --- ### 1.4 Classification of Diaphragms (KTU Taxonomy) | Type | Description | Typical Application | |------|-------------|---------------------| | **Flat Diaphragm** | Smooth, plain circular plate | Low-pressure, clean fluids, gauges | | **Corrugated Diaphragm** | Convoluted (rippled) surface | Higher pressure, larger linear range | | **Convex Diaphragm** | Pre-bent in one direction | Snap-action applications | | **Slotted Diaphragm** | Cut slots to reduce stiffness | Very low pressure (capsule) | | **Capsule** | Two diaphragms welded back-to-back | Differential pressure / absolute pressure | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic > The diaphragm operates on the principle of **elastic deformation of a thin circular plate clamped at its periphery.** 1. **Pressure Application:** The process pressure ($P$) acts uniformly on one side of the diaphragm surface of effective area ($A = \pi a^{2}$), where $a$ is the effective radius. 2. **Force Generation:** The total force on the diaphragm is $W = P \cdot A = P \cdot \pi a^{2}$. 3. **Bending of Plate:** Because the edge is rigidly clamped, the plate bends inward. This bending produces: - A **transverse deflection** $y(r)$ at any radial distance $r$ from the center. - **Bending stresses** (radial stress $\sigma_{r}$ and tangential/hoop stress $\sigma_{t}$) within the plate material. 4. **Deflection at Center is Maximum:** The point at the geometric center ($r=0$) deflects the most. This point is mechanically coupled to a **secondary transducer** (LVDT core, strain gauge, or one plate of a capacitor). 5. **Output Conversion:** The mechanical deflection is converted to an electrical signal by the secondary transducer. --- ### 2.2 Why Use a Diaphragm? | Advantage | Engineering Reasoning | |-----------|----------------------| | Simplicity | Only one moving part — the plate itself | | Small size | Can be fabricated down to a few mm diameter | | Good sensitivity | Center deflection is large for small pressures | | Easy to integrate | Direct mounting for strain gauges / capacitive plates | | Low hysteresis | Elastic limit is not exceeded in normal operation | | Works in clean fluids | No cavities or crevices to trap media (when used with sanitary seals) | **Limitations:** - Small deflection range → secondary transducer must be highly sensitive. - Temperature sensitivity due to material expansion. - Susceptible to fatigue under cyclic loading. - Non-linearity at large deflections ($\approx y/t > 0.5$). --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all derivable formulas** commonly tested in KTU examinations for diaphragms. Memorize the structure, not just the result. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|----------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Total Force on Diaphragm | $W = P \cdot \pi a^{2}$ | $P$ = pressure (Pa), $a$ = effective radius (m), $W$ = N | | 2 | Deflection Profile (flat plate) | $y(r) = \dfrac{3W(1-\mu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}} (a^{2} - r^{2})^{2}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $t$ = thickness (m), $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio, $r$ = radial position (m) | | 3 | Maximum Deflection (at $r=0$) | $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3 W a^{3} (1-\mu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}}$ | meters | | 4 | Maximum Deflection (in terms of $P$) | $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{4} (1-\mu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}}$ | meters | | 5 | Maximum Radial Stress (at $r = a$, $\theta = 0$) | $\sigma_{r,\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{2}}{4 t^{2}}$ | Pa | | 6 | Maximum Tangential Stress (at $r = 0$) | $\sigma_{t,\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{2}}{8 t^{2}}(1+\mu)$ | Pa | | 7 | Natural Frequency (clamped plate) | $f_{n} = \dfrac{10.21}{2\pi a^{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{E t^{2}}{12 \rho (1-\mu^{2})}}$ | Hz | | 8 | Sensitivity (S) | $S = \dfrac{y_{\max}}{P} = \dfrac{3 a^{4}(1-\mu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}}$ | m/Pa | | 9 | Pressure-to-Deflection Ratio | $P = \dfrac{16 E t^{3} y_{\max}}{3 a^{4} (1-\mu^{2})}$ | Pa | | 10 | Deflection-to-Thickness Limit (Linear Range) | $y_{\max} \leq 0.5\, t$ | linear regime | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE (for KTU 2024 board papers):** When transcribing these formulas in your answer sheet, write $\dfrac{3W(1-\mu^{2})}{16Et^{3}}$ **exactly** — examiners look for the structure $(a^{2} - r^{2})^{2}$, not just numerical substitution. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Diaphragms are the **workhorse primary element** in: - **Differential Pressure (DP) Transmitters** (used to measure level in tanks, flow through orifice plates, and filter condition). - **Capsule-type absolute pressure gauges** (two diaphragms welded rim-to-rim form a sealed reference vacuum capsule). - **Micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS)** pressure sensors in smartphones and tire-pressure monitoring. - **Sanitary pressure gauges** in pharmaceutical and food industries (where the diaphragm is the only wetted part, isolated by a sanitary seal). - **Aerospace altimeters and barometric sensors.** ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Deflection Profile $y(r)$ of a Flat Circular Diaphragm > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **HIGH-YIELD derivation** for KTU 2024. Practice writing it cleanly in 6–8 lines on the answer sheet. **Starting Assumptions (Small Deflection Plate Theory):** - Thickness $t$ is small compared to radius $a$ (i.e., $t \ll a$). - Deflection $y \ll t$ (linear elastic regime). - Material obeys Hooke's Law. - Pressure $P$ is uniform across the plate. **Step 1 — Total load on the diaphragm:** $$ W = P \times \text{Area} = P \times \pi a^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Equilibrium of an annular element at radius $r$:** The bending moment in the plate, when integrated across the thickness, must balance the applied pressure. For a circular plate clamped at the edge under uniform load, classical plate theory (Timoshenko & Woinowsky-Krieger) gives the differential equation: $$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left\{ r \frac{d}{dr}\left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dy}{dr} \right) \right] \right\} = \frac{P}{D} $$ where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity of the plate**: $$ D = \frac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\mu^{2})} $$ **Step 3 — Integrate the fourth-order ODE four times.** First integration: $$ r \frac{d}{dr}\left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dy}{dr} \right) \right] = \frac{P r^{2}}{2D} + C_{1} $$ Divide by $r$ and integrate again: $$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dy}{dr} \right) = \frac{P r^{2}}{4D} + C_{1} \ln(r) + C_{2} $$ For finiteness of slope at $r = 0$, we must have $C_{1} = 0$. So: $$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dy}{dr} \right) = \frac{P r^{2}}{4D} + C_{2} $$ Multiply by $r$ and integrate: $$ r \frac{dy}{dr} = \frac{P r^{4}}{16 D} + \frac{C_{2} r^{2}}{2} + C_{3} $$ Divide by $r$: $$ \frac{dy}{dr} = \frac{P r^{3}}{16 D} + \frac{C_{2} r}{2} + \frac{C_{3}}{r} $$ For finite slope at $r=0$, set $C_{3}=0$. Then: $$ \frac{dy}{dr} = \frac{P r^{3}}{16 D} + \frac{C_{2} r}{2} $$ Final integration: $$ y(r) = \frac{P r^{4}}{64 D} + \frac{C_{2} r^{2}}{4} + C_{4} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions.** - **Clamped edge at $r = a$:** Deflection is zero, so $y(a) = 0$. - **Clamped edge at $r = a$:** Slope is zero, so $\dfrac{dy}{dr}\Big\vert_{r=a} = 0$. From slope BC: $$ 0 = \frac{P a^{3}}{16 D} + \frac{C_{2} a}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{2} = -\frac{P a^{2}}{8 D} $$ From deflection BC: $$ 0 = \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} - \frac{P a^{2}}{8 D} \cdot \frac{a^{2}}{4} + C_{4} $$ $$ 0 = \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} - \frac{P a^{4}}{32 D} + C_{4} $$ $$ 0 = -\frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} + C_{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{4} = \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute constants back into the deflection expression.** $$ y(r) = \frac{P r^{4}}{64 D} - \frac{P a^{2} r^{2}}{32 D} + \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} $$ Factor: $$ y(r) = \frac{P}{64 D}\left( a^{4} - 2 a^{2} r^{2} + r^{4} \right) = \frac{P}{64 D} (a^{2} - r^{2})^{2} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\mu^{2})}$ and $W = P \pi a^{2}$.** $$ y(r) = \frac{P (a^{2} - r^{2})^{2}}{64} \cdot \frac{12(1-\mu^{2})}{E t^{3}} $$ $$ \boxed{\, y(r) = \frac{3\,W\,(1-\mu^{2})}{16\,E\,t^{3}} \,(a^{2} - r^{2})^{2} \,} $$ > **Reasoning Recap:** The $(a^{2} - r^{2})^{2}$ term tells us that the deflection falls off **quadratically** from the clamped edge toward the center. The $(1-\mu^{2})$ factor is the **plate-correction factor** — it accounts for the fact that a thin plate cannot contract freely in the radial direction because of the surrounding material, making it effectively stiffer than a 1-D beam would suggest. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity, Range, and Linearity Trade-offs | Design Choice | Increases $y_{\max}$ (Sensitivity) | Affects Range | Affects Linearity | Affects Natural Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | ↑ $a$ (larger radius) | $\propto a^{4}$ — **HUGE** | Larger deflection | Slightly non-linear at high $P$ | ↓ ($f_n \propto 1/a^{2}$) | | ↓ $t$ (thinner plate) | $\propto 1/t^{3}$ — **VERY HIGH** | Lower max pressure | More non-linear | ↓ ($f_n \propto t$) | | ↓ $E$ (softer material) | $\propto 1/E$ | Lower max pressure | Mostly linear | ↓ (mildly) | | Use corrugations | Large linear range | Increases linear range | **Greatly improved** | Marginal change | > [!TIP] > **Design Heuristic (KTU Viva Favorite):** "To increase the *linear range* of a diaphragm, add corrugations. To increase *sensitivity*, reduce thickness. To extend *range* to higher pressures, use thicker diaphragm or smaller diameter." --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU-style):** A flat steel diaphragm has an effective radius $a = 20\,\text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 0.5\,\text{mm}$. Find the maximum deflection when a pressure of $P = 0.5\,\text{MPa}$ is applied. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{11}\,\text{N/m}^{2}$ and $\mu = 0.3$. **Solution:** Given: $a = 20 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $t = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $P = 0.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa}$, $\mu = 0.3$. Step 1 — Compute $(1-\mu^{2})$: $$ 1 - \mu^{2} = 1 - (0.3)^{2} = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91 $$ Step 2 — Apply the formula: $$ y_{\max} = \frac{3 P a^{4} (1-\mu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}} $$ Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values: $$ y_{\max} = \frac{3 \times (0.5 \times 10^{6}) \times (20 \times 10^{-3})^{4} \times 0.91}{16 \times (2 \times 10^{11}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{3}} $$ Step 4 — Evaluate numerator: $$ \text{Num} = 3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.91 = 2.184 \times 10^{2} $$ Step 5 — Evaluate denominator: $$ \text{Den} = 16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10} = 4.0 \times 10^{2} $$ Step 6 — Final answer: $$ \boxed{\, y_{\max} = \frac{2.184 \times 10^{2}}{4.0 \times 10^{2}} = 0.546 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \approx 0.546\,\text{mm} \,} $$ Step 7 — Check linearity limit: $y_{\max} / t = 0.546 / 0.5 \approx 1.09 > 0.5$. **Conclusion:** Operation is in the **non-linear regime** — the linear theory is borderline valid. A corrugated diaphragm would be more appropriate for this pressure level. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Diaphragm-Based Pressure Sensor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process Pressure P] --> B[Diaphragm Plate
Clamped at Edge] B --> C[Mechanical Deflection y] C --> D[Secondary Transducer] D --> E[Signal Conditioning] E --> F[Display or
4-20 mA Output] D -.-> D1[Strain Gauge Bridge] D -.-> D2[LVDT Core] D -.-> D3[Capacitive Plate] D -.-> D4[Piezoelectric Crystal] classDef inputStyle fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processStyle fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputStyle fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A inputStyle class B,C processStyle class F outputStyle ``` ### 4.2 Deflection Profile Shape (Side View Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLAMPED_DIAGRAM["CLAMPED FLAT CIRCULAR DIaphragm - Side View"] direction TB A1[Clamped Edge at r equals a] --- A2[Deflected Center at r equals 0] A2 -.Max deflection y_max.-> A3[Original Undeflected Position] A1 --- A3 end classDef defStyle fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A1,A2,A3 defStyle ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Corrugated vs. Flat Diaphragm Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FLAT_BLOCK["FLAT Diaphragm Behaviour"] F1[High Sensitivity] --> F2[Small Linear Range] F2 --> F3[Non-linearity above y t ratio 0.5] end subgraph CORR_BLOCK["CORRUGATED Diaphragm Behaviour"] C1[Moderate Sensitivity] --> C2[Wide Linear Range] C2 --> C3[Stops Buckling under load] end P[Applied Pressure P] --> FLAT_BLOCK P --> CORR_BLOCK classDef flatStyle fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef corrStyle fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class F1,F2,F3 flatStyle class C1,C2,C3 corrStyle ``` ### 4.4 Functional Flow — Signal Path in a Diaphragm DP Transmitter ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[High Pressure Port] --> M1[Diaphragm Capsule 1] S2[Low Pressure Port] --> M1 M1 --> M2[Differential Deflection delta y] M2 --> M3[Strain Gauge Bridge Whetstone] M3 --> M4[Amplifier and ADC] M4 --> M5[Microcontroller Linearization] M5 --> M6[Current Loop 4-20 mA Output] classDef sigStyle fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef procStyle fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#854D0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outStyle fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class S1,S2 sigStyle class M1,M2,M3 procStyle class M6 outStyle ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Define a diaphragm. List any two advantages and one limitation of using a flat diaphragm as a pressure sensing element.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A diaphragm is a thin, circular, metallic (or non-metallic) plate that is rigidly clamped at its periphery. It acts as a primary sensing element for pressure measurement by converting the applied pressure into a small mechanical displacement at its center. **Advantages (1 mark):** - Simple construction with only one moving part. - High sensitivity at the center due to $(a^{2} - r^{2})^{2}$ deflection profile. - Can be integrated with strain gauges, LVDTs, or capacitive pickups. **Limitation (0.5 mark):** Non-linear behavior when $y_{\max}/t > 0.5$; small deflection requires sensitive secondary transducers. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Why are corrugated diaphragms preferred over flat diaphragms in many industrial pressure gauges?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Corrugated diaphragms have concentric ripples (corrugations) pressed into the plate. They are preferred because: 1. **Larger linear range** — the corrugations allow more deflection before the material reaches the elastic limit, giving a near-linear pressure vs. deflection curve over a wider range. **(1 mark)** 2. **Reduced buckling** — corrugations prevent the plate from snapping or buckling under one-sided overload. **(1 mark)** 3. **Improved fatigue life** — they distribute stress more evenly, so the diaphragm survives many more pressure cycles. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Forgetting to **square the radius** $a$ in $W = P \pi a^{2}$ (common error — losing 1 mark). > - Writing $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3Wa^{2}}{16Et^{3}}$ instead of $\dfrac{3Wa^{3}}{16Et^{3}}$ (missing the **cube** on $a$ — losing 1 mark). > - Omitting the $(1-\mu^{2})$ plate-correction factor (loses 1 mark — examiners are very particular about this). > - Not verifying the linear regime $y_{\max} \le 0.5\,t$ in numerical problems (loses 1 mark). > - Mixing up units: $a$ and $t$ **must be in metres**, not mm. Substituting mm directly is a **silly mistake** that costs 2 full marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a diaphragm used as a pressure sensing element. Derive the expression for the deflection of a flat circular diaphragm clamped at its edges under uniform pressure. **Model Solution:** **Working Principle (3 marks):** The diaphragm is a thin circular plate clamped at its rim. When pressure $P$ acts on one side, the plate deflects inward. The center deflection is maximum, and the deflection decreases as a quadratic function of radial distance $r$: $$ y(r) = \frac{3W(1-\mu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}} (a^{2} - r^{2})^{2} $$ The deflection is sensed by a secondary transducer (LVDT, strain gauge, or capacitive plate) and converted into an electrical signal proportional to pressure. **Sketch (1 mark):** A circular plate clamped at the rim with a smooth concave deflection profile, showing $y_{\max}$ at the center. **Derivation (3 marks):** The full 6-step derivation given in **Section 3.1** above should be reproduced here. The key results are: - $W = P \pi a^{2}$ - $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\mu^{2})}$ - $\boxed{y(r) = \dfrac{3W(1-\mu^{2})}{16Et^{3}} (a^{2} - r^{2})^{2}}$ **(b) [7 marks]** A flat steel diaphragm has an effective diameter of $40\,\text{mm}$ and thickness $1\,\text{mm}$. Calculate: 1. The maximum deflection when a pressure of $0.2\,\text{MPa}$ is applied. 2. The maximum tangential stress at the center. 3. The natural frequency of the diaphragm. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{11}\,\text{N/m}^{2}$, $\mu = 0.3$, and $\rho = 7850\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $a = 20 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $t = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$ - $P = 0.2 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$ - $E = 2 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa}$, $\mu = 0.3$, $\rho = 7850\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ **Part (1): Maximum Deflection [3 marks]** $$ y_{\max} = \frac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \mu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}} $$ $$ (1 - \mu^{2}) = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91 $$ $$ y_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 0.2 \times 10^{6} \times (20 \times 10^{-3})^{4} \times 0.91}{16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times (10^{-3})^{3}} $$ Numerator: $3 \times 0.2 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.91 = 87.36$ Denominator: $16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-9} = 3200$ $$ \boxed{\, y_{\max} = 87.36 / 3200 = 0.0273\,\text{mm} \,} $$ **Part (2): Maximum Tangential Stress at Center [2 marks]** $$ \sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{3 P a^{2}}{8 t^{2}} (1 + \mu) $$ $$ \sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{3 \times 0.2 \times 10^{6} \times (20 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{8 \times (10^{-3})^{2}} \times 1.3 $$ $$ \sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{3 \times 0.2 \times 10^{6} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} \times 1.3 $$ $$ \boxed{\, \sigma_{t,\max} = 39\,\text{MPa} \,} $$ **Part (3): Natural Frequency [2 marks]** $$ f_{n} = \frac{10.21}{2\pi a^{2}} \sqrt{\frac{E t^{2}}{12 \rho (1-\mu^{2})}} $$ $$ f_{n} = \frac{10.21}{2\pi \times (20 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{11} \times (10^{-3})^{2}}{12 \times 7850 \times 0.91}} $$ $$ f_{n} = \frac{10.21}{2\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{85722}} $$ $$ f_{n} = 4062.5 \times \sqrt{2.333} = 4062.5 \times 1.528 $$ $$ \boxed{\, f_{n} \approx 6207\,\text{Hz} \,} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 marks]** Compare flat and corrugated diaphragms in terms of construction, sensitivity, linear range, and typical applications. **Model Answer (Comparison Table + Discussion — 7 marks):** | Parameter | Flat Diaphragm | Corrugated Diaphragm | |---|---|---| | **Construction** | Smooth thin circular plate, clamped at edges. Simple. | Same plate, but with concentric ripples (corrugations) pressed into surface. | | **Sensitivity ($y_{\max}/P$)** | **Higher** — large deflection for small pressure. | **Slightly lower** — corrugations add stiffness. | | **Linear Range** | **Narrow** — non-linearity begins at $y_{\max}/t \approx 0.5$. | **Wide** — corrugations extend the linear region up to several times the thickness. | | **Stress Distribution** | Concentrated at clamped edge. | Distributed across corrugations — lower peak stress. | | **Buckling Resistance** | **Poor** — snaps under one-sided load. | **Excellent** — corrugations prevent buckling. | | **Fabrication Cost** | Lower | Higher (requires corrugation tooling) | | **Typical Applications** | Low-pressure gauges, microphone capsules, MEMS pressure sensors | Differential pressure transmitters, capsule-type absolute pressure gauges, process control | **Discussion (3 marks):** The choice between flat and corrugated is driven by the **range-to-sensitivity trade-off**. For low-pressure clean-room applications (e.g., microphones, barometric sensors), flat diaphragms are preferred due to their high sensitivity. For process industry applications requiring wide linear range and high reliability under varying loads (e.g., DP transmitters, level measurement), corrugated diaphragms are the de-facto standard. **(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain how a diaphragm is used in a **differential pressure (DP) transmitter**. Mention the role of the strain gauge bridge and the 4–20 mA current loop. **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR HP[High Pressure Port] --> CAP[Diaphragm Capsule] LP[Low Pressure Port] --> CAP CAP --> SG[Strain Gauge Bridge] SG --> AMPL[Amplifier and ADC] AMPL --> MC[Microcontroller for Linearization] MC --> LOOP[4-20 mA Current Loop Output] ``` **Explanation (4 marks):** 1. **Diaphragm Capsule (1 mark):** Two corrugated diaphragms are welded rim-to-rim, forming a sealed capsule. High pressure is applied to one side and low (reference) pressure to the other. The **differential pressure** $\Delta P = P_{H} - P_{L}$ causes a small **differential deflection** $\Delta y$ between the two diaphragms. 2. **Strain Gauge Bridge (1 mark):** Strain gauges bonded to the diaphragms form a **Wheatstone bridge**. As the diaphragms flex, the gauges experience strain $\epsilon$, changing their resistance by $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \epsilon$, where $G$ is the gauge factor. The bridge converts this to a millivolt-level voltage proportional to $\Delta P$. 3. **Signal Conditioning (1 mark):** An instrumentation amplifier boosts the bridge output. An ADC digitizes it, and a microcontroller applies **linearization and temperature compensation** using a stored calibration curve. 4. **4–20 mA Current Loop (1 mark):** The microcontroller drives a **voltage-to-current converter** that outputs 4 mA at zero differential pressure and 20 mA at the full-scale pressure. The 4 mA "live zero" allows the loop to also power the transmitter (**two-wire loop-powered design**), which is the industry standard (Hart protocol). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Diaphragm (KTU 2024 Module 2)** - **Definition:** Thin circular plate, clamped at rim, used to convert pressure into displacement. **(Must know)** - **Two main types:** Flat and Corrugated. Corrugated gives wider linear range. **(Compare in 2-marks easily)** - **Key formula to memorize:** $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3Pa^{4}(1-\mu^{2})}{16Et^{3}}$ - **Plate-correction factor $(1-\mu^{2})$** is unique to plate theory — DO NOT omit it. - **Sensitivity $\propto a^{4}/t^{3}$** — large diameter + thin plate = highest sensitivity. - **Linear regime limit:** $y_{\max} \leq 0.5\,t$ (always verify in numerical problems). - **Maximum stress occurs at the clamped edge** (radial stress) and **at the center** (tangential stress). - **Natural frequency** must be $\geq 5\times$ the maximum process fluctuation frequency to avoid resonance. - **Secondary transducers commonly coupled:** LVDT, strain gauge bridge, capacitive plate, piezoelectric crystal. - **Industry-standard application:** Differential pressure (DP) transmitter with 4–20 mA output and HART protocol. - **Common mistake in KTU papers:** Confusing $a$ (radius) with $d$ (diameter) — always substitute $a$, not $d/2$, in the formula directly as **effective radius**. - **Units discipline:** All SI — metres, pascals, newtons, hertz. Convert mm → m before substituting.

topics.title: Types of Variable Inductance and Capacitance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Variable Inductance and Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Variable Inductance Transducer — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Variable Inductance Transducer** is a passive electromechanical transducer in which the **self-inductance (L)** of a coil or the **mutual inductance (M)** between a pair of coils is varied in a definite, mathematically predictable manner in response to the measurand (displacement, force, pressure, vibration, etc.). The variation is achieved by mechanically altering either the **magnetic reluctance** of the flux path, the **air-gap length**, the **effective overlap area**, or the **permeability (μ)** of the core material.

The governing expression is the fundamental inductance of a magnetic circuit:

$$L = \frac{N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,\mu_{r}\,A}{l} = \frac{N^{2}}{R_{m}}$$

where $N$ = number of turns, $A$ = cross-sectional area of the core, $l$ = mean flux path length, and $R_{m}$ = total magnetic reluctance.

### 1.2 Variable Capacitance Transducer — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Variable Capacitance Transducer** is a passive transducer in which the electrical capacitance between a set of conducting plates (or concentric cylinders) is varied by the measurand. The variation occurs by changing any one of the three factors in the parallel-plate capacitance equation: the **plate overlap area (A)**, the **dielectric separation/distance (d)**, or the **dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_{r}$)** of the medium between the plates.

The fundamental expression is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_{r}$ is the relative permittivity, $A$ is the effective plate area in m², and $d$ is the plate separation in metres.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnetic Sliding Door"**
> Think of a **variable inductance transducer** as a sliding door in a window frame. The coil is the frame, the iron core is the sliding door. When the door (core) slides deeper into the frame, the "magnetic openness" shrinks, so the inductance **rises**. When the door slides out, more air-gap remains, magnetic resistance **rises**, and inductance **drops**. The transducer is simply reading the position of the door.
>
> For the **variable capacitance transducer**, imagine **two parallel metal plates facing each other**, with a thin invisible elastic cushion (dielectric) between them. If you squeeze the plates closer, the cushion compresses, more "electrical charge" (electron crowd) can pile up for a given voltage → **C increases**. If you slide one plate sideways so the overlapping area shrinks, the cushion's effective size shrinks → **C decreases**. The transducer reads the geometric change.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_{0}$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_{0}$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| Permittivity of air | $\varepsilon_{r,air}$ | $\approx 1.0006$ | — |
| Permittivity of typical oil | $\varepsilon_{r,oil}$ | $\approx 2$ to $4$ | — |
| Permittivity of water | $\varepsilon_{r,water}$ | $\approx 80$ | — |
| Typical linear range (LVDT) | — | $\pm 100\,\mu m$ to $\pm 25\,cm$ | — |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Variable Inductance Transducers

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups variable-inductance devices into **four** canonical families:

1. **Single-coil variable reluctance type** (core moves inside one coil).
2. **Differential (two-coil) variable reluctance type** — also called the **push-pull** type; one inductance rises while the other falls.
3. **Mutual-inductance type** — only $M$ between a primary and secondary coil varies (no measurable change in individual self-inductances). The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** belongs to this family.
4. **Movable-iron / solenoid-core type** — iron plunger is drawn into a solenoid by the measurand force, varying the saturation and effective permeability.

#### 2.1.1 Variable Reluctance (Single) — Operating Logic

- A coil is wound on a **magnetic core** with a fixed pole-piece.
- A movable **ferromagnetic armature** approaches/leaves a defined **air gap** $x$.
- As $x$ increases, magnetic reluctance $R_{m}$ **rises** and inductance $L = N^{2}/R_{m}$ **falls**.
- Output is a **single-ended, non-linear** signal. Used for pressure cells, vibration pickups.

#### 2.1.2 Differential Variable Reluctance — Operating Logic

- **Two identical coils** are placed on either side of a central armature.
- The armature is displaced by the measurand:
  - Air gap to coil 1 → $x - \delta x$ → $L_{1}$ **increases**.
  - Air gap to coil 2 → $x + \delta x$ → $L_{2}$ **decreases**.
- The transducer electronics measure either the **difference** $(L_{1} - L_{2})$ or the **ratio** $L_{1}/L_{2}$, both of which give a **linear** output about the null position.
- This is the **foundation** of the LVDT and is the most-tested KTU 2024 sub-topic.

#### 2.1.3 LVDT (Mutual-Inductance Type)

- A single **primary winding** is energised by a **carrier excitation** (typically 1–10 kHz sine wave, amplitude 3–24 V rms).
- **Two identical secondary windings** are placed symmetrically on either side of the primary.
- A movable **soft-iron core** slides within the bore.
- At null position: $|e_{1}| = |e_{2}|$, so differential output $E_{o} = e_{1} - e_{2} = 0$.
- For displacement $+\delta x$: $e_{1} > e_{2}$ → output is in-phase with primary.
- For displacement $-\delta x$: $e_{2} > e_{1}$ → output is anti-phase (180° shift). **Phase reversal indicates direction.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the LVDT called the "giant" of KTU exams?**
> Because it is simultaneously a *variable reluctance*, a *mutual-inductance*, and a *differential* transducer. It satisfies all three KTU 2024 syllabus classifications in one device, which is why it appears in **almost every question paper**.

### 2.2 Classification of Variable Capacitance Transducers

| Type | Variable Element | Typical Application |
|------|------------------|---------------------|
| Variable **distance** (air-gap) | $d$ varies | Displacement, pressure (diaphragm) |
| Variable **area** (overlap) | $A$ varies | Angular displacement, large linear displacement |
| Variable **dielectric** | $\varepsilon_{r}$ varies | Level measurement (oil/water), moisture, humidity |
| Differential capacitive | $d$ varies in two chambers | Pressure, force (linearised) |
| Stray-capacitance (proximity) | Effective $A$ and $d$ of body to plate | Liquid level, object detection |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — it covers 90% of the numerical questions on this module.**

| # | Quantity / Configuration | Governing Formula | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|--------------------------|-------------------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | Self-inductance, magnetic-circuit model | $L = \dfrac{N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,\mu_{r}\,A}{l}$ | H | $\mu_r \gg 1$ for iron core |
| 2 | Reluctance of air gap | $R_{g} = \dfrac{x}{\mu_{0}\,A_{g}}$ | A-t/Wb | $x$ = air-gap length |
| 3 | Inductance with single air gap $x$ | $L \approx \dfrac{N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,A_{g}}{x}$ | H | Valid when $x \ll$ core path |
| 4 | Differential variable reluctance output | $L_{1} - L_{2} = \dfrac{2 N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}\,\delta x}{x^{2} - (\delta x)^{2}}$ | H | Linear when $\delta x \ll x$ |
| 5 | LVDT output voltage | $E_{o} = K_{s}\,\delta x$ | V | $K_s$ = sensitivity, V/mm |
| 6 | Mutual inductance (LVDT) | $M = M_{1} - M_{2} = k\,\delta x$ | H | $k$ = coupling coefficient factor |
| 7 | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d}$ | F | Edge effects neglected |
| 8 | Sensitivity, variable $d$ | $\dfrac{dC}{dd} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d^{2}}$ | F/m | Non-linear in $d$ |
| 9 | Differential capacitive output | $\Delta C = C_{1} - C_{2} = \dfrac{2\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,\delta x}{d_{0}^{2} - (\delta x)^{2}}$ | F | Linear when $\delta x \ll d_0$ |
| 10 | Cylindrical (concentric) capacitor | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,L}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}$ | F | $L$ = immersed length |
| 11 | Cylindrical capacitive level (partially filled, $\varepsilon_{r}=\varepsilon_{l}$) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\,\varepsilon_{0}}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}\bigl[\varepsilon_{air}(H-h) + \varepsilon_{l}\,h\bigr]$ | F | $h$ = liquid height |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Inductive:** LVDT-based pressure transmitters in refineries, accelerometer probes in CNC spindles, load cells in weighing bridges, proximity switches in assembly lines.
- **Capacitive:** Capacitive level sensors in chemical tanks (using $\varepsilon_{r}$ jump at liquid surface), touch-screen digitizers, humidity sensors (dielectric varies with adsorbed water), differential-pressure transducers using a diaphragm as the moving plate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inductance of a Coil with a Single Air Gap

We model a coil of $N$ turns wound on a laminated iron core of length $l_c$, cross-section $A_c$, and permeability $\mu_r$. A small air gap of length $x$ is introduced. The total reluctance is the **series sum** of the iron-path reluctance and the air-gap reluctance.

**Step 1** — Iron path reluctance (negligible for high $\mu_r$):

$$R_{c} = \frac{l_{c}}{\mu_{0}\,\mu_{r}\,A_{c}}$$

**Step 2** — Air-gap reluctance (fringing neglected, $A_g = A_c = A$):

$$R_{g} = \frac{x}{\mu_{0}\,A}$$

**Step 3** — Total reluctance (in series):

$$R_{m} = R_{c} + R_{g} = \frac{l_{c}}{\mu_{0}\,\mu_{r}\,A} + \frac{x}{\mu_{0}\,A}$$

**Step 4** — Because $\mu_r$ of iron is typically $\ge 2000$, $R_c \ll R_g$, hence $R_m \approx R_g$:

$$R_{m} \approx \frac{x}{\mu_{0}\,A}$$

**Step 5** — Substituting into $L = N^{2}/R_{m}$:

$$\boxed{\;L(x) = \frac{N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,A}{x}\;}$$

**Physical meaning:** $L$ is **inversely proportional** to air-gap length $x$. A small motion of the armature produces a large change in $L$, which is why this geometry is so useful for small-displacement measurements.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Variable Reluctance Output

Consider two identical coils wound on a yoke with a central armature displaced by a small amount $\delta x$ from the symmetric null. The air gaps become $x - \delta x$ and $x + \delta x$ respectively.

**Step 1** — Inductance of coil 1:

$$L_{1} = \frac{N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,A_{g}}{x - \delta x}$$

**Step 2** — Inductance of coil 2:

$$L_{2} = \frac{N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,A_{g}}{x + \delta x}$$

**Step 3** — Take the difference using the standard algebra identity $\dfrac{1}{a-b} - \dfrac{1}{a+b} = \dfrac{2b}{a^{2}-b^{2}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{1} - L_{2} &= N^{2}\mu_{0}A_{g}\!\left[\frac{1}{x - \delta x} - \frac{1}{x + \delta x}\right] \\[4pt]
&= N^{2}\mu_{0}A_{g}\!\left[\frac{(x+\delta x) - (x-\delta x)}{(x-\delta x)(x+\delta x)}\right] \\[4pt]
&= N^{2}\mu_{0}A_{g}\!\left[\frac{2\delta x}{x^{2} - (\delta x)^{2}}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4** — Final boxed form:

$$\boxed{\;L_{1} - L_{2} = \frac{2\,N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,A_{g}\,\delta x}{x^{2} - (\delta x)^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 5** — **Linearisation (KEY KTU step):** If $\delta x \ll x$, the $(\delta x)^{2}$ term is dropped:

$$L_{1} - L_{2} \;\approx\; \frac{2\,N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,A_{g}}{x^{2}}\,\delta x \;\propto\; \delta x$$

The output is **directly proportional** to displacement, which is the principal engineering advantage of the differential configuration.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LVDT Output Voltage

**Step 1** — When the movable core is exactly at the geometric centre, mutual inductances from the primary to the two secondaries are equal: $M_{1} = M_{2} = M_{0}$.

**Step 2** — When the core is displaced by $\delta x$, the two mutual inductances vary linearly in the linear range:

$$M_{1} = M_{0} + k\,\delta x \qquad M_{2} = M_{0} - k\,\delta x$$

**Step 3** — With primary current $i_{p} = I_{p}\sin(\omega t)$, the induced e.m.f.s are:

$$e_{1} = -j\omega M_{1}\,i_{p}, \quad e_{2} = -j\omega M_{2}\,i_{p}$$

**Step 4** — The secondaries are connected in **series-opposition** (anti-phase), so the net output is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{o} &= e_{1} - e_{2} = -j\omega(M_{1}-M_{2})\,i_{p} \\[4pt]
&= -j\omega\,(2k\,\delta x)\,I_{p}\sin(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5** — Effective output amplitude:

$$\boxed{\;E_{o} = 2\,\omega\,k\,I_{p}\,\delta x = K_{s}\,\delta x\;}$$

**Engineering reading:** The magnitude $|E_{o}|$ is proportional to $\vert \delta x \vert$, while the **phase** (in-phase vs. anti-phase) indicates the **sign (direction)** of displacement.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Variable-Distance Capacitive Transducer

A single parallel-plate capacitor with area $A$, dielectric $\varepsilon_r$, and variable gap $d$.

**Step 1** — Base equation:

$$C(d) = \frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d}$$

**Step 2** — Sensitivity with respect to gap (one differentiation):

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 3** — The **fractional change** in capacitance is:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{dd}{d} = -d(\ln d)$$

**Step 4** — The non-linear term $-dd/d$ shows that the device has a **hyperbolic** transfer characteristic; small gaps give large, highly non-linear responses.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Differential Capacitive Transducer

Two parallel-plate capacitors share a central movable plate. Initial gap each side is $d_{0}$. The plate moves by $\delta x$.

**Step 1** — Capacitances with motion:

$$C_{1} = \frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d_{0} - \delta x}, \quad C_{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d_{0} + \delta x}$$

**Step 2** — Take the difference (using the same algebraic identity as in Section 3.2):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= C_{1} - C_{2} = \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}A\!\left[\frac{1}{d_{0} - \delta x} - \frac{1}{d_{0} + \delta x}\right] \\[4pt]
&= \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}A \cdot \frac{2\delta x}{d_{0}^{2} - (\delta x)^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3** — Final form and linearisation:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta C = \frac{2\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,\delta x}{d_{0}^{2} - (\delta x)^{2}} \;\approx\; \frac{2\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d_{0}^{2}}\,\delta x\;}$$

**Physical reading:** As with the inductive differential, **the differential capacitive configuration linearises the output** about the null position — this is a recurring KTU 2024 question.

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Capacitive Level Measurement (Cylindrical Probe)

A concentric cylindrical capacitor is used as a level sensor. Inner rod of radius $r_i$, outer sheath of radius $r_o$, length $H$, with a liquid of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_l$ filling the probe up to a height $h$.

**Step 1** — Capacitance of empty (air-filled) portion, length $H - h$:

$$C_{air} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\,1\,(H - h)}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}$$

**Step 2** — Capacitance of liquid-filled portion, length $h$:

$$C_{liquid} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{l}\,h}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}$$

**Step 3** — The two portions are in **parallel** (radially stacked along the length):

$$\boxed{\;C(h) = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}\bigl[(H - h) + \varepsilon_{l}\,h\bigr] = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}\bigl[H + (\varepsilon_{l} - 1)h\bigr]\;}$$

**Step 4** — Calibration check: at $h = 0$ (empty), $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}H}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}$; at $h = H$ (full), $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{l}H}{\ln(r_{o}/r_{i})}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `N1`, `CoilA`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
- Node labels are **raw uppercase text** — no bold/italic/HTML tags inside quotes.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — LVDT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXC[Excitation Source 1 To 10 kHz Sine 3 To 24 V rms] --> PRI
    PRI[Primary Winding] --> CORE
    CORE[Movable Soft Iron Core Position x] --> SEC1
    CORE --> SEC2
    SEC1[Secondary 1 Mutual Inductance M1] --> PHASE
    SEC2[Secondary 2 Mutual Inductance M2] --> PHASE
    PHASE[Series Opposition Subtractor e1 Minus e2] --> DEMOD
    DEMOD[Phase Sensitive Demodulator and Filter] --> OUTA
    OUTA[DC Output Magnitude Indicates Displacement] --> DSP1
    DEMOD --> OUTB
    OUTB[Output Polarity Indicates Direction of Motion] --> DSP2
    DSP1[Display and Recorder] 
    DSP2[Display and Recorder]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Differential Variable Reluctance (Yoke + Armature)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ARM[Central Movable Armature Displacement Delta x] --> GAP1
    ARM --> GAP2
    GAP1[Air Gap x Minus Delta x] --> COIL1
    COIL2[Air Gap x Plus Delta x] --> GAP2
    COIL1[Coil 1 L1 Increases] --> SUMM
    COIL2[Coil 2 L2 Decreases] --> SUMM
    SUMM[Difference Detector L1 Minus L2] --> LIN[Linear Output About Null]
    LIN --> IND[Indicator or Bridge Amplifier]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Variable Capacitance Transducer Families

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPT[Variable Capacitance Transducer] --> VD
    CPT --> VA
    CPT --> VE
    VD[Variable Distance d] --> APP1[Diaphragm Pressure Cells]
    VA[Variable Area A Overlap] --> APP2[Angular Displacement and Long Stroke Linear]
    VE[Variable Dielectric Epsilon r] --> APP3[Liquid Level Moisture and Humidity]
    APP1 --> DIFF[Differential Versions for Linearity]
    APP2 --> DIFF
    APP3 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> COND[Signal Conditioning with Bridge and AC Amplifier]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Capacitive Liquid Level Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK[Storage Tank] --> PROBE
    PROBE[Concentric Cylindrical Probe Inner Radius r i Outer Radius r o] --> EMPTY
    PROBE --> LIQ
    EMPTY[Air Section Length H Minus h Epsilon r 1] --> SUM
    LIQ[Liquid Section Length h Epsilon r Epsilon l] --> SUM
    SUM[Parallel Combination Gives Total Capacitance C h] --> COND
    COND[Capacitance to Voltage Converter C to V] --> DISP
    DISP[Digital Level Display in Percent or Litres]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working of a single-coil variable reluctance transducer. State its main disadvantage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A coil is wound on a magnetic core with a fixed pole piece. A movable **ferromagnetic armature** changes the air-gap length $x$. The inductance is $L = N^{2}\mu_{0}A/x$, which **falls** as $x$ increases. **Main disadvantage:** the output is non-linear (hyperbolic) in displacement, and it is a single-ended device — susceptible to temperature drift and supply variations. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any **three advantages** and **two limitations** of an LVDT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Advantages:* (i) **Infinite resolution** since there is no contact / friction. (ii) Very high sensitivity (mV/mm). (iii) **Direction sensing** through output phase reversal. (iv) Rugged, long life.
*Limitations:* (i) Requires **AC excitation**, hence more complex electronics. (ii) Sensitive to stray magnetic fields; needs shielding. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — LVDT Module

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a clear construction diagram, explain the **working principle of an LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Show how the direction of motion of the core is detected.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An LVDT has a sensitivity of 0.5 mV per 0.001 inch. A 5 kHz excitation is applied. If the core is displaced by 0.5 mm, find the **output voltage magnitude**. Comment on the **phase** if the core moves **toward** secondary 1. (Given: 1 inch = 25.4 mm.)

---

**Model Solution to Q(a) — Working Principle, Derivation, and Direction Sensing (7 marks)**

1. **Construction (1.5 marks):** An LVDT consists of a **primary winding (P)** placed centrally, **two identical secondary windings (S₁ and S₂)** wound on either side of the primary, and a movable **soft-iron core** that slides coaxially within the bore. The secondaries are connected in **series-opposition**. A sinusoidal excitation (1–10 kHz) is fed to the primary.

2. **Working at null (1 mark):** With the core at the geometric centre, the mutual inductances $M_1$ and $M_2$ to the two secondaries are equal. The induced e.m.f.s are equal and opposite; **net output = 0**.

3. **Working off-null (1.5 marks):** On displacement $\delta x$ toward $S_1$: $M_1 > M_2$, $e_1 > e_2$, so $E_o = e_1 - e_2$ is **in-phase** with the primary voltage. On displacement toward $S_2$: $E_o$ is **180° out of phase**. The **phase reversal** is therefore the indicator of direction.

4. **Derivation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{1} &= M_{0} + k\,\delta x,\quad M_{2} = M_{0} - k\,\delta x \\
E_{o} &= -j\omega(M_{1} - M_{2})\,i_{p} = -j\omega\,(2k\,\delta x)\,I_{p}\sin\omega t \\
\vert E_{o} \vert &= 2\,\omega\,k\,I_{p}\,\vert\delta x\vert = K_{s}\,\vert\delta x\vert
\end{aligned}$$

The **magnitude** encodes **displacement**; the **phase** encodes **direction**.

---

**Model Solution to Q(b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1** — Convert sensitivity to SI:

$$K_{s} = 0.5\;\text{mV} / 0.001\;\text{inch} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V}}{0.001 \times 25.4 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{25.4 \times 10^{-6}} \;\text{V/m}$$

**Step 2** — Evaluate:

$$K_{s} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{25.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 19.685\;\text{V/m} \approx 19.69\;\text{V/m}$$

**Step 3** — Displacement $\delta x = 0.5\;\text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{o} &= K_{s} \times \delta x = 19.685 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V} \\
&= 9.84 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V} = 9.84\;\text{mV}
