\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4** — Phase comment: Since the core moves **toward secondary 1**, $e_1 > e_2$, so $E_o$ is **in-phase** with the primary excitation.

**Final answer:** $\vert E_o \vert \approx 9.84\;\text{mV}$, **in-phase** with the primary. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Capacitive Module

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the expression for the change in capacitance of a **differential parallel-plate capacitive transducer** when the central plate is displaced by $\delta x$. Show that the output is linear in $\delta x$ for small displacements. What is the **prime advantage** of this configuration?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A **cylindrical capacitive level sensor** is installed in a chemical tank. The probe has inner radius $r_i = 5\;\text{mm}$, outer radius $r_o = 8\;\text{mm}$, total length $H = 1.0\;\text{m}$. The tank contains oil of $\varepsilon_r = 2.4$ to a height of $h = 0.4\;\text{m}$ above the probe bottom. Find the **total capacitance** of the sensor. (Use $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$.)

---

**Model Solution to Q(a) — Differential Capacitive Derivation (7 marks)**

1. **Geometry (1 mark):** Two parallel-plate capacitors share a central movable plate. Initial gap to each fixed plate is $d_0$.

2. **Capacitances on displacement (1.5 marks):**

$$C_{1} = \frac{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \delta x},\quad C_{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A}{d_0 + \delta x}$$

3. **Difference derivation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= C_{1} - C_{2} = \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A\!\left[\frac{1}{d_0 - \delta x} - \frac{1}{d_0 + \delta x}\right] \\[4pt]
&= \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A\cdot\frac{2\delta x}{d_0^{2} - (\delta x)^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

4. **Linearisation & advantage (1.5 marks):** For $\delta x \ll d_0$:

$$\Delta C \;\approx\; \frac{2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A}{d_0^{2}}\,\delta x \;\propto\; \delta x$$

Output is **linear in displacement**. **Prime advantage:** the differential configuration **linearises** the inherently hyperbolic single-plate characteristic, and provides **temperature/self-compensation** (common-mode rejection of drift, lead capacitance, and supply variations).

---

**Model Solution to Q(b) — Capacitive Level Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1** — Identify the air-filled section length and liquid-filled section length:

$$H - h = 1.0 - 0.4 = 0.6\;\text{m}, \quad h = 0.4\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2** — Logarithmic ratio:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{5}\right) = \ln(1.6) = 0.4700$$

**Step 3** — Sum the air and liquid capacitances (in parallel, the sections share the same radial geometry):

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}\bigl[(H-h) + \varepsilon_{l}\,h\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{0.4700}\,\bigl[0.6 + 2.4 \times 0.4\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.562 \times 10^{-11}}{0.4700}\,(0.6 + 0.96) \\[4pt]
&= 1.1834 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.56 \\[4pt]
&= 1.846 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{F}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4** — Final answer:

$$\boxed{\;C \approx 184.6\;\text{pF}\;}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing traps on this module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the series-opposition connection** in the LVDT. If the secondaries are connected in series-aiding, the output at null is **maximum**, not zero — you lose 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing LVDT phase reversal with sign convention.** The phase is the *direction* indicator, not the polarity of supply.
> 3. **Dropping the $(\delta x)^{2}$ term prematurely.** The full unsimplified form is required when the question says "derive"; the linearised form is only valid for the linear-range numerical.
> 4. **For cylindrical capacitors, using $A = \pi(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})$** — this is wrong! The area is the **lateral cylindrical surface**, not the annular face. The correct expression uses the logarithm.
> 5. **For capacitive level probes, not stating that the air and liquid sections are in parallel** along the probe length. If you treat them as series, you lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Neglecting strays in capacitive measurements.** KTU examiners award a half-mark for mentioning the need to use a **guarded three-terminal (Kelvin) measurement** to eliminate stray capacitance to ground.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **L = N²μA/l** — fundamental inductance; any of the four quantities inside can be made the variable.
- **Single variable reluctance** is non-linear (hyperbolic) and single-ended.
- **Differential variable reluctance** is **linear** about null, immune to common-mode drifts.
- **LVDT = mutual-inductance + differential** in one device; **phase reversal ⇒ direction**.
- **C = ε₀ε_r A/d** — fundamental capacitance; vary $A$, $d$, or $\varepsilon_r$ to sense.
- **Variable $d$** is most sensitive but most non-linear; **variable $A$** is most linear.
- **Differential capacitive** = linearised + drift-compensated.
- **Cylindrical capacitive level** uses a logarithmic term $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ and parallel-section combination for air/liquid.
- **Capacitive transducers** dominate where the measurand is a **change in dielectric** (level, moisture, humidity); **inductive transducers** dominate where the measurand is a **displacement or force** (LVDT, load cells, pressure cells).
- **Edge/fringing effects** must be mentioned whenever discussing capacitive transducers — examiners love this one-mark bonus.
- **Stray capacitance and shielding/guarding** are the single biggest practical challenge for capacitive sensors — always mention.
- For small displacement, **always** linearise by dropping $(\delta x)^2$ or $(\delta d)^2$ **after** showing the full expression.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Gauge and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain Gauge and Its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Gauge:** A **passive resistive transducer** that converts mechanical displacement (strain) into a proportional change in electrical resistance. It operates on the principle that the resistance of a conductor changes when it is subjected to mechanical strain (tension or compression).

Mathematically, mechanical strain is defined as the ratio of change in length to original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

The corresponding change in resistance is captured by the **Gauge Factor (GF)**, which is the figure of merit of any strain gauge.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **thin rubber band with a thin metallic wire embedded in it**:
- When you **pull** the rubber band (apply tension) → the wire stretches → becomes **longer and thinner** → its electrical resistance **increases**.
- When you **push/squeeze** it (compression) → the wire shortens → becomes **shorter and thicker** → its resistance **decreases**.

A strain gauge works on **exactly the same principle**, but using precisely calibrated metallic or semiconductor materials. The tiny change in resistance (often in milliohms!) is measured using a **Wheatstone bridge** to determine the unknown strain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Gauge Factor of metal gauges:** typically **2.0 – 2.2** (for Constantan, Nichrome)
> - **Gauge Factor of semiconductor gauges:** typically **100 – 200**
> - **Typical strain measured:** 1 microstrain to 4000 microstrain ($1 \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$)
> - **Poisson's ratio of gauge material:** $\nu \approx 0.3$ (for most metals)

### Working Principle Summary

A strain gauge is fundamentally governed by **three combined effects**:
1. **Change in length** (geometric effect)
2. **Change in cross-sectional area** (Poisson effect)
3. **Change in resistivity** (piezoresistive effect)

The dominance of these effects distinguishes the various types of strain gauges discussed in the upcoming sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Fundamental Resistance Equation

For a conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$, the resistance is:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

Taking the natural logarithm and differentiating gives the fundamental change in resistance:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

### Linking Area Change to Length Change (Poisson Effect)

For a circular conductor of diameter $D$:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta A}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta D}{D}$$

By definition of **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$:

$$\nu = -\frac{\Delta D / D}{\Delta L / L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta D}{D} = -\nu \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

### The Gauge Factor (GF) — The Master Equation

Substituting and rearranging:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\cdot \varepsilon$$

Dividing throughout by strain $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{\text{GF} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

| Term | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $\mathbf{1}$ | Geometric: change in length | always **1** |
| $\mathbf{2\nu}$ | Geometric: Poisson (area change) | ~ **0.6** (since $\nu \approx 0.3$) |
| $\frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Piezoresistive: resistivity change | **Dominant** for semiconductors; **negligible** for metals |

> [!NOTE]
> - For **metallic strain gauges**, piezoresistive effect $\approx 0$, so $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2.0$
> - For **semiconductor strain gauges**, piezoresistive effect is dominant, giving $GF \approx 100\text{–}200$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Mechanical strain | dimensionless (or $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| 2 | $R = \rho \dfrac{L}{A}$ | Resistance of conductor | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $\text{GF} = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge Factor (definition) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{GF} = (1+2\nu) + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Expanded Gauge Factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{GF}_{\text{metal}} \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2$ | For Constantan / Nichrome | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{GF}_{\text{semi}} \approx 100\text{–}200$ | For Si / Ge gauges | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Stress (Hooke's Law) | $N/m^2$ or Pa |
| 8 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{4} \left( \dfrac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \dfrac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \dfrac{\Delta R_4}{R_4} \right)$ | Wheatstone bridge output | Volts |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Strain gauges form the **sensing heart** of:
- **Load cells** (weighing scales, force platforms)
- **Pressure transducers** (diaphragm + strain gauge)
- **Torque sensors** (shaft-mounted gauges)
- **Structural health monitoring** (bridges, aircraft wings, dams)
- **Acceleration sensors** (inertial mass + strain gauge)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Gauge Factor Starting from First Principles

**Given:** A metallic wire of original length $L$, area $A$, resistivity $\rho$, and resistance $R$.

**Step 1 — Base resistance equation:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm of both sides to prepare for differentiation:**

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate both sides with respect to the applied strain:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

> This is the **fundamental resistance change equation** — it states that the fractional change in resistance has three contributions: resistivity change, length change, and area change.

**Step 4 — Express area in terms of diameter (for round wire):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dD}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of Poisson's ratio ($\nu$):**

$$\nu = -\frac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{axial strain}} = -\frac{dD/D}{dL/L}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dD}{D} = -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the area change term:**

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \left( -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} \right) = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into the resistance change equation:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \left( -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} \right)$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} + 2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 8 — Combine like terms:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 9 — Recall that strain $\varepsilon = dL / L$:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 10 — Divide both sides by strain $\varepsilon$ to get Gauge Factor:**

$$\boxed{\text{GF} = \frac{dR / R}{\varepsilon} = \frac{d\rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} + (1 + 2\nu)}$$

This is the **canonical KTU derivation** of the gauge factor.

---

### Derivation 2: Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

**Given:** A balanced Wheatstone bridge with four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ and excitation $V_s$. Only $R_1$ is active (changes by $\Delta R$).

**Step 1 — Apply voltage divider for left leg (R₁ and R₂):**

$$V_a = V_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply voltage divider for right leg (R₃ and R₄):**

$$V_b = V_s \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage is the difference:**

$$V_o = V_a - V_b = V_s \left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right]$$

**Step 4 — At balance, all $R$'s are equal, say $R$. Substitute $R_1 = R + \Delta R$:**

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Since $\Delta R \ll R$, apply the approximation $\dfrac{R+\Delta R}{2R+\Delta R} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}\right)$:**

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon}$$

This shows that **bridge output is directly proportional to strain** — the foundation of all strain-gauge-based measurements.

---

### Derivation 3: Sensitivity of a Semiconductor Strain Gauge

**Given:** For a doped silicon gauge, the fractional resistivity change is:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \cdot \sigma = \pi_l \cdot E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $\pi_l$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient and $E$ is Young's modulus.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Gauge Factor master equation:**

$$\text{GF} = (1 + 2\nu) + \pi_l \cdot E$$

**Step 2 — For silicon, $\pi_l \cdot E$ dominates (around 100–200), so:**

$$\text{GF}_{\text{semi}} \approx \pi_l \cdot E \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$$

**Step 3 — Bridge output becomes (using Derivation 2):**

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \text{GF}_{\text{semi}} \cdot \varepsilon \approx \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot 130 \cdot \varepsilon$$

This explains why **semiconductor gauges are ~50× more sensitive** than metal gauges.

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix: Types of Strain Gauges

| Property | Bonded Wire | Bonded Foil | Semiconductor | Unbonded |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Gauge Factor | ~ 2.0 | ~ 2.0 | 100 – 200 | ~ 2.0 |
| Resistance ($ \Omega $) | 100 – 500 | 100 – 500 | 1000 – 5000 | 100 – 500 |
| Linearity | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (non-linear) | Good |
| Temperature Sensitivity | Low | Low | High | Low |
| Fatigue Life | Good | Excellent | Brittle | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | Low | High | Moderate |
| Sensing Element | Wire (constantan) | Etched foil (constantan) | Doped Si / Ge | Wire under tension |
| Typical Application | General purpose | Load cells, transducers | High-sensitivity sensors | Precision load cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** For most board questions on "types", focus on **Bonded (Wire, Foil, Semiconductor)** and **Unbonded** classifications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Bonded Metal Foil Strain Gauge — Constructional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Applied Mechanical Force F] --> B[Elastic Member Cantilever Beam]
    B --> C[Strain epsilon developed on surface]
    C --> D[Bonded Foil Gauge grid pattern]
    D --> E[Resistance change Delta R]
    E --> F[Wheatstone Bridge circuit]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    G --> H[Display / Output indicator]
    I[Excitation Voltage Vs] --> F
    J[Constantan foil ~ 4 micron thick] --> D
    K[Polyimide carrier backing] --> D
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**
- Mechanical force → beam deflection → strain on surface → foil gauge deforms → $\Delta R$ generated → bridge converts to voltage → amplifier boosts → display.

### Diagram 2: Wheatstone Bridge with Strain Gauge — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1((R1 + Delta R)) --- nA((Node A))
    R2((R2)) --- nA
    nA --- Vs((Excitation Vs))
    R3((R3 + Delta R)) --- nB((Node B))
    R4((R4)) --- nB
    nB --- Gnd((Ground))
    nA --- Vo1((Vo+))
    nB --- Vo2((Vo-))
    Vo1 --> Ampx((Differential Amplifier))
    Vo2 --> Ampx
    Ampx --> Out((Output Vout proportional to strain))
```

### Diagram 3: Classification of Strain Gauges (Tree Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SG[Strain Gauges] --> BG[Bonded Type]
    SG --> UG[Unbonded Type]
    BG --> BW[Wire Bonded]
    BG --> BF[Foil Bonded]
    BG --> BS[Semiconductor Bonded]
    BW --> P1[Wire: Constantan, Nichrome]
    BF --> P2[Foil: Etched pattern, photo-etched]
    BS --> P3[Si: P-type or N-type doped]
    UG --> P4[Wire stretched between anchors in load cell]
```

### Diagram 4: Working of a Bonded Foil Gauge — Cross-Sectional View (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Top layer: Protective coating] --> L2[Grid pattern of constantan foil]
    L2 --> L3[Adhesive epoxy bonding layer]
    L3 --> L4[Test specimen surface under stress]
    L4 --> L5[Strain epsilon transmitted to grid]
    L5 --> L6[Resistance changes by Delta R]
    L6 --> L7[GF multiplied by epsilon gives Delta R by R]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams are **block-level functional architecture** representations — they map interactions, signal flow, and classification hierarchy without attempting photorealistic physical drawings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define gauge factor. What is its typical value for a metallic strain gauge?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gauge factor is defined as the **ratio of fractional change in resistance to the fractional change in length (strain)** of a strain gauge.

$$\text{GF} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

For metallic strain gauges (e.g., Constantan), the piezoresistive effect is negligible, so:
$$\text{GF} \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1 + 2(0.3) = 1.6 \text{ to } 2.0$$

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Typical value with Poisson's relation (1.5).

---

**Q2. List any four different types of strain gauges.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four major types of strain gauges are:
1. **Bonded wire strain gauge**
2. **Bonded foil strain gauge**
3. **Semiconductor (bonded) strain gauge**
4. **Unbonded strain gauge**

*(Bonus mention for full marks: Piezoresistive, Capacitive, Optical — but the above four are the KTU standard list.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In gauge factor derivations, students **often forget the piezoresistive term** $\Delta \rho / \rho$ — losing 2 marks.
> - In Wheatstone bridge questions, students must **clearly state the approximation $\Delta R \ll R$** before linearizing — failing to do so costs 1 mark.
> - For semiconductor gauges, mentioning **temperature compensation** and **non-linearity** is mandatory for full marks.
> - Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** — at least 2 marks are reserved for it in 14-mark questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**(a) Derive an expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge starting from the basic resistance equation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the base equation:
$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Take logarithm and differentiate to obtain:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Use $A = \pi D^2/4$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ to show:
$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Substitute back:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Divide by strain and present final box:
$$\text{GF} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude: For metals, $\Delta \rho / \rho \approx 0$, hence $GF \approx 2$.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a bonded metal foil strain gauge.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- A **thin Constantan (Cu-Ni alloy) or Nichrome foil**, typically 3–5 µm thick, is **photo-etched** into a grid pattern.
- The grid is mounted on a **polyimide or epoxy carrier** (the backing).
- **Solder tabs** are provided at the ends for lead wire connections.
- The grid is **bonded to the test specimen** using a special adhesive (cyanoacrylate or epoxy).
- The grid has **multiple parallel runs** to maximize the active length, and **end loops** transverse to the load to minimize transverse sensitivity.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- When the test specimen is stressed, **strain is transmitted through the adhesive** to the foil grid.
- The grid deforms → its length and cross-section change → resistance changes by $\Delta R$.
- The change $\Delta R$ is detected by a **Wheatstone bridge**, where:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$$
- Higher number of grid runs → higher $\Delta R$ → higher sensitivity.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Neat labelled cross-section showing: **specimen → adhesive → foil grid → carrier backing → solder tabs → lead wires.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**(a) Explain the working of a semiconductor strain gauge. What are its advantages and disadvantages?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- The active element is a **strip of doped semiconductor** (P-type or N-type silicon, typically 0.1 mm thick).
- When stress is applied, the **band structure changes**, causing a large change in resistivity (the **piezoresistive effect**).
- This piezoresistive change **dominates** the geometric effect, giving $GF \approx 100$–$200$.
- The gauge is bonded to a flexible beam; strain on the beam deforms the semiconductor, changing its resistance.
- P-type silicon typically has $GF = 130 \pm 10$, N-type has $GF = -100$ to $-150$ (note the **negative sign** for N-type).

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Very high gauge factor (100–200)** → 50× more sensitive than metal gauges.
2. **Small size** (can be as small as 1 mm) → suitable for miniature applications.
3. **Low fatigue** in static measurements.
4. **High output signal** → less amplification required.

**Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Non-linear response** — output is not proportional to strain over a wide range.
2. **High temperature sensitivity** — requires elaborate temperature compensation.
3. **Brittle** — can crack under shock/vibration.
4. **Expensive** compared to metallic gauges.

---

**(b) With a circuit diagram, explain how a Wheatstone bridge is used with a strain gauge to measure an unknown strain.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bridge Circuit (2.5 marks):**
- A **Wheatstone bridge** consists of four resistive arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ in a diamond configuration, with excitation voltage $V_s$ across one diagonal and output $V_o$ across the other.
- The strain gauge (with active change $\Delta R$) is placed as **one arm (say $R_1$)**.
- Three other fixed resistors (or dummy gauges) complete the bridge.

**Working (3 marks):**
- At balance (no strain): $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$, hence $V_o = 0$.
- When strain is applied: $R_1$ changes to $R_1 + \Delta R$, bridge becomes unbalanced.
- The output voltage is:
$$V_o \approx \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$$
- From measured $V_o$, the unknown strain is:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{4 V_o}{V_s \cdot \text{GF}}$$

**Use of multiple active gauges (1.5 marks):**
- **Quarter bridge** — 1 active gauge: sensitivity factor = 1
- **Half bridge** — 2 active gauges (one in tension, one in compression): sensitivity factor = 2
- **Full bridge** — 4 active gauges: sensitivity factor = 4, also provides **temperature compensation**
- The dummy gauge (in a different arm) compensates for temperature-induced resistance changes.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Strain** is dimensionless: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, often expressed in microstrains ($\mu\varepsilon$).
- [x] **Gauge Factor** $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ is the figure of merit of any strain gauge.
- [x] **Expanded GF** = $(1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$ — three physical origins.
- [x] For **metals**, $GF \approx 2.0$ (geometric effect only).
- [x] For **semiconductors**, $GF \approx 100$–$200$ (piezoresistive effect dominant).
- [x] **Poisson's ratio** of gauge material typically $\nu \approx 0.3$ → $1+2\nu \approx 1.6$.
- [x] **Types**: Bonded (Wire, Foil, Semiconductor) and **Unbonded**.
- [x] **Bonded Foil gauges** use Constantan, etched photolithographically — better fatigue life than wire.
- [x] **Semiconductor gauges** are non-linear and temperature-sensitive but extremely sensitive.
- [x] **Wheatstone bridge** output: $V_o = (V_s / 4) \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$ (for single active gauge).
- [x] **Full bridge > Half bridge > Quarter bridge** in sensitivity (factor of 4 > 2 > 1).
- [x] **Dummy gauges** are used for **temperature compensation**.
- [x] **End-loops** in a foil gauge are placed **transverse** to load to minimize transverse sensitivity.
- [x] **Hooke's Law**: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ — used in load cell calculations.
- [x] **Bonded gauges** are permanently attached; **unbonded** are stretched between anchors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezo Electric Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezo Electric Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Piezoelectric Transducer** is a passive, reversible, electromechanical transduction element that converts physical quantities such as **force, pressure, acceleration, or vibration** into a measurable electrical signal (charge or voltage) by exploiting the **direct piezoelectric effect** exhibited by certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials. It is the most widely used active transducer in the measurement of dynamic mechanical quantities where high-frequency response and self-generating capability (no external excitation required) are critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Piezoelectric transducers are classified under **"Active Transducers – Energy Converters"** and form the backbone of modern accelerometer, force gauge, and dynamic pressure sensor design in industrial process and laboratory instrumentation.

The phenomenon was discovered by **Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880**. The word *piezo* is derived from the Greek word *"piezein"*, meaning **"to press"** — literally, *electricity produced by pressing*.

### 1.1.1 The Two Directional Effects

| Effect | Stimulus | Response | Symbol | Mode of Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** | Mechanical stress (force) | Electrical charge on the crystal faces | $D = d \cdot T$ | **Sensor / Transducer Mode** |
| **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect** | Applied electric field | Mechanical strain (deformation) | $S = d \cdot E$ | **Actuator / Ultrasonic Source Mode** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same constant **$d$** (piezoelectric charge/strain constant) links both effects. This symmetry is the basis of using the same crystal as a transmitter (in ultrasonic flowmeters) and receiver (in ultrasonic level sensors).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Pressure Battery"

Imagine a small **invisible rechargeable battery** that produces a voltage *only* when you squeeze it. The harder you press, the larger the voltage it outputs. The instant you release the pressure, the voltage disappears.

- **Squeeze (Force) → Voltage spike** — this is the direct effect.
- **Apply a voltage from outside → the crystal physically bends or elongates** — this is the converse effect, like a microscopic "muscle" that responds to electricity.
- The crystal is a **two-way energy bridge** between the mechanical and electrical worlds.

Now imagine millions of microscopic dipoles inside the crystal that are normally **randomly oriented** (no net charge on the surface). When you press the crystal, these dipoles **align in a preferential direction**, creating positive charge on one face and negative charge on the other. This is the **microscopic origin** of the piezoelectric effect.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world instances:** the clicker inside a gas stove lighter, the timekeeping quartz crystal inside a digital watch, the pickup in an electric guitar, and the airbag crash sensor in a car — **all** are piezoelectric devices.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants govern the design and analysis of piezoelectric transducers and appear frequently in KTU problems:

| Constant | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Piezoelectric Charge Constant | $d$ | $\text{C/N}$ (Coulomb per Newton) | $2 \times 10^{-12}$ (Quartz) to $600 \times 10^{-12}$ (PZT) |
| Piezoelectric Voltage Constant | $g$ | $\text{V·m/N}$ | $0.05$ (Quartz) to $0.04$ (PZT — *lower because high d*) |
| Permittivity (free space) | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m | Universal constant |
| Relative Permittivity | $\varepsilon_r$ | dimensionless | $4.5$ (Quartz) to $1500$ (PZT-5H) |
| Density of Quartz | $\rho$ | $2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Material property |
| Young's Modulus of Quartz | $Y$ | $8.0 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^2$ | Material property |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: confusing **$d$ (charge constant)** with **$g$ (voltage constant)**. They are related by $g = d / (\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r)$ but used in *different transducer sizing problems*. Read the question carefully — *charge mode* asks for $d$, *voltage mode* asks for $g$.

---

## 1.4 Why Piezoelectric Transducers Dominate Industrial Instrumentation

The piezoelectric principle is **irreplaceable** in the following engineering scenarios:

- **High-frequency vibration** (up to 100 kHz and beyond) — strain gauges cannot follow this.
- **Self-generating output** — no external bridge excitation needed (unlike strain gauges).
- **Extremely small size** — a few millimetres in MEMS accelerometers.
- **Wide dynamic range** — from $\mu g$ accelerations to high-shock applications.
- **Cryogenic to high temperature** operation (quartz to $\approx 573^{\circ}\text{C}$ — the Curie point).

These unique features will be justified in detail in SECTION 2 using mathematical models.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics — Why Crystals Generate Charge Under Stress

A crystal exhibits the piezoelectric effect **only** if its unit cell is **non-centrosymmetric** (lacks a centre of symmetry). In such crystals, the positive and negative charge centres are **coincident at rest**. When mechanical stress is applied, the lattice distorts and the **centres of charge separate**, producing a net electric dipole moment per unit volume — this is the **polarisation vector** $P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Constraint:** Out of the 32 crystal classes, only **20 exhibit the piezoelectric effect**. The 12 centrosymmetric classes (including NaCl, KCl, and most metals) **cannot** show piezoelectricity.

### 2.1.1 Constitutive Equations (KTU Examination Favourite)

The fundamental constitutive relations in matrix form (linear piezoelectricity) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_i &= d_{ij} \cdot T_j + \varepsilon^{T}_{ik} \cdot E_k \quad \text{(Direct effect — sensing)} \\
S_j &= s^{E}_{jl} \cdot T_l + d_{kj} \cdot E_k \quad \text{(Converse effect — actuation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $D_i$ = Electric displacement ($\text{C/m}^2$)
- $T_j$ = Mechanical stress ($\text{N/m}^2$)
- $E_k$ = Electric field ($\text{V/m}$)
- $S_j$ = Mechanical strain (dimensionless)
- $d_{ij}$ = **Piezoelectric charge constant matrix** ($\text{C/N}$)
- $s^E$ = Elastic compliance at constant $E$
- $\varepsilon^T$ = Permittivity at constant $T$

For a **1D analysis** (most KTU problems), the system reduces to the **two scalar equations** in SECTION 1.

---

## 2.2 Operating Modes of a Piezoelectric Element

A piezoelectric disc/plate can be excited in several modes, each producing a different sensitivity:

| Mode | Direction of Force | Direction of Poling | Output Direction | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Thickness Mode (d₃₃)** | Perpendicular to electrodes (along Z) | Along Z | Perpendicular to electrodes | **Pressure sensors, force sensors** |
| **Length Mode (d₃₁)** | Perpendicular to electrodes | Along Z (poling) | Along the length (X or Y) | **Accelerometers, strain sensing** |
| **Shear Mode (d₁₅)** | Shear stress | Perpendicular to Z | Perpendicular to poling | **High-temp accelerometers** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$d_{33}$ mode** is the most common in KTU numerical problems because it directly links applied compressive force to surface charge, giving a simple scalar relationship $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$.

---

## 2.3 The Two Output Forms — Charge Mode vs. Voltage Mode

A piezoelectric crystal connected to electrodes behaves as a **capacitor** $C$ with a **charge source** $Q$ in parallel. The way the load is connected determines the operation:

### Case A — Voltage Mode Output
The crystal faces act like a charged capacitor of capacitance $C$:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}
$$

This requires a **high-input-impedance amplifier** (e.g., FET-input op-amp, MOSFET source follower) to prevent the charge from leaking.

### Case B — Charge Mode Output
The charge itself is the output, conditioned by a **charge amplifier** (op-amp with feedback capacitor $C_f$):

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{-Q}{C_f} = \frac{-d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}
$$

This is **insensitive to cable capacitance** and is the **preferred industrial standard** (e.g., Kistler, PCB Piezotronics, Brüel & Kjær accelerometers).

---

## 2.4 Natural Frequency and Frequency Response

The piezoelectric element is a **mass-spring system** with a natural frequency that sets the **upper usable limit**. For a disc of thickness $t$ vibrating in the thickness mode:

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{c}{\rho}} = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}
$$

Where:
- $c$ or $Y$ = elastic stiffness / Young's modulus ($\text{N/m}^2$)
- $\rho$ = density of the crystal ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $t$ = thickness (m)

For **quartz**: $Y = 8.0 \times 10^{10}\text{ N/m}^2$, $\rho = 2650\text{ kg/m}^3$

$$
c_{\text{quartz}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.0 \times 10^{10}}{2650}} \approx 5494 \text{ m/s}
$$

So a 1 mm thick quartz disc has $f_n \approx 2.7$ MHz — **a very high resonance**, justifying its use in ultrasonic transducers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational rule:** A piezoelectric transducer must be operated at a frequency **well below** $f_n$ (typically $f < 0.2 f_n$) for **flat (linear) response**. Near or above $f_n$, resonance amplifies the output but introduces severe non-linearity.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master reference table** for the entire topic. Memorize the relationships, not just the symbols.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Generated Charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $\text{C}$ (Coulomb) | Direct piezoelectric effect |
| 2 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot t \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$ | $\text{V}$ | Voltage mode output |
| 3 | Charge Sensitivity | $K_q = \dfrac{Q}{F} = d \cdot A$ | $\text{C/N}$ (or pC/N) | Output per unit force |
| 4 | Voltage Sensitivity | $K_v = \dfrac{V}{F} = \dfrac{g \cdot t}{A} = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$ | $\text{V/N}$ | Output per unit force |
| 5 | Piezo Coupling Constant | $g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$ | $\text{V·m/N}$ | Material figure of merit |
| 6 | Crystal Capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ | $\text{F}$ (Farad) | Self-capacitance |
| 7 | Natural Frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\dfrac{Y}{\rho}}$ | $\text{Hz}$ | Upper frequency limit |
| 8 | Strain Output | $S = d_{ij} \cdot E$ | dimensionless | Converse effect |
| 9 | Static Response | $Q$ leaks via $R_L$ with $\tau = R_L C$ | — | Why **static** measurements fail |
| 10 | Charge Amplifier Output | $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{-Q}{C_f}$ | $\text{V}$ | Charge-mode conditioning |

> [!TIP]
> A useful **identity** for the two sensitivities:
> $$K_v = \frac{K_q}{C} = \frac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$
> This is a quick verification step for any numerical problem.

---

## 2.6 Why Quartz Cannot Measure Static (DC) Quantities

The piezoelectric charge has nowhere to "stay" in steady state — it **leaks** through the input resistance $R_L$ of the connected amplifier and through the internal resistance of the crystal. The **time constant** is:

$$
\tau = R_L \cdot C
$$

If $\tau$ is very small (typical $\tau \approx 0.1$ to $10$ seconds for industrial accelerometers), then the charge dissipates before being measured. This is the **fundamental limitation** of piezoelectric transducers: they are **AC / dynamic only**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU trap:** If a question asks "can a piezoelectric transducer measure static pressure?", the answer is **NO** (without special charge-mode stabilisation circuits). The output decays exponentially. Mention this limitation explicitly in long-answer questions for full marks.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Vibration Monitoring:** Online condition monitoring of motors, pumps, turbines, gearboxes using piezoelectric accelerometers.
- **Ultrasonic NDT:** Non-destructive testing of welds, castings, and composite structures (thickness mode at MHz frequencies).
- **Ultrasonic Flowmeters / Level Meters:** Converse effect generates ultrasonic pulses; direct effect receives echoes.
- **Medical Ultrasound (Sonography):** Both effects used simultaneously in a phased-array probe.
- **Automotive Airbags:** Crash accelerometers trigger inflation within 15 ms using $d_{33}$ PZT elements.
- **Piezoelectric Ignition Systems:** Gas stoves, lighters — a mechanical hammer strike produces a high-voltage spark (tens of kV).
- **Microbalances and AFM:** Quartz Crystal Microbalances (QCM) detect nanogram mass changes.
- **Energy Harvesting:** Floors, shoes, and bridges harvest vibration energy using PZT patches.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Sensitivity of a Piezoelectric Transducer in d₃₃ Mode

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A piezoelectric disc of:
- Thickness $= t$
- Electrode area $= A$
- Piezoelectric charge constant $= d_{33}$
- Relative permittivity $= \varepsilon_r$
- Free-space permittivity $= \varepsilon_0$

has a compressive force $F$ applied along the poling (Z) axis. Derive the **charge sensitivity** and **voltage sensitivity**.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Mechanical stress on the disc face**

$$
T = \frac{F}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Direct piezoelectric constitutive relation (1D scalar form)**

The electric displacement produced in the poling direction is:

$$
D = d_{33} \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Convert electric displacement to total charge**

By Gauss's law, the charge on each electrode equals the electric displacement integrated over the area:

$$
Q = D \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot T \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot \left(\frac{F}{A}\right) \cdot A
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = d_{33} \cdot F}
$$

This is the **fundamental result**. Notice that the electrode area cancels out — the charge is **independent of disc size** (a key insight often tested in KTU).

**Step 4 — Charge sensitivity (coulombs per newton)**

$$
K_q = \frac{Q}{F} = d_{33} \quad \text{units: C/N}
$$

**Step 5 — Capacitance of the disc**

The disc forms a parallel-plate capacitor with plate separation $t$ and dielectric $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}
$$

**Step 6 — Open-circuit voltage (voltage mode)**

$$
V_{\text{oc}} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{oc}} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}
$$

**Step 7 — Voltage sensitivity (volts per newton)**

$$
K_v = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}}{F} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = g_{33} \cdot \frac{t}{A}
$$

Where the **piezoelectric voltage constant** is:

$$
g_{33} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}
$$

**Step 8 — Final cross-check identity**

$$
\boxed{K_v = \frac{K_q}{C}}
$$

This is an important identity: voltage sensitivity = charge sensitivity ÷ self-capacitance.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Numerical Practice for KTU

### Problem Statement (KTU Model Question)

A piezoelectric transducer uses a **PZT-5A** crystal disc with the following specifications:
- Diameter $= 10$ mm → $A = \pi (5\times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$
- Thickness $t = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $d_{33} = 374 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- $\varepsilon_r = 1700$
- Applied force $F = 50$ N

Calculate:
1. Generated charge $Q$
2. Crystal capacitance $C$
3. Open-circuit voltage $V_{\text{oc}}$
4. Charge sensitivity $K_q$
5. Voltage sensitivity $K_v$

### Complete Solution

**Part (1) — Generated Charge**

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F = (374 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 50
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 1.87 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C} = 18.7 \text{ nC}}
$$

**Part (2) — Crystal Capacitance**

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1700)(7.854 \times 10^{-5})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$
(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1700) = 1.5052 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
(1.5052 \times 10^{-8})(7.854 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.1822 \times 10^{-12}
$$

Dividing by $t$:

$$
C = \frac{1.1822 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.911 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 591 \text{ pF}}
$$

**Part (3) — Open-Circuit Voltage**

$$
V_{\text{oc}} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{1.87 \times 10^{-8}}{5.911 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{oc}} \approx 31.6 \text{ V}}
$$

**Part (4) — Charge Sensitivity**

$$
K_q = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{1.87 \times 10^{-8}}{50} = 3.74 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C/N}
$$

$$
\boxed{K_q = 374 \text{ pC/N}}
$$

(This confirms $K_q = d_{33}$ — the identity from Step 4.)

**Part (5) — Voltage Sensitivity**

$$
K_v = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}}{F} = \frac{31.6}{50} = 0.632 \text{ V/N}
$$

$$
\boxed{K_v \approx 0.632 \text{ V/N}}
$$

**Cross-verification using the identity:**

$$
K_v = \frac{K_q}{C} = \frac{3.74 \times 10^{-10}}{5.911 \times 10^{-10}} = 0.633 \text{ V/N} \quad \checkmark
$$

The two results match — this is the **KTU Examiner's verification step** that earns full marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Natural Frequency of a Piezoelectric Disc

### Setup
A disc of thickness $t$ with Young's modulus $Y$ and density $\rho$ vibrates in its thickness (longitudinal) mode.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Speed of sound in the crystal medium**

The longitudinal wave speed in a solid is:

$$
c = \sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}
$$

For quartz ($Y = 8.0 \times 10^{10}$ N/m², $\rho = 2650$ kg/m³):

$$
c = \sqrt{\frac{8.0 \times 10^{10}}{2650}} = \sqrt{3.019 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5494 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Wavelength for fundamental thickness mode**

The fundamental resonance occurs when the disc thickness equals **half the wavelength**:

$$
t = \frac{\lambda}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 2t
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency from wave equation**

$$
f = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{c}{2t} = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_n = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for 2 mm thick quartz**

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 5494 = \frac{5494}{4 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_n \approx 1.374 \text{ MHz}}
$$

This **very high resonance** is the reason quartz is the material of choice for ultrasonic probes (typically operated at 1–10 MHz).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Piezoelectric Materials (KTU 2-Mark Tables)

| Property | Quartz | Rochelle Salt | PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) | PVDF (Polymer) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d_{33}$ (pC/N) | $2.3$ | $480$ | $300$–$600$ | $33$ |
| $g_{33}$ (mV·m/N) | $58$ | $95$ | $25$–$40$ | $380$ |
| $\varepsilon_r$ | $4.5$ | $500$ | $1000$–$2000$ | $12$ |
| Curie Temp (°C) | $573$ | $45$ | $200$–$350$ | $100$ |
| Moisture Sensitivity | None | **High** | Low | None |
| Mechanical Q | Very high | Low | Medium | Very low |
| Cost | Medium | Low | Low | Low |
| Best Application | **Stable frequency standard**, high-temp | Low-frequency microphones | **General-purpose accelerometer / actuator** | Flexible large-area sensors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off rule for KTU:** High $d$ (e.g., PZT) → high charge output but high capacitance → lower voltage output. High $g$ (e.g., PVDF) → high voltage but low charge. Choose $d$ for charge-mode, $g$ for voltage-mode applications.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Time Constant and Why Static Measurement Fails

The piezoelectric element with capacitance $C$ discharges through the amplifier input resistance $R_L$ and the leakage resistance $R_p$ of the crystal itself.

**Step 1 — Equivalent parallel resistance**

$$
R_{\text{eq}} = R_L \parallel R_p
$$

**Step 2 — Discharge time constant**

$$
\tau = R_{\text{eq}} \cdot C
$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage under step force $F_0$ applied at $t=0$**

$$
V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

**Step 4 — Lower usable frequency (3 dB point)**

$$
f_{\text{low}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering implication:** For an accelerometer with $C = 1000$ pF and $R_{\text{eq}} = 10^{10}$ Ω, we get $\tau = 10$ s, so the lower usable frequency is about $0.016$ Hz. Below this, the signal decays before it can be measured. This is why **piezoelectric accelerometers are unsuitable for true DC (static) accelerations** like the Earth's gravity (1 g) — they can only measure **dynamic** accelerations above $\approx 0.1$ Hz.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Piezoelectric Transducer Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Input Force or Pressure] --> B[Piezoelectric Crystal Element]
    B --> C[Generated Surface Charge Q and Voltage V]
    C --> D[Equivalent Circuit: Current Source I parallel Capacitance C and Resistance R]
    D --> E{Signal Conditioning Mode}
    E -- Voltage Mode --> F[High Impedance FET Amplifier]
    E -- Charge Mode --> G[Charge Amplifier with Op Amp and C feedback]
    F --> H[Output Signal in Volts to Data Acquisition]
    G --> H[Output Signal in Volts to Data Acquisition]
    H --> I[Filtering and Display Unit]
    I --> J[Measured Quantity on PC or Recorder]
    K[DC Bias Supply optional] --> F
    K --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the **left** with the physical input (force / pressure / vibration), follow the **top branch** through the crystal and equivalent circuit, and **diverge** at the diamond based on whether the system uses voltage-mode (FET) or charge-mode (op-amp) conditioning. Both paths converge at the data acquisition stage.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Crystal Construction and Poling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Piezoelectric Material Quartz or PZT] --> B[Cutting into Disc or Plate Geometry]
    B --> C[Electrodes Deposited on Opposite Faces]
    C --> D[Poling: Apply High DC Field above Curie Temperature]
    D --> E[Cool Down while Maintaining Field]
    E --> F[Permanent Dipole Alignment Achieved]
    F --> G[Poled Piezoelectric Element Ready for Use]
    G --> H[Electrodes Connected to Output Leads]
    H --> I[Sensor Assembly Encapsulated with Seismic Mass for Accelerometer]
    H --> J[Sensor Assembly Mounted on Diaphragm for Pressure Sensor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** **Poling** is the process of aligning the random ferroelectric domains in PZT (and other polycrystalline ceramics) by applying a strong DC electric field ($\approx 2$–$5$ kV/mm) at elevated temperature. Without poling, the polycrystalline ceramic would have **zero net piezoelectric activity** because domains cancel out. Single-crystal quartz is naturally piezoelectric and **does not require poling**.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Direct vs. Converse Piezoelectric Effect

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Direct
        A1[Mechanical Force F applied] --> A2[Crystal Lattice Distorts]
        A2 --> A3[Dipoles Align and Separate]
        A3 --> A4[Net Surface Charge Q generated]
        A4 --> A5[Voltage V appears across Electrodes]
    end
    subgraph Converse
        B1[External Electric Field E applied] --> B2[Crystal Lattice Stretches or Contracts]
        B2 --> B3[Mechanical Strain S produced]
        B3 --> B4[Physical Displacement of Crystal Faces]
    end
    A5 -.same material.-> B1
    B4 -.same material.-> A1
```

---

## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Models of a Piezoelectric Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Voltage Source Model
        V1[Voltage Source V with Series Resistance Rs] --> C1[Crystal Capacitance C]
    end
    subgraph Charge Source Model
        Q1[Current Source I with Parallel Resistance Rp] --> C2[Crystal Capacitance C]
    end
    subgraph Loaded Model
        L1[Source Side] --> CA[Cable Capacitance Ccable]
        CA --> L2[Load Amplifier Input Resistance RL and Capacitance Cin]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Two equivalent representations** are used in KTU problems:
> 1. **Voltage-source model** — preferred when the **open-circuit voltage** is the calculated output (used in Section 3.2).
> 2. **Charge-source model** — preferred for **charge amplifiers** and **dynamic analysis** (where current $i = dQ/dt$ is the natural variable).
>
> Both are mathematically equivalent; the choice depends on what the problem asks.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Accelerometer Cross-Section (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Base of Accelerometer attached to Vibrating Structure] --> B[Piezoelectric Disc Stack]
    B --> C[Seismic Mass M on Top of Crystal]
    D[Inertial Force F equals M times acceleration a] --> C
    C --> E[Compressive Force on Piezo Disc]
    E --> F[Charge Q generated proportional to acceleration]
    F --> G[Charge Amplifier Output Vout]
    H[Housing with Preload Bolt maintains Contact] --> B
    I[Case and Shielding for EMI Protection] --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **seismic mass** $M$ converts acceleration $a$ into an inertial force $F = M \cdot a$ that compresses the piezoelectric disc. This is the **standard operating principle of every industrial piezoelectric accelerometer** (Kistler, PCB, Dytran, Brüel & Kjær).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part A questions are compulsory, each carrying 3 marks. They test direct recall, definitions, and short reasoning. Answers should be 4–6 lines.**

---

### Question A1: Define the piezoelectric effect. List any four commonly used piezoelectric materials.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals to generate an **electric charge** on their surfaces when subjected to **mechanical stress** (direct effect), and conversely to undergo **mechanical deformation** when an **electric field** is applied (converse effect).

Four commonly used piezoelectric materials are:
1. **Quartz (SiO₂)** — single crystal, high stability, used in resonators and high-temperature sensors.
2. **Rochelle Salt (NaKC₄H₄O₆·4H₂O)** — very high $d$ constant, but moisture-sensitive.
3. **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** — most widely used polycrystalline ceramic, high sensitivity.
4. **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)** — flexible polymer, used in large-area and biomedical sensors.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for correctly identifying their application context.)*

---

### Question A2: Why is a piezoelectric transducer unsuitable for measuring static pressure?
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

A piezoelectric transducer produces charge only when the input (force/pressure) **changes**. The generated charge leaks away through:
- The **internal leakage resistance** of the crystal,
- The **input resistance** of the connected amplifier.

The discharge time constant is $\tau = R_{\text{eq}} \cdot C$, which is typically **0.1 to 10 seconds**. For a truly static (DC) pressure, the charge dissipates exponentially and the output voltage **decays to zero**. Therefore, the transducer can measure only **dynamic (time-varying) pressures** above its lower cutoff frequency $f_{\text{low}} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.

*(1 mark for identifying dynamic-only nature, 1 mark for time constant / decay, 1 mark for lower cutoff frequency discussion.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice is mandatory.** Below are **TWO** alternative Part B questions, of which the student answers one.

---

## ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

### Part (a) — Derive the expressions for charge and voltage sensitivity of a piezoelectric transducer. **(7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Description**
Consider a piezoelectric disc of:
- Electrode area $A$, thickness $t$
- Piezoelectric charge constant $d$
- Relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$

A force $F$ is applied perpendicular to the electrodes (thickness mode, $d_{33}$).

**Step 2 — Mechanical Stress**

$$
T = \frac{F}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Direct Piezoelectric Effect**

The electric displacement produced along the poling axis is:

$$
D = d \cdot T
$$

**Step 4 — Total Charge on Electrodes**

By Gauss's law, the total surface charge is the displacement times the area:

$$
Q = D \cdot A = d \cdot \left(\frac{F}{A}\right) \cdot A
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = d \cdot F}
$$

**Step 5 — Charge Sensitivity**

$$
\boxed{K_q = \frac{Q}{F} = d \quad \text{(C/N)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Crystal Capacitance**

The disc behaves as a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}
$$

**Step 7 — Open-Circuit Voltage**

$$
V_{\text{oc}} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{d \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}
$$

**Step 8 — Voltage Sensitivity**

$$
\boxed{K_v = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}}{F} = \frac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = g \cdot \frac{t}{A}}
$$

Where $g = d/(\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r)$ is the **piezoelectric voltage constant** (V·m/N).

**Step 9 — Identity Check**

$$
K_v = \frac{K_q}{C}
$$

**Conclusion:** A piezoelectric transducer has **two output forms** — charge (independent of geometry) and voltage (geometry-dependent via $t/A$). The choice of material ($d$, $\varepsilon_r$) and disc dimensions together determine the sensitivity for a given design.

*(1 mark setup, 2 marks for $Q = dF$ derivation, 2 marks for $V_{\text{oc}}$ derivation, 1 mark for sensitivity definitions, 1 mark for identity check.)*

---

### Part (b) — Draw and explain the equivalent circuit of a piezoelectric transducer. Discuss the difference between voltage-mode and charge-mode signal conditioning. **(7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Equivalent Circuit Diagram (text representation):**

```
Voltage Source Model:
       Rs
  o---/\/\/---+--------o
              |
             (V)
              |
              |
  o-----------+--------o
              =
             === C
              =
              |
  o-----------+--------o
              |
             ___ Rp
              _
  o-----------+--------o
```

In the **charge-source model**, the voltage source $V$ is replaced by a current source $i = dQ/dt$ in parallel with the capacitance $C$ and leakage resistance $R_p$ of the crystal.

**Explanation of Elements:**

- **Source ($V$ or $i$):** Represents the piezoelectric transduction — depends on applied force or its time derivative.
- **$C$ — Self-capacitance:** $\;C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t$ — the disc acts as a capacitor.
- **$R_p$ — Internal Leakage Resistance:** Very high ($\approx 10^{10}$–$10^{14}$ Ω) for quartz; determines the low-frequency cutoff.
- **$R_s$ — Series Resistance:** Of electrodes and connections (usually negligible).

**Voltage-Mode Conditioning:**
- The output voltage $V = Q/C$ is fed directly to a **high-input-impedance amplifier** (FET-input op-amp).
- **Limitation:** Cable capacitance adds in parallel with $C$, changing the voltage output — hence cable length affects calibration.
- Used in low-cost, short-cable applications.

**Charge-Mode Conditioning:**
- The charge $Q$ is converted to voltage by an **op-amp integrator** with feedback capacitor $C_f$.
- **Output:** $V_{\text{out}} = -Q/C_f = -dF/C_f$ (independent of $C$, hence independent of cable length).
- **Limitation:** Requires high-quality op-amp with very low input bias current and a high-value feedback resistor $R_f$ to prevent saturation.
- **Industry standard** for accelerometers and pressure sensors.

| Aspect | Voltage Mode | Charge Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cable capacitance effect | Significant | Negligible |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Sensitivity stability | Lower (cable-dependent) | Higher (set by $C_f$) |
| Amplifier type | FET source follower | Op-amp integrator |
| Application | Laboratory, short cables | Industrial, long cables |

*(2 marks equivalent circuit + element explanation, 2 marks voltage mode discussion, 2 marks charge mode discussion, 1 mark comparison table.)*

---

## ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### Part (a) — Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric transducer with a neat diagram. Discuss thickness, length, and shear modes. **(7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

A piezoelectric transducer operates on the **direct piezoelectric effect** discovered by Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880. When a mechanical force (stress) is applied to certain anisotropic crystals (those lacking a centre of symmetry), the positive and negative charge centres within the unit cell **displace relative to each other**, producing a net **polarisation** and consequently a measurable **electric charge** on the crystal faces.

**Schematic (textual):**

```
        | F (force) downward
        v
    +-----------+
    | Electrode |   <-- top electrode
    +-----------+
    |  PZT or   |
    |  Quartz   |   <-- piezoelectric disc
    |  Crystal  |
    +-----------+
    | Electrode |   <-- bottom electrode
    +-----------+
        |
        +--- to amplifier input
        |
    -V (negative terminal)
```

**Modes of Operation:**

**1. Thickness Mode ($d_{33}$ mode):**
- Force applied **perpendicular** to the electrodes (along the poling direction).
- Charge generated on the electrode faces.
- $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$
- **Use:** Pressure sensors, force sensors, ultrasonic thickness-mode transducers.

**2. Length Mode ($d_{31}$ mode):**
- Force applied **perpendicular** to the poling direction (along the long axis of a bar).
- Charge generated on faces perpendicular to the poling direction.
- $Q = d_{31} \cdot F \cdot (\text{geometric factor})$
- **Use:** Accelerometers with bending elements, flexure sensors.

**3. Shear Mode ($d_{15}$ mode):**
- Shear stress applied parallel to the electrode faces.
- Charge generated on the same electrode faces due to shear deformation.
- **Use:** High-temperature accelerometers, specialised shear-stress sensors.

**Mode Selection Criteria:**

- **$d_{33}$** is the largest constant in PZT → preferred for maximum sensitivity.
- **$d_{15}$** offers better temperature stability for industrial accelerometers.
- **$d_{31}$** is geometrically constrained — used in bimorph benders (two crystals glued back-to-back) for actuators.

*(2 marks principle + diagram, 3 marks thickness mode, 1 mark length mode, 1 mark shear mode.)*

---

### Part (b) — Discuss the various piezoelectric materials used in transducers. Compare natural and synthetic piezoelectric materials. **(7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Piezoelectric Materials:**

**A. Natural (Single-Crystal) Materials:**

1. **Quartz (SiO₂):**
   - **$d_{33}$ ≈ 2.3 pC/N** — relatively low sensitivity.
   - Extremely high **mechanical Q** (≈ 10⁶) and excellent **temperature stability** (Curie point 573 °C).
   - **Zero ageing**, no moisture sensitivity.
   - **Applications:** Precision frequency standards (watches, oscillators), high-temperature accelerometers, ultrasonic NDT probes.

2. **Rochelle Salt (Sodium Potassium Tartrate Tetrahydrate):**
   - **$d_{33}$ ≈ 480 pC/N** — extremely high sensitivity.
   - Very **low Curie temperature (45 °C)** → fails above room temperature.
   - **Highly moisture-sensitive** — must be sealed.
   - **Applications:** Microphones, low-frequency sound pick-ups, gramophone pickups (historical).

3. **Tourmaline:**
   - High sensitivity, durable, expensive.
   - Used in pressure gauges for deep-sea hydrophones.

**B. Synthetic (Polycrystalline Ceramic) Materials:**

1. **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) — BaTiO₃ family:**
   - **$d_{33}$ ≈ 300–600 pC/N** — highest among practical materials.
   - Curie temperature 200–350 °C.
   - **Poling required** (random domains in as-sintered state).
   - **Applications:** Universal accelerometer, piezo buzzer, fuel injector actuators, ultrasonic cleaning tanks.

2. **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride) Polymer:**
   - **$d_{33}$ ≈ 33 pC/N** — moderate, but very high **$g_{33}$ (380 mV·m/N)**.
   - **Flexible and large-area** — can be formed into sheets.
   - **Applications:** Large-area impact sensors, medical pressure pads, flexible keyboards, energy harvesting.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Natural (Quartz) | Synthetic (PZT) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensitivity ($d$) | Low | Very high |
| Cost | Moderate | Low |
| Poling required | No (already poled as a single crystal) | Yes |
| Curie point | High (573 °C) | Medium (200–350 °C) |
| Temperature range | Wide | Limited |
| Mechanical Q | Very high | Medium |
| Aging | Negligible | Slight |
| Best for | Stable reference, high temp | General-purpose sensing |

**Conclusion:** Natural materials (quartz) dominate when **stability, frequency precision, and high temperature** are critical. Synthetic ceramics (PZT) dominate when **high sensitivity, low cost, and mass production** are required.

*(2 marks classification, 2 marks natural materials, 2 marks synthetic materials, 1 mark comparison table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the static limitation:** If asked "list the disadvantages," **always** mention the inability to measure static (DC) quantities with explanation of time constant decay.
> 2. **Mixing $d$ and $g$:** Don't write "$d$ is the voltage constant" — it is the **charge constant**. The **voltage constant is $g$**.
> 3. **Missing units in numerical problems:** KTU examiners **deduct 1 full mark** if final units are omitted. Always state "C/N", "V/N", "F", "Hz" explicitly.
> 4. **Drawing a wrong equivalent circuit:** Many students draw the resistor in *series* with the capacitance. The correct **leakage resistor $R_p$ is in PARALLEL** with $C$.
> 5. **Conflating direct and converse effects:** Be very clear in long answers which effect (direct = sensor, converse = actuator) is being discussed.
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" of poling:** If PZT is mentioned, you **must** explain that polycrystalline ceramics require poling because as-sintered domains are randomly oriented. Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this list for last-minute revision before the KTU examination.**

- [ ] **Definition:** Piezoelectric effect = generation of electric charge on applying mechanical stress (and vice versa).
- [ ] **Discovered by:** Pierre and Jacques Curie, 1880.
- [ ] **"Piezo" origin:** Greek *"piezein"* = to press.
- [ ] **Crystal symmetry rule:** Only **20 of 32 crystal classes** are piezoelectric (those without a centre of symmetry).
- [ ] **Direct effect formula:** $Q = d \cdot F$.
- [ ] **Converse effect formula:** $S = d \cdot E$ (same $d$ for both).
- [ ] **Open-circuit voltage:** $V = \dfrac{d \cdot t \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$.
- [ ] **Charge sensitivity:** $K_q = d$ (independent of disc geometry).
- [ ] **Voltage sensitivity:** $K_v = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = g \cdot t/A$.
- [ ] **Piezo voltage constant:** $g = d/(\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r)$.
- [ ] **Self-capacitance:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t$.
- [ ] **Useful identity:** $K_v = K_q / C$.
- [ ] **Natural frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{Y/\rho}$ — sets the upper frequency limit.
- [ ] **Operating frequency rule:** Always operate at $f < 0.2 f_n$ for flat response.
- [ ] **Lower cutoff:** $f_{\text{low}} = 1/(2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C)$ — sets the **static limitation**.
- [ ] **Static measurement:** **NOT possible** without special charge-mode stabilisation; the output decays exponentially.
- [ ] **Time constant:** $\tau = R_{\text{eq}} \cdot C$ — typical 0.1 to 10 s for industrial sensors.
- [ ] **Modes of operation:** $d_{33}$ (thickness), $d_{31}$ (length/bending), $d_{15}$ (shear).
- [ ] **Material $d_{33}$ values:** Quartz ≈ 2.3; PZT-5A ≈ 374; PZT-5H ≈ 600; PVDF ≈ 33 (all in pC/N).
- [ ] **Quartz vs PZT:** Quartz → high stability, high Q, high temperature; PZT → high sensitivity, low cost, requires poling.
- [ ] **Poling is required** for polycrystalline ceramics (PZT, BaTiO₃) — natural single crystals (quartz) are intrinsically piezoelectric.
- [ ] **Two equivalent circuits:** Voltage-source model ($V$ in series with $R_s$, then $C \parallel R_p$) and Current-source model ($i$ in parallel with $C$ and $R_p$).
- [ ] **Voltage mode vs charge mode:** Voltage mode = cable capacitance affects output; Charge mode = cable capacitance **does NOT** affect output (industry standard).
- [ ] **Charge amplifier output:** $V_{\text{out}} = -Q/C_f$.
- [ ] **Equivalent circuit leakage resistor** $R_p$ is in **parallel** with $C$, not in series.
- [ ] **Major applications:** Accelerometer, force gauge, dynamic pressure sensor, ultrasonic NDT, microphone, gas igniter, ultrasonic flowmeter, medical ultrasound, energy harvester, airbag sensor, QCM microbalance, piezo buzzer.
- [ ] **Curie temperature:** The temperature above which the crystal **loses** its piezoelectric property permanently. For PZT-5A ≈ 350 °C; for Rochelle salt ≈ 45 °C (very low — a major limitation).
- [ ] **Advantage:** Self-generating (no excitation needed), high frequency response, wide dynamic range, small size, rugged.
- [ ] **Disadvantage:** Cannot measure static quantities, high-impedance output (susceptible to noise), some materials (Rochelle salt) are moisture- and temperature-sensitive.
- [ ] **KTU favourite question styles:** (1) Derive $Q = dF$ and $V = gtF/A$. (2) Numerical with $d$, $A$, $t$, $F$ given. (3) Compare voltage vs charge mode. (4) List materials with $d$ values. (5) Why no static measurement. (6) Modes of operation.
- [ ] **Always mention** in long answers: **self-generating nature, AC-only, equivalent circuit, poling for ceramics, and at least 2 applications.**

---
**End of Module 2 — Piezo Electric Type Notes (PCAET602 - Industrial Instrumentation, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potentiometric Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Potentiometric Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Potentiometric Transducer** (also called a **Resistive Position Sensor** or simply a **Pot**) is a passive, variable-resistance electro-mechanical transducer that converts linear or angular mechanical displacement into a proportional change in electrical resistance, which is then measured as an output voltage using a voltage-divider excitation circuit. It belongs to the family of **resistive transducers** in industrial instrumentation and is one of the most widely used, simplest, and lowest-cost displacement-measuring elements in process control, automation, and laboratory instrumentation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2 of PCAET602, potentiometric transducers are studied under *Passive Electrical Transducers – Resistive Type*. The examiner expects students to know construction, types (linear, rotary, helical), the voltage-divider principle, loading error, resolution, and industrial applications.

Mathematically, for an ideal linear potentiometer of total resistance $R_T$ with a wiper displaced by a distance $x$ from one end across a total active length $L$, the resistance tapped by the wiper is:

$$R_x = R_T \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a long horizontal pipe filled with sponge**. As you slide your finger along the pipe, the amount of sponge the water must pass through changes — the further your finger is from the source, the more resistance to flow. A potentiometer works on exactly this idea, but with **electricity instead of water** and a **sliding metal contact instead of a finger**.

In practical terms:
- A **uniform resistance track** (often a thin carbon film, cermet, or tightly wound resistance wire) acts like the sponge-filled pipe.
- A **movable metal wiper (slider)** taps off a portion of the track, exactly like your finger sliding along the pipe.
- A **steady DC excitation voltage** is applied across the two ends of the track, like the water pressure at the pipe's entry.
- The **voltage between the wiper and one terminal** is your output signal — and because the resistance tapped is proportional to displacement, the output voltage is also proportional to displacement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** A potentiometric transducer is essentially a **mechanically adjustable voltage divider**. The mechanical input (position) modulates the electrical output (voltage). It is a *passive* device, meaning it cannot generate electricity on its own — it requires external excitation (typically a stable DC reference voltage of **5 V, 10 V, or 24 V**).

## 1.3 Physical & Electrical Specifications at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Total resistance ($R_T$) | **500 Ω, 1 kΩ, 5 kΩ, 10 kΩ** (standard E-series values) |
| Excitation voltage | **5 V to 24 V DC** (regulated, low-ripple) |
| Power rating | **0.5 W to 5 W** (wire-wound higher, film lower) |
| Linearity | **±0.1 % to ±1 %** of full scale |
| Resolution (wire-wound) | **1/N** where $N$ is number of turns |
| Resolution (film type) | Theoretically infinite (microscopic) |
| Life cycle | **10⁵ to 10⁷** operations depending on type |
| Operating temperature | **−55 °C to +125 °C** (industrial grade) |
| Displacement range | **10 mm to 1000 mm** (linear), **5° to 350°** (rotary) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy — How a Potentiometric Transducer is Built

A potentiometric transducer consists of three fundamental parts:

1. **Resistance Element (Track / Element)** — A uniform resistive path fabricated from one of the following:
   - **Wire-wound resistance**: Fine resistance wire (e.g., constantan, nichrome, Karma alloy) wound on a mandrel.
   - **Carbon film**: Thin carbon layer deposited on a phenolic or ceramic substrate.
   - **Cermet (cermalloy)**: A composite of ceramic and metal (e.g., Cr-SiO) screen-printed and fired on a ceramic substrate.
   - **Conductive plastic**: Polymer loaded with carbon or silver particles — extremely smooth, low noise, high resolution.

2. **Wiper (Slider / Brush)** — A metallic contact that slides along the resistance element. Materials used include precious-metal alloys (palladium-silver, platinum-iridium) for low contact resistance and minimal wear.

3. **Shaft / Actuator** — Mechanical input coupling that converts the external linear or rotary motion into wiper motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is element material important?** The element determines **resolution**, **noise level**, **temperature coefficient (TCR)**, **life**, and **cost**. Wire-wound types are cheap and rugged but have *finite, discrete* resistance steps; conductive-plastic types give *infinite resolution* but are costlier.

## 2.2 Types of Potentiometric Transducers

### (a) Linear (Sliding) Potentiometer
- The wiper moves along a **straight** resistance element.
- Output voltage varies **linearly** with displacement $x$.
- Industrial displacement range: **10 mm to 1000 mm**.
- Used in **LVDT-alternative low-cost applications**, machine-tool slide position feedback, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position feedback.

### (b) Rotary (Circular) Potentiometer
- The wiper rotates around a **circular** resistance track.
- Output voltage varies with **angular displacement** $\theta$.
- Standard rotation: **270° to 350°** (some multi-turn versions reach 3600°).
- Used in **valve position feedback, throttle position sensors (TPS) in automobiles, joystick controls**.

### (c) Helical (Multi-turn) Potentiometer
- Resistance wire wound on a helical (corkscrew) mandrel.
- Multi-turn shaft (typically **3, 5, 10 turns**) gives very fine resolution.
- Used where high precision rotary measurement is needed in a compact package.

### (d) Logarithmic (Tapered) Potentiometer — Non-linear
- The resistance per unit length is *not* constant — it is intentionally tapered.
- Output voltage varies **logarithmically** with displacement.
- Used in **audio volume controls** and applications requiring non-linear response (not common in industrial measurement).

## 2.3 Operating Principle — Voltage Divider Mode

When a fixed excitation voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the two ends of the resistance track, the track behaves as a **two-resistor series voltage divider**. Let $R_x$ be the resistance between the wiper and the *lower* end terminal. Then the output voltage across this portion is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_T}$$

For a *linear* potentiometer where resistance is uniformly distributed along its active length $L$, the displacement $x$ of the wiper from the lower end gives $R_x = R_T \cdot (x/L)$. Substituting:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

This is the **fundamental linear transfer equation** of a potentiometric transducer. Output voltage is *directly and linearly* proportional to wiper displacement.

## 2.4 Sensitivity — How Much Output per Unit Input

**Sensitivity** is defined as the rate of change of output with respect to input. Differentiating the linear transfer equation with respect to $x$:

$$S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dx} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} \quad \text{(V/mm or V/m)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Sensitivity increases proportionally with **excitation voltage** $V_{in}$. To maximize sensitivity (and thus signal-to-noise ratio), engineers choose the *highest stable excitation voltage* allowed by the element's power rating, $P_{max} = V_{in}^2 / R_T$.

## 2.5 The Loading-Error Problem — A Critical Real-World Issue

In practice, the output terminals of the potentiometer are connected to a measuring instrument or amplifier with **finite input impedance $R_L$**. This load resistance is in **parallel** with the lower portion $R_x$, *loading* the voltage divider and causing a measurement error.

With the load connected, the effective lower resistance is:

$$R_{x,eff} = \frac{R_x \cdot R_L}{R_x + R_L}$$

The loaded output voltage becomes:

$$V_{out,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{x,eff}}{R_T - R_x + R_{x,eff}}$$

The **loading error** is:

$$\varepsilon_{loading} = V_{out,ideal} - V_{out,loaded}$$

This error is **maximum at mid-stroke** ($x = L/2$) — the worst case. To minimize it, the rule of thumb is:

$$R_L \geq 10 \cdot R_T$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the loading error is **non-linear** with displacement — it is zero at the two ends of travel and maximum at the centre. Always state this explicitly in your exam answer to earn full marks.

## 2.6 Resolution — The Smallest Detectable Input Change

### (a) Wire-wound potentiometer
The resistance changes in **discrete steps** as the wiper moves from turn to turn. If $N$ is the total number of resistance wire turns on the track, the **theoretical resolution** is:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{N} \times 100 \ \%$$

A practical wire-wound pot with **1000 turns** has a resolution of **0.1 %**. This is a *hard physical limit* — you cannot resolve displacement finer than one turn spacing.

### (b) Film-type (carbon, cermet, conductive plastic)
The resistance is **continuously variable** (no discrete steps), so the theoretical resolution is **infinite (∞)** — limited only by contact noise and amplifier noise.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_x = R_T \cdot (x/L)$ | Resistance tapped for displacement $x$ | Ω |
| 2 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot (x/L)$ | **Ideal (no-load) output voltage** | V |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_T}$ | General voltage-divider output | V |
| 4 | $S = V_{in} / L$ | **Sensitivity** | V/mm or V/m |
| 5 | $R_{x,eff} = \dfrac{R_x R_L}{R_x + R_L}$ | Loaded lower-arm resistance | Ω |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{loading} = V_{out,ideal} - V_{out,loaded}$ | Loading error (max at mid-stroke) | V |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{R_T}{4 R_L} \times 100\ \%$ | Maximum loading error (worst case at $x=L/2$) | % |
