3. **Relay (Power Amplifier):** Bleed-type or non-bleed pneumatic amplifier
4. **Feedback Bellows:** Provides negative mechanical feedback for linearity

#### 2.2.2 Working — Step by Step
- Differential pressure $\Delta P$ acts on the diaphragm producing a force $F_{in} = \Delta P \cdot A_f$.
- The diaphragm deflects, moving the **flapper** closer to / farther from the **nozzle**.
- **Nozzle back-pressure** $P_b$ rises when flapper moves toward nozzle (gap decreases).
- The change in $P_b$ is amplified by the relay, which delivers the output pressure $P_o$ (3–15 psi) to the load AND to the **feedback bellows**.
- The feedback bellows generates an opposing force $F_{fb} = P_o \cdot A_b$.
- At equilibrium (force-balance): $F_{in} = F_{fb}$, i.e.,

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f = P_o \cdot A_b \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_o = \frac{A_f}{A_b}\Delta P + 3\ \text{psi (zero suppression)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Force-Balance Principle:** The flapper deflection is so small (a few micrometers) that it is essentially **invisible to the eye**. This is what gives pneumatic transmitters their excellent linearity and immunity to mechanical wear.

---

### 2.3 LVDT — Operating Theory

#### 2.3.1 Constructional Details
- **Primary winding (P):** Centered between the two secondaries, energized by AC source.
- **Secondary windings ($S_1$, $S_2$):** Identical, wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition**.
- **Core:** Soft-iron or nickel-iron, slides axially inside the former. Length is chosen to span at least one full secondary at any position.

#### 2.3.2 Operating Phases

**Phase A — Null Position:**
- Core is centered so that equal magnetic coupling exists to both secondaries.
- Induced EMFs: $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$
- Net output: $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 0$
- **Null voltage ideally = 0** (in practice a small residual of < 1% FS due to imperfect winding symmetry)

**Phase B — Core Moved Toward $S_1$ (Positive Displacement $x > 0$):**
- Coupling to $S_1$ increases, coupling to $S_2$ decreases.
- $e_{s1} > e_{s2}$, so $e_o > 0$. Output is **in phase** with the primary.

**Phase C — Core Moved Toward $S_2$ (Negative Displacement $x < 0$):**
- Coupling to $S_1$ decreases, coupling to $S_2$ increases.
- $e_{s1} < e_{s2}$, so $e_o < 0$. Output is **180° out of phase** with the primary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase-Sensitive Detection:** The phase of the output voltage tells you the **direction** of motion. This is exploited by **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** circuits to recover both magnitude and sign of displacement from a single LVDT signal.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### Table 2A — Ionization Gauge Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I^+ = I^- \cdot \sigma \cdot n \cdot l$ | Ion current from collision physics | A |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{I^+}{I^- \cdot S}$ | Pressure from ion and emission current | torr or Pa |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{I^+}{I^- \cdot P}$ | Definition of gauge sensitivity | torr⁻¹ or Pa⁻¹ |
| 4 | $n = \dfrac{P}{k_B T}$ | Molecular number density (Ideal gas) | molecules/m³ |
| 5 | $I^- = I_e = A \cdot T^2 \cdot e^{-W/k_B T}$ | Richardson–Dushman thermionic emission | A |
| 6 | $\lambda_{mean} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P\ (\text{torr})}\ \text{cm}$ | Mean free path of gas molecule | cm |

#### Table 2B — Pneumatic Transmitter Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{in} = \Delta P \cdot A_f$ | Input force from differential pressure | N |
| 2 | $F_{fb} = P_o \cdot A_b$ | Feedback force from output bellows | N |
| 3 | $P_o = \dfrac{A_f}{A_b} \cdot \Delta P + 3\ \text{psi}$ | Force-balance output relation | psi |
| 4 | Transfer function $K = \dfrac{A_f}{A_b}$ | Static gain (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{A_f}{A_b} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau s}$ | First-order dynamic model | psi/psi |
| 6 | Span = 15 − 3 = 12 psi | Live zero standard | psi |
| 7 | LRV → 3 psi, URV → 15 psi | Output standard | psi |

#### Table 2C — LVDT Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ | Differential output voltage | V |
| 2 | $e_{s1} = e_0 + kx$ | Secondary 1 EMF (positive $x$) | V |
| 3 | $e_{s2} = e_0 - kx$ | Secondary 2 EMF (positive $x$) | V |
| 4 | $e_o = 2kx$ | Net output in linear range | V |
| 5 | Sensitivity $K_{LVDT} = \dfrac{e_o}{x} = 2k$ | LVDT sensitivity | V/mm |
| 6 | Null voltage = 0 (ideal) | At $x = 0$ | V |
| 7 | $\vert x \vert \le \dfrac{L_{linear}}{2}$ | Valid operating range | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Recap:** Ionization gauges are indispensable in **semiconductor fabrication, fusion reactors, particle accelerators, and space simulation chambers**. Pneumatic transmitters still dominate **hazardous-area chemical/petrochemical plants** because no electrical power is needed inside the field instrument. LVDTs are the workhorse of **aerospace flight-control feedback, hydraulic-cylinder position sensing, and CNC machine tool metrology**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Relation of an Ionization Gauge

#### Statement
For a hot-cathode ionization gauge, derive the fundamental pressure-current relation $P = \dfrac{I^+}{I^- \cdot S}$.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Electron-Molecule Collision Model**
Consider a single electron of current $I^-$ traversing a path of length $l$ inside the gauge volume. The number of ionizing collisions per second is:

$$N_{coll} = \frac{I^-}{e} \cdot \sigma \cdot n \cdot l$$

where $\sigma$ is the ionization cross-section (m²) and $n$ is the molecular number density (molecules/m³). Each collision produces one positive ion, hence the ion current is:

$$I^+ = N_{coll} \cdot e = I^- \cdot \sigma \cdot n \cdot l \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2: Apply Ideal Gas Law to Express $n$**
For an ideal gas at temperature $T$:

$$P \cdot V = N \cdot k_B T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = \frac{N}{V} = \frac{P}{k_B T} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute (2) into (1)**

$$I^+ = I^- \cdot \sigma \cdot \left(\frac{P}{k_B T}\right) \cdot l$$

Grouping all constants together:

$$I^+ = I^- \cdot P \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{\sigma \cdot l}{k_B T}\right)}_{S} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4: Define Sensitivity**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\sigma \cdot l}{k_B T}}$$

with units of **$1/\text{torr}$** or **$1/\text{Pa}$**.

**Step 5: Solve for Pressure**

$$P = \frac{I^+}{I^- \cdot S} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

> **Verification by Dimensional Analysis:**
> $\dfrac{[\text{A}]}{[\text{A}] \cdot [\text{torr}^{-1}]} = [\text{torr}]$ ✓ — Units balance correctly.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard):**
A Bayard-Alpert gauge has emission current $I^- = 5\ \text{mA}$, measured ion current $I^+ = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{A}$, and sensitivity $S = 20\ \text{torr}^{-1}$. Find the pressure.

$$P = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 20} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-8}}{0.1} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{torr}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force-Balance Equation of a Pneumatic Transmitter

#### Statement
Derive the relationship $P_o = \dfrac{A_f}{A_b}\Delta P + 3\ \text{psi}$ for a force-balance pneumatic transmitter.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Input Force from Differential Pressure**
The differential pressure $\Delta P$ acts on the diaphragm of effective area $A_f$, producing a force on the flapper bar:

$$F_{in} = \Delta P \cdot A_f \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2: Feedback Force from Output Bellows**
The amplified output pressure $P_o$ is also fed into the feedback bellows of area $A_b$, producing a force opposing the input force:

$$F_{fb} = P_o \cdot A_b \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3: Force-Balance Equation**
At steady-state (dynamic equilibrium), the flapper bar is essentially stationary (negligible displacement), so:

$$F_{in} = F_{fb} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f = P_o \cdot A_b$$

**Step 4: Solve for $P_o$**

$$P_o = \frac{A_f}{A_b} \cdot \Delta P \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 5: Include Standard Live-Zero (3 psi)**
To incorporate the standard 3-15 psi live-zero, we add a constant bias $P_{zero} = 3\ \text{psi}$:

$$\boxed{P_o = K \cdot \Delta P + 3\ \text{psi}, \quad \text{where}\ K = \frac{A_f}{A_b}} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard):**
A pneumatic transmitter has $A_f = 5\ \text{cm}^2$ and $A_b = 1\ \text{cm}^2$. If the input differential pressure varies from **0 to 200 inches of water column**:
- Span of $\Delta P$ = 200 inH₂O
- Output span = $K \cdot 200 = 5 \times 200 = 1000$ psi??? 

This is obviously wrong — we must calibrate so output span = **12 psi**.

**Step 6: Calibration Adjustment**
In practice, the gain $K = A_f/A_b$ is **designed and adjusted via the range spring** so that for the chosen input span, $P_o$ varies exactly over 12 psi. The output remains anchored to live zero at 3 psi.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LVDT Output Voltage Equation

#### Statement
Derive the output voltage of an LVDT as a function of core displacement, and show the linear relationship in the working range.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Magnetic Coupling Model**
Let the mutual inductance between primary and secondary 1 be $M_1$, and between primary and secondary 2 be $M_2$. With AC excitation voltage $V_p$ at angular frequency $\omega$:

$$e_{s1} = j\omega M_1 \cdot \frac{V_p}{j\omega L_p} = \frac{M_1}{L_p} V_p \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$e_{s2} = \frac{M_2}{L_p} V_p \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2: Effect of Core Position**
When the core is at the geometric center (null position), by symmetry $M_1 = M_2 = M_0$, and:

$$e_{s1} = e_{s2} = \frac{M_0}{L_p} V_p = e_0 \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3: Series-Opposition Connection**
The two secondaries are connected in series-opposition, so the differential output is:

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

At null, $e_o = 0$. ✓

**Step 4: Displacement of Core**
When the core moves by a small displacement $x$ toward secondary 1, by linear approximation within the working range:

$$M_1 = M_0 + \Delta M, \quad M_2 = M_0 - \Delta M$$

where $\Delta M = k' x$ for some constant $k'$.

Substituting:

$$e_{s1} = \frac{(M_0 + k' x)}{L_p} V_p = e_0 + \frac{k' x V_p}{L_p} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

$$e_{s2} = \frac{(M_0 - k' x)}{L_p} V_p = e_0 - \frac{k' x V_p}{L_p} \quad \text{...(6)}$$

**Step 5: Differential Output**

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = \left(e_0 + \frac{k' x V_p}{L_p}\right) - \left(e_0 - \frac{k' x V_p}{L_p}\right)$$

$$e_o = \frac{2 k' x V_p}{L_p} \quad \text{...(7)}$$

Defining $k = \dfrac{k' V_p}{L_p}$ (a constant for a given excitation):

$$\boxed{e_o = 2kx \quad \text{for}\ \vert x \vert \le \frac{L_{linear}}{2}} \quad \text{...(8)}$$

This is the famous **linear LVDT equation**, valid within the specified linear operating range. Outside this range, the relationship becomes nonlinear and unusable for accurate measurement.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard):**
An LVDT has sensitivity $K_{LVDT} = 2k = 0.5\ \text{V/mm}$. If the core is displaced by $+8\ \text{mm}$:

$$e_o = 0.5 \times 8 = +4\ \text{V (in phase with primary)}$$

If displaced by $-6\ \text{mm}$:

$$e_o = 0.5 \times (-6) = -3\ \text{V (180° out of phase with primary)}$$

The **direction** is decoded by phase-sensitive demodulation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Hot-Cathode IG | Cold-Cathode IG | Pneumatic Tx | LVDT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-3}$ torr | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-5}$ torr | 0–400 inH₂O | ±0.1 to ±250 mm |
| Accuracy | ±5% to ±10% | ±10% to ±25% | ±0.5% FS | ±0.1% to ±0.5% FS |
| Output Signal | DC current ($\mu$A) | DC current | 3–15 psi pneumatic | AC voltage |
| Linearity | High | Moderate | Excellent (force-balance) | Excellent (in range) |
| Power Required | AC mains + DC bias | High voltage DC | Air supply | AC excitation |
| Major Limitation | X-ray limit at UHV | Stray magnetic fields | Air supply dependency | Limited core travel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Hot-Cathode (Bayard-Alpert) Ionization Gauge — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Filament Cathode +30V DC] -->|Thermionic Emission| B[Electron Cloud]
    B -->|Accelerated by +180V| C[Grid Anode Cylindrical]
    B -->|Collide with gas molecules| D[Gas Molecules in Chamber]
    D -->|Positive Ions Produced| E[Ion Collector Wire 0V]
    E -->|Microammeter Reading| F[Output Current I+]
    C -->|Electrons absorbed| G[Anode Return Path]
    F -->|I+ / I- S| H[Pressure Readout P in torr]
    
    styleA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleE fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Block-Functional Note:** Electrons emitted from the filament (A) form a cloud (B), are accelerated toward the grid anode (C), collide with gas molecules (D) producing positive ions that are collected by the central wire (E). The measured ion current (F) yields pressure (H) via $P = I^+ / (I^- S)$.

---

### 4.2 Pneumatic Force-Balance Differential Pressure Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[High Pressure Inlet HP] --> D1[High Side Diaphragm]
    P2[Low Pressure Inlet LP] --> D2[Low Side Diaphragm]
    D1 --> FB1[Force Bar Pivot]
    D2 --> FB1
    FB1 -->|Deflection| F1[Flapper]
    N1[Nozzle Fixed] -->|Back Pressure Pb| F1
    F1 -->|Gap Changes| N1
    N1 -->|Pb| R1[Pneumatic Relay Amplifier]
    R1 -->|Output Po 3-15 psi| OUT[Output to Process AND Feedback Bellows]
    OUT --> FB2[Feedback Bellows Area Ab]
    FB2 -->|Opposing Force Pfb| FB1
    
    styleP1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleP2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleFB1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleF1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleR1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleOUT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleFB2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Block-Functional Note:** The HP-LP differential pressure acts on the diaphragms, deflects the force bar, and repositions the flapper relative to the nozzle. The resulting change in nozzle back-pressure is amplified by the relay, producing the 3–15 psi output, which simultaneously pressurizes the feedback bellows to rebalance the force bar — yielding a **linear input-output relation**.

---

### 4.3 LVDT Constructional and Electrical Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AC[AC Excitation Source Vp 1-24V RMS 50Hz-20kHz] --> P1[Primary Coil Center Wound]
    P1 -->|Mutual Inductance M1| S1[Secondary 1 Left Coil]
    P1 -->|Mutual Inductance M2| S2[Secondary 2 Right Coil]
    CORE[Soft Iron Ferromagnetic Core Movable] -->|Position x| S1
    CORE -->|Position x| S2
    S1 -->|es1| J1[Junction Series Opposition]
    S2 -->|es2| J1
    J1 -->|es1 minus es2| OUT[Output Terminal to Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
    OUT -->|DC Voltage Vdc| METER[Displacement Indicator]
    
    styleAC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleP1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleS1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleS2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleCORE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleJ1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleOUT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.4 LVDT Operating States (Null, Positive, Negative Displacement)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST[Core Position State] --> ST1[Null Position Centered]
    ST --> ST2[Positive Displacement x greater than 0]
    ST --> ST3[Negative Displacement x less than 0]
    
    ST1 --> R1[es1 equals es2, eo equals 0V, no output]
    ST2 --> R2[es1 increases, es2 decreases, eo positive, IN PHASE with primary]
    ST3 --> R3[es1 decreases, es2 increases, eo negative, 180 DEG OUT OF PHASE]
    
    styleST fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleST1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleST2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    styleST3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the working principle of a hot-cathode ionization gauge. Mention its pressure range.**

**Model Answer:**
A hot-cathode ionization gauge measures very low pressures by ionizing gas molecules using thermionically emitted electrons from a heated filament. The electrons are accelerated toward a positively biased grid (anode). On their way, they collide with gas molecules and produce positive ions, which are collected by a central ion-collector wire held near ground potential. The resulting ion current $I^+$ is directly proportional to the gas pressure:

$$P = \frac{I^+}{I^- \cdot S}$$

where $I^-$ is the emission current and $S$ is the gauge sensitivity. The pressure range of a hot-cathode (Bayard-Alpert) ionization gauge is from **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-10}$ torr**, making it the standard instrument for **high and ultra-high vacuum** measurement.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Explain the principle of a force-balance pneumatic transmitter with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**
A force-balance pneumatic transmitter converts a measured differential pressure $\Delta P$ into a standard pneumatic output of **3–15 psi** using the **force-balance principle**. The input differential pressure acts on a diaphragm/bellows, producing a force that deflects a flapper relative to a fixed nozzle. The change in nozzle back-pressure is amplified by a pneumatic relay to produce the output pressure $P_o$. This output is simultaneously fed back to a feedback bellows that generates an opposing force equal to the input force at steady state:

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f = P_o \cdot A_b \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_o = \frac{A_f}{A_b}\Delta P + 3\ \text{psi}$$

The flapper deflection remains negligibly small, ensuring excellent linearity.

---

### PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** (KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified)

**(a)** Derive the relation between pressure and ion current in a hot-cathode ionization gauge. Define sensitivity and explain how it depends on the gas species. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ion Current from Collision Physics:**
Each electron traversing the gauge volume of path length $l$ has a probability $\sigma n l$ of ionizing a molecule. Hence:

$$I^+ = I^- \cdot \sigma \cdot n \cdot l$$

**Step 2 — Express $n$ using Ideal Gas Law:**
$$n = \frac{P}{k_B T}$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**
$$I^+ = I^- \cdot \sigma \cdot \frac{P}{k_B T} \cdot l = I^- \cdot P \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{\sigma l}{k_B T}\right)}_{S}$$

**Step 4 — Final Pressure Relation:**

$$\boxed{P = \frac{I^+}{I^- \cdot S}}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity Definition:**
Sensitivity is defined as the ratio of ion current per unit pressure per unit emission current:

$$S = \frac{I^+}{I^- \cdot P}$$

**Step 6 — Dependence on Gas Species:**
Sensitivity depends on $\sigma$, the ionization cross-section, which is gas-specific:
- For **nitrogen** (N₂): $S \approx 10$ to 12 torr⁻¹
- For **helium** (He): $S \approx 2.5$ to 3 torr⁻¹ (lowest, small atomic radius)
- For **argon** (Ar): $S \approx 14$ to 18 torr⁻¹

Therefore, **gauge calibration is gas-dependent** and must be corrected using a relative sensitivity factor $R_S = S_{gas}/S_{N_2}$.

---

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Penning (cold-cathode) ionization gauge**. Compare it with a hot-cathode gauge. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
- Two parallel plate cathodes separated by a ring-shaped anode.
- A strong magnetic field (~1000–2000 Gauss) is supplied by a permanent magnet perpendicular to the plates.
- High voltage (~2 to 6 kV DC) is applied between anode and cathodes.

**Working:**
1. The high voltage creates a gas discharge; free electrons emitted from the cathode are accelerated toward the anode.
2. The magnetic field deflects electrons into long helical paths, dramatically increasing the path length $l$.
3. This increases the ionization probability $\sigma n l$ enormously.
4. Positive ions produced are collected at the cathode, generating an ion current $I^+$ proportional to pressure.

**Pressure Relation:**
$$P = \frac{I^+}{I^- \cdot S}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Hot-Cathode (BA) | Cold-Cathode (Penning) |
|---|---|---|
| Filament | Required | Not required |
| Emission mechanism | Thermionic | Field-induced discharge |
| Magnetic field | Absent | Present (essential) |
| Pressure range | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-10}$ torr | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ torr |
| Sensitivity | 10–25 torr⁻¹ (N₂) | 1–10 torr⁻¹ |
| Stability | Good | Affected by magnetic field drift |
| Switch-on time | Requires filament warm-up | Almost instantaneous |
| Lower limit | X-ray limit at $10^{-10}$ torr | Extinguishes at low P |

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** (KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified)

**(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, explain the construction and working of an **LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
- One **primary coil (P)** centered on a cylindrical former.
- Two **identical secondary coils ($S_1$ and $S_2$)** wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition**.
- A movable **soft-iron core** slides axially inside the former.

**Working:**
- The primary is energized by an AC source $V_p$.
- The AC flux produced links with both secondaries via the core.
- When the core is **centered (null position)**, $M_1 = M_2$, so $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$ and net output $e_o = 0$.
- When the core is **displaced by $x$** toward $S_1$: $M_1 = M_0 + k'x$, $M_2 = M_0 - k'x$.

**Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{s1} &= \frac{(M_0 + k'x)}{L_p} V_p = e_0 + \frac{k' x V_p}{L_p} \\
e_{s2} &= \frac{(M_0 - k'x)}{L_p} V_p = e_0 - \frac{k' x V_p}{L_p} \\
e_o &= e_{s1} - e_{s2} = \frac{2k' x V_p}{L_p} = 2kx
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{e_o = 2kx \quad \text{within linear range}}$$

**Key Features:**
- Infinite resolution
- Output phase indicates direction (positive/negative displacement)
- Phase-sensitive demodulator yields signed DC output proportional to displacement.

---

**(b)** Explain with a block diagram the working of a **force-balance pneumatic differential pressure transmitter**. Derive the input-output relation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A pneumatic transmitter uses **force-balance** to convert a differential pressure input into a proportional 3–15 psi output. The main sub-systems are:

1. **Diaphragm/Bellows** (sensing element)
2. **Nozzle–Flapper** (first-stage pneumatic amplifier)
3. **Pneumatic Relay** (second-stage power amplifier)
4. **Feedback Bellows** (provides negative feedback)

**Step-by-Step Operation:**
1. Differential pressure $\Delta P = P_H - P_L$ acts on the diaphragm.
2. Diaphragm deflects → flapper moves relative to nozzle.
3. Nozzle back-pressure $P_b$ changes (rises if flapper approaches nozzle).
4. Relay amplifies $P_b$ → output pressure $P_o$.
5. Output is fed back to feedback bellows → opposing force on flapper bar.
6. At steady state, input force = feedback force.

**Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{in} &= \Delta P \cdot A_f \\
F_{fb} &= P_o \cdot A_b \\
\text{At equilibrium:}\quad F_{in} &= F_{fb} \\
\Delta P \cdot A_f &= P_o \cdot A_b \\
\boxed{P_o &= \frac{A_f}{A_b} \Delta P + 3\ \text{psi}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Advantages:**
- Excellent linearity (due to closed-loop force-balance)
- High accuracy (±0.5% FS)
- Intrinsically safe — no electrical power in hazardous areas
- Robust and reliable

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Ionization Gauge:** Students frequently forget to mention the **X-ray limit** of a Bayard-Alpert gauge (~10⁻¹⁰ torr), beyond which ion current becomes spurious due to X-ray-induced photoelectrons at the collector. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Pneumatic Transmitter:** Many students write $P_o = \dfrac{A_f}{A_b}\Delta P$ and forget to **add the live-zero offset of 3 psi**. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **LVDT:** Students often write $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ but forget to state that the secondaries are in **series opposition**. Also, the **phase of output** (which gives direction) is a favorite 2-mark question — never omit it.
> 4. **Sensitivity Units:** For ionization gauge, $S$ has units of **1/torr**, not torr. Forgetting units in numerical problems costs full marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Ionization Gauge:**
  - Hot-cathode (BA): **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-10}$ torr**; uses thermionic emission; sensitivity 10–25 torr⁻¹ for N₂.
  - Cold-cathode (Penning): **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ torr**; uses crossed E and B fields.
  - Master relation: $P = I^+ / (I^- \cdot S)$.
  - **X-ray limit** is the floor of BA gauge sensitivity.
  - Calibration is gas-specific; use relative sensitivity factor for non-N₂ gases.

- **Pneumatic Differential Pressure Transmitter:**
  - Standard output: **3–15 psi (0.2–1.0 bar)**.
  - Working principle: **Force-balance** (closed-loop negative feedback).
  - Master relation: $P_o = (A_f/A_b)\Delta P + 3\ \text{psi}$.
  - Key components: **Nozzle–Flapper + Relay + Feedback Bellows**.
  - Major advantage: intrinsically safe for hazardous (Ex) areas.
  - Span = 12 psi; Live zero = 3 psi.

- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer):**
  - Principle: **Mutual inductance variation** with core position.
  - Construction: 1 Primary + 2 Secondaries (series opposition) + movable core.
  - Excitation: AC source (1–24 V RMS, 50 Hz–20 kHz).
  - Null output = 0 V; output **phase** encodes direction.
  - Master equation (linear range): $e_o = 2kx$.
  - Requires **phase-sensitive demodulator** to recover signed DC.
  - Advantages: **infinite resolution, frictionless, low hysteresis, long life**.
  - Limitations: limited linear range; requires AC excitation; core mass limits dynamic response.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the schematic/functional diagram FIRST in any 7- or 14-mark question on these topics — it immediately earns 2–3 marks with minimal effort and anchors the rest of your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **RVDT (Rotary Variable Differential Transformer)** is an electromechanical transducer that converts **angular (rotational) displacement** into a linearly proportional **AC voltage** signal. It operates on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction**, identical in theory to a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT), but specifically designed to sense rotation rather than linear translation.

The device consists of a **stator** carrying one primary winding and two identical secondary windings connected in **series opposition (differential)**, and a **rotor** (ferromagnetic armature) that rotates without physical contact across a precisely engineered angular range (typically **±40°** from the null/zero position).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steering Wheel Volume Knob"

Imagine the **steering wheel-mounted audio control** found in many modern cars. As you rotate the knob clockwise, the volume smoothly increases; rotate it counter-clockwise, and volume decreases. Behind that knob is essentially a small RVDT:

- Your **fingers rotate the knob** → this is the *angular displacement input (θ)*.
- The **change in volume** → this is the *electrical output voltage (E_o)*.
- The **direction you turned** (CW vs. CCW) is indicated by the **phase** of the output AC signal — exactly like a phase-sensitive detector interpreting RVDT output.

Just as the steering knob has a small "dead zone" in the center where nothing happens (the **null position**), the RVDT has a precise central angle (0°) where its output is theoretically zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** RVDT is a high-yield topic in Module 2. Expect direct 3-mark definition questions and 7–14 mark derivations involving the differential output equation and phase-detection logic.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency | **400 Hz to 20 kHz** (typically 2.5 kHz – 5 kHz) |
| Linear Operating Range | **±40°** (some designs up to ±60°) |
| Sensitivity (k) | **2 V/V/degree to 5 V/V/degree** (typical) |
| Null Voltage (Residual) | **< 0.5% of full-scale output** |
| Rotor Type | Non-contacting (no brushes, no wear) |
| Output Type | **AC voltage** (requires phase-sensitive demodulation for DC equivalent) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

The RVDT is built around two main components:

**A) Stator (Stationary Element)**
- Laminated silicon-steel core with slots.
- Houses **three windings**:
  - **Primary (P):** Centered on the stator, energized by an AC excitation voltage $E_p$.
  - **Secondary 1 (S₁):** Wound on one side of the primary.
  - **Secondary 2 (S₂):** Wound on the opposite side of the primary.
- S₁ and S₂ are **connected in series opposition** (anti-phasor connection).

**B) Rotor (Rotating Element)**
- A specially shaped **ferromagnetic armature** (often cardioid or cam-shaped profile).
- Mounted on a shaft coupled to the measured object.
- **No electrical connections** to the rotor → no friction, no wear, infinite resolution.

> [!TIP]
> The **shape of the rotor is the secret to linearity**. Engineers machine the rotor profile so that the *difference* in flux coupling to S₁ and S₂ is a perfect linear function of angle θ over the design range.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

1. The primary winding is energized with a stable **AC reference voltage** $E_p$ of known frequency.
2. This produces an **alternating magnetic flux** $\phi_p$ in the stator core.
3. The rotor, being ferromagnetic, provides a low-reluctance path that **channels the flux** preferentially into one secondary or the other, depending on its angular position $\theta$.
4. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in each secondary:
   - $E_{s1} = k_1(\theta) \cdot E_p$
   - $E_{s2} = k_2(\theta) \cdot E_p$
5. The **differential (net) output** is:
   $$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = [k_1(\theta) - k_2(\theta)] \cdot E_p$$
6. The rotor profile is designed so that $k_1(\theta) - k_2(\theta) = k \cdot \theta$, yielding:
   $$\boxed{E_o = k \cdot E_p \cdot \theta}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_o = k \cdot E_p \cdot \theta$ | RVDT ideal output (linear range) | Volts |
| 2 | $E_{s1} = M_1 \frac{dI_p}{dt}$ | EMF in Secondary 1 (Faraday's Law) | Volts |
| 3 | $E_{s2} = M_2 \frac{dI_p}{dt}$ | EMF in Secondary 2 | Volts |
| 4 | $E_o = (M_1 - M_2)\frac{dI_p}{dt}$ | Differential EMF output | Volts |
| 5 | $k = \dfrac{dE_o}{d\theta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{E_p}$ | Sensitivity constant | V/V/deg |
| 6 | $\phi = \frac{MMF}{Reluctance} = \frac{N_p I_p}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)}$ | Flux coupling (θ-dependent reluctance) | Weber |
| 7 | $E_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_{max}$ | Transformer EMF equation | Volts |
| 8 | $V_{null} \approx 0$ | Ideal null output (in practice, residual) | Volts |
| 9 | $\theta_{FS} = \pm 40°$ | Full-scale angular range | Degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all written solutions, use the **modulus** symbol as $\vert x \vert$ (not `|x|`), and conditional phrases as $\text{when } \theta > 0$ to avoid ambiguity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

RVDTs are deployed wherever a **robust, contactless, high-reliability** rotary position sensor is required:

- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface feedback (ailerons, elevators, rudder trim).
- **Hydraulics & Pneumatics:** Valve stem position feedback in process control loops.
- **Robotics:** Joint angle encoders in robotic arms (where non-contact is critical).
- **Nuclear Reactors:** Control rod position monitoring (high radiation tolerance).
- **Automotive:** Throttle position sensing in legacy drive-by-wire systems.
- **Machine Tools:** Spindle orientation feedback for CNC machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RVDT Output Equation

We will derive $E_o = k \cdot E_p \cdot \theta$ starting from **first principles** (Faraday's Law + transformer action).

---

**Step 1 — Write the primary excitation**

Let the primary be excited by a sinusoidal AC source:
$$E_p = V_p \sin(\omega t)$$

This produces a primary current $I_p$ lagging by a small phase angle due to winding inductance. For an ideal transformer, $I_p$ is in phase with the magnetizing MMF.

---

**Step 2 — Establish the primary magnetomotive force (MMF)**

The MMF produced by the primary winding is:
$$\mathcal{F}_p = N_p \cdot I_p(t)$$

where $N_p$ is the number of primary turns.

---

**Step 3 — Express the magnetic flux**

The flux $\phi$ in the core follows the magnetic Ohm's law:
$$\phi(\theta, t) = \frac{\mathcal{F}_p}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)} = \frac{N_p \cdot I_p(t)}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)}$$

The denominator $\mathcal{R}(\theta)$ is the **reluctance** of the magnetic path, which is a function of the rotor angle $\theta$ because the rotor's position alters the air-gap geometry between stator poles.

---

**Step 4 — Flux linkage in each secondary**

The flux linking Secondary 1 depends on $\theta$:
$$\phi_1(\theta) = \frac{N_p \cdot I_p}{\mathcal{R}_1(\theta)}$$

Similarly, the flux linking Secondary 2:
$$\phi_2(\theta) = \frac{N_p \cdot I_p}{\mathcal{R}_2(\theta)}$$

Because of the differential (mirror-symmetric) geometry, we can write:
$$\mathcal{R}_1(\theta) = \mathcal{R}_0 - \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)$$
$$\mathcal{R}_2(\theta) = \mathcal{R}_0 + \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)$$

where $\mathcal{R}_0$ is the symmetric (null) reluctance and $\Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)$ is the angular variation.

---

**Step 5 — Apply Faraday's Law to each secondary**

By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:
$$E_{s1} = -N_s \frac{d\phi_1}{dt} = -N_s \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{N_p I_p(t)}{\mathcal{R}_0 - \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)}\right]$$

$$E_{s2} = -N_s \frac{d\phi_2}{dt} = -N_s \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{N_p I_p(t)}{\mathcal{R}_0 + \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)}\right]$$

---

**Step 6 — Take the differential output (series opposition)**

Since S₁ and S₂ are connected in series opposition, the net output is:
$$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$

$$E_o = -N_s N_p \frac{dI_p}{dt} \left[\frac{1}{\mathcal{R}_0 - \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)} - \frac{1}{\mathcal{R}_0 + \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)}\right]$$

---

**Step 7 — Simplify the bracket using algebraic identity**

Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{a-b} - \dfrac{1}{a+b} = \dfrac{2b}{a^2 - b^2}$:

$$E_o = -N_s N_p \frac{dI_p}{dt} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)}{\mathcal{R}_0^2 - [\Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)]^2}$$

---

**Step 8 — Apply the small-rotation approximation**

For $\Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta) \ll \mathcal{R}_0$ (valid within the linear range), the denominator simplifies:
$$\mathcal{R}_0^2 - [\Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)]^2 \approx \mathcal{R}_0^2$$

Therefore:
$$E_o \approx -\frac{2 N_s N_p}{\mathcal{R}_0^2} \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt} \cdot \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)$$

---

**Step 9 — Recognize the linear regime**

For a **properly shaped (cardioid/cam-profile) rotor**, the engineer designs $\Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)$ to be **directly proportional to θ**:
$$\Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta) = \mathcal{K} \cdot \theta$$

Substituting back:
$$E_o = -\frac{2 N_s N_p \mathcal{K}}{\mathcal{R}_0^2} \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt} \cdot \theta$$

---

**Step 10 — Final compact form**

Since $E_p \propto \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ (through the primary inductance), we group all constants into a single sensitivity constant $k$:

$$\boxed{E_o = k \cdot E_p \cdot \theta}$$

where $k = \dfrac{2 N_s N_p \mathcal{K}}{L_p \mathcal{R}_0^2}$ is the RVDT sensitivity (in V/V/radian, then converted to V/V/degree).

---

**Step 11 — Phase interpretation (the "directional" property)**

By carefully tracking the sign of $\theta$ in the algebra:

- When $\theta > 0$ (rotor displaced clockwise from null): $E_o$ is **in phase** with $E_p$.
- When $\theta < 0$ (rotor displaced counter-clockwise): $E_o$ is **180° out of phase** with $E_p$.

This **phase reversal** is the key to using RVDT for **bidirectional** measurement and is recovered using a **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operating Regime | Equation | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Null position ($\theta = 0$) | $E_o = 0$ | Perfect flux cancellation |
| Small positive $\theta$ | $E_o = +k E_p \theta$ | Output in-phase with $E_p$ |
| Small negative $\theta$ | $E_o = -k E_p \theta$ | Output anti-phase with $E_p$ |
| Beyond $\pm 40°$ | Non-linear (saturates) | Rotor profile geometry breaks linearity |
| Open-circuited secondary | $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ | No current, no $I^2R$ loading |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — RVDT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["AC Excitation Source EP"] --> nodeB["Primary Winding P"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Stator Core with Mutual Inductance M theta"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Secondary Winding S1"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Secondary Winding S2"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Series Opposition Junction"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Differential Output EO"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Phase Sensitive Demodulator"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["DC Output Voltage VDC proportional to theta"]
    nodeJ["Angular Displacement theta input"] --> nodeK["Rotor Cam Shaped Ferromagnetic Core"]
    nodeK --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeL["Controller or Display Unit"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Mechanical Shaft Rotation theta"] --> nodeB["RVDT Transducer"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Differential AC Output EO"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Oscillator providing EP at 2.5 to 5 kHz"]
    nodeD --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeE["Phase Sensitive Demodulator PSD"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Low Pass Filter LPF"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["DC Amplifier"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Output to PLC or Data Acquisition System"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constructional Cross-Section View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STATOR["STATOR Laminated Silicon Steel Core"]
        nodeA["PRIMARY WINDING P at Center"]
        nodeB["SECONDARY WINDING S1 on Left"]
        nodeC["SECONDARY WINDING S2 on Right"]
    end
    subgraph ROTOR["ROTOR Ferromagnetic Cam Shaped Armature"]
        nodeD["Rotor Profile Designed for Linear Flux Variation"]
    end
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeE["Input Shaft Couples Measured Object to Rotor"] --> ROTOR
    nodeF["Excitation Voltage EP applied to Primary"] --> nodeA
    nodeG["Differential Output EO taken from S1 minus S2"] --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeC
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Phase-Based Directional Discrimination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["RVDT Output EO"] --> nodeB{"Phase Detector Compares with EP"}
    nodeB -->|In Phase| nodeC["Positive DC Output = Positive theta"]
    nodeB -->|180 Degree Out of Phase| nodeD["Negative DC Output = Negative theta"]
    nodeB -->|Zero| nodeE["Null Position theta equals 0"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Controller reads positive value"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define RVDT. Mention any two advantages of RVDT over potentiometric type angular position sensors.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** RVDT (Rotary Variable Differential Transformer) is an electromechanical transducer that converts angular displacement into a proportional AC voltage, using the principle of mutual electromagnetic induction between a primary winding and two differentially connected secondary windings, with a non-contacting ferromagnetic rotor.

**Two Advantages over Potentiometric Sensors:**

1. **Non-contact operation:** The rotor has no physical electrical contact with the stator → no friction, no wear, **infinite resolution**, and **long operational life**.
2. **High reliability in harsh environments:** No brushes to corrode, spark, or contaminate → suitable for **explosive, high-vibration, and high-temperature** environments like aerospace and petrochemical industries.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Why is the output of an RVDT an AC voltage and not a DC voltage? How is the direction of rotation determined from this AC output?**

**Model Answer:**

The RVDT output is inherently **AC** because it operates on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** (Faraday's Law). Faraday's Law requires a *time-varying* flux ($\frac{d\phi}{dt} \neq 0$) to induce an EMF. Since the primary is energized by an AC source, the flux is continuously alternating, producing an AC-induced EMF in the secondaries.

**Direction Determination (Phase Discrimination):**
The RVDT output is passed through a **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)**, which compares the output's phase with that of the excitation $E_p$:

- If $E_o$ is **in-phase** with $E_p$ → rotation is in the **positive (clockwise)** direction.
- If $E_o$ is **180° out-of-phase** with $E_p$ → rotation is in the **negative (counter-clockwise)** direction.

This gives the RVDT its **bidirectional measurement capability**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently lose marks by **forgetting to mention phase-sensitive demodulation** when explaining direction detection.
> - **Never confuse RVDT with LVDT** in definitions — the difference is the input variable (angular vs. linear displacement) and the rotor shape.
> - Always state the **standard linear range of ±40°** explicitly. Vague answers like "rotates" cost marks.
> - In derivations, **do not skip the small-angle approximation step** (Step 8 above). Examiners award 2 marks specifically for that step.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a Rotary Variable Differential Transformer (RVDT). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the output voltage equation of an RVDT and explain how the direction of angular displacement is identified from its output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** A cylindrical laminated silicon-steel core with slots carrying three windings — one **primary winding (P)** at the center and two identical **secondary windings (S₁ and S₂)** symmetrically placed on either side of the primary.
- **S₁ and S₂ are connected in series opposition** (anti-phasor / differential connection).
- **Rotor:** A specially shaped (cardioid/cam-profile) ferromagnetic armature mounted on the input shaft. It is **electrically isolated** from the stator — there are no brushes or slip rings.

**Working:**
- The primary is energized with a stable AC excitation voltage $E_p$ (typically 2.5–5 kHz).
- This produces an alternating flux in the stator core.
- The rotor's angular position $\theta$ alters the **reluctance** of the magnetic paths to S₁ and S₂ asymmetrically.
- At the **null position** ($\theta = 0°$): the flux coupling to S₁ and S₂ is **equal**, so the induced EMFs cancel, giving $E_o = 0$.
- When the rotor is **displaced** by angle $\theta$: one secondary receives more flux, the other receives less. The differential output becomes non-zero.

**Part (b) — Derivation and Direction Detection (7 marks):**

Starting from Faraday's Law applied to each secondary:

$$E_{s1} = -N_s \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{N_p I_p(t)}{\mathcal{R}_0 - \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)}\right]$$

$$E_{s2} = -N_s \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{N_p I_p(t)}{\mathcal{R}_0 + \Delta\mathcal{R}(\theta)}\right]$$

Differential output:
$$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = -\frac{2 N_s N_p \mathcal{K}}{L_p \mathcal{R}_0^2} \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt} \cdot \theta$$

Recognizing $E_p \propto \frac{dI_p}{dt}$ and grouping all constants into $k$:

$$\boxed{E_o = k \cdot E_p \cdot \theta}$$

**Direction Detection (Phase Discrimination):**
The RVDT output is fed to a **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** which uses $E_p$ as a reference.

- $\theta > 0$ (CW rotation) → $E_o$ **in-phase** with $E_p$ → PSD output is **positive DC**.
- $\theta < 0$ (CCW rotation) → $E_o$ **180° out-of-phase** with $E_p$ → PSD output is **negative DC**.
- $\theta = 0$ (null) → $E_o = 0$ → PSD output is **zero**.

This gives a **signed DC output** proportional to angular displacement with directional information preserved.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Compare RVDT with LVDT. List four advantages and two limitations of RVDT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RVDT has a sensitivity of **2.5 V/V/degree** and is excited by a **5 V RMS, 3 kHz** AC source. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
  (i) The output voltage when the rotor is displaced by **+20°**.
  (ii) The output voltage when displaced by **−15°**, specifying its phase relationship with the excitation.
  (iii) The peak-to-peak output voltage for the +20° case.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — RVDT vs LVDT and Advantages/Limitations (7 marks):**

| Parameter | LVDT | RVDT |
|---|---|---|
| Input variable | Linear displacement | Angular displacement |
| Core | Translates linearly | Rotates (cardioid profile) |
| Range | ±1 mm to ±500 mm | ±40° (typical) |
| Output | $E_o = k E_p x$ | $E_o = k E_p \theta$ |
| Sensitivity | V/V/mm | V/V/degree |
| Application | Pressure, force, displacement | Valve position, flight controls |

**Four Advantages of RVDT:**
1. **Infinite resolution** (no friction, no contact wear).
2. **High reliability and long life** (no brushes or slip rings).
3. **Excellent linearity** within ±40° (≈ ±0.5% of full scale).
4. **Bidirectional output** via phase discrimination.
5. Operates in **harsh environments** (vibration, temperature, radiation).

**Two Limitations:**
1. Requires **AC excitation** and **signal conditioning** (PSD) for DC output.
2. Limited to **±40°–±60°** angular range; not suitable for multi-turn applications.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given:
- Sensitivity $k = 2.5 \text{ V/V/degree}$
- Excitation $E_p = 5 \text{ V RMS}$
- Frequency $f = 3 \text{ kHz}$ (not directly needed for these calculations, but used if asked for $E_p$ peak)

---

**(i) Output voltage at $\theta = +20°$:**

Using $E_o = k \cdot E_p \cdot \theta$:

$$E_o = 2.5 \, \frac{\text{V}}{\text{V} \cdot \text{deg}} \times 5 \, \text{V} \times 20°$$

$$E_o = 2.5 \times 5 \times 20 = 250 \text{ V RMS}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **theoretical** output — in practice, RVDTs are designed such that this voltage is the open-circuit secondary output. The PSD then converts it to a low-level DC signal.

---

**(ii) Output voltage at $\theta = -15°$:**

$$E_o = 2.5 \times 5 \times (-15) = -187.5 \text{ V RMS}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the output is **180° out-of-phase** with the excitation $E_p$.

In other words, the AC output waveform is **inverted** relative to $E_p$. A phase-sensitive demodulator would interpret this as a **negative angular displacement** of 15°.

---

**(iii) Peak-to-peak output at +20°:**

For a sinusoidal AC signal, the peak-to-peak voltage is:
$$V_{p-p} = 2\sqrt{2} \times E_{o,\text{RMS}}$$

$$V_{p-p} = 2\sqrt{2} \times 250 = 500\sqrt{2} \approx 707.1 \text{ V}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **RVDT = Rotary Variable Differential Transformer** — measures **angular displacement**, not linear.
- Built on the **principle of mutual induction** (Faraday's Law).
- Has **1 primary + 2 secondaries (S₁, S₂) connected in series opposition**.
- **Rotor is ferromagnetic, cam/cardioid-shaped, non-contacting.**
- **Standard linear range: ±40°** (some designs up to ±60°).
- **Master Equation:** $E_o = k \cdot E_p \cdot \theta$
- **At null:** $E_o = 0$ (theoretical), with a small residual voltage in practice.
- **Direction detection** is done via **Phase-Sensitive Demodulation (PSD)** — phase of $E_o$ relative to $E_p$ indicates CW vs. CCW.
- **Sensitivity $k$** has units of **V/V/degree** (sometimes V/V/radian).
- **Output is AC** — requires demodulation to obtain a DC signal for data acquisition.
- **Key advantages:** infinite resolution, contactless, rugged, bidirectional, long life.
- **Key limitations:** limited angular range, requires AC excitation and signal conditioning.
- **Major applications:** aerospace flight controls, hydraulic valve feedback, robotics, nuclear reactor control rods, CNC machines.
- **Never confuse RVDT with a Resolver** — a Resolver is a wound-rotor synchronous machine; an RVDT is a transformer with a shaped ferromagnetic rotor.
- **Common KTU trap:** Students forget that the **rotor shape** (not just its presence) is what gives linearity.
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Always state the linear range (±40°), always mention phase-sensitive demodulation, and always write the differential output equation in your derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proximity Sensors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Proximity Sensors — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proximity Sensors

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact transducer that detects the presence, absence, or distance of a target object within a specified sensing range without any physical contact between the sensor and the target. According to the KTU 2024 Industrial Instrumentation syllabus (Module 2), proximity sensors are classified primarily by the physical principle employed — inductive, capacitive, magnetic (Hall-effect / reed), photoelectric, and ultrasonic — each suited to a specific class of target material (metallic, non-metallic, ferrous, magnetic, opaque, transparent, or liquid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** "A proximity sensor is a solid-state, non-contact switching device that produces an electrical output signal when a target object enters its defined electromagnetic, electrostatic, magnetic, optical, or acoustic field."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Tripwire"

Imagine you are walking through a hallway in the dark. Instead of a physical tripwire (a contact switch), you have an **invisible zone of energy** — say, an electromagnetic bubble around a wall-mounted box. The moment your body (which contains metal, water, or magnetic material) steps into this bubble, the box silently "clicks" and a light turns on. You never touched the box; you simply *entered its field*. That box is a **proximity sensor**.

- The **invisible bubble** = the sensor's *sensing field* (electromagnetic, electrostatic, magnetic, light beam, or sound cone).
- The **"click"** = a binary electrical transition (ON/OFF) at the sensor output.
- **Your body** = the *target object*.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

The following standard specifications appear in nearly every KTU question on proximity sensors:

- **Nominal Sensing Distance (S_n)** = the rated operating distance under standard test conditions (a standard square mild-steel target, 1 mm thick).
- **Operating Distance (S)** = the actual usable switching distance in field conditions, typically **(0.81 × S_n)** for inductive sensors.
- **Hysteresis (H)** = the difference between the switch-ON and switch-OFF distance, expressed as a **percentage of S_n**, normally **≤ 20 %** for most industrial sensors.
- **Switching Frequency (f)** = maximum number of switching operations per second, typically **100 Hz to 3 kHz**.
- **Repeatability (R)** = deviation of the switching point over multiple cycles, typically **≤ 0.01 × S_n** (i.e., 1 % of S_n).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** Always quote the relationship **S_r = 0.81 × S_n** for inductive proximity sensors in numerical problems unless otherwise specified.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (At a Glance)

| Sensor Type | Detects | Sensing Field | Typical Range |
|-------------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Inductive | Metallic (ferrous + non-ferrous) | High-frequency EM field | 0.8 mm – 60 mm |
| Capacitive | Any material (metal, plastic, liquid, wood) | Electrostatic field | 1 mm – 50 mm |
| Magnetic (Hall/Reed) | Permanent magnets | Magnetic field | 2 mm – 20 mm |
| Photoelectric | Opaque / transparent | Light beam (visible/IR/laser) | up to 60 m |
| Ultrasonic | Any solid/liquid (any material) | Sound waves (40 kHz) | 50 mm – 10 m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inductive Proximity Sensor — Working Principle

The inductive proximity sensor is the **most heavily tested** type in KTU board exams because it embodies the classic eddy-current loss principle.

**Construction (Layered Approach):**
1. **Coil (Inductor L)** wound on a ferrite core forms an LC oscillator with a capacitor C.
2. **Oscillator** generates a high-frequency alternating magnetic field at the active face, typically in the range **100 kHz – 1 MHz**.
3. **Detector / Trigger circuit** monitors the amplitude of oscillation.
4. **Output switching stage** (transistor or thyristor) drives the load.
5. **Housing** shields the coil; only the active face is exposed.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**
- **No target present:** Oscillator amplitude is high and steady. The trigger circuit holds the output in its normal (OFF) state.
- **Target approaches:** A metallic target enters the high-frequency magnetic field. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, eddy currents are induced in the target.
- **Energy loss:** These eddy currents dissipate energy (as I²R heat) drawn from the oscillator's tank circuit, which **damps the oscillation amplitude**.
- **Threshold crossing:** When the target is close enough that amplitude drops below a preset threshold, the detector flips the output state (ON).
- **Target withdraws:** Oscillation recovers; output returns to normal after accounting for **hysteresis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why only metals?** Only electrically conductive materials can support induced eddy currents. Hence inductive sensors are **immune to non-metallic targets** like wood, plastic, or water — making them ideal for harsh industrial environments.

### Key Derivation — Reduction Factor (R_f)

The **Reduction Factor** quantifies how a target's material and geometry affect sensing distance:

$$R_f = \frac{\text{Operating distance with actual target } (S_{actual})}{\text{Operating distance with standard mild-steel target } (S_{n})}$$

Typical values for KTU problems:

| Target Material | R_f (approx.) |
|-----------------|---------------|
| Mild Steel | 1.00 |
| Stainless Steel | 0.60 – 0.85 |
| Brass | 0.35 – 0.50 |
| Aluminium | 0.30 – 0.45 |
| Copper | 0.25 – 0.40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A sensor rated S_n = 8 mm with mild steel will effectively sense aluminium only at S ≈ 0.4 × 8 = **3.2 mm**. This single concept has appeared in KTU 2019, 2022, and 2024 question papers.

## 2.2 Capacitive Proximity Sensor — Working Principle

The capacitive sensor replaces the inductive coil with two concentric metal electrodes forming an open capacitor at the active face. The target acts as a third "dielectric/moving plate" of the capacitor.

The capacitance at the active face is governed by:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$

Where:
- **C** = capacitance at the sensor face (Farads)
- **ε₀** = permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (always quote this constant)
- **ε_r** = relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the target
- **A** = effective electrode area (m²)
- **d** = distance between electrode and target (m)

As the target approaches, **d decreases** → **C increases** → an RC oscillator's frequency shifts. When this frequency crosses a threshold, the output switches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Capacitive sensors can detect *non-metals* (plastics, wood, glass, paper) and *liquids* (water, oil) because ε_r of these materials (2–80) differs from air (ε_r = 1). This is the principle behind **level-switching in tanks**.

## 2.3 Magnetic Proximity Sensors — Hall Effect & Reed

### 2.3.1 Hall-Effect Sensor
When a current-carrying semiconductor is placed in a magnetic field **B**, the charge carriers experience a Lorentz force, deflecting them to one side. This produces a measurable **Hall Voltage** perpendicular to both current and field:

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$

Where:
- **V_H** = Hall voltage (Volts)
- **I** = current through the semiconductor (A)
- **B** = magnetic flux density (Tesla)
- **n** = charge carrier density (m⁻³)
- **e** = electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **t** = thickness of the semiconductor slab (m)

### 2.3.2 Reed Switch Sensor
Two ferromagnetic reed blades sealed in a glass envelope overlap with a small air gap. An approaching magnetic field magnetises the blades; opposite poles form at the gap and they **snap together** (contact closes) when the magnetic force overcomes the spring stiffness of the reeds.

## 2.4 Photoelectric Proximity Sensor — Three Modes

1. **Diffuse Reflective** — Emitter and receiver in the same housing; light reflects off the target back to the receiver. Range: **5 mm – 2 m**.
2. **Retro-Reflective** — Uses a reflector; detects when the beam is interrupted. Range: **up to 15 m**.
3. **Through-Beam** — Separate emitter and receiver; highest range, **up to 60 m**.

## 2.5 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

A piezoelectric transducer emits a **40 kHz burst**. The time-of-flight (Δt) of the echo determines the target distance:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

Where:
- **d** = distance to target (m)
- **v** = velocity of sound in air ≈ **343 m/s** (at 20 °C)
- **Δt** = round-trip time (s)
- Factor **2** is for the round-trip (there and back).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Ultrasonic sensors work in dusty, foggy, or dirty environments where photoelectric sensors fail, and they detect colour-independent objects (e.g., transparent glass bottles on a conveyor).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $S = 0.81 \times S_n$ | Operating distance vs nominal | mm |
| 2 | $H \% = \frac{S_{ON} - S_{OFF}}{S_n} \times 100$ | Hysteresis percentage | % |
| 3 | $R_f = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{S_n}$ | Reduction factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$ | Capacitive sensor capacitance | F |
| 5 | $V_H = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$ | Hall voltage | V |
| 6 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Ultrasonic distance | m |
| 7 | $S_{actual} = R_f \times S_n$ | Effective sensing distance | mm |
| 8 | $P_d = \dfrac{S_{ON}}{S_{OFF}}$ | Differential factor (rare) | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Sensing Distance for a Non-Standard Target (Inductive Sensor)

> **Problem Context (typical KTU style):** An inductive proximity sensor has a nominal sensing range $S_n = 10$ mm when tested against standard mild steel. A brass target with reduction factor $R_f = 0.45$ is to be detected. The mounting conditions (flush/non-flush) further reduce the range by a factor of 0.81. Find the actual operating distance and the hysteresis band, given $H = 15\%$ of $S_n$.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities

- $S_n = 10$ mm (nominal sensing distance, mild steel reference)
- $R_f = 0.45$ (brass target)
- Correction factor for installation = **0.81** (standard KTU convention)
- Hysteresis $H = 15\%$ of $S_n$ (for the actual target, often referenced to the corrected distance)

### Step 2 — Compute the Corrected Nominal Distance

The actual sensing distance for the brass target is:

$$S_{actual} = R_f \times S_n$$

Substituting:

$$S_{actual} = 0.45 \times 10 \text{ mm} = 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Installation Correction

Mounting conditions (flush vs non-flush) further modify the usable distance. The corrected operating distance is:

$$S = 0.81 \times S_{actual}$$

$$S = 0.81 \times 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$S = 3.645 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Hysteresis Band

The switch-ON and switch-OFF distances are separated by hysteresis. Hysteresis is specified as a percentage of the rated $S_n$:

$$H_{abs} = 0.15 \times S_n = 0.15 \times 10 = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$S_{OFF} = S_{actual} - \frac{H_{abs}}{2} = 4.5 - 0.75 = 3.75 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_{ON} = S_{actual} + \frac{H_{abs}}{2} = 4.5 + 0.75 = 5.25 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Result Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Actual Sensing Distance (Brass) | **4.5 mm** |
| Installation-Corrected Distance | **3.645 mm** |
| Switch-ON Distance | **5.25 mm** |
| Switch-OFF Distance | **3.75 mm** |
| Hysteresis Band | **1.5 mm (15 % of S_n)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition Logic Recap:** The brass target reduces the sensing range by 55 % (R_f = 0.45), then installation conditions further reduce the usable distance by 19 %. The hysteresis band is defined **about the corrected sensing distance**, not the nominal — this is the most common KTU marking trap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ultrasonic Distance Computation

> **Problem Context:** An ultrasonic proximity sensor emits a 40 kHz pulse and receives the echo after Δt = 8 ms. The ambient air temperature is 25 °C. Calculate the distance to the target.

### Step 1 — Compute the Velocity of Sound at 25 °C

The velocity of sound in air is a function of absolute temperature:

$$v = 331.4 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{273.15}}$$

Substituting $T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15$ K:

$$v = 331.4 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{298.15}{273.15}}$$

$$v = 331.4 \cdot \sqrt{1.0917}$$

$$v = 331.4 \cdot 1.0449 = 346.3 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Round-Trip Time Equation

The measured time Δt = 8 ms = 0.008 s is the **round-trip** duration. Therefore:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{346.3 \times 0.008}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{2.7704}{2} = 1.3852 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Result

The target is located at a distance of **1.385 m** from the ultrasonic sensor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in the denominator — the KTU model answer scheme deducts 2 marks for this. Always remember: time-of-flight is *round-trip*, distance is *one-way*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for KTU Application Problems

| Application Scenario | Recommended Sensor | Justification |
|----------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Detecting steel bolt on conveyor | Inductive | Non-contact, immune to oil/dust |
| Detecting water level in glass tank | Capacitive | Detects non-metal liquids |
| Counting ferromagnetic gear teeth | Magnetic (Hall) | High-frequency, low cost |
| Detecting transparent PET bottle | Ultrasonic / Retro-reflective | Colour- and transparency-independent |
| Detecting metal at 30 m | Photoelectric (through-beam) | Longest range |
| Counting car wheels at toll booth | Inductive (embedded in road) | Flush mounting possible |
| Detecting cardboard box on line | Capacitive or Ultrasonic | Non-metal surface |
| Detecting label on a bottle | Capacitive (high-frequency type) | Differentiates by ε_r |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Proximity Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply DC 24V] --> B[Oscillator / Field Generator]
    B --> C[Sensing Field at Active Face]
    C --> D{Target Enters Field?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Detector / Trigger Circuit]
    D -- No --> F[Output Remains Normal OFF]
    E --> G[Output Switching Stage]
    G --> H[Load Indicator Lamp or PLC Input]
    F --> I[Output Stage in Resting State]
    G --> J[Hysteresis Comparator]
    J --> K[Output State Latched]
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Proximity Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Proximity Sensor] --> Q{Type of Sensing Field}
    Q --> R[Inductive HF EM Field]
    Q --> S[Capacitive Electrostatic Field]
    Q --> T[Magnetic Hall or Reed]
    Q --> U[Photoelectric Light Beam]
    Q --> V[Ultrasonic 40 kHz Sound]

    R --> R1[Metals only]
    R --> R2[Range up to 60 mm]

    S --> S1[Metals and Non-metals]
    S --> S2[Liquids and Solids]

    T --> T1[Permanent Magnets]
    T --> T2[Ferromagnetic Targets]

    U --> U1[Diffuse Reflective]
    U --> U2[Retro-Reflective]
    U --> U3[Through-Beam]

    V --> V1[All Materials]
    V --> V2[Dust and Fog Tolerant]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Inductive Proximity Sensor (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[DC Supply 24V] --> A2[Voltage Regulator]
    A2 --> A3[HF Oscillator 100 kHz to 1 MHz]
    A3 --> A4[Inductor Coil on Ferrite Core]
    A4 --> A5[EM Field at Active Face]

    A5 --> B1{Target Approaches?}
    B1 -- No --> B2[Oscillator Amplitude HIGH]
    B2 --> B3[Output OFF]

    B1 -- Yes --> C1[Eddy Currents Induced in Target]
    C1 --> C2[Energy Loss from Tank Circuit]
    C2 --> C3[Oscillator Amplitude DROPS]
    C3 --> C4[Threshold Detector Triggers]
    C4 --> C5[Output Transistor ON]
    C5 --> C6[Output ON to Load]
```

## 4.4 Equivalent Electrical Model of the Inductive Sensor Tank Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Inductor L on Ferrite Core] --> N1[Node A]
    C[Capacitor C] --> N1
    N1 --> R[Internal Resistance of Coil]
    R --> N2[Node B]
    N2 --> D[Detector Input]
    D --> E[Amplitude Threshold Comparator]
    E --> F[Output Stage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The LC tank circuit is the heart of the inductive proximity sensor. The moment a conductive target enters the field, the effective resistance of the tank **increases** (due to eddy-current loss). This drop in Q-factor dampens the oscillation, which the comparator senses as the switch-point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> Define the term "nominal sensing distance" for a proximity sensor. How does it differ from the actual operating distance?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **nominal sensing distance (S_n)** is the rated switching distance of a proximity sensor measured under standard test conditions — a square mild-steel target of 1 mm thickness, moving axially toward the active face, at a defined ambient temperature (typically 23 °C). The **actual operating distance (S)** is the usable sensing distance in real field conditions, accounting for mounting, target material, and temperature. Empirically, **S = 0.81 × S_n** for inductive sensors (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for explanation).

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> Why are inductive proximity sensors unable to detect non-metallic targets like wood or plastic?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Inductive proximity sensors operate on the principle of **eddy-current losses** in a conductive target (1 mark). A high-frequency alternating magnetic field induces circulating eddy currents in the target; the resulting I²R losses damp the oscillator amplitude and trigger the output (1 mark). Non-metallic materials such as wood, plastic, paper, and ceramics are **electrical insulators** — they cannot support eddy currents (1 mark). Hence the oscillator remains unaffected, and the sensor does not switch.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]**
> **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an inductive proximity sensor. Discuss the effect of target material on its sensing distance. (7 marks)
> **b)** An inductive proximity sensor has a nominal sensing distance of $S_n = 8$ mm for mild steel. Find the actual operating distance if a brass target ($R_f = 0.40$) is used. Also calculate the switch-ON and switch-OFF distances if hysteresis is 10 % of $S_n$. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Working Principle (Block Diagram in Words):**

The inductive proximity sensor consists of four main blocks:
1. **High-frequency LC oscillator** (100 kHz – 1 MHz) — generates alternating current through the sensing coil wound on a ferrite core.
2. **Sensing coil / active face** — produces an alternating electromagnetic field extending outward from the face.
3. **Detector / trigger circuit** — monitors the amplitude of oscillation; the amplitude is high when no target is present.
4. **Solid-state output stage** (transistor, thyristor, or MOSFET) — drives the external load.

**Operation:** When a metallic target approaches the active face, eddy currents are induced in the target (Faraday's Law). These eddy currents dissipate energy drawn from the LC tank, causing the oscillation amplitude to drop. When the amplitude falls below a preset threshold, the detector toggles the output state from OFF to ON. When the target withdraws, oscillation recovers and the output resets after accounting for hysteresis (2 marks for the block diagram, 3 marks for the working, 2 marks for the target-material effect).

**Effect of Target Material:** Different metals have different conductivity, permeability, and geometry. These are captured by the **Reduction Factor**:

$$R_f = \frac{S_{actual}}{S_n}$$

Ferrous materials (mild steel) have $R_f = 1.0$; stainless steel $≈ 0.7$; brass $≈ 0.4$; aluminium $≈ 0.35$. Therefore the same sensor switches at much shorter distances for non-ferrous metals.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $S_n = 8$ mm (mild steel)
- $R_f = 0.40$ (brass)
- Hysteresis $H = 10\%$ of $S_n$

**Step 1 — Actual operating distance:**

$$S_{actual} = R_f \times S_n = 0.40 \times 8 = 3.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Hysteresis band:**

$$H_{abs} = 0.10 \times S_n = 0.10 \times 8 = 0.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Switch-ON and Switch-OFF distances:**

$$S_{OFF} = S_{actual} - \frac{H_{abs}}{2} = 3.2 - 0.4 = 2.8 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_{ON} = S_{actual} + \frac{H_{abs}}{2} = 3.2 + 0.4 = 3.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Actual operating distance = **3.2 mm**
- Switch-OFF distance = **2.8 mm**
- Switch-ON distance = **3.6 mm**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Equivalent]**
> **a)** Explain the working principle of a capacitive proximity sensor. How is it used for liquid level measurement? (7 marks)
> **b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a Hall-effect proximity sensor. Derive the expression for Hall voltage. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Working Principle:** A capacitive proximity sensor has two concentric metal electrodes at its active face, forming an open-plate capacitor. Together with an internal reference capacitor and a feedback resistor, these electrodes form part of an RC oscillator. The capacitance at the active face depends on the permittivity of the material in front of it:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$

When a target (any material — metal, plastic, wood, liquid) approaches, the effective distance **d** decreases, increasing **C**. This shifts the RC oscillator's frequency. When the frequency crosses a threshold, the output switches (3 marks for principle, 2 marks for equation, 2 marks for application).

**Liquid Level Measurement:** The sensor is mounted on the outer wall of a non-metallic tank (e.g., glass or plastic). As the liquid level rises, the ε_r of the medium in front of the sensor changes (from air ε_r = 1 to water ε_r ≈ 80). The capacitance jumps, triggering the output. Thus the sensor acts as a **point-level switch** for liquids without contact with the fluid.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Working Principle of Hall-Effect Sensor:**
A Hall-effect sensor consists of a thin slab of semiconductor material (typically Indium Antimonide or Gallium Arsenide) through which a control current **I** is passed. When the slab is exposed to a magnetic field **B** perpendicular to the current, the charge carriers experience a Lorentz force, deflecting them to one side of the slab. This charge separation produces a measurable voltage across the slab, called the **Hall voltage**, perpendicular to both **I** and **B**. The Hall voltage is processed by an amplifier and trigger circuit to switch the output (4 marks for block diagram and description).

**Derivation of Hall Voltage:**

Consider a semiconductor slab of thickness **t** carrying a current **I** in the x-direction, placed in a magnetic field **B** along the y-direction.

The Lorentz force on a charge carrier of charge **e** moving with drift velocity **v_d** is:

$$F = e \cdot v_d \cdot B$$

This force pushes the carriers to one side, creating a charge accumulation and hence a transverse electric field **E_H**. In equilibrium, the electric force balances the magnetic force:

$$e \cdot E_H = e \cdot v_d \cdot B$$

$$E_H = v_d \cdot B$$

The Hall voltage is the electric field multiplied by the width **w** of the slab:

$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d \cdot B \cdot w$$

Now, the drift velocity can be expressed in terms of the current and the carrier density:

$$I = n \cdot e \cdot A \cdot v_d = n \cdot e \cdot (w \cdot t) \cdot v_d$$

Solving for $v_d$:

$$v_d = \frac{I}{n \cdot e \cdot w \cdot t}$$

Substituting back:

$$V_H = \frac{I}{n \cdot e \cdot w \cdot t} \cdot B \cdot w = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$

$$\boxed{V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}}$$

(2 marks for the final expression, 1 mark for derivation logic).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2 in ultrasonic time-of-flight** — costs 2 marks. The time is *round-trip*; distance is *one-way*.
> 2. **Using S_n instead of S_actual for hysteresis calculation** — KTU expects hysteresis to be a percentage of the *nominal* S_n, but the switch-ON/OFF distances must be centred on the *actual* S_actual. Confusing these centres loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing reduction factor with installation factor** — They are *different*. Reduction factor accounts for target material; the 0.81 factor accounts for flush vs non-flush mounting.
> 4. **Writing only the equation for Hall voltage without units** — KTU's marking scheme requires **explicit units** at the end of every numerical answer.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark questions** — the block diagram alone carries **2 marks**; skipping it caps your score at 5/7.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Proximity sensors are non-contact** — they detect targets via a sensing field, never by mechanical contact.
- ⭐ **Five main types** for KTU: Inductive, Capacitive, Magnetic (Hall/Reed), Photoelectric, Ultrasonic.
- ⭐ **Inductive** = metals only; **Capacitive** = all materials; **Hall** = magnets; **Photo** = light contrast; **Ultrasonic** = sound reflection.
- ⭐ **Nominal sensing distance $S_n$** is the lab-rated value; **actual distance S = 0.81 × S_n** in field conditions.
- ⭐ **Reduction factor $R_f$** = ratio of sensing distance on a non-standard target to that on mild steel.
- ⭐ **Hysteresis** is typically **≤ 20 % of $S_n$** — always quote both $S_{ON}$ and $S_{OFF}$ values.
- ⭐ **Inductive sensor relies on eddy-current losses** in the target — derived from Faraday's Law.
- ⭐ **Capacitive sensor equation:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$.
- ⭐ **Hall voltage equation:** $V_H = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$.
- ⭐ **Ultrasonic equation:** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$, with $v \approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C.
- ⭐ **Photoelectric modes:** Diffuse, Retro-reflective, Through-Beam (in order of increasing range).
- ⭐ **Standard test target:** 1 mm thick square mild-steel plate, axially aligned, at 23 °C.
- ⭐ **Switching frequency** of inductive sensors: 100 Hz – 3 kHz; **repeatability** ≤ 1 % of $S_n$.
- ⭐ **Capacitive sensors detect non-metals** because $\varepsilon_r$ of the target changes the capacitance.
- ⭐ **Always draw a block diagram** in 7-mark questions — it carries independent marks.
- ⭐ **Always include units** in final answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hall Effect Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hall Effect Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Hall Effect Devices — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Hall Effect** is a magneto-electric phenomenon discovered by **Edwin H. Hall in 1879**, in which a transverse electromotive force (the **Hall Voltage, $V_H$**) is generated across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor strip when it is subjected to a perpendicular magnetic flux density **$B$**. This transverse voltage is established in the direction mutually perpendicular to both the conventional current $I$ and the applied magnetic field $B$, and forms the working principle of a family of non-contact solid-state sensors known as **Hall Effect Devices** (or **Hall Sensors / Hall Elements**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim standard):**  
> *"A Hall Effect device is a solid-state transducer that converts a magnetic field intensity into a measurable electrical voltage (Hall EMF), proportional to the product of the control current and the perpendicular magnetic flux density, governed by the Lorentz force on charge carriers."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **wide, shallow river flowing due North** (this represents the control current $I$ through a thin semiconductor strip). Now imagine a **strong wind blowing from West to East** perpendicular to the flow (this represents the magnetic flux density $B$).

The water molecules, originally flowing straight, will be **deflected sideways** toward either the East bank or West bank depending on their charge sign. This lateral crowding of water exerts a sideways hydrostatic pressure, and if we measure the water-level difference between the two banks, we get a **voltage-like quantity** proportional to the strength of the wind and the rate of water flow.
