# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer Type)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`

**State Bernoulli's Theorem. List any four assumptions made in its derivation.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (2 Marks):**
Bernoulli's Theorem states that in a steady, incompressible, non-viscous, and irrotational flow of a fluid, the total energy (sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy) per unit mass remains constant at every point along a streamline.

Mathematically:

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

**Four Assumptions (1 Mark — any four):**
1. Steady flow (time-independent).
2. Incompressible fluid (constant density).
3. Non-viscous (ideal fluid, no shear stress).
4. Irrotational flow.
5. No external work or heat transfer between the two points.
6. Equation applies along a single streamline.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between the Ideal Bernoulli's Equation and the Modified Bernoulli's Equation for real fluids.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Ideal Bernoulli's Equation | Modified Bernoulli's Equation |
|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Fluid type | Ideal (non-viscous) | Real (viscous) |
| Energy loss | Neglected (h_L = 0) | Included as head loss h_L > 0 |
| Use case | Theoretical analysis only | Practical industrial flow measurement |
| Equation form | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ |

**Practical Example (extra credit):** A venturi meter in a process plant uses the modified form because h_L, though small, becomes significant over long flow paths.

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions for Module 3 carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Below are **TWO complete alternative questions (A and B)**, each with two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Starting from Euler's equation of motion, derive Bernoulli's equation in the head form. State clearly all assumptions used.**

#### **Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State Euler's equation for fluid flow along a streamline:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho \frac{Dv}{Dt}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Add the gravity component along the streamline (component = $-g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$):

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = \rho v \frac{\partial v}{\partial s}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Rewrite as a perfect differential form:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho v \frac{\partial v}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Multiply by ds and integrate from point 1 to point 2:

$$\int_1^2 dP + \rho \int_1^2 v \, dv + \rho g \int_1^2 dz = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Evaluate each integral (assuming ρ is constant due to incompressibility):

$$P_2 - P_1 + \frac{\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2)}{2} + \rho g(z_2 - z_1) = 0$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Rearrange to the energy form:

$$P_1 + \frac{\rho v_1^2}{2} + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{\rho v_2^2}{2} + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Divide by ρg to obtain the head form, and list the assumptions (steady, incompressible, non-viscous, irrotational, no external work):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**A horizontal venturi meter has an inlet diameter of 20 cm and a throat diameter of 10 cm. Water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) flows through it. The differential pressure measured between the inlet and throat is 5 kPa. Calculate: (i) The flow velocity at the throat, (ii) The volumetric flow rate, (iii) The mass flow rate. Assume ideal flow (C_d = 1).**

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- D₁ = 20 cm = 0.20 m, so A₁ = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2$ = **0.03142 m²**
- D₂ = 10 cm = 0.10 m, so A₂ = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2$ = **0.007854 m²**
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³
- ΔP = P₁ - P₂ = 5000 Pa
- z₁ = z₂ (horizontal)

**Step 1: Apply Continuity (1 Mark)**

$$v_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} v_2 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 v_2 = \left(\frac{0.10}{0.20}\right)^2 v_2 = 0.25 v_2$$

**Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation (1 Mark)**

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

$$5000 = \frac{1000}{2}\left[v_2^2 - (0.25 v_2)^2\right]$$

**Step 3: Simplify the Bracket (1 Mark)**

$$5000 = 500 \left[v_2^2 - 0.0625 v_2^2\right] = 500 \times 0.9375 \, v_2^2$$

$$5000 = 468.75 \, v_2^2$$

**Step 4: Solve for Throat Velocity v₂ (1 Mark)**

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{5000}{468.75}} = \sqrt{10.667} = \mathbf{3.266 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 5: Volumetric Flow Rate Q (1 Mark)**

$$Q = A_2 v_2 = 0.007854 \times 3.266 = \mathbf{0.02565 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 92.34 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 6: Mass Flow Rate ṁ (1 Mark)**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.02565 = \mathbf{25.65 \text{ kg/s}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the working of a Pitot tube as a flow measuring device using Bernoulli's principle. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow.**

#### **Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
A Pitot tube is a flow measurement device that uses Bernoulli's principle to measure the **stagnation (total) pressure** of a flowing fluid. It consists of two concentric tubes:
- The **inner tube** has an opening facing the flow → measures **stagnation pressure** (P₀ = P + ½ρv²)
- The **outer tube** has openings perpendicular to the flow → measures **static pressure** (P)

**Derivation (4 Marks):**
Let the undisturbed flow have static pressure P and velocity v. At the opening facing the flow, the fluid is brought to rest (stagnation point), so velocity becomes 0 and pressure rises to P₀.

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the free stream and the stagnation point (same elevation):

$$P + \frac{\rho v^2}{2} + 0 = P_0 + 0 + 0$$

$$\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = P_0 - P$$

Solving for v:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$$

**Discharge Coefficient (1 Mark):**
In practice, a correction factor C_v (velocity coefficient) is applied:

$$v_{actual} = C_v \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$$
where C_v ≈ 0.98 to 1.0 for well-designed Pitot tubes.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**An orifice plate with a 50 mm diameter is installed in a 150 mm diameter pipe carrying oil of density 850 kg/m³. The pressure difference across the orifice is measured as 30 kPa. If the discharge coefficient is 0.62, calculate the volumetric flow rate.**

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- D₁ = 0.15 m → A₁ = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2$ = **0.01767 m²**
- d = 0.05 m → A₂ = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2$ = **0.001963 m²**
- ρ = 850 kg/m³
- P₁ - P₂ = 30,000 Pa
- C_d = 0.62

**Step 1: Write the Flow Equation (1 Mark)**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Area Ratio Term (1 Mark)**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{0.05}{0.15}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = 0.1111$$

$$1 - (0.1111)^2 = 1 - 0.01235 = 0.9877$$

**Step 3: Compute the Numerator Term (1 Mark)**

$$2(P_1 - P_2) = 2 \times 30{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Denominator Term (1 Mark)**

$$\rho \times 0.9877 = 850 \times 0.9877 = 839.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Compute the Square Root Term (1 Mark)**

$$\sqrt{\frac{60{,}000}{839.5}} = \sqrt{71.47} = 8.454 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Compute Q (1 Mark)**

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.001963 \times 8.454 = \mathbf{0.01029 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 37.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the area ratio correction term** $\left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]$ in the denominator — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply C_d** (discharge coefficient) when the problem specifies it — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up gauge and absolute pressure** — always check the problem statement for "gauge" or "absolute."
> 4. **Not converting kPa to Pa** before substituting into SI equations — costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong unit for Q** — final answer must be in **m³/s** (SI base unit) or **m³/hr** (engineering unit). Never leave it in mixed units.
> 6. **Missing the "horizontal" assumption** in the Bernoulli equation — must explicitly state z₁ = z₂ to drop the elevation term.

---

## **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam!)**

- ✅ Bernoulli's Theorem is **conservation of energy per unit weight** for an ideal fluid along a streamline.
- ✅ The three heads are: **Pressure Head** ($P/\rho g$), **Velocity Head** ($v^2/2g$), **Elevation Head** ($z$).
- ✅ The total of these three heads is **constant** at all points along a streamline.
- ✅ All five assumptions (steady, incompressible, non-viscous, irrotational, no external work) are **mandatory** for the ideal form.
- ✅ Real fluids need a **head loss term (h_L)** on the right-hand side of the equation.
- ✅ **Continuity Equation** ($A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$) is the **mandatory companion** of Bernoulli's equation for solving flow problems.
- ✅ Every differential pressure flow meter (orifice, venturi, nozzle, pitot) derives its flow equation from Bernoulli + Continuity.
- ✅ **Discharge Coefficient C_d** is the **fudge factor** that accounts for real-fluid losses. Typical values: Venturi ≈ 0.98, Nozzle ≈ 0.96, Orifice ≈ 0.62.
- ✅ **Pitot tube** measures the difference between **stagnation** and **static** pressure, giving velocity directly: $v = \sqrt{2(P_0 - P)/\rho}$.
- ✅ **Standard KTU-accepted values:** g = 9.81 m/s², ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³.
- ✅ Always convert all units to **SI base units** (Pa, m, kg, m³, s) before substituting into equations.
- ✅ In a **horizontal** system, the elevation term z drops out — simplify the equation immediately.
- ✅ The **final flow rate equation** with discharge coefficient is: $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orifice Plate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Orifice Plate # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Orifice Plate Definition (ISO 5167-1:2003 / ISO 5167-2:2003 Standard)** > An **Orifice Plate** is a thin, flat, concentric (or eccentric/segmental) circular metal plate with a precisely machined sharp-edged (knife-edge) circular bore, installed perpendicular to the axis of flow inside a closed conduit (pipe). It is the most widely used **primary flow element (PFE)** for **differential pressure (DP) type flow measurement** of clean liquids, gases, and steam in industrial process plants, conforming to international standards **ISO 5167-1:2003** and **ASME MFC-3M**. The device operates on the principle of creating a **constriction** in the flow path. As the fluid approaches the orifice, its velocity increases and its static pressure drops. This measurable **differential pressure (ΔP)** is mathematically related to the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** or **mass flow rate (ṁ)** of the fluid. ## 1.2 Working Principle – The Engineering Phenomenon The orifice plate exploits two fundamental fluid mechanics principles simultaneously: 1. **Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass):** When the cross-sectional area of a pipe is reduced, the velocity of the fluid must increase to maintain a constant mass flow rate. 2. **Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy):** An increase in fluid velocity is accompanied by a corresponding decrease in its static pressure head. The plate creates a **vena contracta** (a point downstream of the orifice where the flow streamlines are parallel and the cross-section is minimum) where pressure is minimum. The pressure is sensed **upstream (P₁)** and **downstream (P₂)** of the plate, and the difference **(P₁ – P₂)** is fed to a **DPT (Differential Pressure Transmitter)**. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚿 > [!TIP] > **Think of a Garden Hose with your Thumb!** 💧 > > When you press your thumb partially over the opening of a water hose, you create a constriction similar to an orifice plate. The water shoots out **faster** (velocity increases) and the **pressure behind your thumb drops** (you can feel the suction). > > - The **hole between your thumb and hose** = the orifice bore. > - The **faster water jet** = higher velocity at the vena contracta. > - The **pressure difference** you feel = the ΔP measured across an orifice plate. > - The **size of the gap** = the **Beta ratio (β = d/D)**. > > An industrial orifice plate simply does this on a precise, engineered, instrumented scale using a calibrated DP transmitter. ## 1.4 Physical Standards and Constants | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | **Beta Ratio (β)** | **d/D = 0.20 to 0.75** (ISO 5167 limit) | | Plate Thickness | ≤ 0.05 × D (thin plate) | | Upstream straight pipe | ≥ 10 × D to 50 × D (depends on fittings) | | Material of Construction | **SS-304, SS-316, Monel, Hastelloy** | | Standard | **ISO 5167-1:2003**, **ASME MFC-3M-2004** | | Bore Tolerance | **± 0.0005 × d** (precision machined) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pay special attention to the **types of orifice plates** (concentric, eccentric, segmental), **tap locations** (flange, corner, D and D/2, pipe taps), and the **flow equations involving the discharge coefficient (C_d)**. These are guaranteed 14-mark questions in KTU ESE. --- # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Orifice Plate Assembly An installed orifice plate system consists of: - **Primary Element:** The orifice plate itself (precision-bored disc). - **Pressure Taps:** Two pressure take-off points (upstream and downstream). - **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT):** Converts ΔP to a 4–20 mA signal. - **Manifold Valves (3-valve / 5-valve manifold):** For isolation, equalizing, and calibration. - **Impulse Lines:** Stainless steel tubing connecting taps to DPT. - **Flow Computer / Indicator:** Computes Q from ΔP using the standard flow equation. ## 2.2 Tap Locations (KTU High-Yield Topic) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Focus Area** — Tap locations dictate the practical installation of the orifice plate and influence the **discharge coefficient (C_d)**. | Tap Type | Location (Upstream Tap) | Location (Downstream Tap) | Standard Reference | |---|---|---|---| | **Flange Taps** | **25.4 mm (1 inch)** from upstream face of plate | **25.4 mm (1 inch)** from downstream face of plate | ASME / ISA | | **Corner Taps** | Exactly at the **upstream face** of the plate | Exactly at the **downstream face** of the plate | ISO 5167-1 | | **D and D/2 Taps** | At a distance of **1×D** (one pipe diameter) upstream | At a distance of **0.5×D** (half pipe diameter) downstream | ISO 5167-1 | | **Pipe Taps (vena contracta)** | **2.5 × D** upstream | **8 × D** downstream (at vena contracta) | Not ISO standard | ## 2.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown ### Step 1: Fluid Acceleration at the Orifice By the **Continuity Equation**, as the area reduces from pipe area **A₁ = πD²/4** to orifice area **A₂ = πd²/4**, the velocity must increase: $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_2 = \frac{A_1}{A_2} v_1 = \frac{D^2}{d^2} v_1 = \frac{1}{\beta^2} v_1$$ ### Step 2: Pressure Drop via Bernoulli's Equation Applying Bernoulli's equation between upstream (point 1) and the **vena contracta** (point 2), and accounting for the **coefficient of velocity (C_v)** to handle non-ideal (viscous) flow: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{C_v^2 \cdot v_2^2}{2g}$$ Solving for the actual velocity at the **vena contracta**: $$v_2' = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$$ ### Step 3: Account for Flow Contraction (Vena Contracta) The flow streamlines do not regain full pipe diameter exactly at the orifice — they contract to a minimum section called the **vena contracta**. The **coefficient of contraction (C_c)** is the ratio of the jet area at the vena contracta to the orifice area: $$C_c = \frac{A_{vena\,contracta}}{A_{orifice}}$$ ### Step 4: Combined Discharge Coefficient The product of **C_v** and **C_c** is the overall **coefficient of discharge (C_d)**, which is the most critical parameter in orifice plate flow measurement: $$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$$ For ISO 5167-compliant orifice plates, **C_d ≈ 0.60 to 0.65** (typical value ≈ **0.62**). ### Step 5: Volumetric and Mass Flow Rate Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Master Orifice Flow Equation (Volume Flow):** > $$\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}}$$ > > **The Master Orifice Flow Equation (Mass Flow):** > $$\boxed{\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}}$$ Where: - **Q** = volumetric flow rate (m³/s) - **ṁ** = mass flow rate (kg/s) - **C_d** = coefficient of discharge (dimensionless) - **A₂** = orifice bore area = πd²/4 (m²) - **ΔP** = differential pressure = P₁ – P₂ (Pa) - **ρ** = fluid density (kg/m³) - **β** = diameter ratio = d/D (dimensionless) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 | # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | KTU Importance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Beta Ratio** | $\beta = d/D$ | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | **Volumetric Flow Rate** | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P / [\rho(1-\beta^4)]}$ | m³/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | **Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\rho \Delta P / (1-\beta^4)}$ | kg/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | **Discharge Coefficient** | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | **Typical C_d value** | $C_d \approx 0.60 \text{ to } 0.65$ | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 6 | **Velocity of Approach Factor** | $E = 1 / \sqrt{1-\beta^4}$ | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 7 | **Reynolds Number at Orifice** | $Re_D = \rho v_1 D / \mu$ | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 8 | **Pressure Recovery Factor** | $R = \Delta P_{permanent} / \Delta P_{max}$ | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 9 | **Orifice Bore Area** | $A_2 = \pi d^2 / 4$ | m² | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 10 | **Stagnation Pressure (Pitot)** | $P_0 = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Pa | ⭐⭐ | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Orifice plates are the **backbone of custody transfer flow metering** in oil & gas pipelines, steam flow measurement in power plants, water distribution networks, and HVAC systems. Almost every refinery and petrochemical complex in Kerala (BPCL Kochi, FACT) uses thousands of orifice plates in their DCS/PLC control loops. --- # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Orifice Flow Equation ### Stage 1: Apply the Continuity Equation Consider an incompressible fluid flowing through a pipe of diameter **D** with velocity **v₁**. The orifice bore has diameter **d** with velocity **v₂**. The flow is steady and the fluid is assumed to be ideal (initially). By the **continuity equation** (conservation of mass for incompressible flow): $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$ Substituting the areas: $$\frac{\pi D^2}{4} v_1 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} v_2$$ Solving for **v₂**: $$v_2 = \frac{D^2}{d^2} v_1 = \frac{1}{\beta^2} v_1$$ **Physical meaning:** Since the orifice diameter **d** is much smaller than the pipe diameter **D**, the fluid velocity at the orifice is significantly higher than the upstream velocity. ### Stage 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal, Frictionless Case) Bernoulli's equation between the upstream point (1) and the **vena contracta** (point 2), assuming ideal (frictionless) flow and ignoring the small height difference: $$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$ Rearranging to isolate the velocity at the vena contracta: $$\frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_1 - P_2 = \Delta P$$ $$\frac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2) = \Delta P$$ ### Stage 3: Eliminate v₂ using Continuity Substituting **v₂ = v₁/β²** into the Bernoulli result: $$\frac{1}{2}\rho \left[\left(\frac{v_1}{\beta^2}\right)^2 - v_1^2\right] = \Delta P$$ $$\frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 \left[\frac{1}{\beta^4} - 1\right] = \Delta P$$ Factoring out **v₁²**: $$\frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 \cdot \frac{(1 - \beta^4)}{\beta^4} = \Delta P$$ Solving for **v₁** (the theoretical approach velocity): $$v_1 = \beta^2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$$ ### Stage 4: Account for Real-Fluid Effects (Discharge Coefficient) In real flow, three corrections are needed: - **C_v (Velocity Coefficient):** Accounts for viscous friction losses → v_real < v_ideal. - **C_c (Contraction Coefficient):** The jet at vena contracta is smaller than the orifice area → actual flow area < A₂. - **Expansibility Factor (Y) [for gases]:** For compressible fluids, an additional factor Y < 1 corrects for gas expansion. The **mass flow rate** through the actual jet is: $$\dot{m}_{ideal} = \rho A_2 v_2$$ Correcting with the discharge coefficient **C_d = C_c · C_v**: $$\dot{m}_{real} = C_d \cdot \rho A_2 v_{2,ideal}$$ **After all the algebra and corrections, the final industry-standard flow equation is:** $$\boxed{\dot{m} = C_d \cdot Y \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > For **incompressible fluids (liquids)**, the expansibility factor **Y = 1** and the equation reduces to the simpler form given in Section 2.3. For **compressible fluids (gases, steam)**, **Y < 1** and must be calculated from ISO 5167 charts. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style) > **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]:** > > An orifice plate with a bore diameter **d = 50 mm** is fitted in a pipe of diameter **D = 100 mm**. Water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) is flowing through the pipe. A differential pressure transmitter reads **ΔP = 50 kPa**. Take the coefficient of discharge **C_d = 0.62**. Calculate: > > (a) The Beta ratio β > (b) The mass flow rate ṁ > (c) The volumetric flow rate Q > (d) The approach velocity v₁ ### Solution – Step-by-Step **(a) Beta Ratio:** $$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} = 0.5$$ **(b) Mass Flow Rate ṁ:** First, calculate the orifice area: $$A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ Next, compute the term **(1 - β⁴):** $$\beta^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$ $$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$ Now substitute into the mass flow equation: $$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.62 \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 50{,}000}{0.9375}}$$ Compute the numerator inside the square root: $$2 \times 1000 \times 50{,}000 = 1.0 \times 10^8$$ Divide by **0.9375:** $$\frac{1.0 \times 10^8}{0.9375} = 1.0667 \times 10^8$$ Take the square root: $$\sqrt{1.0667 \times 10^8} = 10{,}327.6 \text{ m/s (intermediate)}$$ Multiply by C_d × A₂: $$\dot{m} = 0.62 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 10{,}327.6$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 12.57 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **(c) Volumetric Flow Rate Q:** $$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{12.57}{1000} = 0.01257 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ Converting to litres per minute (LPM): $$Q = 0.01257 \times 60 \times 1000 = 754.2 \text{ LPM}$$ **(d) Approach Velocity v₁:** $$v_1 = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A_1} = \frac{12.57}{1000 \times \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4}} = \frac{12.57}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{v_1 \approx 1.6 \text{ m/s}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Check:** Always write the **unit at every step** of the calculation. The most common mistake students make is forgetting to convert mm to m or kPa to Pa. Markers specifically deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency. --- # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Orifice Plate Flow Measurement Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD PipeA["PIPE WITH FLOWING FLUID

The difference (P1 – P3) is the **permanent pressure loss** that cannot be recovered. For an orifice plate, this loss is approximately **40% to 60% of the maximum ΔP** measured. This is a major disadvantage of orifice plates compared to Venturi tubes. --- # 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Define an orifice plate. List the different types of orifice plates used in industrial flow measurement."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An orifice plate is a **thin, flat, circular metal plate with a sharp-edged (knife-edge) concentric bore**, installed in a pipeline to create a pressure drop for flow measurement. It is a **primary flow element (PFE)** based on the **differential pressure principle**. **Types of orifice plates:** 1. **Concentric** — circular bore centered on the pipe axis (most common; for clean fluids). 2. **Eccentric** — bore offset from center (for fluids containing suspended solids/slurries). 3. **Segmental** — semicircular opening (for two-phase flow or dirty fluids). 4. **Quadrant edge** — rounded inlet edge (for highly viscous fluids at low Reynolds number). 5. **Conical entrance** — conical inlet profile (for very low Reynolds number flow). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"What is the coefficient of discharge (C_d) of an orifice plate? How is it related to C_c and C_v?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **coefficient of discharge (C_d)** is defined as the ratio of the **actual (real) flow rate** through the orifice to the **theoretical (ideal) flow rate** calculated using the idealized Bernoulli's equation. It is the product of two individual coefficients: $$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$$ - **C_c (Coefficient of Contraction):** Ratio of jet area at the **vena contracta** to the orifice bore area. Typical value: **0.61 to 0.65**. - **C_v (Coefficient of Velocity):** Ratio of actual velocity at the vena contracta to the theoretical velocity. Typical value: **0.95 to 0.99**. For ISO 5167-standard concentric orifice plates, the typical value of **C_d ≈ 0.60 to 0.65**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE) ### ✍️ **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Style]** > **(a)** Derive the **general flow equation for an orifice meter** in terms of discharge coefficient (C_d), orifice area (A₂), differential pressure (ΔP), beta ratio (β), and fluid density (ρ). State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** An orifice meter is installed in a pipe of diameter **D = 200 mm** carrying oil (ρ = 850 kg/m³, μ = 0.009 Pa·s). The orifice bore is **d = 100 mm**. The differential pressure measured is **ΔP = 80 kPa** and the discharge coefficient **C_d = 0.62**. Calculate: > > (i) The mass flow rate of oil. > (ii) The volumetric flow rate in **m³/hr**. > (iii) The Reynolds number at the orifice. > > Take dynamic viscosity μ = 9 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ **Model Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — Derivation of the Orifice Flow Equation (7 Marks) **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, incompressible, Newtonian flow. 2. Frictionless (ideal) flow along streamlines — corrected later using C_d. 3. Uniform velocity profile upstream of the orifice. 4. No significant elevation change between upstream and downstream taps. 5. Pressure taps are located where flow is parallel (no turbulence). **Derivation:** Apply **Continuity Equation** between upstream (point 1, area A₁) and orifice (point 2, area A₂): $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_2 = \frac{A_1}{A_2} v_1 = \frac{D^2}{d^2} v_1 = \frac{1}{\beta^2} v_1$$ Apply **Bernoulli's Equation** (ideal, frictionless) between points 1 and 2: $$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$ Rearranging: $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 \left[\frac{1}{\beta^4} - 1\right]$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 \cdot \frac{(1 - \beta^4)}{\beta^4}$$ Solving for the theoretical velocity: $$v_{1,th} = \beta^2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$$ The theoretical mass flow rate is: $$\dot{m}_{th} = \rho A_2 v_2 = \rho A_2 \cdot \frac{v_1}{\beta^2} = \rho A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$$ Introducing the **discharge coefficient C_d** to account for vena contracta and viscous effects: $$\boxed{\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}}$$ And the volumetric flow rate: $$\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}}$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** D = 200 mm = 0.2 m, d = 100 mm = 0.1 m, ρ = 850 kg/m³, ΔP = 80 kPa = 80,000 Pa, C_d = 0.62, μ = 9 × 10⁻³ Pa·s **Step 1: Beta Ratio** $$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5 \quad ; \quad \beta^4 = 0.0625 \quad ; \quad 1 - \beta^4 = 0.9375$$ **Step 2: Orifice Area** $$A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3: Mass Flow Rate (i)** $$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 850 \times 80{,}000}{0.9375}}$$ Inner calculation: $$2 \times 850 \times 80{,}000 = 1.36 \times 10^8$$ $$\frac{1.36 \times 10^8}{0.9375} = 1.4507 \times 10^8$$ $$\sqrt{1.4507 \times 10^8} = 12{,}044.5$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 12{,}044.5$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 58.63 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **Step 4: Volumetric Flow Rate (ii)** $$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{58.63}{850} = 0.0690 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ Convert to m³/hr: $$Q = 0.0690 \times 3600 = \boxed{248.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$ **Step 5: Reynolds Number (iii)** Pipe area: $$A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.2)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ Velocity in the pipe: $$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.0690}{3.1416 \times 10^{-2}} = 2.196 \text{ m/s}$$ Reynolds number: $$Re = \frac{\rho v_1 D}{\mu} = \frac{850 \times 2.196 \times 0.2}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{373.32}{0.009}$$ $$\boxed{Re \approx 41{,}480 \text{ (Turbulent flow)}}$$ --- ### ✍️ **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]** > **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **different pressure tap locations** used in orifice plate installations. Compare **flange taps**, **corner taps**, and **D-D/2 taps** in terms of installation and accuracy. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A concentric orifice plate with bore **d = 75 mm** is installed in a pipe of internal diameter **D = 150 mm**. The flow is water at **30°C (ρ = 995.6 kg/m³)**. The differential pressure across the orifice is measured as **ΔP = 30 kPa**. If the discharge coefficient **C_d = 0.61**, determine: > > (i) The mass flow rate of water through the pipe. > (ii) The approach velocity v₁. > (iii) Whether the flow is laminar or turbulent (μ for water at 30°C = 8 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s). **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ **Model Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — Pressure Tap Locations (7 Marks) Pressure taps are the small openings through which the upstream pressure (P₁) and downstream pressure (P₂) are sensed and transmitted via impulse lines to the differential pressure transmitter. The **location of these taps** significantly affects the measured ΔP and the calibration accuracy of the orifice meter. **1. Flange Taps:** - **Upstream tap** is located **25.4 mm (1 inch)** upstream of the orifice plate's upstream face. - **Downstream tap** is located **25.4 mm (1 inch)** downstream of the orifice plate's downstream face. - The taps are directly drilled into the **pipe flanges** that hold the orifice plate. - **Advantages:** Most popular in industry; easy to install in existing flanged piping; standardized by ASME. - **Disadvantages:** Not recommended for very small pipes (D < 50 mm). **2. Corner Taps:** - **Upstream tap** is located exactly at the **upstream face** of the orifice plate. - **Downstream tap** is located exactly at the **downstream face** of the orifice plate (at the corner). - **Advantages:** Highest accuracy; preferred for small diameter pipes. - **Disadvantages:** Difficult to install; requires special "corner tap" orifice plate holders. - **Standardized by ISO 5167-1.** **3. D and D/2 Taps (Radius Taps):** - **Upstream tap** is at a distance of **1 × D** (one pipe diameter) from the upstream face. - **Downstream tap** is at a distance of **0.5 × D** (half pipe diameter) from the downstream face. - **Advantages:** Standardized reference; better repeatability over a wide range of β ratios. - **Disadvantages:** Requires long straight pipe runs; not suitable for small pipe diameters. - **Standardized by ISO 5167-1.** **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Flange Taps | Corner Taps | D-D/2 Taps | |---|---|---|---| | Upstream location | 25.4 mm from face | At upstream face | 1 × D from face | | Downstream location | 25.4 mm from face | At downstream face | 0.5 × D from face | | Best for | General industry | Small diameter pipes | Calibration labs | | Standard | ASME | ISO 5167-1 | ISO 5167-1 | | Installation ease | Very easy | Difficult | Moderate | **Sketch (for notebook):** ``` PIPE WALL ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐ ↑ │ ⊕ ← UPSTREAM TAP (P1) │ │ │ (25.4 mm OR 1×D or AT FACE) │ │ │ │ │ PIPE │ ╔════════════╗ │ │ AXIS │ ║ ORIFICE ║ │ │ │ ║ PLATE d ║ │ │ │ ╚════════════╝ │ │ │ │ │ │ ⊕ ← DOWNSTREAM TAP (P2) │ │ │ (25.4 mm OR 0.5×D or AT FACE) │ │ └────────────────────────────────────────┘ ↓ ``` #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** d = 75 mm = 0.075 m, D = 150 mm = 0.15 m, ρ = 995.6 kg/m³, ΔP = 30 kPa = 30,000 Pa, C_d = 0.61, μ = 8 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s **Step 1: Beta Ratio** $$\beta = \frac{0.075}{0.15} = 0.5 \quad ; \quad \beta^4 = 0.0625 \quad ; \quad 1 - \beta^4 = 0.9375$$ **Step 2: Orifice Area** $$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3: Mass Flow Rate (i)** $$\dot{m} = 0.61 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 995.6 \times 30{,}000}{0.9375}}$$ Inner calculation: $$2 \times 995.6 \times 30{,}000 = 5.9736 \times 10^7$$ $$\frac{5.9736 \times 10^7}{0.9375} = 6.3718 \times 10^7$$ $$\sqrt{6.3718 \times 10^7} = 7{,}982.4$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.61 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 7{,}982.4$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 21.51 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **Step 4: Approach Velocity (ii)** Pipe area: $$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 1.7671 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ $$v_1 = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A_1} = \frac{21.51}{995.6 \times 1.7671 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{21.51}{17.594} = 1.222 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{v_1 \approx 1.22 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 5: Reynolds Number and Flow Regime (iii)** $$Re = \frac{\rho v_1 D}{\mu} = \frac{995.6 \times 1.222 \times 0.15}{8 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$Re = \frac{182.5}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 228{,}125$$ $$\boxed{Re \approx 2.28 \times 10^5 \quad \text{(Highly Turbulent flow)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** ΔP in kPa must be converted to Pa (×1000); diameters in mm must be converted to m (÷1000). A single missed conversion can lose **2 full marks**. > 2. **Missing the (1 - β⁴) term:** Many students omit this term in the denominator — a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > 3. **Wrong tap type for a given fluid:** Always mention whether you are using flange, corner, or D-D/2 taps in the assumption. KTU questions often test this specifically. > 4. **Not stating assumptions in derivation:** Forgetting the assumptions (steady, incompressible, ideal flow) costs 1–2 marks. > 5. **Confusing C_d with C_v or C_c:** Students often write them as independent constants instead of relating them as C_d = C_c × C_v. > 6. **Ignoring compressibility:** For gases/steam, students must include the **expansibility factor Y < 1** — failing to do so is a common error worth **1 mark** in 14-mark problems. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:** - ✅ **Orifice plate** = thin flat circular plate with sharp-edged concentric bore; **primary flow element (PFE)** for **DP-type flow measurement**. - ✅ **Working principle:** Continuity (A₁v₁ = A₂v₂) + Bernoulli (P + ½ρv² = constant) → measurable ΔP. - ✅ **Beta ratio (β = d/D)** must be in the range **0.20 to 0.75** (ISO 5167 standard). - ✅ **Master mass flow equation:** $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\rho \Delta P / (1-\beta^4)}$ - ✅ **Master volumetric equation:** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P / [\rho(1-\beta^4)]}$ - ✅ **C_d = C_c × C_v**, typical value **≈ 0.60 to 0.65** (use **0.62** if not given). - ✅ **Vena contracta:** Point of minimum cross-section and minimum pressure, located ≈ 0.5D to 0.8D downstream. - ✅ **Tap types:** Flange (1" from face), Corner (at face), D-D/2 (1D and 0.5D from face), Pipe (2.5D and 8D). - ✅ **Types of orifice plates:** Concentric, Eccentric, Segmental, Quadrant edge, Conical entrance. - ✅ **Permanent pressure loss:** ~40% to 60% of max ΔP (major disadvantage vs. Venturi). - ✅ **Concentric** = clean fluids; **Eccentric/Segmental** = dirty/slurry fluids; **Quadrant** = viscous fluids at low Re. - ✅ **Standards:** ISO 5167-1, ISO 5167-2, ASME MFC-3M, ISA, AGA Report No. 3. - ✅ **Expansibility factor Y = 1** for liquids; **Y < 1** for gases and steam (must be calculated for compressible flow). - ✅ **Material of construction:** SS-304, SS-316, Monel, Hastelloy — selected based on fluid corrosiveness. - ✅ **Square root relationship:** Q ∝ √ΔP — this is why DCS/PLC blocks apply a **square root extractor** to the DP transmitter output. - ✅ **Key applications:** Oil & gas custody transfer, steam flow in boilers, water distribution, chemical dosing, refinery process control. - ✅ **Common error to avoid:** Never confuse the **coefficient of discharge C_d** with the **orifice coefficient K** (which is C_d / √(1-β⁴)). These are different! - ✅ **Flow is laminar if Re < 2300, transitional for 2300 < Re < 4000, turbulent if Re > 4000.** For orifice meters, **Re > 10,000** is recommended for stable C_d values. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** When solving a 14-mark KTU problem, always structure your answer as: **(1) Given Data, (2) Assumptions, (3) Formula, (4) Step-by-step substitution with units, (5) Final answer with a box, (6) Physical interpretation.** This presentation style typically earns **1–2 extra marks** over a casually-written answer. ---

topics.title: Venturi Tubes and Nozzles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Venturi Tubes and Nozzles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Venturi Tube — Formal Definition

A **Venturi tube** (also called a **Venturi meter**) is a primary flow-measurement device consisting of a horizontal, carefully shaped conduit with a **converging conical inlet section**, a short **cylindrical throat**, and a **diverging conical recovery section**. It operates on the principle of **Bernoulli's equation** and the **continuity equation**, converting fluid pressure energy into kinetic energy at the throat. The differential pressure developed between the upstream tapping and the throat tapping is measured and correlated to the volumetric or mass flow rate of the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A Venturi tube is a **primary device** in differential-pressure flow measurement. It is the most accurate, least pressure-recovering-lossy, and most expensive head-flowmeter. Used for clean liquids, gases, and steam in large pipelines (water supply, power plants, petrochemical industries).

## 1.2 Nozzle — Formal Definition

A **flow nozzle** (ISA 1932 nozzle, long-radius nozzle, or Venturi nozzle) is a primary flow element with a **smoothly converging elliptical or toroidal inlet profile** terminating at a **cylindrical throat** (no diffuser). It is essentially a Venturi tube **without the recovery (diverging) section**. The flow jet continues downstream as a free expanding stream that gradually dissipates kinetic energy as turbulence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Venturi = Converger + Throat + **Diverger (Recovery cone)**. Nozzle = Converger + Throat + **No Recovery Section**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (The River-Bend Story):**
> Imagine a wide, lazy river flowing gently toward a **narrow rocky gorge**. As the water enters the gorge, the cross-section shrinks. The water *must* speed up (same volume, smaller area → higher velocity). Anyone who has squeezed the end of a garden hose has seen this: the cross-section shrinks, velocity rises, and simultaneously the *side-pressure* (lateral push) of water drops. This pressure drop is what your fingertips feel and what the Venturi **measures**.

**Mechanically:**
- A car driving into a tunnel: the air column must compress and accelerate, and the static air-pressure on the car's body drops inside the tunnel.
- A perfume atomizer: squeezing the bulb forces air through a narrow throat at high speed, creating a low-pressure region that sucks perfume up the dip-tube.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Many students think "narrower tube = lower flow." This is **false** for incompressible fluids. The volumetric flow is *imposed* by the pump. The narrow throat *increases velocity* and *decreases pressure*, but the same total volume must pass per second (continuity).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.8066 m/s²** (standard) |
| Density of water (4 °C) | $\rho_{water}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Discharge coefficient | $C_d$ | **0.95 – 0.99** (Venturi), **0.96 – 0.99** (Nozzle) |
| Throat-to-inlet area ratio | $\beta^2 = (D_2/D_1)^2$ | typically **0.1 – 0.7** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Underlying Physics

The Venturi tube and nozzle both exploit the **conversion of pressure head to velocity head** in a flowing incompressible fluid. The sequence of physical events is:

1. **Upstream (Section 1 — Inlet):** Fluid enters with low velocity $V_1$ and high static pressure $P_1$. The flow streamlines are parallel and undisturbed.
2. **Converging Section:** The cross-sectional area reduces smoothly from $A_1$ to $A_2$. Velocity rises from $V_1$ to $V_2$. Static pressure drops from $P_1$ to $P_2$. No significant energy is lost (ideal flow).
3. **Throat (Section 2):** Cross-section is minimum ($A_2$). Velocity is maximum ($V_2$). Static pressure is minimum ($P_2$).
4. **Diffuser (Venturi only):** Area gradually expands back. Velocity decreases, pressure partially **recovers** (typically 80–90% of the original pressure drop is recovered in a well-designed Venturi).
5. **Downstream:** Flow returns to (approximately) the original conditions, with minor head loss $h_L$.

## 2.2 Governing Equations

### 2.2.1 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For an **incompressible** fluid:

$$Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}$$

### 2.2.2 Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy, ideal flow, no losses)

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

For a **horizontal device** ($z_1 = z_2$) and using head $h = \Delta P / \rho g$:

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

### 2.2.3 Combined Derivation — Ideal Volumetric Flow

Using $V_1 = (A_2/A_1) V_2$, substitute into Bernoulli:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

Solving for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

The pre-factor $1/\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$ is the **velocity-of-approach factor** (often denoted $1/\sqrt{1-\beta^4}$ since $\beta = D_2/D_1$ implies $A_2/A_1 = \beta^2$).

### 2.2.4 Real Flow — Introduction of Discharge Coefficient $C_d$

Real fluids have viscous losses. We multiply by $C_d$:

$$V_2 = \frac{C_d}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

### 2.2.5 Final Volumetric Flow Rate

$$\boxed{\;Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1-P_2)}{\rho}}\;}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation (LaTeX-safe) | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A_2 V_2$ | Volumetric flow at throat | m³/s |
| 2 | $V_2 = C_d \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}$ | Throat velocity, real fluid | m/s |
| 3 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}$ | **Master volumetric equation** | m³/s |
| 4 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2\Delta P \vert \rho}}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}$ | In terms of pressure difference | m³/s |
| 5 | $m = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2\rho \Delta P}}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}$ | **Mass flow rate** | kg/s |
| 6 | $\beta = D_2 / D_1$ | Diameter ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\beta^2 = A_2 / A_1$ | Area ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{ideal}}$ | Discharge coefficient definition | 0.95–0.99 |
| 9 | $E = \frac{\Delta P_{recovered}}{\Delta P_{produced}}$ | Pressure recovery factor | 0.80–0.90 (Venturi) |
| 10 | $h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2')^2}{2g}$ | Permanent head loss | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** The pressure-difference term is written as $\Delta P \vert \rho$ (instead of $\vert \Delta P / \rho \vert$) only for table compatibility. In actual derivation and exam scripts, always use the form $\Delta P / \rho$ or $2\Delta P/\rho$ inside a square root.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Water supply & treatment | Large-pipe clean-water flow metering in municipal networks |
| Power generation | Boiler feedwater and main steam flow measurement |
| Petrochemical | Crude oil, refined product, and gas flow on risers |
| HVAC | Chilled-water and condenser-water flow |
| Chemical processing | Acid/alkali flow in pipelines (lined Venturi) |
| Aerospace | Sub-sonic wind-tunnel test sections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not use an orifice plate everywhere?** A sharp-edged orifice is cheaper but loses **50–60%** of the pressure head permanently (no recovery diffuser). A Venturi recovers **80–90%**, dramatically reducing pumping costs over the life of the installation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Volumetric Flow Rate through a Venturi

**Step 1 — State the two governing laws.**
For an incompressible, steady, frictionless fluid in a horizontal Venturi:
- **Continuity:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$
- **Bernoulli:** $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$

**Step 2 — Apply the continuity equation to eliminate $V_1$.**
Rearranging for $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$:

$$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

Squaring both sides (we will need $V_1^2$ in Bernoulli):

$$V_1^2 = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Bernoulli's equation.**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{1}{2g}\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 4 — Group the velocity terms on one side.**

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $V_2^2/(2g)$.**

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 6 — Define the differential head $h$.**
Let $h = (P_1 - P_2)/(\rho g)$. Then:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $V_2$.**

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Multiply by throat area to get ideal volumetric flow.**

$$Q_{ideal} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 9 — Introduce the discharge coefficient for real fluid.**

$$\boxed{\;Q_{real} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}\;}$$

This is the **canonical Venturi flow equation** as used in KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass Flow Rate in Terms of Pressure Difference

Starting from:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 1 — Replace $h$ with $\Delta P/(\rho g)$.**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g}}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $g$ in numerator and denominator.**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by density $\rho$ to obtain mass flow.**

$$m = \rho Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}$$

This is the **mass-flow form** of the Venturi equation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Head Loss in a Venturi Tube

The permanent head loss equals the difference between the head difference $h$ and the head recovered in the diffuser $h_r$:

$$h_L = h - h_r$$

For a well-designed Venturi with divergence angle $\theta \approx 5°$ to $7°$:

$$h_r \approx 0.8 \, h \quad \text{(80% recovery)}$$

$$h_L \approx 0.2 \, h \quad \text{(20% loss)}$$

Hence:

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2')^2}{2g}$$

where $V_2'$ is the velocity at the exit of the diffuser (close to $V_1$ for a long diffuser).

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem.** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) flows through a Venturi meter with inlet diameter $D_1 = 0.20$ m and throat diameter $D_2 = 0.10$ m. The differential pressure manometer reads $\Delta P = 30$ kPa. Given $C_d = 0.98$, calculate:
(a) Volumetric flow rate $Q$
(b) Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$
(c) Throat velocity $V_2$

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta$ and $\beta^4$.**

$$\beta = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.5$$

$$\beta^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 2 — Compute $1 - \beta^4$.**

$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Compute throat area $A_2$.**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Plug into the master equation.**

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{0.9375}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 30000}{1000}}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9682} \cdot \sqrt{60}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot 8.111 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 7.746$$

$$\boxed{\;Q \approx 0.0616 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 61.6 \text{ L/s}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow.**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.0616 = 61.6 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Throat velocity.**

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.0616}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 7.84 \text{ m/s}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture — Venturi Tube / Nozzle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upstream Pipe Section 1] --> B[D1 Inlet Diameter]
    B --> C[Converging Cone Angle 21 deg]
    C --> D[Throat Section 2]
    D --> E[D2 Throat Diameter minimum]
    E --> F{Venturi or Nozzle}
    F -->|Venturi Path| G[Diverging Recovery Cone 5 to 7 deg]
    F -->|Nozzle Path| H[Free Expanding Jet no recovery]
    G --> I[Pressure Tap P2 at Throat]
    G --> J[Pressure Tap P1 at Upstream]
    H --> I
    H --> J
    I --> K[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Flow Computer Q and m]
    L --> M[Indicator / Recorder / Controller]
    A --> N[Static Pressure P1 high]
    E --> O[Static Pressure P2 low]
    N --> P[Bernoulli Energy Conversion]
    O --> P
    P --> L
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph process [Process Pipe]
        fluid1[Incompressible Fluid] --> venturi[Venturi or Nozzle Primary Element]
        venturi --> fluid2[Downstream Fluid Stream]
    end
    venturi -->|Taps P1 and P2| dpt[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    dpt -->|4 to 20 mA or HART Signal| fc[Flow Computer SQRT Extraction]
    fc -->|Linear Flow Signal| indicator[Digital Indicator]
    fc --> recorder[Chart Recorder]
    fc --> dcs[DCS PLC SCADA Controller]
    dcs --> control[Control Valve Actuator]
    control --> venturi
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Venturi vs Nozzle vs Orifice

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph venturiBlock [Venturi Tube]
        direction LR
        v1[Inlet] --> v2[Converger] --> v3[Throat] --> v4[Diffuser] --> v5[Outlet]
    end
    subgraph nozzleBlock [Flow Nozzle]
        direction LR
        n1[Inlet] --> n2[Converger] --> n3[Throat] --> n4[Free Jet]
    end
    subgraph orificeBlock [Orifice Plate]
        direction LR
        o1[Inlet] --> o2[Sharp Edge] --> o3[Vena Contracta] --> o4[Outlet]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (`venturiBlock`, `nodeA`, etc.). No reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as standalone node IDs. All labels are plain uppercase text without markdown bold/italic tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model)]**
Define a Venturi tube. State Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation as applied to it.

**Model Answer:**
A Venturi tube is a converging-diverging pipe section used as a primary flow-measurement device, consisting of an inlet, a throat, and a recovery (diffuser) section. The governing equations are:

- **Continuity equation** (incompressible flow): $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$
- **Bernoulli's equation** (horizontal, frictionless): $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$

The differential pressure $(P_1 - P_2)$ developed between the inlet and throat is measured and used to compute the flow rate.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]**
Differentiate between a Venturi tube and a flow nozzle in terms of construction, discharge coefficient, and pressure recovery.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Venturi Tube | Flow Nozzle |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Converger + Throat + **Diffuser (recovery cone)** | Converger + Throat only (no diffuser) |
| Discharge coefficient $C_d$ | 0.95 – 0.99 | 0.96 – 0.99 |
| Pressure recovery | 80 – 90 % | Negligible (≈ 0 %) |
| Permanent head loss | 10 – 20 % of differential head | 50 – 60 % of differential head |
| Cost | High (machined cone) | Moderate (cast or forged) |
| Best application | Large clean-water mains, power-plant feedwater | High-velocity steam, gas pipelines |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each module provides a choice between two 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **one** full question.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the volumetric flow rate through a Venturi meter, starting from Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Frictionless along the streamline (no viscous loss) — corrected later by $C_d$.
3. Horizontal installation ($z_1 = z_2$).
4. Uniform velocity profile at sections 1 and 2.
5. No heat transfer.

**Step 1 — Continuity equation:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \Rightarrow V_1 = (A_2/A_1) V_2$.

**Step 2 — Bernoulli's equation (horizontal):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_1$:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{1}{2g}\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 5 — Define $h = (P_1-P_2)/\rho g$ and solve for $V_2$:**

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}\sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by $A_2$ and apply $C_d$:**

$$\boxed{\;Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}\sqrt{2gh}\;}$$

---

**(b) A Venturi meter has an inlet diameter of 200 mm and a throat diameter of 100 mm. Water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) flows through it and the differential pressure measured is 50 kPa. If $C_d = 0.98$, find the volumetric and mass flow rates. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diameters and ratio.**
$D_1 = 0.20$ m, $D_2 = 0.10$ m, $\beta = 0.5$, $\beta^4 = 0.0625$.

**Step 2 — Throat area.**
$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

**Step 3 — Apply master equation.**

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50000}{1000}}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9682} \cdot \sqrt{100}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot 8.111 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10$$

$$\boxed{\;Q \approx 0.0795 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow.**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.0795 = 79.5 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Venturi tube. Mention the role of the diffuser cone. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
- **Inlet section (1):** Cylindrical pipe of diameter $D_1$ carrying the undisturbed flow.
- **Converging cone:** Cone with half-angle ≈ 20° that gradually reduces the diameter from $D_1$ to $D_2$ (throat diameter).
- **Throat (2):** Short parallel cylinder of diameter $D_2$ where the velocity is maximum and the static pressure is minimum.
- **Diverging cone (diffuser):** A long, gently expanding cone (half-angle 5° to 7°) that gradually returns the area to $D_1$.
- **Pressure tappings:** Located at the upstream section and the throat, connected to a differential manometer or transmitter.

**Working:**
As fluid enters the converging cone, the area reduces, velocity rises, and static pressure falls. At the throat, the velocity is maximum and pressure minimum. In the diffuser, the area expands, velocity drops, and a major portion of the kinetic energy is **recovered as pressure** (typically 80–90 %).

**Role of the diffuser cone:**
The diffuser **recovers pressure** that would otherwise be permanently lost, dramatically reducing the pumping power required to push the fluid through the meter. It is the key engineering reason why Venturi tubes are preferred for **large pipelines** where long-term energy cost dominates the initial capital cost.

---

**(b) A flow nozzle of throat diameter 50 mm is fitted in a 150 mm pipe. Water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) is flowing and the pressure difference between the inlet and the throat is 80 kPa. Given $C_d = 0.97$, compute the flow rate. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ratio.**
$D_2 = 0.050$ m, $D_1 = 0.150$ m, $\beta = 0.333$, $\beta^4 = 0.01235$.

**Step 2 — Throat area.**
$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

**Step 3 — Velocity-of-approach factor.**
$\sqrt{1 - \beta^4} = \sqrt{0.98765} = 0.9938$.

**Step 4 — Master equation.**

$$Q = 0.97 \cdot \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9938} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 80000}{1000}}$$

$$Q = 0.97 \cdot 1.9756 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{160}$$

$$Q = 0.97 \cdot 1.9756 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 12.649$$

$$\boxed{\;Q \approx 0.02423 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 24.23 \text{ L/s}\;}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity-of-approach factor** $1/\sqrt{1-\beta^4}$: This alone costs 1–2 marks per problem. Many students use the simplified orifice-plate form and write $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$, which is **wrong** for Venturi and nozzle (only valid when $A_2 \ll A_1$).
> 2. **Confusing $h$ with $\Delta P/(\rho g)$ vs $\Delta P/\rho$**: Pressure *head* is $\Delta P/(\rho g)$ in metres of fluid; pressure *divided by density* is $\Delta P/\rho$ in m²/s². The square-root argument must be **dimensionally m²/s²** (energy per unit mass). Pick the form carefully.
> 3. **Dropping the discharge coefficient $C_d$**: Ideal Bernoulli gives $C_d = 1$, but real Venturis have $C_d = 0.95$–$0.99$. Forgetting $C_d$ loses one full mark.
> 4. **Using wrong diameter ratio** $\beta = D_2/D_1$ vs $(D_2/D_1)^2$: The velocity-of-approach factor uses $\beta^4$ because $(A_2/A_1)^2 = (D_2/D_1)^4$. Mixing these is a frequent error.
> 5. **Inlet vs throat area for the continuity equation**: Use $A_2$ for $V_2$ in the final answer. Some students compute $Q = A_1 V_2$ instead of $A_2 V_2$, giving a wrong answer by a factor of $\beta^2$.
> 6. **No units in final answer**: KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing or wrong units (e.g., m³/s vs L/s vs cm³/s).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Venturi tube = **converger + throat + diverging recovery cone**; flow nozzle = **converger + throat only**.
- ✅ Operating principle: **Bernoulli's equation (energy conservation) + continuity (mass conservation)**.
- ✅ Master equation: $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 / \sqrt{1 - \beta^4} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$.
- ✅ Mass-flow form: $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 / \sqrt{1 - \beta^4} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$.
- ✅ Velocity-of-approach factor $1/\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}$ corrects for finite upstream velocity.
- ✅ **Never omit $C_d$** for a real fluid.
- ✅ Typical $C_d$: Venturi 0.95–0.99; Nozzle 0.96–0.99; Orifice 0.60–0.65.
- ✅ Pressure recovery: Venturi **80–90 %** > Nozzle **0 %** > Orifice negligible.
- ✅ Tappings: corner, D and D/2, or flange tappings — for Venturi, pressure tappings are at the inlet section and at the throat.
- ✅ Head loss in Venturi ≈ 10–20 % of differential head $h$.
- ✅ Applications: water supply mains, power-plant feedwater, large gas lines, chemical process piping.
- ✅ Advantages of Venturi: high accuracy (±1 %), high pressure recovery, low permanent loss.
- ✅ Disadvantages of Venturi: large physical size, very expensive to machine, not suitable for dirty/sticky fluids (use nozzle or orifice).
- ✅ Flow nozzle: shorter than Venturi, lower cost, no pressure recovery, used for **high-velocity steam** and **gas service**.
- ✅ $\beta = D_2/D_1$; $\beta^2 = A_2/A_1$; $\beta^4$ appears in the velocity-of-approach factor.
- ✅ $\Delta P$ for liquid is read by inverted U-tube manometer using a heavier mercury (or similar) fluid.
- ✅ **Sizing rule of thumb:** keep $\beta$ between **0.3 and 0.7** for good accuracy and reasonable pressure drop.
- ✅ Reynolds-number correction may be needed for very low Re (laminar or transitional flow).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pitot Tubes. Positive Displacement Flowmeters - Reciprocating Piston Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pitot Tubes. Positive Displacement Flowmeters - Reciprocating Piston Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pitot Tube

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Pitot Tube** is a differential pressure flow measurement device consisting of one or more concentric tubes with openings oriented to sense both the **stagnation (impact) pressure** $P_0$ and the **static pressure** $P$ of a flowing fluid. The difference between these two pressures is used to determine the local **flow velocity** at the point of measurement by applying **Bernoulli's equation** for an ideal, incompressible, steady, frictionless flow.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**

Imagine you are standing by the side of a river holding a small open straw. If you point the straw directly into the current (facing upstream), water will rush *up* into the straw and the water level inside will be *higher* than the river surface. That extra height represents the **kinetic energy** of the moving water being converted into pressure (potential) energy. Now, if you punch a small hole on the *side* of the straw, water will simply enter up to the river's surface level — that represents the **static pressure**.

A Pitot tube does exactly the same thing — it "captures" the moving fluid at one opening (impact) and reads the natural pressure at the other (static). The difference is proportional to the square of the fluid velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Constants Used:**
> - Density of water at **4 °C**: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of air at **STP (15 °C, 101.325 kPa)**: $\rho_a \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Specific weight of water: $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$

---

### 1.2 Positive Displacement Flowmeters — Reciprocating Piston Meter

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Reciprocating Piston Flowmeter** is a positive-displacement (PD) flow-measurement device in which a precisely-machined piston oscillates within a cylindrical measuring chamber, dividing the chamber into known volumes. Each complete oscillation cycle traps and discharges a fixed, metered volume of fluid, allowing the **total volumetric flow rate** to be obtained by counting the number of cycles per unit time.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**

Think of an old-fashioned hand-pump or a bicycle pump with a piston. Every time the piston goes forward, it sweeps out a fixed volume of air/water and pushes it out. If you count how many "sweeps" occur in one minute and multiply by the volume of each sweep, you get the total flow. A reciprocating piston flowmeter does this automatically, continuously, and with much higher precision — only the piston is driven *by* the flowing fluid itself, not by hand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PD Meter Family Context:**
> Positive-displacement flowmeters form a major class that includes:
> 1. Reciprocating (Oscillating) Piston meters
> 2. Nutating-disc meters (used in domestic water meters)
> 3. Oval-gear meters
> 4. Lobed-impeller (Roots) meters
> 5. Sliding-vane meters
>
> All of them share one principle: **flow rate ∝ (cycles per unit time) × (known volume per cycle)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pitot Tube — Operational Logic

The working of a Pitot tube is governed by the **steady-flow energy equation (Bernoulli's equation)** between two points along a streamline in an ideal incompressible flow:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step-by-step logic for a Pitot-static tube:**

1. **Point 1 — Free Stream (Static Tap):** Far ahead of the tube, the fluid moves with the undisturbed line velocity $v$, and the opening measures the **static pressure** $P$ (no velocity is brought to rest).
2. **Point 2 — Stagnation Point (Impact Tap):** The fluid is brought to rest at the open end facing the flow. Velocity $v_2 = 0$, and the pressure rises to the **stagnation (impact) pressure** $P_0$.
3. **Applying Bernoulli** between these two points (same elevation, $z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{P_0}{\rho} + 0$$

4. **Solving for velocity:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$$

5. **Real fluids deviate from ideal flow** — viscosity and turbulence cause some loss. This is captured by the **Coefficient of Pitot Tube, $C_v$** (also called the velocity coefficient or calibration factor):

$$\boxed{\;v = C_v \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}\;}$$

6. **Expressed as a differential head** $h$ (in metres of the flowing fluid):

$$h = \frac{P_0 - P}{\rho g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation of $C_v$:**
> - $C_v = 1.0$ → ideal, frictionless, isentropic flow.
> - $C_v = 0.98 - 0.99$ → typical value for a well-designed Pitot-static tube.
> - $C_v < 0.95$ → indicates a poorly designed or fouled tube.

---

### 2.2 Why Use a Pitot Tube? — Engineering Utility

- Measures **local point velocity** (useful for velocity profiling in ducts & wind tunnels).
- **No moving parts** → extremely low maintenance, minimal pressure loss.
- Can be inserted into a pipe under **pressure (hot-tap installation)**.
- The classical device for **aircraft airspeed indicators** (airspeed is a direct function of $P_0 - P$).
- Foundational building block for **annubar, averaging Pitot, and multi-port flow sensors**.

---

### 2.3 Reciprocating Piston Meter — Operational Logic

**Mechanical construction:**
- A precisely-machined cylindrical measuring chamber.
- A **hollow piston** (or solid piston with central slot) oscillates inside.
- A **central partition (or disc)** divides the chamber, with a **control slot** allowing the piston to slide past it.
- Two non-return valves (inlet & outlet) direct flow.

**Working steps:**

1. Fluid enters via the **inlet port** and pushes the piston.
2. The piston moves linearly, **sweeping a fixed volume** of fluid from the inlet side to the outlet side.
3. On reaching the end of the stroke, the geometry of the chamber causes the piston to **oscillate (rock/pivot)** slightly so it reverses direction.
4. Each **complete cycle = one full forward stroke + one full return stroke = 2 swept volumes**.
5. A mechanical counter (or magnetic/sensor pickup) registers each cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volumetric flow equation for any PD meter (universal form):**
> $$Q = N \times V_c$$
> where:
> - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
> - $N$ = number of cycles per second (Hz)
> - $V_c$ = metered (displaced) volume per cycle (m³)

For a reciprocating piston of diameter $D$ and stroke length $L$:

$$V_c = 2 \times A \times L = 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{2}$$

(The factor of **2** accounts for one complete cycle = forward + return stroke.)

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Ideal Pitot velocity | $v = \sqrt{2(P_0 - P)/\rho}$ | $v$ = m/s, $P$ = Pa, $\rho$ = kg/m³ | Incompressible, inviscid |
| 2 | Real Pitot velocity | $v = C_v\sqrt{2(P_0 - P)/\rho}$ | $C_v \approx 0.98$ | Multiply ideal by coefficient |
| 3 | Pitot head form | $v = C_v\sqrt{2gh}$ | $h$ = m, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | $h$ = differential head of flowing fluid |
| 4 | Differential pressure | $P_0 - P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Pa | Dynamic pressure component |
| 5 | Volume flow in pipe | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $A$ = m², $Q$ = m³/s | $A$ = pipe cross-section |
| 6 | PD meter flow | $Q = N \cdot V_c$ | $N$ = cycles/s, $V_c$ = m³/cycle | Universal PD equation |
| 7 | Reciprocating piston $V_c$ | $V_c = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{2}$ | $D$ = piston dia (m), $L$ = stroke (m) | One full oscillation = 2 strokes |
| 8 | Total volume passed | $V_{total} = N_{total} \cdot V_c$ | — | Used for billing/metering |
| 9 | Reynolds for PD | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | — | Used to size chamber to avoid leakage |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Vertical Bars in Formulas:** All absolute-value or "divided-by" expressions use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to keep markdown table rows valid. Do **not** use the bare pipe `|`.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pitot tubes:** aircraft altimeters/airspeed, HVAC duct balancing, stack-gas monitoring, water/wind-tunnel flow research.
- **Reciprocating piston meters:** domestic & industrial **water metering**, **fuel-oil & lube-oil** measurement, **chemical dosing**, **custody-transfer** in petroleum, **beverage bottling lines**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pitot Tube Velocity Equation

**Starting point — Bernoulli's equation** (incompressible, steady, inviscid flow, same streamline, horizontal):

$$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1:** Identify the two points:
- Point A = free stream, far upstream, where static pressure tap reads $P_A$ and velocity is $v_A = v$.
- Point B = stagnation point at the impact tap, where the fluid is brought to rest, $v_B = 0$ and pressure is $P_B = P_0$.

**Step 2:** Eliminate gravity by choosing the two openings at the same elevation, $z_A = z_B$.

**Step 3:** Write Bernoulli between A and B:

$$P_A + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_A^2 + \rho g z_A = P_B + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_B^2 + \rho g z_B$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $P_A = P$, $v_A = v$, $P_B = P_0$, $v_B = 0$, $z_A = z_B$:

$$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = P_0 + 0$$

**Step 5:** Subtract $P$ from both sides:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = P_0 - P$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $v^2$:

$$v^2 = \frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}$$

**Step 7:** Take the positive square root (velocity is positive by convention):

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 8:** Introduce the **velocity coefficient $C_v$** to account for non-ideal effects:

$$v = C_v \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 9:** Convert the pressure difference into a head $h$ of the flowing fluid itself:

$$h = \frac{P_0 - P}{\rho g}$$

**Step 10:** Substitute into the velocity equation:

$$v = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If the U-tube manometer shows a manometric head $h_m$ of mercury (or any other manometer fluid), the **differential head of the flowing fluid** is:
> $$h = h_m \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$$
> This is a very common KTU exam twist — do not confuse manometer fluid with flowing fluid.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Pitot Tube with Manometer (KTU Standard)

**Problem:**
Water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) flows in a pipe. A Pitot-static tube connected to a differential U-tube mercury manometer ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$) shows a manometric deflection of $h_m = 100 \text{ mm}$. The Pitot coefficient is $C_v = 0.98$. Calculate the velocity at the Pitot point and the volumetric flow rate if the pipe diameter is $D = 200 \text{ mm}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert units.
- $h_m = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$
- $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.200 \text{ m}$

**Step 2:** Convert manometric head to head of flowing fluid (water):

$$h = h_m \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right) = 0.100 \times \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right)$$

$$h = 0.100 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.100 \times 12.6 = 1.26 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the velocity equation:

$$v = C_v \sqrt{2gh} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.26}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the radicand:

$$2 \times 9.81 \times 1.26 = 24.7212$$

**Step 5:** Take square root:

$$\sqrt{24.7212} = 4.9721 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $C_v$:

$$v = 0.98 \times 4.9721 = 4.8726 \text{ m/s} \approx 4.87 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Pipe cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.200)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.04}{4} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 8:** Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = A \cdot v = 0.03142 \times 4.8726 = 0.1531 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 9:** Convert to litres per second for intuitive comparison:

$$Q = 0.1531 \times 1000 = 153.1 \text{ L/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** $v \approx 4.87 \text{ m/s}$ and $Q \approx 0.153 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Reciprocating Piston Flowmeter

**Problem:**
A reciprocating piston flowmeter has a piston diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ and stroke length $L = 40 \text{ mm}$. The counter records **240 complete cycles per minute**. Determine (a) the displaced volume per cycle, (b) the volumetric flow rate in m³/s and L/s, and (c) the total volume passed in 1 hour.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert dimensions to SI units.
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- $L = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$

**Step 2:** Piston cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 0.0019635 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Displaced volume per complete cycle (forward + return = 2 strokes):

$$V_c = 2 \times A \times L = 2 \times 0.0019635 \times 0.040$$

$$V_c = 1.5708 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \approx 0.1571 \text{ L per cycle}$$

**Step 4:** Convert cycles/min to cycles/s (Hz):

$$N = \frac{240}{60} = 4 \text{ cycles/s}$$

**Step 5:** Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = N \times V_c = 4 \times 1.5708 \times 10^{-4} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to litres per second:

$$Q = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.6283 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 7:** Total volume in 1 hour:
- 1 hour = 3600 s
- Total cycles in 1 hour = $240 \times 60 = 14400$ cycles
- $V_{total} = 14400 \times 1.5708 \times 10^{-4} = 2.262 \text{ m}^3$

> **Final Answer:** $V_c \approx 1.571 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$, $Q \approx 6.28 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (≈ 0.628 L/s), and $V_{total} \approx 2.262 \text{ m}^3$ per hour.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pitot-Static Tube — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS["Free Stream Fluid"] -->|carries velocity v| IP1["Impact Tap Opposing Flow"]
    FS -->|static pressure| SP1["Static Tap Perpendicular to Flow"]
    IP1 --> HP1["High Pressure Line P0"]
    SP1 --> LP1["Low Pressure Line P"]
    HP1 --> MAN["Differential Manometer"]
    LP1 --> MAN
    MAN -->|reads h_m| CALC["Calculator v = Cv sqrt 2gh"]
    CALC --> OUT["Flow Velocity Output"]

    classDef tap fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef man fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class IP1,SP1 tap
    class HP1,LP1 line
    class MAN,CALC,OUT man
```

**Functional flow description:**
1. The free-stream fluid carries a velocity $v$ past the tube.
2. The **impact tap** (orifice facing the flow) decelerates the fluid to zero, registering the **stagnation pressure $P_0$**.
3. The **static tap** (orifice perpendicular to the flow) registers only the **static pressure $P$**.
4. Both taps are connected by tubing to a **differential manometer**, which displays $h_m$.
5. The manometer reading is converted into a velocity using $v = C_v\sqrt{2gh}$.

---

### 4.2 Pitot Tube — Anatomy (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TIP["Stagnation Tip Region"]
        A1["A1: Impact Opening - Faces Flow - Captures P0"]
        A2["A2: Static Openings - On Side Walls - Captures P"]
    end
    subgraph SHAFT["Outer Shaft"]
        B1["B1: Central Tube Carries P0 to High Port"]
        B2["B2: Annular Space Carries P to Low Port"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output to Manometer"]
        C1["C1: High Pressure Connector"]
        C2["C2: Low Pressure Connector"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2

    classDef tip fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef sh fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class A1,A2 tip
    class B1,B2 sh
    class C1,C2 out
```

---

### 4.3 Reciprocating Piston Meter — Operating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Start: Inlet Valve Open - Outlet Valve Closed - Piston at Left End"] --> B["Flow Pushes Piston Right - Sweeps Volume A1 x L"]
    B --> C["Piston Reaches Right End - Geometry Rocks Piston - Inlet Closes - Outlet Opens"]
    C --> D["Flow Pushes Piston Left - Sweeps Volume A1 x L"]
    D --> E["Piston Reaches Left End - Geometry Rocks Back - Counter Increments by 1 Cycle"]
    E --> A
```

> **Note:** The "rocking" of the piston is purely a geometric trick — a slotted control partition in the chamber forces the piston to oscillate around a central disc, producing the same forward-then-return motion automatically with each breath of flow.

---
