### 4.4 Reciprocating Piston — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN["Inlet Pipe - Fluid Enters Chamber"] --> INV["Inlet Non-Return Valve"]
    INV --> CHAMBER["Cylindrical Measuring Chamber"]
    CHAMBER -->|piston sweeps| VOL["Fixed Volume Vc Swept per Stroke"]
    VOL --> ONV["Outlet Non-Return Valve"]
    ONV --> OUT["Outlet Pipe - Fluid Exits"]
    CHAMBER --> COUNTER["Mechanical or Magnetic Cycle Counter"]
    COUNTER --> DISP["Flow Rate and Totalizer Display"]
    DISP --> Q["Q = N x Vc"]

    classDef pipe fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef valve fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef inst fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class IN,OUT,CHAMBER,VOL pipe
    class INV,ONV valve
    class COUNTER,DISP,Q inst
```

---

### 4.5 Comparative Feature Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT["Pitot Tube"] --> P1["Differential Pressure Device"]
    PT --> P2["No Moving Parts"]
    PT --> P3["Measures Local Point Velocity"]
    PT --> P4["Low Pressure Loss"]
    PT --> P5["Used in Air Ducts, Stacks, Aircraft"]

    RP["Reciprocating Piston Meter"] --> R1["Positive Displacement Device"]
    RP --> R2["Has Moving Piston"]
    RP --> R3["Measures Total Volume"]
    RP --> R4["High Accuracy at Low Flow"]
    RP --> R5["Used in Water and Oil Metering"]

    classDef pitot fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef pd fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 pitot
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 pd
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Define a Pitot tube. State the principle on which it works.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Pitot tube is a flow-measurement device used to determine the local velocity of a fluid at a point in a flow stream by measuring the difference between the **stagnation (impact) pressure** and the **static pressure** of the fluid. It works on the principle of **Bernoulli's equation** for an ideal, incompressible, steady, frictionless flow. By applying Bernoulli's equation between a free-stream point and the stagnation point at the tip of the tube, the velocity is given by $v = C_v\sqrt{2(P_0 - P)/\rho}$, where $P_0$ is the stagnation pressure, $P$ is the static pressure, $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $C_v$ is the velocity coefficient.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is a positive displacement flowmeter? Briefly explain the working of a reciprocating piston type flowmeter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A positive displacement (PD) flowmeter is a flow-measuring device that operates by trapping and discharging **fixed, known volumes** of fluid sequentially, with the flow rate being proportional to the number of times this fixed volume is filled per unit time. In a **reciprocating (oscillating) piston** type, a hollow piston inside a precisely-machined cylindrical chamber oscillates back and forth as the flowing fluid pushes it. Each complete oscillation cycle (one forward stroke + one return stroke) sweeps and discharges a fixed metered volume $V_c = (\pi D^2 L)/2$. A counter records the cycles, and the volumetric flow rate is given by $Q = N \cdot V_c$, where $N$ is the number of cycles per second.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a Pitot-static tube used for the measurement of fluid velocity. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing through a pipe. The differential mercury manometer shows a deflection of **60 mm**. Take $C_v = 0.98$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate the velocity of water at the Pitot point. Also, find the discharge through the pipe if its diameter is **150 mm**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Explanation + Derivation (7 marks):**

A Pitot-static tube consists of two concentric tubes. The **inner tube** has an opening (impact tap) at its tip facing directly into the flow. The **outer tube** has one or more side openings (static taps) located a short distance behind the impact tip. The two openings are connected through separate passages to a differential manometer.

**Working:**
- The impact tap captures the **stagnation pressure $P_0$** because the fluid is brought to rest at that point.
- The static taps sense only the **static pressure $P$** of the undisturbed flow.
- The differential manometer displays the pressure difference $P_0 - P$.

**Derivation:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between point 1 (free stream, static tap) and point 2 (stagnation, impact tap), at the same elevation:

$$\frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{P_0}{\rho} + 0$$

Solving for velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$$

Introducing the velocity coefficient $C_v$:

$$v = C_v \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}} = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$$

(7 marks distributed: 3 for sketch + working, 4 for derivation steps)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $h_m = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- $C_v = 0.98$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Equivalent head of water:**

$$h = h_m \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right) = 0.060 \times (13.6 - 1)$$

$$h = 0.060 \times 12.6 = 0.756 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity:**

$$v = C_v \sqrt{2gh} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.756}$$

$$2 \times 9.81 \times 0.756 = 14.8327$$

$$\sqrt{14.8327} = 3.8514 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v = 0.98 \times 3.8514 = 3.7744 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Pipe cross-section:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.150)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Discharge:**

$$Q = A \cdot v = 0.01767 \times 3.7744 = 0.0667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 3.77 \text{ m/s}, \quad Q \approx 0.0667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 66.7 \text{ L/s}}$$

(Marks: 2 for head conversion, 2 for velocity, 2 for area, 1 for final Q)

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **reciprocating piston type positive displacement flowmeter** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the volume of fluid metered per cycle. (7 marks)

**(b)** A reciprocating piston flowmeter has a piston diameter of **80 mm** and a stroke length of **60 mm**. The flow meter completes **180 cycles per minute**. Calculate:
   (i) The volume of fluid metered per cycle.
   (ii) The volumetric flow rate in **L/s**.
   (iii) The total volume of fluid metered in **24 hours**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A reciprocating piston flowmeter consists of a cylindrical measuring chamber with a precisely-machined oscillating (reciprocating) piston. The chamber has a **central control partition** with a curved slot. The piston is connected to a **central disc or post** that slides within this slot. Two **non-return valves** (inlet and outlet) direct the fluid flow.

**Working:**
1. Fluid enters the chamber through the inlet port and pushes the piston forward.
2. As the piston moves, it sweeps a fixed volume of fluid from the inlet side to the outlet side.
3. When the piston reaches the end of its stroke, the geometry of the control slot forces the piston to **oscillate (rock)** so that it can reverse direction.
4. The piston is then pushed back by incoming fluid, sweeping another fixed volume.
5. Each complete oscillation cycle (forward + return) discharges a fixed metered volume.
6. A counter records each cycle.

**Derivation:** For a piston of diameter $D$ and stroke length $L$:
- Cross-sectional area of piston: $A = \pi D^2/4$
- Volume swept per single stroke: $V_s = A \cdot L$
- One complete cycle = 2 strokes → Volume per cycle:

$$\boxed{V_c = 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{2}}$$

(Marks: 3 for diagram + working, 4 for derivation)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$
- $L = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- $N = 180 \text{ cycles/min} = 3 \text{ cycles/s}$

**(i) Volume per cycle:**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{2} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2 \times 0.060}{2}$$

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064 \times 0.060}{2} = \frac{0.0012064}{2} = 6.032 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}$$

$$V_c \approx 6.032 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \approx 0.6032 \text{ L/cycle}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = N \cdot V_c = 3 \times 6.032 \times 10^{-4} = 1.8096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 1.8096 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1.8096 \text{ L/s} \approx 1.81 \text{ L/s}$$

**(iii) Total volume in 24 hours:**
- 24 hours = 24 × 3600 = 86400 s
- Total cycles = $3 \times 86400 = 259200$ cycles

$$V_{total} = N \cdot V_c \cdot 86400 = 1.8096 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400$$

$$V_{total} = 156.35 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_c \approx 6.03 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3, \quad Q \approx 1.81 \text{ L/s}, \quad V_{total} \approx 156.35 \text{ m}^3}$$

(Marks: 2 for Vc, 2 for Q, 3 for total volume with unit conversion)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **For Pitot tube problems:** Students most often **forget the coefficient $C_v$** (losing 1 mark), or **confuse $h_m$ (manometer head) with $h$ (flowing fluid head)** by failing to apply the manometric correction $(\rho_m/\rho - 1)$ (loses 2–3 marks).
> 2. **For PD meter problems:** Students frequently compute **only one stroke's volume** instead of a **complete cycle** (which requires the factor of 2) — losing 1–2 marks. Also, watch out for **units**: a "cycle per minute" must be converted to "cycles per second" before computing $Q$ in m³/s.
> 3. **Always write the correct number of significant figures** and **state the units explicitly** in the final answer box.
> 4. **For Part A questions** in KTU, you must produce a *complete* answer with a definition AND working principle in 3 marks — definitions alone fetch only 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Pitot tube principle** = Bernoulli's equation between a **stagnation point** and a **static point** in an incompressible flow.
- **Velocity formula (real):** $v = C_v\sqrt{2(P_0 - P)/\rho} = C_v\sqrt{2gh}$.
- **Typical $C_v$ value:** $0.98$ for a well-designed Pitot-static tube.
- **Manometer head conversion:** $h = h_m \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$ — never use $h_m$ directly when the manometer fluid is denser than the flowing fluid.
- **Pitot tube measures point velocity** — multiply by pipe area to get volumetric flow.
- **Pitot-static tube** uses one stagnation opening + one or more side static openings.
- **PD meter principle:** $Q = N \times V_c$ — flow = cycles/s × volume per cycle.
- **Reciprocating piston $V_c$:** $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2 L}{2}$ — one full cycle = 2 strokes.
- **Reciprocating piston meter** uses a cylindrical chamber, oscillating piston, central control partition, and two non-return valves.
- **Pitot tubes have NO moving parts** → high reliability, low pressure loss; used in aircraft, HVAC, stack-gas, wind tunnels.
- **Reciprocating piston meters have moving parts** → excellent accuracy for low-viscosity liquids (water, oil) in custody-transfer applications.
- **Convert all dimensions to SI units** (m, s, kg) before plugging into formulas.
- **Always show the units** in your final answer to earn the unit-mark in KTU valuation.
- **Compare $C_v$ with 1** in your answer to justify whether the design is ideal or non-ideal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oval Gear Meters
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oval Gear Meters

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Oval Gear Meter** is a type of **positive displacement (PD) flow meter** that uses two precisely-machined, oval-shaped rotating gears (rotors) enclosed within a measuring chamber of matching geometry. As the process fluid enters the chamber, it imparts a torque on the gears, causing them to rotate in a fixed volumetric relationship. Each complete revolution of the gear pair displaces a **fixed, finite, and repeatable volume** of fluid, independent of flow velocity or viscosity variations. The cumulative rotation count directly corresponds to the total volumetric flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAET602 – Module 3):**
> Oval Gear Meters fall under the broader category of **Positive Displacement Flow Meters** in Industrial Instrumentation. For KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the focus is on constructional features, working principle, displacement volume derivations, characteristic curves, and industrial applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two-Door Revolving Gate Analogy:**
> Imagine two oval-shaped revolving doors (like at a hotel entrance) that are meshed together so when one rotates, the other rotates in the opposite direction. Every time the doors make a complete turn, they let through exactly **one compartment-full** of people. If you count how many complete turns the doors make in an hour, you can determine exactly how many "compartment-fulls" of people entered the building. The oval gear meter does **exactly the same thing** with a liquid — it counts the "compartment-fulls" (discrete volumes) of fluid that pass through it.

In simpler terms:
- The **oval gears** are the revolving compartments.
- The **measuring chamber walls** define the boundary of each compartment.
- The **rotation counter** (magnetic pickup) tells you how many compartments passed by.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Accuracy Range:** **±0.5% to ±1% of actual flow** (high accuracy class).
- **Turndown Ratio:** Typically **10:1 to 20:1** (range of measurable flow rates).
- **Viscosity Suitability:** Operates effectively from **1 cP (water-like)** to over **1,000,000 cP (thick oils/honey)**.
- **Standard Output Units:** Pulses per unit volume (e.g., **pulses/litre**), **cubic metres (m³)**, or **gallons**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** Oval gear meters are the **go-to choice** when the process fluid is **viscous, clean, and non-lubricating**, where other flow meters (like orifice plates or turbine meters) fail to deliver accurate readings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of an oval gear meter can be broken down into **four distinct phases** of the rotation cycle:

**Phase 1 — Fluid Entry (Top of Chamber):**
- Pressurized fluid enters the inlet port of the measuring chamber.
- The fluid contacts the curved surfaces of the two oval gears.

**Phase 2 — Gear Rotation Initiation:**
- The pressure differential between the inlet and outlet creates a net force (torque) on the gears.
- This torque causes the gears to rotate in opposite directions.
- Due to the **meshing teeth design**, the rotation is **synchronized** and **lossless**.

**Phase 3 — Fluid Entrapment & Displacement:**
- A precisely known volume of fluid is **trapped** between the gear tooth, the chamber wall, and the adjacent gear.
- This trapped volume is **carried around** the chamber periphery by the rotating gear.

**Phase 4 — Fluid Discharge (Bottom of Chamber):**
- As the gear completes its revolution, the trapped fluid is **squeezed out** through the outlet port.
- The cycle repeats continuously.

### Why It Works — The Core "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| Pressure differential | Fluid pushes the gear | Provides energy for rotation without external power |
| Meshing teeth | Gears rotate in sync | Prevents backflow and ensures volumetric accuracy |
| Fixed chamber geometry | Each turn = fixed volume | Enables calibration-independent measurement |
| Magnetic pickup | Counts rotations | Converts mechanical motion to electrical pulses |

## 2.2 Constructional Features

> [!NOTE]
> A **labeled component diagram** is provided in Section 4. The key constructional elements are:

1. **Oval Rotors (Gears):** Precision-machined from hardened steel, stainless steel, or engineering plastics (e.g., **PEEK**, **PTFE**) for corrosive fluids.
2. **Measuring Chamber:** Cylindrical bore with inlet and outlet ports, sized to provide **tight clearances** (typically **0.05 mm to 0.2 mm**) with the gear teeth.
3. **Bearings:** High-precision **ball bearings** or **sintered bronze bushings** to support gear shafts.
4. **Drive Shaft & Coupling:** Transmits gear rotation to the external counter/sensor.
5. **Pulse Pickup:** Either a **magnetic pickup** (detects gear-tooth passage via Hall effect sensors) or a **reed switch** arrangement.
6. **Casing & Body:** Pressure-rated housing, often with **flanged** or **threaded** end connections.
7. **Seals & O-rings:** Prevent leakage along the shaft and between chambers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU University Exam — they account for ~70% of numerical problems on PD meters.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Displacement Volume per Revolution** | $V_d = 4 \cdot V_g$ | m³/rev | $V_g$ = volume between one gear tooth and chamber wall |
| 2 | **Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)** | $Q = N \cdot V_d$ | m³/s | $N$ = rotational speed (revolutions/sec) |
| 3 | **Total Volume (Cumulative)** | $V_t = N_{total} \cdot V_d$ | m³ | $N_{total}$ = total number of revolutions |
| 4 | **Pulse Frequency (f)** | $f = N \cdot z$ | Hz | $z$ = number of teeth per gear (typically $z = 4$ for oval gears) |
| 5 | **Flow Rate from Pulse Frequency** | $Q = \dfrac{f \cdot V_d}{z}$ | m³/s | Direct electrical read-out relationship |
| 6 | **K-Factor (Meter Constant)** | $K = \dfrac{f}{Q} = \dfrac{z}{V_d}$ | pulses/m³ | Calibration constant (given on meter nameplate) |
| 7 | **Instantaneous Flow from Pulses** | $Q = \dfrac{f}{K}$ | m³/s | Practical form used in instrumentation |
| 8 | **Reynolds Number for Viscosity Check** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Used to verify laminar flow assumption |
| 9 | **Pressure Drop Across Meter** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \cdot \rho \cdot Q^2}{\pi^2 \cdot D^4} \cdot K_{loss}$ | Pa | Indicates energy loss in the meter |
| 10 | **Slip (Leakage) Loss** | $Q_{slip} = k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu}$ | m³/s | $k$ = slip constant; lower viscosity = more slip |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU 2024:** The **K-factor formula** $K = z / V_d$ is the single most-tested relationship. It links **mechanical design** ($V_d$, $z$) to **electrical output** ($f$), and examiners love to ask numerical problems on it.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Oval gear meters are extensively used in industries where **high accuracy** and **viscosity tolerance** are paramount:

- **Petroleum Industry:** Custody transfer of crude oil, diesel, gasoline, and aviation fuel at refineries and fuel depots.
- **Chemical Processing:** Measurement of aggressive chemicals, solvents, and polymeric resins.
- **Lubricant & Hydraulic Systems:** Engine oil, gear oil, and hydraulic fluid monitoring.
- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Edible oils, syrups, chocolate, and milk products (**sanitary-grade stainless steel** versions with CIP/SIP capability).
- **Paint & Coatings:** High-viscosity latex paints and pigments.
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Precise dosing of liquid ingredients.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike orifice plates or venturi meters, oval gear meters do **not** require long straight pipe runs upstream or downstream — a significant advantage in congested plant layouts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Displacement Volume ($V_d$) Per Revolution

The displacement volume per revolution is the **sum of four crescent-shaped volumes** trapped between the gear teeth and the chamber wall.

### Setup & Assumptions
- Let the **major axis** of the oval gear = $2a$ (semi-major axis = $a$)
- Let the **minor axis** of the oval gear = $2b$ (semi-minor axis = $b$)
- Let the **chamber radius** = $R$ (where $R > a$)
- Each oval gear has $z = 4$ teeth (standard design).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Area trapped between one gear tooth and the chamber wall:**

The volume between a single tooth and the chamber wall (assumed constant along the chamber length $L$) is:

$$
V_g = L \cdot \left( \pi R^2 - \pi a b \right)
$$

*Explanation:* The chamber cross-section is a circle of area $\pi R^2$, and the gear occupies an oval area of $\pi a b$. Their difference, multiplied by chamber length $L$, gives the volume.

**Step 2 — Total displacement per revolution:**

Since there are **two oval gears**, and each gear traps fluid on **two sides** (inlet side and outlet side), the total displacement volume per revolution is:

$$
V_d = 4 \cdot V_g
$$

*Explanation:* With 2 gears × 2 fluid-trapping regions each = 4 distinct trapped volumes per revolution.

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_g$ into the equation:**

$$
V_d = 4 \cdot L \cdot \left( \pi R^2 - \pi a b \right)
$$

$$
V_d = 4 \pi L \left( R^2 - a b \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate:**

If the gears rotate at $N$ revolutions per second:

$$
Q = N \cdot V_d
$$

$$
Q = 4 \pi L N \left( R^2 - a b \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the K-factor (Meter Constant):**

The K-factor is the **number of pulses per unit volume**, which is the **reciprocal** of displacement volume per tooth-pass:

$$
K = \frac{z}{V_d} = \frac{4}{4 \pi L (R^2 - a b)} = \frac{1}{\pi L (R^2 - a b)}
$$

*Explanation:* Since each gear has $z = 4$ teeth, and each revolution produces 4 tooth-passes, the pulse frequency per unit volume is $4/V_d$, simplified to the expression above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The K-factor is **independent of fluid properties** (density, viscosity) — this is the **fundamental reason** oval gear meters are so accurate for custody transfer applications.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

### Problem Statement
An oval gear meter has the following specifications:
- Chamber radius: $R = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- Gear semi-major axis: $a = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- Gear semi-minor axis: $b = 15$ mm $= 0.015$ m
- Chamber length: $L = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m
- Number of teeth per gear: $z = 4$
- Gear rotational speed: $N = 10$ rev/s

**Calculate:**
(a) Displacement volume per revolution, $V_d$
(b) Volumetric flow rate, $Q$
(c) K-factor of the meter
(d) Pulse frequency output

### Solution

**(a) Displacement Volume Per Revolution:**

$$
V_d = 4 \pi L (R^2 - a b)
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
V_d = 4 \pi \times 0.050 \times \left( (0.025)^2 - (0.020)(0.015) \right)
$$

$$
V_d = 4 \pi \times 0.050 \times \left( 0.000625 - 0.000300 \right)
$$

$$
V_d = 4 \pi \times 0.050 \times 0.000325
$$

$$
V_d = 4 \pi \times 1.625 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
V_d = 2.0419 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 204.19 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
$$

**(b) Volumetric Flow Rate:**

$$
Q = N \cdot V_d
$$

$$
Q = 10 \times 2.0419 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
Q = 2.0419 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.0419 \text{ L/s}
$$

**(c) K-Factor (Meter Constant):**

$$
K = \frac{z}{V_d} = \frac{4}{2.0419 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
K = 19{,}588 \text{ pulses/m}^3
$$

**(d) Pulse Frequency Output:**

$$
f = N \cdot z = 10 \times 4 = 40 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Verification using the alternate relationship:**

$$
f = K \cdot Q = 19{,}588 \times 2.0419 \times 10^{-3} \approx 40 \text{ Hz} \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** Always verify your K-factor calculation by checking that $f = K \cdot Q$ yields the same result as $f = N \cdot z$. This is a self-check technique examiners appreciate seeing in your answer scripts.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance Characteristics

| Characteristic | Typical Value | KTU Exam Relevance |
|----------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 0.5\%$ of reading | Often asked in MCQ/comparison sections |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm 0.05\%$ | Demonstrates PD meter superiority |
| **Turndown Ratio** | 10:1 to 20:1 | Compared with turbine/orifice meters |
| **Pressure Drop** | 0.1 to 1 bar at full scale | Energy loss analysis question |
| **Viscosity Range** | 1 cP to 1,000,000 cP | Application-selection question |
| **Temperature Range** | $-40°\text{C}$ to $+300°\text{C}$ | Material selection question |
| **Maximum Pressure** | Up to **100 bar** (ANSI 600#) | Process compatibility |
| **Calibration Drift** | $< 0.1\%$ per year | Long-term reliability |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Oval Gear Meter Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressurized Process Fluid Inlet] --> B[Inlet Port of Measuring Chamber]
    B --> C[Fluid Contacts Oval Gear Teeth]
    C --> D[Pressure Differential Creates Torque]
    D --> E[Oval Gears Rotate in Opposite Directions]
    E --> F[Fluid Trapped in Crescent-Shaped Volume]
    F --> G[Trapped Fluid Carried Around Chamber]
    G --> H[Trapped Fluid Squeezed Out at Outlet Port]
    H --> I[Fluid Exits to Process Line]
    E --> J[Gear Shaft Rotation]
    J --> K[Magnetic Pickup or Reed Switch]
    K --> L[Pulse Signal Output]
    L --> M[Flow Indicator / Totalizer / Controller]
    M --> N[Display: Instantaneous Flow Rate]
    M --> O[Display: Cumulative Total Volume]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Signal Flow & Instrumentation Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OV[Oval Gear Meter Assembly]
        A1[Oval Rotor Pair] --> A2[Magnetic Coupling]
        A2 --> A3[Pulse Pickup Sensor]
    end
    subgraph SIG[Signal Conditioning Unit]
        A3 --> B1[Signal Amplifier]
        B1 --> B2[Frequency-to-Voltage Converter]
        B1 --> B3[Pulse Counter]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output & Display]
        B2 --> C1[Flow Rate Indicator]
        B3 --> C2[Totalizer Display]
        B2 --> C3[4-20 mA Current Output]
        C3 --> C4[PLC / DCS / SCADA]
    end
```

## 4.3 Constructional Block Schematic — Internal Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CH[Measuring Chamber Assembly]
        D1[Inlet Port Flange] --> D2[Oval Gear 1 - Top]
        D2 --> D3[Oval Gear 2 - Bottom]
        D3 --> D4[Outlet Port Flange]
        D1 --- D5[Bearing Housing 1]
        D3 --- D6[Bearing Housing 2]
        D5 --- D7[Drive Shaft]
        D6 --- D8[Idler Shaft]
    end
    D7 --> E1[External Pulse Pickup Coupling]
    E1 --> E2[Magnetic Pickup Coil]
    E2 --> E3[Pulse Output to Indicator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams represent a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the interactions of the physical components. For physical cutaway views of the meter, refer to your standard KTU 2024 Scheme textbook diagrams for **PCAET602 – Industrial Instrumentation**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"List any three salient features of Oval Gear Meters and mention one specific industrial application where they are preferred over turbine meters."**

**Model Answer:**

**Three salient features of Oval Gear Meters:**

1. **High accuracy** ($\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 1\%$ of actual flow), making them suitable for custody transfer.
2. **Viscosity independence** — they can accurately measure highly viscous fluids (up to 1,000,000 cP) without calibration changes.
3. **No straight pipe run requirement** — they can be installed directly downstream of fittings without losing accuracy.

**Preferred industrial application over turbine meters:**

In **petroleum refineries** for **measuring heavy fuel oil and lubricating oils** (high-viscosity hydrocarbons). Turbine meters lose accuracy at high viscosities because the rotor drag becomes comparable to the driving torque, whereas oval gear meters use the **pressure differential** to drive the rotors and remain accurate.

*(Total: 3 marks — 2 marks for features, 1 mark for application justification)*

---

### Question 2: Short-Answer Definition

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"Define the term 'K-factor' of an oval gear meter and state its SI unit."**

**Model Answer:**

The **K-factor** of an oval gear meter is defined as the **number of output pulses generated per unit volume of fluid** passing through the meter. It is a **calibration constant** that relates the meter's mechanical design to its electrical output.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$
K = \frac{f}{Q} = \frac{z}{V_d}
$$

where $f$ is the pulse frequency, $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate, $z$ is the number of teeth per gear, and $V_d$ is the displacement volume per revolution.

**SI Unit:** **pulses per cubic metre (pulses/m³)** — though in practice, manufacturers often quote it in **pulses/litre** or **pulses/gallon**.

*(Total: 3 marks — 2 marks for definition + formula, 1 mark for SI unit)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle** of an Oval Gear Meter with a neat functional block diagram. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** An oval gear flow meter has a displacement volume of $150$ cm³/rev. If the meter registers an average pulse frequency of $120$ Hz and has 4 teeth per gear, calculate:
> - (i) The rotational speed of the gears in rpm
> - (ii) The K-factor of the meter
> - (iii) The volumetric flow rate in litres/minute *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Construction:**

An oval gear meter consists of the following key components:
- **Two oval-shaped precision-machined rotors** with interlocking teeth, mounted on parallel shafts.
- A **cylindrical measuring chamber** with precisely bored inlet and outlet ports.
- **Bearings and shaft seals** to support and seal the rotor shafts.
- A **magnetic pickup** (Hall-effect sensor or variable reluctance coil) to detect rotor tooth passage.
- A **pressure-rated external casing** with flanged process connections.

**Working Principle:**

When pressurized process fluid enters the inlet port, it exerts force on the curved surfaces of the two oval gears. Due to the **pressure differential** between inlet and outlet, a **net torque** is developed on the gears, causing them to rotate in **opposite directions** with their teeth meshed together. With each rotation, a **fixed volume** of fluid (trapped between the gear, the chamber wall, and the adjacent gear) is **displaced** from inlet to outlet. The **magnetic pickup** senses each tooth passing and produces a **pulse signal** whose frequency is proportional to the flow rate.

**Functional Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid flow diagram.)

**Key Formula:**

$$
Q = N \cdot V_d \quad \text{and} \quad K = \frac{z}{V_d}
$$

*(7 marks: 3 for construction, 3 for working, 1 for key formula/diagram)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:**
- Displacement volume per revolution: $V_d = 150$ cm³/rev $= 150 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev
- Pulse frequency: $f = 120$ Hz
- Teeth per gear: $z = 4$

**(i) Rotational speed in rpm:**

Each revolution produces 4 tooth-pulses (one per tooth). So pulse frequency relates to rotational speed as:

$$
f = N \cdot z
$$

$$
N = \frac{f}{z} = \frac{120}{4} = 30 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Convert to rpm:

$$
N_{rpm} = 30 \times 60 = 1800 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) K-factor of the meter:**

$$
K = \frac{z}{V_d} = \frac{4}{150 \times 10^{-6}} = 26{,}667 \text{ pulses/m}^3
$$

**(iii) Volumetric flow rate in litres/minute:**

Using the K-factor relation:

$$
Q = \frac{f}{K} = \frac{120}{26{,}667} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to litres/minute:

$$
Q = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 60 = 270 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Verification using $Q = N \cdot V_d$:**

$$
Q = 30 \text{ rev/s} \times 150 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \checkmark
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii) with unit conversion)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and industrial applications** of Oval Gear Meters. Compare their performance with **Turbine Flow Meters** in terms of accuracy, viscosity tolerance, and pressure drop. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With the help of a **characteristic curve**, explain how the **K-factor of an oval gear meter varies with flow rate**. A 50 mm oval gear meter has a nominal K-factor of 5,000 pulses/m³. At a flow rate of 20 m³/h, the actual K-factor is 5,100 pulses/m³. Calculate the **percentage error** in flow measurement. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Advantages of Oval Gear Meters:**
1. High accuracy ($\pm 0.5\%$) and excellent repeatability.
2. Viscosity-independent measurement.
3. No straight pipe run requirement.
4. Suitable for very low flow rates.
5. Bidirectional measurement capability (with proper sensor placement).

**Limitations:**
1. Not suitable for fluids containing suspended solids (gears can jam).
2. Higher pressure drop than turbine meters.
3. Moving parts subject to wear; periodic maintenance required.
4. Not suitable for gases or two-phase flow.
5. Limited to clean, lubricating or non-corrosive fluids unless exotic materials are used.

**Industrial Applications:**
- Petroleum custody transfer, fuel dispensing, chemical dosing, food-grade liquid measurement, paint and resin flow, hydraulic oil monitoring.

**Comparison with Turbine Flow Meters:**

| Parameter | Oval Gear Meter | Turbine Flow Meter |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Accuracy | $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 1\%$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 1.5\%$ |
| Viscosity tolerance | Excellent (1 cP to $10^6$ cP) | Poor above 50 cP |
| Pressure drop | Higher (0.5–1 bar) | Lower (0.1–0.3 bar) |
| Suspended solids | Intolerant | More tolerant |
| Turndown ratio | 10:1 to 20:1 | 10:1 to 30:1 |

*(7 marks: 2 for advantages, 2 for limitations/applications, 3 for comparison table)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**K-Factor vs Flow Rate Characteristic:**

For an **ideal positive displacement meter**, the K-factor is **constant** across the entire flow range (a horizontal line on the K-vs-Q plot). In **real oval gear meters**, the K-factor shows a slight **upward trend** at low flow rates (due to slip leakage) and a **mild downward drift** at high flow rates (due to bearing friction and gear clearances).

```
K (pulses/m³)
   |
   |   ___---""""""---___
   |  /                   \
   | /                     \
   |/                       \
   +---------------------------→ Q (m³/h)
        Low      Nominal      High
       Flow     Flow Rate     Flow
```

The **useful operating range** is the region where K-factor remains within $\pm 1\%$ of the nominal value.

**Percentage Error Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Nominal K-factor: $K_{nom} = 5{,}000$ pulses/m³
- Actual K-factor: $K_{act} = 5{,}100$ pulses/m³
- Flow rate: $Q = 20$ m³/h

**Step 1 — Calculate actual flow from the actual K-factor:**

$$
Q_{act} = \frac{f}{K_{act}} = \frac{f}{5{,}100}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate indicated flow from the nominal K-factor:**

$$
Q_{ind} = \frac{f}{K_{nom}} = \frac{f}{5{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage error:**

Since flow is inversely proportional to K-factor (at constant frequency), the percentage error in flow is:

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{K_{act} - K_{nom}}{K_{act}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{5{,}100 - 5{,}000}{5{,}100} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{100}{5{,}100} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} = 1.96\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> A **very common mistake** students make in this type of question is to compute the error as $\frac{K_{act} - K_{nom}}{K_{nom}} \times 100$, which gives $2.0\%$. This is **incorrect** for flow measurement. The correct denominator is the **actual K-factor** because flow $Q = f/K$ — so the error in $Q$ is governed by the deviation in $K$ **from the actual value**, not from the nominal. Always identify the **denominator** carefully when computing percentage errors in metrology problems.
>
> **Other common pitfalls:**
> - Forgetting to convert cm³ to m³ (factor of $10^{-6}$). This alone can cost **2–3 marks**.
> - Mixing up the units of $N$ (rev/s vs rpm) in flow rate calculations.
> - Not showing the **K-factor verification step** — examiners reward cross-checking.

*(7 marks: 3 for characteristic curve explanation, 4 for the numerical with proper error formula)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ Oval gear meters are **positive displacement (PD) flow meters** — measure **actual volume**, not velocity.
- ✅ Two **oval-shaped, meshing rotors** rotate in opposite directions driven by the **process pressure differential**.
- ✅ **Displacement volume per revolution:** $V_d = 4 \pi L (R^2 - a b)$
- ✅ **K-factor (meter constant):** $K = z / V_d$ — measured in **pulses/m³**.
- ✅ **Flow rate relationship:** $Q = f / K = N \cdot V_d$
- ✅ **Pulse frequency:** $f = N \cdot z$ (z = 4 teeth for standard oval gears).
- ✅ **Accuracy:** $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 1\%$ — among the highest in industrial flow metering.
- ✅ **Viscosity range:** 1 cP to **$10^6$ cP** — ideal for **heavy oils, resins, and chemicals**.
- ✅ **No straight pipe run** required upstream or downstream.
- ✅ **Not suitable** for fluids with **suspended solids** (gears jam) or for **gases**.
- ✅ **Slip leakage** increases at **low flow rates** and **low viscosities** — main source of error.
- ✅ **Bidirectional** measurement possible with dual pickup sensors.
- ✅ **Custody transfer** applications: petroleum, chemicals, lubricants, food-grade liquids.
- ✅ **Characteristic curve:** K-factor is **nearly constant** in the useful flow range; drifts at extremes.
- ✅ **Self-verification trick:** Always check $f = K \cdot Q$ against $f = N \cdot z$ to validate numerical answers.
- ✅ **Exam focus areas (KTU 2024):** Construction, working, $V_d$ derivation, K-factor problems, and comparison with turbine/orifice meters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nutating Disc Flow Meter. Mass Flowmeters - Radiation Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Nutating Disc Flow Meter. Mass Flowmeters - Radiation Type ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Nutating Disc Flow Meter — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The **Nutating Disc Flow Meter** is a **positive displacement (PD) flowmeter** that measures the volumetric flow rate of liquids by using a precisely machined disc that performs a *nutating* (wobbling) motion inside a circular measuring chamber. Each nutation cycle of the disc displaces a fixed, well-defined volume of liquid, making the device inherently accurate for clean, low-to-medium viscosity fluids. It belongs to the family of **oscillating-piston and nutating-disc PD meters** widely used in residential water metering, fuel dispensing, and chemical batching. The principle of **positive displacement** ensures that the meter is virtually independent of flow profile, viscosity changes (within limits), and Reynolds number — a key advantage for KTU numerical problems involving flow accuracy. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coin-Spinning Water Wheel" Imagine a **round disc** the size of a dinner plate sealed inside a cylindrical bowl filled with water. The disc is not allowed to spin flat — instead, it is forced to **wobble (nutate)** like a spinning coin losing momentum. With every complete wobble: - A **fixed volume of water** is pushed from the inlet side to the outlet side. - A mechanical **counter registers one "click"** for each complete wobble. If the disc wobbles **N times per second**, and each wobble moves a known volume **Vd**, then: > **Volume Flow Rate = N × Vd (litres/second)** This is exactly the principle used in your **domestic water meter** (often a nutating disc meter from brands like **Kent, Itron, or Elster**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Nutating Disc meter is a **volumetric flowmeter**, NOT a mass flowmeter. To obtain mass flow, the measured volume must be multiplied by the fluid density. For a 14-mark question, students often confuse this — examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ### 1.3 Mass Flowmeter — Radiation Type — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** > A **Radiation (Radiometric) Type Mass Flowmeter** is a non-intrusive flow measurement device that uses the **attenuation of gamma radiation** through a flowing fluid to determine its **mass per unit length (line density)**, which when multiplied by the **cross-sectional area** of the pipe and the **velocity of the fluid**, yields the **true mass flow rate**. It is a **true mass flowmeter** (Category II in the KTU classification alongside **Coriolis** and **Thermal** mass flowmeters) and the only one capable of measuring **slurries, highly corrosive fluids, two-phase mixtures**, and fluids inside **high-temperature/pressure sealed vessels without any pipe penetration**. --- ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport Security Scanner for Pipes" Think of a **gamma-ray source** mounted on one side of a pipe and a **radiation detector** on the opposite side — exactly like the **baggage scanner at an airport**. As fluid flows through the pipe: - Heavier/more dense fluid → **fewer gamma rays reach the detector** (more attenuation). - Lighter/less dense fluid → **more gamma rays pass through**. The detector therefore indirectly senses the **mass of fluid** between the source and detector. Combine this with the **cross-sectional area** of the pipe, and you have the **mass per unit length**, which is the foundation of mass flow measurement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Radioactive Isotopes Used:** > - **Cs-137 (Cesium-137)** — half-life **~30.17 years**, emits **0.662 MeV gamma rays** (most common). > - **Co-60 (Cobalt-60)** — half-life **~5.27 years**, emits **1.17 & 1.33 MeV gamma rays** (used for thick walls/high-pressure pipes). > - **Am-241 (Americium-241)** — used for very thin walls / low-density fluids. --- ### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Highlights) | Metric | Nutating Disc Meter | Radiation Mass Flowmeter | |---|---|---| | **Accuracy** | **±0.5% to ±2% of reading** | **±0.5% to ±1%** | | **Flow Range** | **0.5 L/h to 1500 m³/h** | **No upper theoretical limit** | | **Turndown Ratio** | **10:1 typical** | **100:1 or higher** | | **Pressure Drop** | **Moderate (0.2–1 bar)** | **ZERO (non-intrusive)** | | **Fluid Type** | Clean liquids only | **Slurries, sludges, two-phase** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Nutating Disc Flow Meter — Operational Concept The nutating disc meter operates on a **cyclic positive displacement** principle. Let us break down the *why* and *how* of each operational stage: #### Stage 1 — Fluid Entry & Chamber Division - Liquid enters the **measuring chamber** through the inlet port. - A **central thrust ball** holds the disc in a fixed tilted orientation (a *nutation axis*). - The disc separates the chamber into two compartments: one **above** the disc (inlet) and one **below** (outlet). #### Stage 2 — The Nutating Motion - Inlet pressure pushes the disc, causing it to **wobble (nutate)** around the central ball. - A **drive spindle** transmits this wobbling motion to a **control roller**, which in turn rotates the **calibrated counter** outside the chamber. - The wobble angle is typically **±4° to ±8°** depending on meter design. #### Stage 3 — Displacement & Counting - Each complete nutation cycle ejects a **discrete, fixed volume Vd** from the outlet. - The mechanical (or electronic) counter **increments by one unit** per cycle. - The volume flow rate is obtained by: > **Q = N × Vd** ... (1) where: - **Q** = volumetric flow rate (m³/s or L/s) - **N** = number of nutation cycles per second (Hz) - **Vd** = displaced volume per cycle (m³ or L) #### Stage 4 — Totalisation - For total volume over time **t**: > **V_total = ∫₀ᵗ N(τ) · Vd dτ = N_total · Vd** ... (2) where **N_total** is the cumulative pulse count. --- #### 2.1.1 Why Positive Displacement Works Independent of Viscosity > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Reasoning for KTU Exams:** > In PD meters, the displaced volume is **fixed by the geometry of the chamber**, not by the velocity profile of the fluid. Therefore, the meter counts *packets of fluid* — each packet is geometrically defined. This is why **PD meters remain accurate** even when the Reynolds number changes (unlike orifice plates or Venturis whose discharge coefficient `Cd` varies with Re). --- ### 2.2 Radiation Type Mass Flowmeter — Operational Concept #### Stage 1 — Gamma Ray Emission - A sealed **radioactive source** (Cs-137 or Co-60) is mounted on one side of the pipe using a **source holder** with a **shutter mechanism** (closed during maintenance, open during measurement). - Gamma photons are emitted in a **collimated beam** perpendicular to the pipe axis. #### Stage 2 — Attenuation Through Fluid - The photons pass through the **pipe wall + flowing fluid + opposite pipe wall**. - Their intensity decreases exponentially per the **Beer-Lambert Law**: > **I = I₀ · e^(−μₘ · ρ · L)** ... (3) where: - **I** = transmitted intensity (counts/s at detector) - **I₀** = incident intensity (counts/s with empty pipe) - **μₘ** = **mass attenuation coefficient** of the fluid (m²/kg) - **ρ** = fluid density (kg/m³) - **L** = **internal diameter** of the pipe (m) > [!NOTE] > **Why the term "MASS attenuation coefficient"?** > Because μₘ is a property of the material **per unit mass**, not per unit volume. This is precisely what makes the device a **true mass flowmeter** — attenuation depends on **ρL (kg/m²)**, which is the **areal mass density**. #### Stage 3 — Density Derivation - Rearranging Eq. (3) for density: > **ρ = (1 / (μₘ · L)) · ln(I₀ / I)** ... (4) - This gives the **fluid density in real time**. #### Stage 4 — Velocity Measurement - The velocity **v** is measured independently using: - **Cross-correlation** (most common in radiometric systems) using **two radiation detectors** spaced a known distance **D** apart. - Or an **auxiliary flow sensor** (ultrasonic, magnetic, etc.) for hybrid mass flow systems. #### Stage 5 — Mass Flow Calculation - The final mass flow rate: > **ṁ = ρ · A · v** ... (5) where **A = πD²/4** is the cross-sectional area of the pipe. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Units (SI)** | **Equation / Definition** | |---|---|---|---| | Q | Volumetric flow rate (Nutating Disc) | m³/s | $Q = N \cdot V_d$ | | Vd | Displaced volume per cycle | m³ | Geometry-dependent constant | | N | Cycles per second | Hz (= s⁻¹) | Measured by counter | | Vtotal | Totalised volume | m³ | $V_{total} = N_{total} \cdot V_d$ | | I | Transmitted gamma intensity | counts/s | Detector output | | I₀ | Reference (empty pipe) intensity | counts/s | Calibrated value | | μm | Mass attenuation coefficient | m²/kg | Fluid/material property | | ρ | Fluid density | kg/m³ | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\mu_m L} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | | L | Pipe internal diameter | m | Geometry | | v | Fluid velocity | m/s | From cross-correlation or aux sensor | | A | Pipe cross-section | m² | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | | ṁ | Mass flow rate | kg/s | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | | K | Meter factor (K-factor) | pulses/L | Calibration constant | | Cd | Discharge coefficient | dimensionless | ≈ 1 for PD meters | > [!IMPORTANT] > **NOTE on Markdown Table Pipes:** All absolute values in math are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to keep the markdown table parser stable. Do NOT use raw `|` inside table cells. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Nutating Disc Meter** is the **workhorse of urban water distribution** (KTU-recommended example: Kerala Water Authority domestic meters, IOCL petrol pumps). - **Radiation Mass Flowmeter** is the **only option** for: - **Slurry pipelines** in mining (iron ore, copper tailings). - **Cement industry** — raw meal, clinker, kiln feed. - **Dredging operations** — sand-water mixtures. - **High-temperature molten metal** in foundries. - **Toxic/corrosive chemicals** where no sensor should contact the fluid. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Totalised Volume from Nutating Disc Pulses > **Problem Statement (KTU Standard):** > A nutating disc flow meter has a displacement volume of **Vd = 0.5 L per cycle**. The pulse counter records **N_total = 12,480 pulses in 4 minutes**. Determine the (a) average volumetric flow rate in L/s, and (b) total volume passed in m³. #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Given:** - $V_d = 0.5 \text{ L per cycle}$ - $N_{total} = 12{,}480 \text{ pulses}$ - $t = 4 \text{ min} = 240 \text{ s}$ **Part (a): Average Volumetric Flow Rate** The volumetric flow rate is the total displaced volume per unit time: $$ Q = \frac{N_{total} \cdot V_d}{t} $$ Substituting the values: $$ Q = \frac{12{,}480 \times 0.5 \text{ L}}{240 \text{ s}} $$ $$ Q = \frac{6{,}240 \text{ L}}{240 \text{ s}} $$ $$ \boxed{Q = 26 \text{ L/s}} $$ **Part (b): Total Volume Passed** $$ V_{total} = N_{total} \cdot V_d $$ $$ V_{total} = 12{,}480 \times 0.5 \text{ L} $$ $$ V_{total} = 6{,}240 \text{ L} $$ Converting to cubic metres (1 m³ = 1000 L): $$ V_{total} = \frac{6{,}240}{1{,}000} = \boxed{6.24 \text{ m}^3} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fluid Density from Gamma-Ray Attenuation > **Problem Statement (KTU Standard):** > A radiation-type mass flowmeter uses a **Cs-137 source** across a pipe of internal diameter **L = 250 mm**. The reference intensity (empty pipe) is **I₀ = 8,000 counts/s**, and the transmitted intensity when fluid is flowing is **I = 4,200 counts/s**. The mass attenuation coefficient of the fluid is **μm = 0.0075 m²/kg**. Determine the fluid density. #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Given:** - $I_0 = 8{,}000 \text{ counts/s}$ - $I = 4{,}200 \text{ counts/s}$ - $L = 0.25 \text{ m}$ - $\mu_m = 0.0075 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$ **Step 1 — Apply the Beer-Lambert Equation** The transmitted intensity is: $$ I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_m \rho L} $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange for Density** Divide both sides by I₀: $$ \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu_m \rho L} $$ Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$ \ln\!\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu_m \rho L $$ Multiply both sides by −1: $$ -\ln\!\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \mu_m \rho L $$ Solve for ρ: $$ \rho = \frac{-\ln(I/I_0)}{\mu_m \cdot L} $$ This can also be written as: $$ \rho = \frac{1}{\mu_m L} \cdot \ln\!\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values** $$ \rho = \frac{1}{(0.0075)(0.25)} \cdot \ln\!\!\left(\frac{8{,}000}{4{,}200}\right) $$ Compute the denominator: $$ \mu_m L = 0.0075 \times 0.25 = 0.001875 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg} \cdot \text{m} = 0.001875 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ Compute the logarithm term: $$ \ln\!\!\left(\frac{8{,}000}{4{,}200}\right) = \ln(1.90476) = 0.64436 $$ Compute density: $$ \rho = \frac{0.64436}{0.001875} $$ $$ \boxed{\rho \approx 343.66 \text{ kg/m}^3} $$ This is suspiciously low — it would correspond to a **light hydrocarbon or LPG**. Always sanity-check with the KTU examiner's expected value range. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **negative sign** and report ρ as a negative number, or they use **log base 10** instead of **natural log (ln)**. The Beer-Lambert law in this derivation **requires natural log**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Flow Rate from Combined Density + Velocity > **Problem Statement (KTU Standard):** > Using the fluid density from Derivation 2 (ρ = 343.66 kg/m³), and given that the cross-correlation velocity measurement yields **v = 2.4 m/s** in a pipe of internal diameter **D = 250 mm**, compute the mass flow rate. #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Given:** - $\rho = 343.66 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $v = 2.4 \text{ m/s}$ - $D = 0.25 \text{ m}$ **Step 1 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area** $$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.25)^2}{4} $$ $$ A = \frac{\pi \times 0.0625}{4} = \frac{0.19635}{4} $$ $$ A = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Volumetric Flow Rate** $$ Q = A \cdot v = 0.04909 \times 2.4 $$ $$ Q = 0.11781 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Mass Flow Rate** $$ \dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 343.66 \times 0.11781 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m} \approx 40.49 \text{ kg/s}} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Theory Questions | **Parameter** | **Nutating Disc** | **Radiation Mass Flowmeter** | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Positive displacement (cyclic) | Gamma-ray attenuation + cross-correlation | | **Output Variable** | Volumetric flow (then × ρ) | **True mass flow** (direct) | | **Intrusive?** | Yes (disc inside pipe) | **No (clamp-on style)** | | **Pressure Drop** | 0.2–1 bar | **Zero** | | **Suitable for Slurries?** | **No** (clogs disc) | **Yes (ideal)** | | **Suitable for Gas?** | No (typical) | Yes (with high-energy source) | | **Hazard** | Mechanical wear | **Ionising radiation — licensed handling** | | **Calibration Drift** | Yes (bearing wear) | Stable (source decay compensable) | | **Cost** | Low (₹2k–₹50k) | High (₹5L–₹30L + source license) | | **Accuracy** | ±0.5% to ±2% | ±0.5% to ±1% | | **KTU Tag** | Common in **domestic/municipal** metering | Common in **mining, cement, dredging** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Nutating Disc Flow Meter — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet Port
Fluid Entry] --> B[Measuring Chamber
Circular Cylindrical] B --> C[Central Thrust Ball
Holds Disc Pivot] C --> D[Nutating Disc
Wobbles plus or minus 4 to 8 deg] D --> E[Drive Spindle
Transfers Nutation to Roller] E --> F[Control Roller
Converts Wobble to Rotation] F --> G[Reduction Gear Train
Step Down Speed] G --> H[Register Counter
Displays Total Volume] H --> I[Mechanical or Electronic
Pulse Output] B --> J[Outlet Port
Fluid Exit] D --> K[Chamber Wall
Geometrically Fixed Vd] ``` **Explanation of Block Flow:** - The **measuring chamber** is the heart of the meter; its internal volume is calibrated to yield a precise **Vd** (e.g., 0.5 L/cycle). - The **central thrust ball** is the *pivot* of the nutation motion — it fixes the disc's tilt axis. - The **drive spindle → control roller** chain converts the small wobble into a usable mechanical rotation that the **counter** can register. - The **register counter** is calibrated in **litres or m³** — the display a user sees on a domestic water meter. --- ### 4.2 Radiation Type Mass Flowmeter — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SourceAssembly[Source Assembly] S1[Radioactive Source
Cs137 or Co60] --> S2[Collimator
Focuses Gamma Beam] S2 --> S3[Shutter Mechanism
Open or Closed] end S3 --> PipeWall1[Pipe Wall 1
Carbon Steel] PipeWall1 --> FluidFlow[Flowing Fluid
Slurry or Liquid] FluidFlow --> PipeWall2[Pipe Wall 2
Carbon Steel] PipeWall2 --> D1[Detector 1
Scintillation Counter] FluidFlow --> D2[Detector 2
Scintillation Counter] D1 --> Amp1[Charge Amplifier] D2 --> Amp2[Charge Amplifier] Amp1 --> ADC1[ADC and Pulse Counter] Amp2 --> ADC2[ADC and Pulse Counter] ADC1 --> CC[Cross Correlator
Computes Transit Time] ADC2 --> CC CC --> Vcalc[Velocity Calculator
v = D divided by delta t] ADC1 --> Atten[Attenuation Module
Beer Lambert Solver] Atten --> Denscalc[Density Calculator
rho from ln I0 by I] Vcalc --> MF[Mass Flow Computer
m dot = rho A v] Denscalc --> MF MF --> Out[Display and 4 to 20 mA Output] ``` **Explanation of Block Flow:** - The **source assembly** uses a collimator to direct a focused beam of gamma photons across the pipe. - **Two detectors** (D1 and D2) are placed a known distance D apart along the pipe. The upstream detector (D1) and downstream detector (D2) each record the time-varying fluctuation in the beam. - **Cross-correlation** of the two noise-like signals yields the **transit time Δt** of the fluid "slugs" passing between D1 and D2. Velocity: `v = D / Δt`. - The **attenuation module** uses Eq. (3) to compute density. - The **mass flow computer** combines the two to yield ṁ. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept Hook:** The cross-correlation principle is the same as that used in **ultrasonic flowmeters** and **cross-correlation flowmeters** (Module 3 alternative topic). Recognising this link scores easy marks. --- ### 4.3 Nutating Disc — Working Cycle Sequence ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Stage1: Fluid enters chamber Stage1: Inlet pressure pushes disc
Disc begins to nutate Stage1 --> Stage2: Disc reaches maximum tilt Stage2: Maximum nutation angle
Disc wobble = 4 to 8 deg Stage2 --> Stage3: Spindle rotates control roller Stage3: Control roller drives gear train
Counter increments by one Stage3 --> Stage4: Outlet valve closes Stage4: One displacement volume Vd ejected
Cycle complete Stage4 --> [*]: Next cycle begins ``` > [!NOTE] > **State Diagram Tip:** Use this in KTU viva when asked "Explain the working of a nutating disc meter in 4 stages." Examiners love numbered, stage-by-stage answers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. Define positive displacement flowmeter. Why is the nutating disc meter classified as one?** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q2(a)]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **positive displacement (PD) flowmeter** is a device that measures flow by **repeatedly entrapping and discharging fixed volumes of fluid** using moving mechanical elements. The total flow is determined by **counting the number of displacement cycles**. > The **nutating disc meter** is classified as a PD meter because: > 1. A precisely machined disc **wobbles (nutates)** inside a chamber of **fixed geometric volume Vd**. > 2. Each complete nutation cycle **ejects a discrete, fixed volume** of liquid through the outlet. > 3. The total flow is obtained by **counting cycles**: $V_{total} = N_{total} \cdot V_d$. > *(Allocate: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the 3-point justification.)* --- #### **Q2. List any three applications of radiation-type mass flowmeters.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Q1(c)]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Mining & mineral processing** — measurement of iron-ore slurry, copper tailings, and coal-water mixtures in pipelines. 2. **Cement industry** — monitoring raw meal, kiln feed, and clinker flow in pre-heater towers. 3. **Dredging operations** — measuring sand-water slurry in suction pipelines. 4. *(Bonus valid answer)* **High-temperature molten metals** in foundries where contact sensors fail. 5. *(Bonus valid answer)* **Toxic / corrosive chemicals** (e.g., HCl, HF) where no wetted-part sensor is acceptable. > *(Allocate: 1 mark per valid application; mention any 3.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 3]` **Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle of a Nutating Disc Flow Meter**. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A nutating disc flow meter has a displacement volume of **Vd = 1.2 L/cycle**. During a 6-minute test, the counter records **9,000 pulses**.     **(i)** Calculate the **average volumetric flow rate** in m³/h.     **(ii)** If the fluid is **kerosene (ρ = 780 kg/m³)**, find the **average mass flow rate** in kg/h.     **(iii)** State the assumption regarding flow constancy that your answer relies on. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Measuring chamber** — precision-machined cylinder. - **Nutating disc** — flat disc with a central spherical depression. - **Central thrust ball** — sits in the disc's depression, fixed at the chamber top. - **Drive spindle & control roller** — converts nutation to rotation. - **Reduction gear train** — slows the rotation to readable speeds. - **Register counter** — displays total volume in litres / m³. - **Inlet and outlet ports** — separated by a partition. **Working (3 marks):** 1. Fluid enters via the **inlet port**, creating differential pressure. 2. Pressure forces the disc to **nutate (wobble)** around the thrust ball. 3. The **wobble angle** is geometrically limited; each cycle ejects volume Vd. 4. Spindle → roller → gear → counter registers **one count per cycle**. 5. Volumetric flow: $Q = N \cdot V_d$. **Advantages (0.5 marks each, any 2):** - High accuracy (±0.5% to ±2%). - Independent of viscosity and flow profile. - Suitable for clean, low-viscosity liquids. - Simple, no external power needed (mechanical). **Limitations (0.5 marks each, any 2):** - Moving parts → wear and tear. - Not suitable for slurries or dirty fluids. - Pressure drop (0.2–1 bar). - Limited turndown ratio (~10:1). --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Given:** - $V_d = 1.2 \text{ L/cycle}$ - $N_{total} = 9{,}000 \text{ pulses}$ - $t = 6 \text{ min} = 0.1 \text{ h}$ - $\rho = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Part (i) — Volumetric Flow Rate** $$ V_{total} = N_{total} \cdot V_d = 9{,}000 \times 1.2 = 10{,}800 \text{ L} $$ Convert to m³: $10{,}800 / 1{,}000 = 10.8 \text{ m}^3$ $$ Q = \frac{V_{total}}{t} = \frac{10.8 \text{ m}^3}{0.1 \text{ h}} $$ $$ \boxed{Q = 108 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}} $$ **Part (ii) — Mass Flow Rate** $$ \dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 780 \times 108 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m} = 84{,}240 \text{ kg/h}} $$ **Part (iii) — Assumption** > The flow is assumed to be **steady and uniform** over the 6-minute duration. If the flow were pulsating or transient, the average value over `t` would be used and the instantaneous Q would vary. The PD meter would still record correctly because of its volumetric-by-cycle nature, but the **reported "average"** is meaningful only when N is constant or averaged appropriately. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 1]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a Radiation (Radiometric) Type Mass Flowmeter** with a block diagram. State the role of **Cs-137** and the **Beer-Lambert Law** in the measurement. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A radiation mass flowmeter uses a **Co-60 source** across a pipe of inner diameter **D = 200 mm**. The empty-pipe reference intensity is **I₀ = 10,000 counts/s**, and the operating intensity is **I = 6,500 counts/s**. The fluid's mass attenuation coefficient is **μm = 0.0082 m²/kg**. The cross-correlation velocity sensor reports **v = 1.8 m/s**.     **(i)** Calculate the **fluid density** in kg/m³.     **(ii)** Calculate the **mass flow rate** in kg/s.     **(iii)** If the source decays to **85% of its original activity over 1 year**, by what percentage does the apparent density change if the meter is **not recalibrated**? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Principle (3 marks):** - A radioactive source (typically Cs-137 or Co-60) emits a **collimated beam of gamma photons** across the pipe. - The photons pass through the **pipe wall + flowing fluid** and reach a **scintillation detector** on the opposite side. - The intensity of the transmitted beam is governed by the **Beer-Lambert Law**: $$ I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_m \rho L} $$ - From this, the **fluid density ρ** is computed. - The **fluid velocity v** is obtained using **cross-correlation** of two detector signals. - The mass flow rate is: $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2 above — the Mermaid block diagram.) **Role of Cs-137 (1 mark):** - Cs-137 emits mono-energetic **0.662 MeV gamma rays**, ideal for industrial pipe diameters (50–500 mm). - Long half-life (~30 years) ensures stable calibration over decades. - Well-suited to medium-density fluids (water, slurries, hydrocarbons). --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Given:** - $D = 0.20 \text{ m} \Rightarrow L = 0.20 \text{ m}$ - $I_0 = 10{,}000 \text{ counts/s}$ - $I = 6{,}500 \text{counts/s}$ - $\mu_m = 0.0082 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$ - $v = 1.8 \text{ m/s}$ **Part (i) — Fluid Density** $$ \rho = \frac{1}{\mu_m L} \cdot \ln\!\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) $$ $$ \rho = \frac{1}{(0.0082)(0.20)} \cdot \ln\!\!\left(\frac{10{,}000}{6{,}500}\right) $$ Compute denominator: $$ \mu_m L = 0.0082 \times 0.20 = 0.00164 $$ Compute the log term: $$ \ln\!\!\left(\frac{10{,}000}{6{,}500}\right) = \ln(1.53846) = 0.43078 $$ Therefore: $$ \rho = \frac{0.43078}{0.00164} = 262.67 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{\rho \approx 262.67 \text{ kg/m}^3} $$ **Part (ii) — Mass Flow Rate** Cross-sectional area: $$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.20)^2}{4} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2 $$ Volumetric flow rate: $$ Q = A \cdot v = 0.03142 \times 1.8 = 0.05655 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ Mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 262.67 \times 0.05655 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m} \approx 14.85 \text{ kg/s}} $$ **Part (iii) — Effect of Source Decay Without Recalibration** If the source decays to 85% of original activity, the **measured transmitted intensity I** also reduces to 85% of the original (assuming fluid density unchanged). The meter still uses the **original I₀ = 10,000** in its calculation. Apparent density becomes: $$ \rho_{apparent} = \frac{1}{\mu_m L} \cdot \ln\!\!\left(\frac{10{,}000}{0.85 \times 6{,}500}\right) $$ $$ = \frac{1}{0.00164} \cdot \ln\!\!\left(\frac{10{,}000}{5{,}525}\right) $$ $$ = 609.76 \times \ln(1.80995) = 609.76 \times 0.5930 $$ $$ \rho_{apparent} = 361.62 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ True density was 262.67 kg/m³. Apparent density reading is **higher than true**: $$ \text{Error} = \frac{361.62 - 262.67}{262.67} \times 100\% = 37.67\% $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Apparent density error} \approx +37.7\%} $$ > This is a **KTU trap question** — students often conclude the answer is "−15%" (just the source decay) without re-running the Beer-Lambert math. **Examiners award full marks only to those who show the logarithmic re-calculation.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Units mismatch:** Forgetting to convert L in mm to m before using it in the Beer-Lambert equation. Always use SI units. > 2. **Sign confusion:** `ln(I/I₀)` is **negative** because `I < I₀`; flipping the ratio gives a **positive** log. > 3. **Forgetting the cross-section A:** Mass flow requires both ρ and Q = A·v, not just ρ and v. > 4. **Calling the nutating disc meter a "mass flowmeter":** It is a **volumetric** flowmeter. Mass flow is only obtained by multiplying by density. > 5. **Confusing Cs-137 with Co-60 energies:** Cs-137 → 0.662 MeV; Co-60 → 1.17 + 1.33 MeV. Mention the right one for the application. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3** - ✅ **Nutating Disc Meter** = **Positive Displacement (PD) Volumetric** flowmeter. - ✅ Working: disc **wobbles** around a central ball; each cycle ejects a **fixed volume Vd**. - ✅ Formula: $Q = N \cdot V_d$, $V_{total} = N_{total} \cdot V_d$. - ✅ **Strengths:** High accuracy, viscosity-independent, simple, no power needed. - ✅ **Weaknesses:** Moving parts wear out, **not for slurries**, has pressure drop. - ✅ **Typical uses:** Domestic water meters, petrol pumps, chemical batching. - ✅ **Radiation Mass Flowmeter** = **Non-intrusive, true mass flow** measurement. - ✅ Uses **Cs-137 (0.662 MeV)** or **Co-60 (1.17/1.33 MeV)** gamma sources. - ✅ Core law: $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_m \rho L}$ → **Beer-Lambert Law**. - ✅ Density formula: $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\mu_m L} \cdot \ln(I_0 / I)$. - ✅ Velocity from **cross-correlation of two detectors** (separation D, transit time Δt): $v = D / \Delta t$. - ✅ Mass flow: $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$. - ✅ **Best for:** Slurries, mining, cement, dredging, toxic/corrosive fluids, molten metals. - ✅ **Key advantage:** **Zero pressure drop**, no wetted parts. - ✅ **Key hazard:** **Ionising radiation** → requires **AERB license** in India. - ✅ **Source decay** (e.g., to 85%) causes a **non-linear** apparent density error (logarithmic amplification) → must be **compensated** or **recalibrated** annually. - ✅ **Kt = K-factor** (pulses per litre) is the meter calibration constant — check it during installation. - ✅ **Comparison Hook:** Coriolis (inertia-based), Thermal (heat-transfer-based), Radiation (attenuation-based) are the **three KTU-classified true mass flowmeters**.

topics.title: Angular Momentum Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Angular Momentum Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angular Momentum Type Mass Flowmeter (KTU 2024 Definition):** A mass flow rate measuring instrument based on the principle of **conservation of angular momentum**, in which a motor-driven impeller imparts a precisely controlled angular momentum to the flowing fluid, and the reactive torque (or the torque developed by a downstream turbine receiving the spinning fluid) is measured to infer the true **mass flow rate** — independent of fluid density, viscosity, temperature, and pressure.

The meter belongs to the family of **true mass flowmeters** (as opposed to inferential or volumetric meters), and is governed directly by **Newton's Second Law for rotating systems**:

$$T = \frac{dL}{dt}$$

where $T$ is the applied torque and $L$ is the angular momentum of the fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Tag:** This topic falls under the *Mass Flow Measurement* sub-module of Module 3 in PCAET602 and is frequently linked to the **Coriolis principle** in subsequent modules — students must master the angular momentum equation before progressing.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a frictionless rotating platform (a "lazy Susan") holding a basketball. A friend gently tosses a tennis ball onto the spinning platform:

- The tennis ball picks up some spin (gains angular momentum) from the platform.
- To keep spinning at the same rate, **you must push the platform forward** to compensate — you feel a *reactive torque* in your arms.
- If your friend starts throwing tennis balls **faster** (higher mass flow rate), the **torque you feel increases**.
- The reactive torque is **directly proportional to the mass flow rate of balls** — not their size, not their speed across the floor, not the air density.

The **Angular Momentum Mass Flowmeter works exactly on this principle**:

| Component in Analogy | Physical Element in Meter |
|---|---|
| Lazy Susan | Motor-driven **impeller** |
| Tennis balls | Process **fluid** |
| Reactive force in your arms | **Torque sensor** (spring deflection / turbine reaction) |
| Rate of throwing balls | **Mass flow rate** $q_m$ |

The crucial insight: the measured quantity (torque) is a **direct, primary indication of mass flow** — no density correction, no Reynolds number correction, no temperature compensation required.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters industrially:** In a chemical plant measuring concentrated sulfuric acid, steam, or crude oil — all fluids with wildly varying densities and viscosities — the angular momentum meter reads *the same* $q_m$ for the same mass throughput. This is its defining industrial advantage.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_m$ | Mass flow rate | **kg/s** | $0.01$ – $500$ kg/s |
| $T$ | Torque | **N·m** | $10^{-4}$ – $10$ N·m |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | **rad/s** | $10$ – $300$ rad/s |
| $r$ | Impeller radius | **m** | $0.01$ – $0.1$ m |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | **kg/m³** | (does not appear in final equation) |
| $G$ | Gravitational constant | **9.81 m/s²** | (only for weight-flow comparison) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The defining elegance:** Notice that the fluid density $\rho$ and the volumetric flow rate $Q$ **cancel out** in the governing equation. This is *why* the angular momentum meter is classified as a **true mass flowmeter** under ISA/ISO standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physical Principles

The operation rests on **three cascading laws of mechanics**:

### Step 1 — Newton's Second Law (Rotational Form)
A torque applied to a body equals the rate of change of its angular momentum:

$$T = \frac{dL}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Angular Momentum of a Fluid Element
A small fluid element of mass $dm$ moving with tangential velocity $v_\theta$ at radius $r$ possesses angular momentum:

$$dL = r \cdot v_\theta \cdot dm$$

If the impeller imparts tangential velocity $v_\theta = \omega r$ (no-slip condition), then:

$$dL = r \cdot (\omega r) \cdot dm = \omega r^2 \, dm$$

### Step 3 — Rate of Angular Momentum Transfer
For a steady mass flow rate $q_m = dm/dt$:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \omega r^2 \cdot q_m$$

This rate of angular momentum transfer **must equal the externally applied torque** $T$ (action-reaction via the impeller shaft):

$$T = q_m \cdot \omega \cdot r^2$$

Solving for the **measured quantity** (mass flow rate):

$$\boxed{q_m = \frac{T}{\omega \cdot r^2}}$$

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Term

- **$T$ in the numerator:** A larger torque is needed to maintain impeller speed when more mass passes through per second — physically intuitive.
- **$\omega$ in the denominator:** Faster impeller spin imparts *more* angular momentum per unit mass, so *less* mass is required to produce a given torque — inverse relationship.
- **$r^2$ in the denominator:** Larger impeller radius gives each mass element a *larger lever arm* and *higher tangential velocity* ($\omega r$), so angular momentum transfer scales with $r^2$ — quadratic amplification.

---

## 2.3 Two Industrial Realizations

### (a) Twin-Turbine (Reaction-Type) Design
Two turbines on a common shaft: the upstream turbine is **driven by the motor** (impeller), the downstream turbine is **driven by the spinning fluid** (reactor). The angular deflection between them is proportional to torque $T$.

$$q_m = \frac{T}{\omega \cdot (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}$$

where $r_1$ = driven impeller radius, $r_2$ = downstream turbine radius.

### (b) Spring-Coupled Impeller Design
A single impeller is connected to the motor via a **calibrated torsion spring**. The angular twist $\theta$ of the spring (measured by an LVDT or strain gauge) is directly proportional to torque $T$:

$$T = k_s \cdot \theta$$

Substituting:

$$q_m = \frac{k_s \cdot \theta}{\omega \cdot r^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The *spring constant $k_s$* and the *impeller radius $r$* are **fixed geometric/mechanical parameters** of the meter. Once calibrated, the output $\theta$ (or the equivalent voltage from the LVDT) is **directly proportional to $q_m$** — a beautifully linear transducer.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = q_m \cdot \omega \cdot r^2$ | Torque–flow fundamental relation | N·m | Basic single-impeller analysis |
| 2 | $q_m = \dfrac{T}{\omega \cdot r^2}$ | Mass flow rate from torque | kg/s | Solving for $q_m$ |
| 3 | $q_m = \dfrac{T}{\omega(r_2^2 - r_1^2)}$ | Twin-turbine variant | kg/s | Reaction-type meter |
| 4 | $T = k_s \cdot \theta$ | Spring torque relation | N·m | Spring-coupled design |
| 5 | $q_m = \dfrac{k_s \cdot \theta}{\omega \cdot r^2}$ | Complete transducer equation | kg/s | Calibration / output |
| 6 | $L = m \cdot \omega \cdot r^2$ | Angular momentum of point mass | kg·m²/s | Derivation check |
| 7 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Motor power required | W (J/s) | Energy / efficiency analysis |
| 8 | $v_\theta = \omega r$ | No-slip tangential velocity | m/s | Velocity field analysis |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Volumetric to mass conversion | kg/s | Comparison with volumetric meters |
| 10 | $q_m = \dfrac{k_s \cdot \theta}{\omega \cdot r^2} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Validation against volumetric | kg/s | Cross-check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline Trick:** Memorize formula **#5** above all others. Every KTU numerical on this topic reduces to: *find $k_s$, $\theta$, $\omega$, $r$ → plug in → get $q_m$*.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why Angular Momentum Meter? |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrochemical** | Crude oil pipelines | Density varies with temperature; need true mass flow |
| **Food & Beverage** | Tomato paste, cream | High-viscosity, non-Newtonian fluids |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Sterile liquid batching | Accuracy ±0.5% directly in mass units |
| **Power Plants** | Feedwater to boilers | Mass (not volume) determines energy balance |
| **Aerospace** | Rocket propellant (LOX/RP-1) | Density changes drastically with $T$ and $P$ |
| **Cement & Slurries** | Coal-water slurries | Suspended solids defy volumetric meters |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial alternative:** The **Coriolis mass flowmeter** is the modern descendant of the angular momentum meter and dominates the current market. However, KTU examiners still ask the classical angular momentum derivation because it forms the *theoretical foundation* on which Coriolis mathematics is built.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Newton's Law to $q_m = T/(\omega r^2)$

We start with a single-impeller meter (spring-coupled design) and derive the governing equation in **every single algebraic step**.

### **STEP 1 — State the governing physical law**

By Newton's Second Law for rotational motion, the net external torque on the impeller-fluid system equals the time rate of change of its total angular momentum:

$$T_{ext} = \frac{dL_{total}}{dt}$$

### **STEP 2 — Identify the angular momentum of one fluid element**

Consider an infinitesimal mass element $dm$ of fluid in contact with the impeller blade at radius $r$. After being struck by the spinning blade (no-slip), the element acquires tangential velocity:

$$v_\theta = \omega \cdot r$$

The angular momentum of this element about the impeller axis is:

$$dL = r \cdot v_\theta \cdot dm$$

### **STEP 3 — Substitute the no-slip condition**

Replace $v_\theta$ with $\omega r$:

$$dL = r \cdot (\omega r) \cdot dm = \omega r^2 \, dm$$

### **STEP 4 — Sum over all mass elements passing per unit time**

In a steady-state flow, the mass passing the impeller per unit time is the mass flow rate $q_m$ (in kg/s). Therefore, the total rate of angular momentum transfer is:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \omega r^2 \cdot \frac{dm}{dt} = \omega r^2 \cdot q_m$$

### **STEP 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law**

The motor must supply exactly this torque to maintain constant $\omega$ (action = reaction on the impeller shaft):

$$T = \omega r^2 \cdot q_m$$

### **STEP 6 — Solve for the mass flow rate $q_m$**

Rearrange the equation to isolate $q_m$:

$$T = q_m \cdot \omega \cdot r^2$$
