$$\boxed{q_m = \frac{T}{\omega \cdot r^2}}$$

### **STEP 7 — Introduce the spring constant $k_s$**

In the spring-coupled design, the torque is sensed via the twist angle $\theta$ of a torsion spring of stiffness $k_s$ (N·m/rad):

$$T = k_s \cdot \theta$$

### **STEP 8 — Substitute back into the mass flow equation**

$$q_m = \frac{k_s \cdot \theta}{\omega \cdot r^2}$$

This is the **final transducer equation** — the master result for KTU problems.

### **STEP 9 — Express the output in measurable electrical form**

The twist $\theta$ is sensed by an LVDT producing output voltage $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot \theta$, where $K_{LVDT}$ is the LVDT sensitivity (V/rad). Substituting:

$$q_m = \frac{k_s \cdot V_{out}}{K_{LVDT} \cdot \omega \cdot r^2}$$

The instrument is now fully described end-to-end: **fluid mass flow → mechanical twist → electrical voltage**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: KTU-Style Numerical Walkthrough

> **Sample Problem (KTU-style):** A spring-coupled angular momentum mass flowmeter has an impeller radius $r = 0.05$ m, rotating at $\omega = 100$ rad/s. The torsion spring has stiffness $k_s = 50$ N·m/rad, and the measured twist is $\theta = 0.02$ rad. Calculate (a) the torque $T$, (b) the mass flow rate $q_m$, and (c) the volumetric flow rate if the fluid is water at $20°C$.

### **Part (a) — Torque**

$$T = k_s \cdot \theta = 50 \cdot 0.02$$

$$T = 1.0 \text{ N·m}$$

### **Part (b) — Mass flow rate**

$$q_m = \frac{T}{\omega \cdot r^2} = \frac{1.0}{100 \cdot (0.05)^2}$$

$$q_m = \frac{1.0}{100 \cdot 0.0025} = \frac{1.0}{0.25}$$

$$q_m = 4.0 \text{ kg/s}$$

### **Part (c) — Volumetric flow rate**

For water at $20°C$, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³:

$$Q = \frac{q_m}{\rho} = \frac{4.0}{998}$$

$$Q \approx 4.008 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q \approx 4.008 \text{ L/s}$$

### **Cross-Verification**

Check via the master equation directly:

$$q_m = \frac{k_s \cdot \theta}{\omega \cdot r^2} = \frac{50 \cdot 0.02}{100 \cdot 0.0025} = \frac{1.0}{0.25} = 4.0 \text{ kg/s} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Sensitivity** $S$ is defined as the rate of change of output ($\theta$) with respect to input ($q_m$):

$$S = \frac{d\theta}{dq_m} = \frac{\omega \cdot r^2}{k_s}$$

| Parameter | Effect on Sensitivity | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| Increase $\omega$ | Sensitivity **increases linearly** | Spin faster for better resolution |
| Increase $r$ | Sensitivity **increases quadratically** | Use larger impeller |
| Increase $k_s$ | Sensitivity **decreases** | Use *softer* spring |
| Increase $q_m$ | $\theta$ increases linearly | Output is inherently linear — a major advantage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students are often asked *"What happens to $\theta$ if $r$ is doubled?"* — the answer is $\theta$ becomes **4 times larger** (quadratic dependence on $r$). This is a favorite 2-mark sub-question.

---

## 3.4 Error Sources & Error Analysis Matrix

| Error Source | Effect on $q_m$ Reading | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Bearing friction | Overestimates $T$ → overestimates $q_m$ | Use magnetic / air bearings |
| Fluid swirl at inlet | Pre-existing angular momentum subtracts from imparted $\omega r^2$ | Install **flow conditioner** (straightening vanes) upstream |
| Temperature drift in $k_s$ | Spring constant changes → calibration error | Use Invar or constant-modulus alloys |
| Mechanical wear of impeller | Effective $r$ reduces → $\theta$ mis-scaled | Periodic re-calibration on primary standard |
| Impeller speed fluctuation | $\omega$ varies → apparent $q_m$ changes | Closed-loop speed control with tachogenerator feedback |
| Cavitation / flashing | Two-phase flow alters momentum transfer | Avoid operation near vapor pressure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** If the inlet flow already has significant **swirl** (residual angular momentum from upstream piping), the meter will read *less* than the true mass flow. The swirl subtracts from the angular momentum the impeller *adds*, reducing the net torque. Always specify **5–10 pipe diameters of straight upstream pipe** or install a flow straightener.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Fluid Inlet qm] --> B[Inlet Flow Straightener]
    B --> C[Motor Driven Impeller omega r]
    C --> D[Torsion Shaft with Spring ks]
    D --> E[Angular Deflection theta]
    E --> F[LVDT / Strain Gauge Transducer]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioner and Amplifier]
    G --> H[Display or Controller Output]
    I[Speed Controller with Tachogenerator Feedback] --> C
    C --> I
    J[Calibration Constant k divider omega r squared] --> H
```

## 4.2 Spring-Coupled Impeller Cross-Section (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PipeWall["Pipe Wall"]
        Inlet["Inlet qm entering axially"]
    end

    Inlet --> Impeller["Motor Driven Impeller rotating at omega"]
    Impeller -->|"Angular momentum imparted omega r squared per kg"| SpinningFluid["Spinning fluid column"]
    SpinningFluid --> TorsionShaft["Torsion Spring Shaft"]
    TorsionShaft -->|"Twist angle theta proportional to T"| LVDT["LVDT Sensor"]
    LVDT -->|"Voltage Vout proportional to qm"| Output["Output to Indicator"]
    SpinningFluid --> Outlet["Outlet Fluid"]
```

## 4.3 Twin-Turbine Reaction-Type Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Fluid["Process Fluid Inlet"] --> T1["Upstream Driven Impeller Radius r1"]
    T1 -->|"Imparts angular momentum"| SpinningZone["Spinning Fluid Zone"]
    SpinningZone --> T2["Downstream Reaction Turbine Radius r2"]
    T2 -->|"Reaction torque T opposes motor"| ShaftDeflection["Shaft Angular Deflection between T1 and T2"]
    ShaftDeflection --> Sensor["Torque Sensor Tachogenerator"]
    Sensor --> SignalCond["Signal Conditioner"]
    SignalCond --> Display["Digital Mass Flow Display qm in kg per s"]
```

## 4.4 Complete Transducer Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MassFlow["Physical Quantity qm kg per s"] --> MechanicalTrans["Mechanical Transducer Torque T N m"]
    MechanicalTrans --> ElasticElement["Elastic Element Torsion Spring ks"]
    ElasticElement --> Displacement["Displacement theta rad"]
    Displacement --> ElectricalTrans["Electrical Transducer LVDT"]
    ElectricalTrans --> Voltage["Output Vout volts"]
    Voltage --> Conditioner["Amplifier and Linearizer"]
    Conditioner --> FinalOut["Final Output 4 to 20 mA or Digital"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents one **energy-domain conversion** in the measurement chain. A KTU examiner may ask students to identify *all* domain conversions — this meter performs **4 conversions**: Fluid-flow → Mechanical-torque → Elastic-displacement → Electrical-voltage → Standardized-signal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

### **Q1. Define an angular momentum type mass flowmeter. State its governing equation.**

**Model Answer:**

An **angular momentum type mass flowmeter** is a true mass flow measuring instrument that operates on the principle of **conservation of angular momentum**. A motor-driven impeller imparts a known angular momentum $\omega r^2$ to each kilogram of fluid passing through it. The reactive torque $T$ on the impeller shaft is measured and is **directly proportional to the mass flow rate** $q_m$, independent of fluid density, viscosity, temperature, and pressure.

**Governing equation:**

$$q_m = \frac{T}{\omega \cdot r^2}$$

where $T$ = torque (N·m), $\omega$ = angular velocity of impeller (rad/s), $r$ = impeller radius (m).

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for principle, 1 for equation]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### **Q2. Why is the angular momentum mass flowmeter classified as a "true mass flowmeter"?**

**Model Answer:**

The angular momentum mass flowmeter is classified as a *true mass flowmeter* because its output (torque $T$) is **directly and primarily** proportional to the **mass flow rate** $q_m$, as expressed by $q_m = T / (\omega r^2)$. The equation contains **no fluid density $\rho$**, **no viscosity $\mu$**, and **no temperature or pressure terms**. Unlike volumetric flowmeters (orifice, venturi, turbine) which measure volume and require density correction to obtain mass, the angular momentum meter inherently measures mass — hence the classification. This makes it ideal for fluids with **variable density** (e.g., hydrocarbon mixtures, slurries, cryogenic liquids). **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, 14 Marks]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Derive the governing equation** of an angular momentum type mass flowmeter starting from Newton's Second Law for rotational motion. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] In a spring-coupled angular momentum mass flowmeter, the impeller radius $r = 4$ cm, angular speed $\omega = 80$ rad/s, torsion spring constant $k_s = 40$ N·m/rad, and the measured spring twist is $\theta = 0.015$ rad. Calculate:
  (i) the reactive torque $T$,
  (ii) the mass flow rate $q_m$,
  (iii) the volumetric flow rate if the fluid is oil with density $\rho = 880$ kg/m³.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Assumptions** [1 mark]:
- Steady, fully-developed, axisymmetric flow
- No-slip between impeller blades and fluid: $v_\theta = \omega r$
- Constant impeller speed (motor speed regulated)
- Inlet flow has zero pre-existing angular momentum (swirl-free)
- Impeller radius $r$ is constant and well-defined

**Derivation** [6 marks]:

**Step 1:** Newton's Second Law for a rotating system:

$$T_{ext} = \frac{dL}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Angular momentum of a fluid element of mass $dm$ at radius $r$ moving with tangential velocity $v_\theta$:

$$dL = r \cdot v_\theta \cdot dm$$

**Step 3:** No-slip condition gives $v_\theta = \omega r$:

$$dL = r \cdot (\omega r) \cdot dm = \omega r^2 \, dm$$

**Step 4:** Rate of angular momentum transfer for mass flow rate $q_m = dm/dt$:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \omega r^2 \cdot q_m$$

**Step 5:** Equating to applied torque:

$$T = \omega r^2 \cdot q_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{q_m = \frac{T}{\omega r^2}}$$

**Why this equation is independent of fluid properties:** Note that the derivation never invoked $\rho$, $\mu$, $T_{fluid}$ or $P$ — the meter inherently measures mass. [Last mark]

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $r = 4$ cm $= 0.04$ m
- $\omega = 80$ rad/s
- $k_s = 40$ N·m/rad
- $\theta = 0.015$ rad
- $\rho = 880$ kg/m³ (oil)

**(i) Torque $T$** [2 marks]:

$$T = k_s \cdot \theta = 40 \cdot 0.015 = 0.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Mass flow rate $q_m$** [3 marks]:

$$q_m = \frac{T}{\omega \cdot r^2} = \frac{0.6}{80 \cdot (0.04)^2} = \frac{0.6}{80 \cdot 0.0016} = \frac{0.6}{0.128}$$

$$q_m = 4.6875 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(iii) Volumetric flow rate $Q$** [2 marks]:

$$Q = \frac{q_m}{\rho} = \frac{4.6875}{880}$$

$$Q = 5.327 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 5.33 \text{ L/s}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **construction and working** of a twin-turbine angular momentum mass flowmeter with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for mass flow rate in terms of the two turbine radii.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An angular momentum mass flowmeter has an impeller of radius $r = 5$ cm rotating at $\omega = 120$ rad/s. The fluid (density 1000 kg/m³) is flowing at $Q = 6$ L/s. Find:
  (i) the mass flow rate,
  (ii) the torque required to maintain the impeller speed,
  (iii) the spring twist if $k_s = 60$ N·m/rad.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Twin-Turbine Design [7 Marks]**

**Construction** [3 marks]: A twin-turbine (or reaction-type) angular momentum flowmeter consists of:
1. **Upstream driven turbine (impeller)** of radius $r_1$ coupled to a constant-speed motor.
2. **Downstream reaction turbine** of radius $r_2$ mounted on the same shaft as the impeller (or on a parallel shaft coupled through a torsion spring).
3. **Torque sensor** (tachogenerator / angular deflection transducer) to measure the relative angular displacement.
4. **Flow conditioner** upstream to eliminate pre-existing swirl.

**Working** [2 marks]: The motor-driven impeller spins the fluid at $\omega r_1$. The fluid carries this angular momentum downstream and strikes the reaction turbine, which spins at a rate determined by the angular momentum it absorbs. The **angular lag** between the two turbines is proportional to the **torque transmitted through the fluid**, which in turn is proportional to the mass flow rate.

**Derivation** [2 marks]: The angular momentum gained by fluid at the impeller per unit mass is $\omega r_1^2$; the angular momentum retained at the reaction turbine radius $r_2$ is $\omega r_2^2$. Net angular momentum transferred per unit mass = $\omega(r_2^2 - r_1^2)$ (assuming reaction turbine spins at the fluid speed). Thus:

$$T = q_m \cdot \omega \cdot (r_2^2 - r_1^2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_m = \frac{T}{\omega(r_2^2 - r_1^2)}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $\omega = 120$ rad/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $Q = 6$ L/s $= 0.006$ m³/s, $k_s = 60$ N·m/rad.

**(i) Mass flow rate** [2 marks]:

$$q_m = \rho \cdot Q = 1000 \cdot 0.006 = 6.0 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(ii) Torque** [3 marks]:

$$T = q_m \cdot \omega \cdot r^2 = 6.0 \cdot 120 \cdot (0.05)^2$$

$$T = 6.0 \cdot 120 \cdot 0.0025 = 6.0 \cdot 0.30 = 1.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Spring twist** [2 marks]:

$$\theta = \frac{T}{k_s} = \frac{1.8}{60} = 0.03 \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta = 0.03 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \approx 1.72°$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — most failures occur when students use $r$ in cm instead of m, or $Q$ in L/s instead of m³/s. The numerical answer is **wiped to zero** for unit error. **[−2 to −3 marks]**
> 2. **Confusing the impeller-radius formula with the twin-turbine formula** — $r^2$ vs $(r_2^2 - r_1^2)$. Read the question stem carefully! **[−1 mark]**
> 3. **Skipping the no-slip assumption in the derivation** — examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for stating $v_\theta = \omega r$. **[−1 mark]**
> 4. **Forgetting to convert rad to degrees** when asked for $\theta$ in degrees. **[−1 mark]**
> 5. **In Part B (numerical), forgetting to include the factor $k_s$** when calculating the spring twist — students sometimes divide $T$ by $\omega r^2$ instead of by $k_s$. **[−2 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Angular Momentum Type Mass Flowmeter**

- ☐ **Core equation to memorize:** $q_m = T/(\omega r^2)$ — single-impeller, spring-coupled design.
- ☐ **Twin-turbine variant:** $q_m = T/[\omega(r_2^2 - r_1^2)]$ — note the difference of squared radii.
- ☐ **Spring-coupled complete transducer equation:** $q_m = k_s \cdot \theta / (\omega r^2)$.
- ☐ **Why it is a *true* mass flowmeter:** Governing equation contains no density $\rho$, no viscosity $\mu$, no temperature $T$ — the mass flow emerges as a *primary* measurement.
- ☐ **Three cascading principles:** Newton's 2nd Law (rotational) → Angular momentum of fluid element → Rate of angular momentum transfer.
- ☐ **Key assumption:** No-slip at blade-fluid interface: $v_\theta = \omega r$.
- ☐ **Energy-domain conversions in the signal chain:** Fluid-flow → Torque → Spring-twist → LVDT-voltage → Standardized 4–20 mA (or digital).
- ☐ **Sensitivity** $S = \omega r^2 / k_s$ — increases with $\omega$ and $r$, decreases with $k_s$.
- ☐ **Quadratic dependence on $r$** is a favorite KTU sub-question — doubling $r$ increases $\theta$ fourfold.
- ☐ **Inlet swirl is the #1 installation pitfall** — always specify 5–10 D of straight pipe upstream.
- ☐ **Sensitivity is linear** in $q_m$ — no square-root extraction needed (unlike orifice/differential-pressure meters).
- ☐ **Modern descendant:** Coriolis mass flowmeter — same theoretical root, different mechanism.
- ☐ **Unit conversion discipline:** $r$ in **m**, $Q$ in **m³/s**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $T$ in **N·m**, $q_m$ in **kg/s**.
- ☐ **Industrial applications to remember:** Petrochemical (crude oil), power (boiler feedwater), food (high-viscosity fluids), pharmaceutical (sterile batching), aerospace (cryogenic propellants).
- ☐ **Drawing to practice:** Block diagram of twin-turbine design — frequently asked for **5 marks** in KTU.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Torque over omega-r-squared gives the mass flow — density, viscosity, and temperature need not apply."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impeller Turbine Flow Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impeller Turbine Flow Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impeller Turbine Flow Meter (Definition)**
> An **Impeller Turbine Flow Meter** is a volumetric flow measuring instrument that uses a multi-bladed rotor (impeller) mounted coaxially inside a circular flow conduit. The kinetic energy of the flowing fluid impinges on the angled blades, causing the rotor to spin at an angular velocity that is **linearly proportional** to the average fluid velocity. The rotational speed is transduced into an electrical pulse train (frequency), where the pulse frequency is directly proportional to the volumetric flow rate.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **pinwheel (toy windmill)** held in front of a garden hose. The faster you allow water to flow out of the hose, the faster the pinwheel spins. Now, suppose someone glued a small magnet to one of the pinwheel blades and placed a wire coil (like a pickup) next to it. Every time the magnet passes the coil, a tiny voltage pulse is generated. By simply **counting these pulses per second**, you can figure out exactly how fast the pinwheel is rotating — and therefore, how fast the water is flowing.

> [!NOTE]
> A turbine flow meter is essentially an industrial, precision-engineered version of this exact principle, but designed for harsh process environments like petroleum pipelines, chemical reactors, and aerospace fuel lines.

### 1.3 Working Principle (Plain English)

1. The process fluid enters the meter body through a flow conditioner (honeycomb/straightener).
2. It passes over the angled blades of a freely suspended rotor.
3. The fluid's momentum exerts a tangential force on the blades, generating torque.
4. The rotor reaches a steady-state rotational speed where driving torque equals retarding torque.
5. Each blade passing a pickup generates a **voltage pulse**.
6. Pulses are counted per unit time → **frequency (Hz)**.
7. Frequency is converted to flow rate using the **K-factor (pulses per unit volume)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Turbine Meters:** The meter's linearity is specified in terms of its **K-factor**, expressed in **pulses per gallon (PPG)** or **pulses per cubic meter (pulses/m³)**. A typical industrial K-factor ranges from **100 to 1000 PPG**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Components of an Impeller Turbine Flow Meter

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Meter Body (Housing)** | Houses the rotor; provides flanged/welded process connections |
| 2 | **Flow Straightener** | Eliminates swirl/vortices to ensure laminar axial flow |
| 3 | **Rotor (Impeller)** | Multi-bladed (typically 4–8 blades) ferromagnetic or non-magnetic wheel |
| 4 | **Bearings (Journal/Sleeve)** | Supports the rotor shaft; minimizes friction torque |
| 5 | **Pickup Coil (Magnetic Pickup)** | Detects rotor blade passage and converts it to voltage pulses |
| 6 | **Signal Conditioner** | Amplifies, shapes, and transmits the pulse output |
| 7 | **Preamplifier (Optional)** | Boosts weak signals in long cable runs |

### 2.2 The Underlying Physics (Why It Works)

- **Driving Torque (T_d):** Generated by fluid momentum striking the blades. Depends on fluid density ($\rho$), velocity (V), and blade geometry.
- **Retarding Torque (T_r):** Caused by bearing friction, fluid drag on rotor, and magnetic eddy current drag.
- **Steady State Condition:** When $T_d = T_r$, the rotor reaches a constant angular velocity $\omega$.
- The linear relationship between $\omega$ and V emerges because viscous retarding forces scale linearly with velocity.

### 2.3 Mathematical Formulation

Let:
- $N$ = rotational speed of rotor (revolutions per second, rps)
- $f$ = frequency of output pulse train (Hz)
- $B$ = number of blades on the rotor
- $V$ = average axial fluid velocity (m/s)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of meter bore (m²)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $K$ = K-factor (pulses per unit volume)

**Step 1 — Frequency vs. Rotation:**
$$f = N \times B$$

**Step 2 — Flow Rate vs. Velocity:**
$$Q = V \times A$$

**Step 3 — K-Factor Definition:**
$$K = \frac{f}{Q} \quad \text{(pulses per unit volume)}$$

**Step 4 — Combined Output Equation:**
$$Q = \frac{f}{K}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit (SI) |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| $f$ | $f = N \times B$ | Pulse frequency = RPM × blade count | **Hz (s⁻¹)** |
| $V$ | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{Q^{-1}}$ | Average fluid velocity | **m/s** |
| $Q$ | $Q = V \cdot A$ | Volumetric flow rate | **m³/s** |
| $K$ | $K = \dfrac{f}{Q}$ | Meter constant (K-factor) | **pulses/m³** |
| $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{f}{K}$ | Flow rate from measured frequency | **m³/s** |
| $Re_D$ | $Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (pipe) | dimensionless |
| $\omega$ | $\omega = 2\pi N$ | Angular velocity | **rad/s** |
| $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{N}{V}$ | Slope of linear calibration curve | **pulses/m** |
| $V_{tip}$ | $V_{tip} = \pi D_r N$ | Tip speed of rotor | **m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Turbine flow meters are the workhorse of **custody transfer** in the oil & gas industry (e.g., measuring crude oil in pipelines between platforms and refineries), **aircraft fuel management** (Boeing/Airbus use them in APUs and engines), and **high-purity water** measurement in pharmaceutical plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Establishing the Linear Flow–Frequency Relationship

**Goal:** Prove that the output frequency $f$ is directly proportional to the volumetric flow rate $Q$.

#### Step 1: Force on a Single Blade

When a fluid element of mass $m$ strikes a stationary blade at velocity $V$ and is redirected by the blade geometry, the change in axial momentum produces a force $F$ on the blade.

$$F = \rho \, A_b \, V^2 \cdot \sin^2\theta$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $A_b$ = effective blade area projected normal to flow (m²)
- $V$ = fluid velocity (m/s)
- $\theta$ = blade angle of inclination with respect to the flow axis

> **Logic:** The component of fluid velocity that does useful work on the blade is $V \sin\theta$. The force therefore scales with the square of this component, but the *effective mass flow* striking the blade also scales with $V \sin\theta$, leading to an overall linear response in steady state.

#### Step 2: Torque on the Rotor

With $B$ blades arranged at mean radius $r_m$ from the rotor axis, the total driving torque is:

$$T_d = B \cdot r_m \cdot F = B \cdot r_m \cdot \rho \, A_b \, V^2 \sin^2\theta$$

#### Step 3: Retarding (Drag) Torque

Retarding torque arises from viscous drag on the rotor surfaces and bearing friction. In the linear operating region, this is modeled as:

$$T_r = C_r \cdot \omega$$

where $C_r$ is the mechanical drag coefficient (N·m·s/rad) and $\omega = 2\pi N$ is the rotor's angular velocity.

#### Step 4: Steady-State Equilibration

At steady state, the rotor reaches a constant speed where net torque = 0:

$$T_d = T_r$$

$$B \cdot r_m \cdot \rho \, A_b \, V^2 \sin^2\theta = C_r \cdot \omega$$

#### Step 5: Solving for Angular Velocity

Rearranging for $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{B \cdot r_m \cdot \rho \, A_b \sin^2\theta}{C_r} \cdot V^2$$

> **Wait — this is quadratic!** This is the *unsteady* form. In practice, the rotor reaches a terminal velocity where driving torque balances retarding torque *linearly* in $\omega$ because the fluid's effective driving force depends on the **relative velocity** $(V - V_{tip} \cos\theta)$ between the fluid and the moving blade.

**Refined (Relative Velocity) Model:**

$$F_{eff} = \rho \, A_b \, V_{rel}^2 \sin^2\theta$$

where $V_{rel} = V - \omega r_m \cos\theta$ (relative tangential component).

$$T_d = B \cdot r_m \cdot \rho \, A_b \, (V - \omega r_m \cos\theta)^2 \sin^2\theta$$

At steady state, ignoring higher-order viscous nonlinearities and linearizing (valid for $V_{tip} \ll V$):

$$T_d \approx k_1 \cdot V - k_2 \cdot \omega$$

Equating with $T_r = C_r \cdot \omega$:

$$k_1 \cdot V - k_2 \cdot \omega = C_r \cdot \omega$$

$$\omega = \frac{k_1}{k_2 + C_r} \cdot V$$

#### Step 6: Establishing Linearity

$$\omega = K_1 \cdot V \quad \text{where} \quad K_1 = \frac{k_1}{k_2 + C_r}$$

Since $f = N \cdot B$ and $\omega = 2\pi N$, we have $N = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$. Thus:

$$f = B \cdot N = \frac{B \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{B \cdot K_1}{2\pi} \cdot V$$

#### Step 7: Final Linear Form in Terms of Q

Substituting $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$:

$$f = \frac{B \cdot K_1}{2\pi A} \cdot Q$$

Defining the **K-factor** as the constant of proportionality inverse:

$$K = \frac{B \cdot K_1}{2\pi A}$$

$$\boxed{\,f = K \cdot Q \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad Q = \frac{f}{K}\,}$$

> **Logic:** This derivation proves the central KTU result — that the pulse frequency is *strictly linear* with volumetric flow rate, provided the meter's mechanical drag terms remain in the linear regime (Reynolds number above the laminar threshold, typically **Re > 4000**).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: K-Factor Determination Problem

**Problem:** A turbine flow meter with 6 blades produces a pulse output of **420 Hz** when water flows through it. If the meter is calibrated with a K-factor of **285 pulses per gallon**, calculate the volumetric flow rate in **L/min** and the average fluid velocity if the pipe bore diameter is **50 mm**.

#### Solution Walkthrough

**Given:**
- $f = 420$ Hz
- $K = 285$ pulses/gallon
- $B = 6$ (extra info, not needed for Q calculation)
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m

**Step 1 — Flow Rate in GPM (gallons per minute):**
$$Q_{gpm} = \frac{f}{K} = \frac{420 \text{ pulses/s}}{285 \text{ pulses/gal}} = 1.4737 \text{ gal/s}$$

Converting to gallons per minute:
$$Q_{gpm} = 1.4737 \times 60 = 88.42 \text{ GPM}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Liters per minute** (1 US gallon = 3.785 L):
$$Q = 88.42 \times 3.785 = 334.7 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to m³/s** for velocity calculation:
$$Q = \frac{334.7 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5.578 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-sectional Area of Pipe:**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Average Fluid Velocity:**
$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5.578 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.841 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Verification: Compute Revolutions per Second:**
$$N = \frac{f}{B} = \frac{420}{6} = 70 \text{ rev/s} = 4200 \text{ RPM}$$

> This is reasonable for a 50 mm water service turbine meter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the full signal path and physical architecture of an Impeller Turbine Flow Meter from process input to electronic output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Fluid Inlet] --> B[Flow Straightener]
    B --> C[Meter Body Housing]
    C --> D[Multi Blade Rotor Impeller]
    D --> E[Journal Bearings]
    D --> F{Magnetic Pickup Coil}
    F --> G[Preamplifier Stage]
    G --> H[Signal Shaper Schmitt Trigger]
    H --> I[Frequency to Voltage Converter]
    H --> J[Pulse Counter and Totalizer]
    I --> K[Analog Flow Indicator]
    J --> L[Digital Flow Display and Totalizer]
    K --> M[Control Room DCS PLC]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Flow Control Valve Actuator]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Component-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Inlet Section]
        A1[Flanged Inlet] --> A2[Strainer and Filter] --> A3[Flow Conditioner Honeycomb]
    end
    subgraph B[Measurement Section]
        A3 --> B1[Upstream Straightener Vanes] --> B2[Rotor Hub with 6 Blades] --> B3[Downstream Throat] --> B4[Shaft and Jewel Bearings]
    end
    subgraph C[Detection Section]
        B2 --> C1[Variable Reluctance Pickup Coil] --> C2[Permanent Magnet] --> C3[Preamplifier]
    end
    subgraph D[Signal Processing]
        C3 --> D1[Band Pass Filter] --> D2[Schmitt Trigger Shaper] --> D3[Frequency Divider]
        D3 --> D4[V to F or F to V Converter]
    end
    subgraph E[Output and Display]
        D2 --> E1[Pulses per Second Counter]
        D4 --> E2[Analog 4 to 20 mA Output]
        E1 --> E3[Digital Rate and Total Display]
        E3 --> E4[SCADA DCS Communication]
    end
```

### 4.3 Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoFlow
    NoFlow --> Accelerating: Fluid Enters
    Accelerating --> SteadyState: Driving Torque equals Retarding Torque
    SteadyState --> Accelerating: Flow Rate Increase Detected
    SteadyState --> Decelerating: Flow Rate Decrease Detected
    SteadyState --> NoFlow: Flow Stops
    Decelerating --> NoFlow: Flow Stops
    Accelerating --> Fault: Bearing Seizure or Blockage
    SteadyState --> Fault: Coil Open or Short
    Fault --> Maintenance: Operator Alert
    Maintenance --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The state diagram illustrates that under normal operation, the rotor exists in one of three physical states — accelerating, steady-state, or decelerating — with the **steady-state** being the only valid measurement region. Any deviation (e.g., bearing seizure) triggers a fault state.

### 4.4 Physical Cross-Section Schematic (Textual)

```
          INLET FLOW          ↘
  ═════════════════════════════╗
  ║  [Strainer] [Straightener]║
  ║                            ║
  ║         ╱─Blade─╲           ║
  ║        ╱  ╱│╲  ╲           ║
  ║   ───▶ │  │●│   │ ◀───     ║
  ║        ╲  ╲│╱  ╱           ║
  ║         ╲─────╱            ║
  ║           Shaft            ║
  ║            │               ║
  ║       [Pickup Coil]        ║
  ║             ⓘ              ║
  ║  [Outlet Throat]           ║
  ═════════════════════════════╝
                ↘
          OUTLET FLOW
```

Where:
- `●` = Rotor hub center
- `╱│╲` = Blade inclination at angle θ
- `ⓘ` = Magnetic pickup coil sensing each blade tip

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define K-factor in a turbine flow meter. Why is it the most important calibration parameter?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **K-factor** is defined as the number of output pulses generated by the pickup coil per unit volume of fluid passing through the meter.
>
> **Mathematically:** $K = \dfrac{f}{Q}$, expressed in **pulses per gallon (PPG)** or **pulses per m³**.
>
> It is the most important calibration parameter because it is the **direct proportionality constant** between the measured frequency (electrical output) and the volumetric flow rate (physical quantity). Every flow calculation in service uses $Q = f/K$. The K-factor depends on rotor geometry, blade count, pickup position, and fluid viscosity — so it must be determined empirically by **calibration against a master meter or gravimetric/volumetric standard**.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for engineering justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**
*List any three advantages and two limitations of Impeller Turbine Flow Meters.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **High accuracy** (typically ±0.5% of reading) — among the best for clean fluids.
2. **Excellent repeatability** (±0.05%) — ideal for custody transfer and batching.
3. **Wide flow range** (turndown ratio of 10:1 to 20:1) — versatile across process conditions.
4. **Fast dynamic response** — suitable for pulsating flows and control loops.

**Limitations:**
1. **Moving parts** — bearings wear out, requiring periodic maintenance and recalibration.
2. **Sensitive to fluid cleanliness** — particulates damage blades and bearings; upstream strainers are mandatory.
3. **Causes permanent pressure drop** — energy loss in the line due to flow obstruction.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for advantages, 1 mark for limitations, 1 mark for concluding sentence on application)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of an Impeller Turbine Flow Meter**. Discuss the role of the magnetic pickup coil in detail. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A turbine flow meter has a **K-factor of 380 pulses/gallon**. When mounted in a 4-inch (100 mm) schedule-40 pipe carrying oil of density **870 kg/m³** and kinematic viscosity **4.5 cSt**, the pickup coil outputs a frequency of **525 Hz**. Determine:
  (i) The volumetric flow rate in **m³/hr**
  (ii) The mass flow rate in **kg/hr**
  (iii) The average fluid velocity in **m/s**
  (iv) Comment on whether the flow is in the **laminar or turbulent regime** (Take μ = ρν). *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Construction and Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Constructional Details (3 Marks):**

A turbine flow meter consists of the following major components:

1. **Meter Body (Housing):** A precision-machined cylindrical pipe section with flanged or threaded ends, manufactured to ASME B16.5 standards. The internal bore is held to tight tolerances (±0.05 mm) to maintain laminar flow profiles.

2. **Rotor (Impeller):** A multi-bladed wheel (typically **4 to 8 blades**) made of ferromagnetic stainless steel (SS-420) or, for non-magnetic applications, of engineering plastics like PVDF. The blades are helically inclined at an angle of **30° to 45°** to the flow axis to maximize torque transfer.

3. **Bearings:** The rotor is supported on **jewel bearings** (synthetic ruby/sapphire) or **tungsten carbide journal bearings** to minimize friction torque. Some designs use a **ball-bearing + thrust plate** combination.

4. **Magnetic Pickup Coil:** A permanent magnet (AlNiCo or rare-earth NdFeB) wrapped with thousands of turns of fine copper wire. The magnetic field extends across the rotor tip path.

5. **Flow Straightener (Upstream):** A bundle of thin-walled tubes or vanes that break up swirl and provide a flat axial velocity profile.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

> As the process fluid enters the meter and flows over the angled rotor blades, the **axial momentum of the fluid is converted into rotational mechanical energy**. The rotor accelerates until the driving torque (from fluid momentum) equals the retarding torque (from bearing friction, fluid viscous drag, and magnetic eddy current damping).
>
> At steady state, the rotor spins at a **constant angular velocity $\omega$** that is linearly proportional to the average fluid velocity $V$, and hence to the volumetric flow rate $Q$.

**Role of the Magnetic Pickup Coil (1 Mark):**

> The pickup coil operates on the principle of **variable reluctance**. As each ferromagnetic blade tip approaches the coil, the magnetic reluctance of the magnetic circuit decreases, increasing the flux through the coil. As the blade moves away, the reluctance increases again, decreasing the flux. This **cyclic variation in flux** induces an AC voltage (EMF) in the coil according to Faraday's Law: $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
>
> Each blade passing generates one pulse. For a rotor with $B$ blades rotating at $N$ revolutions per second, the output frequency is $f = N \times B$. This pulse train is the meter's primary output.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- K-factor: $K = 380$ pulses/gallon
- Frequency: $f = 525$ Hz
- Pipe nominal size: 4-inch Schedule 40 → **ID = 102.3 mm = 0.1023 m**
- Oil density: $\rho = 870$ kg/m³
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = 4.5$ cSt $= 4.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**(i) Volumetric Flow Rate in m³/hr (2 Marks):**

$$Q_{gal/s} = \frac{f}{K} = \frac{525}{380} = 1.3816 \text{ gal/s}$$

Converting gallons to cubic meters (1 US gallon = $3.785 \times 10^{-3}$ m³):

$$Q = 1.3816 \times 3.785 \times 10^{-3} = 5.2293 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to m³/hr:

$$Q = 5.2293 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = \boxed{18.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

**(ii) Mass Flow Rate in kg/hr (2 Marks):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 18.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$\dot{m} = \boxed{16,381 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**(iii) Average Fluid Velocity in m/s (2 Marks):**

Cross-sectional area of pipe:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1023)^2}{4} = 8.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Volume flow rate in m³/s:

$$Q = 18.83 / 3600 = 5.230 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Velocity:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5.230 \times 10^{-3}}{8.221 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{0.6362 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(iv) Flow Regime Check (1 Mark):**

Dynamic viscosity:
$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu = 870 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-6} = 3.915 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$

Reynolds number:
$$Re_D = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{870 \times 0.6362 \times 0.1023}{3.915 \times 10^{-3}} = 14,478$$

Since $Re_D \approx 14{,}478 > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **linear relationship** between output frequency and volumetric flow rate for an impeller turbine flow meter, starting from the **driving torque and retarding torque balance**. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the **three common types of pickup mechanisms** used in turbine flow meters (magnetic, RF/inductive, and Hall-effect). Compare their relative merits. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Derivation of Linear Flow-Frequency Relationship (7 Marks)

*(Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step mathematical proof. Key points to write in the exam:)*

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. Steady-state, incompressible, single-phase flow.
2. The flow entering the meter is axially symmetric (no swirl) due to upstream flow straightener.
3. Rotor drag torque is linear in $\omega$ (valid for moderate Reynolds numbers).
4. Blade tip speed is much less than fluid velocity ($V_{tip} \ll V$) — slip regime.
5. Constant fluid properties (density, viscosity) over the measurement period.

**Key Steps Summary (6 Marks):**
- Define driving torque $T_d$ and retarding torque $T_r$.
- Use relative velocity model: $V_{rel} = V - \omega r_m \cos\theta$.
- Linearize and apply $T_d = T_r$ at steady state.
- Derive $\omega = K_1 \cdot V$ where $K_1$ is a meter geometry constant.
- Convert to $f = N \times B = \dfrac{B\omega}{2\pi}$.
- Substitute $V = Q/A$ to obtain $\boxed{f = K \cdot Q}$.

### Part (b) — Pickup Mechanism Comparison (7 Marks)

| Pickup Type | Working Principle | Advantages | Limitations |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Magnetic (Variable Reluctance)** | Blade tip changes magnetic flux; coil EMF induced by Faraday's Law | No external power; rugged; low cost; works with any conductive or non-conductive fluid | Low signal amplitude for non-ferrous blades; signal-to-noise issues |
| **RF / Inductive (Eddy Current)** | RF oscillator detects eddy current losses in blades | Works with non-ferrous blades (aluminum, plastic with metal inserts); higher frequency output | Requires oscillator power; more complex electronics |
| **Hall-Effect (Solid-State)** | Hall sensor detects blade-induced change in magnetic field | DC operation; clean digital pulse; immune to EM noise; works at very low flow rates | Requires DC power supply; temperature-sensitive |

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The magnetic pickup is the **most widely used** in custody-transfer oil & gas applications due to its ruggedness and passive operation. Hall-effect pickups are preferred in **low-flow / pharmaceutical** applications where the meter must operate at very low velocities without signal loss.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** A frequent error is computing flow rate in m³/s but leaving it unconverted when the question asks for L/min or m³/hr. Always re-read the units in the problem statement.
> 2. **Confusing K-factor units:** K-factor is *pulses per unit volume* — students often invert it as *volume per pulse* and then divide instead of multiply. Remember: $Q = f / K$ (pulses per second divided by pulses per gallon = gallons per second).
> 3. **Skipping the flow-regime check:** Whenever a viscosity is given, the examiner expects a **Reynolds number calculation** to classify the flow. Missing this loses 1 mark instantly.
> 4. **Omitting Reynolds number verification for linearity:** The linear relationship $f = K \cdot Q$ is valid **only in the turbulent regime** (Re > 4000). Always state this assumption explicitly in derivations.
> 5. **Drawing the pickup coil incorrectly:** The coil must be placed **outside** the flow path, mounted on the meter body's external surface, with its magnetic field bridging the rotor tip path. Drawing it inside the pipe is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Turbine flow meter = **volumetric** flow device (output is m³/hr, not kg/hr).
- ✅ Core output equation: $\boxed{Q = \dfrac{f}{K}}$
- ✅ Frequency equation: $\boxed{f = N \times B}$ (RPM × number of blades).
- ✅ **K-factor** is the meter constant in **pulses per unit volume** (e.g., PPG or pulses/m³).
- ✅ Linearity holds in the **turbulent regime** ($Re_D > 4000$).
- ✅ The driving principle is **variable reluctance** in the magnetic pickup coil.
- ✅ Faraday's Law applies: $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ for each blade passage.
- ✅ Typical accuracy: **±0.5% of reading**; repeatability: **±0.05%**.
- ✅ Turndown ratio: **10:1 to 20:1** (linear operating range).
- ✅ **Mandatory upstream straight pipe length:** typically **10D** upstream and **5D** downstream of the meter.
- ✅ **Upstream strainer** is compulsory to prevent blade damage from particulates.
- ✅ Bearings: jewel/sapphire for low flow; tungsten carbide for high-pressure service.
- ✅ Applications: oil & gas custody transfer, aircraft fuel, water treatment, pharmaceutical.
- ✅ Major advantage: high accuracy for **clean, single-phase fluids**.
- ✅ Major limitation: **moving parts → wear and tear**; not suitable for slurries or dirty fluids.
- ✅ Always convert units carefully: **1 US gallon = 3.785 L = 3.785 × 10⁻³ m³**.
- ✅ For mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \times Q$ (multiply volumetric flow by density).
- ✅ Schematic must always show: **inlet → straightener → rotor → pickup → preamp → display**.
- ✅ The relationship between rotor angular velocity and fluid velocity is **linear** due to viscous drag balancing (this is a KTU frequently-tested conceptual point).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 📘 Module 3: Flow Measurement & Viscosity

## 1.1 Constant Torque Type Flow Meter

### 🎯 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)
A **Constant Torque Type Flow Meter** (also known as a *Variable Speed Type* or *True Mass Flowmeter* in certain configurations) is a flow measurement device in which a constant, predetermined retarding torque is applied to a freely rotating impeller/vane placed in the flow stream. The rotational speed of the impeller becomes directly proportional to the flow rate, and because the impeller mass is constant, the mass flow rate can be directly inferred.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the Constant Torque type, the impeller is loaded with a **constant retarding torque** (mechanical brake/magnetic eddy current brake), and the resulting rotational speed is proportional to the **mass flow rate**, NOT the volumetric flow rate.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **pinwheel** placed in a stream of water. If you apply a brake pad that grips the pinwheel with a constant friction (constant torque), the pinwheel will spin at a steady speed that depends on how hard the water pushes it. Faster water → faster spin. The constant friction ensures that the spin speed is a direct measure of the water's momentum (mass flow), independent of water temperature or density. If the water were thick (like oil), the same volume would still produce less spin — but with a constant torque brake, the meter naturally corrects for this, giving you a **mass flow** reading.

### 🔑 Key Characteristics
- **Input:** Fluid flow in a conduit
- **Output:** Rotational speed (RPM) of the impeller
- **Working principle:** Momentum exchange between fluid and rotor
- **Output signal type:** Mass flow rate
- **Calibration dependency:** Requires calibration for fluid density variations (since momentum depends on mass)

---

## 1.2 Electromagnetic Flow Meter (EMF / Magmeter)

### 🎯 Core Technical Definition
An **Electromagnetic Flow Meter** is a volumetric flow measuring device based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a conductive fluid flows through a magnetic field, a voltage (EMF) is induced across the fluid, perpendicular to both the flow direction and the magnetic field. The magnitude of this induced EMF is directly proportional to the average velocity of the fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Governing Equation (Faraday's Law):**
> $$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$
> Where: E = induced EMF (V), B = magnetic flux density (T), L = conductor length = pipe inner diameter D (m), v = average fluid velocity (m/s).

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine sliding a metal rod through the poles of a strong magnet. As the rod moves, a voltage appears across its ends — your voltimeter will read a value. Now replace the solid rod with a **pipe full of seawater** (which is also conductive) flowing through the magnet's field. The flowing seawater acts like a moving conductor, and a voltage appears across the diameter of the pipe, between two electrodes placed perpendicular to the magnetic field. The faster the water flows, the larger the voltage.

### 🔑 Why It Is Special
- **No moving parts** → no pressure drop, no wear
- **Obstructionless** → ideal for slurries, sewage, pulp, and corrosive chemicals
- **Independent of fluid density, viscosity, and turbulence**
- **Limitation:** Only works for **electrically conductive fluids** (> 5 μS/cm)

---

## 1.3 Ultrasonic Flow Meter

### 🎯 Core Technical Definition
An **Ultrasonic Flow Meter (USM)** is a non-intrusive flow measuring device that determines fluid velocity by measuring the difference in **propagation time (transit time)** or **frequency shift (Doppler effect)** of high-frequency sound waves (typically 0.5 – 5 MHz) traveling with and against the flow direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Operating Principles (KTU Must-Know):**
> 1. **Transit-Time Method** (Time-of-Flight): Measures difference in upstream/downstream travel time. Best for clean liquids.
> 2. **Doppler Method** (Frequency Shift): Measures frequency shift of sound reflected by particles/bubbles. Best for dirty liquids or slurries.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
Stand at the side of a river and shout across it. If a boat is moving downstream, the sound takes a *little longer* to reach a person downstream and *less time* to reach one upstream — because the sound "rides" the current a bit. Ultrasonic flow meters exploit this tiny time difference. In the **Doppler version**, imagine a submarine sonar ping hitting a fish — the returning echo is shifted in pitch because the fish is moving (just like a police siren changes pitch as it passes you).

---

## 1.4 Measurement of Viscosity — Principle

### 🎯 Core Technical Definition
**Viscosity** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow — a measure of internal friction between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. The **principle of viscosity measurement** relies on quantifying the **shear stress** required to produce a unit **rate of shear strain** in the fluid.

The fundamental governing relationship is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions:**
> - $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
> - $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
> - $du/dy$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹) — also called **shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}$

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
Pour honey and water side by side. Honey flows slowly because its internal molecular "stickiness" resists sliding. This stickiness is viscosity. Now imagine a deck of cards — if you push the top card, the next one moves a tiny bit, the next one less, and so on. The "friction" between layers in such sliding is exactly what viscosity measures. **Thicker fluid = more friction between layers = higher viscosity.**

### 🔑 Types of Viscosity (KTU 2024 must know)
| Type | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic / Absolute viscosity | $\mu$ | Pa·s | Resistance to shear stress |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | m²/s | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ (ratio of dynamic viscosity to density) |
| Saybolt Universal viscosity | SSU | seconds | Empirical — time for 60 cm³ to flow through a calibrated orifice |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constant Torque Type Flow Meter — Theory

### 🛠️ Operational Sequence
1. Fluid enters the meter body and strikes the **impeller blades**.
2. The fluid exerts a **driving torque** ($T_d$) on the impeller proportional to the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$:
$$T_d = K_1 \cdot \dot{m} = K_1 \cdot \rho \cdot Q \cdot v$$
3. A **constant retarding torque** ($T_r$) is applied via:
   - Eddy current brake (magnetic damping)
   - Mechanical friction brake
   - Viscous coupling
4. At **steady state (dynamic equilibrium)**: $T_d = T_r$ (constant)
5. The impeller therefore rotates at an angular velocity $\omega$ where:
$$\omega = \frac{T_d - T_{friction}}{I} \cdot t \quad \text{→ at equilibrium: } \omega = K \cdot \dot{m}$$

### ⚙️ Why "Constant Torque" → Mass Flow?
Because the retarding torque is independent of speed (or held constant by design), the angular acceleration of the impeller is purely a function of **mass-driven torque**. Therefore, the **equilibrium speed** (and hence the pulse output frequency) becomes a direct measure of **mass flow rate $\dot{m}$** (kg/s), independent of fluid density.

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Applications
- Aviation fuel flow measurement
- Cryogenic liquid flow (LNG, LOX)
- High-pressure process lines where volumetric flow is misleading due to density changes
- Chemical injection skids in oil & gas

---

## 2.2 Electromagnetic Flow Meter — Deep Theory

### ⚡ Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction
When a conductor of length $L$ moves with velocity $v$ through a magnetic field of flux density $B$, an EMF is induced:
$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

In a cylindrical pipe of diameter $D$ with the entire fluid acting as the moving conductor:
$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v_{avg}$$

Since volumetric flow $Q = v_{avg} \cdot A = v_{avg} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$, we get:
$$E = \frac{4B \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Observation:** The output EMF $E$ is **independent of fluid density, viscosity, temperature, and turbulence profile** — provided the flow profile is axisymmetric. This is why magmeters are highly accurate (±0.5% of rate) in process industries.

### 🛠️ Key Components
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Magnetic coils (AC or DC excited) | Generate transverse magnetic field B |
| Pair of electrodes | Sense the induced EMF (perpendicular to both B and v) |
| Liner (PTFE, rubber, ceramic) | Insulates electrodes, isolates process fluid |
| Coils driver / pulse excitation circuit | Provides switched DC excitation to suppress polarization |
| Signal converter | Amplifies and conditions the millivolt-level signal |

### 🔌 Excitation Modes
| Mode | Description | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| DC excitation | Steady magnetic field | Simple, but suffers from electrode polarization |
| AC excitation (50/60 Hz) | Sinusoidal field | Better SNR, but prone to quadrature noise |
| **Pulsed DC (bipolar)** | Modern standard | Eliminates polarization, suppresses zero drift |

### 🏭 Real-World Applications
- Water and wastewater treatment plants
- Chemical industry (acids, alkalis, slurries)
- Food & beverage (milk, beer, fruit juices)
- Mining and dredging (ore slurries, dredged mud)
- Pulp and paper stock lines

---

## 2.3 Ultrasonic Flow Meter — Deep Theory

### 🌊 Transit-Time Principle (Time-of-Flight)
Two transducers (T₁, T₂) are mounted on the pipe, one upstream and one downstream, at an angle $\theta$ to the flow axis.

**Path length** along the flow direction:
$$L = \frac{D}{\sin\theta}$$

**Downstream transit time** (sound travels *with* the flow):
$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + v\cos\theta}$$

**Upstream transit time** (sound travels *against* the flow):
$$t_2 = \frac{L}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Time difference** (the key measurement):
$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{2 L \cdot v\cos\theta}{c^2 - v^2\cos^2\theta}$$

Since $c \gg v$, $v^2\cos^2\theta$ is negligible:
$$\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L \cdot v\cos\theta}{c^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Solving for $v$:
> $$v = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}$$
> Since $c$ (speed of sound in fluid) depends on fluid properties, **clamp-on ultrasonic meters require temperature compensation**.

### 📡 Doppler Principle (Frequency Shift)
A single transducer emits ultrasound of frequency $f_0$ at angle $\theta$. Reflectors (particles, bubbles, entrained solids) in the fluid scatter the sound back. The frequency of the reflected signal $f_r$ is shifted by the Doppler effect:
$$f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

For $c \gg v$:
$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 \approx \frac{2 f_0 \cdot v \cos\theta}{c}$$

Solving for velocity:
$$v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Doppler method requires scattering particles** in the fluid. It will fail in ultra-clean fluids. Use Transit-Time for clean liquids, Doppler for dirty liquids/slurries.

### 🏭 Real-World Applications
- District heating and cooling networks (water)
- Natural gas custody transfer (multi-path ultrasonic)
- Crude oil pipelines
- Clean-in-place (CIP) flow monitoring
- Custody transfer in oil & gas

---

## 2.4 Viscosity Measurement — Deep Theory

### 📐 Newton's Law of Viscosity
For a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress is linearly proportional to the velocity gradient:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress [Pa or N/m²]
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s]
- $du/dy$ = shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ [s⁻¹]

### 📊 Newton’s Classification of Fluids
| Type | Behavior | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | Linear: $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Water, air, oil, alcohol |
| Non-Newtonian (Pseudoplastic) | Viscosity decreases with shear rate | Paint, ketchup, blood |
| Non-Newtonian (Dilatant) | Viscosity increases with shear rate | Corn starch slurry, wet sand |
| Bingham Plastic | Requires yield stress, then flows linearly | Toothpaste, drilling mud |

### 🔑 Kinematic vs. Dynamic Viscosity
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Where $\rho$ = fluid density [kg/m³]. Units: $\nu$ in **m²/s** or **Stokes (St)**; 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s = 1 cm²/s; 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.

### 🛠️ Viscosity Measurement Methods (Principle-Based)

| Principle | Type of Viscosity Measured | Device Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Capillary flow** (Poiseuille's law) | Kinematic | Ostwald, Ubbelohde viscometer |
| **Falling ball / falling piston** | Dynamic | Höppler viscometer |
| **Rotational (concentric cylinder / cone-plate)** | Dynamic | Brookfield viscometer |
| **Orifice / efflux cup** | Empirical (Saybolt, Redwood) | Saybolt viscometer |
| **Vibrating element** | Dynamic, in-line | Ultrasonic viscometer |
| **Capillary rheometer** | Shear-dependent | Industrial process viscometer |

### 📐 Poiseuille's Law (Capillary Viscometer Principle)
For laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a tube of radius $R$ and length $L$, the volumetric flow rate is:
$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

Solving for viscosity:
$$\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 Q L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Viscosity measurement in a capillary relies on **Hagen-Poiseuille flow** (laminar, steady, incompressible, Newtonian). The pressure drop $\Delta P$ is measured across a known length $L$, and $Q$ is controlled or measured.

### 🏭 Engineering Applications
- Lubricating oil quality monitoring (engines, turbines)
- Polymer melt viscosity in extrusion (plastics manufacturing)
- Food industry (sauces, syrups, dairy)
- Pharmaceutical formulation
- Petroleum refining (crude oil, bitumen)
- Paint and coating manufacturing

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: EMF in an Electromagnetic Flow Meter

We start from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, which states that the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the rate of cutting of magnetic flux lines:

$$E = \oint (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 1: Set up the geometry.**
Consider a circular pipe of inner diameter $D$ carrying a conductive fluid with average velocity $v_{avg}$ along the pipe axis (x-direction). A uniform magnetic field $B$ is applied perpendicular to the flow (y-direction). The induced EMF is sensed between two electrodes placed on opposite sides of the pipe, perpendicular to both $B$ and $v$ (z-direction).

**Step 2: Define the velocity vector and magnetic flux density.**
The fluid velocity along the pipe:
$$\vec{v} = v_{avg} \cdot \hat{i}$$

The magnetic flux density perpendicular to flow:
$$\vec{B} = B \cdot \hat{j}$$

**Step 3: Compute the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.**
The cross product of two perpendicular unit vectors is perpendicular to both:
$$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = v_{avg} \cdot B \cdot (\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) = v_{avg} \cdot B \cdot \hat{k}$$

This induced force per unit charge points along $\hat{k}$ — the direction between the two electrodes. ✅

**Step 4: Integrate along the electrode path.**
The path between the two electrodes runs across the pipe diameter $D$:
$$E = \int_0^D v_{avg} \cdot B \cdot dl = v_{avg} \cdot B \cdot D$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E = B \cdot D \cdot v_{avg}}$$

**Step 5: Relate EMF to volumetric flow rate $Q$.**
For a circular pipe of cross-sectional area $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$:
$$Q = v_{avg} \cdot A = v_{avg} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Solving for $v_{avg}$:
$$v_{avg} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

Substituting back:
$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 B Q}{\pi D}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4 B}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The volumetric flow rate is **directly proportional** to the induced EMF and **inversely proportional** to the magnetic flux density. Doubling $B$ halves the required $E$ for the same $Q$ — which is why modern magmeters use strong permanent magnets or high-current coils.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Transit-Time (Time-of-Flight) Ultrasonic Flow Meter

**Step 1: Define the geometric setup.**
Two transducers T₁ (upstream) and T₂ (downstream) are clamped on a pipe of inner diameter $D$ at an angle $\theta$ to the flow axis. The acoustic path length along the pipe is:
$$L = \frac{D}{\sin\theta}$$

**Step 2: Sound velocity along the path.**
The speed of sound relative to the fluid is $c$. The fluid itself moves at axial velocity $v$. Decomposing the fluid velocity along the acoustic path:
$$v_{path} = v \cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Downstream transit time $t_1$.**
Sound travels from upstream transducer T₁ to downstream transducer T₂ — *with* the flow:
$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + v\cos\theta}$$

This is the time when sound "rides" the current — it's **shorter** than the static case.

**Step 4: Upstream transit time $t_2$.**
Sound travels from T₂ to T₁ — *against* the flow:
$$t_2 = \frac{L}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

This time is **longer** than the static case.

**Step 5: Compute the time difference $\Delta t$.**
$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{L}{c - v\cos\theta} - \frac{L}{c + v\cos\theta}$$

**Step 6: Combine the fractions (common denominator).**
$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{(c + v\cos\theta) - (c - v\cos\theta)}{(c - v\cos\theta)(c + v\cos\theta)}$$

$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c^2 - v^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

**Step 7: Apply the practical approximation.**
For liquids and gases in industrial flow, $v \ll c$ (typically $v < 10$ m/s vs $c \approx 1480$ m/s in water). Hence $v^2 \cos^2\theta \ll c^2$, and:
$$\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L \cdot v \cos\theta}{c^2}$$

**Step 8: Solve for velocity $v$.**
Substitute $L = D / \sin\theta$:
$$\Delta t = \frac{2 (D / \sin\theta) \cdot v \cos\theta}{c^2} = \frac{2 D \cdot v \cot\theta}{c^2}$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{v = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 D \cot\theta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Substituting $L$ in original form:** $v = \dfrac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Doppler Shift Ultrasonic Flow Meter

**Step 1: Source emits ultrasound of frequency $f_0$.**
A transducer emits sound at frequency $f_0$ in a fluid of sound speed $c$. The fluid contains particles moving at velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the sound beam.

**Step 2: Particle perceives a shifted frequency (moving observer).**
The moving particle "sees" a frequency $f_1$ from the stationary source:
$$f_1 = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c}$$

**Step 3: Particle re-emits $f_1$ (moving source).**
The particle re-scatters sound at $f_1$. The stationary transducer now "sees" this scattered sound from a moving source. The transducer perceives:
$$f_r = f_1 \cdot \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Step 4: Combine the two Doppler shifts.**
$$f_r = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \cdot \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Step 5: Compute the frequency difference $\Delta f$.**
$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} - 1 \right) = f_0 \cdot \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Step 6: Apply approximation $c \gg v\cos\theta$.**
$$\Delta f \approx \frac{2 f_0 \cdot v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Step 7: Solve for velocity.**
$$\boxed{v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention:** $\Delta f$ is positive when particles move **toward** the transducer. The velocity magnitude is obtained from the absolute value of $\Delta f$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Viscosity from Capillary Flow (Hagen-Poiseuille Law)

**Step 1: Start with a cylindrical pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$.**
A Newtonian fluid flows under a steady pressure difference $\Delta P$. The flow is **laminar** (Re < 2300) and **steady** (steady-state Navier-Stokes).

**Step 2: Write the Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates.**
For fully-developed, axisymmetric, steady flow in a pipe, the z-component of Navier-Stokes reduces to:
$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}$$

**Step 3: Apply constant pressure gradient.**
The pressure gradient $dP/dz$ is constant = $-\Delta P / L$ (negative because pressure drops along the flow direction).
$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

**Step 4: Integrate once with respect to $r$.**
Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate:
$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{2\mu L} + C_1$$

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L} + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 5: Apply boundary condition at $r = 0$.**
Velocity gradient must be finite at the centerline (no singularity):
$$\lim_{r \to 0} \frac{dv_z}{dr} \neq \infty \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$$

So:
$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}$$

**Step 6: Integrate again with respect to $r$.**
$$v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4\mu L} + C_2$$

**Step 7: Apply no-slip boundary condition at $r = R$.**
At the wall, the fluid velocity is zero (no-slip):
$$v_z(R) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 8: Obtain the parabolic velocity profile (Hagen-Poiseuille).**
$$\boxed{v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)}$$

This is the famous **parabolic velocity profile** for laminar pipe flow. Maximum velocity occurs at the centerline ($r = 0$):
$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 9: Compute the volumetric flow rate $Q$ by integration.**
$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} \right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

**Step 10: Solve for dynamic viscosity $\mu$.**
$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 Q L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Average velocity:** $v_{avg} = Q/A = Q/(\pi R^2) = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8 \mu L} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{2}$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

### Example Problem
An electromagnetic flow meter has a pipe inner diameter of **50 mm** and operates with a magnetic flux density of **0.1 T**. If the induced EMF is measured as **2.5 mV**, calculate:
(a) The average fluid velocity
(b) The volumetric flow rate

### Solution
**Given:** $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $B = 0.1$ T, $E = 2.5$ mV $= 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ V

**(a) Average velocity:**

Using $E = B \cdot D \cdot v_{avg}$:
$$v_{avg} = \frac{E}{B \cdot D} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 0.05} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.5 \, \text{m/s}$$

**(b) Volumetric flow rate:**

Pipe cross-sectional area:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

Volumetric flow:
$$Q = v_{avg} \cdot A = 0.5 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 9.817 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to L/s:
$$Q = 0.9817 \, \text{L/s} \approx 0.982 \, \text{L/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always include units in your final answer. Most valuation errors come from missing unit conversions (mm → m, mV → V).

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Explain the principle of operation of an Electromagnetic Flow Meter. Why is it suitable for measuring flow of slurries and corrosive chemicals?**

**Model Answer (Expected 3-mark structure):**

A. **Principle:** The electromagnetic flow meter operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a conductive fluid flows through a pipe placed in a transverse magnetic field, an EMF is induced across the fluid proportional to the average flow velocity.

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v_{avg}$$

B. **Components:** Magnetic coils generate the field; two electrodes (placed perpendicular to the field) sense the induced EMF.

C. **Suitability for slurries and corrosive chemicals:** (i) **No moving parts** — no obstruction to flow, no wear, no pressure drop, hence ideal for slurries with suspended solids. (ii) The inner **liner** (PTFE, rubber, ceramic) isolates the process fluid from metal parts — perfect for corrosive acids, alkalis, and chemicals. (iii) The output is independent of viscosity, density, and turbulence. (iv) **Limitation:** Fluid must be electrically conductive (> 5 μS/cm).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between Transit-Time and Doppler type ultrasonic flow meters.**

**Model Answer (Tabular 3-mark structure):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Transit-Time (Time-of-Flight) | Doppler (Frequency-Shift) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Working principle | Measures difference in upstream/downstream travel time $\Delta t$ | Measures frequency shift $\Delta f$ of reflected sound from particles |
| 2 | Equation | $v = c^2 \Delta t / (2L\cos\theta)$ | $v = c \Delta f / (2f_0 \cos\theta)$ |
| 3 | Fluid requirement | Clean, homogeneous liquid (no particles) | Must contain reflectors (bubbles, particles) |
| 4 | Accuracy | Higher (±0.5%) | Lower (±2 to 5%) |
| 5 | Application | Pure water, oils, chemicals | Slurries, sewage, dirty fluids |
| 6 | Sensitivity to flow direction | Bidirectional | Can be unidirectional |
