## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In **EMF derivation questions**, students often forget to convert **mm → m** and lose 1 mark.
> - In **Doppler shift** derivations, omitting the approximation $c \gg v$ loses 1 mark.
> - In **viscosity questions**, students confuse **dynamic (μ, Pa·s)** with **kinematic (ν, m²/s)** viscosity. KTU explicitly tests this distinction.
> - Always state the **assumption** (laminar flow, Newtonian fluid, no-slip wall) in viscosity derivations.
> - In **constant torque meter** answers, students forget to justify *why* the device measures **mass flow** rather than volumetric flow — losing 2 marks.

---

### ❓ Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A [a] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *(7 Marks)*

> **Derive the expression for the EMF generated in an Electromagnetic Flow Meter. State clearly the assumptions made. How is the volumetric flow rate related to the induced EMF?**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Statement of Faraday's Law.**
The induced EMF in a conductor of length $L$ moving with velocity $v$ through a magnetic field $B$ is:
$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Geometry.**
Consider a pipe of inner diameter $D$ carrying conductive fluid with average velocity $v_{avg}$. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is applied perpendicular to the flow direction. Two electrodes are placed diametrically opposite, perpendicular to both flow and field.

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law to the fluid column.**
The fluid column between the electrodes acts as the moving conductor of length $D$:
$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v_{avg}$$

**Step 4 — Relate to volumetric flow rate $Q$.**
Cross-sectional area of the pipe: $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. Therefore:
$$Q = v_{avg} \cdot A = v_{avg} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Solving for $v_{avg}$:
$$v_{avg} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

Substituting:
$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4BQ}{\pi D}$$

Solving for $Q$:
$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The flow velocity profile is **axisymmetric and uniform** (plug flow).
2. The magnetic field $B$ is **uniform** across the pipe cross-section.
3. The fluid is **electrically conductive** with conductivity > 5 μS/cm.
4. **No end effects** — pipe is sufficiently long.
5. The pipe wall is **non-conductive** (liner prevents short-circuiting of EMF).
6. The fluid is **incompressible**.

**Conclusion:** The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is **directly proportional** to the induced EMF $E$ and **inversely proportional** to the magnetic flux density $B$. Hence, measuring $E$ directly gives $Q$ (after calibration).

---

**Q.A [b] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *(7 Marks)*

> **With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Transit-Time Ultrasonic Flow Meter. Derive the expression for fluid velocity in terms of time difference.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (Functional).**
Refer to the **Transit-Time Ultrasonic Flow Meter block diagram** drawn in Section 4.3 above.

The key components are:
- Two ultrasonic transducers T₁ (upstream) and T₂ (downstream) mounted on the pipe at angle $\theta$ to the flow.
- Time-measurement circuit (high-resolution timer).
- Microcontroller for computation.
- Display and 4-20 mA output.

**Step 2 — Working Principle.**
Each transducer acts alternately as transmitter and receiver. T₁ transmits an ultrasonic pulse that travels through the pipe wall, the fluid, and reaches T₂. The time taken is $t_1$ (downstream — sound aided by flow). T₂ then transmits, and T₁ receives — taking time $t_2$ (upstream — sound opposed by flow). The difference $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$ is a tiny but measurable value proportional to flow velocity.

**Step 3 — Derivation.**
The acoustic path length along the flow direction:
$$L = \frac{D}{\sin\theta}$$

The fluid velocity component along the acoustic path: $v \cos\theta$.

**Downstream transit time:**
$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + v\cos\theta}$$

**Upstream transit time:**
$$t_2 = \frac{L}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Time difference:**
$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{2 L \cdot v \cos\theta}{c^2 - v^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

Since $c \gg v$, $v^2 \cos^2\theta \ll c^2$:
$$\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L \cdot v \cos\theta}{c^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for velocity.**
$$\boxed{v = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta} = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin\theta}{2 D \cos\theta} = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \tan\theta}{2 D}}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\Delta t$ is in the order of nanoseconds, the time measurement circuit must have **picosecond resolution**. Also, the speed of sound $c$ is temperature-dependent, so **temperature compensation** is essential for accurate readings.

---

### ❓ Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B [a] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Alternative]** *(7 Marks)*

> **Explain with a block diagram the working of a Constant Torque Type Flow Meter. Show that the rotational speed is proportional to the mass flow rate.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (Functional).**
Refer to the **Constant Torque Flow Meter block diagram** in Section 4.1 above.

**Step 2 — Working.**
The fluid enters the meter and is guided by flow straighteners onto the **multi-blade impeller**. The fluid imparts a driving torque on the impeller. A **constant retarding torque** is applied to the impeller via a magnetic eddy current brake (or mechanical friction brake). At steady state, the impeller reaches an equilibrium rotational speed $\omega$.

**Step 3 — Torque Balance.**
Driving torque (from fluid momentum):
$$T_d = K_1 \cdot \dot{m} = K_1 \cdot \rho \cdot Q \cdot v$$

Retarding torque (constant by design):
$$T_r = \text{constant} = C$$

**At dynamic equilibrium:**
$$T_d = T_r \Rightarrow K_1 \cdot \dot{m} = C$$

Hence:
$$\dot{m} = \frac{C}{K_1} = \text{constant} \cdot \omega \quad \text{(since } \omega \propto \dot{m} \text{ via impulse balance)}$$

**Step 4 — Proportionality Proof.**
The impeller experiences angular acceleration:
$$I \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_d - T_r$$

At steady state $d\omega/dt = 0$, so $T_d = T_r$. Since $T_r$ is **fixed** and $T_d \propto \dot{m}$, the equilibrium **rotational speed $\omega$ is directly proportional to the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$**:
$$\boxed{\omega = K \cdot \dot{m}}$$

**Step 5 — Pulse Output.**
A pickup coil senses a tooth on the impeller rotating past it, generating N pulses per revolution. The pulse frequency $f = N \omega / 2\pi$ is therefore proportional to $\dot{m}$:
$$f = K' \cdot \dot{m}$$

This frequency is converted to an analog 4-20 mA or digital (HART) signal representing mass flow rate.

**Conclusion:** The constant-torque design inherently compensates for fluid density variations, making it a true **mass flow meter** — a significant advantage in applications like cryogenic liquid and aviation fuel measurement.

---

**Q.B [b] [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Alternative]** *(7 Marks)*

> **Define viscosity. Derive an expression for the viscosity of a Newtonian fluid from the Hagen-Poiseuille equation. A capillary viscometer has an internal radius of 1.5 mm and length 200 mm. The pressure drop across the capillary is 500 Pa when the volumetric flow rate is 1.2 × 10⁻⁷ m³/s. Calculate the dynamic viscosity of the fluid.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Definition of Viscosity.**
Viscosity is the property of a fluid that offers **resistance to deformation under shear stress**. For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress $\tau$ is linearly proportional to the velocity gradient (shear rate) $du/dy$:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** (Pa·s).

**Step 2 — Hagen-Poiseuille Derivation.**
For laminar, steady, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$ under pressure drop $\Delta P$, the volumetric flow rate is given by:
$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

(For derivation steps, refer to Section 3.4 of these notes — steps 1 through 9 with no-slip boundary condition and parabolic velocity profile.)

**Solving for $\mu$:**
$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 Q L}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Solution.**

**Given:**
- $R = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- $\Delta P = 500$ Pa
- $Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s

**Step 4 — Compute $R^4$.**
$$R^4 = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^4 = (1.5)^4 \times 10^{-12} = 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{m}^4$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the equation.**
$$\mu = \frac{\pi \cdot 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 500}{8 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.2}$$

**Numerator:**
$$\pi \cdot 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 500 = \pi \cdot 2.53125 \times 10^{-9} = 7.952 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Denominator:**
$$8 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.2 = 1.92 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 6 — Compute final result.**
$$\mu = \frac{7.952 \times 10^{-9}}{1.92 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.0414 \, \text{Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 4.14 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{Pa·s} = 41.4 \, \text{cP}}$$

**Note:** Water has $\mu \approx 1$ cP at 20°C. The fluid in this problem (~41.4 cP) is approximately **40 times more viscous than water** — characteristic of a light oil or viscous syrup.

**Step 7 — Verify laminar flow (Reynolds number check).**
Assuming fluid density $\rho \approx 1000$ kg/m³ (light oil) and pipe diameter $D = 2R = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m:
$$v_{avg} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2} = 0.01696 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \cdot 0.01696 \cdot 0.003}{0.0414} \approx 1.23$$

Since $Re \ll 2300$, the laminar assumption is justified. ✅

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Section 3.4**

- **Constant Torque Type Flow Meter:**
  - 🔑 Constant retarding torque ⇒ $\omega \propto \dot{m}$ (mass flow)
  - Uses eddy current brake or viscous coupling
  - Independent of fluid density → TRUE mass flow meter
  - Limitation: Has moving parts → wear, limited life

- **Electromagnetic Flow Meter (Magmeter):**
  - 🔑 Operates on **Faraday's Law**: $E = B \cdot D \cdot v_{avg}$
  - $Q = \pi D E / (4B)$ — linear relationship
  - Output EMF is **independent of viscosity, density, and turbulence**
  - Requires **electrically conductive fluid** (> 5 μS/cm)
  - **No moving parts**, no pressure drop, ideal for slurries and corrosive fluids
  - Modern design uses **pulsed DC excitation** to suppress electrode polarization

- **Ultrasonic Flow Meter:**
  - 🔑 Two principles: **Transit-Time** (clean fluids) and **Doppler** (dirty fluids)
  - **Transit-Time:** $v = c^2 \Delta t / (2L \cos\theta)$ — needs temperature compensation
  - **Doppler:** $v = c \Delta f / (2f_0 \cos\theta)$ — needs scattering particles
  - Non-invasive clamp-on types available
  - $\Delta t$ values are in **nanoseconds** — high-resolution timing required

- **Viscosity Measurement — Principle:**
  - 🔑 Newton's law: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — defines **dynamic viscosity** $\mu$
  - **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ (units: m²/s or cSt)
  - **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \mu L)$ — capillary viscometer principle
  - Viscometer types: Capillary, Rotational, Falling ball, Orifice, Vibrating element
  - **Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian** distinction is critical for instrument selection
  - **Assumption check:** Laminar flow, Newtonian fluid, no-slip wall, steady-state, fully-developed

- **🧠 Master Conversions to Memorize:**
  - 1 cP (centipoise) = 10⁻³ Pa·s
  - 1 cSt (centistoke) = 10⁻⁶ m²/s
  - 1 in = 25.4 mm; 1 mm Hg ≈ 133.32 Pa
  - Velocity of sound in water: c ≈ 1480 m/s at 20°C

- **⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exams:**
  1. Forgetting unit conversions (mm → m, mV → V)
  2. Confusing dynamic ($\mu$) and kinematic ($\nu$) viscosity
  3. Applying Hagen-Poiseuille to **turbulent flow** or **non-Newtonian fluids** — it is valid only for laminar Newtonian flow
  4. Claiming that the magmeter measures mass flow — it measures **volumetric flow** (mass flow requires density input)
  5. Forgetting the $c \gg v$ approximation in ultrasonic derivations
  6. Omitting the no-slip boundary condition in viscosity derivations

> **🎓 KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** For any 7-mark derivation question, structure your answer as: (1) State the law/principle, (2) Diagram/geometry, (3) Step-by-step algebra, (4) Final boxed answer, (5) State assumptions, (6) Brief engineering interpretation. This template consistently scores 7/7 with KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newtonian Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Newtonian Fluids # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Newtonian Fluid (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):** > A Newtonian fluid is a fluid in which the **shear stress ($\tau$)** is **directly proportional** to the **rate of shear strain (velocity gradient, $du/dy$)**, and the constant of proportionality is the **dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)**. The proportionality constant is **independent** of the magnitude of the shear rate. This relationship is governed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, expressed mathematically as: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa) - $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (N·s/m² or Pa·s) - $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹) The viscosity $\mu$ depends only on **temperature and pressure** (for gases) or **temperature alone** (for liquids at moderate pressures), and is **NOT** a function of the applied shear rate. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCAET602 – Module 3):** > Newtonian fluids form the foundational assumption for almost all industrial flow measurement devices — orifice plates, Venturi meters, rotameters, turbine flowmeters, and electromagnetic flowmeters. Understanding the Newtonian behavior is critical because **all calibration equations** of these instruments are derived assuming the working fluid is Newtonian. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are spreading **honey** on a slice of bread versus spreading **water** on a kitchen counter: - The **honey resists flow** more — you have to push harder and apply more shear stress to make it move. Yet if you push *even harder*, the honey simply resists proportionally more. Its resistance **scales linearly** with the effort applied. - The **water flows almost effortlessly** — very little shear stress is needed to produce a velocity gradient. Again, the relationship is **linear**. Both honey and water (in their normal conditions) follow Newton's law. They are both **Newtonian fluids** — the difference is only in the magnitude of the proportionality constant $\mu$. Now, contrast this with **toothpaste or ketchup**: - When you squeeze a ketchup bottle **gently**, nothing comes out. - When you squeeze **harder**, suddenly it flows out easily. - This **non-linear, shear-dependent** behavior makes them **Non-Newtonian fluids**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** "**N**ewtonian = **N**ice and **N**ormal" — the viscosity does not change with how hard you push. If you double the force, you double the flow rate. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Conversion | |---|---|---|---|---| | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | **N·s/m² = Pa·s** | **poise (P)** | $1 \; \text{Pa·s} = 10 \; \text{P}$ | | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **m²/s** | **stokes (St)** | $1 \; \text{m}^2/\text{s} = 10^4 \; \text{St}$ | | Shear Stress | $\tau$ | **N/m² = Pa** | dyne/cm² | $1 \; \text{Pa} = 10 \; \text{dyne/cm}^2$ | | Shear Rate | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **s⁻¹** | s⁻¹ | same | > [!NOTE] > **Water at 20 °C** has $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}$ and $\rho \approx 998 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, giving $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. These are the reference values used in calibration charts. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Operational Breakdown Consider a fluid layer flowing over a stationary surface (e.g., fluid between a moving upper plate and a fixed lower plate, **Couette flow**): - The fluid in contact with the **bottom plate** is stationary ($u = 0$). - The fluid in contact with the **top plate** moves at velocity $U$ (same velocity as the plate — no-slip condition). - Fluid in between has a **linear velocity profile** (for laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid). The mechanism works in **5 logical steps**: 1. **Momentum Transfer at Molecular Level:** Fluid molecules in faster-moving layers have higher momentum. They continuously migrate to slower layers (and vice versa) due to random molecular motion, transferring momentum. 2. **Shear Stress Generation:** This momentum exchange manifests macroscopically as a **tangential force per unit area**, called shear stress $\tau$. 3. **Proportionality:** Experimental observations by **Isaac Newton** showed $\tau \propto \dfrac{du}{dy}$ (linear relationship for common fluids). 4. **The Constant of Proportionality:** The constant is **dynamic viscosity** $\mu$, which is an **intrinsic fluid property**. 5. **Independence from Shear Rate:** Unlike non-Newtonian fluids, $\mu$ does **NOT** change as $\dot{\gamma}$ changes. Doubling the shear rate simply doubles the shear stress. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter for Industrial Instrumentation?** > Every flowmeter calibration equation (e.g., orifice plate: $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$ or Venturi: $Q = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$) is derived from **Bernoulli's equation** plus a **momentum correction**, both of which require a **laminar, Newtonian flow regime** for strict validity. If the process fluid is non-Newtonian (slurries, polymers, paints), the discharge coefficient $C_d$ will be **significantly different** and must be re-calibrated. --- ## 2.2 Properties of Newtonian Fluids (Board-Exam Favourite) - **Linear Rheogram:** Plot of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **straight line through the origin** with slope $\mu$. - **Viscosity is Constant:** For a given temperature and pressure, $\mu$ is fixed. - **No Yield Stress:** Newtonian fluids begin to deform under **any** infinitesimally small shear stress. There is no threshold. - **Isotropic:** Viscosity is the same in all directions (no structural orientation effects). - **Time-Independent:** Viscosity does not change with duration of applied shear (no thixotropy or rheopexy). ### Common Newtonian Fluids | Fluid | Typical $\mu$ at 20 °C (Pa·s) | Category | |---|---|---| | **Air** | $1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ | Gas | | **Water** | $1.00 \times 10^{-3}$ | Light liquid | | **Mercury** | $1.55 \times 10^{-3}$ | Heavy liquid metal | | **Light oil (machine)** | $\sim 0.1$ | Lubricant | | **Heavy fuel oil** | $\sim 0.5$ to 2.0 | Industrial | | **Glycerine** | $\sim 1.5$ | Viscous liquid | | **Honey** | $\sim 2$ to 10 | Highly viscous | > [!NOTE] > Industrial process fluids that are **NEVER** Newtonian include: **paints, blood, slurries, polymer melts, toothpaste, starch suspensions, printer inks, and drilling muds.** These require special rheological models (Bingham plastic, Power-law, Casson, etc.). --- ## 2.3 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity > [!TIP] > **This is a HIGH-YIELD board topic!** The viscosity–temperature relationship is **opposite** for liquids and gases. **For Liquids (e.g., water, oil):** Viscosity **decreases** sharply with increasing temperature. Empirical correlation: $$\mu = \mu_0 \, e^{-b(T - T_0)}$$ where $\mu_0$ is viscosity at reference temperature $T_0$, and $b$ is a fluid-specific constant. An alternative is the **Andrade equation**: $$\mu = A \, e^{B/T}$$ **For Gases (e.g., air, N₂):** Viscosity **increases** with temperature (Sutherland's formula): $$\mu = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$ where $S$ is the **Sutherland constant** (for air, $S \approx 110.4$ K). **Physical reason:** - In **liquids**, viscosity is dominated by **intermolecular cohesive forces**; heating weakens these forces → molecules slide past each other more easily → $\mu$ decreases. - In **gases**, viscosity is dominated by **momentum transfer via molecular motion**; heating increases molecular speeds and collision rates → $\mu$ increases. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Defining Newtonian behaviour | | 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | Reynolds number, momentum diffusivity | | 3 | $\tau = \nu \dfrac{d(\rho u)}{dy}$ | Alternate form (using $\nu$) | Compressible flow analysis | | 4 | $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force on area $A$ | Force on bearings, fluid film | | 5 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Shear rate definition | Viscometer calibration | | 6 | $\mu_{\text{liq}} = A e^{B/T}$ | Andrade's equation (liquids) | Temperature correction | | 7 | $\mu_{\text{gas}} = \mu_0 \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \dfrac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$ | Sutherland's formula (gases) | Gas viscosity correction | | 8 | $\mu_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{\sum x_i \mu_i \sqrt{M_i T_{ci}}{\sum x_i \sqrt{M_i T_{ci}}}$ | Mixing rule (gases) | Multi-component gas pipelines | | 9 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{vD}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | Flow regime determination | | 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille wall shear stress | Pipe flow instrumentation | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **dynamic viscosity $\mu$** (Pa·s) with **kinematic viscosity $\nu$** (m²/s). They differ by density: $\nu = \mu/\rho$. A common examiner trick is to give you $\mu$ and ask for $\nu$ (or vice versa) **without** reminding you to divide/multiply by $\rho$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from Momentum Transfer Consider two parallel fluid layers separated by a small distance $dy$. - Upper layer moves with velocity $u + du$. - Lower layer moves with velocity $u$. The velocity gradient is $\dfrac{du}{dy}$. **Step 1: Force per unit area (definition of shear stress)** By Newton's second law applied to a fluid element, the tangential force per unit area required to sustain this velocity gradient is defined as the shear stress $\tau$. **Step 2: Linear proportionality assumption** Empirically, for a class of "ideal" fluids, the shear stress is found to be **linearly proportional** to the velocity gradient: $$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$ **Step 3: Introduce the proportionality constant** The constant of proportionality is given the symbol $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity), giving: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ **Step 4: Vectorial (3D) form** In full tensorial notation, the viscous stress tensor $\tau_{ij}$ for a Newtonian fluid is: $$\tau_{ij} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} \right) - \frac{2}{3} \mu (\nabla \cdot \vec{u}) \delta_{ij}$$ For an **incompressible** Newtonian fluid ($\nabla \cdot \vec{u} = 0$), this reduces to the simple form used in most engineering problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity **Step 1: Recall the definitions** - Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has SI unit $\text{Pa·s} = \text{N·s/m}^2 = \text{kg/(m·s)}$. - Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density: $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$ **Step 2: Verify the unit** $$\frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$ This matches the SI unit of $\nu$. ✓ **Step 3: Physical meaning** - $\mu$ represents the fluid's resistance to **shear deformation** (internal friction). - $\nu$ represents the fluid's ability to **transfer momentum** relative to its inertia — it is the **momentum diffusivity**. **Worked Numerical Example (Board Style):** > **Problem:** A lubricating oil has a density of $880 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.11 \; \text{Pa·s}$. Find its kinematic viscosity in (a) m²/s and (b) stokes. **Solution:** (a) In SI units: $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.11 \; \text{Pa·s}}{880 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$$ $$\nu = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$ (b) Converting to stokes: $$\nu = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^4 \; \text{cSt} = 1.25 \; \text{St} = 125 \; \text{cSt}$$ This is typical for a **medium-grade machine oil** — a Newtonian fluid. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Velocity Profile in Laminar Pipe Flow (Hagen–Poiseuille) This derivation directly demonstrates Newtonian fluid behaviour and is the **theoretical basis of capillary viscometers** used in industrial instrumentation. **Step 1: Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element** Consider a pipe of radius $R$ with steady, laminar, fully-developed flow. Take a fluid cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$. Two forces act along the axis: - **Pressure force:** $\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$ (pushing forward) - **Viscous force:** $\tau \cdot 2\pi r L$ (resisting) At equilibrium (Newton's second law with zero acceleration): $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$ **Step 2: Solve for shear stress** $$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ **Step 3: Apply Newton's law of viscosity** $$\mu \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ **Step 4: Integrate with respect to r** $$\int du = \int \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L} dr$$ $$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} r^2 + C$$ **Step 5: Apply the no-slip boundary condition** At the pipe wall, $r = R$, $u = 0$: $$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} R^2 + C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C = -\frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$$ **Step 6: Final parabolic velocity profile** $$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)}$$ This is the famous **parabolic (Poiseuille) profile** — a direct consequence of Newtonian behaviour. The **maximum velocity** at the centerline ($r = 0$) is: $$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$ The **volumetric flow rate** is: $$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Instrumentation Application:** The Hagen–Poiseuille equation is the **operating principle of capillary viscometers**. By measuring $\Delta P$ for a known $Q$ (or vice versa) in a capillary of known $R$ and $L$, the **dynamic viscosity $\mu$** can be directly calculated. This is the **ASTM D445 standard test method** for kinematic viscosity of petroleum products. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Viscosity from Falling Sphere (Stokes' Law) — Viscometer Application The **falling ball viscometer** (Höppler viscometer) is a key industrial instrument for Newtonian fluid viscosity measurement. **Step 1: Forces on a sphere falling in a viscous fluid** A sphere of radius $r_s$ and density $\rho_s$ falling at terminal velocity $v_t$ in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ experiences three forces: - Weight (downward): $W = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_s^3 \rho_s g$ - Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_s^3 \rho_f g$ - Viscous drag (Stokes' law, upward): $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r_s v_t$ **Step 2: Terminal velocity condition (net force = 0)** $$W = F_b + F_d$$ $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r_s^3 \rho_s g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_s^3 \rho_f g + 6 \pi \mu r_s v_t$$ **Step 3: Solve for viscosity** $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r_s^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6 \pi \mu r_s v_t$$ $$\mu = \frac{2 r_s^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$$ This is the **calibration equation** for falling-ball viscometers — a direct industrial application of Newtonian fluid mechanics. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Viscometer Selection Guide for Newtonian Fluids | Instrument | Working Principle | Suitable Viscosity Range | Accuracy | Industrial Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Capillary (Ubbelohde)** | Hagen–Poiseuille flow | $0.4$ – $50,000 \; \text{cSt}$ | $\pm 0.1\%$ | Petroleum, lubricants | | **Falling-ball (Höppler)** | Stokes' law | $0.5$ – $10^5 \; \text{cSt}$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ | Polymer solutions, oils | | **Rotational (Brookfield)** | Torque on spindle | $1$ – $10^8 \; \text{cSt}$ | $\pm 1\%$ | Paints, food, cosmetics | | **Vibrational (SV-10)** | Damping of vibration | $0.3$ – $10^5 \; \text{cSt}$ | $\pm 1\%$ | Inline process monitoring | | **Cone-and-plate** | Shear in narrow gap | $0.1$ – $10^5 \; \text{Pa·s}$ | $\pm 2\%$ | High-shear rheology | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Rheological Classification of Fluids (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([All Real Fluids]) --> N1{Is viscosity
constant with
shear rate?} N1 -- YES --> NewT["NEWTONIAN FLUIDS
tau equals mu times du over dy
Straight-line rheogram
Examples: Water, Air, Oil"] N1 -- NO --> N2{Does fluid have
yield stress?} N2 -- YES --> N3{Linear after
yielding?} N3 -- YES --> BingT["BINGHAM PLASTIC
Examples: Toothpaste, Drilling mud"] N3 -- NO --> N4{Shear-thinning
or thickening?} N4 -- Thinning --> PsT["PSEUDOPLASTIC
Examples: Blood, Polymer solutions"] N4 -- Thickening --> DilT["DILATANT
Examples: Starch suspension, Wet sand"] N2 -- NO --> N5{Time-dependent
behaviour?} N5 -- YES --> N6{Thixotropic or
Rheopectic?} N6 -- Thixotropy --> ThxT["THIXOTROPIC
Examples: Ketchup, Printing ink"] N6 -- Rheopexy --> RheT["RHEOPECTIC
Examples: Gypsum paste"] N5 -- NO --> N4 NewT --> IndApp["Used in: Orifice, Venturi, Rotameter,
Turbine, Magnetic flowmeters"] BingT --> IndApp2["Requires empirical calibration"] PsT --> IndApp2 DilT --> IndApp2 ThxT --> IndApp2 RheT --> IndApp2 ``` --- ## 4.2 Newtonian Fluid — Shear Stress vs Shear Rate Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HighShear["HIGH SHEAR RATE REGION"] H1[du/dy large] end subgraph MedShear["MEDIUM SHEAR RATE REGION"] M1[du/dy moderate] end subgraph LowShear["LOW SHEAR RATE REGION"] L1[du/dy small] end H1 --> Linear1["Linear relationship holds:
tau = mu times du over dy
Slope is constant
Viscosity mu unchanged"] M1 --> Linear2["Linear relationship holds:
tau = mu times du over dy
Slope is constant
Viscosity mu unchanged"] L1 --> Linear3["Linear relationship holds:
tau = mu times du over dy
Slope is constant
Viscosity mu unchanged"] Linear1 --> Conclusion["Viscosity mu = constant
at all shear rates
for Newtonian fluids"] Linear2 --> Conclusion Linear3 --> Conclusion Conclusion --> EngApp["Industrial implication:
Flowmeter calibration is
shear-rate independent"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Capillary Viscometer for Newtonian Fluids ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reservoir with
test Newtonian fluid] --> B[Constant head
device maintains
constant h] B --> C[Capillary tube
of length L and
radius R] C --> D[Measuring cylinder
to collect outflow] D --> E[Stopwatch for
time measurement] E --> F[Calculation block
nu = pi times R to power 4
times g times h times t
divided by 8 times L times V] F --> G[Display of
kinematic viscosity
in cSt] C --> H[Differential pressure
sensor across capillary] H --> F ``` **Description of Flow:** A constant-head device ensures a steady hydrostatic pressure $\Delta P = \rho g h$ drives the Newtonian fluid through the capillary. The volume $V$ collected in time $t$ yields the flow rate $Q = V/t$. Substituting into Poiseuille's law gives: $$\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \rho g h t}{8 L V}$$ This is the **canonical experimental method** for verifying Newtonian fluid behaviour in an instrumentation lab. --- ## 4.4 Schematic — Couette Flow Visualisation (Newtonian Velocity Profile) ```mermaid flowchart TB Top[Top plate moves with
velocity U rightward] --> TopArrow[No slip condition
Top fluid layer has velocity U] TopArrow --> Layer1[Layer 1 at y = 3h over 4
velocity = 3U over 4] Layer1 --> Layer2[Layer 2 at y = h over 2
velocity = U over 2] Layer2 --> Layer3[Layer 3 at y = h over 4
velocity = U over 4] Layer3 --> Bottom[Bottom plate stationary
Bottom fluid layer velocity = 0] Layer1 -.-> DuDy1["du over dy = U over h
CONSTANT for Newtonian fluid"] Layer2 -.-> DuDy1 Layer3 -.-> DuDy1 DuDy1 --> ShearStress[Shear stress tau = mu times U over h
Uniform across entire gap] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Define a Newtonian fluid. Give two examples and state Newton's law of viscosity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Newtonian fluid is one in which the **shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient)**, and the constant of proportionality — the dynamic viscosity $\mu$ — is **independent of the shear rate** at constant temperature and pressure. $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ **Two examples (1 mark each):** 1. **Water** at moderate temperatures ($\mu \approx 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) 2. **Air** at standard conditions ($\mu \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s) Other acceptable examples: light lubricating oil, alcohol, gasoline, mercury. --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Measure of fluid's internal resistance to flow (shear) | Measure of resistance to flow under the influence of gravity / momentum diffusivity | | **Formula** | $\mu$ from $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | | **SI Unit** | **Pa·s** (or N·s/m²) | **m²/s** | | **CGS Unit** | poise (P) | stokes (St) | | **Physical meaning** | Absolute viscosity — fluid property | Relative viscosity — includes density effect | **Relationship:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ → they are related through fluid density. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and may have sub-parts. Standard split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, or **(a) 8 + (b) 6**. Students answer **ONE full question** per module slot. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Theoretical + Problem Solving* **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`** **(a)** State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. With the help of a neat sketch, show that for a Newtonian fluid, the velocity profile between two parallel plates (one stationary, one moving) is **linear**, and the shear stress is **uniform** across the gap. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu = 0.85 \; \text{Pa·s}$ and density $\rho = 920 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ flows through a horizontal capillary tube of length $L = 0.5 \; \text{m}$ and diameter $D = 4 \; \text{mm}$ under a pressure drop of $\Delta P = 80 \; \text{kPa}$. Calculate: - (i) The maximum velocity at the centerline - (ii) The average velocity - (iii) The volumetric flow rate - (iv) The Reynolds number, and comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Statement of Newton's Law (2 marks):** > *"The shear stress in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, the constant of proportionality being the dynamic viscosity."* $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ **Sketch description (2 marks):** [Refer to the Couette flow diagram in Section 4.4] - Top plate moves with velocity $U$; bottom plate is stationary. - Distance between plates: $h$. - Fluid adheres to plates (no-slip condition). **Derivation of linear profile and uniform shear stress (3 marks):** From the no-slip condition: - At $y = 0$: $u = 0$ - At $y = h$: $u = U$ Assuming a linear profile: $u(y) = \dfrac{U}{h} y$ Differentiating: $\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{U}{h}$ (constant, independent of $y$) Therefore, the shear stress: $$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h} = \text{constant}$$ Both the **velocity profile is linear** and the **shear stress is uniform** across the gap — these are signatures of Newtonian fluid behaviour. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - $\mu = 0.85 \; \text{Pa·s}$ - $\rho = 920 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - $L = 0.5 \; \text{m}$ - $D = 4 \; \text{mm} = 0.004 \; \text{m}$ → $R = 0.002 \; \text{m}$ - $\Delta P = 80 \; \text{kPa} = 80,000 \; \text{Pa}$ **(i) Maximum velocity (centerline) (2 marks):** From Poiseuille's equation derived in Section 3.3: $$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$ $$u_{\max} = \frac{80{,}000 \times (0.002)^2}{4 \times 0.85 \times 0.5}$$ $$u_{\max} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{1.7}$$ $$u_{\max} = \frac{0.32}{1.7} = 0.1882 \; \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Average velocity (2 marks):** For laminar parabolic flow, the average velocity is exactly **half** the centerline velocity: $$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{u_{\max}}{2} = \frac{0.1882}{2} = 0.0941 \; \text{m/s}$$ **(iii) Volumetric flow rate (1.5 marks):** $$Q = u_{\text{avg}} \times A = 0.0941 \times \frac{\pi (0.004)^2}{4}$$ $$Q = 0.0941 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$Q = 1.182 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1.182 \; \text{mL/s}$$ **(iv) Reynolds number (1.5 marks):** $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho u_{\text{avg}} D}{\mu} = \frac{920 \times 0.0941 \times 0.004}{0.85}$$ $$\text{Re} = \frac{0.3463}{0.85} = 0.407$$ Since $\text{Re} \ll 2300$, the flow is **deeply laminar** — consistent with the Poiseuille assumption used in the entire derivation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`** **(a)** Derive the relationship between dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) and kinematic viscosity ($\nu$). Explain, with a temperature-viscosity sketch, how viscosity varies with temperature for **(i)** liquids and **(ii)** gases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel ball of diameter $d = 4 \; \text{mm}$ and density $\rho_s = 7800 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ falls through a Newtonian oil of density $\rho_f = 850 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. The terminal velocity is observed to be $v_t = 0.018 \; \text{m/s}$. Calculate the **dynamic viscosity** of the oil. If the same oil is used in a **capillary viscometer** with $L = 0.2 \; \text{m}$, $D = 2 \; \text{mm}$, and the pressure drop is $20 \; \text{kPa}$, find the **volumetric flow rate**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Derivation of $\nu = \mu / \rho$ (3 marks):** Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units of $\text{kg/(m·s)}$. Fluid density $\rho$ has units of $\text{kg/m}^3$. Their ratio: $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [\nu] = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$ This is the **kinematic viscosity**, named so because the units contain only **kinematic** quantities (length²/time). It represents the **diffusion coefficient for momentum** in the fluid. **Temperature dependence — Liquids (2 marks):** For liquids, viscosity **decreases exponentially** with increasing temperature (Andrade's equation): $$\mu_{\text{liq}} = A \, e^{B/T}$$ *Physical reason:* Increased thermal energy overcomes intermolecular cohesive forces, allowing layers to slide more easily. ``` mu |\ | \ | \ Liquids | \_____ | \____ |_____________\___ T ``` **Temperature dependence — Gases (2 marks):** For gases, viscosity **increases** with temperature (Sutherland's formula): $$\mu_{\text{gas}} = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$ *Physical reason:* Higher temperature means faster molecular motion and more frequent momentum-exchanging collisions. ``` mu | / Gases | / | / | / |__/_______________ T ``` --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - Ball diameter $d = 4 \; \text{mm} = 0.004 \; \text{m}$ → radius $r_s = 0.002 \; \text{m}$ - $\rho_s = 7800 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - $\rho_f = 850 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - $v_t = 0.018 \; \text{m/s}$ - Capillary: $L = 0.2 \; \text{m}$, $D = 2 \; \text{mm} = 0.002 \; \text{m}$, $\Delta P = 20 \; \text{kPa} = 20{,}000 \; \text{Pa}$ - $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$ **Step 1: Verify Stokes' regime (1 mark)** We need to check that $\text{Re}_{\text{ball}} < 0.1$ for Stokes' law validity (we'll verify after finding $\mu$). **Step 2: Calculate $\mu$ using Stokes' formula (3 marks):** From Section 3.4 derivation: $$\mu = \frac{2 r_s^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$$ $$\mu = \frac{2 \times (0.002)^2 \times 9.81 \times (7800 - 850)}{9 \times 0.018}$$ $$\mu = \frac{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 6950}{0.162}$$ $$\mu = \frac{0.5456}{0.162} = 3.368 \; \text{Pa·s}$$ **Step 3: Verify Stokes' regime (1 mark):** $$\text{Re}_{\text{ball}} = \frac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu} = \frac{850 \times 0.018 \times 0.004}{3.368} = 0.0182 \ll 0.1 \;\; ✓$$ The Stokes' assumption is valid. **Step 4: Volumetric flow rate through capillary (2 marks):** From Poiseuille's law: $$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$ $$R = 0.001 \; \text{m}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \times (0.001)^4 \times 20{,}000}{8 \times 3.368 \times 0.2}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-12} \times 20{,}000}{5.389}$$ $$Q = \frac{6.283 \times 10^{-8}}{5.389}$$ $$Q = 1.166 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 0.01166 \; \text{mL/s}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation for "Newtonian Fluids":** > > 1. **Forgetting the no-slip condition** in Couette flow derivations (loses 1–2 marks). > 2. **Unit confusion:** Writing $\mu$ in poise without converting to Pa·s before using in SI equations (loses 1 mark). > 3. **Mixing up $\mu$ and $\nu$:** Always check what the question is asking for. If density is given along with one viscosity, you must convert to the other. > 4. **Forgetting to verify Stokes' regime** in falling-ball problems — examiners will deduct 1 mark if $\text{Re}_{\text{ball}} < 0.1$ is not stated/checked. > 5. **Plotting the rheogram incorrectly:** Newtonian fluids give a **straight line through the ORIGIN** (not offset). Shear-thickening curves upward; shear-thinning curves downward. Mislabeling these costs 2 marks. > 6. **Not commenting on the flow regime** after computing Reynolds number — examiners expect "laminar / transitional / turbulent" as a concluding remark. > 7. **Applying Poiseuille's law for turbulent flow:** The parabolic profile and $Q$ formula are valid **only for laminar flow** of a Newtonian fluid in a circular pipe. State this assumption explicitly. > 8. **Sign error in the force balance:** In Stokes' derivation, the **buoyancy force is upward** and **drag is upward**, while **weight is downward**. Mixing directions costs 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Newtonian Fluids (Module 3)** - ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — the *defining* equation of a Newtonian fluid. - ✅ **Key property:** $\mu$ is **constant** (independent of shear rate) for Newtonian fluids. - ✅ **Rheogram:** Straight line through origin; slope = $\mu$. - ✅ **No yield stress** — fluid flows under any infinitesimal shear. - ✅ **Dynamic viscosity $\mu$:** SI unit **Pa·s**; CGS unit **poise (P)**; $1 \; \text{Pa·s} = 10 \; \text{P}$. - ✅ **Kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$:** SI unit **m²/s**; CGS unit **stokes (St)**. - ✅ **Temperature effect:** Liquids — $\mu$ **decreases** with $T$ (Andrade). Gases — $\mu$ **increases** with $T$ (Sutherland). - ✅ **Examples:** Water, air, light oils, alcohol, mercury, gasoline, glycerine, honey (all Newtonian). - ✅ **Non-Newtonian examples** (to contrast): Blood, paint, ketchup, toothpaste, drilling mud, polymer melts, starch. - ✅ **Poiseuille flow (laminar, circular pipe):** $u(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$ — parabolic profile. - ✅ **Maximum velocity** (centerline): $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$. - ✅ **Average velocity:** $u_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{u_{\max}}{2}$ (laminar Newtonian only). - ✅ **Volumetric flow rate (Poiseuille):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$. - ✅ **Stokes' law (falling sphere):** $\mu = \dfrac{2 r_s^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$. - ✅ **Industrial viscometers for Newtonian fluids:** Capillary (Ubbelohde), Falling-ball (Höppler), Rotational (Brookfield). - ✅ **Validity condition** for Poiseuille's law: laminar flow ($\text{Re} < 2300$), Newtonian fluid, steady state, fully developed. - ✅ **Validity condition** for Stokes' law: $\text{Re}_{\text{ball}} < 0.1$ (creeping flow). - ✅ **Instrumentation link:** Most flowmeters (orifice, Venturi, rotameter, turbine, magnetic) are calibrated assuming **Newtonian working fluid**. Non-Newtonian fluids require empirical re-calibration of the discharge coefficient $C_d$.

topics.title: Viscometer Types - Saybolt and Red Wood. Capillary Viscometers - Differential Pressure Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Viscometer Types - Saybolt and Red Wood. Capillary Viscometers - Differential Pressure Type # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Viscosity — The Industrial Foundation **Viscosity** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow. It is a fundamental property that dictates pump selection, pipeline sizing, lubrication efficiency, paint flow, and the performance of every process fluid in the chemical, petroleum, food, and polymer industries. There are two principal forms of viscosity: | Quantity | Symbol | Units | Definition | |---|---|---|---| | Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity | $\mu$ | Pa·s or Poise (P) | Resistance to shear stress | | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | m²/s or Stokes (St) | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ (resistance to flow under gravity) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s** and **1 Stokes = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s**. In industrial practice, kinematic viscosity is often quoted in **centistokes (cSt)** where 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s. ## 1.2 Saybolt Viscometer The **Saybolt Universal Viscometer (SUV)** is an empirical, efflux-type viscometer developed in the United States. It measures the time (in seconds) required for **60 mL of the sample fluid** to flow out through a standardized orifice of diameter **1.765 mm to 1.785 mm** under gravity at a controlled temperature. - **Standard reference temperatures:** $100^{\circ}\text{F}$ $(37.8^{\circ}\text{C})$ and $210^{\circ}\text{F}$ $(98.9^{\circ}\text{C})$ - **Efflux time** is called **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)** or **SSU (Saybolt Seconds Universal)** - A variant called **Saybolt Furol** uses a larger orifice (≈ 3.15 mm) for heavy fuels and is quoted as **SFS** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Saybolt viscometer is an *empirical* instrument. It does **not** directly give viscosity in SI units; instead, an empirical equation converts SUS into kinematic viscosity (cSt). ## 1.3 Redwood Viscometer The **Redwood Viscometer** is the British counterpart of the Saybolt. It was developed by **Sir Boverton Redwood** in the UK and is widely used for petroleum products in Commonwealth countries. - Two models exist: **Redwood No. I** (standard, 50 mL, orifice ≈ 1.62 mm) and **Redwood No. II** (larger orifice ≈ 3.8 mm for highly viscous oils) - It measures the time (in **Redwood Seconds**) for **50 mL** of fluid to discharge under gravity - Standard bath temperatures: $60^{\circ}\text{C}$, $98.9^{\circ}\text{C}$, $100^{\circ}\text{C}$, $120^{\circ}\text{C}$, $150^{\circ}\text{C}$ ## 1.4 Capillary Viscometer — Differential Pressure Type The **Capillary Differential-Pressure Viscometer** is a *fundamental* (absolute) instrument based on the **Hagen–Poiseuille law**. A fluid of known density is forced through a precision-bore capillary tube; the **pressure drop** across the tube and the **volumetric flow rate** are measured simultaneously, and dynamic viscosity is computed from these two measurements. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview):** > Imagine sucking a thick milkshake through a narrow straw. The harder you have to suck (pressure difference) and the slower it comes out (flow rate), the thicker (more viscous) the milkshake. A capillary viscometer does exactly this — it measures *how much pressure* is needed to push fluid *at what rate* through a known-diameter tube. The Saybolt and Redwood instruments are, in contrast, like tipping a cup of milkshake and *timing* how long it takes to empty. Both measure the same property, but the capillary method is more accurate and absolute. ## 1.5 Classification of Viscometers (Context Map) Viscosity is measured by three physical principles: 1. **Efflux / Discharge** (Saybolt, Redwood, Engler) — empirical, time-based 2. **Capillary flow** (Differential pressure type) — fundamental, derived from Poiseuille's law 3. **Rotational / Falling-body / Oscillating** (Brookfield, Hoppler, Vibrational) — covered in adjacent modules The present topic covers **(1)** and **(2)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Saybolt Universal Viscometer — Construction & Working ### Construction - A vertical **cylindrical oil cup** of standard dimensions holds the sample - The cup bottom has a precision **orifice** (≈ 1.76 mm diameter) closed by a **plug / cork** - The cup sits inside a **water/oil bath** with a stirrer and electric heater to maintain a fixed temperature (e.g., $100^{\circ}\text{F}$) - A **thermometer** reads the sample temperature - A **receiving flask** of 60 mL capacity is placed below the orifice - A **levelling bubble** ensures the orifice is truly vertical ### Operating Procedure 1. Clean the oil cup and orifice 2. Pour the sample **up to the mark** 3. Stir and bring sample to desired test temperature (allow ≈ 5 min for thermal equilibrium) 4. Place the 60 mL flask below 5. Withdraw the cork and **simultaneously start a stopwatch** 6. Stop the watch when the flask is just full to the brim (60 mL mark) 7. Record time in **seconds** — this is the **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)** ### Empirically Measured Viscosity The kinematic viscosity (in cSt) is related to SUS by the empirical ASTM correlation: $$ \nu \;[\text{cSt}] \;=\; 0.226 \, t \;-\; \dfrac{195}{t} $$ Valid in the range $t \in [100, 400]\,\text{s}$. A simpler engineering approximation often used in textbooks: $$ \nu \;[\text{cSt}] \;\approx\; 0.0022 \, t \;-\; \dfrac{1.8}{t} \quad (t > 100\,\text{s}) $$ where $t$ is the Saybolt efflux time in seconds. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Saybolt Furol Viscometer (used for heavy fuel oils, lubricants) uses the same principle but with an orifice roughly 3× larger. **Saybolt Furol Seconds (SFS)** are ≈ 10 × SUS for the same fluid. ## 2.2 The Redwood Viscometer — Construction & Working ### Construction - A polished **brass / silvered oil cup** with a **jet** (orifice) at the bottom - Orifice of **Redwood No. I** ≈ 1.62 mm diameter, 10 mm long - Surrounded by a **water bath** with heater and stirrer - A **50 mL Kohlrausch flask** with a scribed mark catches the discharge - A **thermometer** and a **levelling gauge** are fitted ### Operating Procedure 1. Fill the oil cup up to the jet tip 2. Heat to test temperature ($60^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $98.9^{\circ}\text{C}$, etc.) 3. Place the 50 mL flask under the jet 4. Open the jet and **start the stopwatch** simultaneously 5. Stop the watch when the **50 mL mark** is exactly reached 6. The recorded time = **Redwood No. I seconds** ### Empirically Measured Viscosity For Redwood No. I (efflux time $t$ in seconds, between 30 s and 2000 s): $$ \nu \;[\text{cSt}] \;=\; 0.0026 \, t \;-\; \dfrac{1.72}{t} $$ For Redwood No. II (larger orifice, used when $t > 2000$ s on No. I): $$ \nu \;[\text{cSt}] \;=\; 0.027 \, t \;-\; \dfrac{6.18}{t} $$ ## 2.3 Capillary Viscometer — Differential Pressure Type ### Construction - A **precision capillary tube** of known length $L$ and known bore radius $R$ - An **upper reservoir** for the test fluid - A **lower collection tank** (or a precision pump if forced flow is used) - A **differential pressure transducer** (or **U-tube manometer**) measures $\Delta P$ across the capillary - A **flow meter** (e.g., turbine, rotameter) or a **measuring cylinder + stopwatch** determines $Q$ - A **thermostatic bath** controls temperature to within $\pm 0.01^{\circ}\text{C}$ ### Working Principle — Hagen–Poiseuille Law For **steady, laminar, fully-developed** flow of a Newtonian fluid through a circular capillary: $$ Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, R^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L} $$ Solving for the **dynamic viscosity**: $$ \mu \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, R^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, L \, Q} \quad [\text{Pa·s}] $$ Dividing by density $\rho$ gives the **kinematic viscosity**: $$ \nu \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, R^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \rho \, L \, Q} \quad [\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}] $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Capillary viscometers are the **national standard** instruments in metrology labs. The same principle is used in *glass-capillary kinematic viscometers* (e.g., Cannon-Fenske, Ubbelohde) certified by ASTM D445. In process plants, differential-pressure capillary viscometers are used for in-line viscosity measurement of crudes, polymer melts, and food pastes. ### Reynolds-Number Constraint The Hagen–Poiseuille law is valid only for **laminar flow**. The governing condition is: $$ Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, \bar{u} \, D_{h}}{\mu} \;<\; 2300 $$ Substituting $\bar{u} = Q / (\pi R^{2})$ and $D_{h} = 2R$: $$ Re \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, \rho \, Q}{\pi \, R \, \mu} \;<\; 2300 $$ If $Re$ exceeds 2300, the Hagen–Poiseuille law is no longer valid and corrections (or a smaller capillary) are required. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Definition of kinematic viscosity | m²/s | | 2 | $\nu = 0.226\,t - 195/t$ | Saybolt → cSt conversion (ASTM) | t in s | | 3 | $\nu \approx 0.0022\,t - 1.8/t$ | Approximate Saybolt → cSt (t > 100 s) | t in s | | 4 | $\nu = 0.0026\,t - 1.72/t$ | Redwood No. I → cSt | t in s | | 5 | $\nu = 0.027\,t - 6.18/t$ | Redwood No. II → cSt | t in s | | 6 | $Q = \pi R^{4}\Delta P / (8\mu L)$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow rate | m³/s | | 7 | $\mu = \pi R^{4}\Delta P / (8LQ)$ | Dynamic viscosity from capillary | Pa·s | | 8 | $\nu = \pi R^{4}\Delta P / (8\rho L Q)$ | Kinematic viscosity from capillary | m²/s | | 9 | $\Delta P = 32\mu L \bar{u} / D^{2}$ | Pressure drop across capillary | Pa | | 10 | $Re = 2\rho Q / (\pi R \mu) < 2300$ | Laminar-flow validity check | dimensionless | | 11 | $\tau = \mu \,(du/dr)$ | Newton's law of viscosity (basis) | Pa | | 12 | $u(r) = (\Delta P/4\mu L)(R^{2}-r^{2})$ | Parabolic velocity profile in capillary | m/s | > [!WARNING] > **Constant traps to avoid:** > 1 cSt = **10⁻⁶** m²/s. Always convert. 1 Poise = **0.1** Pa·s. When using the Saybolt/Redwood empirical equations, **time $t$ MUST be in seconds**, not minutes. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Foundation of the Capillary Viscometer) This is the most important derivation for KTU exams on this topic. We derive it from first principles using a **force balance on a cylindrical fluid element** inside a horizontal capillary tube. ### Setup Consider a horizontal capillary tube of length $L$ and internal radius $R$. A Newtonian fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is flowing **steadily and laminarly** under a constant pressure difference $\Delta P$ between the inlet and outlet. Isolate a coaxial cylindrical element of fluid of radius $r$ and length $L$. ### Step A — Force balance in the axial (flow) direction The element has two axial forces: 1. **Pressure thrust at the inlet face** (area $\pi r^{2}$): $\;\;F_{1} = P_{1} \cdot \pi r^{2}$ 2. **Pressure thrust at the outlet face** (area $\pi r^{2}$): $\;\;F_{2} = P_{2} \cdot \pi r^{2}$ 3. **Viscous shear drag** on the lateral surface (area $2\pi r L$): $\;\;F_{\text{visc}} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$ For **steady, fully-developed flow**, the net force is zero (no acceleration): $$ F_{1} - F_{2} - F_{\text{visc}} \;=\; 0 $$ $$ P_{1}\,\pi r^{2} \;-\; P_{2}\,\pi r^{2} \;-\; \tau \cdot 2\pi r L \;=\; 0 $$ $$ (P_{1} - P_{2})\,\pi r^{2} \;=\; \tau \cdot 2\pi r L $$ Define $\Delta P = P_{1} - P_{2}$: $$ \Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} \;=\; 2\pi r L \, \tau $$ Solve for shear stress $\tau$: $$ \tau \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L} $$ > **Comment:** This is a *linear* function of $r$ — wall shear stress $\tau_{w}$ occurs at $r = R$ and equals $\tau_{w} = \Delta P R / (2L)$. ### Step B — Apply Newton's law of viscosity For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient: $$ \tau \;=\; -\mu \, \dfrac{du}{dr} $$ The negative sign appears because velocity $u$ decreases as $r$ increases (no-slip at the wall). Equate with the force-balance result: $$ -\mu \, \dfrac{du}{dr} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L} $$ Rearrange: $$ \dfrac{du}{dr} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \, r $$ ### Step C — Integrate to obtain velocity profile Integrate with respect to $r$: $$ u(r) \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \, r^{2} \;+\; C $$ Apply the **no-slip boundary condition** at the wall: $u(R) = 0$. $$ 0 \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \, R^{2} \;+\; C \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4\mu L} $$ Therefore the **parabolic velocity profile** (Hagen–Poiseuille profile) is: $$ \boxed{\,u(r) \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\,(R^{2} - r^{2})\,} $$ Maximum velocity occurs at the centreline ($r = 0$): $$ u_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4\mu L} $$ ### Step D — Integrate velocity over the cross-section to get flow rate A thin annular ring at radius $r$ of thickness $dr$ has area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The flow rate through the ring is $u(r)\,dA$. Total volumetric flow rate: $$ Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr $$ Substitute $u(r)$: $$ Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^{2}-r^{2}) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr $$ $$ Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2}r - r^{3})\, dr $$ $$ Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\dfrac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} $$ $$ Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\dfrac{R^{4}}{2} - \dfrac{R^{4}}{4}\right] \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \dfrac{R^{4}}{4} $$ $$ \boxed{\,Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}\,} $$ This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**, the cornerstone of capillary viscometry. ### Step E — Invert to obtain viscosity Solving for $\mu$: $$ \boxed{\,\mu \;=\; \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 L Q}\,} $$ Dividing by density $\rho$ for the kinematic viscosity: $$ \boxed{\,\nu \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \rho L Q}\,} $$ ### Step F — Mean velocity and average shear rate The mean (average) velocity is: $$ \bar{u} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L} \;=\; \dfrac{u_{\max}}{2} $$ The **apparent wall shear rate** (used in non-Newtonian rheology): $$ \dot{\gamma}_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}} \;=\; \dfrac{4\bar{u}}{R} $$ The **wall shear stress**: $$ \tau_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2L} $$ These three relations complete the analytical picture. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Velocity and Wall Shear Stress From Step D, the mean velocity is exactly half the centreline velocity. We now derive wall shear stress directly: $$ \tau_{w} \;=\; \left. -\mu \dfrac{du}{dr} \right|_{r=R} \;=\; \mu \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot R \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} $$ Substituting $R = D/2$: $$ \boxed{\,\tau_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}\,} $$ In terms of the mean velocity $\bar u$: $$ \tau_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{8 \mu \bar{u}}{D} $$ This is the well-known **Fanning-style expression** for pipe flow. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Viscometers | Feature | Saybolt | Redwood | Capillary (Diff. Pressure) | |---|---|---|---| | Principle | Empirical efflux time | Empirical efflux time | Fundamental Hagen–Poiseuille | | Sample volume | 60 mL | 50 mL | Continuous / variable | | Output | Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS) | Redwood Seconds | Direct $\mu$ in Pa·s | | Conversion to $\nu$ | Empirical formula | Empirical formula | Direct (no empirical step) | | Typical accuracy | ± 1 % | ± 1 % | ± 0.1 % (master) | | Orifice / Capillary | 1.76 mm orifice | 1.62 mm (No. I) or 3.8 mm (No. II) | 0.1 – 1.0 mm bore capillary | | Flow regime | Gravity-driven, mostly turbulent at the orifice | Gravity-driven | Strictly laminar ($Re < 2300$) | | Standard temperatures | $100^{\circ}\text{F}$, $210^{\circ}\text{F}$ | $60^{\circ}\text{C}$, $100^{\circ}\text{C}$, etc. | Any (controlled by bath) | | Kinematic viscosity range | 2 – 1500 cSt (No. I), up to 7000 cSt (Furol) | 0.5 – 50000 cSt | 0.1 – 10⁵ cSt | | Country of origin / use | USA (ASTM D88, D2161) | UK / Commonwealth (IP 70) | International (ISO 3104, ASTM D445) | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A capillary viscometer has a bore of $R = 0.5\,\text{mm}$ and length $L = 250\,\text{mm}$. The pressure drop across the capillary is $\Delta P = 1200\,\text{Pa}$ when a flow rate of $Q = 2.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ is measured. The fluid density is $\rho = 870\,\text{kg}/\text{m}^{3}$. Calculate (a) the dynamic viscosity, (b) the kinematic viscosity, and (c) the Reynolds number. Comment on the validity of Poiseuille's law. ### Solution **(a) Dynamic viscosity** Use: $$ \mu \;=\; \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 L Q} $$ Substitute the values (note $R = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, so $R^{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{m}^{4}$): $$ \mu \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times (6.25 \times 10^{-14}) \times 1200}{8 \times (0.250) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-7})} $$ Compute the numerator: $$ \pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 1200 \;=\; 2.356 \times 10^{-10} $$ Compute the denominator: $$ 8 \times 0.250 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \;=\; 4.0 \times 10^{-7} $$ Therefore: $$ \mu \;=\; \dfrac{2.356 \times 10^{-10}}{4.0 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 5.89 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{Pa·s} \;=\; 0.589\,\text{mPa·s} $$ **(b) Kinematic viscosity** $$ \nu \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{5.89 \times 10^{-4}}{870} \;=\; 6.77 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s} \;=\; 0.677\,\text{cSt} $$ **(c) Reynolds number** Mean velocity: $$ \bar{u} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times (2.5 \times 10^{-7})} \;=\; 0.2546\,\text{m/s} $$ Hydraulic diameter $D_{h} = 2R = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$: $$ Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \bar{u} D_{h}}{\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{870 \times 0.2546 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.89 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 376 $$ Since $Re = 376 \ll 2300$, the flow is **laminar**, and the Hagen–Poiseuille law is valid. ✓ # 4.
