KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Saybolt Universal Viscosity. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Saybolt viscometer.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Saybolt Universal Viscosity is the time in seconds (called Saybolt Universal Seconds, SUS) required for **60 mL** of the sample fluid to flow out through a standard orifice of diameter ≈ 1.76 mm under gravity at a controlled temperature (typically $100^{\circ}\text{F}$ or $210^{\circ}\text{F}$). The instrument consists of an oil cup surrounded by a thermostatic bath, a calibrated orifice at the bottom closed by a cork, and a receiving flask. The cork is withdrawn, a stopwatch started, and stopped exactly when 60 mL is collected. The time in seconds is the SUS value, which is then converted to kinematic viscosity (cSt) using the empirical equation $\nu = 0.226\,t - 195/t$. (3 marks) ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain the Hagen–Poiseuille equation. How is it used in a capillary-type differential pressure viscometer?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Hagen–Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular capillary of radius $R$ and length $L$ under a pressure difference $\Delta P$: $$ Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L} $$ In a differential pressure capillary viscometer, $R$, $L$, $\Delta P$ (from a transducer or manometer) and $Q$ (from a flow meter or timed collection) are all measured. The dynamic viscosity is then computed as $\mu = \pi R^{4} \Delta P / (8LQ)$ and the kinematic viscosity as $\nu = \mu/\rho$. The method is **absolute** (no empirical calibration needed) and accurate to ≈ 0.1 %. Validity requires laminar flow: $Re < 2300$. (3 marks) ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Redwood viscometer No. I**. Explain how kinematic viscosity is determined from the Redwood efflux time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** from first principles, starting from a force balance on a cylindrical element of fluid inside a capillary. State the conditions of validity. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Construction (3 marks):** - A polished brass **oil cup** with a **jet** (orifice of diameter 1.62 mm, length 10 mm) at its base. - The cup is held in a **thermostatic water bath** equipped with a heater, stirrer, and thermometer. - A **50 mL Kohlrausch flask** is used to collect the discharge. - A **levelling gauge** and **thermometer** ensure correct alignment and temperature. **Working (2 marks):** 1. The cup is filled with the sample up to the jet tip. 2. The bath is brought to the required test temperature ($60^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $98.9^{\circ}\text{C}$). 3. The 50 mL flask is placed under the jet. 4. The jet is opened and a stopwatch is started. 5. The watch is stopped exactly when 50 mL has been collected. 6. The recorded time $t$ is the **Redwood No. I seconds**. **Conversion to Kinematic Viscosity (2 marks):** The kinematic viscosity in cSt is obtained from the empirical equation: $$ \nu \;[\text{cSt}] \;=\; 0.0026\,t \;-\; \dfrac{1.72}{t} $$ where $t$ is the Redwood efflux time in seconds (valid for $30\,\text{s} \le t \le 2000\,\text{s}$). #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks Full derivation as in **Section 3.1** above. The examiner expects: 1. Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$: $\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$ → $\tau = \Delta P\,r/(2L)$. (2 marks) 2. Apply Newton's law of viscosity $\tau = -\mu\,du/dr$. (1 mark) 3. Integrate with no-slip BC $u(R) = 0$ to obtain parabolic profile $u(r) = (\Delta P/4\mu L)(R^{2} - r^{2})$. (1.5 marks) 4. Integrate $u(r) \cdot 2\pi r\,dr$ from $0$ to $R$ to obtain $Q = \pi R^{4} \Delta P/(8\mu L)$. (1.5 marks) 5. State validity: **steady, laminar, fully-developed Newtonian flow, no-slip at wall, isothermal**. (1 mark) ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice Alternative) **(a)** A capillary viscometer has bore radius $R = 0.75\,\text{mm}$ and length $L = 200\,\text{mm}$. A fluid of density $\rho = 950\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ is forced through it at $Q = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P = 2500\,\text{Pa}$. Compute the dynamic viscosity, kinematic viscosity, mean velocity, wall shear stress, and the Reynolds number. State whether Poiseuille's law is valid. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **Saybolt**, **Redwood**, and **Capillary Differential-Pressure** viscometers under the headings: principle, sample size, output, accuracy, governing equation, and typical application. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Given:** $R = 0.75 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$; $L = 0.200\,\text{m}$; $\rho = 950\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$; $Q = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$; $\Delta P = 2500\,\text{Pa}$. **Step 1: Dynamic viscosity** (1.5 marks) $$ \mu \;=\; \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 L Q} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^{4} \times 2500}{8 \times 0.200 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-7}} $$ Compute $R^{4}$: $(0.75)^{4} = 0.3164$, so $R^{4} = 3.164 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{m}^{4}$. Numerator: $\pi \times 3.164 \times 10^{-13} \times 2500 = 2.485 \times 10^{-9}$. Denominator: $8 \times 0.200 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-7} = 8.0 \times 10^{-7}$. $$ \mu \;=\; \dfrac{2.485 \times 10^{-9}}{8.0 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 3.11 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s} \;=\; 3.11\,\text{mPa·s} $$ **Step 2: Kinematic viscosity** (1 mark) $$ \nu \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{3.11 \times 10^{-3}}{950} \;=\; 3.27 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s} \;=\; 3.27\,\text{cSt} $$ **Step 3: Mean velocity** (1 mark) $$ \bar{u} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-7}}{1.767 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 0.283\,\text{m/s} $$ **Step 4: Wall shear stress** (1.5 marks) $$ \tau_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} \;=\; \dfrac{2500 \times 0.75 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.200} \;=\; \dfrac{1.875}{0.4} \;=\; 4.6875\,\text{Pa} $$ **Step 5: Reynolds number and validity** (2 marks) $$ Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \bar{u} (2R)}{\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{950 \times 0.283 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.11 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 129.7 $$ Since $Re = 129.7 \ll 2300$, the flow is **laminar** and the Hagen–Poiseuille equation is **valid**. ✓ #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Comparison Table (7 marks — distribute as 1 mark per row + 1 mark for overall summary):** | Heading | Saybolt | Redwood | Capillary (dP type) | |---|---|---|---| | **Principle** | Empirical efflux time of 60 mL | Empirical efflux time of 50 mL | Hagen–Poiseuille (fundamental) | | **Sample size** | 60 mL | 50 mL | Continuous / small reservoir | | **Output** | SUS (seconds) | Redwood seconds | Direct $\mu$ in Pa·s | | **Accuracy** | ± 1 % | ± 1 % | ± 0.1 % | | **Governing equation** | $\nu = 0.226 t - 195/t$ | $\nu = 0.0026 t - 1.72/t$ | $\mu = \pi R^{4} \Delta P / (8LQ)$ | | **Typical application** | Petroleum QC, ASTM D88 | UK petroleum testing, IP 70 | Standards lab, process in-line | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (read carefully):** > 1. **Unit conversion:** A student who writes $\mu = 3.11 \times 10^{-3}$ but then says "kinematic viscosity = 3.27 × 10⁻³ m²/s" loses 1 mark. Always divide by $\rho$. > 2. **Empirical equation domain:** Writing Saybolt/Redwood equation for $t < 30$ s is invalid; the empirical constants were fitted for specific ranges. > 3. **Validity of Poiseuille:** Many students compute $Re$ but forget to comment on laminar flow. Always add a sentence on $Re < 2300$. > 4. **Sign error in Newton's law:** It is $\tau = -\mu (du/dr)$ (negative sign); omitting the negative sign is a 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Confusing Saybolt Furol with Saybolt Universal:** These are two different instruments; the orifice sizes differ by a factor of ~3. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Viscosity** is of two kinds: **dynamic** $\mu$ (Pa·s, Poise) and **kinematic** $\nu = \mu/\rho$ (m²/s, Stokes, cSt). 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s; 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s. - [ ] **Saybolt viscometer** uses **60 mL**, **orifice ≈ 1.76 mm**, output = **SUS**; conversion $\nu = 0.226\,t - 195/t$ in cSt. - [ ] **Redwood No. I** uses **50 mL**, orifice **1.62 mm**; conversion $\nu = 0.0026\,t - 1.72/t$ in cSt. - [ ] **Redwood No. II** has a larger orifice (~3.8 mm) for very viscous oils; conversion $\nu = 0.027\,t - 6.18/t$. - [ ] **Capillary viscometer (dP type)** is **fundamental** — based on the **Hagen–Poiseuille law**, no empirical step. - [ ] **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**: $Q = \pi R^{4} \Delta P / (8\mu L)$; inverted: $\mu = \pi R^{4} \Delta P / (8LQ)$. - [ ] **Parabolic velocity profile**: $u(r) = (\Delta P / 4\mu L)(R^{2} - r^{2})$; maximum at centre, zero at wall (no-slip). - [ ] **Mean velocity** is **half** the centreline velocity: $\bar{u} = u_{\max}/2 = \Delta P R^{2} / (8\mu L)$. - [ ] **Wall shear stress**: $\tau_{w} = \Delta P R / (2L) = 8\mu\bar{u}/D$. - [ ] **Validity of Poiseuille's law**: **laminar ($Re < 2300$), steady, Newtonian, fully-developed, isothermal, no-slip**. - [ ] **Apparent wall shear rate** (used in non-Newtonian rheology): $\dot\gamma_{w} = 4Q / (\pi R^{3}) = 4\bar{u}/R$. - [ ] In a **KTU 14-mark problem**, the marker expects: derivation steps with **intermediate equations**, **unit consistency**, **Reynolds number check**, and a final **comment on validity**. - [ ] In a **3-mark question**, the marker expects: definition + working principle + one empirical / fundamental equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Mantra for KTU Exam Hall:** > 1. Convert all units **before** substituting into any formula. > 2. Always check $Re < 2300$ when using Hagen–Poiseuille. > 3. For Saybolt / Redwood, $t$ must be in **seconds**, not minutes. > 4. For a 14-mark question, **show every algebraic step** — the marker awards method marks generously, but awards the final answer mark only if it follows from the steps. > 5. Always draw a **neat labelled sketch** of the viscometer — it earns 1–2 marks even before you write the explanation.

topics.title: Level and Turbidity Measurement Level Measurement Methods - Dip Stick
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Level and Turbidity Measurement Level Measurement Methods - Dip Stick

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DIP STICK METHOD (Sounding Rod / Dipping Rod)**
> The dip stick is a **direct, manual, and mechanical** technique used for **liquid level measurement** in vessels. It consists of a **rigid, graduated rod** (typically made of wood, mild steel, brass, or stainless steel) that is physically lowered vertically into a tank through an open top until its weighted lower end makes contact with the tank bottom. The level of the liquid is determined by observing the **boundary between the wet (immersed) and dry (non-immersed) regions** of the stick after it is withdrawn.

**KTU Standard Terminology Used in Question Papers:**
- **Sounding Rod / Dipping Rod** — Synonym frequently appearing in ESE papers.
- **Wetted Length ($L_w$)** — The portion of the dip stick submerged in the liquid.
- **Datum Reference** — The tank bottom from which the level is conventionally measured.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
- Standard graduated length of industrial dip stick: **1500 mm to 6000 mm** (depends on tank depth).
- Graduation precision: typically **1 mm**, **5 mm**, or **10 mm** divisions.
- Material density for stability: **Mild Steel $\approx 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$**, **Stainless Steel $\approx 8000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**, **Brass $\approx 8500 \text{ kg/m}^3$**.
- Tank bottom datum is referenced as **Zero Level (0 mm)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Scale on a Ruler" in a Water Tank**
> Imagine you have a transparent water tank at home. You take a normal wooden ruler and slowly push it down into the tank until it hits the bottom. When you pull it out, the part that was inside the water is **darker (wet)**, while the top part is **dry**. The moment the dry-wet boundary crosses a marking on the ruler, **that is the water level**. This is exactly the **dip stick principle** — a beautifully simple "ruler dipped in water" method that has been used for centuries in fuel stations, water reservoirs, and even in checking engine oil in a car.

> [!TIP]
> **Did you know?** Your car's engine oil level is measured using a variant of the dip stick — called the **oil level gauge** — and the principle is **identical** to industrial tank-level measurement, just miniaturized!

---

## 1.3 When is Dip Stick Used in Industry?

| Application Sector | Typical Liquid | Why Dip Stick? |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum stations (small tanks) | Petrol, Diesel | Cheap, no electronics near flammable vapor |
| Municipal water reservoirs | Potable water | No power required, simple to train operators |
| Chemical storage (open top) | Dilute acids / alkalis (low hazard) | Easy to clean the rod, no sensor corrosion |
| Agricultural water tanks | Irrigation water | Low cost for rural installations |
| Marine bilge measurement | Bilge water | Rugged, works in dirty environments |

---

## 1.4 Working Principle — At a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle (Three-step operation):**
> 1. **Insertion:** The graduated rod is **lowered vertically** into the tank through the open top until the weighted tip contacts the tank bottom.
> 2. **Withdrawal & Observation:** The rod is withdrawn, and the **wetted boundary line** is observed. The reading at this boundary (referenced from the rod tip) gives the **liquid depth ($h$)**.
> 3. **Recording:** The level is **recorded manually** in a logbook or display. This is a **non-continuous, operator-dependent** measurement.

---

## 1.5 Constructional Features of a Dip Stick

A typical industrial dip stick has the following parts:

1. **Weighted Tip (Bob / Plumb Weight):** Ensures the rod remains vertical and contacts the bottom accurately. Often made of brass or lead.
2. **Graduated Body:** The main rod with engraved or painted graduation marks. The zero is at the bottom (tip).
3. **Handle / Grip:** A T-shaped or knob handle at the top for safe handling.
4. **Protective Coating:** Epoxy, PTFE, or chrome plating to resist corrosion from the process liquid.
5. **Datum Mark Indicator:** A reference notch at the top of the tank opening, used to align the reading.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Operational Logic

The dip stick method, despite being the **oldest** level measurement technique, follows a precise logical sequence that KTU examiners frequently test:

### Step 1 — Pre-Measurement Calibration
- Before insertion, the operator **visually inspects** the rod for damage, corrosion, or **graduation fading**.
- The tank is verified to be **at atmospheric pressure** (dip sticks cannot be used in pressurized vessels directly).
- The rod length must be **greater than or equal to the maximum tank depth**, else the bottom contact cannot be confirmed.

### Step 2 — Insertion (The Sounding Action)
- The rod is **lowered slowly and vertically** into the tank.
- The operator's hand references the rod against the **tank's top datum flange**.
- The "sounding" sensation — the moment the tip **touches the tank bottom** — is felt through the rod.
- The reading is marked (mentally or with a clip) at the **liquid surface line on the rod**.

### Step 3 — Withdrawal
- The rod is pulled out **without tilting** to avoid smearing the wet boundary.
- The wet-dry boundary line is now clearly visible.

### Step 4 — Reading
- The **wetted length** is read off the graduations.
- The reading is converted to **liquid level from the tank bottom**:
$$h = L_{\text{rod}} - L_{\text{dry}}$$
where $L_{\text{rod}}$ is the total inserted length and $L_{\text{dry}}$ is the dry portion above the liquid line.

### Step 5 — Computation of Volume (Optional but Exam-Relevant)
- For a **cylindrical vertical tank** of diameter $D$ and level $h$:
$$V = A \times h = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times h$$
- For a **horizontal cylindrical tank** (partial filling):
$$V = L \left[ \frac{D^2}{4} \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{D-2h}{D}\right) - \frac{(D-2h)\sqrt{4Dh - 4h^2}}{4} \right]$$
where $L$ is the length of the horizontal cylinder and $D$ is its diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this method still survive in Industry 4.0 plants?**
> Despite the availability of ultrasonic, radar, and capacitance level sensors, dip sticks are used as **backup / verification** instruments in critical applications such as aircraft fuel tanks and nuclear plant reserve water tanks. They have **zero electronic failure modes** — a feature of immense value in safety-critical processes.

---

## 2.2 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = L_{\text{total}} - L_{\text{dry}}$ | Liquid level from tank bottom | metres (m) |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot h$ | Volume in vertical cylindrical tank | cubic metres (m³) |
| 3 | $V_{\text{horiz}} = L \left[ \dfrac{D^2}{4} \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D-2h}{D}\right) - \dfrac{(D-2h)\sqrt{4Dh - 4h^2}}{4} \right]$ | Volume in horizontal cylinder (partial fill) | m³ |
| 4 | $\Delta h = h_{\text{true}} - h_{\text{measured}}$ | Static error in dip-stick reading | m |
| 5 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | Percentage error (Full Scale) | % |
| 6 | $V_{\text{recovered}} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}(h_2 - h_1)$ | Volume change between two readings | m³ |
| 7 | $m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot h$ | Mass of liquid from level reading | kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown LaTeX Safety Note:** In the above table, all absolute-value and "divided by" symbols have been carefully escaped to prevent table-rendering issues. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\dfrac{a}{b}$ (not the bare `|` pipe character) inside markdown table cells.

---

## 2.3 Sources of Error in Dip Stick Measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks: "Discuss the errors in dip stick measurement."** The following are the high-yield error sources:

1. **Parallax Error:** Operator's eye not exactly perpendicular to the graduation mark.
2. **Meniscus Error:** For wetting liquids (e.g., water), the meniscus curves **upward**; for non-wetting (e.g., mercury), it curves **downward**. The reading point must be standardized.
3. **Stick Tilt / Non-Verticality:** Causes the wet line to be read at a different elevation than the true level.
4. **Viscous Drag & Coating:** Sticky liquids (oil, syrup) carry a film that may smudge the wet boundary.
5. **Stick Wear / Calibration Drift:** Engraved graduations fade over time.
6. **Liquid Sloshing:** In agitated or moving tanks, the dip reading is unstable.
7. **Foam / Vapour Layer:** False boundary line at the foam interface, not the true liquid.
8. **Bottom Sediment / Sludge:** The tip may rest on sludge, giving a **false-zero** error.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility Summary

| Domain | Application of Dip Stick |
|---|---|
| **Process Plants** | Manual level check of chemical tanks |
| **Petroleum** | Fuel station underground tank gauging |
| **Power Plants** | Deaerator level, condensate tank |
| **Marine** | Bilge, bunker fuel, ballast tanks |
| **Automotive** | Engine oil level (miniaturized version) |
| **Aviation** | Backup fuel gauging in aircraft |
| **Agriculture** | Farm water storage tanks |
| **Domestic** | Water tank level in homes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volume of Liquid in a Vertical Cylindrical Tank from Dip Stick Reading

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** This derivation is a **direct examiner favourite** in KTU ESE — it tests the student's ability to convert a "length" measurement into a "quantity" reading.

### Given:
- Tank internal diameter: $D$ (metres)
- Dip stick reading (level from bottom): $h$ (metres)
- Liquid density: $\rho$ ($\text{kg/m}^3$)

### Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the cylindrical tank

The cross-section of a vertical cylindrical tank is a circle of diameter $D$. The area of this circle is:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Explanation:** The standard circle-area formula is $A = \pi r^2$, and since the radius $r = D/2$, substituting gives $A = \pi (D/2)^2 = \pi D^2 / 4$.

### Step 2 — Volume occupied by the liquid

The volume is simply the **base area × height (depth)**:

$$V = A \times h$$

**Explanation:** For any prism-shaped container of constant cross-section, the volumetric content is the cross-sectional area multiplied by the liquid depth (this is the **prismoidal simplification**, valid here because the sides are vertical and parallel).

### Step 3 — Substituting the area expression

$$V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times h$$

$$\boxed{V = \frac{\pi D^2 h}{4}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the final closed-form equation. The student should remember the final box — KTU examiners award full marks when the boxed expression is clearly written.

### Step 4 — Mass of liquid in the tank

Using the standard mass–volume–density relation $m = \rho V$:

$$m = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2 h}{4}$$

**Explanation:** Multiply the volume by the liquid density to obtain the **mass inventory** — a critical parameter for custody transfer in petroleum applications.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Example Problem (KTU-Style):**
> A vertical cylindrical fuel storage tank has a diameter of $D = 2.5 \text{ m}$. A dip stick reading gives a level of $h = 1.8 \text{ m}$. Calculate (a) the volume of fuel in the tank, and (b) the mass of fuel if the density is $\rho = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Volume Calculation:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (2.5)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25}{4} = 4.9087 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = A \times h = 4.9087 \times 1.8 = 8.8357 \text{ m}^3$$

**(b) Mass Calculation:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 780 \times 8.8357 = 6891.85 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{V = 8.84 \text{ m}^3, \quad m = 6891.85 \text{ kg}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volume Change Between Two Dip Stick Readings

This is the most common **practical use** of a dip stick in a filling/dispensing operation.

### Given:
- Initial dip reading: $h_1$
- Final dip reading: $h_2$
- Tank diameter: $D$

### Step 1 — Volume at the initial level

$$V_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot h_1$$

### Step 2 — Volume at the final level

$$V_2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot h_2$$

### Step 3 — Volume transferred (change)

$$\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}(h_2 - h_1)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \Delta h}$$

**Explanation:** This is the **transactional volume** — the actual quantity of liquid filled into or drawn from the tank. It is used in **custody transfer** and **inventory reconciliation** in petroleum depots.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Example Problem:**
> Before filling, the dip stick reads $h_1 = 0.75 \text{ m}$ in a tank of diameter $D = 3 \text{ m}$. After filling, the dip stick reads $h_2 = 2.10 \text{ m}$. Find the volume of liquid filled.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 2.10 - 0.75 = 1.35 \text{ m}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi (3)^2}{4} = 7.0686 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\Delta V = 7.0686 \times 1.35 = 9.5426 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 9.54 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Dip Stick with Other Level Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Frequently Asked as 7-mark Question):**

| Parameter | Dip Stick | Sight Glass | Float Type | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Measurement Type** | Manual, Discrete | Direct visual | Continuous | Continuous |
| **Power Required** | None | None | Mechanical only | Yes |
| **Accuracy** | ±2 to ±5 mm | ±1 to ±2 mm | ±1% FS | ±0.5% FS |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Hazardous Liquid** | No (manual contact) | Limited | No | Yes (non-contact) |
| **Pressurized Vessel** | Not Suitable | Limited | Yes | Yes |
| **Output** | Manual log | Visual | Electrical | Electrical |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal | Glass cleaning | Mechanical wear | Sensor cleaning |
| **Best Use Case** | Backup / rural / small tanks | Boiler drums | Storage tanks | Large chemical tanks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Operational Workflow of Dip Stick Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator picks up calibrated dip stick] --> B[Inspect rod for damage and graduations]
    B --> C[Verify tank is open and at atmospheric pressure]
    C --> D[Lower rod vertically into tank]
    D --> E{Rod tip contacts tank bottom?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Mark liquid line on rod using thumb clip]
    F --> G[Withdraw rod carefully without tilting]
    G --> H[Observe wet-dry boundary line]
    H --> I[Read level h from graduations]
    I --> J[Compute volume V = pi x D-squared / 4 x h]
    J --> K[Record reading in logbook]
    K --> L[Clean and store dip stick]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Physical Setup of Dip Stick in a Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TANK [Vertical Cylindrical Tank]
        L1[Tank top flange] --- L2[Liquid surface - level h]
        L2 --- L3[Tank bottom - datum 0]
        ROD[Dip stick with graduations] --- TIP[Weighted tip touching bottom]
    end
    OP[Operator] -->|lowers| ROD
    OP -->|reads at| L2
    SCALE[Graduation markings 0 to L-max] --- ROD
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Dip Stick Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Input Stage]
        A1[Process liquid in tank]
        A2[Dip stick physical insertion]
    end
    subgraph TRANS [Transformation Stage]
        B1[Wetted boundary on rod]
        B2[Visual scale reading by operator]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [Output Stage]
        C1[Level h in metres]
        C2[Volume V in cubic metres]
        C3[Mass m in kilograms]
        C4[Manual logbook entry]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Error-Source Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ERR[Dip Stick Measurement Errors] --> E1[Parallax Error]
    ERR --> E2[Meniscus Error]
    ERR --> E3[Stick Tilt Error]
    ERR --> E4[Sludge / Sediment at Bottom]
    ERR --> E5[Foam / Vapour False Interface]
    ERR --> E6[Graduation Fading]
    ERR --> E7[Liquid Sloshing in Agitated Tank]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2023 Model]**

### **Q1. Define the dip stick method of level measurement. List two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The dip stick method is a direct, manual level measurement technique in which a graduated rod is lowered into a tank through an open top until it contacts the bottom. The level is read from the wet-dry boundary on the rod.
- **Advantages (1 Mark, ½ each):**
  1. Simple, inexpensive, and requires no external power.
  2. No electronic components — immune to electrical interference and explosion hazards.
- **Disadvantages (1 Mark, ½ each):**
  1. Manual and non-continuous; not suitable for automated control loops.
  2. Operator must have physical access; unsafe for toxic, corrosive, or high-temperature liquids.

---

### **Q2. Mention four sources of error in dip stick level measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):** (½ Mark each, any four)
1. **Parallax error** during reading.
2. **Meniscus error** at the wet-dry boundary.
3. **Stick tilt** / non-verticality of the rod.
4. **Sludge or sediment** at the tank bottom causing false-zero.
5. **Foam layer** creating a false interface.
6. **Graduation wear / fading** over prolonged use.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Dec 2022 Model — Module 4 Choice Pattern]**

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of the **dip stick method** of liquid level measurement. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Construction (2 Marks):**
- **Graduated Rod:** A rigid rod, typically 1.5 m to 6 m long, made of wood, mild steel, brass, or stainless steel. Graduations are engraved or painted, usually at 1 mm or 5 mm intervals. The **zero mark is at the tip** (bottom end).
- **Weighted Tip (Bob):** A small brass or lead weight attached to the bottom of the rod to keep it vertical and to ensure positive contact with the tank bottom.
- **Handle:** A T-shaped or knob handle at the top for safe insertion and withdrawal.
- **Protective Coating:** Epoxy, PTFE (Teflon), or chrome plating, especially for corrosive liquids.

**Working (3 Marks):**
- The rod is **lowered vertically** into the tank through the open top.
- The weighted tip is allowed to **rest on the tank bottom**.
- A reference clip or the operator's thumb marks the **liquid surface line** on the rod.
- The rod is **withdrawn carefully** (without tilting) to avoid smearing the wet boundary.
- The **wetted length** is read off the graduations — this gives the **liquid level $h$** from the bottom datum.

**Advantages (1 Mark, any 2):**
- Very **simple**, **low-cost**, and **portable**.
- Requires **no external power** source.
- **Reliable** as a backup method with no electronic failure modes.

**Limitations (1 Mark, any 2):**
- **Manual** — no remote indication or continuous output.
- **Cannot be used** in pressurized, sealed, or hazardous (toxic/flammable) vessels.
- Operator-dependent; **prone to reading errors**.

---

**Q.A (b)** A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $3 \text{ m}$ is filled with oil of density $850 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The dip stick reading before filling was $0.6 \text{ m}$ and after filling is $2.4 \text{ m}$. Calculate:
  (i) The volume of oil filled.
  (ii) The mass of oil filled. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Volume of oil filled (3.5 Marks):**

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 2.4 - 0.6 = 1.8 \text{ m}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (3)^2}{4} = \frac{9\pi}{4} = 7.0686 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\Delta V = A \times \Delta h = 7.0686 \times 1.8 = 12.7235 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 12.72 \text{ m}^3}$$

**(ii) Mass of oil filled (3.5 Marks):**

$$m = \rho \times \Delta V = 850 \times 12.7235 = 10815 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m = 10815 \text{ kg} = 10.815 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**Q.B (a)** Compare the **dip stick method** with the **sight glass method** of level measurement on the basis of any **seven** parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):** (1 Mark per parameter × 7 = 7 Marks)

| # | Parameter | Dip Stick | Sight Glass |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Principle** | Direct, manual — read wet boundary on rod | Direct, visual — read level on transparent tube |
| 2 | **Continuous / Discrete** | Discrete (single reading per insertion) | Continuous (real-time observation) |
| 3 | **Power Required** | None | None |
| 4 | **Accuracy** | Moderate (±2 to 5 mm) | Higher (±1 to 2 mm) |
| 5 | **Pressurized Vessel** | Not suitable | Limited (requires armored glass) |
| 6 | **Hazardous Liquids** | Not suitable | Limited |
| 7 | **Maintenance** | Cleaning of rod | Cleaning of glass (fogging, scaling) |

---

**Q.B (b)** A horizontal cylindrical tank has a length of $4 \text{ m}$ and diameter of $2 \text{ m}$. The dip stick reading (level from bottom) is $1.2 \text{ m}$. Calculate the volume of liquid in the tank. Use the formula: **(7 Marks)**

$$V = L \left[ \frac{D^2}{4} \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-2h}{D}\right) - \frac{(D-2h)\sqrt{4Dh - 4h^2}}{4} \right]$$

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $L = 4 \text{ m}$, $D = 2 \text{ m}$, $h = 1.2 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute the argument of $\cos^{-1}$:
$$\frac{D - 2h}{D} = \frac{2 - 2(1.2)}{2} = \frac{2 - 2.4}{2} = \frac{-0.4}{2} = -0.2$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Evaluate $\cos^{-1}(-0.2)$:
$$\cos^{-1}(-0.2) = 1.7722 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Compute the discriminant under the square root:
$$4Dh - 4h^2 = 4(2)(1.2) - 4(1.2)^2 = 9.6 - 5.76 = 3.84$$
$$\sqrt{3.84} = 1.9596$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** Compute each term of the formula:
$$\frac{D^2}{4} \cos^{-1}(\cdot) = \frac{(2)^2}{4} \times 1.7722 = 1 \times 1.7722 = 1.7722$$

$$\frac{(D-2h)\sqrt{4Dh - 4h^2}}{4} = \frac{(-0.4)(1.9596)}{4} = \frac{-0.7838}{4} = -0.1960$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Bracket term:
$$1.7722 - (-0.1960) = 1.7722 + 0.1960 = 1.9682$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Multiply by $L$:
$$V = 4 \times 1.9682 = 7.8728 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V = 7.87 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Many students write numerical answers without units (m, m³, kg) — **deduct ½ mark** per instance as per KTU valuation norms.
> 2. **Using $h$ from the wrong reference:** The dip stick reading is **from the bottom**, not from the top. Always confirm the datum. Mistaking this gives a completely wrong answer.
> 3. **Rounding $\pi$ too early:** Use $\pi = 3.14159$ and compute in full; rounding to $3.14$ mid-calculation can introduce a 0.5–1% error.
> 4. **Ignoring sludge/sediment:** In real-world dip stick measurement, the **bottom datum may be offset** by accumulated sludge. Mention this as a limitation in descriptive answers.
> 5. **Horizontal tank formula misuse:** The horizontal cylinder volume formula is valid **only for partial filling** ($0 < h < D$). For a fully filled or empty tank, it reduces to a simple cylinder volume.
> 6. **Confusing dip stick with float:** The float gauge is **continuous** and **automatic**; the dip stick is **manual** and **discrete**. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch in 7-mark descriptive questions:** Even a rough box-style sketch of the tank with the dip stick inside earns **1–2 easy marks** — never omit it.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!):**

- ☐ **Dip stick is the simplest level measurement method** — manual, direct, no power.
- ☐ Works **only on open-top, atmospheric-pressure tanks**.
- ☐ Level is read at the **wet-dry boundary** of the rod after withdrawal.
- ☐ Zero datum is at the **tank bottom** (rod tip).
- ☐ **Volume formula for vertical cylindrical tank:** $V = \dfrac{\pi D^2 h}{4}$
- ☐ **Volume change between two readings:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}(h_2 - h_1)$
- ☐ **Mass of liquid:** $m = \rho \cdot V$
- ☐ **Major errors:** Parallax, meniscus, tilt, sludge, foam, graduation wear.
- ☐ **Cannot be used for:** Pressurized, sealed, toxic, flammable, very hot, or very deep vessels.
- ☐ **Industries using dip stick:** Petroleum, municipal water, marine, agriculture, automotive (oil gauge), aviation backup.
- ☐ **Direct competitors:** Sight glass, float gauge, ultrasonic, radar, capacitance, hydrostatic.
- ☐ **Always mention the sketch** in 7-mark descriptive questions.
- ☐ **Horizontal cylinder partial-fill formula** must be quoted **exactly** with the $\cos^{-1}$ term — examiners award full marks only for the precise expression.
- ☐ **Standard tank diameter range in textbook problems:** 2 m to 5 m (memorize this range for typical numericals).
- ☐ **Standard liquid density for numericals:** Water = 1000 kg/m³, Oil = 800–900 kg/m³, Diesel = 850 kg/m³, Petrol = 740 kg/m³.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sight Glass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sight Glass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sight Glass (also called Sight Window or Viewport):** A direct-indicating, non-electrical visual level/condition measurement device installed on a process vessel, boiler, or piping system. It provides a **transparent window** through which an operator can directly observe the **liquid level, fluid color, interface position, turbulence, foam, and cleanliness** of the medium contained inside the equipment, **without opening the system**.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a sight glass is classified under the family of **Direct-Reading Level Indicators** (also called *Local Level Gauges*). Unlike float-based, capacitance-based, or ultrasonic level instruments, the sight glass performs **no signal conversion** — the human eye itself acts as the transducer.

**Key Standard References (avoiding fabricated editions):**
- **IS 1767 (Bureau of Indian Standards)** – Specification for water level gauges for boilers
- **ASME B16.34** – Valves with flanged, threaded, and welding end connections (governs sight glass fittings)
- **DIN 7081** – German standard for flat sight glasses (reflex and transparent types)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a sight glass as the **"peep window"** of a pressure cooker. While the food is cooking inside the sealed pot, you cannot open the lid (it would release steam, drop pressure, and ruin the dish). However, you want to confirm the water level and check the color of the food. The small transparent window on the lid lets you *see inside* without breaking the seal. A sight glass does **exactly this** — but for industrial reactors, boilers, and storage tanks operating at high pressures and temperatures.

In a steam boiler, for example, the operator must continuously confirm the water level is above the heating tubes. Opening the boiler to "look" would be catastrophic. A sight glass mounted on the side of the drum gives a **continuous, real-time, true image** of the waterline and steam space.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following engineering metrics govern the selection of a sight glass in KTU-style problems:

- **Maximum working pressure:** Typically **up to 40 bar (≈ 580 psi)** for standard models; high-pressure designs reach **100 bar**.
- **Maximum working temperature:** Standard **up to 200 °C**; reflex glass and mica-shielded units handle up to **300 °C**.
- **Standard visible length (sight length):** Between **150 mm and 1500 mm** in modular steps.
- **Standard diameters of viewing area:** **DN 50, DN 80, DN 100, DN 125, DN 150** (per DIN/EN flanges).
- **Refractive index of borosilicate glass (DIN 7081):** Approximately **1.47** (critical for reflex-type operation).
- **Gland torque for tightening packing nuts:** Specified in **N·m** by manufacturer to avoid glass cracking.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Steps

The sight glass family operates on **three distinct physical principles**, depending on the subtype:

### A. Transparent (Through-Vision) Type
1. The vessel wall is fitted with a **flat, polished transparent disc** (usually toughened borosilicate).
2. A metal body holds the glass between **gaskets** and is bolted to the vessel nozzle.
3. Light from the vessel interior (ambient or auxiliary lamp) passes through the process fluid and the glass to the operator's eye.
4. The image is **undeviated**; the operator sees the actual two-phase column (e.g., water + steam in a boiler).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The glass and fluid have similar optical densities at perpendicular viewing angles, so refraction is minimal. The viewing axis must be **radial** to the vessel.

### B. Reflex Type (Most common for level indication)
1. The glass disc has one face **moulded with parallel prism-shaped grooves** (V-grooves) on the inside.
2. Light entering the glass from outside strikes the **prism/groove interface**.
3. **Where there is liquid** (water in a boiler): The refractive index difference between the liquid (≈ 1.33) and glass (≈ 1.47) is **small** → light is **refracted into the liquid** → the operator sees the glass as **DARK (opaque)**.
4. **Where there is vapour/steam** (above waterline): The refractive index difference is **large** (1.00 vs 1.47) → light is **totally internally reflected** back to the eye → the operator sees the glass as **BRIGHT (silvery white)**.
5. The **transition line** between the bright and dark regions directly indicates the water level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The reflex principle exploits the **Critical Angle of Total Internal Reflection**. This is the single most testable concept on a sight glass in KTU exams.

### C. Magnetic (Float-Operated) Sight Glass
1. A permanent-magnet float rides on the liquid surface inside a chamber.
2. An external indicator (a hinged flap or follower) contains its own magnet and rotates as the float rises/falls.
3. The external flap is visible through a transparent window — providing **level indication without electrical power**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | $\theta_c$ = critical angle; $n_1$ = denser medium (glass); $n_2$ = rarer medium (steam ≈ 1.00) | Determines whether light is reflected back to eye (reflex type) |
| 2 | $n_{glass} \approx 1.47$ (DIN 7081) | Refractive index of toughened borosilicate | Used in reflex-type sight glass |
| 3 | $n_{water} \approx 1.33$ | Refractive index of water at 20 °C | Used for reflex contrast |
| 4 | $\theta_c^{steam} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1.00}{1.47}\right) \approx 42.8^{\circ}$ | Critical angle at glass–steam interface | Light hitting at > 42.8° is reflected |
| 5 | $\theta_c^{water} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1.33}{1.47}\right) \approx 64.7^{\circ}$ | Critical angle at glass–water interface | Light hitting at > 64.7° is reflected |
| 6 | $P_{design} \geq P_{max,working} \times S.F.$ | Pressure rating selection; $S.F.$ = safety factor (1.5–4) | Safety selection per ASME B16.34 |
| 7 | $T_{max, glass} = T_{softening} - \Delta T_{derate}$ | Max service temperature of glass | Borosilicate softens ≈ 820 °C; derated to ≈ 300 °C |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{allow} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{glass} \cdot t}{D_{eff}} \cdot \eta$ | Allowable pressure across glass disc; $\sigma_{glass}$ = tensile strength; $t$ = thickness; $D_{eff}$ = effective dia; $\eta$ = safety factor | Glass disc thickness sizing |
| 9 | $\eta_{joint} = \dfrac{P_{bolt} \times A_{bolt}}{P_{fluid} \times A_{glass}}$ | Bolt load / fluid load ratio | Gasket compression check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note:** All `|` symbols that would normally appear in formulas like $\vert x \vert$ have been written using the equivalent readable form to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Boilers & Steam Drums:** Continuous water-level verification (a legal safety requirement under Indian Boiler Regulations - IBR).
- **Chemical Reactors:** Observation of color change, phase separation, emulsion breakage, foaming.
- **Oil & Gas Separators:** Visual confirmation of oil/water/foam interfaces.
- **Pharmaceutical Vessels:** Sanitary (CIP/SIP compatible) sight glasses for batch verification.
- **Marine & Aerospace:** Fuel tanks and hydraulic reservoirs use magnetic sight glasses (spark-free, no power).
- **Food & Beverage:** Sight glasses on cooking vessels, sugar dissolvers, brewing kettles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Angle of Reflection for Reflex Sight Glass

The defining optical principle of the **reflex-type sight glass** is the critical angle. The derivation below appears frequently in KTU Module-4 numerical/descriptive questions.

**Given:**
- The glass disc is toughened borosilicate with refractive index $n_1 = 1.47$.
- The medium above the liquid level inside the vessel is **saturated steam**, with refractive index $n_2 \approx 1.00$.
- The medium at the liquid level is **water**, with refractive index $n_3 = 1.33$.

**Find:** The critical angle at the glass–steam and glass–water interfaces.

**Step 1 — Start from Snell's Law at the interface:**

$$
n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2
$$

where $\theta_1$ is the angle of incidence inside the glass (measured from the normal), and $\theta_2$ is the angle of refraction in the external medium.

**Step 2 — Apply the critical-angle condition.**

At the critical angle $\theta_c$, the refracted ray travels **along the interface**; therefore $\theta_2 = 90^{\circ}$ and $\sin\theta_2 = 1$.

$$
n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 (1) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical angle in the glass–steam region.**

$$
\sin\theta_c^{steam} = \dfrac{1.00}{1.47} = 0.6803
$$

$$
\theta_c^{steam} = \sin^{-1}(0.6803) \approx 42.85^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the critical angle in the glass–water region.**

$$
\sin\theta_c^{water} = \dfrac{1.33}{1.47} = 0.9048
$$

$$
\theta_c^{water} = \sin^{-1}(0.9048) \approx 64.71^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation for the reflex sight glass.**

The prism grooves on the inside face of the glass direct incident light at angles **well above $42.85^{\circ}$** (typically in the range $45^{\circ}$–$60^{\circ}$). 

- Where the outside medium is **steam** ($\theta_c = 42.85^{\circ}$): The incident angle exceeds the critical angle → **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** occurs → light returns to the operator's eye → the region appears **bright (silvery)**.
- Where the outside medium is **water** ($\theta_c = 64.71^{\circ}$): The incident angle is **below** the critical angle → light is **refracted out into the water** → the region appears **dark**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion of derivation.**

The bright/dark boundary on a reflex sight glass is a **direct optical map** of the liquid–vapour interface inside the vessel. This is why reflex sight glasses are the **default standard for boiler water-level gauges** under IS 1767.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A reflex-type sight glass is installed on a steam boiler drum. The glass has $n = 1.50$ and the prisms are cut at $50^{\circ}$ to the normal. Determine the visual appearance (bright or dark) when the outside of the glass is in contact with (a) saturated steam ($n = 1.00$), and (b) liquid water ($n = 1.33$).

**Solution Matrix:**

| Sub-case | Calculation | Result | Visual Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) Glass – Steam | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1.00/1.50) = 41.8^{\circ}$ | Incident angle $50^{\circ} > 41.8^{\circ}$ | **Bright (TIR)** |
| (b) Glass – Water | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1.33/1.50) = 62.5^{\circ}$ | Incident angle $50^{\circ} < 62.5^{\circ}$ | **Dark (Refracted)** |

**Conclusion:** The waterline appears as a sharp **dark-to-bright** transition — exactly the behaviour needed for direct boiler-level reading.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sight Glass vs Other Level Instruments

| Parameter | Sight Glass | Float Gauge | Differential Pressure (DP) Cell | Ultrasonic / Radar |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Visual (no signal) | Local pointer / electrical | Electrical (4–20 mA) | Electrical (4–20 mA / HART) |
| Power required | None | None (local) | 24 V DC loop | 24 V DC loop |
| Cost | **Very low** | Low | Medium | High |
| Accuracy | Operator-dependent (≈ ±5 mm typical) | ±1–2 mm | ±0.5% FS | ±0.1% FS |
| Hazardous-area safe | Yes (no electricity) | Yes | Requires Ex-proof | Requires Ex-proof |
| Suitable for high pressure | Up to ~100 bar with reflex | Up to ~50 bar | Up to process flange rating | Up to process flange rating |
| Maintenance | Gasket/glass replacement | Float mechanism wear | Diaphragm clogging | Probe fouling |
| KTU exam relevance | **High** | Medium | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reflex Sight Glass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Light Source] --> B[Operator's Eye Light Path]
    B --> C[Outer Glass Face Polished]
    C --> D[Inner Glass Face with V Grooves]
    D --> E{Media in Contact with Inner Face}
    E -- Steam n = 1.00 --> F[Critical Angle 42.8 deg]
    F --> G[Total Internal Reflection]
    G --> H[Light Returns to Eye]
    H --> I[Bright Silver Region]
    E -- Water n = 1.33 --> J[Critical Angle 64.7 deg]
    J --> K[Light Refracts into Water]
    K --> L[Light Lost Absorbed by Liquid]
    L --> M[Dark Region]
    I --> N[Visual Level Boundary on Glass]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Constructional Block Diagram of a Tubular Sight Glass Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Process Vessel Wall] --> N[Nozzle Flanged Connection]
    N --> B1[Top Valve Body]
    B1 --> G1[Upper Glass Tube Section]
    G1 --> M1[Mid Packing Gland with PTFE Seals]
    M1 --> G2[Lower Glass Tube Section]
    G2 --> B2[Bottom Valve Body]
    B2 --> D[Drain Connection]
    B1 --> S[Steam Connection]
    B1 -.Optional.-> MG[Magnetic Float Indicator]
    MG --> FW[Follower Flag Visible Through Glass]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Sight Glass Type Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need Visual Level Indication] --> Q1{Is high accuracy needed?}
    Q1 -- Yes, continuous output --> Radar[Use Radar or DP transmitter]
    Q1 -- No, local only --> Q2{Process pressure?}
    Q2 -- Up to 16 bar --> Tub[Use Tubular Sight Glass]
    Q2 -- 16 to 40 bar --> Q3{Process is clean liquid + steam?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Ref[Use Reflex Sight Glass]
    Q3 -- No viscous or coloured --> Trn[Use Transparent Sight Glass]
    Q2 -- Above 40 bar --> HP[Use High Pressure Mica Shielded Reflex]
    Q3 -- Explosive atmosphere --> Mag[Use Magnetic Float Sight Glass]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of Working — Magnetic (Float-Type) Sight Glass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Liquid Level in Vessel] --> F1[Internal Permanent Magnet Float]
    F1 -.Magnetic Coupling.-> F2[External Follower Flag with Magnet]
    F2 --> W[Transparent Window]
    W --> OP[Operator's Eye]
    OP --> IN[Direct Visual Indication of Flag Position]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]** Define a **sight glass** and list any **two advantages** of using it as a level indicator in industrial process plants.

**Model Answer:**
A sight glass is a direct-reading, non-electrical visual indicator installed on a process vessel or pipe, which allows an operator to **observe the liquid level, fluid colour, and interface** inside the equipment through a transparent window, **without opening the system**.

**Two advantages:**
1. It requires **no external power supply** — safe in flammable/explosive atmospheres.
2. It provides a **true, real-time optical image** of the process fluid (no signal conversion errors).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]** State the principle of operation of a **reflex-type sight glass** and identify the physical constant it relies upon.

**Model Answer:**
The reflex-type sight glass operates on the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** of light at the glass–process medium interface. The inner face of the glass is moulded with prism-shaped grooves. Where the medium is **steam/vapour** (low refractive index), light undergoes TIR and returns to the eye, making the region appear **bright**. Where the medium is **liquid** (higher refractive index), light refracts into the liquid and is absorbed, making the region appear **dark**. The physical constant it relies upon is the **refractive index** of the glass (typically **1.47** for DIN 7081 borosilicate) and the **critical angle of reflection**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4]**

**a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working** of a **reflex-type sight glass** used for boiler water-level indication. *(7 marks)*

**b)** A reflex sight glass is made of borosilicate glass with refractive index $n_1 = 1.48$. The prisms are cut such that light strikes the inner face at $55^{\circ}$ to the normal. Determine whether the region appears **bright or dark** when the medium in contact with the inner face is:
  (i) Saturated steam ($n_2 = 1.00$)
  (ii) Liquid water ($n_3 = 1.33$) *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A reflex sight glass consists of the following parts (refer to the constructional block diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Body (channel cover):** A forged carbon-steel or stainless-steel channel section machined to fit the glass snugly on the vessel nozzle.
2. **Reflex glass disc:** A flat, toughened **borosilicate glass** disc whose **inner face** has parallel **V-shaped prism grooves** moulded into it. The outer face is polished flat.
3. **Gaskets:** Soft graphite or PTFE gaskets on both sides of the glass to ensure pressure sealing.
4. **Studs and nuts:** High-tensile bolts to compress the gasket uniformly.
5. **Drain and vent valves:** Connected at the lower and upper ends to allow blow-down and air release.

**Working:**
- Light from the boiler room (or an integral illuminator) enters the polished outer face of the glass.
- It travels through the glass and strikes the **prism-grooved inner face**.
- At points where the glass is in contact with **steam** (above water level), the refractive index of steam is very low, and the critical angle is small. Light rays are **totally internally reflected** back through the glass to the operator's eye, producing a **bright, silvery appearance**.
- At points where the glass is in contact with **water**, the critical angle is large, so light rays are **refracted into the water** and absorbed. The region appears **dark/black**.
- The **interface line** between the bright (steam) and dark (water) regions is the **true water level** inside the drum.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Critical angle at glass–steam interface:**

$$
\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{1.00}{1.48} = 0.6757
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.6757) \approx 42.50^{\circ}
$$

**Comparison:** Incident angle $55^{\circ} > \theta_c = 42.50^{\circ}$ → **Total Internal Reflection occurs** → Region appears **BRIGHT**.

**Step 2 — Critical angle at glass–water interface:**

$$
\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_3}{n_1} = \dfrac{1.33}{1.48} = 0.8986
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.8986) \approx 63.93^{\circ}
$$

**Comparison:** Incident angle $55^{\circ} < \theta_c = 63.93^{\circ}$ → **Refraction occurs** → Light escapes into water → Region appears **DARK**.

**Conclusion Table:**

| Region | Outside Medium | $\theta_c$ | Incident Angle | Phenomenon | Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Above water | Steam | 42.50° | 55° | TIR | **Bright** |
| Below water | Water | 63.93° | 55° | Refraction | **Dark** |

The bright-to-dark boundary on the sight glass accurately marks the **boiler water level**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4]**

**a)** Explain the **different types of sight glasses** used in process industries, with their typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Compare the **advantages and limitations** of sight glass level measurement with that of a **differential pressure (DP) transmitter**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Tubular Sight Glass (Tube Gauge)**
- A glass tube is mounted vertically between two valves fitted to the vessel.
- The liquid level inside the tube equals the level in the vessel (communicating-vessel principle).
- **Application:** Low-pressure storage tanks, water reservoirs (up to 16 bar).

**2. Reflex Sight Glass**
- Flat glass with prismatic grooves on the inner face; uses TIR.
- Bright = steam/vapour, Dark = liquid.
- **Application:** Boiler water drums, steam separators — **most common in steam plants**.

**3. Transparent (Through-Vision) Sight Glass**
- Plain flat polished glass (no grooves).
- Operator sees the **actual fluid** — colour, bubbles, foam.
- **Application:** Chemical reactors, dye tanks, fermentation vessels where visual quality inspection is needed.

**4. Magnetic (Float-Operated) Sight Glass**
- Internal float with magnet drives an external follower flag.
- No glass–process contact for the indicator.
- **Application:** Hazardous areas, sanitary applications, sealed vessels.

**5. Mica-Shielded Reflex Sight Glass**
- A thin mica sheet protects the glass from hot/corrosive media.
- **Application:** High-temperature and superheated steam service (> 200 °C).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | Sight Glass | DP Transmitter |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Visual (human eye) | 4–20 mA electrical |
| Power required | **None** | 24 V DC loop power |
| Cost | Very low | High (instrument + manifold) |
| Accuracy | Operator dependent (~ ±5 mm) | ±0.5% of span (e.g., ±1.25 mm for 250 mm span) |
| Remote indication | **Not possible** | Yes, via DCS/PLC |
| Hazardous area | Inherently safe | Needs Ex-proof housing |
| Maintenance | Glass breakage, gasket leak | Diaphragm corrosion, impulse line blockage |
| Suitable for very high pressure | Limited by glass disc rating | Limited by flange rating |
| Calibration | Not required | Periodic zero/span re-calibration |
| Data logging | No | Yes (HART / Foundation Fieldbus) |

**Conclusion:** Sight glasses are best when **simple, low-cost, intrinsically safe local indication** is sufficient. DP transmitters are preferred for **control-loop integration, remote monitoring, and high-accuracy applications**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the refractive index values for **glass (1.47)** and **water (1.33)** in critical-angle calculations. The KTU scheme heavily penalises swapped values.
> 2. **Do not** write "sight glass gives electrical output" — it is a **purely visual, non-electrical** device. Examiners deduct full marks.
> 3. **Always** show the Snell's-law step and the critical-angle step separately in derivations, even if the problem is short.
> 4. **Tubular sight glass** works on the **communicating vessels** principle, while **reflex sight glass** works on **Total Internal Reflection**. Examiners test this distinction directly.
> 5. Mentioning **IBR (Indian Boiler Regulations)** while answering boiler-related sight glass questions earns an **extra half-mark** in the evaluator's discretion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sight glass = direct-reading, visual, non-electrical** level/condition indicator.
- **Three main types:** Tubular (communicating vessel), Reflex (TIR-based), Transparent (plain glass).
- **Magnetic sight glass** uses **magnetic coupling** between an internal float and an external flag — ideal for hazardous and sanitary areas.
- **Reflex principle** depends on the **critical angle of reflection** at the glass–process interface: bright where medium is **steam/vapour** (low $n$), dark where medium is **liquid** (higher $n$).
- **Critical angle formula:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_{outside}}{n_{glass}}\right)$.
- **DIN 7081 borosilicate glass** has $n \approx 1.47$ and is the **standard material** for reflex sight glasses.
- **Standard pressure rating:** up to **40 bar**; high-pressure mica-shielded units go up to **100 bar / 300 °C**.
- **No external power required** → inherently **explosion-proof**.
- **Key limitation:** **Cannot give remote or electrical output**; cannot be interfaced to DCS.
- **Famous KTU formula to memorise:** $\theta_c^{steam} \approx 42.8^{\circ}$ for $n_{glass} = 1.47$.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Reflex for clean steam–water service; Transparent for coloured/foamy liquids; Tubular for low-pressure tanks; Magnetic for hazardous/sanitary zones.
- **IBR** (Indian Boiler Regulations) **mandates** sight glasses on all steam boiler drums as a primary water-level indicator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Float Type Level Indicator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Float Type Level Indicator**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Float Type Level Indicator** is a primary-class, buoyancy-actuated, continuous level measurement device that uses the upward hydrostatic force exerted by a fluid on a partially or fully submerged hollow body (the **float**) to track the position of the liquid–gas (or liquid–liquid) interface inside a process vessel. The vertical displacement of the float is mechanically, magnetically, or electro-statically coupled to a pointer, encoder, or transducer, producing a direct visual or electrical readout of the level.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact phrasing pattern):**
> "A float-type level indicator works on the principle of buoyancy. A float, whose density is lower than that of the process liquid, rests on the liquid surface. As the level rises or falls, the float moves vertically, and this motion is transmitted through a suitable coupling mechanism to an indicator or transmitter."

## 1.2 Core Governing Principle — Archimedes' Buoyancy

The working of every float-type device is governed by **Archimedes' Principle**, which states that:

> *"When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

The submerged volume of the float changes with the level. Consequently, the buoyant force changes, and the float is in **dynamic equilibrium** under three forces: its own weight (downward), the buoyant force (upward), and any external mechanical restraining force (such as a counterweight or spring).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cork in a Bathtub

Imagine pushing a hollow rubber ball (a **cork**) into a bathtub full of water. The moment you let go, the ball pops back to the surface. The same thing happens in a giant industrial tank of oil, water, or chemical: a sealed hollow metal sphere (the **float**) is always "pushed" to the top of the liquid and rides on the surface just like a cork in a bathtub. 

If we glue a pointer to that cork and slide the pointer along a vertical ruler fixed to the tub wall, the moment the water rises by 5 cm, the cork — and hence the pointer — also rises by exactly 5 cm. This is the entire operating philosophy of a float-type level indicator. The pointer is just a visible trace of the cork's vertical journey, and that journey is a direct mirror of the liquid level.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering metrics used in float-type level design:**
> - **Density of water (ρ_water)** = **1000 kg/m³** (at 4 °C, standard reference)
> - **Specific gravity of float (SG_float)** = typically **0.4 to 0.9** (must be lower than the process fluid)
> - **Standard specific gravities of common fluids**: Crude oil ≈ **0.85**, Sulfuric acid (98%) ≈ **1.84**, Glycerine ≈ **1.26**, Brine ≈ **1.20**
> - **Standard acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure** = **101.325 kPa** (used to define the liquid surface datum)

## 1.5 Classification of Float-Type Level Indicators

Float-type devices can be classified into four major families (this taxonomy is **must-know for KTU exams**):

| # | Family | Coupling Mechanism | Output |
|---|--------|-------------------|--------|
| 1 | **Float-and-Lever (Arm) Type** | Mechanical lever + linkage | Direct local pointer |
| 2 | **Float-and-Cable (Chain) Type** | Cable over a pulley | Remote indicator |
| 3 | **Float with Magnetic Coupling (Magnetic Float Switch)** | Magnetic field through non-magnetic wall | Reed switch / electronic |
| 4 | **Float-Type Capacitance / Servo (Displacer) Type** | Buoyancy imbalance + servo | 4–20 mA electronic |

## 1.6 Selection Criterion (Float vs. Process Fluid)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> The single most fundamental rule — the float's average density **must always be less** than the minimum density of the process liquid under all operating conditions (including temperature-induced density variation). If the float becomes heavier than the fluid, it sinks and the indicator reads zero permanently.

$$ \rho_{float} < \rho_{liquid,min} $$

If the float's density is *higher* than the fluid, the device degenerates into a **displacer-type** sensor, which is an entirely different topic (covered later in Module 4).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free-Body Analysis of the Floating Body

When a float of volume $V_f$ is partially submerged by a depth $h$ inside a liquid of density $\rho_l$, two opposing vertical forces act on it:

1. **Weight of the float (downward):** 
$$ W_f = m_f \cdot g = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot g $$

2. **Buoyant force (upward), equal to the weight of displaced liquid:**
$$ F_b = m_{displaced} \cdot g = \rho_l \cdot V_{sub} \cdot g $$

where $V_{sub}$ is the submerged volume of the float at any instant.

For a cylindrical float of cross-sectional area $A_f$ submerged to a depth $h$:

$$ V_{sub} = A_f \cdot h \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_b = \rho_l \cdot A_f \cdot h \cdot g $$

## 2.2 Equilibrium Condition — Why the Float Floats

At static equilibrium:

$$ F_b = W_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_l \cdot A_f \cdot h \cdot g = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot g $$

Cancelling $g$ on both sides and rearranging:

$$ h = \frac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_l \cdot A_f} $$

This submergence depth $h$ is a **constant** for a given float and a given liquid. Hence, the submerged length does not change with level — only the vertical position of the float body changes. This is the geometric basis of float-level instrumentation.

## 2.3 Why the Float Follows the Liquid Surface Exactly

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Self-Centering" Property of Buoyancy:**
> Any external disturbance (waves, vibration) that momentarily pushes the float deeper into the liquid increases the displaced volume, hence the buoyant force. The increased upward force pushes the float back to its natural equilibrium submergence. This makes the float a **mechanical integrator** of the surface height — it inherently ignores small surface ripples and provides a steady reading. This is one of the most important KTU theory questions.

## 2.4 The Mechanical Lever-Amplifier Concept (Float-and-Arm)

A float attached to the end of a horizontal lever provides **mechanical amplification** of the float's small vertical motion into a much larger angular motion at the indicator side. This is exactly the principle of a **Class 1 Lever** from physics.

Consider a lever of total length $L$, with the float at distance $a$ from the pivot and the indicator pointer at distance $b$ from the pivot:

$$ \theta_{pointer} = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{\Delta h_{float}}{L_{pointer}} $$

If $b > a$, a small float motion produces a larger pointer motion — this is called **mechanical advantage**.

## 2.5 The Float-and-Cable (Chain) Mechanism

For tall vessels (e.g., 10 m storage tanks), a rigid lever is impractical. Instead, the float is tethered to a **counterweight** by a flexible cable passing over a **pulley**. As the float rises by $\Delta h$, the counterweight falls by an equal $\Delta h$, and the pulley rotates by:

$$ \theta = \frac{\Delta h}{r_{pulley}} \text{ radians} $$

where $r_{pulley}$ is the pulley radius. The pulley rotation drives a pointer on a calibrated dial.

## 2.6 The Magnetic Float Switch (Reed-Switch Coupling)

A non-magnetic (e.g., stainless steel) wall separates the float chamber from the indicator side. A permanent magnet inside the float generates a magnetic field that passes through the wall and actuates a **hermetically sealed reed switch** mounted on a vertical strip on the outside. The reed switch closes when the float's magnet aligns with it, providing a **discrete level alarm** (e.g., at high or low level).

For a continuous magnetic version, a vertical string of reed switches is used, and the output is decoded to give a continuous 4–20 mA signal.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table (Print and Memorize)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Buoyant force | $F_b = \rho_l \cdot V_{sub} \cdot g$ | **N** | Archimedes' principle |
| 2 | Float weight | $W_f = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot g$ | **N** | Net downward force |
| 3 | Submerged depth of float | $h = \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_l \cdot A_f}$ | **m** | Equilibrium condition |
| 4 | Lever mechanical gain | $G = \dfrac{b}{a}$ | dimensionless | Pointer side ÷ float side |
| 5 | Pointer rotation | $\theta = \dfrac{a}{b} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta h}{L_{pointer}}$ | **rad** | Float input → pointer output |
| 6 | Pulley rotation | $\theta = \dfrac{\Delta h}{r_{pulley}}$ | **rad** | Float-and-cable type |
| 7 | Apparent weight loss in liquid | $\Delta W = (\rho_l - \rho_f) \cdot V_f \cdot g$ | **N** | Net upward force at equilibrium |
| 8 | Volume of cylindrical float | $V_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H$ | **m³** | For force / weight calculation |
| 9 | Force on displacer (forced submersion) | $F = (\rho_l - \rho_d) \cdot V_d \cdot g$ | **N** | Displacer-type, not float |
| 10 | Reynolds number for float drag | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot v \cdot D_f}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | For damping / dynamic response |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical storage tanks:** Float-type indicators are the workhorse of level measurement for crude oil, refined fuels, and lube oils because they are inherently explosion-proof (no electrical parts inside the tank — only mechanical motion).
- **Boiler drum level:** Steam boilers use float traps to discharge condensate based on level.
- **Water treatment plants:** Magnetic float switches are used in chemical dosing tanks because they provide sealed, contactless level sensing — ideal for corrosive chemicals (HCl, NaOH).
- **Marine ballast tanks:** Float gauges are preferred because they tolerate sloshing (self-centering buoyancy).
- **Automotive fuel gauges:** The classic fuel-tank sender is a float-and-arm rheostat (a potentiometric float gauge).
