# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equilibrium Submergence Depth of a Hollow Spherical Float

> **Problem Setup:**
> A hollow spherical float of outer diameter **D = 120 mm** and effective density **ρ_f = 600 kg/m³** floats in a liquid of density **ρ_l = 1200 kg/m³**. Derive an expression for the depth submerged and compute it numerically.

**Step 1 — Volume of the spherical float:**
For a sphere, the total volume is:
$$ V_f = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} $$

**Step 2 — Equate buoyant force to weight at equilibrium:**
$$ \rho_l \cdot V_{sub} \cdot g = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot g $$

**Step 3 — Cancel g from both sides:**
$$ \rho_l \cdot V_{sub} = \rho_f \cdot V_f $$

**Step 4 — Solve for the submerged volume:**
$$ V_{sub} = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_l} \cdot V_f $$

This is a beautifully simple result — the submerged volume is just a fixed **fraction** $\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_l}$ of the total float volume, independent of the absolute level in the tank.

**Step 5 — Submerge fraction (volume ratio):**
$$ \frac{V_{sub}}{V_f} = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_l} $$

For our numbers:
$$ \frac{V_{sub}}{V_f} = \frac{600}{1200} = 0.5 $$

**Step 6 — Total float volume:**
$$ V_f = \frac{\pi (0.120)^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.728 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = 9.0478 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 7 — Submerged volume:**
$$ V_{sub} = 0.5 \times 9.0478 \times 10^{-4} = 4.5239 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 8 — Convert submerged volume into equivalent spherical cap height:**
The volume of a spherical cap of height $h$ on a sphere of radius $R$ is:
$$ V_{cap} = \frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3} $$

For $D = 0.120$ m → $R = 0.060$ m. With $V_{cap} = 4.5239 \times 10^{-4}$ m³, solving the cubic (or using iteration) yields:
$$ h \approx 0.0402 \text{ m} = 40.2 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:**
The float sits with **40.2 mm** of its diameter submerged at all times, regardless of whether the tank is full or empty. Only the *vertical position* of the float changes with level.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Upward Force on a Counterweighted Float in a Float-and-Cable System

> **Problem Setup:**
> A float of mass **m_f = 0.85 kg** floats in a liquid of density **ρ_l = 950 kg/m³**. The float's volume is **V_f = 1.4 × 10⁻³ m³**. A counterweight of mass **m_c** hangs on the other side of the pulley. The cable is massless and the pulley is frictionless. Determine the value of $m_c$ such that the system is in static equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Compute float weight (downward on the float-side rope):**
$$ W_f = m_f \cdot g = 0.85 \times 9.81 = 8.3385 \text{ N} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum buoyant force (when the float is fully submerged):**
$$ F_{b,max} = \rho_l \cdot V_f \cdot g = 950 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 13.047 \text{ N} $$

**Step 3 — Define the net force pulling the float *up* when fully submerged:**
The buoyant force exceeds the float weight. The difference is what the cable must resist on the float side:
$$ F_{net,float} = F_{b,max} - W_f = 13.047 - 8.3385 = 4.7085 \text{ N} $$

**Step 4 — The counterweight on the other side of the cable must balance this force:**
At equilibrium, the tension on the float-side equals the tension on the counterweight-side (massless cable, frictionless pulley):
$$ T = m_c \cdot g $$

**Step 5 — Solve for the counterweight mass:**
$$ m_c = \frac{F_{net,float}}{g} = \frac{4.7085}{9.81} = 0.4800 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 6 — Verification with the empty-tank condition:**
When the tank is empty, the float hangs in air. The cable tension then becomes:
$$ T_{empty} = m_f \cdot g = 8.3385 \text{ N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{c,empty} = 0.85 \text{ kg} $$

This shows that the counterweight balances *differently* for empty vs. full tank — a real system needs either:
- A **constant-tension spring** instead of a fixed counterweight, OR
- A counterweight sized for the **mid-point** of the level range (most common industrial practice).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mechanical Gain of a Float-and-Lever Indicator

> **Problem Setup:**
> A lever-arm float indicator has a pivot located **a = 80 mm** from the float and **b = 320 mm** from the pointer. The float rises by **Δh = 50 mm**. The pointer is mounted at a radius of **L_p = 60 mm** from the indicator shaft. Determine:
> (a) The mechanical gain
> (b) The pointer-tip linear displacement
> (c) The pointer angular rotation in degrees

**Step 1 — Mechanical gain:**
$$ G = \frac{b}{a} = \frac{320}{80} = 4 $$

The pointer side moves **4 times** as much as the float side.

**Step 2 — Linear displacement at the indicator shaft:**
The shaft displacement equals the float displacement scaled by the gain:
$$ \Delta y_{shaft} = G \cdot \Delta h = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3 — Pointer-tip linear displacement:**
The pointer is a rigid arm of length $L_p$ pivoting at the indicator shaft:
$$ \Delta s_{tip} = L_p \cdot \theta $$

For small angles, $\theta$ (in radians) ≈ $\Delta y_{shaft} / b$:
$$ \theta = \frac{\Delta y_{shaft}}{b} = \frac{200}{320} = 0.625 \text{ rad} $$

Converting to degrees:
$$ \theta = 0.625 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 35.81^\circ $$

**Step 4 — Pointer-tip linear displacement:**
$$ \Delta s_{tip} = L_p \cdot \theta = 60 \times 0.625 = 37.5 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 5 — Summary table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Float motion | $\Delta h$ | 50 mm |
| Mechanical gain | $G = b/a$ | 4 |
| Shaft vertical motion | $\Delta y_{shaft}$ | 200 mm |
| Shaft angular rotation | $\theta$ | 0.625 rad = 35.81° |
| Pointer-tip motion | $\Delta s_{tip}$ | 37.5 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the gain matters:**
> The gain is what allows a 50 mm float motion to be read as a 200 mm displacement on a distant indicator. This is the same principle as a **seesaw with a long pointer side** — a small motion at one end is amplified into a large motion at the other end.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Buoyant Force on a Cylindrical Float Immersed in Two Immiscible Liquids

> **Problem Setup:**
> A cylindrical float of diameter **D = 100 mm**, height **H = 150 mm**, and density **ρ_f = 750 kg/m³** rests at the interface of oil (top, **ρ_oil = 820 kg/m³**, layer thickness 1.0 m) floating on water (**ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**). Determine the position of the float (i.e., how much is in water vs. in oil).

**Step 1 — Total float volume:**
$$ V_f = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 (0.150) = 1.1781 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Float weight:**
$$ W_f = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot g = 750 \times 1.1781 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 8.668 \text{ N} $$

**Step 3 — Assume the float is fully in oil (test this hypothesis first):**
If the float were entirely in oil, the buoyant force would be:
$$ F_{b,oil} = \rho_{oil} \cdot V_f \cdot g = 820 \times 1.1781 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 9.476 \text{ N} $$

**Step 4 — Compare with weight:**
$$ F_{b,oil} = 9.476 \text{ N} > W_f = 8.668 \text{ N} $$

Buoyant force in oil exceeds the weight → the float would pop up. So the float must extend into the water below the oil layer to lose some buoyancy.

**Step 5 — Let $h_w$ be the depth of the float immersed in water, and $h_o$ the depth in oil:**
$$ h_w + h_o = H = 0.150 \text{ m} $$

**Step 6 — Force balance at equilibrium:**
$$ \rho_{water} \cdot A_f \cdot h_w \cdot g + \rho_{oil} \cdot A_f \cdot h_o \cdot g = W_f $$

where $A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

**Step 7 — Substitute and simplify:**
$$ \rho_{water} \cdot h_w + \rho_{oil} \cdot h_o = \frac{W_f}{A_f \cdot g} = \frac{8.668}{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81} = 112.50 \text{ kg/m}^2 $$

**Step 8 — Use the constraint $h_w + h_o = 0.150$ m:**
$$ 1000 \cdot h_w + 820 \cdot h_o = 112.50 $$
$$ 1000 \cdot h_w + 820 \cdot (0.150 - h_w) = 112.50 $$
$$ 1000 h_w + 123 - 820 h_w = 112.50 $$
$$ 180 h_w = -10.50 $$
$$ h_w = -0.0583 \text{ m} $$

A **negative** $h_w$ is physically impossible. This means our assumption was wrong: the float does **not** submerge into the water at all — it floats entirely within the oil layer.

**Step 9 — Re-compute the true equilibrium inside oil only:**
Equating buoyant force to weight for the cylindrical float in oil:
$$ \rho_{oil} \cdot A_f \cdot h_{sub} \cdot g = W_f $$

$$ h_{sub} = \frac{W_f}{\rho_{oil} \cdot A_f \cdot g} = \frac{8.668}{820 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81} = 0.1372 \text{ m} $$

**Step 10 — Final position:**
The float sits with its **bottom 137.2 mm** submerged in oil (out of 150 mm total), and its **top 12.8 mm** projects above the oil surface. It does **not** enter the water. The oil layer of 1.0 m thickness is thick enough to fully accommodate the float.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> This result is crucial for **interface-level measurement** between two immiscible liquids (e.g., oil–water separators in petroleum refineries). The float density is chosen to be intermediate between the two fluid densities so that it floats *at* the interface.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"State Archimedes' Principle and explain how it governs the operation of a float-type level indicator."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Archimedes' Principle states that *when a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body*. In a float-type level indicator, the float is a hollow body whose density is **less than** that of the process liquid. As the liquid level rises, the float rises with it, and as the level falls, the float descends. The buoyant force exactly balances the float's weight at all positions; the submergence depth stays constant and only the float's *vertical position* changes. This vertical motion is then converted into a pointer or electrical signal by a coupling mechanism. (3 marks)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Compare float-type and displacer-type level sensors in three points."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Float Type | Displacer Type |
|---|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Float density **less than** liquid density | Displacer density **greater than** liquid density |
| 2 | Float rides **on the surface** | Displacer is **partially or fully submerged always** |
| 3 | Used for **clean** liquids and free surfaces | Used for **interface** and **dense** liquids including slurries |

(3 marks – one mark per correct, distinct comparison point)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question Choice A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**"With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a float-type level indicator using a lever mechanism. Derive an expression for the equilibrium submergence depth of the float and explain the effect of changing liquid density on the indicator reading."**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Construction (3 marks):**
The float-and-lever level indicator consists of:
- A **hollow metallic float** (usually SS-304 or SS-316) shaped as a sphere or cylinder
- A **rigid lever arm** (forked at the float end) connected to a **pivot point** on the side of the tank
- An **indicator shaft** carrying a **pointer** that moves over a calibrated scale
- A **counterweight or spring** providing the restraining torque
- A **stillwell chamber** (optional) that shields the float from surface turbulence

**Step 2 — Working (2 marks):**
As the liquid level rises inside the tank, the float — riding on the surface — moves up. The float's vertical motion is transmitted to one end of the lever, which rotates about the pivot. The other end of the lever, fitted with a pointer, swings across a calibrated scale. The scale is marked directly in **millimeters** or **percentage of full level**.

**Step 3 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The block diagram should show: **Liquid → Float → Lever → Pivot → Shaft → Pointer → Scale**. (Use the diagrams in Section 4 of these notes for reference.)

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Submergence Depth & Effect of Density [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition (2 marks):**
At equilibrium, buoyant force equals weight:
$$ \rho_l \cdot A_f \cdot h \cdot g = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot g $$
$$ h = \frac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_l \cdot A_f} $$

**Step 2 — Numerical Verification with Sample Data (1 mark):**
For a float with $\rho_f = 700$ kg/m³, $V_f = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $A_f = 7.85 \times 10^{-3}$ m², in a liquid of $\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³:
$$ h = \frac{700 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1000 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1338 \text{ m} = 133.8 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3 — Effect of Liquid Density on Reading (2 marks):**
- If $\rho_l$ **increases** (e.g., liquid becomes denser due to cooling), $h$ **decreases** → float sits **higher** in the liquid (less submerged). The **position of the float body is unchanged** for the same level, but the **calibration** assumes a fixed $\rho_l$.
- If the **same float** is used in a **denser liquid**, the same physical level will appear at the same float position, BUT if the float's density becomes *equal to or greater than* the liquid, the float sinks and the indicator fails (zero output).

**Step 4 — Calibration Rule (1 mark):**
$$ \frac{\Delta h_{reading}}{H_{full}} = \frac{\Delta \rho_l}{\rho_{l,cal}} \quad \text{(small change linearization)} $$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The float-type indicator is inherently **self-calibrating for height** but **density-dependent for absolute submergence**. Industrial systems therefore specify the **process liquid density** as part of the float design.

---

### Question Choice B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Explain the construction and working of a magnetic float-type level indicator with a reed-switch strip. Discuss its advantages over a float-and-lever type. A magnetic float-type indicator is used to measure the interface level between oil (ρ = 850 kg/m³) and water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³). If the float has a density of 920 kg/m³ and a volume of 2.0 × 10⁻³ m³, determine the position of the float."**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Advantages [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Construction (2 marks):**
A **non-magnetic chamber** (typically SS-316) is welded onto the side of the tank and connected to the tank interior by upper and lower process connections. Inside the chamber, a **float with a permanent magnet** (e.g., AlNiCo or rare-earth) is free to slide vertically. Outside the chamber, a **vertical strip of reed switches** is mounted. Each reed switch is a hermetically sealed glass envelope with two ferromagnetic contacts that close under a magnetic field.

**Step 2 — Working (2 marks):**
As the float moves up or down with the liquid, its magnet aligns with successive reed switches on the strip. The closed switch(es) produce a discrete electrical signal. With a **continuously mounted strip** of closely spaced reed switches (or a Hall-effect sensor strip), the float's position can be resolved into a continuous 4–20 mA output using a resistive divider or encoder circuit.

**Step 3 — Advantages over Float-and-Lever (3 marks):**
1. **No mechanical coupling through the wall** — the magnetic field crosses the wall, so the chamber is fully sealed; no leakage, no friction, no torque-tube wear.
2. **Safe in hazardous areas** — no spark risk; ideal for flammable fluids (petrol, naphtha, LNG).
3. **Suitable for corrosive and ultra-pure fluids** — chamber is SS-316 or Hastelloy; the float has no external mechanical linkage to corrode.
4. **Higher reliability** — fewer moving parts; reed switches have lifetimes > 10⁹ operations.
5. **Easy to add multiple level alarms** — just add more reed switches at desired heights.

#### Part (b) — Interface-Level Position Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Float Weight (1 mark):**
$$ W_f = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot g = 920 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 18.05 \text{ N} $$

**Step 2 — Buoyant Force if Float is Entirely in Oil (1 mark):**
$$ F_{b,oil} = \rho_{oil} \cdot V_f \cdot g = 850 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 16.677 \text{ N} $$

**Step 3 — Buoyant Force if Float is Entirely in Water (1 mark):**
$$ F_{b,water} = 1000 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N} $$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Results (1 mark):**
- $F_{b,oil} = 16.677$ N **<** $W_f = 18.05$ N → float would **sink** in pure oil.
- $F_{b,water} = 19.62$ N **>** $W_f = 18.05$ N → float would **float** in pure water.

Therefore, the float naturally settles **at the interface**, with part of it in oil and part in water.

**Step 5 — Force Balance (2 marks):**
Let $V_o$ = volume of float in oil, $V_w$ = volume in water. Then $V_o + V_w = V_f = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³.

At equilibrium:
$$ \rho_{oil} \cdot V_o \cdot g + \rho_{water} \cdot V_w \cdot g = W_f $$

$$ 850 \cdot V_o + 1000 \cdot V_w = \frac{18.05}{9.81} = 1.840 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 6 — Solve the System (1 mark):**
Substitute $V_w = V_f - V_o = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} - V_o$:
$$ 850 V_o + 1000 (2.0 \times 10^{-3} - V_o) = 1.840 $$
$$ 850 V_o + 2.000 - 1000 V_o = 1.840 $$
$$ -150 V_o = -0.160 $$
$$ V_o = 1.0667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ V_w = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} - 1.0667 \times 10^{-3} = 0.9333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 7 — Physical Position (assumed cylindrical float of D = 100 mm, A_f = 7.854 × 10⁻³ m²):**

Height of float submerged in oil:
$$ h_o = \frac{V_o}{A_f} = \frac{1.0667 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1358 \text{ m} = 135.8 \text{ mm} $$

Height of float submerged in water:
$$ h_w = \frac{V_w}{A_f} = \frac{0.9333 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1188 \text{ m} = 118.8 \text{ mm} $$

Total submerged height = 254.6 mm. (Total float height would need to be at least 254.6 mm for this configuration to be valid — the float would need to be designed with sufficient length to span the interface region.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $V_o + V_w = V_f$** — this is the most common mistake; without it, the problem is unsolvable. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Incorrectly writing buoyant force as $\rho V g$ without the volume term** — students often write $F_b = \rho g$ (wrong) instead of $F_b = \rho V g$. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not interpreting the result physically** — the float must lie across the interface. Students who report the float as being "fully in oil" or "fully in water" lose credit. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Unit errors in converting mm³ to m³** — $1 \text{ cm}^3 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$, not $10^{-3}$. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to state that the float density lies *between* the two fluid densities** — this is the key selection criterion for interface floats. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for the night before the exam):**

- ✅ A **float-type level indicator** works on **Archimedes' Principle** — the buoyant force on the float equals the weight of displaced liquid.
- ✅ The float density **must be lower** than the minimum process liquid density — otherwise the float sinks.
- ✅ The submerged depth of a freely floating float is **constant** — only the float's *position* changes with level. This is the geometric basis of float-level sensing.
- ✅ The **submerged depth formula** is: $h = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_l A_f}$.
- ✅ **Lever-arm type** is used for small tanks; the mechanical gain is $G = b/a$.
- ✅ **Float-and-cable (chain) type** is used for tall tanks; a counterweight or constant-tension spring balances the float.
- ✅ **Magnetic float switch** uses a non-magnetic wall and reed switches; ideal for **corrosive**, **hazardous**, and **ultra-pure** fluids.
- ✅ **Interface measurement** is possible when the float density lies *between* the two fluid densities; the float straddles the interface.
- ✅ The **self-centering property of buoyancy** (any disturbance increasing submergence increases buoyant force, pushing the float back) gives a smooth, ripple-filtered reading.
- ✅ The four **buoyant forces** in increasing order for a given float are: $F_{b,air} \approx 0$ < $F_{b,oil}$ < $F_{b,water}$ < $F_{b,brine}$ (because buoyant force scales with fluid density).
- ✅ For a counterweighted float-and-cable system, the counterweight is sized for the **mid-point** of the float's buoyant-force range to avoid slack or over-tension across the full span.
- ✅ Float-type indicators are **inherently safe in flammable atmospheres** (mechanical motion only — no electrical parts inside the tank) unless the float carries a magnet, in which case a non-magnetic chamber wall is mandatory.
- ✅ Common applications: **fuel tanks, lube-oil tanks, boiler drums, water reservoirs, chemical dosing tanks, marine ballast tanks, and petroleum storage tanks.**
- ✅ **Calibration rule:** float-type indicators are calibrated for a *specific* liquid density; using them in a different liquid without re-calibration introduces a systematic error proportional to the density ratio.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems, always **draw a free-body diagram first**, then write the equilibrium equation, then solve. This is the valuation key the examiner expects.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ball Float
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ball Float

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Ball Float** is a buoyancy-actuated, mechanical level-sensing device in which a hollow, sealed spherical element (typically manufactured from stainless steel, Monel, or specialized alloys) partially or fully submerged in a process fluid experiences a net vertical buoyant force proportional to the displaced volume of the liquid. This buoyant force, or the mechanical displacement of the float, is then transduced—via a lever arm, torque tube, or magnetic coupling—into a measurable output signal (rotational angle, pneumatic pressure, or electrical signal) that is linearly correlated to the instantaneous liquid level within the vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ball floats fall under the broader category of **Buoyancy-Based Level Sensors** and are extensively used for **continuous level measurement** in open and pressurized tanks, particularly for liquids with specific gravities in the range **0.5 to 1.5**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine placing a hollow rubber ball in a swimming pool. As the water level rises, the ball floats higher; as the water level falls, the ball sinks lower. The ball "tracks" the water surface. Now, attach a fishing line to that ball and connect the other end to a spinning wheel on the pool deck. The ball's vertical movement rotates the wheel, and you have a simple level indicator! A **Ball Float** operates on exactly this principle—except, in industrial versions, the "fishing line" is replaced by a precision **lever arm** or **torque tube**, and the "spinning wheel" is replaced by a calibrated gauge, transmitter, or controller.

## 1.3 Classification of Ball Floats

| Sub-Type | Measurement Mode | Typical Output | Common Application |
|----------|------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Side-Mounted Ball Float** | Continuous | Pneumatic / Electrical | Boiler water drums |
| **Top-Mounted Ball Float** | Continuous | Mechanical / Electrical | Storage tanks |
| **Magnetic Ball Float (Float Switch)** | Point (On/Off) | Switch Contact | High/Low level alarms |
| **Torque-Tube Ball Float** | Continuous | Pneumatic (3–15 psi) | Pressurized process vessels |

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU 2024 Scheme** specifically emphasizes the **torque-tube type ball float** transmitter because of its prevalence in chemical and petrochemical process industries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The operation of a ball float is governed by **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy**, which states that any object fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the object.

The ball float system comprises the following sequential operational stages:

- **Stage 1 — Buoyant Force Generation:** As the process liquid level rises, the ball float becomes increasingly submerged. The displaced volume of liquid (`V_d`) increases linearly with level, generating a proportional buoyant force (`F_b`).

- **Stage 2 — Mechanical Translation:** The buoyant force acts on the float arm/lever, which is mechanically coupled to a **torque tube** (a precision elastic element).

- **Stage 3 — Elastic Deformation:** The torque tube twists by an angle (`θ`) proportional to the applied torque (`T`), which is in turn proportional to the buoyant force.

- **Stage 4 — Signal Conversion:** The angular twist is converted, either pneumatically (via a baffle-nozzle-flapper arrangement) or electronically (via an LVDT/RVDT), into a standard output signal (**3–15 psi** or **4–20 mA**).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why a hollow sphere?** A sphere offers the highest volume-to-weight ratio with uniform stress distribution, allowing it to float stably with minimal submergence sensitivity to surface turbulence.
- **Why a torque tube?** It provides a frictionless, leak-proof rotational coupling ideal for **pressurized vessels**, eliminating the need for dynamic seals.
- **Why a flapper-nozzle system?** It converts minute angular deflections into a strong, linear pneumatic signal suitable for long-distance transmission.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-memorize** equations for any KTU exam question on Ball Floats.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Buoyant Force on Float | $F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_d$ | $\rho_l$ = liquid density, $g$ = gravitational acceleration, $V_d$ = displaced volume | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | Displaced Volume (Sphere) | $V_d = \frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3}$ | $h$ = submerged depth, $R$ = sphere radius | m³ |
| 3 | Net Force on Float | $F_{net} = F_b - W_{float}$ | $W_{float}$ = weight of float | N |
| 4 | Apparent Weight in Liquid | $W_{app} = W_{float} - F_b$ | — | N |
| 5 | Torque on Shaft | $T = F_{net} \cdot L$ | $L$ = lever arm length | N·m |
| 6 | Angular Twist of Torque Tube | $\theta = \frac{T \cdot L_t}{G \cdot J}$ | $L_t$ = tube length, $G$ = shear modulus, $J$ = polar moment of inertia | radians |
| 7 | Specific Gravity Condition (Float Sinks) | $\rho_l > \rho_{float}$ | $\rho_{float}$ = float material density | kg/m³ |
| 8 | Float Sensitivity (Δh → ΔV_d) | $\frac{dV_d}{dh} = \pi h (2R - h)$ | — | m² |
| 9 | Pneumatic Output | $P_{out} = P_{min} + \left(\frac{\theta - \theta_{min}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{min}}\right) \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})$ | $P$ = pneumatic pressure | psi |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ball float systems are indispensable in:
- **Power Generation:** Boiler drum level control in thermal and nuclear plants.
- **Oil & Gas:** Interface level detection in separators and crude oil storage.
- **Chemical Processing:** Acid/alkali tank level monitoring (with chemically resistant floats).
- **Water Treatment:** Clarifier and reservoir level instrumentation.
- **Marine:** Ballast tank level indication on ships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Displaced Volume of a Spherical Float as a Function of Submergence Depth

**Objective:** To derive the relationship $V_d = \frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3}$ for a sphere of radius `R` submerged to a depth `h` in a liquid.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Consider a sphere of radius $R$ with its center at the origin. The liquid surface intersects the sphere at depth $h$ below the top of the sphere. The submerged volume is a spherical cap.

**Step 2 — Parametrize the sphere.**
The equation of the sphere in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = R^2$$

**Step 3 — Slice the submerged cap into horizontal disks.**
Consider a thin horizontal disk at height $z$ (measured from the bottom of the sphere). The radius of the disk at height $z$ is $r = \sqrt{R^2 - z^2}$. The thickness of the disk is $dz$.

**Step 4 — Express the volume of the disk.**
The volume of an elementary disk is:

$$dV = \pi r^2 \, dz = \pi (R^2 - z^2) \, dz$$

**Step 5 — Set the integration limits.**
The submerged cap extends from $z = (R - h)$ at its lowest point to $z = R$ at its highest point (where it meets the liquid surface).

**Step 6 — Perform the integration.**

$$V_d = \int_{R-h}^{R} \pi (R^2 - z^2) \, dz$$

$$V_d = \pi \left[ R^2 z - \frac{z^3}{3} \right]_{R-h}^{R}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the upper limit (z = R).**

$$\pi \left[ R^2 \cdot R - \frac{R^3}{3} \right] = \pi \left[ R^3 - \frac{R^3}{3} \right] = \pi \cdot \frac{2R^3}{3}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the lower limit (z = R - h).**

$$\pi \left[ R^2 (R - h) - \frac{(R - h)^3}{3} \right]$$

Expand $(R - h)^3$ using the binomial theorem:

$$(R - h)^3 = R^3 - 3R^2 h + 3R h^2 - h^3$$

Substitute:

$$\pi \left[ R^3 - R^2 h - \frac{R^3 - 3R^2 h + 3R h^2 - h^3}{3} \right]$$

$$\pi \left[ R^3 - R^2 h - \frac{R^3}{3} + R^2 h - R h^2 + \frac{h^3}{3} \right]$$

$$\pi \left[ \frac{2R^3}{3} - R h^2 + \frac{h^3}{3} \right]$$

**Step 9 — Subtract lower limit from upper limit.**

$$V_d = \pi \cdot \frac{2R^3}{3} - \pi \left[ \frac{2R^3}{3} - R h^2 + \frac{h^3}{3} \right]$$

$$V_d = \pi \left[ R h^2 - \frac{h^3}{3} \right]$$

**Step 10 — Factor out the common term.**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The cubed term is often mistakenly dropped. Always recheck your integration limits and the binomial expansion.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Buoyant Force for Full Submergence

**Step 1 — When the ball is fully submerged ($h = 2R$):**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi (2R)^2 (3R - 2R)}{3} = \frac{\pi \cdot 4R^2 \cdot R}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3$$

This is the volume of a complete sphere, as expected.

**Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' Principle:**

$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_d = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3$$

**Step 3 — Apparent Weight:**

$$W_{app} = W_{float} - F_b = m_{float} \cdot g - \rho_l \cdot g \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3$$

$$W_{app} = g \left( m_{float} - \rho_l \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \right)$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** A hollow spherical ball float has an outer radius of **$R = 5$ cm** and weighs **$0.5$ N** in air. It is used to measure the level of water ($\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³). Determine the buoyant force when the ball is fully submerged and the apparent weight of the float.

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of the sphere.**

$$V_d = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.05)^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.25 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$V_d = 5.236 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the buoyant force.**

$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_d = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5.236 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_b = 5.136 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the apparent weight.**

$$W_{app} = W_{float} - F_b = 0.5 - 5.136 = -4.636 \text{ N}$$

The negative apparent weight confirms the float will remain fully submerged (since $F_b > W_{float}$), which is desirable for a stable level-sensing float.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Torque-Tube Ball Float Transmitter

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional block architecture of a torque-tube ball float level transmitter, mapping signal flow from the process liquid to the standard output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Vessel with Liquid] --> B[Hollow Spherical Ball Float]
    B --> C[Buoyant Force Acts on Lever Arm]
    C --> D[Torque Tube Twists by Angle theta]
    D --> E[Flapper Nozzle Mechanism]
    E --> F[Pneumatic Amplifier Relay]
    F --> G[3 to 15 psi Output Signal]
    D --> H[Optional LVDT Coupling]
    H --> I[Electronic Transmitter]
    I --> J[4 to 20 mA Output Signal]
    G --> K[Receiver: Indicator or Controller]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Control Valve or Alarm]
```

## 4.2 Constructional Cross-Section (Block-Level Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Tank Wall] --> P2[Nozzle / Process Connection]
    P2 --> P3[Torque Tube Housing]
    P3 --> P4[Torque Tube Shaft]
    P4 --> P5[Lever Arm with Ball Float]
    P5 --> P6[Spherical Hollow Float]
    P6 --> P7[Process Liquid Level]
    P3 --> P8[Output Coupling to Transmitter]
    P8 --> P9[Pneumatic or Electronic Output]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Ball Float vs. Other Float Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Float Based Level Sensors] --> R1[Ball Float]
    R --> R2[Displacer Type]
    R --> R3[Buoyancy Probe]
    R --> R4[Magnetic Float Switch]
    R1 --> R1a[Side Mounted]
    R1 --> R1b[Top Mounted with Torque Tube]
    R2 --> R2a[Coupled to Torque Tube]
    R2 --> R2b[Coupled to Force Balance]
    R3 --> R3a[Submerged Probe with Strain Gauge]
    R4 --> R4a[Reed Switch Inside Stem]
    R4 --> R4b[On Off Point Level Only]
```

## 4.4 Signal Conditioning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Liquid Level Change] --> S2[Float Vertical Displacement]
    S2 --> S3[Force on Lever Arm]
    S3 --> S4[Torque on Shaft]
    S4 --> S5[Angular Twist theta]
    S5 --> S6[Sensor: LVDT or Flapper]
    S6 --> S7[Signal Conditioning]
    S7 --> S8[Standardized Output]
    S8 --> S9[3 to 15 psi or 4 to 20 mA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In all diagrams, the **leftmost** block represents the **physical process variable** (liquid level), and the **rightmost** block represents the **standardized industrial output signal** suitable for long-distance transmission and control room display.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q: Define a ball float level sensor. List any two advantages of using a spherical float over a cylindrical float.**

**Model Answer:**

A **ball float level sensor** is a buoyancy-based continuous level measurement device in which a hollow sealed sphere partially submerged in a process liquid experiences a buoyant force proportional to the liquid level. This force is transmitted via a lever arm and torque tube assembly to produce a standardized output signal (3–15 psi or 4–20 mA).

**Two advantages of a spherical float over a cylindrical float:**

1. **Higher volume-to-weight ratio:** A sphere encloses the maximum volume for the least surface area, making it inherently lightweight yet highly buoyant.
2. **Symmetric hydrodynamic profile:** The spherical shape ensures equal response from all horizontal directions, providing superior stability against surface turbulence and vortex shedding.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Q: State Archimedes' Principle. How does it apply to the operation of a ball float?**

**Model Answer:**

**Archimedes' Principle** states that *any object, fully or partially immersed in a fluid, experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the object.*

**Application to ball float operation:**

When the liquid level rises, a greater portion of the spherical float becomes submerged. The displaced volume of liquid increases, and consequently, the buoyant force on the float increases linearly. This increasing buoyant force is mechanically transmitted to a torque tube, which twists proportionally. The angular twist is then converted into a standard output signal, thereby providing a continuous indication of the liquid level.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently **lose marks** on the following issues:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cm → m** in numerical problems (unit mismatch = 0 marks).
> 2. **Skipping the integration step** in displaced volume derivations.
> 3. **Confusing the sign convention** of buoyant force vs. apparent weight.
> 4. **Omitting the function of the torque tube** in long-answer questions.
> 5. **Drawing messy, unlabelled diagrams** — always label every part (float, arm, torque tube, nozzle, output).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice-1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block-level diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a torque-tube type ball float level transmitter**. Discuss how the buoyant force is converted into a 3–15 psi pneumatic output. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hollow stainless-steel ball float of outer radius **$R = 6$ cm** and mass **$m = 0.4$ kg** is used to measure the level of an oil with density **$\rho_l = 850$ kg/m³**. Calculate:
- (i) The buoyant force when the float is fully submerged.
- (ii) The apparent weight of the float.
- (iii) The submerged depth `h` at which the float is in neutral equilibrium (i.e., buoyant force equals weight). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction:**
The torque-tube ball float transmitter consists of a process chamber connected to the side of a tank, inside which a **lever arm** carries a **hollow spherical float** at its end. The other end of the lever is rigidly attached to a **torque tube** (a precision thin-walled elastic shaft). The torque tube is anchored at the housing wall and connected externally to a **flapper-nozzle assembly** followed by a **pneumatic relay amplifier**.

**Working Principle:**
- As the liquid level rises, the float rises with it.
- The float's buoyant force is transmitted to the lever arm.
- The lever arm applies a torque to the torque tube.
- The torque tube twists by a small angle $\theta$ (typically < 4°).
- The twist moves the flapper closer to or farther from the nozzle.
- This changes the nozzle back-pressure.
- The pneumatic relay amplifies this back-pressure to a standard **3–15 psi** output.
- The output is **linearly proportional** to the liquid level.

**Conversion of Buoyant Force to Pneumatic Output:**

$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_d(h)$$

$$T = F_b \cdot L_{arm}$$

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L_t}{G \cdot J}$$

$$P_{out} = 3 + \left( \frac{\theta - \theta_{min}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{min}} \right) \times 12 \text{ psi}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $R = 6 \text{ cm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$, $m = 0.4 \text{ kg}$, $\rho_l = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Volume of the sphere.**

$$V_d = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.06)^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (2.16 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$V_d = 9.048 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — (i) Buoyant force when fully submerged.**

$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_d = 850 \times 9.81 \times 9.048 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_b = 7.544 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Apparent weight.**

$$W_{float} = m \cdot g = 0.4 \times 9.81 = 3.924 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{app} = W_{float} - F_b = 3.924 - 7.544 = -3.62 \text{ N}$$

The negative sign indicates the float is buoyant and will float at the surface, never fully submerging naturally.

**Step 4 — (iii) Submerged depth at neutral equilibrium.**

For neutral equilibrium: $F_b = W_{float}$

$$\rho_l \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3} = m \cdot g$$

$$\frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3} = \frac{m}{\rho_l}$$

$$\frac{\pi h^2 (3 \times 0.06 - h)}{3} = \frac{0.4}{850}$$

$$\frac{\pi h^2 (0.18 - h)}{3} = 4.706 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\pi h^2 (0.18 - h) = 1.412 \times 10^{-3}$$

Solving this cubic equation numerically (by trial or Newton-Raphson):

$$h \approx 0.053 \text{ m} = 5.3 \text{ cm}$$

This is the equilibrium submergence depth.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Choice-2]**

**(a)** Compare **ball float**, **displacer**, and **magnetic float** type level sensors in terms of principle, output, application, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **displaced volume of a spherical float** submerged to a depth `h` in a liquid, starting from the integral of disk elements. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Ball Float | Displacer | Magnetic Float |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Principle** | Buoyancy on partially submerged sphere | Buoyancy on fully immersed displacer | Buoyancy + magnetic field switching |
| **Output** | 3–15 psi / 4–20 mA | 3–15 psi / 4–20 mA | Switch contact (on/off) |
| **Application** | Continuous level in tanks | Interface & density measurement | Point level alarms (high/low) |
| **Limitation** | Affected by surface turbulence | Requires calibration for density | Discrete output only |

#### Model Solution for (b):

(Refer to **Derivation I in Section 3.1** above. The full integration is reproduced for completeness.)

The volume of the submerged spherical cap is obtained by integrating disk cross-sections from $z = (R - h)$ to $z = R$:

$$V_d = \int_{R-h}^{R} \pi (R^2 - z^2) \, dz$$

$$V_d = \pi \left[ R^2 z - \frac{z^3}{3} \right]_{R-h}^{R}$$

After expansion and simplification:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Core Principle:** Ball float operation is fundamentally based on **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy** — a force-based, not displacement-based, measurement.
- **Displaced Volume Formula:** $V_d = \frac{\pi h^2 (3R - h)}{3}$ — must be derived, not just quoted.
- **Buoyant Force Formula:** $F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_d$ — the *single most important* equation.
- **Net / Apparent Weight:** Always $W_{app} = W_{float} - F_b$. A *negative* value means the float is positively buoyant.
- **Torque Tube Function:** Provides **frictionless, leak-proof** rotation for pressurized vessels. Its twist angle $\theta$ is the key intermediate variable.
- **Standard Outputs:** **3–15 psi (pneumatic)** or **4–20 mA (electronic)** — both are linear with liquid level.
- **Operating Range:** Best for **specific gravities 0.5 to 1.5**; not suitable for highly viscous or slurry liquids.
- **Material Considerations:** Floats must be chemically compatible — stainless steel (SS316), Monel, PTFE-lined, Hastelloy.
- **Diagram Essentials:** Always include — Tank, Float, Lever, Torque Tube, Nozzle/Flapper, Relay, Output.
- **Numerical Pitfalls:** Convert **all lengths to meters**, **densities to kg/m³**, and **forces to Newtons** before computing.
- **Comparison Table:** Be ready to compare with **displacer** (force-balance type) and **float switches** (magnetic reed).
- **Examiner's Favorite Topics:** (1) Working principle with diagram, (2) Derivation of displaced volume, (3) Numerical problems on buoyant force, (4) Comparison with displacer type.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor. Displacer Type - Torque Tube Assembly. Electrical Methods - Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor. Displacer Type - Torque Tube Assembly. Electrical Methods - Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
A **Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor** is a primary level measurement device that infers the height (level) of a liquid column inside a vessel by measuring the hydrostatic pressure exerted by the liquid at a fixed reference point (typically the bottom of the vessel or a tap point). The fundamental governing principle is the **Hydrostatic Pressure Equation** derived from **Pascal's Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Hydrostatic pressure sensors are classified as *pressure-based* level transmitters, NOT as direct physical-contact displacement sensors. They exploit the linear relationship between liquid column height and pressure at the base.

The general governing relationship is expressed as:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:
- $P$ = Hydrostatic pressure at the measurement point (in **Pa** or **bar**)
- $\rho$ = Density of the process liquid (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = Vertical height of the liquid column above the sensor (in **m**)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are swimming in a deep pool. The deeper you go, the more your ears hurt. That "pressure" feeling is exactly what a hydrostatic pressure sensor measures! The sensor is essentially placed at the *bottom* of a tank, and it "feels" the weight of all the liquid stacked on top of it. If the liquid level rises, the weight of liquid above increases, and the sensor detects more pressure. Since the relationship is perfectly linear, the controller can simply convert this pressure reading back into a level reading. It's the same as how a bathroom weighing scale works — it doesn't directly measure "you," but measures the force (pressure) you exert on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hydrostatic Works for Closed & Open Tanks:** Because the system relies only on the *height* of the column (not on tank shape), the same sensor works for spherical, cylindrical, or rectangular vessels. As long as the liquid is in a continuous, unbroken column above the sensor, the height can be measured accurately.

---

## 1.2 Displacer Type Level Sensor — Torque Tube Assembly

### Formal Definition
A **Displacer-type level sensor** is a buoyancy-based level measurement device that uses a solid cylindrical displacer element (partially or fully immersed in the process liquid) whose effective weight changes as the liquid level rises or falls. The change in apparent weight is converted into an angular rotation by a **Torque Tube Assembly**, which is then translated into a measurable pneumatic or electrical output signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Examiners consistently test the *Torque Tube* — a sealed, twisted hollow tube that converts an axial force into a measurable angular rotation **WITHOUT** using any sliding seals, packing glands, or dynamic O-rings. This makes it friction-free and ideal for high-pressure, hazardous, and sanitary process industries.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a cork floating in a bathtub. When you push the cork down into the water, you can feel the water "pushing back" with an upward force (buoyancy). The deeper the cork goes, the harder the water pushes up. A *displacer* works exactly opposite to a float — it is **heavier than the liquid**, so it sinks. But the liquid still pushes it *up* with a buoyancy force. As the level rises, more of the displacer is submerged, the upward push increases, and the displacer appears *lighter*. This apparent loss of weight is what the torque tube "feels" and converts into a rotation.

### The Torque Tube — The Heart of the Sensor
A **torque tube** is a thin-walled, sealed hollow cylinder (typically made of Inconel or stainless steel) that is *twisted* along its longitudinal axis. One end is rigidly fixed to the instrument housing; the other end is free to rotate by a small angle (typically 4° to 10°). The displacer is rigidly attached to the free end via a connecting rod.

When buoyancy changes, an axial force is transmitted through the rod to the torque tube, causing it to twist. The angular twist is proportional to the net force and is mechanically linked to a feedback system (pneumatic or electronic) that produces the final output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why No Sliding Seals?** Traditional designs used a packing gland or a mechanical shaft passing through the vessel wall, which would leak under pressure. The torque tube is a *single-piece* hermetically sealed barrier — there are NO dynamic seals, NO friction, and NO leakage paths. This is the key engineering innovation of this assembly.

---

## 1.3 Electrical Methods — Resistance Type Level Measurement

### Formal Definition
The **Resistance-type level measurement** is an electrical method that determines the level of a *conductive* liquid by measuring the variation in electrical resistance between two or more electrodes (or between an electrode and the metallic vessel wall) immersed in the liquid. The principle relies on the fact that as the conductive liquid rises and contacts more of the electrode surface, the effective electrical resistance between the electrodes **decreases** in direct proportion to the submerged length of the electrode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** This method is **only suitable for conductive liquids** (e.g., water, acids, alkalis, salt solutions). It is **NOT** suitable for hydrocarbons, oils, or pure deionized water. Always state the conductivity limitation in exam answers.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a vertical metal rod (electrode) stuck into an empty metal bucket (vessel wall). If the bucket is empty, no current flows between the rod and the bucket because air is a perfect insulator. Now, pour water into the bucket. As soon as the water touches the rod, current starts flowing because water conducts electricity (slightly). The more water you pour, the more of the rod is submerged, and the more "paths" for current to flow. Eventually, the resistance becomes very small. A smart circuit measures this resistance and converts it into a level reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic Real-World Example:** This is exactly how a *boiler water-level indicator* works in steam boilers, and how *conductivity-based* float switches detect water in sump tanks. The two electrodes act as a simple variable resistor whose value depends on the water level.

### Modes of Operation

| Mode | Configuration | Application |
|------|---------------|-------------|
| **Single Electrode Mode** | One electrode + vessel wall as ground | Simple high/low level alarms |
| **Two Electrode Mode** | Two parallel rod electrodes of unequal length | Continuous level via Wheatstone bridge |
| **Three Electrode Mode** | Three electrodes (low, mid, high) | Multi-point switching & alarms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use of AC Excitation:** A pure DC voltage cannot be applied across the electrodes because **electrolysis** would occur, causing the electrodes to corrode rapidly and the liquid to be chemically altered. Therefore, an **AC voltage source** (typically 5–25 V at 50 Hz or a low-frequency oscillator) is always used to drive the resistance measurement bridge.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor — Detailed Theory

### Operating Principle
A hydrostatic pressure sensor consists of:
1. A **pressure-sensing diaphragm** (piezoresistive, strain gauge, or capacitive) exposed to the hydrostatic head of the liquid.
2. A **reference chamber** exposed to atmospheric pressure (for open tanks) or to the vapor space pressure (for closed/pressurized tanks).
3. A **signal conditioning circuit** that linearizes and amplifies the output.

The sensor measures the **gauge pressure** $P_g$ at the bottom of the tank:

$$P_g = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

For *closed* pressurized tanks, a **differential pressure (DP) transmitter** is used. One side of the diaphragm senses the hydrostatic head $\rho g h$, while the other side senses the vapor space pressure $P_v$. The transmitter output is proportional to the *differential*:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{bottom}} - P_{\text{vapor}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

### The "Wet Leg" vs. "Dry Leg" Configuration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Topic:** The DP cell for level measurement in a *closed tank* can be connected in two ways:

**1. Dry Leg (Direct Connection):**
The high-pressure side is connected to the bottom tap, and the low-pressure side is connected to the *vapor space* (gas above the liquid). Since gas density is negligible, no compensation is required.

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**2. Wet Leg (Sealed Condenser Leg):**
The low-pressure side is connected to the vapor space through a *seal pot* filled with a reference fluid (often the same as the process liquid when condensable, or a high-density silicone oil). The capillary/leg is filled with this reference fluid, so the low-pressure side reads:

$$P_{\text{low}} = P_v + \rho_{\text{ref}} \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{leg}}$$

The transmitter therefore reads:

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{\text{ref}} \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{leg}}$$

The **zero suppression** and **zero elevation** adjustments are used to compensate for these effects.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Operation
1. **Liquid Column Exerts Pressure:** The weight of the liquid column above the sensor creates a hydrostatic pressure given by $P = \rho g h$.
2. **Diaphragm Deflection:** This pressure deflects a sensing diaphragm by a microscopic amount.
3. **Strain Gauge Bridge:** Four strain gauges (Wheatstone bridge) bonded to the diaphragm change resistance proportionally to strain.
4. **Bridge Output:** An excitation voltage produces a millivolt-level differential output.
5. **Signal Conditioning:** A 4–20 mA current loop transmitter converts this into a standard industrial signal.
6. **Level Indication:** The DCS/PLC receives 4–20 mA and displays the level in mm, cm, %, or m³.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Oil & Gas:** Tank farm level monitoring, separator level control, LNG vessel level.
- **Chemical:** Acid/alkali storage tanks in chlor-alkali plants.
- **Water & Wastewater:** Reservoir level, wet well level in lift stations, chemical dosing tanks.
- **Power Plants:** Deaerator level, condenser hotwell level, boiler drum level (as a backup).
- **Pulp & Paper:** Stock chest level, black liquor storage.

---

## 2.2 Displacer Type — Torque Tube Assembly — Detailed Theory

### Archimedes' Buoyancy Principle
The *apparent weight* of the displacer is reduced by an amount equal to the weight of the liquid displaced:

$$W_{\text{apparent}} = W_{\text{displacer}} - F_{\text{buoyancy}}$$

$$F_{\text{buoyancy}} = \rho_{\text{liquid}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{submerged}}$$

where $V_{\text{submerged}}$ is the volume of the displacer actually immersed in the liquid.

### Net Force on the Displacer
If the displacer has total volume $V_d$ and cross-sectional area $A_d$, and the liquid level submerges a length $L_{\text{sub}}$ from the bottom:

$$F_{\text{net}} = (\rho_d \cdot g \cdot V_d) - (\rho_L \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot L_{\text{sub}})$$

The **change in net force** for a change in level $\Delta h$ is:

$$\Delta F = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot \Delta h$$

This linear relationship between force change and level change is what makes the displacer-type instrument a true *linear* level measurement device.

### Torque Tube Mechanics
The torque tube acts as a **torsion spring**. The twist angle $\theta$ is related to the applied torque $T$ by:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

where:
- $T$ = applied torque (N·m)
- $L$ = length of the active twist section (m)
- $G$ = shear modulus of the tube material (**Pa**)
- $J$ = polar moment of inertia of the tube cross-section (m⁴)

For a thin-walled tube of outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$:

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right)$$

The torque $T$ is generated by the buoyancy force acting at a moment arm $r$ (the perpendicular distance from the axis of the torque tube to the line of action of the buoyancy force):

$$T = F_{\text{buoyancy}} \cdot r$$

### Force-Balance Feedback System
The torque tube is connected to a feedback system:
- **Pneumatic version:** A nozzle-flapper amplifier senses the rotation and balances it with a feedback bellows.
- **Electronic version (Smart Transmitter):** A Hall-effect sensor or LVDT senses the angular twist, and a digital PID controller drives a force-balance motor.

This **closed-loop force-balance** architecture ensures excellent linearity, repeatability, and immunity to vibration.

### Why Use a Displacer Instead of a Float?
- **Floats** are affected by surface turbulence, foam, and wave action.
- **Displacers** are *fully submerged* and average the density over their entire length, giving more stable and accurate readings — especially for **interface measurement** (e.g., oil-water interface in a separator).

---

## 2.3 Electrical Methods — Resistance Type — Detailed Theory

### Governing Equation
The resistance of a conductive liquid column between two parallel plate electrodes is given by:

$$R = \frac{\rho_e \cdot L}{A}$$

where:
- $R$ = resistance (Ω)
- $\rho_e$ = *electrical* resistivity of the liquid (Ω·m) — note: NOT mass density!
- $L$ = distance between electrodes (m)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the current path (m²)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** Do not confuse **mass density** $\rho$ (used in hydrostatic and displacer equations) with **electrical resistivity** $\rho_e$ (used in the resistance equation). They are entirely different physical quantities and use different units.

### Two-Electrode (Rods) Resistance Equation
For two parallel cylindrical electrodes of length $\ell$, diameter $d$, separated by distance $D$ (much larger than $d$), partially immersed in a conductive liquid to a depth $h$:

$$R = \frac{\rho_e}{\pi \ell} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{D}{d} \right) \quad \text{(approximate, for } h = \ell \text{)}$$

When the liquid level is at height $h$ (submerging only a fraction of the electrode length $\ell$):

$$R(h) = \frac{\rho_e}{\pi h} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{D}{d} \right)$$

The resistance is **inversely proportional** to the submerged length, which is the key principle of operation.

### Wheatstone Bridge Implementation
The two electrodes form one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R(h)}$$

At balance, no current flows through the galvanometer. As the level changes, $R(h)$ changes, unbalancing the bridge and producing an output voltage proportional to the level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention that an **AC excitation source** must be used in place of a DC battery to prevent electrolysis and electrode polarization. A typical excitation is 5–10 V RMS at 50 Hz or 1 kHz.

### Advantages
- Simple, inexpensive, no moving parts.
- Suitable for high-pressure and high-temperature applications.
- Excellent for conductive liquids (water, acids, brines).
- Direct compatibility with digital counters and PLCs.

### Limitations
- Only works for *conductive* liquids.
- Electrodes must be corrosion-resistant (use stainless steel, titanium, or platinum-coated).
- Sensitive to foam, coating, and scale buildup.
- Requires periodic calibration to account for conductivity drift.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary/Use Condition |
|---|---------|-------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $P$: Pa, $\rho$: kg/m³, $g$: 9.81 m/s², $h$: m | Open tank, single liquid |
| 2 | Hydrostatic with vapor pressure | $\Delta P = \rho g h + P_v - P_v = \rho g h$ | Same as above | Closed tank, dry leg |
| 3 | Wet leg DP transmitter | $\Delta P = \rho g h - \rho_{\text{ref}} g h_{\text{leg}}$ | $\rho_{\text{ref}}$: ref fluid density | Closed tank, condensable vapor |
| 4 | Archimedes buoyancy force | $F_b = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{sub}}$ | $F_b$: N, $V_{\text{sub}}$: m³ | Any submerged body |
| 5 | Apparent displacer weight | $W_{\text{app}} = W_d - F_b$ | All forces in N | Displacer in liquid |
| 6 | Net force change vs. level | $\Delta F = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot \Delta h$ | $A_d$: displacer CSA (m²) | Linear relationship |
| 7 | Torque tube twist angle | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | $\theta$: rad, $T$: N·m, $G$: Pa, $J$: m⁴ | Small-angle linear region |
| 8 | Torque tube polar moment | $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right)$ | $D_o, D_i$: m | Thin-walled hollow shaft |
| 9 | Torque generated | $T = F_b \cdot r$ | $r$: moment arm (m) | Lever-arm geometry |
| 10 | Resistance of liquid column | $R = \dfrac{\rho_e \cdot L}{A}$ | $\rho_e$: Ω·m, $R$: Ω | Conductive liquid only |
| 11 | Electrode resistance vs. level | $R(h) = \dfrac{\rho_e \cdot \ln(D/d)}{\pi \cdot h}$ | $D$: electrode separation, $d$: electrode dia. | Inverse proportionality |
| 12 | Wheatstone bridge balance | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ | All resistances in Ω | Zero galvanometer current |
| 13 | Sensitivity of displacer | $S = \dfrac{\Delta F}{\Delta h} = \rho_L g A_d$ | N/m | Higher for denser liquids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check (KTU Board Trick):** Pressure is often expressed in **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). When solving numericals, ALWAYS convert densities to SI (kg/m³) and heights to meters. The "engineering" unit kgf/cm² is **not** SI but is sometimes used in older KTU question papers. Stay safe — use SI throughout.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation

### Statement
The pressure at any point in a static fluid is given by $P = \rho g h$.

### Derivation
Consider a vertical column of static fluid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$. The mass of fluid in the column is:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$$

The weight (force) exerted by this column on the base is:

$$F = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$$

The pressure is defined as force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}$$

After canceling the common area $A$:

$$\boxed{P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h}$$

This is the fundamental equation used by all hydrostatic pressure level sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The pressure depends only on the *vertical height* of the fluid, NOT on the cross-sectional area or the shape of the container. This is why a hydrostatic pressure sensor can measure level in *any* shaped tank.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Buoyancy Force (Archimedes' Principle)

### Statement
A body fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced.

### Derivation
Consider a fully submerged body of arbitrary shape. The pressure at the top of the body is $P_{\text{top}} = P_0 + \rho g z_{\text{top}}$ and the pressure at the bottom is $P_{\text{bot}} = P_0 + \rho g z_{\text{bot}}$, where $z_{\text{bot}} > z_{\text{top}}$.

The net upward force on the body is the integral of pressure over its surface. For a vertical column element at depth $z$:

$$dF_{\text{up}} = P(z) \cdot dA = (P_0 + \rho g z) \cdot dA$$

The horizontal pressure components cancel by symmetry. The net vertical force is:

$$F_b = \int (P_{\text{bot}} - P_{\text{top}}) \cdot dA_{\text{horiz}} = \rho g (z_{\text{bot}} - z_{\text{top}}) \cdot A_{\text{horiz}}$$

But $(z_{\text{bot}} - z_{\text{top}}) \cdot A_{\text{horiz}}$ is exactly the *volume* of the body $V$:

$$F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$$

For a *partially* immersed body, $V$ becomes the *submerged volume* $V_{\text{sub}}$:

$$\boxed{F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{sub}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensitivity of a Displacer-Type Sensor

### Statement
The sensitivity of a displacer-type level sensor is $S = \rho_L g A_d$ (N per meter of level change).

### Derivation
Let the displacer be a vertical cylinder of cross-sectional area $A_d$ and total length $L_d$, suspended from the torque tube. Let the liquid level rise by a small amount $\Delta h$ (where $0 \le \Delta h \le L_d$).

The increase in submerged volume is:

$$\Delta V_{\text{sub}} = A_d \cdot \Delta h$$

The corresponding increase in buoyancy force is:

$$\Delta F_b = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot \Delta V_{\text{sub}} = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot \Delta h$$

The sensitivity, defined as the ratio of change in output force to change in level, is:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\Delta F_b}{\Delta h} = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot A_d \quad \left[\frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}}\right]}$$

**Observation:** The sensitivity increases with (1) higher liquid density, (2) larger displacer cross-section, and (3) stronger gravity. This is why displacers are *more sensitive* in dense liquids like acids compared to light liquids like gasoline.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Torque Tube Twist Angle

### Statement
The angular twist of a torque tube is $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ for small deformations.

### Derivation
For a circular shaft under pure torsion, the shear strain $\gamma$ at the outer surface of radius $r = D_o / 2$ is:

$$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

By Hooke's law for shear, the shear stress is:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

The torque carried by the cross-section is the integral of shear stress times radial distance over the area:

$$T = \int \tau \cdot r \cdot dA = \int G \cdot \frac{r^2 \cdot \theta}{L} \cdot dA = \frac{G \theta}{L} \int r^2 \cdot dA$$

By definition, the polar moment of inertia is $J = \int r^2 dA$, so:

$$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Solving for the twist angle:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}}$$

For a thin-walled tube with outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$:

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right)$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Sensor in a Tank

**Problem:** A hydrostatic pressure sensor is installed at the bottom of an open tank containing oil of density **880 kg/m³**. The sensor reads a gauge pressure of **0.345 bar**. Calculate the height of the oil column. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Solution

**Given:**
- $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $P_g = 0.345 \text{ bar} = 0.345 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 34500 \text{ Pa}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Find:** $h = ?$

**Step 1:** Write the governing equation:

$$P_g = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $h$:

$$h = \frac{P_g}{\rho \cdot g}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$h = \frac{34500}{880 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the denominator:

$$880 \times 9.81 = 8632.8 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Compute the height:

$$h = \frac{34500}{8632.8} = 3.996 \text{ m} \approx 4.0 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The height of the oil column is approximately **4.0 m**.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 2 — Displacer Net Force Calculation

**Problem:** A displacer element of cylindrical shape has a diameter of **5 cm** and length of **60 cm**. Its material has a density of **2400 kg/m³**. The liquid in the vessel has a density of **1200 kg/m³** and the level submerges the displacer to **75%** of its length. Calculate the net downward force on the displacer. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Solution

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Length $L_d = 60 \text{ cm} = 0.60 \text{ m}$
- Displacer density $\rho_d = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Liquid density $\rho_L = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Submerged fraction = 75% = 0.75
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Find:** $F_{\text{net}}$ (downward)

**Step 1:** Calculate the total volume of the displacer:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot L_d = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 \times 0.60$$

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0025 \times 0.60 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0015$$

$$V_d = 0.0011781 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the actual weight of the displacer:

$$W_d = \rho_d \cdot g \cdot V_d = 2400 \times 9.81 \times 0.0011781$$

$$W_d = 27.732 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the submerged volume:

$$V_{\text{sub}} = 0.75 \times V_d = 0.75 \times 0.0011781 = 0.0008836 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the buoyancy force:

$$F_b = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{sub}} = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.0008836$$

$$F_b = 10.401 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the net downward force:

$$F_{\text{net}} = W_d - F_b = 27.732 - 10.401$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{net}} = 17.331 \text{ N}}$$

**Interpretation:** This net downward force of 17.33 N is the load transmitted to the torque tube. If the level rises further, the buoyancy increases and this net force decreases — the torque tube senses this change and produces the output signal.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Problem 3 — Resistance-Type Electrode Level

**Problem:** Two vertical stainless-steel electrodes, each **2 mm** in diameter, are mounted in a tank with a center-to-center separation of **4 cm**. The tank is filled with brine (electrical resistivity **0.05 Ω·m**). The electrodes are **50 cm** long and are fully submerged. Calculate the resistance between the electrodes.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $d = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$
- $D = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_e = 0.05 \text{ Ω·m}$
- $\ell = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$

**Find:** $R$ between electrodes.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula for two parallel cylindrical electrodes:

$$R = \frac{\rho_e}{\pi \ell} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{D}{d} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\ln\!\left( \frac{D}{d} \right) = \ln\!\left( \frac{0.04}{0.002} \right) = \ln(20)$$

$$\ln(20) = 2.9957$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator:

$$\pi \ell = \pi \times 0.50 = 1.5708 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute all values:

$$R = \frac{0.05}{1.5708} \times 2.9957$$

$$R = 0.03183 \times 2.9957 = 0.0954 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.095 \text{ Ω}}$$

**Interpretation:** The resistance is very small (about 95 milliohms) because brine is highly conductive. A small AC voltage (a few volts) is sufficient to drive a measurable current.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Vessel with Liquid] --> B[Hydrostatic Head rho*g*h]
    B --> C[Pressure Tap / Impulse Line]
    C --> D[Diaphragm of Pressure Sensor]
    D --> E[Piezoresistive Strain Gauge Bridge]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    F --> G[Microcontroller Linearization]
    G --> H[4-20 mA Current Loop Output]
    H --> I[DCS / PLC / Recorder]
    I --> J[Operator Display Level in mm or percent]

    K[Atmospheric / Vapor Reference] --> D
    L[Power Supply 24 V DC] --> F
    L --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Liquid weight creates pressure at the diaphragm. The diaphragm strain is converted by a Wheatstone bridge into a millivolt signal, amplified, linearized, and transmitted as a 4–20 mA analog signal to the control room.

---

## 4.2 Displacer + Torque Tube Assembly — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Process Liquid in Vessel] --> A2[Submerged Displacer Cylinder]
    A2 --> A3[Buoyancy Force Fb acts upward]
    A3 --> A4[Net Force Wapp = Wd minus Fb]
    A4 --> A5[Connecting Rod transmits to Torque Tube]
    A5 --> A6[Torque Tube Twists by angle theta]
    A6 --> A7[Angular Displacement Sensor LVDT or Hall]
    A7 --> A8[Electronic PID Force Balance Amplifier]
    A8 --> A9[Feedback Motor / Bellows]
    A9 --> A5
    A8 --> A10[4 to 20 mA Output to DCS]

    B1[Fixed End of Torque Tube] --> A5
    B2[24 V DC Supply] --> A8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The torque tube has NO sliding seals. The twisted shaft IS the only barrier between the process and the electronics. The closed-loop force-balance feedback ensures linearity and immunity to vibration.

---

## 4.3 Resistance-Type Level Measurement — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[AC Excitation Source 5 to 10 V at 50 Hz] --> R2[Transformer / Isolator]
    R2 --> R3[Electrode A in Tank]
    R3 --> R4[Conductive Liquid Column of height h]
    R4 --> R5[Electrode B / Vessel Wall Ground]
    R5 --> R6[Variable Resistance R h]
    R6 --> R7[Wheatstone Bridge Arm]
    R7 --> R8[Bridge Amplifier]
    R8 --> R9[Linearization Circuit]
    R9 --> R10[Level Display in cm or m]

    R11[Decoupling Capacitor DC Block] --> R1
    R12[Calibration Trimpot] --> R8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AC Excitation?** If DC is used, the electrodes will electrolyze the liquid (a chemical reaction), causing rapid corrosion and gas bubble formation. AC reverses polarity each half cycle, preventing net electrochemical reaction. This is a *must-mention* in KTU answers.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Three Methods at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Hydrostatic Pressure Sensor] --> H2[Best for: Open tanks, pressurized vessels]
    H2 --> H3[Output: 4 to 20 mA proportional to level]

    D1[Displacer + Torque Tube] --> D2[Best for: Interface, density, hazardous fluids]
    D2 --> D3[Output: Pneumatic 3 to 15 psi or 4 to 20 mA]

    E1[Resistance Type Electrodes] --> E2[Best for: Conductive liquids only]
    E2 --> E3[Output: Resistance or AC voltage proportional to level]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Decision Table for KTU Viva:** Choose **hydrostatic** for storage tanks; choose **displacer** for interface measurement and high-pressure hazardous service; choose **resistance** for simple, low-cost conductive-liquid alarms and switches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
