# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of a hydrostatic pressure type level sensor with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A hydrostatic pressure type level sensor measures the level of a liquid by detecting the **hydrostatic pressure** exerted by the liquid column at a fixed reference point. The governing equation is **$P = \rho g h$**, where $\rho$ is the liquid density, $g$ is gravity, and $h$ is the height of the liquid column. The pressure sensor (typically a diaphragm with strain gauges) is mounted at the bottom of the tank or at a fixed tap. As the level changes, the pressure varies linearly, and the output is transmitted as a **4–20 mA** signal proportional to level. For closed tanks, a **differential pressure (DP) transmitter** is used with dry-leg or wet-leg configuration. *(Diagram: pressure tap → diaphragm → bridge → amplifier → 4–20 mA output.)* **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a torque tube? Why is it used in displacer-type level instruments?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **torque tube** is a thin-walled, sealed, twisted hollow shaft that converts an axial force (or torque) into a measurable **angular rotation** without any sliding seals, packing glands, or friction. In displacer-type level instruments, the buoyancy force acting on the displacer creates a net force that is transmitted via a connecting rod to the torque tube, causing it to twist. The twist angle is proportional to the net force, and hence to the liquid level. The torque tube is used because it:
1. Provides a **hermetically sealed** barrier between the process and the instrument (no leakage).
2. Operates with **zero friction** and hence excellent sensitivity and repeatability.
3. Is suitable for **high-pressure, high-temperature, and hazardous** process fluids. **(3 Marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks. The typical sub-division is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative questions (Choice A and Choice B) are provided below — students may answer either one.

---

### ✦ Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **displacer-type level measurement system using a torque tube assembly**. Discuss its advantages for interface measurement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
The system consists of:
1. **Displacer** — A hollow cylindrical element (typically stainless steel or Inconel), heavier than the process liquid, suspended vertically from a connecting rod.
2. **Connecting Rod** — Transmits the net force from the displacer to the torque tube.
3. **Torque Tube Assembly** — A sealed, twisted hollow shaft (one end fixed to the housing, the other end free to rotate by a small angle).
4. **Force-Balance Mechanism** — A pneumatic nozzle-flapper or electronic (LVDT/Hall-effect) feedback system.
5. **Housing** — Explosion-proof or weather-proof enclosure with terminal block and adjustments.
6. **Output Stage** — 3–15 psi pneumatic or 4–20 mA electronic output.

**Working (3 Marks):**
When the liquid level rises, more of the displacer is immersed, increasing the **buoyancy force** $F_b = \rho_L g V_{\text{sub}}$. The apparent weight of the displacer decreases. The connecting rod transmits this reduced downward force to the torque tube, which **twists** by an angle $\theta$ proportional to the change in net force. The angular twist is sensed by the feedback mechanism, which restores equilibrium via a force-balance loop. The position of the feedback element (nozzle pressure or motor position) is converted into a standard 4–20 mA output signal proportional to the level.

**Advantages for Interface Measurement (1 Mark):**
The displacer integrates the buoyancy effect over its entire immersed length. At an oil-water interface, the *average density* of the fluid surrounding the displacer changes, providing a continuous and stable reading of the interface position. This is far superior to float-based sensors, which are affected by surface waves, foam, and emulsion layers.

---

**(b)** A cylindrical displacer of diameter **8 cm** and length **80 cm** is immersed in a liquid of density **950 kg/m³** to a depth of **60%**. The displacer material density is **2600 kg/m³**. Calculate:
(i) The buoyant force on the displacer.
(ii) The net downward force on the displacer.
(iii) The sensitivity of the system in N/m. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d = 0.08 \text{ m}$, $L_d = 0.80 \text{ m}$, $A_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $\rho_d = 2600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_L = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Submerged fraction = 0.60
- $V_d = A_d \times L_d = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.80 = 4.0212 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$

**Step 1: Buoyant Force**
$$V_{\text{sub}} = 0.60 \times V_d = 0.60 \times 4.0212 \times 10^{-3} = 2.4127 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$F_b = \rho_L g V_{\text{sub}} = 950 \times 9.81 \times 2.4127 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_b = 22.486 \text{ N}} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 2: Net Downward Force**
$$W_d = \rho_d g V_d = 2600 \times 9.81 \times 4.0212 \times 10^{-3} = 102.56 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{\text{net}} = W_d - F_b = 102.56 - 22.486$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{net}} = 80.07 \text{ N}} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 3: Sensitivity**
$$S = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot A_d = 950 \times 9.81 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{S = 46.84 \text{ N/m}} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Interpretation (1 Mark):** A sensitivity of 46.84 N/m means that for every 1 m rise in level, the net downward force on the displacer decreases by 46.84 N. This change is faithfully reproduced as a 4–20 mA output by the torque tube electronics.

---

### ✦ Choice B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **resistance type method of level measurement** for conductive liquids. Why is AC excitation used instead of DC? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
The resistance-type level sensor measures the level of a *conductive* liquid by detecting the change in electrical resistance between two electrodes (or between an electrode and the metallic vessel wall) as the liquid level rises and falls. The resistance is **inversely proportional** to the immersed length of the electrodes.

**Construction & Circuit (3 Marks):**
- Two vertical rod electrodes of suitable material (stainless steel, titanium, or platinum-coated) are mounted inside the tank at a fixed separation.
- An **AC voltage source** (5–10 V at 50 Hz) is connected across the electrodes through a transformer and current-limiting resistor.
- The electrodes form one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**; the other three arms are precision fixed resistors.
- A bridge amplifier detects the unbalance voltage, which is proportional to the level.
- The output is calibrated and displayed in engineering units (cm, m, %).

**Working (1 Mark):**
When the liquid rises and contacts more of the electrode surface, the resistance between electrodes falls, unbalancing the bridge. The amplifier output rises, indicating higher level.

**Why AC Excitation (1 Mark):**
DC excitation causes **electrolysis** of the conductive liquid, leading to:
- Rapid corrosion of the electrodes.
- Generation of gas bubbles that alter the resistance.
- Polarization of the electrodes, causing unstable readings.

AC alternates polarity at high frequency (50 Hz or 1 kHz), preventing any net electrochemical reaction. A series capacitor is often used to block any DC offset.

---

**(b)** A hydrostatic pressure sensor at the bottom of an open tank reads **1.85 bar** when filled with a liquid of density **1180 kg/m³**. Calculate:
(i) The height of the liquid column.
(ii) The new pressure reading if **25% of the liquid is drained out**.
(iii) The pressure at the **midpoint** of the tank when full. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1.85 \text{ bar} = 1.85 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho = 1180 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Full-Tank Height (2 Marks)**
$$h_{\text{full}} = \frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^5}{1180 \times 9.81} = \frac{185000}{11575.8}$$

$$\boxed{h_{\text{full}} = 15.98 \text{ m} \approx 16.0 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2: Pressure after 25% Drain (3 Marks)**
After draining 25%, the remaining level is 75% of the full height:

$$h_{\text{new}} = 0.75 \times 15.98 = 11.985 \text{ m}$$

$$P_2 = \rho g h_{\text{new}} = 1180 \times 9.81 \times 11.985$$

$$P_2 = 13873.5 \times 11.985 \approx 1.3887 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 1.389 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 3: Pressure at Midpoint when Full (2 Marks)**
At mid-height, $h_{\text{mid}} = 15.98 / 2 = 7.99 \text{ m}$:

$$P_{\text{mid}} = \rho g h_{\text{mid}} = 1180 \times 9.81 \times 7.99 = 92493 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{mid}} \approx 0.925 \text{ bar}}$$

**Sanity Check (1 Mark):** $P_{\text{mid}} \approx P_1 / 2$, which is consistent with the linear hydrostatic relationship.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing mass density $\rho$ with electrical resistivity $\rho_e$.** They are different quantities with different units. The hydrostatic equation uses kg/m³; the resistance equation uses Ω·m. Using one in the other gives nonsense.
> 2. **Forgetting the vapor pressure correction in closed tanks.** A common error is to write $\Delta P = \rho g h$ for a closed tank, ignoring the vapor space pressure. The correct equation is $\Delta P = \rho g h - P_v$ for a *wet leg* configuration. Always check whether the tank is open or closed.
> 3. **Using DC excitation for resistance-type level sensors.** This is an instant 1-mark deduction in KTU papers. The correct answer is always **AC excitation** at 50 Hz or higher.
> 4. **Drawing a torque tube with a sliding seal or shaft.** This is the *single most common* mistake in KTU diagrams. The defining feature of a torque tube is that it is a **sealed, twisted hollow shaft** with NO sliding parts. Drawing a packing gland around it is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify units** in the final numerical answer. Always write Pa or bar for pressure, N for force, m for height, and Ω for resistance. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
> 6. **Inverting the displacer force equation.** Some students write $F_{\text{net}} = F_b - W_d$, which gives a *negative* value for a heavy displacer. The correct form is $F_{\text{net (downward)}} = W_d - F_b$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Recall Points for KTU Module 4 (Level Measurement):**

- ☐ **Hydrostatic Pressure Sensor:** Uses $P = \rho g h$ at the bottom of the tank. Output is 4–20 mA. Open tanks use gauge pressure; closed tanks use a DP transmitter (dry leg or wet leg).
- ☐ **Bubbler System:** An alternative hydrostatic method that bubbles air/gas through a dip tube; the back-pressure equals the hydrostatic head. Useful for corrosive or slurry liquids.
- ☐ **Displacer-Type Sensor:** Operates on **Archimedes' Buoyancy Principle**; the displacer is *denser* than the liquid. As level rises, buoyancy increases, apparent weight decreases.
- ☐ **Torque Tube:** A **sealed, twisted hollow shaft** with NO sliding seals. Converts axial force into a small angular twist ($\theta = TL / GJ$).
- ☐ **Sensitivity of Displacer:** $S = \rho_L g A_d$ — higher for denser liquids and larger displacer cross-section.
- ☐ **Force-Balance Architecture:** Closed-loop feedback (pneumatic nozzle-flapper or electronic LVDT/Hall-effect) ensures linearity and stability.
- ☐ **Resistance-Type Level Sensor:** Uses **two electrodes** in a **Wheatstone bridge**, excited by **AC voltage** to prevent electrolysis. Works only for **conductive liquids**.
- ☐ **Equation:** $R(h) = \dfrac{\rho_e \ln(D/d)}{\pi h}$ — resistance is *inversely* proportional to submerged length.
- ☐ **Typical Applications:** Boiler water level, sump pump alarms, acid tank level, oil-water interface (using displacer).
- ☐ **Standard Output:** 4–20 mA current loop, 3–15 psi pneumatic, or 1–5 V for electronic controllers.
- ☐ **Always Quote SI Units:** Pa, kg/m³, m, N, Ω, m/s². Convert bar → Pa by multiplying by 10⁵.
- ☐ **Memo Line for KTU:** "Buoyancy varies with submersion; Hydrostatic varies with depth; Resistance varies with conductivity."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conductance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Conductance # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Conductance (G)** in the context of industrial instrumentation is defined as the reciprocal of electrical resistance offered by an electrolytic solution to the flow of electric current. It is a quantitative measure of the ability of an aqueous solution to conduct electricity, which is directly dependent on the concentration and mobility of dissolved ionic species (cations and anions). Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$G = \frac{1}{R}$$ where **R** is the resistance in Ohms (Ω). The standard SI unit of conductance is **Siemens (S)**, which is equivalent to the obsolete unit **mho (℧)**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4 (Analytical Instrumentation), Conductance forms the foundation of **Conductometry** — an electroanalytical technique used for determining the concentration of ionic species, end-point detection in titrations, and purity assessment of industrial water/chemicals. ### Distinction: Conductance vs. Conductivity | Term | Symbol | Definition | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Conductance | $G$ | Reciprocal of resistance ($1/R$) of a given solution volume | Siemens (S) | | Conductivity (Specific Conductance) | $\kappa$ | Conductance of a unit cube (1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm) of solution | S/cm or S/m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conductivity is an intrinsic/intensive property** (independent of geometry), while **conductance is an extensive property** (depends on the dimensions of the conductor). This is analogous to the difference between density and mass. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > **Real-World Analogy — The Crowded Hallway:** > > Imagine a narrow hallway filled with people. Some people walk quickly (highly mobile ions like $\text{H}^+$ and $\text{OH}^-$), while others shuffle slowly (large, heavy ions). Conductance is the measure of **how easily the crowd can flow from one end of the hallway to the other** when a push (voltage) is applied. > > - **Pure distilled water** → an empty hallway → almost no flow → very low conductance. > - **Seawater** → a packed, mixed crowd → ions ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$, etc.) flow easily → high conductance. > - **A salt solution in a titration** → as we add more salt, the crowd gets denser → conductance rises. As we add a reagent that *removes* ions (forms a precipitate or weak electrolyte), the crowd thins → conductance falls. > > This is exactly what an industrial **conductivity sensor** detects — changes in the "crowd density" of ions in a process stream. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following values must be memorized for KTU numerical problems: > > - **Standard cell constant** for typical laboratory cells: $K = 0.1$ to $10 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ > - **Temperature coefficient of conductivity** for most aqueous salt solutions: approximately **+2% per °C** (between 0 °C–100 °C) > - **Conductivity of ultra-pure water** at 25 °C: $\kappa = 0.055 \text{ μS/cm}$ > - **Conductivity of seawater** at 25 °C: $\kappa \approx 53 \text{ mS/cm}$ > - **Ionic mobility of $\text{H}^+$** at infinite dilution at 25 °C: $349.8 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}$ (the highest of all ions — why acid-base titrations produce sharp breaks). --- ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping | CO | Description | How this topic addresses it | |---|---|---| | **CO4** | Apply electro-analytical methods for industrial process variables | Conductance measurement is the basis of conductometry, pH sensing, and ion-selective electrodes | | **CO5** | Select appropriate sensors for specific industrial applications | Differentiating between contacting, inductive, and 4-electrode conductivity sensors | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Electrolytic Conduction Mechanism Unlike metallic conduction (where *electrons* are the charge carriers), electrolytic conduction occurs via the **migration of ions** under the influence of an applied electric field. The key differences are summarized below. | Parameter | Metallic Conduction | Electrolytic Conduction | |---|---|---| | Charge carrier | Free electrons | Cations and anions | | Direction of current | Same as electron flow | Opposite to cation flow | | Effect of temperature | Resistance ↑ with T | Conductance ↑ with T | | Effect of frequency | Negligible | Significant (polarization at high freq.) | | Faraday's laws obeyed? | No | **Yes** (electrolysis occurs) | --- ## 2.2 Ohms Law Applied to Electrolytic Solutions The fundamental governing equation is identical to metallic conduction: $$V = I \cdot R \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I = \frac{V}{R} = V \cdot G$$ However, the resistance of an electrolytic cell depends on the **electrode geometry** and the **intrinsic specific conductivity** of the electrolyte: $$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$ where: - $L$ = distance between the electrodes (cm) - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the electrolyte column (cm²) - $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·cm) - $\kappa$ = specific conductivity (S/cm) --- ## 2.3 The Cell Constant (K) The **cell constant** $K$ encapsulates the geometric dependence of the measurement: $$K = \frac{L}{A} \quad \left[\text{units: cm}^{-1}\right]$$ It is the ratio of the electrode separation to the active electrode area. The relationship between conductivity and measured conductance is therefore: $$\boxed{\kappa = G \cdot K}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the cell constant matters in industry:** No two conductivity probes are physically identical. To compare measurements from different cells, we multiply the raw conductance (S) by the cell constant to get the geometry-independent **conductivity** (S/cm). Manufacturers typically calibrate cells using a **standard KCl solution** of known $\kappa$ (e.g., 0.01 M KCl → $\kappa = 1413 \text{ μS/cm}$ at 25 °C). --- ## 2.4 Equivalent Conductance and Molar Conductance For a more fundamental understanding (essential for derivations in conductometric titrations), two more quantities are defined. **Equivalent Conductance ($\Lambda$):** The conductance of a solution containing **one gram-equivalent** of solute dissolved in $V \text{ cm}^3$ of solvent. $$\Lambda = \kappa \cdot V \quad \left[\text{units: S·cm}^2/\text{eq}\right]$$ **Molar Conductance ($\Lambda_m$):** The conductance of a solution containing **one mole** of solute dissolved in $V \text{ cm}^3$ of solvent. $$\Lambda_m = \kappa \cdot V = \frac{\kappa \cdot 1000}{C} \quad \left[\text{units: S·cm}^2/\text{mol}\right]$$ where $C$ is the molar concentration (mol/L) and the factor 1000 converts litres to cm³. --- ## 2.5 Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions (High-Yield for KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > At **infinite dilution**, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the individual ionic contributions. This is **Kohlrausch's Law**: $$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \cdot \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \cdot \lambda_-^{\circ}$$ where $\nu_+$ and $\nu_-$ are the stoichiometric numbers of cations and anions, and $\lambda^{\circ}$ are the **limiting molar ionic conductivities**. **Application to KTU:** This is the theoretical basis for predicting the conductivity of any strong electrolyte at infinite dilution and explains why acid-base conductometric titrations produce sharp, predictable V-shaped or L-shaped curves. --- ## 2.6 Temperature Dependence of Conductivity The conductivity of an electrolyte varies strongly with temperature, primarily because ionic mobility increases with temperature (decreased viscosity of the solvent): $$\kappa_T = \kappa_{25} \cdot \left[1 + \alpha \cdot (T - 25)\right]$$ where: - $\kappa_T$ = conductivity at temperature $T$ (°C) - $\kappa_{25}$ = conductivity at reference temperature 25 °C - $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient (typically $\approx 0.019$ to $0.025$ /°C, i.e., ~2% per °C) > [!WARNING] > **Industrial Pitfall:** Failing to temperature-compensate a conductivity reading is the single largest source of measurement error in field installations. A 1 °C drift in process temperature causes a ~2% error in measured conductivity. --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are tested repeatedly in KTU University Exams. Memorize the entire table. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $G = 1/R$ | Definition of conductance | S (Siemens) | | 2 | $\kappa = G \cdot K$ | Conductivity from conductance & cell constant | S/cm | | 3 | $K = L/A$ | Cell constant (geometry) | cm⁻¹ | | 4 | $R = \rho \cdot L/A$ | Resistance of electrolyte column | Ω | | 5 | $\Lambda = \kappa \cdot V$ | Equivalent conductance | S·cm²/eq | | 6 | $\Lambda_m = 1000\kappa/C$ | Molar conductance | S·cm²/mol | | 7 | $\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \lambda_i^{\circ}$ | Kohlrausch's Law | S·cm²/mol | | 8 | $\kappa_T = \kappa_{25}[1+\alpha(T-25)]$ | Temperature compensation | S/cm | | 9 | $\alpha = (\kappa_2-\kappa_1)/[\kappa_1(T_2-T_1)]$ | Temperature coefficient from data | per °C | | 10 | $\% \text{ Error} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot 100$ | Temperature-induced error estimate | % | --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility Conductance/conductivity measurement is one of the **cheapest, most reliable, and most widely deployed** analytical methods in industry. Key applications include: 1. **Power plant boiler feedwater monitoring** — even ppb-level contamination of high-purity water must be detected to prevent turbine scaling. 2. **Pharmaceutical CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems** — verifying the concentration of caustic/acid cleaning solutions via in-line conductivity. 3. **Dairy industry** — detecting the end-point of the precipitation reaction in cheese-making (salt → curd). 4. **Wastewater treatment plants** — continuous monitoring of TDS (Total Dissolved Solids), which is empirically related to conductivity by $\text{TDS (ppm)} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7 \times \kappa \text{ (μS/cm)}$. 5. **Conductometric titrations** — precipitation, acid-base, and complexometric titrations in process analytical chemistry. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conductivity from Ohm's Law and Cell Geometry **Given:** A conductivity cell has two parallel plate electrodes of area $A$ separated by distance $L$, filled with an electrolyte of resistivity $\rho$. **To Derive:** An expression for the measured conductivity $\kappa$ in terms of measurable quantities (current $I$, voltage $V$, and the cell constant $K$). ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Start with the resistance of the electrolyte column** (analogous to a metallic conductor): $$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$ **Step 2 — Convert resistance to conductance using the definition:** $$G = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{\rho} \cdot \frac{A}{L} = \sigma \cdot \frac{A}{L}$$ where $\sigma = 1/\rho$ is the conductivity (we use $\sigma$ here to avoid confusion with $\kappa$; both notations are used in literature). **Step 3 — Substitute the cell constant $K = L/A$:** $$G = \sigma \cdot \frac{A}{L} = \frac{\sigma}{K}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for conductivity:** $$\boxed{\sigma = G \cdot K}$$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of Ohm's law measurements** (since $G = I/V$): $$\sigma = \frac{I}{V} \cdot K$$ This is the **operational equation** used in all conductivity meters: measure the current $I$ flowing when a known voltage $V$ is applied across the cell, multiply by the cell constant, and obtain the conductivity directly. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cell Constant Calibration Using a Standard Solution **Given:** A conductivity cell of unknown cell constant $K_x$ is immersed in a standard KCl solution of known conductivity $\kappa_s = 1413 \text{ μS/cm}$ at 25 °C. The measured conductance is $G_s = 2.5 \text{ mS}$. **To Derive:** The cell constant $K_x$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Use the fundamental relation:** $$\kappa_s = G_s \cdot K_x$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the unknown cell constant:** $$K_x = \frac{\kappa_s}{G_s}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values** (careful with units!): $$K_x = \frac{1413 \times 10^{-6} \text{ S/cm}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S}}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify:** $$K_x = \frac{1413}{2500} \text{ cm}^{-1} = 0.5652 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{K_x \approx 0.565 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** The cell constant of 0.565 cm⁻¹ corresponds to a relatively **wide-spaced, small-area** electrode geometry (used for high-conductivity samples). For low-conductivity (pure water) measurements, cell constants of 0.01–0.1 cm⁻¹ are preferred. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Compensation Factor **Given:** A process stream shows $\kappa_1 = 500 \text{ μS/cm}$ at $T_1 = 30 \text{ °C}$. The temperature coefficient is $\alpha = 0.02 \text{ /°C}$. **To Derive:** The conductivity at the reference temperature 25 °C. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Write the temperature compensation equation:** $$\kappa_T = \kappa_{25} \cdot [1 + \alpha(T - 25)]$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $\kappa_{25}$:** $$\kappa_{25} = \frac{\kappa_T}{1 + \alpha(T - 25)}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$\kappa_{25} = \frac{500 \text{ μS/cm}}{1 + 0.02 \times (30 - 25)}$$ $$\kappa_{25} = \frac{500}{1 + 0.10} = \frac{500}{1.10}$$ **Step 4 — Final result:** $$\boxed{\kappa_{25} \approx 454.5 \text{ μS/cm}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** Without compensation, a controller would interpret the same NaCl concentration as ~10% more concentrated at 30 °C than at 25 °C, triggering a false alarm in the dosing system. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Kohlrausch's Law Application **Given:** The limiting molar conductivities at 25 °C are: - $\lambda^{\circ}(\text{H}^+) = 349.8 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}$ - $\lambda^{\circ}(\text{Cl}^-) = 76.3 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}$ **To Derive:** The molar conductivity of HCl at infinite dilution, $\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{HCl})$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Apply Kohlrausch's Law for a 1:1 strong electrolyte:** $$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{HCl}) = \nu_+ \lambda^{\circ}(\text{H}^+) + \nu_- \lambda^{\circ}(\text{Cl}^-)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the stoichiometric coefficients** (both 1 for HCl): $$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{HCl}) = 1 \times 349.8 + 1 \times 76.3$$ **Step 3 — Compute the sum:** $$\boxed{\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{HCl}) = 426.1 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** This high value (compared to ~149 S·cm²/mol for NaCl, dominated by the much slower Na⁺ ion of $\lambda^{\circ} \approx 50.1$) explains why **HCl is the most conductive strong acid** in solution. This is why strong acid–strong base conductometric titrations show a very **steep V-shaped curve** at the equivalence point. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Conductance Equations at a Glance | Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Insight | |---|---|---| | Measuring a known solution | $\kappa = G \cdot K$ | Geometry-independent value | | Calibrating a new cell | $K = \kappa_s / G_s$ | Use standard KCl solution | | Compensating for temperature | $\kappa_{25} = \kappa_T / [1+\alpha(T-25)]$ | Avoid ~2%/°C error | | Predicting strong electrolyte behaviour | $\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \lambda_i^{\circ}$ | Kohlrausch's Law | | Relating to concentration | $\Lambda_m = 1000\kappa/C$ | Useful for weak electrolytes | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conductance Measurement System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[Process Side] PS["Process Stream
(Electrolyte Solution)"] end subgraph B[Sensor Assembly] C["Conductivity Cell
(Two/Four Electrodes)"] CC["Cell Constant K = L/A"] end subgraph C[Signal Conditioning] EXC["AC Excitation Source
(1 kHz to 10 kHz)"] AMP["Current-to-Voltage
Amplifier (Transimpedance)"] REC["AC Rectifier and
Low-Pass Filter"] end subgraph D[Compensation and Display] TC["Temperature Sensor
(PT100 / RTD)"] TCOMP["Temperature
Compensation Block
(Equation based)"] DSP["Microcontroller /
Display Unit"] OUT["Conductivity Reading
(S/cm)"] end PS --> C C --> CC C --> EXC EXC --> AMP AMP --> REC REC --> DSP TC --> TCOMP TCOMP --> DSP DSP --> OUT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The AC excitation is critical — DC would cause electrode polarization (electrolysis products accumulate on the plates), drastically altering the measured resistance. The use of an RTD in a feedback loop ensures the displayed conductivity is referenced to 25 °C. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Contacting vs. Inductive Sensors ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Type1[Contacting Sensor] E1["Electrode 1
(Inner)"] E2["Electrode 2
(Outer)"] S1["Direct Ionic Contact
with Process Fluid"] end subgraph Type2[Inductive Electrodeless Sensor] C1["Primary Coil
(Excitation)"] C2["Secondary Coil
(Pickup)"] S2["No Direct Contact
with Process Fluid"] end E1 --- E2 C1 --- C2 P1["PROS: Simple, Low Cost,
Good for Clean Liquids
CONS: Polarization, Fouling"] --> E1 P2["PROS: No Polarization,
Handles Fouling and Corrosive
CONS: Higher Cost, Less Accurate
at Low Conductivity"] --> C1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selecting a Conductivity Sensor ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"What is the
conductivity range?"} A1["< 1 mS/cm
(Pure Water / DI Water)"] A2["1 to 100 mS/cm
(Drinking / Process Water)"] A3["> 100 mS/cm
(Brines, Acids, Concentrates)"] Q2{"Is the fluid
clean or fouling?"} Clean["Clean Liquid"] Fouling["Fouling / Oily / Corrosive"] R1["Use 4-Electrode Cell
with Flow-Through Design"] R2["Use 2-Electrode Contacting
Cell, Epoxy Body"] R3["Use Inductive
Electrodeless Sensor"] Q1 --> A1 Q1 --> A2 Q1 --> A3 A1 --> Q2 A2 --> R2 A3 --> R3 Q2 --> Clean Q2 --> Fouling Clean --> R1 Fouling --> R3 ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conductometric Titration Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Titration[Conductometric Titration Rig] BT["Burette
(Titrant)"] BKR["Beaker with
Sample + Dip Cell"] STIR["Magnetic Stirrer"] COND["Conductivity Meter
(Dip Cell + RTD)"] PLOT["Plot of Conductivity
vs. Volume Added"] end BT --> BKR BKR --> COND STIR --> BKR COND --> PLOT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why conductometric titration over potentiometric?** In turbid, coloured, or dilute solutions where indicator-based or potentiometric end-point detection fails, the **breakpoint in the conductivity curve** remains crisp and unambiguous. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. Define conductance. Differentiate between conductance and conductivity.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Conductance (G):** It is defined as the reciprocal of electrical resistance offered by a conductor or solution to the flow of current. $$G = \frac{1}{R} \quad [\text{unit: Siemens, S}]$$ **Conductivity (κ):** It is the conductance of a unit cube (1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm) of a material. It is the **intrinsic property** of the electrolyte, independent of geometry. $$\kappa = G \cdot \frac{L}{A} = G \cdot K \quad [\text{unit: S/cm}]$$ | Conductance | Conductivity | |---|---| | Extensive property (depends on $L, A$) | Intensive property (independent of geometry) | | Measured in Siemens (S) | Measured in S/cm | | Refers to a specific cell | Refers to the solution itself | *(1.5 marks for definitions + 1.5 marks for difference table)* --- ### **Q2. What is a cell constant? Why is it necessary in conductivity measurement?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **cell constant $K$** is defined as the ratio of the distance between the electrodes ($L$) to the cross-sectional area of the electrolyte column ($A$): $$K = \frac{L}{A} \quad [\text{unit: cm}^{-1}]$$ **Necessity:** - It converts the **geometry-dependent** measured conductance (S) to the **geometry-independent** conductivity (S/cm). - It allows comparison of measurements made with **different cells** by normalising to a standard geometry. - It is determined experimentally by **calibrating** with a standard KCl solution of known conductivity. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation of necessity)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — Conductance Measurement and Instrumentation** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **(a) Derive the relationship between conductance, conductivity, and the cell constant. Explain the significance of the cell constant with a numerical example.** (7 marks) **Step 1 — Starting equation (2 marks):** The resistance $R$ of an electrolyte column of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is given by Ohm's law applied to bulk matter: $$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$ where $\rho$ is the resistivity (Ω·cm). **Step 2 — Convert to conductance (2 marks):** Taking the reciprocal of both sides, and recognizing $G = 1/R$ and $\kappa = 1/\rho$: $$G = \kappa \cdot \frac{A}{L} = \frac{\kappa}{K}$$ where $K = L/A$ is defined as the **cell constant**. **Step 3 — Final relation (1 mark):** $$\boxed{\kappa = G \cdot K}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical example and significance (2 marks):** A cell with $K = 1.0 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ is used to measure a 0.01 M KCl solution. The instrument reads $G = 1.413 \text{ mS}$. The conductivity is therefore: $$\kappa = 1.413 \text{ mS} \times 1.0 \text{ cm}^{-1} = 1.413 \text{ mS/cm}$$ This value matches the literature value for 0.01 M KCl at 25 °C, confirming both the calibration of the cell and the integrity of the measurement. The cell constant therefore acts as a **scaling factor** that allows us to report a value intrinsic to the solution, not the apparatus. --- #### **(b) With a neat diagram, describe the working of a four-electrode conductivity cell. What are its advantages over a two-electrode cell?** (7 marks) **Diagram (3 marks):** See Section 4.1 reference. A four-electrode cell uses: - **Two outer (current) electrodes** — for forcing AC current $I$ through the electrolyte. - **Two inner (voltage) electrodes** — for sensing the potential drop $V$ across a defined portion of the solution. The two current electrodes eliminate the influence of polarization (since no current flows through the voltage-sensing electrodes), and the two voltage electrodes have high input impedance, so they draw negligible current. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - An AC current $I$ of known amplitude is passed between the outer electrodes (C1, C2). - The voltage drop $V$ is measured between the inner electrodes (P1, P2) using a high-impedance voltmeter. - The resistance is computed: $R = V/I$. - Conductivity follows: $\kappa = (I/V) \cdot (L/A) = (I/V) \cdot K$. **Advantages over two-electrode cells (2 marks):** 1. **Eliminates polarization errors** — the sensing electrodes carry no current, so no electrolytic products accumulate on them. 2. **Extended range** — can measure from ultra-pure water (~0.05 μS/cm) to concentrated brines (>1 S/cm) without cell change. 3. **Self-checking capability** — by independently measuring the resistance of the inner and outer sections, the cell can detect fouling or electrode damage. 4. **Better long-term stability** — no need for frequent re-calibration. --- ### **Question B — Temperature Effects and Kohlrausch's Law** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` #### **(a) Discuss the effect of temperature on conductivity of electrolytes. Derive the temperature compensation equation used in industrial conductivity meters.** (7 marks) **Theoretical Discussion (3 marks):** The conductivity of an electrolyte is **highly temperature-dependent** because: 1. **Ionic mobility increases** with temperature (kinetic energy gain overcomes solvent drag). 2. **Viscosity of water decreases** with temperature, reducing the drag force on ions. 3. For most aqueous salt solutions, the conductivity increases by approximately **2% per °C** of temperature rise. This is expressed quantitatively via the **temperature coefficient $\alpha$**. For pure water, however, the temperature dependence is non-linear and much steeper because the auto-ionization equilibrium ($K_w$) is itself strongly temperature-dependent. Industrial meters must therefore use a non-linear compensation curve for pure-water applications. **Derivation of the Compensation Equation (4 marks):** **Step 1:** Define the fractional change in conductivity per unit temperature change: $$\alpha = \frac{1}{\kappa_{25}} \cdot \frac{d\kappa}{dT}$$ Assuming $\alpha$ is constant over the operating range, this is a linear differential equation that integrates to: $$\frac{d\kappa}{\kappa} = \alpha \cdot dT \implies \ln \kappa = \alpha T + C$$ **Step 2:** Apply the boundary condition at the reference temperature 25 °C, where $\kappa = \kappa_{25}$: $$\ln \kappa_{25} = \alpha \cdot 25 + C \implies C = \ln \kappa_{25} - 25\alpha$$ **Step 3:** Substitute back to eliminate $C$: $$\ln \kappa - \ln \kappa_{25} = \alpha (T - 25)$$ **Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the linear temperature compensation equation: $$\boxed{\kappa_T = \kappa_{25} \cdot \exp[\alpha(T-25)] \approx \kappa_{25} \cdot [1 + \alpha(T-25)]}$$ The approximation in the second form is valid when $\alpha(T-25) \ll 1$ (e.g., for $T$ within ±5 °C of 25 °C), and is the form used in most industrial transmitters. --- #### **(b) State and explain Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions. A solution contains 0.05 M HCl. Given $\lambda^{\circ}(\text{H}^+) = 349.8$ and $\lambda^{\circ}(\text{Cl}^-) = 76.3$ S·cm²/mol, calculate the molar conductivity of HCl at infinite dilution.** (7 marks) **Statement (2 marks):** At infinite dilution, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the **sum of the limiting molar conductivities of its constituent ions**, each multiplied by the number of ions of that type per formula unit: $$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \lambda_-^{\circ}$$ **Explanation (2 marks):** Kohlrausch's Law rests on the principle that at infinite dilution: - Ion-ion interactions become negligible. - Each ion contributes **independently** to the total current. - The mobility of a given ion is **unaffected** by the nature of the counter-ion. This is why we can **predict** the conductivity of a strong electrolyte without ever measuring it — we simply add the tabulated ionic contributions. **Numerical Solution (3 marks):** **Step 1:** Write the Kohlrausch equation for HCl (which dissociates as $\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$, with $\nu_+ = \nu_- = 1$): $$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{HCl}) = \lambda^{\circ}(\text{H}^+) + \lambda^{\circ}(\text{Cl}^-)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the given values: $$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{HCl}) = 349.8 + 76.3$$ **Step 3:** Compute the final answer: $$\boxed{\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{HCl}) = 426.1 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}}$$ **Engineering note:** The unusually high value is due to the **Grotthuss proton-hopping mechanism**, which allows $\text{H}^+$ to migrate ~5× faster than any other cation. This is why strong acid–strong base conductometric titrations show such a sharp V-shaped minimum at the equivalence point. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Conductance Questions:** > > 1. **Unit confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often write conductivity in S (Siemens) instead of S/cm, or report cell constant in cm instead of cm⁻¹. Always carry units in every calculation step. > > 2. **Mixing up conductance and conductivity (–2 marks):** Conductance ($G$) and conductivity ($\kappa$) are *not* synonyms. If a problem gives a cell constant and asks for conductivity, you must multiply $G$ by $K$ — do not report the raw conductance. > > 3. **Forgetting temperature compensation (–1 mark):** Any industrial problem involving real process conditions (not at 25 °C) requires compensation. Examiners specifically test for awareness of the ~2% per °C error. > > 4. **DC vs. AC excitation confusion (–2 marks):** State explicitly that **AC excitation is mandatory** in conductivity cells to avoid polarization. Examiners look for this phrase in instrumentation questions. > > 5. **Skipping steps in Kohlrausch calculations (–1 mark):** Always state the form of the equation, substitute stoichiometric coefficients explicitly, *then* compute. No single-line jumps. > > 6. **No diagram on a 7-mark sub-part:** A block diagram or circuit sketch is worth 2–3 marks on its own. Always draw one. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Conductance (Module 4, PCAET602)** > > - **Definition:** $G = 1/R$, unit Siemens (S); $\kappa = G \cdot K$, unit S/cm. > - **Cell constant:** $K = L/A$, unit cm⁻¹; experimentally determined using standard KCl. > - **Kohlrausch's Law:** $\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \lambda_i^{\circ}$ — predicts $\Lambda$ at infinite dilution. > - **Molar conductance:** $\Lambda_m = 1000 \kappa / C$ — fundamental link between conductivity and concentration. > - **Temperature effect:** $\kappa_T = \kappa_{25}[1 + \alpha(T-25)]$; $\alpha \approx 2\%/°\text{C}$ for most salt solutions. > - **Measurement essentials:** Use **AC excitation** (1–10 kHz); use **4-electrode cells** for wide range and to avoid polarization; use **inductive sensors** for fouling/corrosive fluids. > - **Calibration standard:** 0.01 M KCl with $\kappa = 1413 \text{ μS/cm}$ at 25 °C. > - **Conductometric titrations:** Plot $\kappa$ vs. titrant volume; locate the break-point (V-shape for acid-base, L-shape for precipitation, etc.). > - **Industrial applications:** Boiler feedwater purity, CIP cleaning verification, TDS monitoring in wastewater, dairy/brewery process control. > - **Common pitfall:** Never confuse conductance (extensive, S) with conductivity (intensive, S/cm) — examiners test this every year.

topics.title: Inductive and Capacitive Level Gauging. Ultrasonic Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inductive and Capacitive Level Gauging. Ultrasonic Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Inductive Level Gauging — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Level Measurement** is a method of continuous liquid level measurement based on the variation of **inductance (L)** or **mutual inductance (M)** of one or more coils caused by the vertical displacement of a **ferromagnetic float (core)** that follows the liquid surface. The change in inductance is converted into a proportional electrical signal (voltage or current) for indication, recording, or control.

The phenomenon obeys the general relation for a solenoid with a movable ferromagnetic core:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \, \mu_r \, N^2 \, A}{\ell}$$

where the effective permeability $\mu_r$ changes as the core moves relative to the coil.

**Types of Inductive Level Transducers:**
1. **Single-coil with moving-core (float-type)** — A hollow ferromagnetic float rises/falls along a non-magnetic tube housing the coil.
2. **Differential Transformer (LVDT) type** — A core attached to a float moves between the primary and two secondary windings, producing a differential output voltage.
3. **Eddy-current / proximity type** — Used for non-magnetic tanks; eddy currents induced in a conductive target change coil impedance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically focuses on the **displacement-type inductive level gauge** and the **LVDT-based float system** for continuous level measurement in industrial tanks containing conductive and non-conductive liquids (water, oils, chemicals).

---

### 1.2 Capacitive Level Gauging — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Capacitive Level Measurement** is a technique in which the **liquid (or solid) level inside a vessel acts as a variable dielectric** between two electrodes (or modifies the effective area of a capacitor). The resulting change in capacitance $\Delta C$ is measured using an AC bridge, oscillator, or charge/discharge circuit and is directly proportional to the level height $h$.

For a parallel-plate geometry, the fundamental relation is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$$

For the more common **coaxial cylindrical (rod-in-tube) geometry** used in industrial tanks:

$$C = \frac{2\pi \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, L}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Two operating modes are emphasized — (a) **dielectric-variation mode** (for non-conductive liquids like oil, solvents) and (b) **conductivity-variation / insulated-electrode mode** (for conductive liquids like water, acids).

---

### 1.3 Ultrasonic Level Measurement — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultrasonic Level Gauging** is a **non-contact** level measurement technique based on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF) principle**. A piezoelectric transducer emits a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically **20 kHz to 200 kHz**), which travels through the gas/vapour above the liquid, reflects from the surface, and returns to the same (or a separate) transducer. The round-trip time $t$ is measured and converted to distance using the known velocity of sound $v$ in the medium.

The governing distance equation is:

$$D = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where $D$ is the distance from the transducer face to the liquid surface, and the factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU emphasizes the **pulsed-echo (reflective) mode** as it is the industry standard (e.g., Siemens SITRANS, Endress+Hauser Prosonic), along with compensation for **temperature**, **vapour**, and **foam** effects.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| Method | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Inductive** | Imagine a **sliding metal ring on a stick with a wire coil** — push the ring up and the magnetic pull on the stick increases (inductance rises). A level gauge works the same way: the liquid lifts a magnetic float, and the inductance changes. |
| **Capacitive** | Think of a **water level in a drinking glass with two metal strips stuck to its sides**. As water rises, more of the gap between the strips is filled with water (dielectric). The "electrical storage capacity" (capacitance) increases smoothly with level. |
| **Ultrasonic** | Like a **bat or submarine sonar** — the device shouts a sound burst, listens for the echo from the water surface, and the time delay tells how far away the surface is. Higher surface = shorter echo time. |

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- Speed of sound in dry air at **20 °C**: **v ≈ 343 m/s**
- Speed of sound in air varies as: $v \propto \sqrt{T}$ (T in Kelvin)
- Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of water: $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$
- Relative permittivity of air: $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0006$
- Typical ultrasonic frequency: **40 kHz – 200 kHz**
- Float density for inductive gauges: usually **< 0.7 g/cm³** to ensure buoyancy
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inductive Level Gauging — Operational Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Construction:** A hollow non-magnetic (usually stainless steel) **standpipe** is installed vertically inside the tank. Inside this pipe is wound a solenoid coil. A **ferromagnetic float** (ring-shaped) surrounds the pipe and is free to slide up and down.
2. **Buoyancy action:** The liquid level exerts a buoyant force on the float, causing it to follow the surface precisely.
3. **Inductance change:** As the float moves upward, more of the coil is "filled" by the high-$\mu_r$ magnetic core material. The effective permeability $\mu_r^{eff}$ of the magnetic circuit increases, raising the inductance $L$.
4. **Signal conditioning:** The variable inductor is connected to an **AC bridge** (similar to a Maxwell–Wien bridge) or to a **frequency-determining tank circuit** of an LC oscillator. The output is calibrated so that frequency (or voltage) is linear with level.
5. **For LVDT version:** A primary is excited by an AC carrier (1–5 kHz). Two secondaries are wound in series opposition. As the float-attached core moves up, the upper secondary EMF $E_{s1}$ increases and the lower $E_{s2}$ decreases. The differential output $(E_{s1} - E_{s2})$ is linear and phase-sensitive.

**Why and How (Intuitive):**
- *Why inductance?* A ferromagnetic core concentrates magnetic flux. More core inside the coil = more flux = more inductance.
- *How is level recovered?* The inductance is a monotonic function of float position; calibrating $L$ vs. $h$ gives a direct transfer function.

### 2.2 Capacitive Level Gauging — Operational Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Construction:** Two electrodes are placed either:
   - **Concentric (rod-in-cylinder):** A central rod (one electrode) inside a metal tank wall acting as the other electrode. Common in large storage tanks.
   - **Parallel plates:** Two vertical plates immersed in the tank (used for non-conductive liquids).
   - **Insulated probe:** For conductive liquids, the central rod is coated with PTFE/ceramic, and the conductive liquid itself becomes one "plate".
2. **Capacitance variation modes:**
   - **Mode 1 – Dielectric change (non-conductive liquids):** The rising liquid replaces air (low $\varepsilon_r$) with the liquid (higher $\varepsilon_r$). The total capacitance is the sum of the empty portion and the filled portion:
     $$C_{total} = C_{air}(L-h) + C_{liq}(h) = C_0 + (\varepsilon_{r,l} - 1)\cdot C_0 \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$
   - **Mode 2 – Area change (conductive liquids with insulated probe):** The liquid forms a coaxial cylinder. The filled portion contributes a high capacitance (insulator + conductive liquid forms the other plate).
3. **Signal conditioning:** The variable capacitor is part of an **AC bridge** (Schering/De Sauty), a **tuned oscillator** (frequency $f = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$), or a **charge amplifier**. The output (frequency or voltage) is proportional to level.

**Why and How (Intuitive):**
- *Why capacitance?* Capacitance depends on the dielectric between two conductors. Replacing air (weak dielectric) with a liquid (stronger dielectric) literally "stores more charge per volt" — a measurable, level-dependent effect.
- *How is linearity ensured?* Cylindrical geometry gives a linear $C$ vs. $h$ relationship when only the liquid height varies (permittivity is constant).

### 2.3 Ultrasonic Level Measurement — Operational Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Pulse transmission:** A piezo-electric transducer is excited by a short HV pulse (~1 µs) at 40–200 kHz. This generates an acoustic wave packet.
2. **Propagation:** The wave travels through the gas/vapour column at velocity $v = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$.
3. **Reflection:** At the gas–liquid interface, the large acoustic impedance mismatch ($\rho v$ difference) reflects almost the entire wave back.
4. **Reception:** The same transducer (or a paired one) receives the echo and converts it back to an electrical pulse.
5. **Time measurement:** A high-resolution timer (TDC or counter) measures the round-trip time $t$.
6. **Computation:** $D = v \cdot t / 2$. Subtracting $D$ from the total tank height gives the level $h$.
7. **Temperature compensation:** A built-in temperature sensor updates $v$ using $v(T) = v_0 \sqrt{T / T_0}$, where $T_0 = 293\,\text{K}$ and $v_0 = 343$ m/s.

**Why and How (Intuitive):**
- *Why ultrasonic?* Sound travels at a known, predictable speed. Measure the echo time, and you know the distance — without touching the liquid.
- *How is accuracy maintained?* Temperature sensors, signal processing (cross-correlation, leading-edge detection), and rejection of false echoes from tank internals.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations are verified against standard physics derivations. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (not the pipe character) inside table cells for absolute value to keep Markdown intact.**

| # | Quantity / Method | Governing Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solenoid inductance (with core) | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, \mu_r \, N^2 \, A}{\ell}$ | H (Henry) | $\mu_r$ is effective, depends on core position |
| 2 | Inductance change (small displacement $\Delta h$) | $\Delta L \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 \, N^2 \, A}{\ell^2} \cdot \Delta \mu_r \cdot \Delta h$ | H | Linear approximation; small float travel |
| 3 | LVDT differential output | $E_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = K \cdot x$ | V | $K$ = sensitivity (V/mm), $x$ = float displacement |
| 4 | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$ | F | $A$ = plate area, $d$ = gap |
| 5 | Coaxial cylindrical capacitance | $C = \dfrac{2\pi \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, L}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$ | F | $L$ = length of overlap, $R_2 > R_1$ |
| 6 | Capacitance with two dielectrics (level) | $C_{total} = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}\bigl[\varepsilon_{r,air}(L-h) + \varepsilon_{r,liq}\,h\bigr]$ | F | Linear in $h$ if $\varepsilon_{r}$ constants |
| 7 | Capacitance change vs. level | $\Delta C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \, h}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}\,(\varepsilon_{r,liq} - 1)$ | F | Valid for $h$ in metres, $C$ in F |
| 8 | Ultrasonic distance | $D = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | m | $t$ = round-trip time, factor 2 for echo |
| 9 | Speed of sound in ideal gas | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}$ | m/s | $T$ in K, $M$ in kg/mol |
| 10 | Temperature-corrected velocity | $v(T) = v_0\,\sqrt{T/T_0}$ | m/s | $v_0 = 343$ m/s at $T_0 = 293$ K |
| 11 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot v$ | kg/(m²·s) | Impedance mismatch causes reflection |
| 12 | Reflection coefficient (pressure) | $R_p = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | — | At gas–liquid interface, $\vert R_p \vert \approx 0.999$ |
| 13 | Resonant frequency of LC tank | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | Used in capacitance-to-frequency conversion |
| 14 | Faraday's law (LVDT primary) | $E_1 = -N_1 \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | V | AC excitation at 1–5 kHz |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Inductive gauges** are workhorses in the **oil & gas, petroleum refineries, and chemical plants** for crude oil, diesel, and LPG bullet tanks where mechanical floats are not ideal.
- **Capacitive gauges** dominate in **food & beverage, pharmaceutical, and water-treatment** industries — especially for sticky liquids, slurries, and powders (granular solids).
- **Ultrasonic gauges** are indispensable for **corrosive chemicals, molten metals, sewage, slurries** and any application demanding a **non-contact, hygienic, explosion-proof** solution (e.g., milk storage, acid tanks, cement silos).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of a Cylindrical Capacitor (from Gauss's Law)

We start with a coaxial geometry: inner rod of radius $R_1$, outer cylinder of radius $R_2$, length $L$. The space between is filled with dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$. A charge $+Q$ per unit length is assumed on the inner conductor.

**Step 1:** Apply Gauss's law to a cylindrical surface of radius $r$ (with $R_1 < r < R_2$) and length $\ell$:

$$\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enclosed}$$

By symmetry, $\vec{D}$ is radial and constant over the Gaussian surface:

$$D \cdot (2 \pi r \ell) = \lambda \ell \quad\Rightarrow\quad D = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 2:** Electric field:

$$E = \frac{D}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, r}$$

**Step 3:** Potential difference between the conductors (integrate E from $R_1$ to $R_2$):

$$V = \int_{R_1}^{R_2} E \, dr = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \int_{R_1}^{R_2} \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \ln\!\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Capacitance per unit length, with $C = Q/V = \lambda \ell / V$:

$$C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, \ell}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$$

This is the classical expression. Replacing $\ell$ with the active length $L$ and writing $\lambda = Q/\ell$ yields the same form as in the cheat sheet.

> **Conversion logic:** We went from microscopic field lines (Gauss's law) → macroscopic voltage (line integral) → charge-to-voltage ratio (definition of capacitance). This is the exact reverse of how a parallel-plate capacitor is derived.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitance of a Partially-Filled Level Sensor

Consider a vertical cylindrical tank acting as the outer electrode (radius $R_2$) and an inner rod (radius $R_1$). The tank is filled with a non-conductive liquid up to height $h$, with air above up to total height $L$. Both regions are in parallel (electrically) because they share the same voltage.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of the air-filled portion (height $L - h$):**

$$C_{air} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot (L - h)}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$$

We use $\varepsilon_{r,air} \approx 1$ for dry air.

**Step 2 — Capacitance of the liquid-filled portion (height $h$):**

$$C_{liq} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,liq} \cdot h}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$$

**Step 3 — Total capacitance (parallel combination):**

$$C_{total} = C_{air} + C_{liq} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}\bigl[(L - h) + \varepsilon_{r,liq}\,h\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify and identify the level-dependent term:**

$$C_{total} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R_2/R_1)} + \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 h}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}\,(\varepsilon_{r,liq} - 1)$$

$$C_{total} = C_0 + \Delta C(h)$$

where

$$C_0 = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R_2/R_1)} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta C(h) = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_{r,liq} - 1)}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}\,h$$

> **Conversion logic:** The empty-tank capacitance $C_0$ is a fixed offset; the level-dependent part $\Delta C(h)$ is **linear in $h$**. Thus, the gauge output is a linear function of liquid level — the most desirable property for instrumentation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ultrasonic Distance from Time of Flight

**Step 1 — Wave equation solution for sound in a gas:**

For an ideal gas, acoustic waves obey the adiabatic compressibility relation $v = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ (derivable from Newton's second law and adiabatic gas law $PV^\gamma = \text{const.}$).

**Step 2 — Round-trip geometry:**

The transducer is mounted at the top of the tank. It sends a pulse that travels a distance $D$ down to the liquid surface, reflects, and travels the same distance $D$ back. Total path length = $2D$.

**Step 3 — Kinematic relation:**

$$\text{Total distance} = v \cdot t \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2D = v \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D$:**

$$D = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute temperature-corrected velocity $v(T) = v_0 \sqrt{T/T_0}$:**

$$D = \frac{v_0 \, t}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_0}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor of 2 is the most-forgotten detail in KTU exams. Always state that it accounts for the round-trip nature of the echo.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — LVDT Output Voltage

An LVDT has a primary winding (P) and two secondaries (S1, S2) wound in series opposition on a common bobbin. An AC carrier $V_p \sin(\omega t)$ excites the primary. A movable ferromagnetic core couples flux into S1 and S2.

**Step 1 — Flux linkage in S1 (upper secondary) and S2 (lower secondary):**

$$\Phi_{s1} = k_1 (x_0 + x) V_p \quad,\quad \Phi_{s2} = k_1 (x_0 - x) V_p$$

where $x_0$ = nominal (mid) position and $x$ = displacement from centre.

**Step 2 — Induced EMFs (Faraday's law):**

$$E_{s1} = -N_s \frac{d\Phi_{s1}}{dt}, \qquad E_{s2} = -N_s \frac{d\Phi_{s2}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Series-opposition connection yields:**

$$E_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 2 N_s k_1 \frac{dV_p}{dt} \cdot x$$

For $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$E_{out} = 2 N_s k_1 V_0 \omega \cos(\omega t) \cdot x = K \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

> **Conversion logic:** The differential (subtractive) connection is what makes the LVDT linear and cancels the common-mode voltage. The factor 2 emerges from the symmetry: when the core moves up by $x$, S1 gains exactly as much flux as S2 loses.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Capacitive Level Gauge)

**Problem:** A cylindrical capacitive level sensor has $R_1 = 5$ mm, $R_2 = 25$ mm, total active length $L = 2$ m. The dielectric liquid has $\varepsilon_r = 4$. The tank is 60 % full (i.e., $h = 1.2$ m). Find $C_{total}$ and the change $\Delta C$ from the empty condition.

**Given:**
- $R_1 = 0.005$ m, $R_2 = 0.025$ m
- $L = 2$ m, $h = 1.2$ m
- $\varepsilon_{r,liq} = 4$, $\varepsilon_{r,air} = 1$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

**Step 1 — Geometric factor:**

$$\ln(R_2/R_1) = \ln(25/5) = \ln 5 = 1.6094$$

**Step 2 — Empty-tank capacitance:**

$$C_0 = \frac{2\pi (8.854\times 10^{-12})(2)}{1.6094} = \frac{1.1127 \times 10^{-10}}{1.6094} = 6.915 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{F} \approx 69.15\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance with two dielectrics:**

$$C_{total} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}\bigl[(L-h) + \varepsilon_{r,liq}\,h\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{2\pi (8.854\times 10^{-12})}{1.6094}\bigl[(0.8) + (4)(1.2)\bigr]$$

$$= (3.4566 \times 10^{-11})\,(0.8 + 4.8) = (3.4566 \times 10^{-11})(5.6)$$

$$C_{total} = 1.9357 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{F} \approx 193.6\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 4 — Change in capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = C_{total} - C_0 = 193.6 - 69.15 = 124.45\,\text{pF}$$

**Verification using the direct formula:**

$$\Delta C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 h (\varepsilon_{r,liq} - 1)}{\ln(R_2/R_1)} = \frac{2\pi (8.854\times 10^{-12})(1.2)(3)}{1.6094} = 1.243 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{F} \approx 124.3\,\text{pF}$$

✅ Matches within rounding error. **Result: $C_{total} \approx 193.6$ pF, $\Delta C \approx 124.3$ pF.**

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (Ultrasonic Level Gauge)

**Problem:** An ultrasonic sensor mounted at the top of a tank measures a round-trip time of $t = 12$ ms. The ambient temperature is 35 °C. Find the distance to the liquid surface and hence the level if the tank is 5 m tall.

**Given:**
- $t = 12 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $T = 35 + 273 = 308$ K
- $v_0 = 343$ m/s at $T_0 = 293$ K

**Step 1 — Temperature-corrected velocity:**

$$v = 343 \sqrt{308/293} = 343 \sqrt{1.0512} = 343 \times 1.0253 = 351.7\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Distance from transducer to surface:**

$$D = \frac{v t}{2} = \frac{351.7 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{4.220}{2} = 2.110\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Liquid level (height of liquid column):**

$$h = H - D = 5.0 - 2.110 = 2.890\,\text{m}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Always subtract the air-gap distance $D$ from the *total* tank height $H$, because ultrasonic gauges measure the *empty* space above the liquid.

✅ **Result: Surface is 2.11 m below the sensor; liquid level is 2.89 m.**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Inductive (LVDT-based) Level Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[AC Excitation Source 1 to 5 kHz] --> primA[LVDT Primary Winding]
    primA --> coreA[Ferrite Core on Float]
    coreA --> sec1A[Secondary S1 Upper]
    coreA --> sec2A[Secondary S2 Lower]
    sec1A --> diffA[Differential Subtractor]
    sec2A --> diffA
    diffA --> demodA[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
    demodA --> ampA[DC Amplifier]
    ampA --> outA[Indicator or Controller 0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA]
    tankA[Tank with Liquid] -.rises.-> floatA[Floats on Surface]
    floatA -.mechanically coupled.-> coreA
```

**Description:** The float sits on the liquid surface inside the tank. It is mechanically linked to a ferromagnetic core that slides inside the LVDT. The two secondaries are connected in series-opposition, and the differential output is demodulated and amplified.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Capacitive Level Gauge with AC Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    oscB[AC Oscillator 1 to 10 kHz] --> bridgeB[AC Bridge with Cx as one arm]
    bridgeB --> detB[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    detB --> ampB[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    ampB --> vtoiB[Voltage to Current Converter]
    vtoiB --> outB[Output 4 to 20 mA or 0 to 10 V]
    tankB[Tank with Liquid] --> cxB[Variable Capacitance Cx Rod in Cylinder]
    cxB --> bridgeB
    compB[Reference Capacitor Cr] --> bridgeB
    tempB[RTD Temperature Sensor] --> compB
```

**Description:** The variable capacitor $C_x$ is one arm of an AC Wheatstone-style bridge. A reference capacitor $C_r$ (also inside the bridge housing, but not exposed to the process) compensates for ambient temperature drift. The bridge off-balance voltage is demodulated and scaled to industrial standards.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Level Gauge (Pulsed-Echo Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    trigC[Microcontroller Trigger] --> pulserC[Pulse Generator 100 to 400 V peak]
    pulserC --> xdcrC[Piezo Transducer 40 to 200 kHz]
    xdcrC --> tankC[Air Column to Liquid Surface]
    tankC --> echoC[Reflected Echo]
    echoC --> xdcrC
    xdcrC --> rxC[Receive Amplifier with TGC]
    rxC --> threshC[Threshold and Echo Detection]
    threshC --> tdcC[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    rtdC[RTD Temperature Sensor] --> vcompC[Velocity Compensator v of T]
    vcompC --> tdcC
    tdcC --> mcuC[Microcontroller computes D and h]
    mcuC --> outC[Display and 4 to 20 mA Output]
    mcuC --> hhsC[False Echo Suppression Algorithm]
    hhsC --> threshC
```

**Description:** A single piezo transducer alternates between transmit and receive. Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) resolution of 10–100 picoseconds gives sub-millimetre distance resolution. The RTD compensates the sound velocity, and the false-echo suppression algorithm ignores reflections from ladders, agitators, and tank walls.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparative Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph I1[Inductive Method]
        i1A[Tank Liquid Level] --> i1B[Ferromagnetic Float]
        i1B --> i1C[Coil Inductance L]
        i1C --> i1D[AC Bridge or LC Oscillator]
        i1D --> i1E[Output Signal]
    end
    subgraph I2[Capacitive Method]
        j1A[Tank Liquid Level] --> j1B[Variable Capacitance Cx]
        j1B --> j1C[AC Bridge or Oscillator]
        j1C --> j1D[Output Signal]
    end
    subgraph I3[Ultrasonic Method]
        k1A[Tank Liquid Surface] --> k1B[Sound Pulse Echo Time t]
        k1B --> k1C[TDC and Microcontroller]
        k1C --> k1D[Output Signal]
    end
```

**Description:** Side-by-side functional comparison: the inductive method transduces level → float position → inductance; the capacitive method transduces level → dielectric coverage → capacitance; the ultrasonic method transduces level → echo time → distance.

---

- All node IDs are alphanumeric and descriptive (e.g., `primA`, `sec1A`, `bridgeB`, `tdcC`).
- No reserved keywords used as node names.
- No markdown bold/italic inside double-quoted labels — only clean uppercase text and standard punctuation.
- All arrows flow left-to-right or top-to-bottom; no ambiguous `subgraph` nesting.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **displacement-type inductive level gauge** for continuous measurement of liquid level.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A hollow ferromagnetic float surrounds a non-magnetic standpipe installed vertically inside the tank. The float rides on the liquid surface due to buoyancy.
- **(1 mark)** Inside the standpipe is wound a solenoid coil. As the float rises, a greater length of the coil is "filled" by the high-permeability core, increasing the coil's inductance $L$.
- **(1 mark)** This variable inductance is connected to an AC bridge or to the frequency-determining tank of an LC oscillator. The output frequency (or voltage) is calibrated to be linear with liquid level, giving a continuous readout.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between **dielectric-variation** and **conductivity-variation** modes of capacitive level measurement. Mention one example liquid for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Dielectric variation:* Used for **non-conductive liquids** (e.g., oil, petroleum, solvents). The rising liquid replaces air between the electrodes, changing the dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ in proportion to the filled height.
- **(1 mark)** *Conductivity variation (insulated-electrode mode):* Used for **conductive liquids** (e.g., water, acids, brine). The probe is coated with an insulator (PTFE); the conductive liquid forms one "plate" and the capacitance depends on the area of liquid contact.
- **(1 mark)** *Key difference:* Dielectric mode exploits change in $\varepsilon_r$; conductivity mode exploits change in effective electrode area — and the latter requires a non-conductive coating to prevent short-circuiting.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> Each sub-part is **7 marks**, totalling **14 marks** per main question. KTU ESE convention: present both options; examiners will give either.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a capacitance level gauge** for a non-conductive liquid stored in a metallic tank. Derive the relation between the measured capacitance and the liquid level $h$. State the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** A capacitance-type level sensor uses a coaxial geometry with inner radius $R_1 = 10$ mm, outer radius $R_2 = 30$ mm, and active length $L = 3$ m. The dielectric constant of the liquid is $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$. If the tank is 75 % full, calculate (i) the empty-tank capacitance, (ii) the capacitance at the given level, and (iii) the change in capacitance from the empty condition. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** Two vertical electrodes — a central rod and the metal tank wall (or an outer cylinder) — form a coaxial capacitor. The dielectric between them is air above the liquid and the liquid itself below. The liquid is non-conductive, so the electric field lines penetrate it freely.

2. **Working (2 marks):** As the level rises, the air column (low $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$) is progressively replaced by the liquid (high $\varepsilon_r$). The empty portion and the filled portion of the capacitor are in **parallel** (same voltage, separate spatial regions). Hence the total capacitance is the sum:
   $$C_{total} = C_{air} + C_{liq}$$

3. **Derivation (2 marks):** Using the cylindrical-capacitor formula for each section (as derived in Section 3.2):
   $$C_{total} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}\bigl[\varepsilon_{r,air}(L - h) + \varepsilon_{r,liq}\,h\bigr]$$
   Since $\varepsilon_{r,air} \approx 1$, this reduces to a linear function of $h$ with a fixed offset $C_0$.

4. **Assumptions (1 mark):**
   - The liquid is non-conductive (no short between electrodes).
   - The dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{r,liq}$ is constant with temperature and level.
   - No fringing effects at the liquid surface (valid for $L \gg R_2 - R_1$).
   - Temperature and pressure are constant.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

*Given:* $R_1 = 0.010$ m, $R_2 = 0.030$ m, $L = 3$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$, $h = 0.75 \times 3 = 2.25$ m.

**Step 1 — Geometric factor (1 mark):**

$$\ln(R_2/R_1) = \ln(30/10) = \ln 3 = 1.0986$$

**Step 2 — Empty-tank capacitance $C_0$ (2 marks):**

$$C_0 = \frac{2\pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(3)}{1.0986} = \frac{1.6691 \times 10^{-10}}{1.0986} = 1.5193 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{F} \approx 151.93\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance at 75 % full (2 marks):**

$$C_{total} = \frac{2\pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})}{1.0986}\bigl[(3 - 2.25) + (2.5)(2.25)\bigr]$$

$$= (1.5193 \times 10^{-10})\bigl[0.75 + 5.625\bigr] = (1.5193 \times 10^{-10})(6.375)$$

$$C_{total} = 9.686 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{F} \approx 968.6\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 4 — Change in capacitance (2 marks):**

$$\Delta C = C_{total} - C_0 = 968.6 - 151.93 = 816.67\,\text{pF}$$

✅ **Final Answers:** (i) $C_0 \approx 151.93$ pF, (ii) $C_{total} \approx 968.6$ pF, (iii) $\Delta C \approx 816.67$ pF.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **principle of ultrasonic level measurement** using the **pulsed-echo (Time-of-Flight) method**. Derive the relation between the echo time and the distance to the liquid surface. What is the role of temperature compensation? (7 marks)

**(b)** An ultrasonic level sensor is installed at the top of a 6 m tall chemical tank. The measured echo time is $t = 22$ ms. The ambient temperature is 40 °C. Calculate (i) the velocity of sound at 40 °C, (ii) the distance from the sensor to the liquid surface, and (iii) the liquid level. Take $v_0 = 343$ m/s at $T_0 = 293$ K. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** A piezo-electric transducer emits a short burst of high-frequency sound (20–200 kHz) into the air column above the liquid. The acoustic wave reflects from the gas–liquid interface (large acoustic impedance mismatch) and returns to the transducer, which now acts as a receiver. The round-trip time $t$ is measured electronically.

2. **Derivation (2 marks):** If $v$ is the speed of sound and $D$ is the one-way distance, then:
   $$2D = v \cdot t \quad\Rightarrow\quad D = \frac{v t}{2}$$
   The factor of 2 is essential because the wave travels the same path twice (down and back up).

3. **Temperature compensation (2 marks):** The velocity of sound in air depends strongly on absolute temperature: $v = v_0 \sqrt{T/T_0}$. A change of 10 °C in ambient temperature alters $v$ by about 1.7 %, causing a corresponding error in $D$. An in-built RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) feeds the live temperature to the microcontroller, which continuously updates $v$ before computing $D$.

4. **Additional considerations (1 mark):** False echoes from tank walls, agitators, and ladders are rejected by time-windowing and echo-amplitude thresholds. Foam, vapours, and turbulence degrade performance; in such cases radar (EM-wave) gauges are preferred over ultrasonic.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

*Given:* $T = 40 + 273 = 313$ K, $t = 22$ ms, $H = 6$ m, $v_0 = 343$ m/s, $T_0 = 293$ K.

**Step 1 — Velocity at 40 °C (2 marks):**

$$v = 343 \sqrt{313/293} = 343 \sqrt{1.0683} = 343 \times 1.0337 = 354.55\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Distance from sensor to liquid surface (2 marks):**

$$D = \frac{v t}{2} = \frac{354.55 \times 22 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{7.800}{2} = 3.900\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Liquid level (3 marks):**

$$h = H - D = 6.0 - 3.900 = 2.100\,\text{m}$$

✅ **Final Answers:** (i) $v \approx 354.55$ m/s, (ii) $D \approx 3.900$ m, (iii) $h = 2.10$ m.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (deducted heavily in KTU valuation):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ultrasonic distance equation. Writing $D = v t$ instead of $D = v t / 2$ will cost **2–3 marks outright**.
> 2. **Using air at 1 atm with $\varepsilon_r = 1.0006$ instead of $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$** in capacitive calculations — KTU examiners expect the approximation $\varepsilon_{r,air} \approx 1$.
> 3. **Mixing up "level" and "empty distance"** in ultrasonic problems. The gauge measures the air gap; level = total height − air gap.
> 4. **Drawing the LVDT secondaries in series-aiding** instead of series-opposition. This is a 1-mark loss for incorrect diagram.
> 5. **Not mentioning temperature compensation** in ultrasonic gauges — KTU explicitly tests this in every module.
> 6. **Using degrees Celsius in the temperature formula** $v = v_0 \sqrt{T/T_0}$. Always convert to Kelvin.
> 7. **Stating units inconsistently** — e.g., mixing cm and m, or pF with F. The examiner deducts ½ mark per unit mismatch.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Level Measurement, Gauging Methods):**

- **Inductive Gauge:**
  - Uses a **ferromagnetic float** moving along a coil or LVDT.
  - Output: change in $L$ (or differential voltage for LVDT).
  - Best for: conductive and non-conductive liquids, oils, fuels.
  - Limitation: float may stick in viscous fluids; affected by sludge.

- **Capacitive Gauge:**
  - $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$ for cylindrical geometry.
  - **Two modes:** dielectric-variation (non-conductive liquids) and conductivity-variation (conductive liquids with insulated probe).
  - Output is **linear in level $h$** when permittivity is constant.
  - Best for: liquids, slurries, powders, granular solids.

- **Ultrasonic Gauge:**
  - Non-contact, Time-of-Flight method: $D = v t / 2$.
  - $v = v_0 \sqrt{T/T_0}$; **always use Kelvin**.
  - Typical frequency: 40–200 kHz.
  - **Compensate** for temperature, vapours, foam, and false echoes.
  - Best for: corrosive, hygienic, explosive, and dirty environments.

- **Comparative Snapshot:**

| Property | Inductive | Capacitive | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Contact with liquid | Yes (float) | Yes (probe) | **No (non-contact)** |
| Output | $L$ / $V$ | $C$ / $f$ | $t$ / $D$ |
| Linearity | Good (LVDT) | Excellent (cylindrical) | Good (with $T$ comp.) |
| Sensitive to foam/vapour | No | No | **Yes** |
| Hygienic suitability | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Cost | Low | Low | Moderate–High |

- **Golden Formulas to Memorise:**
  1. $C_{cyl} = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$
  2. $C_{total}(h) = C_0 + \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_{r,liq} - 1)}{\ln(R_2/R_1)} h$
  3. $D = v t / 2$
  4. $v = v_0 \sqrt{T/T_0}$
  5. $L = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A / \ell$ (inductance with core)

- **Always-in-Exams Mnemonics:**
  - **Inductive = "Float follows; Flux grows; $L$ rises."**
  - **Capacitive = "Liquid rises; Dielectric shifts; $C$ rises."**
  - **Ultrasonic = "Pulse goes; Echo returns; $t$ tells the truth."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microwave Level Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microwave Level Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4: MICROWAVE LEVEL SWITCHES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Microwave Level Switch (MLS)** — A *point-level* (discrete, on/off) detection instrument that transmits low-power, high-frequency electromagnetic waves (typically in the **5.8 GHz**, **10 GHz**, **24 GHz**, or **26 GHz** ISM band) through a process medium and detects the **presence or absence of material** at a specific location by analyzing the reflected energy, time-of-flight, or frequency shift of the returning microwave signal.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the microwave level switch falls under the category of **non-contact or guided-wave radar-based point level measurement devices** used in industrial process control.
