RPS is **suitable for high-temperature and high-pressure vessels** (up to $250 \text{ °C}$, $10 \text{ bar}$) using extended shaft and high-temperature seals — capacitive probes often fail under such conditions due to electronic drift. *(1 mark per advantage, with 1 mark for clear technical justification.)* ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **"What is the function of the slip clutch in a Rotating Paddle Switch?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The slip clutch is a **torque-limiting mechanical coupling** placed between the synchronous motor and the paddle shaft. Its functions are: 1. To **protect the motor** from mechanical overload when the paddle is jammed by bulk material — the clutch slips instead of stalling the motor. 2. To **provide a precise, repeatable torque threshold** $T_{slip}$ that defines the exact material drag level at which the microswitch will be triggered — this is the **calibration parameter** of the instrument. 3. To **convert rotational slip into linear lever motion** via a cam mechanism, which directly actuates the SPDT microswitch. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** confuse the rotating paddle switch with a *vibrating fork* (tuning fork) level switch — they look similar but use different principles (mechanical stall vs. piezoelectric frequency shift). > - **Always state both** the motor type (synchronous) and the clutch type (slip) in construction questions — losing either costs 2 marks. > - When asked for "advantages," give **application-specific** advantages (e.g., "insensitive to dust"), not generic ones (e.g., "low cost"). > - In numerical problems, **show the unit** of every intermediate step — this is the single most common place students lose 1 mark. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Rotating Paddle Switch used for solid level measurement. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (Part a):** **Construction (3 marks):** A Rotating Paddle Switch consists of the following sub-assemblies: 1. **Enclosure (Hazardous-Area Rated):** A die-cast aluminium or stainless-steel housing containing the motor, clutch, microswitch, and terminal block. Typically IP65 / IP67 ingress protection, with ATEX/IECEx certification for Zone 21 dust atmospheres. 2. **Synchronous AC Motor:** A small shaded-pole synchronous motor (typically $2.5$ to $5$ W) that runs at a fixed speed $N_s = 120f/P$ determined only by supply frequency. 3. **Slip Clutch Assembly:** A spring-loaded friction clutch that disengages when the resisting torque exceeds a set value $T_{slip}$. Includes a cam that converts the slip displacement into a linear throw of the microswitch actuator. 4. **Microswitch (SPDT):** An electromechanical switch rated at $5 \text{ A}$, $250 \text{ V AC}$ that provides the output signal. 5. **Output Terminal Block / Electronic Module:** Modern versions may include a solid-state relay (SSR) or $4{-}20 \text{ mA}$ output. 6. **Shaft Seal & Mounting Flange:** A rotary seal (typically graphite or PTFE) preventing dust ingress into the enclosure. The shaft passes through a standard flange (DN50, DN100, or 2" BSP/NPT thread). 7. **Paddle Blade:** A replaceable metal blade (stainless steel 304/316) in vee, crowfoot, or fork geometry, sized according to bulk density. **Working Principle (4 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. The motor continuously rotates the paddle via the slip clutch. In free air, paddle drag torque $T_{air} \ll T_{slip}$, so the paddle rotates freely and the microswitch is in the **NORMAL** state. When bulk solid rises and buries the paddle, the material exerts drag torque $T_{mat}$. When $T_{mat} \geq T_{slip}$, the clutch slips, the cam lifts the microswitch lever, and the relay contact transfers — providing a **HIGH-LEVEL** (or **LOW-LEVEL**, depending on installation) alarm to the plant controller. When the level falls and the paddle is uncovered, drag torque drops below $T_{slip}$, the clutch re-engages, and the relay resets. **(b) A 4-pole, 50 Hz synchronous motor is used in a Rotating Paddle Switch. The paddle has a tip radius of 60 mm. A gear reduction of 800:1 is used. Calculate: (i) Motor speed, (ii) Paddle speed in rpm and rad/s, (iii) Paddle tip speed in m/s. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (Part b):** **Given:** - Poles $P = 4$ - Frequency $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ - Tip radius $R_p = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$ - Gear ratio $G = 800:1$ **(i) Motor speed (1 mark):** $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **(ii) Paddle speed in rpm (2 marks):** $$N_{paddle} = \frac{N_s}{G} = \frac{1500}{800} = 1.875 \text{ rpm}$$ **Paddle speed in rad/s (2 marks):** $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N_{paddle}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1.875}{60} = 0.1963 \text{ rad/s}$$ **(iii) Paddle tip speed (2 marks):** $$v_t = \omega \cdot R_p = 0.1963 \times 0.06 = 0.01178 \text{ m/s} \approx 11.78 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Verification of slow tip speed:** $v_t \approx 1.18 \text{ cm/s}$ — well below the threshold where fine powders would be thrown off, confirming the design is robust for light bulk solids. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **(a) Compare Rotating Paddle Switches with Capacitive Probes and Vibrating Fork (Tuning Fork) Level Switches for bulk solid level detection. State at least four comparison points. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (Part a):** | # | Parameter | Rotating Paddle Switch | Capacitive Probe | Vibrating Fork | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Operating Principle** | Mechanical stall detection by motor slip | Change in capacitance between probe and vessel wall | Piezoelectric resonance frequency shift | | 2 | **Sensitivity to Material** | Independent of dielectric, conductivity, moisture | Highly dependent on dielectric constant of material | Mostly independent, but affected by heavy buildup | | 3 | **Effect of Dust Build-up** | Minimal — paddle rotation sheds dust | Severe — dust causes false high-level readings | Moderate — fork must be cleaned periodically | | 4 | **Moving Parts** | Yes (paddle, motor, clutch) | No (solid-state) | Yes (tuning fork tines) | | 5 | **Maintenance** | Periodic bearing/clutch check, ~2 yr | Low | Very low | | 6 | **Suitable Bulk Density** | Medium to high ($>200 \text{ kg/m}^3$) | All densities | Light to medium ($>50 \text{ kg/m}^3$) | | 7 | **Power Consumption** | $2.5$ to $5 \text{ W}$ continuous | < $0.5 \text{ W}$ | < $1 \text{ W}$ | | 8 | **Cost (INR, typical)** | ₹ 8000 to ₹ 25000 | ₹ 5000 to ₹ 15000 | ₹ 20000 to ₹ 60000 | *(Any four well-explained comparison points = 7 marks. Each point with at least one distinguishing technical feature = full credit.)* **(b) Explain the installation procedure and the typical three-position high-mid-low mounting strategy for Rotating Paddle Switches in a cement silo. Justify the choice of paddle geometry for clinker (bulk density 1500 kg/m³). (7 marks)** **Model Solution (Part b):** **Installation Procedure (4 marks):** 1. **Pre-Installation Checks:** - Verify supply voltage matches the motor nameplate ($24 \text{ V DC}$ / $110 \text{ V AC}$ / $220 \text{ V AC}$). - Inspect the rotary seal and flange gasket for damage. - Confirm the paddle blade is the correct geometry for the bulk material. 2. **Mechanical Mounting:** - Weld a flanged nozzle (DN100, PN16) on the silo wall at the desired elevation. - Insert the RPS shaft through the nozzle and bolt the mounting flange with a graphite or PTFE gasket. - For top-mount (high-level) installation, the switch is typically mounted on the **vertical section of the silo cone** (not the cylindrical wall) to avoid asymmetric material flow. - For side-mount (mid- or low-level), use a horizontal flange on the cylindrical section. 3. **Cable Glanding & Earthing:** - Use double-compression cable glands suitable for dust environments (IP65 minimum). - Connect the protective earth (PE) terminal to the silo body to dissipate static. 4. **Electrical Termination & Loop Check:** - Wire the SPDT contacts (Common / NO / NC) to the PLC digital input module. - Energise the motor and verify paddle rotation by listening for the slow "tick-tick" (or by stroboscope if available). - Manually stall the paddle to confirm the relay output toggles — this is the **commissioning verification** step. 5. **Initial Run & Calibration:** - Fill the silo in test mode and verify switch trigger level. - If the switch does not trip on light material, change to a larger paddle area. **Three-Position High-Mid-Low Mounting Strategy (2 marks):** - **RPS-A (High-Level, 90% fill):** Top cone section. Output goes to PLC → **stop filling conveyor** and **trigger horn/strobe alarm**. - **RPS-B (Mid-Level, 50% fill):** Cylindrical mid-section. Output goes to PLC → **logging/display** (operator awareness). - **RPS-C (Low-Level, 10% fill):** Bottom cone section. Output goes to PLC → **start filling conveyor** and **interlock** (low-level protection for the discharge screw). **Paddle Geometry Justification for Clinker ($\rho_b = 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3$) (1 mark):** Clinker is an **abrasive, heavy, granular material** with sharp edges. A **three-blade crowfoot (or single robust flat blade) in 316L stainless steel** is recommended because: - It has a higher drag-torque margin, easily exceeding the clutch slip torque of $0.08 \text{ N·m}$ for medium-density clinker. - 316L SS resists abrasion and the alkaline cement dust environment. - The crowfoot geometry sheds adhered material on each rotation, preventing false "stuck" alarms. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rotating Paddle Switches** - ☐ **Principle:** Mechanical impedance — paddle drag torque exceeding clutch slip torque. - ☐ **Motor type:** Shaded-pole **synchronous** AC motor (fixed speed, voltage-independent). - ☐ **Motor speed formula:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — *not* $120f(1-s)/P$ (synchronous, not induction). - ☐ **Slip clutch:** A **torque limiter** that protects the motor and triggers the switch. - ☐ **Switch output:** SPDT relay, **$5$ A at $250$ V AC** standard. - ☐ **Bulk density range:** $100$ to $3000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. - ☐ **Paddle speed:** Slow ($\sim 1.5 \text{ rpm}$) to prevent throwing off fine powders. - ☐ **Output is point-level (discrete ON/OFF), NOT continuous** — do not confuse with continuous level transmitters. - ☐ **Three sub-assemblies to mention in every construction answer:** motor, slip clutch, microswitch. - ☐ **Key advantage:** Insensitive to material colour, conductivity, dielectric, and dust — this is *the* one-line reason for choosing RPS over capacitive probes. - ☐ **Key limitation:** Cannot be used for **fluids** (it is for bulk solids only) and has a **minimum bulk density** below which it will not trip. - ☐ **Installation topologies:** High, mid, low-level mounting on silo cone or cylindrical wall. - ☐ **Paddle material:** Stainless Steel 304/316 for corrosion resistance. - ☐ **Industry sectors:** Cement, grain, plastic pellets, coal, fertilizer, chemical powders. - ☐ **Synchronous motor speed is determined by frequency only** — not by voltage (this is a frequently asked short-answer question). - ☐ **Paddle tip speed** $\sim 10 \text{ mm/s}$ — slow enough to be embedded, fast enough to self-clean.

topics.title: Laser Sensor. Turbidity Measurement - Electronic Turbidity Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET602 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laser Sensor. Turbidity Measurement - Electronic Turbidity Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Laser Sensor — Formal Academic Definition

A **Laser Sensor** is an optoelectronic transducer that utilizes the highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of photons emitted from a **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation (LASER)** source to detect, measure, or monitor physical parameters such as displacement, distance, velocity, vibration, level, and surface characteristics. In the context of industrial instrumentation, laser sensors are categorized as **non-contact, high-precision, long-operational-life** measurement devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):** "A laser sensor is a device which uses a laser beam to measure physical quantities. The laser beam is directed at the target object and the reflected or transmitted light is analyzed to determine the parameter of interest."

### Properties of Laser Light (Essential to Memorize)

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Monochromaticity** | Single wavelength (very narrow spectral line width, $\Delta \lambda \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ nm) |
| **Coherence** | Spatial + Temporal — wavefronts are in phase in both space and time |
| **Directionality** | Extremely low divergence (typically 0.1 to 1 milliradian) |
| **High Intensity / Brightness** | Energy concentrated in a narrow beam → $10^{6}$ to $10^{9}$ times brighter than sunlight per unit area |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine shining a *normal torchlight* at a wall — the spot is fuzzy, large, and spreads out. Now imagine a thin, pencil-like beam that travels kilometers without spreading — that is a laser. Because this "pencil beam" is so precise, we can use it like a *ruler made of light* to measure tiny distances, detect the presence of objects on a fast-moving conveyor, or even "see" microscopic particles suspended in water. The laser doesn't "feel" the object — it merely illuminates it, and a smart detector reads the returning light to deduce the answer.

### Engineering Relevance

Laser sensors are indispensable in **Level Measurement** (in vessels with dust, steam, or foam), **Thickness Gauging** (metals, paper, films), **Vibration Analysis** (rotating shafts, turbine blades), **Speed Measurement** (vehicles, projectiles via LIDAR principle), and **Turbidity Measurement** (the focus of our second topic in this module).

---

## 1.2 Turbidity & Electronic Turbidity Meters — Formal Academic Definition

**Turbidity** is an optical property of a fluid (typically water) that causes light to be **scattered and absorbed** rather than transmitted in a straight line through the sample. It is fundamentally a measure of the **suspended particulate matter** (clay, silt, organic matter, algae, microorganisms) in a liquid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU / IS 3025 / ISO 7027 Definition:** "Turbidity of water is the reduction of transparency of a sample due to the presence of finely divided suspended matter. It is measured optically, either by the attenuation of a transmitted light beam (absorptiometric method) or by measuring the intensity of scattered light at right angles to the incident beam (nephelometric method)."

### Standard Units of Turbidity

| Unit | Full Form | Measurement Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **NTU** | Nephelometric Turbidity Units | Scattered light (90°) — US EPA standard |
| **FTU** | Formazin Turbidity Units | Same principle using Formazin reference |
| **FNU** | Formazin Nephelometric Units | ISO 7027 European standard |
| **JTU** | Jackson Turbidity Units | Obsolete — visual candle turbidimeter |
| **mg/L (SiO₂)** | Equivalent silica concentration | Older gravimetric comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Conversion Note:** 1 NTU ≈ 1 FTU ≈ 1 FNU for most practical purposes. The Formazin primary reference standard is used to calibrate all nephelometric instruments worldwide. 1 NTU of Formazin ≈ 0.58 mg/L of silica equivalence in older scales.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a glass of clear water held against a beam of sunlight — you can clearly see the light spot. Now add a spoonful of milk or stir up some mud — the light gets scattered in all directions and the beam becomes "fuzzy" or invisible. The **dirtier the water, the more the light gets scattered**. An electronic turbidity meter simply measures *how much* the light is scattered (or how much is blocked), and translates that into a numerical turbidity value (e.g., 5 NTU = clean tap water; 100+ NTU = muddy river water).

### Industrial Relevance

Turbidity measurement is **critical** in:
- **Drinking water treatment plants** (regulatory limit < 1 NTU for potable water)
- **Wastewater treatment** (effluent quality monitoring)
- **Pharmaceutical & beverage industry** (clarity of products)
- **Power plant boiler feed water** (high purity, < 0.1 NTU)
- **Aquaculture & aquarium management**
- **Pulp & paper industry** (process control)

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Values for the Examiner:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> - Wavelength of typical He-Ne laser: $\lambda = 632.8$ nm (red)
> - Wavelength of typical laser diode: $\lambda = 650$ nm to $905$ nm (near-IR)
> - Standard ISO 7027 wavelength: $\lambda = 860$ nm ± 30 nm
> - EPA method 180.1 wavelength: $\lambda = 400$ nm to 600 nm (tungsten white light)
> - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Primary calibration standard: **Formazin suspension** (4000 NTU stock)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Laser Sensor — Operational Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1: Population Inversion (The Pumping Action)
At thermal equilibrium, more atoms exist in the **lower energy state** ($E_1$) than in the **upper energy state** ($E_2$). For lasing to occur, we must *pump* energy into the medium (via flash lamp, electrical discharge, or diode current) to create a **population inversion**:

$$
N_2 > N_1
$$

where $N_1$ and $N_2$ are the number densities of atoms in lower and upper states respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" behind it:** Stimulated emission must dominate over absorption for net amplification. This requires more atoms ready to drop down ($E_2 \rightarrow E_1$) than atoms ready to absorb ($E_1 \rightarrow E_2$).

### Step 2: Stimulated Emission
An incoming photon of energy $E = h\nu$ (where $h$ is Planck's constant and $\nu$ is frequency) triggers an excited atom to drop to the lower state, emitting a **second photon** that is *identical* in phase, frequency, direction, and polarization.

The emitted photon frequency is given by Bohr's relation:

$$
\nu = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{h}
$$

### Step 3: Optical Resonator (Feedback)
The lasing medium is placed between two mirrors — a **fully reflective (100%) mirror** and a **partially transmissive (~95–99%) output coupler**. Photons bounce back and forth, inducing more stimulated emission → **light amplification**. The output coupler transmits a fraction as the **laser beam**.

### Step 4: Detection of Returned Light
In an industrial sensor, the laser beam is aimed at a target. Depending on the measurement principle, the system detects one of:
- **Reflected light intensity** (for distance/level via triangulation or time-of-flight)
- **Time of Flight (TOF)** for the round trip → distance
- **Doppler-shifted frequency** for velocity
- **Interference fringes** for ultra-precise displacement

### Step 5: Signal Processing
The photodetector (Photodiode, APD, or Position-Sensitive Detector) converts the returned light to an electrical signal, which is amplified, filtered, and processed by embedded electronics to compute the measured parameter.

---

## 2.2 Types of Industrial Laser Sensors

| Type | Working Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Laser Triangulation Sensor** | Geometry of reflected spot on PSD | Short-range distance (mm to m) |
| **Laser Time-of-Flight (ToF) Sensor** | Measures $t$ for round trip; $d = ct/2$ | Long-range distance (m to km) |
| **Laser Doppler Vibrometer (LDV)** | Measures Doppler shift $\Delta f = 2v/\lambda$ | Vibration, velocity |
| **Laser Diffuse-Reflective Sensor** | Detects presence/absence based on retroreflection | Object detection on conveyors |
| **Laser Through-Beam Sensor** | Source and detector in separate housings | High-speed counting, edge detection |
| **LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** | Scanning pulsed laser; 3D mapping | Autonomous vehicles, terrain mapping |
| **Laser Interferometer (Michelson)** | Interference fringe counting | Sub-micron displacement (metrology) |

---

## 2.3 Turbidity Measurement — Two Fundamental Principles

### A. Nephelometric Method (90° Scattering) — Industry Standard

The light source and detector are arranged at a **90° angle**. The detector measures the intensity of light *scattered* by suspended particles.

**Key Equation (Rayleigh-Debye-Gans scattering for small particles):**

$$
I_s = I_0 \cdot K \cdot \frac{N \cdot V^2}{\lambda^4} \cdot (1 + \cos^2 \theta)
$$

where:
- $I_s$ = intensity of scattered light (W/m²)
- $I_0$ = intensity of incident light (W/m²)
- $K$ = instrument constant
- $N$ = number of particles per unit volume
- $V$ = volume of a single particle
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident light
- $\theta$ = scattering angle (90° in nephelometry)

**Why 90°?** It minimizes the effect of incident light and directly captures the side-scatter proportional to particle concentration.

### B. Absorptiometric (Transmittance) Method

The detector is placed **in line (180°)** with the light source, measuring how much light is *not* absorbed/blocked by the sample. Based on the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$
I_t = I_0 \cdot e^{-\tau \cdot x}
$$

where:
- $I_t$ = transmitted light intensity
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $\tau$ = turbidity coefficient (analogous to absorption coefficient)
- $x$ = optical path length (typically 1–10 cm)

In turbidity form, this is often written as:

$$
\tau = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\left(\frac{I_t}{I_0}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Absorptiometric method is simpler and cheaper, but saturates at high turbidities (because $I_t \to 0$, making the ratio noisy). Nephelometric method is more sensitive at low turbidities and is the **regulatory standard** for drinking water.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = \lambda \nu$ | Speed of light relation | $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | $E = h\nu$ | Photon energy | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 3 | $\nu = (E_2 - E_1)/h$ | Bohr frequency condition | Hz |
| 4 | $d = (c \cdot t) / 2$ | Laser ToF distance measurement | $c$ in m/s, $t$ in s |
| 5 | $\Delta f = 2 v \cos\alpha / \lambda$ | Doppler shift for laser vibrometer | $v$ = target velocity, $\alpha$ = angle |
| 6 | $I_s \propto I_0 \cdot N \cdot V^2 / \lambda^4$ | Rayleigh scattering intensity (small particles) | $\lambda^{-4}$ explains why blue sky is blue |
| 7 | $I_t = I_0 \cdot e^{-\tau x}$ | Beer-Lambert law for turbidity | $\tau$ = turbidity coefficient, $x$ = path length |
| 8 | $\tau = -\ln(I_t/I_0)/x$ | Turbidity coefficient calculation | m$^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $P = P_0 \cdot e^{-k_{ext} \cdot c \cdot x}$ | Extinction-based turbidity (Mie regime) | $k_{ext}$ = extinction efficiency |
| 10 | $\text{NTU}_{sample} = \text{NTU}_{formazin} \cdot (I_{s,sample}/I_{s,formazin})$ | Nephelometric calibration | Direct proportionality in linear range |
| 11 | $\theta_{div} = \lambda / (\pi w_0)$ | Laser beam divergence (Gaussian beam) | $w_0$ = beam waist radius |
| 12 | $N_2 > N_1$ | Population inversion condition | Necessary for lasing |

> [!IMPORTANT]

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Laser Distance Measurement using Time-of-Flight

### Problem Setup
A laser pulse is emitted, strikes a target at distance $d$, and returns to the detector. The round-trip time measured by the electronics is $t$. Derive the distance equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the basic relationship between distance, speed, and time:**

The light travels from source to target (distance $d$) and back to detector (another distance $d$). Total optical path length $L_{total}$:

$$
L_{total} = d + d = 2d
$$

**Step 2 — Relate path length to time using the speed of light:**

By the definition of constant speed:

$$
L_{total} = c \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $L_{total}$:**

$$
2d = c \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$:**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — State the explicit form with units:**

$$
d = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}) \cdot t}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 1.499 \times 10^{8} \cdot t \quad \text{(meters, with } t \text{ in seconds)}}
$$

### Numerical Example
If the measured round-trip time is $t = 6.67$ ns (typical for a 1 m target), then:

$$
d = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (6.67 \times 10^{-9})}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{1.999 \times 10^{0}}{2} = 0.9995 \text{ m} \approx 1 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment to Examiner:** Time-of-flight systems need **picosecond-level timing resolution** to achieve millimeter accuracy. This is why modern laser ToF sensors use **Time-to-Digital Converters (TDC)** or **phase-shift detection** in the continuous-wave (CW) variant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Beer-Lambert Law Applied to Turbidity (Absorptiometric Method)

### Problem Setup
A beam of monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ enters a sample cell of length $x$ containing a turbid medium. The transmitted intensity is $I_t$. Derive the relationship between the turbidity coefficient $\tau$ and the measured intensities.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Consider an infinitesimally thin slice $dx$ of the medium:**

The decrease in intensity $dI$ as light passes through the slice is proportional to the incident intensity $I(x)$ and the slice thickness $dx$:

$$
dI = -\tau \cdot I(x) \cdot dx
$$

The negative sign indicates that intensity *decreases* as $x$ increases.

**Step 2 — Separate the variables:**

$$
\frac{dI}{I(x)} = -\tau \cdot dx
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from $x = 0$ (entry) to $x$ (exit):**

On the left, $I$ goes from $I_0$ to $I_t$:

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I_t} \frac{dI}{I} = -\tau \int_{0}^{x} dx
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$
[\ln I]_{I_0}^{I_t} = -\tau \cdot [x]_0^x
$$

$$
\ln I_t - \ln I_0 = -\tau \cdot x
$$

**Step 5 — Use logarithm properties:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{I_t}{I_0}\right) = -\tau \cdot x
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
\frac{I_t}{I_0} = e^{-\tau x}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_t = I_0 \cdot e^{-\tau x}}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\tau$ (when $I_0$, $I_t$, and $x$ are measured):**

$$
\tau = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\left(\frac{I_t}{I_0}\right)
$$

### Numerical Example
A sample cell of path length $x = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m. Incident intensity $I_0 = 1000$ (arbitrary units). Transmitted intensity $I_t = 500$.

$$
\tau = -\frac{1}{0.05} \cdot \ln(500/1000) = -\frac{1}{0.05} \cdot (-0.6931) = 13.86 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment to Examiner:** This $\tau$ is *not* NTU — it must be **calibrated against Formazin standards** to convert to NTU. The instrument's firmware stores a calibration curve $\tau \leftrightarrow \text{NTU}$ (typically linear in the 0–40 NTU range, polynomial beyond).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nephelometric Response for Small Particles (Rayleigh Regime)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Rayleigh Scattering Intensity (per particle):**

For a single spherical particle of diameter $D$ (radius $r$), refractive index $n$, illuminated by light of wavelength $\lambda$ and incident intensity $I_0$:

$$
I_{s,1} = I_0 \cdot \frac{24 \pi^3}{r^2 \lambda^4} \cdot \left(\frac{n^2 - n_0^2}{n^2 + 2n_0^2}\right)^2 \cdot V^2
$$

where $n_0$ is the refractive index of the medium (water $\approx 1.33$) and $V = (4/3)\pi r^3$ is the particle volume.

**Step 2 — For $N$ independent particles per unit volume:**

Total scattered intensity is the linear sum (incoherent addition):

$$
I_s = N \cdot I_{s,1} = I_0 \cdot N \cdot V^2 \cdot \frac{24 \pi^3}{r^2 \lambda^4} \cdot \left(\frac{n^2 - n_0^2}{n^2 + 2n_0^2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Angular dependence at 90° (nephelometric):**

The factor $(1 + \cos^2 \theta)$ at $\theta = 90°$ gives $(1 + 0) = 1$, so the angular term drops out:

$$
I_s(90°) = I_0 \cdot N \cdot V^2 \cdot \frac{24 \pi^3}{r^2 \lambda^4} \cdot \left(\frac{n^2 - n_0^2}{n^2 + 2n_0^2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion — Key proportionality:**

$$
\boxed{I_s(90°) \propto N \cdot V^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** For fixed particle size (constant $V$), scattered intensity at 90° is *directly proportional to particle number concentration $N$*, which is itself proportional to mass concentration (turbidity in NTU). This is the foundation of nephelometric calibration linearity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Nephelometric Method | Absorptiometric Method |
|---|---|---|
| Detector angle | 90° (side scatter) | 180° (forward transmittance) |
| Best range | 0 – 40 NTU (low turbidity) | 40 – 4000 NTU (high turbidity) |
| Sensitivity to low turbidity | **High** | Low (signal saturates) |
| Sensitivity to high turbidity | Poor (multiple scattering) | **High** |
| Particle size effect | Strong ($\propto V^2$) | Moderate |
| Color interference | Minimal | Severe (colored samples absorb) |
| Regulatory acceptance (EPA, ISO) | **Standard** | Limited to specific applications |
| Instrument cost | Higher (precision optics) | Lower (simple setup) |
| Typical light source | Tungsten (EPA) or IR LED 860 nm (ISO) | Any monochromatic source |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `pumpA`, `laserBlock1`).
- No reserved Mermaid keywords used as node IDs.
- Labels contain only raw uppercase/lowercase alphanumeric text and spaces (no bold/italic/HTML).

---

## 4.2 Diagram A — Laser Sensor Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    sourceA[PUMP SOURCE ELECTRICAL OR OPTICAL] --> laserBlock1[LASING MEDIUM HELIUM NEON OR SEMICONDUCTOR]
    laserBlock1 --> mirror1[FULLY REFLECTIVE MIRROR R equals 100 PERCENT]
    laserBlock1 --> mirror2[PARTIALLY TRANSMISSIVE OUTPUT COUPLER T equals 95 PERCENT]
    mirror1 <--> laserBlock1
    laserBlock2 <--> mirror2
    mirror2 --> beamOut[COLLIMATED LASER BEAM WAVELENGTH LAMBDA]
    beamOut --> targetX[INDUSTRIAL TARGET OBJECT]
    targetX --> returnLight[REFLECTED OR SCATTERED LIGHT]
    returnLight --> photoDet[PHOTODETECTOR APD OR PHOTODIODE]
    photoDet --> ampStage1[TRANSIMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER]
    ampStage1 --> filterB[LOW PASS FILTER AND NOISE GATE]
    filterB --> procUnit[MICROCONTROLLER OR DSP]
    procUnit --> displayY[DIGITAL DISPLAY AND ANALOG OUTPUT 4 TO 20 MA]
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**
- The **lasing medium + two mirrors** form the **optical resonator**.
- The pump source creates **population inversion** ($N_2 > N_1$).
- Stimulated emission produces the **collimated beam**.
- The returned light is converted to an **electrical signal** and processed to extract the measured parameter.

---

## 4.3 Diagram B — Nephelometric Turbidity Meter Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    lightSrc1[STABLE LIGHT SOURCE TUNGSTEN 400 TO 600 NM OR IR LED 860 NM] --> lens1[COLLIMATING OPTICS LENS L1]
    lens1 --> sampleCell[SAMPLE CUVETTE PATH LENGTH 1 TO 10 CM]
    sampleCell --> beamStop[BEAM STOP TO ABSORB TRANSMITTED LIGHT]
    sampleCell --> scatterPath[SCATTERED LIGHT AT 90 DEGREE]
    scatterPath --> lens2[FOCUSING LENS L2]
    lens2 --> photoDiode1[SILICON PHOTODIODE OR PMT DETECTOR]
    photoDiode1 --> ampStage2[LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER]
    ampStage2 --> adcBlock1[16 BIT ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    adcBlock1 --> mcuUnit1[MICROCONTROLLER WITH FORMAZIN CALIBRATION CURVE]
    mcuUnit1 --> lcdOut[DIGITAL DISPLAY IN NTU]
    mcuUnit1 --> signalOut[4 TO 20 MA OR RS485 OUTPUT]
```

**Key Annotation:**
- Notice the **beam stop** in the 180° path — it blocks transmitted light so that the detector at 90° only measures scattered light.
- The **calibration curve** stored in the MCU converts raw scattered intensity (in counts) → NTU using Formazin standards.

---

## 4.4 Diagram C — Absorptiometric (Transmittance) Turbidity Meter Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    lightSrc2[STABLE LIGHT SOURCE LED OR LASER DIODE] --> lens3[COLLIMATING LENS L3]
    lens3 --> sampleCell2[SAMPLE CUVETTE PATH LENGTH 2 TO 10 CM]
    sampleCell2 --> lens4[FOCUSING LENS L4]
    lens4 --> photoDiode2[SILICON PHOTODIODE IN LINE 180 DEGREE]
    photoDiode2 --> logAmp[LOGARITHMIC AMPLIFIER FOR BEER LAMBERT LAW]
    logAmp --> adcBlock2[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    adcBlock2 --> mcuUnit2[MICROCONTROLLER WITH CALIBRATION TABLE]
    mcuUnit2 --> displayUnit[NTU DISPLAY]
```

**Reading Guide:** The **logarithmic amplifier** is essential because the Beer-Lambert equation involves $\ln(I_t/I_0)$. Performing the logarithm in hardware simplifies the firmware.

---

## 4.5 Diagram D — Laser Triangulation Sensor (Short-Range Distance)

```mermaid
graph TD
    laserDiode1[SEMICONDUCTOR LASER DIODE 650 NM] --> projLens[PROJECTION LENS]
    projLens --> targetObj[MEASUREMENT TARGET]
    targetObj --> reflBeam[REFLECTED BEAM AT ANGLE THETA]
    reflBeam --> recLens[RECEIVER LENS]
    recLens --> psdUnit[POSITION SENSITIVE DETECTOR PSD OR CMOS]
    psdUnit --> signalProc[TRIANGULATION ALGORITHM COMPUTE X]
    signalProc --> distOutput[DISTANCE OUTPUT D]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Triangulation Principle:** The position of the spot on the PSD changes as the target moves. By calibrating spot position ↔ distance, the system achieves **micron-level resolution** over a working range of 10–500 mm.

---

## 4.6 Diagram E — Laser Time-of-Flight Long-Range Distance Sensor

```mermaid
graph TD
    pulseGen[PULSE GENERATOR NANOSECOND WIDTH] --> laserEmitP[LASER DIODE EMITTER 905 NM]
    laserEmitP --> freeSpace[FREE SPACE OPTICAL PATH]
    freeSpace --> tgtObjF[TARGET AT DISTANCE D]
    tgtObjF --> returnSig[RETURN PULSE]
    returnSig --> apdUnit[AVALANCHE PHOTODIODE APD]
    pulseGen --> tdcStart[START TRIGGER TO TIME TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    apdUnit --> tdcStop[STOP TRIGGER TO TIME TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    tdcStart --> tdcChip[TDC CHIP PICOSECOND RESOLUTION]
    tdcStop --> tdcChip
    tdcChip --> calcUnit[CALCULATE D EQUALS C TIMES T DIVIDED BY 2]
    calcUnit --> outPulse[OUTPUT DISTANCE]
```

**Engineering Note:** A TDC chip measures time intervals with ~10 picosecond resolution, corresponding to ~1.5 mm distance resolution per count.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question Paper Style)]

**Q: Define the term "Turbidity". List any two standard units used to express turbidity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Turbidity is an optical property of a fluid that causes light to be scattered and absorbed by suspended particles, rather than transmitted in a straight line. It is a measure of the cloudiness or haziness of a fluid caused by the presence of finely divided suspended solids.
2. **Units (1.5 marks, 0.75 each):** The two standard units are:
   - **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)** — measured by 90° scattered light, US EPA standard.
   - **FTU (Formazin Turbidity Units)** — based on the Formazin primary calibration standard. (Alternatively, JTU, FNU may also be given.)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question Paper Style)]

**Q: What is a Laser Sensor? Mention any two advantages of laser sensors over conventional LED-based photoelectric sensors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** A laser sensor is a non-contact optoelectronic device that uses a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated laser beam to measure physical quantities such as distance, displacement, level, velocity, or surface condition by analyzing the reflected, scattered, or transmitted light.
2. **Advantages (1.5 marks, 0.75 each):**
   - **Higher precision:** Narrow beam enables measurement of very small targets and detection of minute position changes (sub-millimeter accuracy).
   - **Longer sensing range:** Low divergence allows detection up to several hundred meters vs. a few meters for LED sensors.
   - *(Bonus acceptable: immunity to ambient light interference, visible aiming spot for easy alignment.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module Internal Choice. Student answers EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** from a module. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[a] Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a Laser Time-of-Flight (ToF) distance measurement sensor. Derive the relation between distance and measured time of flight. (7 marks)**
**[b] With a neat diagram, explain the principle of a Nephelometric Turbidity Meter. How does it differ from an Absorptiometric turbidity meter? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The system consists of:
1. A **pulse generator** producing narrow electrical pulses (nanosecond width).
2. A **semiconductor laser diode** (typically 905 nm near-IR) as the emitter, triggered by the pulse.
3. An **optical transmit lens** to collimate the beam toward the target.
4. A **receiver lens** that collects the reflected pulse from the target.
5. An **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** as the photodetector — chosen for high sensitivity and fast response.
6. A **Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) chip** with picosecond resolution. The *start* trigger is the pulse generator output; the *stop* trigger is the APD signal.
7. A **microcontroller / DSP** that reads the time interval $t$ and computes $d = ct/2$.
8. A **display / 4-20 mA output** for industrial integration.

**Derivation of Distance Equation (4 marks):**

Let $d$ be the one-way distance to the target. The light travels a total optical path of $2d$ (from source to target and back). Since light moves at speed $c$, the time $t$ required is:

$$
2d = c \cdot t
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

Substituting $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:

$$
d = 1.499 \times 10^{8} \cdot t \quad \text{(meters, with } t \text{ in s)}
$$

**For phase-shift variant (CW ToF):** If a continuous wave of frequency $f_{mod}$ is used and the phase shift of the return signal is $\phi$:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Nephelometric Principle (3.5 marks):**
- A **collimated light source** (tungsten or IR LED at 860 nm per ISO 7027) illuminates a sample cell.
- A **photodetector** is placed at **90°** to the incident beam.
- Suspended particles in the sample **scatter** light in all directions. The detector measures the intensity of side-scattered light $I_s$.
- According to the **Rayleigh scattering** law, $I_s \propto N \cdot V^2$ (for small particles), which is directly proportional to turbidity in NTU.
- The instrument is **calibrated using Formazin standards** (a polymer suspension of known NTU), and a calibration curve (NTU vs. detector signal) is stored in firmware.

**Differences from Absorptiometric Method (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Nephelometric | Absorptiometric |
|---|---|---|
| Detector angle | 90° | 180° (in line) |
| Measured quantity | Scattered intensity | Transmitted intensity |
| Equation | $I_s \propto$ concentration | $I_t = I_0 e^{-\tau x}$ |
| Best range | 0 – 40 NTU | 40 – 4000 NTU |
| Sensitivity | High at low turbidity | Better at high turbidity |
| Color interference | Low | High |
| Standard | EPA / ISO 7027 | Process industry only |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[a] Explain the principle of operation of a Laser Doppler Vibrometer (LDV) with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the Doppler frequency shift. (7 marks)**
**[b] Describe the construction and working of an electronic turbidity meter using the absorption (transmittance) principle. State the Beer-Lambert law used. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Principle of Operation (3.5 marks):**
- A Laser Doppler Vibrometer measures the **vibration velocity of a surface** by exploiting the **Doppler effect** on laser light reflected from the moving surface.
- The laser beam from a He-Ne or laser diode source is split into two beams using a **beam splitter**:
  - **Reference beam (local oscillator)** — goes directly to the photodetector.
  - **Probe beam** — focused onto the target surface via a lens.
- Light reflected from the moving target undergoes a **Doppler frequency shift** proportional to the target velocity component along the beam direction.
- The reflected beam is recombined with the reference beam at the photodetector. The photodetector produces a **beat signal** at the **difference frequency** (= Doppler shift $\Delta f$).
- The beat frequency is measured electronically to obtain the target velocity.

**Derivation of Doppler Shift (3.5 marks):**

Consider a target moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\alpha$ to the laser beam direction. The light from the laser has frequency $f$. The reflected light has a Doppler shift given by the classical two-way Doppler formula:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{2 v \cos\alpha}{\lambda}
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

*Step 1:* The target acts as a moving observer. The frequency observed by the target is:

$$
f' = f \cdot \left(\frac{c - v\cos\alpha}{c}\right)^{-1} \approx f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{v\cos\alpha}{c}\right)
$$

(using first-order binomial for $v \ll c$).

*Step 2:* The target re-radiates at $f'$ and acts as a moving source. The detector sees the doubly shifted frequency:

$$
f'' = f' \cdot \left(1 + \frac{v\cos\alpha}{c}\right) = f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{v\cos\alpha}{c}\right)^2
$$

*Step 3:* For $v \ll c$, $(1 + v\cos\alpha/c)^2 \approx 1 + 2v\cos\alpha/c$. The beat frequency observed at the photodetector is:

$$
\Delta f = f'' - f \approx f \cdot \frac{2v\cos\alpha}{c} = \frac{2v\cos\alpha}{\lambda}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot \lambda}{2\cos\alpha}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** LDV is used for non-contact vibration measurement of rotating machinery, turbine blades, MEMS devices, and audio speaker characterization. Sensitivity is typically on the order of nanometers of displacement.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Light source:** A stable LED or laser diode (monochromatic) — usually IR at 860 nm to minimize color interference.
- **Collimating optics:** Lens L1 converts the source to a parallel beam.
- **Sample cell (cuvette):** Optical path length typically 2 to 10 cm, with optically flat glass windows.
- **Receiver optics:** Lens L2 focuses the transmitted light onto the detector.
- **Photodetector:** Silicon photodiode placed in-line (180°) with the source.
- **Logarithmic amplifier:** Computes $\ln(I_t/I_0)$ directly in analog domain (per Beer-Lambert law).
- **ADC + Microcontroller:** Digitizes and applies calibration to display in NTU.
- **Display and output:** Digital LCD in NTU; analog 4-20 mA / RS-485 for SCADA integration.

**Working (2 marks):**
Light of intensity $I_0$ passes through the sample. Particles in the sample scatter and absorb light, so the transmitted intensity $I_t$ reaching the detector is less than $I_0$. The ratio $I_t/I_0$ is a measure of optical attenuation, which is calibrated against Formazin standards to give turbidity in NTU.

**Beer-Lambert Law (2 marks):**

$$
I_t = I_0 \cdot e^{-\tau x}
$$

where $\tau$ is the turbidity coefficient (m$^{-1}$) and $x$ is the optical path length (m). Solving for $\tau$:

$$
\tau = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\left(\frac{I_t}{I_0}\right)
$$

The instrument's $\tau$-to-NTU conversion is performed using a stored calibration table generated from Formazin standards of 0, 10, 20, 40, 100, 400, and 4000 NTU.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in laser ToF distance equation. Students often write $d = ct$ instead of $d = ct/2$ — this gives *double* the actual distance. Examiner typically deducts **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing NTU and JTU** — JTU is obsolete (Jackson candle method) and should not be cited for modern electronic meters. Examiner may deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing Beer-Lambert without the negative sign** — students write $I_t = I_0 \cdot e^{+\tau x}$ which is physically impossible. Always include the negative sign.
> 4. **Using $I_s \propto I_0 \cdot N \cdot V$ (linear in $V$ instead of $V^2$)** — Rayleigh scattering has $V^2$ dependence. The squared term is critical and worth **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not drawing a proper block diagram** — KTU typically awards 2-3 marks for a neat, labeled block diagram. Skipping the diagram is a guaranteed loss.
> 6. **No units in the final answer** — Always include units (m, NTU, Hz, m/s) in numerical answers. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 7. **Confusing nephelometric (90°) with absorptiometric (180°)** — This is a frequently asked concept and worth full **2 marks** in a 7-mark question.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize These Points Before the Exam:**

- **LASER** = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation. Three essentials: **active medium, pumping source, optical resonator**.
- **Population inversion** ($N_2 > N_1$) is the prerequisite for lasing.
- **Properties of laser:** Monochromaticity, Coherence (spatial + temporal), Directionality, High intensity.
- **Laser ToF distance:** $d = ct/2$ (the factor of 2 is for round trip).
- **Laser Doppler shift:** $\Delta f = 2v\cos\alpha/\lambda$ — used in LDV.
- **Laser triangulation** is used for short range (mm to ~500 mm); **ToF** for long range.
- **Turbidity** is measured optically; defined as light scattering due to suspended particles.
- **Nephelometric method:** Detector at **90°**, best for **low turbidity** (0–40 NTU), regulatory standard.
- **Absorptiometric method:** Detector at **180°**, best for **high turbidity** (40–4000 NTU).
- **Beer-Lambert law:** $I_t = I_0 e^{-\tau x}$; $\tau = -\frac{1}{x}\ln(I_t/I_0)$.
- **Rayleigh scattering intensity:** $I_s \propto N \cdot V^2 / \lambda^4$ — direct proportionality to particle count and inverse fourth-power with wavelength.
- **Primary calibration standard:** **Formazin** (a polymer suspension of hydrazine sulfate + hexamethylenetetramine), stable for 1 year, 4000 NTU stock.
- **EPA Method 180.1** uses tungsten white light (400-600 nm); **ISO 7027** mandates 860 ± 30 nm IR source to avoid color interference.
- **Doppler frequency** is measured by beating reference and reflected beams on a photodetector (heterodyne detection).
- **Typical applications of laser sensors:** Level, distance, vibration, speed, thickness, surface profiling.
- **Typical applications of turbidity meters:** Water treatment plants, pharmaceutical QC, beverage clarity, boiler feed water, aquaculture.
- **NTU ≈ FTU ≈ FNU** for practical purposes; 1 NTU Formazin ≈ 0.58 mg/L silica (old scale).
